🎭 Full Mock Interview #2 — Java Mid/Senior Engineer

Company: LogiTech Solutions (Logistics & Supply Chain SaaS)

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (Phone Call, 25 min)

Setting: 10:00 AM, Tuesday. Phone call. Candidate is at home office.

👩 Rachel (HR): Hello! Is this Khanh?

👨 Khanh: Yes, hi! Good morning!

👩 Rachel: Hi Khanh! I'm Rachel from LogiTech Solutions. Thanks for applying — I'm really glad we could connect today. How's your day going?

👨 Khanh: It's going well, thank you! I'm excited about this conversation. I've been reading about LogiTech's warehouse management platform and it looks like you're doing some really interesting engineering work.

👩 Rachel: Oh that's great to hear! Yes, we're growing fast — just raised Series B and expanding the engineering team. So let me tell you a little about us first: LogiTech builds real-time inventory tracking and route optimization software for logistics companies across Southeast Asia. We process about 2 million shipment events per day. The engineering team is 45 people, and we're looking for senior Java engineers for the core platform team.

Does that sound like something that excites you?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. Real-time event processing at that scale — that's exactly the kind of problem I want to work on. In my current role I've been building middleware that handles API integrations, but the throughput is much lower. I want to level up to systems that need to handle millions of events reliably.

👩 Rachel: Love that. OK, so tell me — walk me through your background. What's your story?

👨 Khanh: Sure! I'm a Java backend engineer with about 4 and a half years of experience. I started my career at a healthcare IT company — spent about 2 years there building patient management systems. It was my bootcamp for learning how to write production-quality Java code. Strict data validation, audit logging, compliance — healthcare forces you to be careful.

Then I moved to my current company, a mid-size outsourcing firm, where I've been for about 2.5 years. Here I work on a middleware platform that sits between our client's customer-facing app and their global account management system. I handle authentication flows, API routing, and data transformation — about 15,000 API calls per day going through my services.

I chose healthcare first because I wanted a domain where bugs have real consequences — it taught me discipline. And I chose middleware because I wanted to understand how systems integrate at scale. Now I feel ready for a role where I'm closer to the product and working on higher-throughput systems.

👩 Rachel: That's a really clear progression. I like that you can articulate why you made each move. Now — why leave your current company? Be honest with me.

👨 Khanh: Honestly, my current project has become very stable. The middleware is mature — most of my work now is monitoring, minor bug fixes, and coordination with the client's development team. I'm learning less each month.

I want to be in an environment where I'm designing new features, solving performance problems, and working with a team that's building something that's actively growing. I also want to transition from outsourcing to a product company — I want to own the product, not just deliver it to someone else.

LogiTech is interesting because you're at that Series B stage where engineering decisions really matter — the systems you build now will be the foundation for the next 5 years. I want to be part of that.

👩 Rachel: That resonates. We do need people who want to own things. Let me ask about compensation — what range are you targeting?

👨 Khanh: Based on my research for senior Java roles in product companies in this market, I'm looking at the range of $X to $Y. But I'm genuinely more motivated by the technical environment and growth opportunity — if the role is right, I have some flexibility on the number. What does your budget look like for this position?

👩 Rachel: We're within that range, so that's good news. Are you interviewing elsewhere?

👨 Khanh: Yes, I'm in conversations with two other companies — one fintech and one e-commerce platform. Both are at the first technical round stage. But I'll be transparent — LogiTech is very high on my list because of the real-time data processing aspect and the fact that you're a product company, not outsourcing.

👩 Rachel: Great, we'll try to move quickly then. The process is 3 rounds: this call, then a behavioral with the engineering manager, then a technical round. Can you do all three within the next 10 days?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. I can be flexible with scheduling — evenings, weekends, or I can take time off during the week.

👩 Rachel: Perfect. Before I let you go — any questions for me?

👨 Khanh: Two questions. First — what's the team structure like? Would I be joining a specific squad, and how many engineers are on it?

👩 Rachel: You'd join the Platform Squad — 6 engineers, 1 tech lead, 1 PM. They own the core event processing pipeline and the warehouse management APIs.

👨 Khanh: That sounds great — small enough to have impact. Second question — what does success look like for this role in the first 6 months? What would make you say "this hire was a great decision"?

👩 Rachel: Hmm, great question! Honestly — someone who can ramp up in 2-3 months, own a service end-to-end by month 4, and start contributing to architecture discussions by month 6. We don't expect you to know everything on day one.

👨 Khanh: That's exactly the kind of growth trajectory I'm looking for. Thanks Rachel — really appreciate the transparency!

👩 Rachel: Likewise! I'll send the next round invite by tomorrow. Have a great day!

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL INTERVIEW (Video Call, 40 min)

Setting: Thursday, 3 PM. Google Meet. Engineering Manager + Senior Engineer.

👨‍💼 Tom (Engineering Manager): Hey Khanh! I'm Tom, EM for the Platform team. And this is Priya — she's a senior engineer you'd be working closely with.

👩‍💻 Priya: Hi!

👨 Khanh: Great to meet you both!

👨‍💼 Tom: So this round is all about understanding how you work — your problem-solving style, how you handle pressure, teamwork. No coding today. Just stories and conversation. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Ready!

👨‍💼 Tom: First one. Tell me about a time when a **production system you were responsible for had a critical issue**. What happened, what did you do, and what was the outcome?

👨 Khanh: Sure, this happened about 8 months ago.

[Situation] Our middleware handles authentication token exchange between the client app and the global identity provider. One Monday morning, we started getting alerts — about 30% of login requests were failing with 401 errors. Customer support tickets were flooding in.

[Task] I was the on-call engineer that week, so it was on me to investigate and coordinate the fix.

[Action] First thing I did was check our dashboards — Grafana showed the error rate spike started at 6 AM, exactly when a scheduled job runs to rotate API keys. I pulled the logs and found that the key rotation job had succeeded, but one of three downstream services hadn't picked up the new key — it was still using the cached old key.

The root cause: our key refresh mechanism used a polling interval of 30 minutes, but the old key was being invalidated immediately after rotation. So there was a 30-minute window where that service was using an invalid key.

For the immediate fix, I manually pushed the new key to the affected service's cache and restarted it — that stopped the bleeding in about 15 minutes. Then over the next 2 days, I redesigned the key rotation to use a pub/sub notification pattern: when keys rotate, all services get notified immediately via a Redis pub/sub channel instead of polling.

I also added a grace period — old keys stay valid for 5 minutes after rotation — as a safety net.

[Result] We haven't had a key-related outage since. The total downtime was about 40 minutes, and the permanent fix was deployed within the same week. I also wrote a post-mortem document and presented it to the team to prevent similar issues in other services.

👨‍💼 Tom: That's solid. I like that you distinguished between the immediate fix and the long-term fix. Priya, any follow-up?

👩‍💻 Priya: Yeah — when you were investigating, how did you decide where to look first? With 30% failures, there could be many causes.

👨 Khanh: Good question. My mental framework is: check what changed recently. 30% failures starting at exactly 6 AM — that's not a gradual degradation, it's a cliff. So something triggered it. I checked our deployment log — no deploys that morning. Then I checked scheduled jobs — and the key rotation matched perfectly. Once I had the timing correlation, I looked at which specific service was failing and traced back from there.

Basically: timeline first, then narrow down by correlation.

👨‍💼 Tom: Next question. Tell me about a time you **received critical feedback** from a manager or peer. How did you handle it?

👨 Khanh: This one stung a little at the time, honestly.

[Situation] About a year ago, during a quarterly review, my tech lead told me that my code was solid but my documentation was lacking. He said: "When you're on vacation, nobody can understand your services. There's no runbook, no architecture diagram, no README beyond setup instructions."

[Task] I needed to improve not just my documentation but my mindset — I had always thought "clean code documents itself." My tech lead challenged that assumption.

[Action] I spent the next sprint creating proper documentation for my two main services. Not just code comments — I wrote operational runbooks: "what to do when this alert fires," "how to restart this service safely," and architecture decision records explaining why we chose certain approaches.

I also started a practice of writing a "handoff document" before any leave — current state of my work, known issues, who to contact.

[Result] Six months later, when I took a 2-week vacation, the team handled two production issues on my services without needing to call me. My tech lead specifically mentioned the improvement in my next review. It completely changed how I think about code ownership — it's not just about writing it, it's about making sure others can operate it.

👨‍💼 Tom: That's a mature response. A lot of people get defensive about feedback. I appreciate that you actually changed behavior.

👩‍💻 Priya: My turn! Khanh, tell me about a time you had to learn a new technology or tool quickly to deliver a project. How did you approach the learning?

👨 Khanh: Yes! When I joined my current project, they were using Kafka for event streaming, which I had zero experience with. I had about 3 weeks before I needed to implement a new consumer for processing account update events.

[Action] My approach was three layers:

First, conceptual understanding — I spent 2 days reading the Confluent documentation and watching their free course. I focused on understanding partitions, consumer groups, offsets, and exactly-once semantics. Not the code yet — just the mental model.

Second, hands-on prototype — I set up a local Kafka cluster using Docker Compose and wrote a simple producer/consumer in Spring Boot. Intentionally broke things: what happens when a consumer crashes mid-processing? What happens with duplicate messages? I wanted to understand the failure modes.

Third, production patterns — I read our team's existing Kafka consumers to understand the conventions: error handling, dead letter queues, monitoring. Then I pair-programmed my first real consumer with a senior teammate who knew Kafka well.

[Result] I delivered the consumer on time. It processes about 5,000 account update events per day now. And more importantly, I've since become the person on the team that others ask about Kafka-related questions — because I invested in understanding the fundamentals, not just copy-pasting.

👩‍💻 Priya: Love the structured approach. Especially the part about intentionally breaking things — that's how you really learn.

👨‍💼 Tom: Last one from me. Imagine this scenario: you've been at LogiTech for 6 months. You realize that **a core service has a design flaw** that will cause scaling issues in 6 months when we double our traffic. But the team is focused on feature delivery. What do you do?

👨 Khanh: I wouldn't stay silent about it — that would be irresponsible. But I also wouldn't just say "stop everything, we need to refactor."

My approach would be:

First, I'd quantify the risk. Not just "this might break" — I'd do a load test or back-of-envelope calculation showing exactly at what traffic level we hit the bottleneck. Numbers convince people, opinions don't.

Second, I'd propose a plan with options. Maybe: Option A — full redesign, takes 4 weeks, blocks feature work. Option B — targeted fix on the critical bottleneck, takes 1 week, buys us 6 more months. Option C — do nothing, accept the risk, plan a fix for next quarter.

Third, I'd present it to Tom — the EM — with the data and options. Let the team decide together. I'd advocate for my recommendation but ultimately respect the team's decision on priority.

And if we choose to defer it, I'd make sure it's documented — a tech debt ticket with the analysis attached, not just a vague "refactor service X" ticket that gets ignored forever.

👨‍💼 Tom: Exactly the answer I was hoping for. Data-driven, pragmatic, collaborative. Not a cowboy, not passive. Good.

Any questions for us before we wrap up?

👨 Khanh: Yes, two! First — what's the most painful technical problem the Platform team is facing right now?

👨‍💼 Tom: Ha! Honestly — our event processing pipeline is a monolith. We need to break it into independent processors, but it's tightly coupled. That's actually one of the projects the new hire would help with.

👨 Khanh: That sounds like a great challenge. Second question for Priya — what does code review culture look like? Is it thorough-and-slow or fast-and-lightweight?

👩‍💻 Priya: It's thorough. Every PR needs at least one approval, and we actually discuss design in comments, not just syntax. Reviews usually take a few hours, not days. We value it a lot.

👨 Khanh: That's my preference too. Thanks both!

👨‍💼 Tom: Great session, Khanh. Technical round is next week with Alex. Good luck!

🎭 Full Mock Interview #2 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: LogiTech Solutions | Role: Senior Java Engineer

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL INTERVIEW (Video Call, 60 min)

Setting: Monday, 2 PM. Google Meet + shared code editor. Staff Engineer.

👨‍💻 Alex (Staff Engineer): Hey Khanh, I'm Alex. I lead architecture on the Platform team. Today we'll cover three parts:

  1. Java & Spring deep-dive — 15 min
  2. System design — 20 min
  3. Live coding — 25 min

No gotcha questions. I care more about your thinking process than getting a perfect answer. Sound good?

👨 Khanh: Sounds great, let's do it!

PART A — Java & Spring Deep-Dive (15 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: Let's start with something practical. In your middleware project — you're using Spring Boot, right? Can you explain **how a request flows through a Spring Boot application** — from the moment an HTTP request hits the server to when the response goes back?

👨 Khanh: Sure. Let me trace it:

  1. Tomcat receives the request — Spring Boot embeds Tomcat by default.

It assigns the request to a thread from its thread pool (default 200 threads).

  1. Filter chain — the request passes through servlet filters in order.

This includes Spring Security filters if configured — authentication, CORS, CSRF checks happen here.

  1. DispatcherServlet — the central front controller. It receives all

requests and delegates to the right handler.

  1. Handler mapping — DispatcherServlet consults HandlerMapping to find

which controller method matches the URL pattern and HTTP method. It checks @RequestMapping annotations.

  1. Interceptors — if any HandlerInterceptors are registered, their

preHandle() runs before the controller. Good for logging, auth checks.

  1. Controller method execution — the matched @RestController method runs.

Spring resolves @RequestBody using HttpMessageConverters (Jackson for JSON), validates @Valid annotations using Bean Validation, injects @PathVariable and @RequestParam values.

  1. Service → Repository — controller calls service layer, which calls

repository. If @Transactional is on the service method, Spring's AOP proxy wraps it in a transaction before calling.

  1. Response serialization — the return object is serialized to JSON

by Jackson, interceptor's postHandle() runs, then afterCompletion().

  1. Response sent — goes back through the filter chain (in reverse)

and Tomcat sends the HTTP response.

The key insight is: it's a pipeline. Filters → DispatcherServlet → Interceptors → Controller → Service → Repository, then back up.

👨‍💻 Alex: Really thorough. Follow-up — what happens if that Tomcat thread pool (200 threads) gets fully exhausted?

👨 Khanh: New requests queue up. If the queue also fills, requests get rejected with connection timeouts. This is why long-blocking operations inside controllers are dangerous — they hold threads hostage.

Solutions: make external calls async or non-blocking, use @Async for background work, introduce timeouts on HTTP clients (like RestTemplate or WebClient), or switch to WebFlux for reactive non-blocking I/O if the use case supports it.

In my current project, we set RestTemplate timeout to 5 seconds and added circuit breakers with Resilience4j to fail-fast when downstream services are slow.

👨‍💻 Alex: Good. Next — explain the Spring Bean lifecycle. What happens from creation to destruction?

👨 Khanh: The lifecycle:

  1. Instantiation — Spring creates the bean via constructor

(or factory method).

  1. Dependency Injection — Spring injects dependencies:

constructor injection, setter injection, or field injection via @Autowired. Constructor is preferred because it makes dependencies explicit and supports immutability.

  1. Aware interfaces — if the bean implements BeanNameAware,

ApplicationContextAware, etc., Spring calls those setters. This gives the bean access to its own metadata.

  1. @PostConstruct — runs after all dependencies are injected.

This is where I put initialization logic: loading config, warming caches, validating required properties.

  1. InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet() — another init hook

(I prefer @PostConstruct — it's cleaner).

  1. Bean is ready — it's in the application context, available

for injection into other beans.

  1. @PreDestroy — runs during application shutdown. Good for

cleanup: closing connections, flushing buffers, graceful shutdown.

  1. DisposableBean.destroy() — another destruction hook.

Most beans are singleton scope by default — one instance per application context. For request-scoped beans (like per-HTTP-request), Spring creates and destroys them per request using a proxy.

👨‍💻 Alex: One more. What's the difference between **@Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller**? Is it just cosmetic?

👨 Khanh: Functionally, @Service and @Controller are just specializations of @Component — they all register the class as a Spring-managed bean. So yes, for component scanning, they're equivalent.

But there are real differences:

  • @Repository — has a special behavior: Spring wraps it with a

persistence exception translation proxy. Database-specific exceptions (like Hibernate's PersistenceException) get translated to Spring's DataAccessException hierarchy. This decouples your code from the specific database vendor.

  • @Controller — tells DispatcherServlet this class handles HTTP

requests. Without it (or @RestController), handler mapping won't pick it up.

  • @Service — truly is cosmetic today, but it's important for

clarity. When I read code, @Service tells me "this is business logic," @Repository tells me "this is data access." It's a form of documentation in code.

And in the future, Spring could add behavior to @Service — like automatic transaction wrapping — so using the right annotation is future-proofing.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN (20 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: OK, let's switch to design. Here's the problem:

*"Design a real-time shipment tracking system. Logistics companies have drivers with GPS devices that send location updates every 30 seconds. Customers want to see their package's current location on a map. Expected scale: 50,000 active drivers, 200,000 concurrent customers viewing tracking pages."*

Walk me through your approach.

👨 Khanh: [Takes 60 seconds to think]

Let me start with requirements, then architecture.

Functional Requirements:

  • Drivers send GPS coordinates every 30 seconds
  • Customers can view real-time location of their shipment on a map
  • Historical route tracking (where has the package been?)

Non-Functional:

  • 50K drivers × 1 update/30s = ~1,700 location events/second (write)
  • 200K concurrent viewers → need real-time push, not polling
  • Low latency: location update should reach viewer within 2-3 seconds
  • High availability: customers expect the map to always work

High-Level Architecture:

Drivers (GPS)
    │
    ▼ (MQTT or HTTP POST)
┌──────────────────┐
│  Ingestion API   │  (stateless, auto-scaled)
│  (Spring Boot)   │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│     Kafka         │  Topic: "location-updates"
│   (partitioned    │  Partition key: driver_id
│    by driver_id)  │  (ensures ordering per driver)
└────┬─────────┬───┘
     │         │
     ▼         ▼
┌─────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐
│ Writer  │  │  Real-Time Push   │
│ Service │  │  Service          │
│         │  │                   │
│ Writes  │  │ Consumes Kafka    │
│ to DB   │  │ Pushes to clients │
│ (Timescale│ │ via WebSocket     │
│  DB)    │  │ or SSE            │
└─────────┘  └─────────┬─────────┘
                        │
                        ▼
               ┌─────────────────┐
               │  WebSocket      │
               │  Connections    │
               │  (200K clients) │
               └─────────────────┘

Database Choice:

For current location — Redis. Key: shipment:{id}, value: {lat, lng, timestamp}. Every GPS update overwrites the previous. This gives O(1) lookup when a customer first opens the map.

For historical route — TimescaleDB (PostgreSQL extension for time-series). Append-only writes, great for "show me the route on a map" queries with time-range filters.

Real-Time Push to Customers:

I'd use WebSocket connections. When a customer opens the tracking page:

  1. HTTP request fetches current location from Redis (instant)
  2. WebSocket connection established
  3. Real-Time Push Service subscribes to that shipment's updates
  4. When new GPS data arrives in Kafka, the Push Service sends it

through the WebSocket to all viewers of that shipment

For 200K concurrent WebSocket connections — a single server handles about 50K connections. So 4-5 Push Service instances behind a load balancer with sticky sessions.

Scaling Considerations:

  • Kafka partitioning by driver_id ensures ordered processing per driver
  • Redis cluster for current location cache — sharded by shipment_id
  • WebSocket scaling: use Redis pub/sub as a broadcast layer between

Push Service instances. When one instance gets a GPS update, it publishes to Redis channel → all instances with viewers for that shipment push the update

  • Backpressure: if a driver sends too fast, the Ingestion API

rate-limits by driver_id

👨‍💻 Alex: Good design. What if a customer's WebSocket disconnects and reconnects 2 minutes later — how do they catch up on missed updates?

👨 Khanh: On reconnect, the client sends its last_seen_timestamp. The server queries Redis for the current location (latest). If the customer needs the path during the gap, a quick query to TimescaleDB with WHERE shipment_id = ? AND timestamp > last_seen fills the gap.

In practice, for a tracking map, the customer only cares about the current position — so just fetching from Redis on reconnect is usually enough. The historical route is for the "full trip view" feature, not real-time tracking.

👨‍💻 Alex: Smart distinction. Last question on design — what's your monitoring strategy? How do you know if this system is healthy?

👨 Khanh: Key metrics I'd track:

  • Ingestion lag: time between GPS event and Kafka produce. Alert if > 1s.
  • Kafka consumer lag: if the Push Service falls behind. Alert if > 1000 events.
  • WebSocket connection count: per instance. Alert if nearing capacity.
  • Redis hit rate: for current location lookups. Should be > 99%.
  • End-to-end latency: GPS event to WebSocket push. SLA: < 3 seconds.

Measure with distributed tracing (Jaeger or OpenTelemetry).

  • Error rate: per service. Dashboard in Grafana, alert to Slack/PagerDuty.

PART C — LIVE CODING (25 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: Great design. Let's do some coding now.

*"Given an array of strings, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order."*

Example:

Input:  ["eat","tea","tan","ate","nat","bat"]
Output: [["eat","tea","ate"], ["tan","nat"], ["bat"]]

Think out loud, then code.

👨 Khanh: OK, let me think through this.

Anagrams have the same characters in different orders. So "eat" and "tea" both have {a, e, t}.

If I sort each string, anagrams will produce the same sorted key:

  • "eat" → "aet"
  • "tea" → "aet" → same key!
  • "bat" → "abt" → different key

So the approach is: use a HashMap where the key is the sorted string, and the value is the list of original strings that share that key.

Time complexity: O(n × k log k) where n = number of strings, k = max string length (for sorting each string).

Space: O(n × k) for the HashMap.

Let me also think — can I avoid sorting? Yes, I could use a frequency count array as the key (e.g., "1a1e1t" → string form). That would be O(n × k) time. But for simplicity and clarity, I'll go with the sort approach first and mention the optimization.

public List<List<String>> groupAnagrams(String[] strs) {
    // Key = sorted form of string, Value = list of anagrams
    Map<String, List<String>> map = new HashMap<>();

    for (String s : strs) {
        // Sort characters to create canonical key
        char[] chars = s.toCharArray();
        Arrays.sort(chars);
        String key = new String(chars);

        // Add to the group
        map.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(s);
    }

    return new ArrayList<>(map.values());
}

Let me trace through the example:

"eat" → sort → "aet" → map: {"aet": ["eat"]}
"tea" → sort → "aet" → map: {"aet": ["eat","tea"]}
"tan" → sort → "ant" → map: {"aet": ["eat","tea"], "ant": ["tan"]}
"ate" → sort → "aet" → map: {"aet": ["eat","tea","ate"], "ant": ["tan"]}
"nat" → sort → "ant" → map: {"aet": [...], "ant": ["tan","nat"]}
"bat" → sort → "abt" → map: {"aet": [...], "ant": [...], "abt": ["bat"]}

return values = [["eat","tea","ate"], ["tan","nat"], ["bat"]] ✓

Edge cases:

  • Empty array → return empty list
  • Single string → return list with one group
  • All strings are the same → one big group
  • Empty strings → "" sorted is "" → all group together ✓

👨‍💻 Alex: Clean solution, good trace-through. You mentioned an O(n×k) optimization — how would that work?

👨 Khanh: Instead of sorting, I'd count character frequencies and encode them as a string key:

private String getFreqKey(String s) {
    int[] freq = new int[26];
    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) freq[c - 'a']++;
    
    // Build key like "1#0#0#...#1#...#1#0..."
    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
    for (int f : freq) {
        sb.append(f).append('#'); // separator needed!
    }
    return sb.toString();
}

The # separator is critical — without it, "abc" (1,1,1,0...) and a string with freq "11,1,0..." would collide. With separators: "1#1#1#0#..." vs "11#1#0#..." — different keys.

This avoids the k log k sorting cost. Better for very long strings, but in practice both are fast for typical inputs.

👨‍💻 Alex: Excellent — great detail on the separator issue. One more follow-up: if the input was a stream of millions of strings and you needed to find anagram groups in real-time, how would you adapt this?

👨 Khanh: In a streaming scenario, I'd use a distributed approach:

  1. Hash each string's sorted form (or frequency key) to determine

a partition — this ensures all anagrams go to the same worker.

  1. Use Kafka with the sorted key as the partition key.
  2. Each consumer maintains its local HashMap of anagram groups.
  3. For output, each consumer emits group updates when new strings

arrive for an existing group.

If the groups need to be queried in real-time, I'd store them in Redis: key = sorted form, value = set of original strings. SADD sorted_key original_string for each incoming string. Query any group with SMEMBERS.

👨‍💻 Alex: Outstanding. That's all from me. Really strong session. Any questions?

👨 Khanh: Thanks Alex! Two questions:

First — what's the tech stack for the Platform team? Java version, Spring Boot version, message broker?

👨‍💻 Alex: Java 17, Spring Boot 3.2, Kafka for messaging, PostgreSQL + Redis for storage, deployed on AWS EKS.

👨 Khanh: Nice, that's a modern stack. Second — how does the team handle incident response? Is there a formal on-call rotation?

👨‍💻 Alex: Yes, weekly on-call rotation. We use PagerDuty, runbooks in Notion, and post-mortems for every P1/P2 incident. No blame culture — we focus on systems, not people.

👨 Khanh: That's really healthy. Thanks for the detailed answers!

👨‍💻 Alex: Thank you Khanh — we'll be in touch soon!

📊 FULL INTERVIEW SCORECARD

Round 1 — HR Screening ✅

Category Score Notes
Self-introduction 9/10 Clear story with WHY behind each move
Motivation to leave 9/10 Honest, no negativity, growth-focused
Company research 9/10 Mentioned Series B, event processing scale
Salary discussion 8/10 Range given, turned question back to recruiter
Questions asked 10/10 "Success in 6 months?" is an elite question

Round 2 — Behavioral ✅

Category Score Notes
Production incident (STAR) 10/10 Root cause, immediate fix, long-term fix, post-mortem
Receiving feedback 9/10 Vulnerable, showed real behavior change
Learning new tech 9/10 3-layer approach: concept → prototype → production
Hypothetical: design flaw 10/10 Data-driven, options, pragmatic, documented
Questions asked 9/10 "Most painful problem?" = smart intel-gathering

Round 3 — Technical ✅

Category Score Notes
Spring request lifecycle 10/10 Full pipeline, mentioned thread exhaustion
Bean lifecycle 9/10 Complete lifecycle, mentioned scopes
Annotations 9/10 @Repository exception translation = deep knowledge
System Design 9/10 Kafka + Redis + WebSocket, reconnect strategy
Monitoring awareness 10/10 6 specific metrics with thresholds
Coding: Group Anagrams 10/10 Clean solution, trace, edge cases, optimization
Streaming follow-up 10/10 Kafka partitioning + Redis SADD = production-grade

🏆 OVERALL VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

🔑 Key Techniques That Made This Interview Excellent

HR Round

  1. Story arc: "healthcare (discipline) → middleware (integration) → now want scale"
  2. Reverse the salary question: "What's your budget?" shifts power
  3. "Success in 6 months?" — shows you think about outcomes, not just tasks

Behavioral Round

  1. STAR + Post-mortem: always mention what you did AFTER to prevent recurrence
  2. Vulnerability on feedback: "it stung" — then show the growth. Very human.
  3. Quantified options: "Option A = 4 weeks, Option B = 1 week" — EM loves this

Technical Round

  1. Trace the full pipeline: request → filter → controller → service → response
  2. Mention failure modes: "what if thread pool exhausts?" before being asked
  3. Design with monitoring built in: not an afterthought
  4. Code + trace + edge cases + optimization: the complete package

🎭 Full Mock Interview #3 — Java Mid/Senior Engineer

Company: CloudMart (B2C E-Commerce Platform, 1200 employees)

Context: CloudMart is a large e-commerce company selling electronics and lifestyle products across 8 countries. ~500K orders/day. Engineering team of 200+. They need senior Java engineers for the Order & Checkout team.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (Video Call, 25 min)

👩 Diana (Talent Acquisition Lead): Hi Khanh! I'm Diana. Welcome! Before we begin — have you used CloudMart as a customer before?

👨 Khanh: Hi Diana! Actually yes — I ordered headphones last month. The checkout was smooth, but I noticed the delivery tracking page could be faster — it took a few seconds to load each time I checked. So I'm already thinking about problems I could help solve!

**[📝 Brilliant opener — turned a casual question into showing product awareness AND engineering observation.]**

👩 Diana: Ha! I love that. Our tracking team would love to hear that. OK so — I know you've talked to recruiters before, so I'll skip the company pitch. Let me jump right in.

👩 Diana: Tell me — if I called your current manager right now and asked them to describe you in 3 words, what would they say?

👨 Khanh: Hmm, good question. I think they'd say: reliable, thorough, and quiet leader.

Reliable — because in 2.5 years, I've never missed a deployment deadline or dropped the ball on an on-call incident. My manager knows that if something lands on my plate, it gets done.

Thorough — because I write detailed investigation reports and my PRs come with context: what I changed, why, what I considered but rejected. My manager once told me "your PRs are the easiest to review on the team."

Quiet leader — I don't lead by being the loudest voice. But I'm the person juniors come to when they're stuck, and I often end up facilitating technical discussions because I ask the right clarifying questions. My manager called me "the team's glue" in my last review.

**[📝 Shows self-awareness + backs each word with evidence. "Quiet leader" is honest and authentic — more believable than "visionary innovator".]**

👩 Diana: Great self-awareness. Now — be honest with me. What's a weakness you're actively working on?

👨 Khanh: I tend to over-research before acting. When I get a complex task, I want to understand every edge case before writing the first line of code. That's good for quality, but sometimes I spend too long in the analysis phase when a quick prototype would answer my questions faster.

I've been working on this by setting time-boxes: "I'll research for 2 hours max, then build a proof-of-concept, even if I'm not 100% sure." I've found that the prototype often reveals things that research never would — like performance issues you can only find by actually running code.

My manager noticed the improvement and said I'm shipping faster this quarter without sacrificing quality.

**[📝 Real weakness (not a disguised strength like "I'm too perfectionist"). Shows active improvement with a specific technique. Manager validation.]**

👩 Diana: Let's talk compensation. We pay between $X and $Z for senior Java engineers. Where in that range do you see yourself?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, I'd position myself in the upper-mid to high part of that range — around $Y. Here's my reasoning:

I bring 4.5 years of production experience, I've owned services end-to-end including on-call, and I've worked in both healthcare (compliance-heavy) and fintech (integration-heavy) — so I'm adaptable to new domains quickly.

That said, I'm also evaluating the full picture — I'd value things like learning budget, conference attendance, and internal mobility between teams. Does CloudMart offer any of those?

👩 Diana: Yes! We have a $2K annual learning budget, we sponsor conference attendance, and engineers can do a "team rotation" every 18 months if they want to explore a different domain. Very popular with our senior engineers.

👨 Khanh: That's fantastic. The rotation program alone is worth a lot to me — it solves the "plateau" problem I'm experiencing now.

**[📝 Justified salary with evidence. Asked about non-cash benefits. Made the rotation program part of his compensation narrative.]**

👩 Diana: One more — what does your ideal work-life balance look like? We want to be upfront about expectations.

👨 Khanh: I'm most productive with a structured schedule — focused coding in the morning, meetings in the afternoon. I'm happy to do on-call rotations — I've done weekly on-call for the past 2 years.

For urgent production issues, I'm absolutely available outside hours. But I don't think "grinding 12-hour days" is sustainable or productive. I'd rather deliver high-quality work in focused 8-hour days than mediocre work in 12.

What does CloudMart's on-call look like? Is it shared across the team?

👩 Diana: Yes — weekly rotation, shared across 6 engineers, so roughly once every 6 weeks. Alerts go to PagerDuty, and P1 incidents are rare — maybe once a quarter.

👨 Khanh: That sounds very manageable. Appreciate the transparency!

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL (Video Call, 45 min)

Setting: Wednesday, 10 AM. Panel: Engineering Manager + Staff Engineer.

👨‍💼 Marcus (EM, Order Team): Hey Khanh! I'm Marcus, EM for Orders & Checkout. And this is Hana — Staff Engineer on the same team.

👩‍💻 Hana: Hi Khanh!

👨 Khanh: Great to meet you both! I'm excited to learn more about how checkout works at 500K orders/day.

👨‍💼 Marcus: Let's start with a tough one. Tell me about a time you made a mistake that affected production. Not someone else's mistake — YOUR mistake. What happened?

👨 Khanh: Yes — and this is one I learned the most from.

[Situation] About a year ago, I was implementing a change to our token refresh logic. The middleware refreshes OAuth tokens every 25 minutes (tokens expire at 30). Straightforward change — I was updating the retry logic for failed refreshes.

[Task] Simple code change, small PR. Reviewed, approved, deployed on a Wednesday afternoon.

[Mistake] I had a typo in the retry delay configuration. Instead of setting the retry delay to 5000 milliseconds (5 seconds), I wrote 5 (which was interpreted as 5 milliseconds). So when a token refresh failed, the service retried every 5ms — essentially a tight infinite loop hammering the identity provider.

[Impact] The identity provider started rate-limiting us within minutes. All token refreshes failed. 100% of authenticated requests started returning 401 for about 20 minutes until I rolled back.

[What I did] First — rollback immediately once I identified the cause. That took about 12 minutes from first alert to fix.

Then I asked myself: "How did this get past code review AND testing?" The answer: the unit test mocked the HTTP client, so it never actually called the identity provider. And the code reviewer focused on the retry logic correctness, not the configuration values.

[Changes I made]

  1. Added a validation rule: retry delays below 100ms throw a

startup exception. The system refuses to start with dangerous config.

  1. Introduced an integration test that runs against a local mock

identity provider (WireMock) and verifies actual retry behavior.

  1. Started a team practice: for any config value change, the PR

description must include "before value: X, after value: Y" explicitly.

[Result] We've had zero config-related incidents since. And the validation rule has actually caught two similar mistakes by other team members before they reached production.

👨‍💼 Marcus: That's exactly the kind of answer I look for. You owned it completely — no excuses, no blaming the reviewer. And you turned it into systemic improvements.

👩‍💻 Hana: I love the startup validation idea. We should do that.

👩‍💻 Hana: My question — tell me about a time you had to work with a difficult dependency on another team. Maybe they were slow, unresponsive, or their API kept changing.

👨 Khanh: Oh, this is a familiar pain.

[Situation] Our middleware integrates with the global account management team — they're based in Europe, so we have a 6-hour timezone gap. We needed them to add a new field to their API response for a feature our client urgently needed. The client had a hard deadline of 4 weeks.

[Problem] After submitting the request, it sat in their backlog for 2 weeks. We were halfway through the deadline with no API change. Their PM kept saying "next sprint, next sprint."

[Action] I realized that going through formal channels wasn't working. So I did three things:

First, I reached out directly to their senior engineer — not to complain, but to understand their constraints. Turned out they were in a crunch for their own quarterly release. Legitimate reason.

Second, I proposed a compromise: instead of them adding a new field to their existing API, could we access the data through their existing internal event stream? They already published account events to a Kafka topic. The data I needed was already there — just not in the REST API response.

Third, I prototyped a Kafka consumer on our side that consumed their account events and cached the field I needed locally. This required zero changes from them.

[Result] I built the consumer in 3 days. We met the client deadline with a week to spare. The European team actually liked the approach so much that they recommended it as a pattern for other teams. And later — when they had bandwidth — they did add the REST field for other consumers who couldn't use Kafka.

👩‍💻 Hana: Smart. You unblocked yourself without creating conflict. That's senior-level problem solving.

👨‍💼 Marcus: Scenario question. You're on the Order team at CloudMart. A PM comes to you and says: *"We need to add a Buy Now Pay Later feature to checkout. Our competitor launched it last week. Can you estimate how long it'll take?"*

You've never built BNPL before. What do you do?

👨 Khanh: First — I don't give a number. Not yet. I've been burned by quick estimates.

What I'd do:

Day 1 — Scope clarification. I'd ask the PM:

  • Are we building BNPL in-house or integrating with a provider (Atome,

Kredivo, Afterpay)?

  • Which payment methods and countries first?
  • What's the user flow — does approval happen at checkout or in advance?
  • What's the real deadline — "ASAP" or a specific business event?

Most "2-day estimates" fail because the scope wasn't clear.

Day 2-3 — Technical investigation. I'd spend 2 days doing:

  • Read the BNPL provider's API docs (assuming we're integrating)
  • Map out what changes to our checkout flow, order model, and

payment service

  • Identify risks: do we need legal/compliance review? New DB tables?

Changes to the refund flow?

  • Talk to the payments team senior engineer — they might know gotchas

Day 4 — Estimate with ranges. I'd present:

  • Optimistic: 3 weeks (if BNPL provider has clean SDK, no compliance blockers)
  • Realistic: 5-6 weeks (integration + testing + edge cases like partial refunds)
  • Pessimistic: 8 weeks (if compliance review is slow or provider API has issues)

I'd also recommend a phased rollout: launch for one country first, learn from it, then expand. That way we ship something in 3 weeks instead of waiting 6 weeks for everything.

👨‍💼 Marcus: Perfect. You didn't panic-estimate, you scoped first, and you offered a phased approach. That's exactly what I'd want from a senior engineer.

👨‍💼 Marcus: Last question. What kind of engineering culture do you thrive in? And what kills your motivation?

👨 Khanh: I thrive in environments where:

  • Engineers have ownership — not just writing tickets someone else

designed. I want to be part of "what should we build?" not just "how should we build it?"

  • Code review is real — I learn more from good reviews than

from any course

  • Failure is treated as learning — blameless post-mortems, not

"whose fault is this?"

What kills my motivation:

  • Being a "feature factory" — shipping features without measuring

if they actually work for users

  • No tech debt budget — always building new stuff on a shaky foundation
  • Politics over merit — if promotions depend on visibility rather than

impact, I'll disengage

I've heard from your engineering blog that CloudMart does "build weeks" where teams can work on internal tools and tech debt. That's a huge green flag for me.

👨‍💼 Marcus: We do! One week per quarter. Engineers love it. Great answers Khanh. Hana and I are very impressed. Let's move to the technical round.

🎭 Full Mock Interview #3 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: CloudMart | Role: Senior Java Engineer, Order & Checkout

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL (Video Call, 65 min)

Setting: Friday, 2 PM. Google Meet + CoderPad. Two interviewers.

👨‍💻 Ryan (Principal Engineer): Hey Khanh! I'm Ryan, Principal Engineer on the Platform team. And Jun here is a Senior on the Order team — the team you'd be joining.

👩‍💻 Jun: Hey!

👨‍💻 Ryan: Three sections today:

  1. Java concurrency & performance (15 min)
  2. Database & ORM (10 min)
  3. System design: flash sale checkout (20 min)
  4. Live coding (20 min)

PART A — Java Concurrency & Performance (15 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: At CloudMart, checkout calls 5 external services in parallel: inventory check, price validation, fraud detection, payment gateway, and loyalty points. How would you orchestrate these calls in Java?

👨 Khanh: I'd use CompletableFuture for parallel execution: 

CompletableFuture<InventoryResult> inventory =
    CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> inventoryService.check(items), executor);

CompletableFuture<PriceResult> price =
    CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> priceService.validate(items), executor);

CompletableFuture<FraudResult> fraud =
    CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fraudService.assess(order), executor);

// Wait for all to complete (with timeout)
CompletableFuture.allOf(inventory, price, fraud)
    .get(3, TimeUnit.SECONDS); // hard timeout at 3 seconds

// Get results
InventoryResult inv = inventory.get();
PriceResult pri = price.get();
FraudResult fra = fraud.get();

Key design decisions:

  1. Custom thread pool — I'd NOT use ForkJoinPool.commonPool()

(the default). In a web server, the common pool is shared across the entire application. If checkout exhausts it, unrelated features break. I'd create a dedicated ThreadPoolExecutor with bounded size — say 20 threads — named "checkout-executor" for debugging visibility.

  1. Timeout — allOf().get(3, SECONDS) ensures we fail fast.

Without it, one slow service blocks the entire checkout.

  1. Partial failure handling — not all services are equal.

Inventory and price are mandatory — if they fail, checkout fails. Loyalty points is optional — if it times out, proceed without points. I'd use handle() or exceptionally() for graceful degradation: 

CompletableFuture<LoyaltyResult> loyalty =
    CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> loyaltyService.calculate(user), executor)
        .exceptionally(ex -> {
            log.warn("Loyalty service failed, proceeding without points", ex);
            return LoyaltyResult.empty(); // fallback
        });
  1. Circuit breaker — I'd wrap each service call with

Resilience4j circuit breaker. If fraud service is down for 10 consecutive calls, stop calling it for 30 seconds. This prevents cascading failures.

👨‍💻 Ryan: Excellent. What are the differences between CompletableFuture, ExecutorService.submit(), and virtual threads in Java 21?

👨 Khanh:

  • ExecutorService.submit() returns a Future — it's blocking.

You call future.get() and the calling thread blocks. No way to chain operations or compose results without blocking.

  • CompletableFuture is non-blocking and composable. You can

chain .thenApply(), .thenCompose(), .thenCombine(). It's functional-style — great for orchestrating parallel tasks without callback hell.

  • Virtual threads (Java 21) — Project Loom. They're lightweight

threads managed by the JVM, not the OS. You can spawn millions of them. The big shift: you write blocking code that behaves like non-blocking. Instead of CompletableFuture chains: 

try (var scope = new StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure()) {
    var inventory = scope.fork(() -> inventoryService.check(items));
    var price = scope.fork(() -> priceService.validate(items));
    var fraud = scope.fork(() -> fraudService.assess(order));
    scope.join().throwIfFailed();
    // results available: inventory.get(), price.get(), fraud.get()
}

This is cleaner, debuggable (real stack traces!), and scales better than platform threads. If I were starting a new project on Java 21, I'd use virtual threads + structured concurrency instead of CompletableFuture.

👩‍💻 Jun: Quick follow-up — have you ever diagnosed a deadlock in production? How?

👨 Khanh: I haven't had a full deadlock in production, but I've had thread starvation which looks similar — requests hang, no errors in logs.

My diagnostic approach:

  1. Thread dump — jstack <pid> or trigger via actuator

(/actuator/threaddump). Look for threads in BLOCKED state and what lock they're waiting for.

  1. Visual analysis — tools like FastThread.io parse thread

dumps and highlight deadlock cycles.

  1. Prevention — I follow a rule: always acquire locks in

the same order. If service A needs lock X then lock Y, ALL code must do X then Y, never Y then X.

For thread pool starvation specifically, I monitor:

  • Active thread count vs pool size
  • Queue depth of the executor
  • Thread state distribution (how many RUNNABLE vs BLOCKED vs WAITING)

All exposed via Micrometer metrics → Grafana dashboard.

PART B — Database & ORM (10 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: You use JPA/Hibernate, right? Explain the N+1 query problem and how you fix it.

👨 Khanh: The N+1 problem: when you load a parent entity, and each parent has a collection of children, Hibernate does:

  • 1 query to fetch N parents
  • N queries to fetch children (one per parent)

So if you fetch 100 orders with order items, you get 101 queries instead of 1 or 2.

How I detect it: enable Hibernate query logging (spring.jpa.show-sql=true) or use datasource-proxy to count queries per request. In tests I use @DataJpaTest with query counting assertions.

How I fix it — 3 approaches depending on the situation:

  1. JOIN FETCH (eager loading for this specific query):
@Query("SELECT o FROM Order o JOIN FETCH o.items WHERE o.userId = :userId")
List<Order> findOrdersWithItems(@Param("userId") Long userId);

One query, problem solved. But be careful with pagination — Hibernate fetches ALL results into memory when JOIN FETCH is used with pagination.

  1. @EntityGraph — declarative approach:
@EntityGraph(attributePaths = {"items", "items.product"})
List<Order> findByUserId(Long userId);

Cleaner than JPQL, same effect.

  1. @BatchSize on the collection — if JOIN FETCH causes

cartesian products with multiple collections:

@BatchSize(size = 20)
@OneToMany(mappedBy = "order")
private List<OrderItem> items;

Instead of N individual queries, Hibernate batches them: WHERE order_id IN (?, ?, ?, ...) — fewer round trips.

For CloudMart's order history (potentially thousands of orders), I'd use approach 3 with pagination, NOT JOIN FETCH.

👩‍💻 Jun: How do you decide what to index in a database?

👨 Khanh: My approach:

  1. Start with queries, not tables. I look at the actual

queries the application runs (from slow query log or Hibernate's SQL output) and index the WHERE clause columns.

  1. Composite indexes follow query patterns. If I frequently

query WHERE user_id = ? AND status = ? ORDER BY created_at DESC, I create: INDEX (user_id, status, created_at). Column order matters — put equality conditions first, range/sort last.

  1. Check the EXPLAIN plan. Before and after adding an index,

run EXPLAIN ANALYZE to verify it's actually being used. Sometimes the query optimizer ignores your index if the table is small or the selectivity is low.

  1. Don't over-index. Each index slows down INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE

because the index must be maintained. For a write-heavy table like order_events (500K inserts/day), I'd be very selective.

  1. Monitor in production. Track slow queries (> 100ms),

unused indexes (PostgreSQL's pg_stat_user_indexes), and table/index bloat over time.

PART C — System Design: Flash Sale Checkout (20 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: CloudMart runs flash sales — a product goes on sale at exactly 12:00 PM, 500 units available, and 100,000 users are refreshing the page waiting to click "Buy Now" simultaneously.

Design the checkout flow for this flash sale. Focus on:

  • How to avoid overselling (selling >500 units)
  • How to handle 100K concurrent requests
  • How to keep checkout fast (<2 seconds)

👨 Khanh: [Thinks for 90 seconds]

This is a high-concurrency inventory problem. The key challenge is: 100K users clicking at once, but only 500 can succeed. The other 99,500 must get a clean rejection, not errors.

Architecture:

100K Users click "Buy Now"
        │
        ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│   CDN / Edge     │ ← serve static page, absorb read traffic
│   (Cloudflare)   │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │ only POST /checkout reaches backend
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│   Rate Limiter   │ ← 1 attempt per user per 5 seconds
│   (Redis-based)  │    prevents bot spam
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│ Checkout Service  │ ← stateless, 10-20 instances
│ (Spring Boot)     │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │
         ▼ Inventory reservation
┌──────────────────┐
│     Redis         │ ← Single source of truth for remaining stock
│  DECR + Lua       │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │ if reserved successfully
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│   Order Queue     │ ← Kafka: asynchronous order processing
│   (Kafka)         │
└────────┬─────────┘
         │ consumers process payment, create order record
         ▼
┌──────────────────┐
│   PostgreSQL      │ ← Final order record
└──────────────────┘

Key Design: Redis Atomic Reservation

This is the most critical piece. I'd use a Lua script in Redis to make the check-and-decrement atomic:

-- flash_sale_reserve.lua
local stock = redis.call('GET', KEYS[1])
if tonumber(stock) > 0 then
    redis.call('DECR', KEYS[1])
    return 1  -- reserved!
else
    return 0  -- sold out
end

Why Lua in Redis? Because Redis executes Lua scripts atomically — no race condition, no overselling. Even with 100K concurrent requests, each executes sequentially inside Redis. And Redis can handle 100K+ commands/second on a single instance.

Before the sale starts: SET flash:product:123 500 (pre-load stock)

User Flow:

  1. User clicks "Buy Now"
  2. Rate limiter checks: 1 attempt per user per 5s (prevent spam)
  3. Call Redis Lua script: attempt to reserve
  4. If return 0 → show "Sold Out" page immediately (fast rejection)
  5. If return 1 → publish order event to Kafka
  6. Return to user: "Order confirmed! Processing payment..."
  7. Kafka consumer: validate payment, create DB record, send email
  8. If payment fails → INCR flash:product:123 (release stock back)

Why this works:

  • No overselling: Lua script is atomic in Redis
  • Fast rejection: 99,500 users get "Sold Out" in <50ms

(Redis responds in <1ms, no DB query needed)

  • Scalable: checkout service is stateless — add more instances

for more throughput. Redis is the bottleneck but handles 100K/s

  • Decoupled: heavy work (payment, email) is async via Kafka.

Checkout response time stays under 500ms.

Failure handling:

  • Payment fails after reservation: Kafka consumer retries

3 times. If still fails, release stock (INCR) and notify user.

  • Redis goes down: circuit breaker closes checkout. Show

"Please try again later." Better than overselling.

  • Kafka goes down: requests queue in checkout service memory

briefly (with bounded queue), retry sending to Kafka. If Kafka is down for too long, return error to user.

👨‍💻 Ryan: What about users who reserve but never complete payment? That holds stock hostage.

👨 Khanh: Reservation timeout. Each reservation gets a 5-minute TTL. I'd track reservations in Redis: SET reservation:{orderId} 1 EX 300. A background job checks: if reservation exists but no payment completed after 5 minutes, release the stock.

This prevents "ghost reservations" from blocking real buyers.

PART D — Live Coding (20 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: Last section — coding problem.

*"Design and implement an LRU Cache with get(key) and put(key, value). Both must run in O(1) time. When the cache exceeds capacity, evict the least recently used item."*

Think out loud!

👨 Khanh: OK. LRU Cache — classic design problem. Let me think about the data structures needed.

Requirements: O(1) get, O(1) put, O(1) eviction of LRU item.

HashMap alone can't do it — no way to know which item is least recently used.

LinkedList alone gives O(n) lookup.

Solution: HashMap + Doubly Linked List.

  • HashMap: key → Node — O(1) lookup
  • Doubly Linked List: maintains usage order. Most recent at head,

least recent at tail. Doubly-linked because we need O(1) removal from the middle (when we access an existing item).

Operations:

  • get(key): find node in map → move to head (mark as recently used)
  • put(key, value): if exists → update + move to head.

If new → add to head + add to map. If over capacity → remove tail.

  • Dummy head and tail simplify edge cases (no null checks).
class LRUCache {
    // Doubly linked list node
    private static class Node {
        int key, val;
        Node prev, next;
        Node(int k, int v) { key = k; val = v; }
    }

    private final int capacity;
    private final Map<Integer, Node> map;
    private final Node head, tail; // dummy sentinels

    public LRUCache(int capacity) {
        this.capacity = capacity;
        this.map = new HashMap<>();
        // Dummy nodes: head <-> tail
        head = new Node(0, 0);
        tail = new Node(0, 0);
        head.next = tail;
        tail.prev = head;
    }

    public int get(int key) {
        if (!map.containsKey(key)) return -1;
        Node node = map.get(key);
        moveToHead(node); // mark as recently used
        return node.val;
    }

    public void put(int key, int value) {
        if (map.containsKey(key)) {
            // Update existing
            Node node = map.get(key);
            node.val = value;
            moveToHead(node);
        } else {
            // Insert new
            Node node = new Node(key, value);
            map.put(key, node);
            addAfterHead(node);

            if (map.size() > capacity) {
                // Evict LRU (node before tail)
                Node lru = tail.prev;
                removeNode(lru);
                map.remove(lru.key); // need key to remove from map!
            }
        }
    }

    // === Doubly linked list helpers ===

    private void removeNode(Node node) {
        node.prev.next = node.next;
        node.next.prev = node.prev;
    }

    private void addAfterHead(Node node) {
        node.next = head.next;
        node.prev = head;
        head.next.prev = node;
        head.next = node;
    }

    private void moveToHead(Node node) {
        removeNode(node);  // detach from current position
        addAfterHead(node); // re-insert at front
    }
}

Let me trace through:

LRUCache cache = new LRUCache(2);

cache.put(1, 1);  → head ↔ [1:1] ↔ tail
cache.put(2, 2);  → head ↔ [2:2] ↔ [1:1] ↔ tail

cache.get(1);     → returns 1, move [1:1] to head
                   → head ↔ [1:1] ↔ [2:2] ↔ tail

cache.put(3, 3);  → over capacity! evict tail.prev = [2:2]
                   → head ↔ [3:3] ↔ [1:1] ↔ tail

cache.get(2);     → returns -1 (evicted)
cache.get(1);     → returns 1 ✓
cache.get(3);     → returns 3 ✓

Edge cases:

  • capacity = 1: every put evicts the previous
  • put same key twice: update value, don't increase size
  • get non-existent key: return -1

Why I stored key in the Node: when evicting from the tail, I need the key to also remove it from the HashMap. Without the key in the node, I'd have to scan the map — breaking O(1).

👨‍💻 Ryan: Clean implementation. The dummy sentinel trick and the key-in-node insight show experience.

One follow-up — in production, would you use this or something else?

👨 Khanh: In production I'd use Caffeine (Java's best caching library) or Guava Cache — both are thread-safe, support TTL-based expiry, size-based eviction, and async loading. Caffeine uses a W-TinyLFU eviction policy which actually outperforms pure LRU for most workloads — it considers both frequency and recency.

For distributed caching, I'd use Redis with a TTL-based eviction policy (maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru) rather than implementing LRU myself.

But understanding how LRU works internally helps me debug cache behavior and choose the right eviction policy for each use case.

👨‍💻 Ryan: Outstanding session, Khanh. Really strong across all areas. Any questions for us?

👨 Khanh: Yes! How do you handle database migrations at CloudMart? With 500K orders/day, a bad migration could be catastrophic. Do you use Flyway/Liquibase? Blue-green deployments?

👨‍💻 Ryan: Great question. We use Flyway for schema migrations, and we follow the "expand-contract" pattern: first add new columns as nullable, deploy the code that writes to both old and new, then backfill, then drop the old column. Never a breaking schema change in one deployment.

👨 Khanh: That's very disciplined. Last one — does CloudMart use feature flags? And if so, how granular — per-user, per-region?

👩‍💻 Jun: Yes! LaunchDarkly. We can target by user, region, percentage rollout, even by device type. Every new feature goes through a flag.

👨 Khanh: Perfect — I've been wanting to work with feature flags in practice. Thanks for the great conversation!

📊 FULL SCORECARD — Interview #3

Round Category Score
HR "3 words" self-description 9/10
Weakness answer 10/10
Salary negotiation 9/10
Work-life balance 9/10
Behavioral Own mistake → production impact 10/10
Cross-team dependency 10/10
Estimation (BNPL) 10/10
Engineering culture values 9/10
Technical CompletableFuture + Virtual Threads 10/10
Deadlock diagnosis 9/10
N+1 problem + 3 fixes 10/10
Indexing strategy 9/10
