Behavioral Conflict, Mentor Incident, Learning Mistake, Estimate Say No, Ambiguity Deadline push-back, Legacy
Java JVM, volatile Spring lifecycle CompletableFuture GC, Streams Design patterns, SOLID
Spring @Transactional Beans — Security/JWT Auto-config, @Cacheable
Design URL Shortener GPS Tracking Flash Sale Notifications Hotel Booking
Coding Top K Frequent Group Anagrams LRU Cache Merge Intervals Coin Change (DP)

🎭 Full Mock Interview #6 — Java Mid/Senior Engineer

Company: HealthSync (HealthTech SaaS, Series B, 120 engineers)

Context: HealthSync builds a patient appointment & health records platform used by 800+ clinics. They process 1M+ appointments/month. Strict HIPAA-style compliance. Need senior Java engineers for the Platform API team.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (Video Call, 20 min)

👩 Mai (HR Manager): Hi Khanh! I'm Mai. Thanks for applying — we got a lot of interest for this role so I want to make our time count.

👩 Mai: I've read your resume. On paper you're qualified. But so are 50 other candidates. What makes you genuinely unique compared to another Java engineer with 4-5 years of experience?

👨 Khanh: Most Java engineers at my level can write clean code and build APIs. That's table stakes. What makes me different is the combination of three things you rarely find together:

One — I've operated in compliance-heavy environments. Healthcare was my first job. I understand that audit trails, data encryption, and access control aren't optional add-ons — they're core architecture decisions. For HealthSync, that means I won't need training on why compliance matters. I already think that way.

Two — I fix systems, not just symptoms. When our middleware had intermittent 401 errors that everyone accepted as "normal," I volunteered to investigate. Root cause: a race condition in token caching. I didn't just patch it — I added startup validation rules that have since caught two similar bugs from other team members before they hit production.

Three — I make the team better, not just myself. I introduced CI/CD where there was none (cut deployment time from 20 min to 3). I write operational runbooks so on-call engineers don't need to call me at 2 AM. I document architecture decisions. A lot of engineers optimize for personal output — I optimize for team velocity.

**[📝 Structured: three distinct points, each with evidence. Point three especially resonates with small teams like HealthSync's.]**

👩 Mai: You come from outsourcing. Some hiring managers worry that outsourcing engineers are used to being told what to build rather than deciding what to build. How do you respond to that?

👨 Khanh: That's a fair concern, and honestly, it's partially true — outsourcing does have more prescriptive work. But it depends on the engineer.

In my case, even within outsourcing I've pushed beyond "just execute":

  • When requirements for multi-tenancy came as a one-sentence ask,

I wrote a 12-question RFC that shaped the entire architecture

  • When we had no CI/CD, nobody asked me to build one — I did it

on my own initiative

  • When I disagreed with a code structure decision, I didn't just

complain — I documented a target architecture and incrementally migrated the codebase over 6 months

So yes, outsourcing is where I've been. But product-company thinking is how I operate. I'm looking for HealthSync specifically because I want to own a product, not just deliver someone else's spec.

👩 Mai: We're a hybrid team — 3 days office, 2 days remote. How do you work best, and how do you stay productive when remote?

👨 Khanh: I'm very productive remote because I'm disciplined about structure. My approach:

Morning block (8-12): Deep work — coding, design docs, complex problem-solving. Slack notifications off. This is when I produce my best work.

Afternoon (1-5): Meetings, code reviews, pair programming, responding to messages. Social and collaborative work.

I over-communicate on remote days — I post daily standups in Slack even if we don't have a formal standup, and I'm responsive on Slack within 15 minutes during work hours.

For the 3 office days, I prioritize in-person collaboration: whiteboard sessions, 1-on-1s with teammates, and informal knowledge sharing that's hard to do over Zoom.

Hybrid is actually my ideal setup — deep focus at home, human connection in the office.

👩 Mai: Last question — any concerns or hesitations about this role that I can address right now?

👨 Khanh: One question that would help me: how mature is the engineering culture? Specifically — do engineers have input into product decisions, or is it purely top-down from product management?

I ask because I'm most motivated when I understand the why behind what I'm building. If I know that this API endpoint reduces appointment no-shows by 20%, I'll design it differently than if I'm just implementing a spec.

👩 Mai: Great question. Our squads are cross-functional — engineer, PM, designer together. Engineers are expected to push back on requirements and propose alternatives. You'd have a lot of input.

👨 Khanh: That's exactly what I'm looking for. Thank you Mai!

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL (Video Call, 40 min)

👨‍💼 Phong (EM, Platform API): Hey Khanh! I'm Phong. Let's talk about how you work and lead.

👨‍💼 Phong: Tell me about a time you **led a project or initiative without having formal authority**. You weren't the tech lead or manager, but you drove something forward anyway.

👨 Khanh: The CI/CD initiative I mentioned is the best example.

[Situation] Deployments were manual: build JAR locally, SSH to server, stop service, upload, restart. 20 minutes, error-prone, and stressful. It required a senior person every time. Nobody was assigned to fix this.

[Why I had no authority] I was a mid-level engineer at the time, 6 months into the project. The tech lead was aware of the problem but had higher priorities. No budget was allocated.

[How I led without authority]

Step 1 — I built credibility first. I didn't walk in on day one saying "everything is broken." I spent my first 5 months delivering features well, earning trust. By the time I proposed CI/CD, the team knew I was reliable.

Step 2 — I showed, didn't tell. Instead of writing a proposal and asking for permission, I spent a weekend building a proof of concept with GitLab CI + Docker. On Monday, I showed the team: "Here's the same deployment, but it takes 3 minutes and requires one click." Seeing a working demo is 10x more convincing than a slide deck.

Step 3 — I made adoption easy. I didn't ask the team to learn Docker and CI pipelines. I wrote a README, created templates, and offered to pair with anyone who had questions. I removed friction instead of creating it.

Step 4 — I let others take credit. When the tech lead presented our improved deployment process to management, I didn't insist on being mentioned. The win for the team was enough. And frankly, the tech lead became my biggest advocate afterward.

[Result] Deployment went from 2x/month to 2-3x/week. Team velocity increased measurably. And 6 months later, I was promoted to senior — partly because of this initiative.

👨‍💼 Phong: "Show don't tell" and "make adoption easy" — those are leadership principles, not just engineering tactics. Really strong.

👨‍💼 Phong: Tell me about a time you had **multiple urgent tasks competing for your attention** at the same time. How did you decide what to do first?

👨 Khanh: This happened during a particularly hectic week about 4 months ago.

[Situation] On Tuesday morning, three things landed simultaneously:

  1. A production alert: our token refresh was failing for 10% of users
  2. A feature deadline: the client demo was Thursday (2 days away),

and my feature was 80% done

  1. A code review blocker: a teammate's PR needed my review to unblock

their sprint work

All three were "urgent." I couldn't do all three at once.

[How I prioritized]

I used a simple framework: Impact × Reversibility.

Task Impact if delayed Reversible? Priority
Production alert Users affected NOW No — every minute costs trust 🔴 P1
Code review Blocks teammate for hours Yes — can review by end of day 🟡 P2
Feature (demo Thurs) Client demo at risk Partially — can demo 80% version 🟠 P3

Actions:

  • First 2 hours: Fixed the production alert. Root cause: a config

change from yesterday's deployment caused a mismatch. Rolled back the config, verified fix, wrote a 2-line incident summary in Slack.

  • Next 30 minutes: Did the code review. I focused on correctness

and blockers only — didn't nitpick style. Left a comment: "Approved with minor suggestions. Don't block on those." This unblocked my teammate's day.

  • Rest of Tuesday + Wednesday: Completed the remaining 20% of

my feature. Deployed Wednesday evening. Demo on Thursday went well.

[Key lesson] The mistake most engineers make is either: (a) trying to do everything in parallel (nothing gets done well), or (b) freezing because they can't decide (nothing gets done at all).

Having a simple prioritization framework — even one you create in 30 seconds — cuts through the paralysis.

👨‍💼 Phong: Last one. Have you ever had to give **tough feedback to a peer** — not a junior, but someone at your level or above? How did you handle it?

👨 Khanh: Yes — and it was uncomfortable.

[Situation] A fellow senior engineer on my team — let's call him Vinh — consistently wrote PRs with no test coverage. His code worked, but there were no unit tests, no integration tests. When bugs appeared later, they were always in his modules.

[Why it was hard] Vinh was senior, had been on the team longer than me, and was technically skilled. I didn't want to come across as arrogant or accusatory. But the lack of tests was causing real problems: other team members were afraid to refactor his code because there was no safety net.

[How I handled it]

I chose the right setting. Not in a PR comment (public shaming), not in a team meeting (embarrassing). I asked for a 1-on-1 coffee chat.

I started with genuine respect. "Vinh, I really admire how fast you ship features. You're one of the most productive engineers on the team." This wasn't manipulation — it was true.

I used "I" language, not "you" language.

❌ "You never write tests and it causes bugs." ✅ "I've noticed that when I need to modify code in the payment module, I'm hesitant because there aren't tests to catch if I break something. I end up spending extra time manually verifying. Could we add some tests for the critical paths?"

I offered to help, not just criticize. "I could pair with you on the first test file — we can set up the test structure together, and after that it'll be quick to add tests for new features."

[Result] Vinh was initially defensive — "my code doesn't need tests, it works." But after I showed him a specific bug that would have been caught by a simple test, he came around. We pair-programmed the first test file. Within 2 months, he was writing tests consistently — not perfect coverage, but the critical paths were covered. Bug rate in his modules dropped noticeably.

👨‍💼 Phong: That's exactly how senior engineers should handle it. Private, respectful, "I" language, offer help. Well done.

🎭 Full Mock Interview #6 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: HealthSync | Role: Senior Java Engineer, Platform API

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL (Video Call, 65 min)

👨‍💻 Bao (Staff Engineer): Hey Khanh! Three parts today:

  1. Java internals: equals/hashCode, HashMap, immutability (15 min)
  2. Spring testing & ops (10 min)
  3. System design: API rate limiter (20 min)
  4. Live coding (20 min)

PART A — Java Internals (15 min)

👨‍💻 Bao: Explain the equals/hashCode contract in Java. Why must you override both? What breaks if you don't?

👨 Khanh: The contract has three rules:

  1. If a.equals(b) is true → a.hashCode() must equal b.hashCode()
  2. If a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(), equals() may or may not be true

(hash collisions are allowed)

  1. equals() must be consistent, reflexive, symmetric, and transitive

What breaks if you override equals() but NOT hashCode(): 

class Patient {
    String id;

    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object o) {
        return this.id.equals(((Patient) o).id); // compares by id
    }
    // hashCode NOT overridden — uses Object.hashCode() (memory address)
}

Patient p1 = new Patient("123");
Patient p2 = new Patient("123");

p1.equals(p2); // true ✅

Set<Patient> set = new HashSet<>();
set.add(p1);
set.contains(p2); // FALSE ❌ — BROKEN!

Why? HashSet.contains() first checks hashCode() to find the bucket. p1 and p2 have different hash codes (different memory addresses), so p2 lands in a different bucket. equals() is never even called.

The fix:

@Override
public int hashCode() {
    return Objects.hash(id); // same id → same hash
}

My rule: If you override equals, always override hashCode. In modern Java, I use record for data classes — it auto-generates both correctly:

record Patient(String id, String name) {}
// equals, hashCode, toString — all generated from fields

👨‍💻 Bao: Good. Now explain how HashMap works internally. What happens when two keys have the same hashCode?

👨 Khanh: HashMap internals:

Structure: An array of buckets. Default initial capacity = 16.

put(key, value) flow:

  1. Compute hash = key.hashCode() then spread it: hash ^ (hash >>> 16)

— this reduces collisions by mixing high and low bits.

  1. Find bucket index: index = hash & (capacity - 1) — bitwise AND

instead of modulo, works because capacity is a power of 2.

  1. If bucket is empty → create new Node, store there.
  2. If bucket has entries → collision handling:

Collision resolution (Java 8+):

  • Each bucket starts as a linked list of nodes.
  • If two keys have the same bucket index, the new entry is added

to the linked list. On get(), it walks the list comparing hashCode() first, then equals() to find the right key.

  • Treeification: when a single bucket's list exceeds 8 nodes,

it converts to a Red-Black Tree — improving worst-case from O(n) to O(log n). When it shrinks below 6, it converts back.

Load factor and rehashing:

  • Default load factor = 0.75
  • When size > capacity × 0.75, HashMap rehashes: creates a

new array of 2× capacity, re-distributes all entries.

  • This is O(n) but amortized across many puts.

Why capacity is always a power of 2: So that hash & (capacity - 1) works as a fast modulo. If capacity = 16 (10000), then capacity - 1 = 15 (01111), and hash & 01111 extracts the last 4 bits — uniform distribution.

Thread safety: HashMap is NOT thread-safe. Two threads doing concurrent put() can cause infinite loops during rehashing (in Java 7, less likely in Java 8 but still unsafe). Use ConcurrentHashMap for multithreaded access.

👨‍💻 Bao: What's immutability in Java and why does it matter for concurrent applications?

👨 Khanh: An immutable object's state can't change after creation. All fields are final, no setters, defensive copies of mutable fields.

// Immutable class — traditional way
public final class Money {
    private final BigDecimal amount;
    private final String currency;

    public Money(BigDecimal amount, String currency) {
        this.amount = amount;
        this.currency = currency;
    }
    // getters only, no setters
    public BigDecimal amount() { return amount; }
    public String currency() { return currency; }
}

// Java 16+ — use record (immutable by design)
public record Money(BigDecimal amount, String currency) {}

Why it matters for concurrency:

  • Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe — no synchronization

needed because state never changes. Multiple threads can read simultaneously without locks.

  • No defensive copying needed when sharing between threads.
  • Great for cache keys — their hashCode never changes.
  • String in Java is immutable — that's why it's safe to use as

HashMap key and in the String pool.

When NOT to use: when you need to update frequently. Creating a new object on every change has GC overhead. For hot paths: use mutable objects with proper synchronization.

Java Records (16+): record gives you immutability, equals, hashCode, toString, and a compact constructor — all generated. It's my default for DTOs, events, and value objects now.

PART B — Spring Testing & Operations (10 min)

👨‍💻 Bao: How do you test a Spring Boot REST API? Walk me through your testing strategy with specific tools.

👨 Khanh: I test in layers:

Layer 1 — Unit tests (service layer):

@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)
class AppointmentServiceTest {
    @Mock AppointmentRepository repo;
    @Mock NotificationService notifier;
    @InjectMocks AppointmentService service;

    @Test
    void shouldCreateAppointment() {
        // Given
        var request = new CreateAppointmentRequest("patient-1", LocalDateTime.now());
        when(repo.save(any())).thenReturn(new Appointment("apt-1", ...));

        // When
        var result = service.create(request);

        // Then
        assertThat(result.id()).isEqualTo("apt-1");
        verify(notifier).sendConfirmation("patient-1");
    }
}

Fast, no Spring context. Tests business logic in isolation.

Layer 2 — Controller tests (MockMvc):

@WebMvcTest(AppointmentController.class)
class AppointmentControllerTest {
    @Autowired MockMvc mockMvc;
    @MockBean AppointmentService service;

    @Test
    void shouldReturn201OnCreate() throws Exception {
        when(service.create(any())).thenReturn(new AppointmentDto("apt-1"));

        mockMvc.perform(post("/api/appointments")
                .contentType(APPLICATION_JSON)
                .content("""
                    {"patientId": "p1", "dateTime": "2026-01-15T10:00:00"}
                    """))
            .andExpect(status().isCreated())
            .andExpect(jsonPath("$.id").value("apt-1"));
    }
}

Tests HTTP layer: routing, serialization, validation, status codes. Loads only the web slice, not full context. Fast.

Layer 3 — Integration tests (Testcontainers):

@SpringBootTest
@Testcontainers
class AppointmentIntegrationTest {
    @Container
    static PostgreSQLContainer<?> pg =
        new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:15");

    @DynamicPropertySource
    static void props(DynamicPropertyRegistry r) {
        r.add("spring.datasource.url", pg::getJdbcUrl);
        r.add("spring.datasource.username", pg::getUsername);
        r.add("spring.datasource.password", pg::getPassword);
    }

    @Autowired AppointmentRepository repo;

    @Test
    void shouldPersistAndRetrieve() {
        // Tests against REAL PostgreSQL in Docker
        repo.save(new Appointment(...));
        var found = repo.findByPatientId("p1");
        assertThat(found).isNotEmpty();
    }
}

Tests real database queries, Hibernate behavior, constraints. Slower but catches real issues that mocks hide.

Layer 4 — Contract tests: If other services depend on my API, I use Spring Cloud Contract to generate stubs. If I change my response format, their build breaks.

👨‍💻 Bao: Quick ones — what's @Async and what's a gotcha? And how do you monitor a Spring Boot app in production?

👨 Khanh: @Async makes a method run in a separate thread:

@Async
public void sendNotification(String patientId) {
    // runs in a separate thread, doesn't block caller
    emailService.send(patientId, ...);
}

Gotcha 1: Must enable with @EnableAsync on a config class. Without it, @Async is silently ignored — method runs synchronously.

Gotcha 2: Self-invocation — same as @Transactional and @Cacheable. If the same class calls its own @Async method, the proxy is bypassed. Must call from another bean.

Gotcha 3: Default uses SimpleAsyncTaskExecutor which creates a new thread per call — no pooling! Always configure a custom ThreadPoolTaskExecutor with bounded pool size.

Gotcha 4: Exceptions are swallowed silently if return type is void. Use AsyncUncaughtExceptionHandler or return CompletableFuture<T>.

Production monitoring — Spring Boot Actuator:

  • /actuator/health — liveness/readiness probes for Kubernetes
  • /actuator/metrics — JVM metrics (heap, GC, threads) via Micrometer
  • /actuator/prometheus — export metrics to Prometheus → Grafana
  • /actuator/info — build info, git commit hash
  • /actuator/threaddump — debug thread issues without SSH

I wire Actuator → Micrometer → Prometheus → Grafana for dashboards, with PagerDuty alerts on key thresholds (error rate > 1%, p99 latency > 2s, heap > 85%).

PART C — System Design: API Rate Limiter (20 min)

👨‍💻 Bao: Design an API rate limiter for HealthSync. We need to protect our APIs from abuse — both external partners and internal services. Requirements:

  • Configurable rules per client (e.g., "Partner A: 100 req/min")
  • Must work across multiple API server instances
  • Should return 429 Too Many Requests when exceeded
  • Low latency — rate check must add <5ms to each request

👨 Khanh: [Thinks for 60 seconds]

Core Algorithm: Sliding Window Counter

I'd use the sliding window counter approach — it's a practical middle ground between fixed window (inaccurate) and sliding log (memory-heavy).

How it works: Divide time into windows (e.g., 1-minute windows). Track counts for the current AND previous window. Estimate the current rate by weighted interpolation:

rate = prevWindowCount × overlapPercentage + currWindowCount

Example: limit = 100/min, we're 30 seconds into current minute
  Previous minute: 80 requests
  Current minute so far: 40 requests
  Estimated rate = 80 × 0.5 + 40 = 80 → under limit ✅

More accurate than fixed window, simpler than sliding log.

Architecture:

Client Request
      │
      ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│   API Gateway        │  (Spring Boot / Nginx)
│   ┌──────────────┐   │
│   │ Rate Limit   │   │  ← Filter/Interceptor
│   │ Filter       │   │
│   └──────┬───────┘   │
│          │            │
└──────────┼────────────┘
           │
           ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│       Redis          │  ← Distributed counter
│  (shared across all  │
│   API instances)     │
└─────────────────────┘

Redis Implementation (Lua script for atomicity):

-- rate_limit.lua
-- KEYS[1] = "ratelimit:{clientId}:{currentWindow}"
-- KEYS[2] = "ratelimit:{clientId}:{prevWindow}"
-- ARGV[1] = limit (e.g., 100)
-- ARGV[2] = window size in seconds (e.g., 60)
-- ARGV[3] = elapsed ratio in current window (e.g., 0.5)

local prev = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[2]) or "0")
local curr = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]) or "0")
local elapsed = tonumber(ARGV[3])

local rate = prev * (1 - elapsed) + curr
if rate >= tonumber(ARGV[1]) then
    return 0  -- REJECTED
end

redis.call('INCR', KEYS[1])
redis.call('EXPIRE', KEYS[1], tonumber(ARGV[2]) * 2)
return 1  -- ALLOWED

Spring Filter implementation:

@Component
public class RateLimitFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter {
    private final RedisTemplate<String, String> redis;
    private final RateLimitConfig config;

    @Override
    protected void doFilterInternal(HttpServletRequest request,
            HttpServletResponse response, FilterChain chain)
            throws ServletException, IOException {

        String clientId = extractClientId(request); // from API key or JWT
        RateRule rule = config.getRuleFor(clientId); // 100/min, etc.

        boolean allowed = checkRateLimit(clientId, rule);
        if (!allowed) {
            response.setStatus(429);
            response.setHeader("Retry-After", "60");
            response.getWriter().write(
                "{\"error\": \"Rate limit exceeded\"}");
            return;
        }

        // Add rate limit headers (GitHub-style)
        response.setHeader("X-RateLimit-Limit",
            String.valueOf(rule.limit()));
        response.setHeader("X-RateLimit-Remaining",
            String.valueOf(rule.limit() - getCurrentCount(clientId)));

        chain.doFilter(request, response);
    }
}

Configuration per client:

rate-limits:
  rules:
    partner-a:
      requests-per-minute: 100
      burst: 20            # allow 20 extra in short burst
    partner-b:
      requests-per-minute: 500
    internal-services:
      requests-per-minute: 10000  # higher limit for internal
    default:
      requests-per-minute: 60

Stored in a config file or database. Can be updated without restart using Spring Cloud Config or a DB-backed config with polling.

Why Redis?

  • Shared across all API instances (distributed state)
  • Lua scripts are atomic (no race conditions)
  • Sub-millisecond response time (meets <5ms requirement)
  • Built-in EXPIRE handles cleanup automatically

Edge cases:

  • Redis down: Fail-open (allow requests) or fail-closed (reject).

For health APIs: fail-open with alerting. Denying a doctor access to patient data because of a rate limiter issue is worse than allowing a few extra requests.

  • Clock skew: use Redis server time, not client time.
  • Burst handling: allow short bursts with Token Bucket variant —

tokens refill at steady rate, but can accumulate up to burst limit.

PART D — Live Coding (20 min)

👨‍💻 Bao: Coding problem:

*"There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to numCourses-1. You're given prerequisites: [a, b] means you must take b before a. Return true if you can finish all courses (no cycles)."* 

Input: numCourses = 4, prerequisites = [[1,0],[2,0],[3,1],[3,2]]
Output: true  (order: 0 → 1 → 2 → 3)

👨 Khanh: This is a cycle detection problem in a directed graph. If there's a cycle, it's impossible to finish (circular dependency).

Two approaches:

  1. DFS with 3-state coloring — my preference
  2. BFS topological sort (Kahn's algorithm)

I'll use DFS with 3 states:

  • 0 = unvisited
  • 1 = visiting (currently in DFS stack — if we hit this, CYCLE!)
  • 2 = done (fully explored, no cycle through this node)
public boolean canFinish(int numCourses, int[][] prerequisites) {
    // Build adjacency list
    List<List<Integer>> graph = new ArrayList<>();
    for (int i = 0; i < numCourses; i++) graph.add(new ArrayList<>());
    for (int[] pre : prerequisites) {
        graph.get(pre[1]).add(pre[0]); // b → a (must take b before a)
    }

    // 0=unvisited, 1=visiting, 2=done
    int[] state = new int[numCourses];

    for (int i = 0; i < numCourses; i++) {
        if (state[i] == 0) { // unvisited
            if (hasCycle(graph, state, i)) return false;
        }
    }
    return true; // no cycles found
}

private boolean hasCycle(List<List<Integer>> graph, int[] state, int node) {
    state[node] = 1; // mark as visiting

    for (int neighbor : graph.get(node)) {
        if (state[neighbor] == 1) return true;  // CYCLE! back edge
        if (state[neighbor] == 0) {              // unvisited
            if (hasCycle(graph, state, neighbor)) return true;
        }
        // state[neighbor] == 2 → already done, skip
    }

    state[node] = 2; // fully explored, no cycle
    return false;
}

Trace: numCourses=4, prereqs=[[1,0],[2,0],[3,1],[3,2]]

Graph: 0→[1,2], 1→[3], 2→[3], 3→[]

DFS from 0:
  state[0]=1, visit neighbor 1
    state[1]=1, visit neighbor 3
      state[3]=1, no neighbors → state[3]=2 ✓
    state[1]=2 ✓
  visit neighbor 2
    state[2]=1, visit neighbor 3 → state[3]=2 (done, skip)
    state[2]=2 ✓
  state[0]=2 ✓

All nodes visited, no cycle → return true ✓

Cycle example: [[0,1],[1,0]]

Graph: 1→[0], 0→[1]
DFS from 0: state[0]=1 → visit 1 → state[1]=1 → visit 0 →
  state[0]==1 → CYCLE! return false ✓

Complexity: Time: O(V + E), Space: O(V + E)

Edge cases:

  • No prerequisites → always true
  • Single course → true
  • Self-loop [0,0] → cycle → false
  • Disconnected graph → DFS from each unvisited node

👨‍💻 Bao: Perfect. If the question asked to return the order (Course Schedule II), how would you modify this?

👨 Khanh: Two changes:

  1. Add a List<Integer> order to collect results
  2. After state[node] = 2 (DFS complete), add node to the list
  3. Reverse the list at the end (DFS postorder gives reverse

topological order)

Or use Kahn's algorithm (BFS) — which naturally gives topological order:

  • Compute in-degree for each node
  • Queue all nodes with in-degree 0
  • Process queue: remove node, add to result, decrement

neighbors' in-degree. If neighbor reaches 0, add to queue.

  • If result size < numCourses → cycle exists.

👨‍💻 Bao: Excellent session. Any questions?

👨 Khanh: How does HealthSync handle data privacy technically? Do you encrypt at rest, and do you use row-level security or application-level filtering for multi-tenant clinic data?

👨‍💻 Bao: Both. AES-256 at rest, TLS in transit. Application-level tenant filtering via a @TenantContext annotation that auto-adds WHERE clinic_id = ? to all queries. Very transparent to developers.

👨 Khanh: That's elegant. Thanks Bao!

📊 FULL SCORECARD — Interview #6

Round Category Score
HR What makes you unique 10/10
Outsourcing → product transition 10/10
Remote work style 9/10
Behavioral Leading without authority 10/10
Multiple priorities 10/10
Giving tough feedback to peer 10/10
Technical equals/hashCode contract 10/10
HashMap internals 10/10
Immutability + Records 9/10
Spring testing (MockMvc, Testcontainers) 10/10
@Async gotchas + Actuator 10/10
API Rate Limiter design 10/10
Course Schedule (graph cycle) 10/10

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete 6-Interview Master Map

#1 #2 #3 #4 #5 #6
Company FinCore LogiTech CloudMart VietBank TravelNow HealthSync
Domain Fintech Logistics E-Commerce Banking Travel HealthTech
HR Salary Career why Weakness Proud project Why hire, Failure Unique, Outsource→Product
Behavioral Conflict, Mentor Incident, Learning Mistake, Estimate Say No, Ambiguity Deadline, Legacy Lead w/o authority, Priority, Feedback
Java JVM, volatile Spring lifecycle CompletableFuture GC, Streams Design patterns equals/hashCode, HashMap
Spring @Transactional Beans — Security/JWT Auto-config, Cache Testing, @Async, Actuator
Design URL Shortener GPS Tracking Flash Sale Notifications Hotel Booking API Rate Limiter
Coding Top K Frequent Group Anagrams LRU Cache Merge Intervals Coin Change Course Schedule

🎭 Full Mock Interview #7 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: GrabTech (Super-App, Singapore HQ, 4000+ engineers)

Context: GrabTech is a super-app operating in 8 SEA countries — ride-hailing, food delivery, payments, and financial services. 500M+ transactions/month. The Payments team processes $15B+ GMV/year. They're hiring Senior Java Engineers for the Payments Platform team based in Ho Chi Minh City, reporting to Singapore. Why this company matters for Khanh: Matches your AWS/cloud-native experience, Sony-scale distributed systems, and Axon Active banking background. This interview is harder — FAANG-adjacent with strong bar.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREENING (Video Call, 30 min)

Setting: 10:00 AM, Wednesday. Google Meet. Talent Acquisition Partner.

👩 Sarah (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! I'm Sarah from GrabTech's talent team in Singapore. Thanks for taking the time — I've reviewed your profile and I'm excited to chat. How are you today?

👨 Khanh: Hi Sarah! I'm doing well, thanks for reaching out. I've been following Grab's engineering blog — the recent post about migrating to event-driven payments architecture was really impressive.

👩 Sarah: Oh nice, you read that! That's actually the team you'd be joining. So let me give you some context first.

The Payments Platform team owns the core payment processing pipeline — every time someone pays for a Grab ride, orders food, or sends money through GrabPay, it flows through our systems. We process about 20,000 transactions per second at peak, and reliability is non-negotiable.

The team is 12 engineers — mix of senior and staff — split between Singapore and Ho Chi Minh City. You'd be in the HCMC pod, working on the same codebase and on-call rotation as the Singapore team.

Does that sound interesting?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. Processing payments at that scale with zero tolerance for errors — that's a fascinating engineering challenge. My background in banking at Axon Active and high-availability systems at Sony Global gives me relevant experience, but Grab's scale is a significant step up, which is exactly what I'm looking for.

👩 Sarah: Great. Walk me through your career — I've read your resume but I want to hear it in your words.

👨 Khanh: Sure. I'll keep it focused on what's most relevant.

I started at FPT Software in a training program — that's where I built my Java and cloud foundations. Then I moved to TMA Solutions, where I worked on a healthcare system for Humana. That's where I learned to build Spring Boot microservices properly — clean architecture, CI/CD with GitHub Actions and Jenkins, and Kubernetes deployments across multiple environments. Healthcare taught me that reliability and security aren't optional.

Next, I joined Axon Active to work on Valiant — a banking platform. This was my first major architecture challenge: helping migrate from a monolithic system to microservices using Quarkus and Docker. I learned domain-driven design, financial data modeling, and how to decompose a system without breaking it. We also added Elasticsearch for search capabilities.

Now I'm back at FPT Software as a Senior Engineer on the Sony Global project. This is the highest-scale system I've worked on — it serves global users with strict SLA requirements. I design cloud-native microservices on AWS, and I own system reliability — monitoring, incident response, root cause analysis, and production troubleshooting.

The progression has been deliberate: healthcare gave me discipline, banking gave me domain complexity, and Sony gave me scale. I feel ready for Grab-level scale and impact.

👩 Sarah: That's a very clear narrative. I can see the thread. Let me ask the harder question — why leave Sony/FPT now?

👨 Khanh: A few honest reasons. First, I want to work on a product I can see the end-user impact of. At FPT, I'm building for Sony's internal systems — great engineering, but I'm far from the end user. At Grab, when I improve the payment pipeline, millions of people in Vietnam feel the difference. That motivates me.

Second, I want to work with a world-class engineering team that operates at internet scale. FPT has strong engineers, but the problems are bounded by the client's requirements. At Grab, the engineering culture drives innovation internally.

Third, career growth. I want to progress toward a Staff Engineer role, and Grab's engineering ladder is well-defined and respected in the industry.

👩 Sarah: Fair points. Let me ask about your certifications — you have AWS Solutions Architect Professional and GCP Professional Cloud Architect. That's unusual. Why both?

👨 Khanh: AWS SA Pro was natural — my Sony project is entirely on AWS, and I wanted to formalize my knowledge. The certification process forced me to understand services I hadn't used in production, like AWS Organizations, Control Tower, and advanced networking.

GCP PCA was strategic. I believe a senior engineer shouldn't be locked into one cloud vendor. Understanding both AWS and GCP gives me perspective — I can evaluate trade-offs objectively. For example, GCP's BigQuery is genuinely superior to AWS Athena for ad-hoc analytics, while AWS has better enterprise integration. Knowing both makes me more valuable in architecture discussions.

👩 Sarah: That's a mature answer. OK, compensation — what range are you targeting?

👨 Khanh: For a senior role at a company of Grab's caliber in HCMC, based on my research and experience level, I'm looking at the market range for senior engineers at SEA tech companies. But honestly, the opportunity to work on payments at this scale matters more to me than optimizing the offer by a few percent. I'd love to hear what the band looks like for this role.

👩 Sarah: We're competitive with the market, and we have equity as part of the package. I think we can make it work. Let me tell you about the process:

  • Round 2: Behavioral with the Engineering Manager (45 min)
  • Round 3: Technical — system design + coding (90 min)
  • Round 4: Bar raiser — cross-team evaluation (45 min)

It's rigorous, but we can schedule everything within 2 weeks.

👨 Khanh: That works perfectly. I'm ready to go through the full process. Two questions for you —

First, what's the on-call situation like? I've done on-call at Sony, so I'm comfortable with it, but I'd like to understand the rotation and support structure.

👩 Sarah: Great question. It's a weekly rotation shared between Singapore and HCMC — so you'd be on-call roughly every 6 weeks. We have comprehensive runbooks, and there's always a secondary on-call. Most pages are auto-resolved by our self-healing systems now.

👨 Khanh: That sounds reasonable. Second question — what makes someone succeed vs struggle on this team?

👩 Sarah: Hmm. The ones who succeed are proactive communicators. We're a distributed team across timezones, so if you wait for someone to ask you for updates, things fall through the cracks. The engineers who thrive write clear design docs, over-communicate in Slack, and raise concerns early — not after the sprint is over.

👨 Khanh: That resonates — I've learned that lesson through distributed collaboration with Sony's teams. Thanks Sarah!

👩 Sarah: Thanks Khanh! Behavioral round will be scheduled for next Monday. Good luck!

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL INTERVIEW (Video Call, 45 min)

Setting: Monday, 2 PM. Zoom. Engineering Manager + Tech Lead.

👨‍💼 Wei (EM, Singapore): Hey Khanh, I'm Wei, EM for Payments Platform. And this is Minh — tech lead on the HCMC side.

👩‍💻 Minh (TL, HCMC): Hey!

👨 Khanh: Great to meet you both!

👨‍💼 Wei: So this round is behavioral — we want to understand how you work, especially under pressure. Payments is high-stakes. When things go wrong, real money is involved. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Ready.

👨‍💼 Wei: First. Tell me about the **most complex distributed system problem** you've debugged in production. Not a simple bug — I want something where the root cause wasn't obvious.

👨 Khanh: [Thinks for 5 seconds]

This happened at Sony about 6 months ago.

[Situation] We have a microservice that processes user session tokens. On a Tuesday afternoon, we noticed intermittent 500 errors — not consistent, not tied to load. About 3-5% of requests were failing randomly. The tricky part: the errors weren't in our service logs. Our service returned 200, but the downstream client reported 500s.

[Task] I was the senior on-call, and this was P2 escalating toward P1 because the error rate was slowly increasing.

[Action] First, I checked the obvious things: our service metrics, DB latency, recent deployments. Nothing abnormal. Then I looked at the ALB access logs — and found it. The ALB was returning 502 Bad Gateway for some requests, but our application never received them.

This pointed to a connection issue between the ALB and our ECS tasks. I dug into the ECS task health checks and found the root cause: we had recently updated our Spring Boot version, which changed the default graceful shutdown behavior. During rolling deployments, tasks were deregistering from the target group, but they were closing connections before in-flight requests completed. The ALB was routing requests to targets that had already started shutting down.

The fix was two-fold:

  1. Added server.shutdown=graceful with spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase=30s

to let in-flight requests complete

  1. Configured the ALB target group's deregistration delay to 30 seconds,

matching the application's shutdown timeout

[Result] Error rate dropped to zero within one deployment cycle. I documented this as a deployment checklist item: "verify graceful shutdown configuration whenever upgrading Spring Boot version." I also added a synthetic monitor that simulates requests during deployments to catch this class of issue early.

👨‍💼 Wei: That's a good one. The ALB → ECS connection drain issue is something we've hit too. Follow-up: how did you narrow it down to the ALB being the problem, not your application?

👨 Khanh: Two signals. First, our application logs showed successful request processing — no errors logged. If the error was in our code, we'd see it. Second, I compared the ALB access log timestamps with our application logs and found requests that appeared in the ALB logs as 502 but never appeared in our application logs at all. That means our application never received the request — the ALB couldn't connect to the target.

That narrowed it to the network path between ALB and ECS task. Combined with the timing correlation to our deployment window, the graceful shutdown theory emerged.

👩‍💻 Minh: My turn. Khanh, tell me about a time you had to push back on a product requirement because it would create technical debt or risk. How did you handle it?

👨 Khanh: [Thinks]

At TMA, on the Humana healthcare project.

[Situation] The PM wanted to add a feature where patients could bulk-export their medical records as PDF — hundreds of records at once. The requirement was to do it synchronously in the API response, with a 30-second timeout.

[Task] I knew this would fail. Generating hundreds of PDFs takes minutes, not seconds. And even if we stretched the timeout, holding HTTP connections for minutes would exhaust our thread pool and impact all other API users.

[Action] I didn't just say "no." I came to the meeting with three things:

First, a proof of concept showing the timing: I generated 100 PDFs locally and measured 4 minutes. With 30-second timeout, this would never work — data, not opinion.

Second, a counter-proposal: make it asynchronous. The API accepts the request, returns a job ID immediately. A background worker generates the PDFs, uploads to S3, and sends the patient a download link via email/notification. Better UX anyway — the patient doesn't have to stare at a loading screen.

Third, a comparison table: sync approach (risks: thread pool exhaustion, timeouts, poor UX) vs async approach (reliable, scalable, better UX, minimal extra infra since we already had Kafka).

[Result] The PM agreed immediately once he saw the data. We shipped the async version in one sprint. It now handles exports of any size — 10 records or 10,000 — without impacting the API. The PM later told me he appreciated that I came with a solution, not just a complaint.

👩‍💻 Minh: Good. You didn't just block it, you came with data and an alternative. That's what we expect from seniors here.

👨‍💼 Wei: Next one — this one's important for our team. Describe how you've worked across timezones with a distributed team. What challenges did you face and how did you solve them?

👨 Khanh: This is my daily reality at Sony.

[Situation] The Sony project has stakeholders in Japan, our development team in Vietnam, and sometimes QA in different timezones. The overlap window is only about 3-4 hours per day with Japan.

[Task] I needed to ensure that critical technical decisions, incident responses, and project updates didn't get blocked by timezone gaps.

[Action] I adopted several practices:

Async-first communication. I write detailed Slack messages and Confluence documents rather than waiting for meetings. When I complete an investigation or make a design decision, I write a summary with context, options, and my recommendation — so the Japan team can review and respond in their morning without needing a synchronous call.

Overlap hours are sacred. I reserve the 3-hour overlap for discussions that actually need real-time conversation — design reviews, incident coordination, ambiguous requirements. Everything else is async.

Daily handoff notes. At the end of my day, I post a brief update: what I did, what's blocked, what I need from them. This became a team practice — everyone does it now, and it eliminates the "what happened while I was sleeping?" problem.

Incident bridge. For production issues, we have a documented escalation process. If I detect an incident during my hours, I handle it and write a full incident report. The Japan team wakes up to a resolved incident with full context — not an active fire.

[Result] Our team was recognized by the client for having the smoothest cross-timezone collaboration. The handoff notes practice was adopted by other teams in the project. And personally, I've never had a situation where a timezone gap caused a project delay.

👨‍💼 Wei: That's exactly the working style we need. Our HCMC and Singapore pods have 1-hour overlap. Async communication quality is make-or-break for us.

👩‍💻 Minh: Last question from me. **What's the biggest mistake you've made in your career**, and what did you learn from it?

👨 Khanh: [Pauses — this is a vulnerability question]

Early at Axon Active, about 2 years ago. I was tasked with optimizing a slow database query on the Valiant banking platform. I found that adding an index dramatically improved the query — from 3 seconds to 50 milliseconds. I was excited, tested it in staging, and pushed it to production during a maintenance window.

What I didn't realize: the index creation on a large table with 20 million rows took 8 minutes and locked the table during that time. In those 8 minutes, all transactions hitting that table failed. For a banking platform, 8 minutes of transaction failures is a serious incident.

[What I learned — three things]

First, always use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY in PostgreSQL. It takes longer but doesn't lock the table. I simply didn't know this existed — a knowledge gap.

Second, never assume staging represents production. Our staging had 100K rows. The index built in 2 seconds. Production had 20M rows. The behavior was completely different.

Third, database migrations need the same rigor as code deployments. After this incident, I advocated for a migration checklist: test on production-sized data, use non-blocking DDL operations, have a rollback plan, and always have a DBA review for large tables.

I'm honestly grateful it happened early in my career. It taught me a respect for production databases that I carry into every project now.

👩‍💻 Minh: Great answer. The fact that you can articulate exactly three lessons tells me you genuinely reflected on it.

👨‍💼 Wei: Agreed. Last thing — questions for us?

👨 Khanh: Yes, two.

First, Wei — what's the team's biggest technical challenge in the next 6 months?

👨‍💼 Wei: We're migrating from a synchronous payment processing pipeline to an event-driven architecture using Kafka. The challenge is doing it with zero downtime and maintaining exactly-once payment semantics during the transition. It's the most complex migration in Grab Payments' history.

👨 Khanh: That sounds exactly like the kind of problem I want to work on. My experience with the monolith-to-microservices migration at Axon Active and event-driven patterns at Sony would be directly applicable.

Second question for Minh — what do code reviews look like on the team? What do you look for?

👩‍💻 Minh: Every PR needs two approvals — one from the same pod and one from a cross-pod reviewer. We look for correctness obviously, but also: error handling, observability (are you adding metrics and logs?), and backward compatibility. We reject PRs that don't have tests. Reviews are thorough but we try to complete them within 4 hours.

👨 Khanh: That's a high bar — I love it. Thorough reviews are how teams maintain quality at scale. Thanks both!

👨‍💼 Wei: Great conversation, Khanh. Technical round is Thursday — 90 minutes with two staff engineers. Come prepared.

📊 ROUND 1–2 SCORECARD

Round 1 — Recruiter Screening ✅

Category Score Notes
Career narrative 10/10 "Healthcare=discipline, banking=complexity, Sony=scale" — perfect arc
Why leave current 9/10 Product impact + engineering culture + growth. Honest, no negativity
Why Grab 10/10 Referenced their engineering blog. Connected experience to role
Certifications story 10/10 "Multi-cloud gives architectural perspective" — not just résumé padding
Questions asked 10/10 On-call structure + "what makes people succeed here" — elite questions

Round 2 — Behavioral ✅

Category Score Notes
Complex debugging 10/10 ALB→ECS connection drain is advanced. Showed systematic narrowing
Pushback on requirements 10/10 Data + counter-proposal + comparison table. Not just "no"
Cross-timezone work 10/10 Async-first, overlap hours, handoff notes. Battle-tested practices
Biggest mistake 10/10 Vulnerable, specific, three concrete lessons. Shows growth
Questions asked 9/10 Connected migration question to own experience

🔑 Key Techniques in This Interview

What Made This Different From Mocks #2–#6

Technique Example
Reference their content "I read your engineering blog about event-driven payments"
Connect YOUR experience to THEIR problem "My monolith migration at Axon Active applies to your Kafka migration"
Show vulnerability strategically The database lock mistake — honest, specific, with concrete lessons
Ask about failure modes "What makes people succeed vs struggle?" reveals team values
Demonstrate timezone maturity Handoff notes, async-first — not generic "we use Slack"

Next: See [Part 2](file:///Users/tuongkhanh/Desktop/Mock%20Interview/mock_interview_7_part2.md) for the 90-minute Technical Round — System Design (payment processing pipeline) + Live Coding + Bar Raiser.

🎭 Full Mock Interview #7 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: GrabTech | Role: Senior Java Engineer, Payments Platform

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL INTERVIEW (Video Call, 90 min)

Setting: Thursday, 2 PM. Zoom + CoderPad. Two Staff Engineers.

👨‍💻 Alex (Staff Eng, Singapore): Hey Khanh, I'm Alex — Staff Engineer on Payments Core. And this is Linh — Staff on Payments Infrastructure.

👩‍💻 Linh (Staff Eng, HCMC): Hey!

👨‍💻 Alex: Today is 90 minutes, three parts:

  1. Java & architecture deep-dive (20 min)
  2. System design: payment processing (40 min)
  3. Live coding (30 min)

We want to see how you think, not just what you know. If you don't know something, say so — we'd rather see honest reasoning than BS. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Let's go!

PART A — Java & Architecture Deep-Dive (20 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: First question. In your Sony project, you're handling global traffic with strict SLAs. **How do you ensure your Spring Boot services handle high concurrency without degradation?**

Walk me through your actual configuration and thought process.

👨 Khanh: Sure. There are several layers I think about:

Thread pool tuning. Tomcat default is 200 threads. For IO-bound services like ours (calling DynamoDB, other services), we actually increase it to 400 threads with server.tomcat.threads.max=400. The key insight: IO-bound work spends most time waiting, so more threads than CPU cores is fine. We monitor active thread count via Micrometer metrics — if it approaches 80% of max, we scale out the ECS task count.

Connection pool. HikariCP for Aurora. We set maximumPoolSize based on the formula: connections = (2 × CPU_cores) + disk_spindles. For our 4-vCPU containers, that's about 10 connections. The critical settings: connectionTimeout=3000ms (fail fast if pool is exhausted), maxLifetime=600000ms (10 min — force fresh connections before Aurora DNS failover issues).

Async where possible. Non-critical work like audit logging and notifications are pushed to Kafka, not processed in the request thread. This keeps the P99 latency tight for the main response path.

Timeouts everywhere. Every external call has a timeout: RestTemplate/WebClient calls at 3 seconds, Resilience4j circuit breaker opens after 50% failure rate in a 10-call sliding window. Without timeouts, one slow downstream service can consume all your threads — a cascading failure.

Backpressure. We use a rate limiter at the API Gateway level (AWS WAF) and application level (Resilience4j @RateLimiter) to reject excess traffic with 429 rather than trying to serve it and degrading for everyone.

👨‍💻 Alex: Good. Follow-up — you mentioned increasing Tomcat threads to 400. Have you considered reactive (WebFlux)? Why or why not?

👨 Khanh: Yes, I've evaluated it. We chose to stay with servlet-based for three reasons:

First, team readiness. Reactive programming requires a fundamentally different mental model — everything is Publisher/ Subscriber, you can't use ThreadLocal (MDC for logging breaks), and debugging stack traces becomes harder. Our team of 8 engineers would need significant ramp-up time.

Second, ecosystem compatibility. Some of our libraries and the AWS SDK v1 we were using weren't reactive-native. Wrapping blocking calls in reactive pipelines is worse than just using threads — you get complexity without the benefit.

Third, diminishing returns. At our scale (tens of thousands of RPS, not millions), tuned Tomcat threads + async Kafka offloading gives us sufficient concurrency. Reactive shines when you have extremely high connection counts (100K+ concurrent) with minimal CPU work — like a chat server or WebSocket gateway. Our services do real computation (validation, transformation), so thread-per-request with proper pooling works fine.

If I were building a new proxy/gateway layer from scratch, I'd consider WebFlux. But for domain services doing business logic, servlet-based with async patterns is my recommendation.

👩‍💻 Linh: I like that answer because it shows pragmatism. We actually went through that same debate here and reached the same conclusion.

👩‍💻 Linh: Next question. **Explain how you would design an idempotent API.** This is critical for payments — a user taps "pay" twice, we should only charge once.

👨 Khanh: Idempotency in payments is non-negotiable. Here's my approach:

Client-generated idempotency key. The client sends a header Idempotency-Key: <UUID> with every payment request. This UUID is generated once and reused for retries of the same logical operation.

Server-side implementation:

@PostMapping("/payments")
public ResponseEntity<PaymentResult> processPayment(
        @RequestHeader("Idempotency-Key") String idempotencyKey,
        @RequestBody PaymentRequest request) {

    // Step 1: Check if we've seen this key before
    Optional<PaymentResult> cached = idempotencyStore.get(idempotencyKey);
    if (cached.isPresent()) {
        return ResponseEntity.ok(cached.get()); // return same result
    }

    // Step 2: Acquire distributed lock on the key
    // (prevents concurrent duplicate processing)
    if (!lockService.tryLock("pay:" + idempotencyKey, 30, SECONDS)) {
        return ResponseEntity.status(409).body(PaymentResult.conflict());
    }

    try {
        // Step 3: Double-check after acquiring lock
        cached = idempotencyStore.get(idempotencyKey);
        if (cached.isPresent()) {
            return ResponseEntity.ok(cached.get());
        }

        // Step 4: Process payment
        PaymentResult result = paymentService.charge(request);

        // Step 5: Store result with the idempotency key (TTL: 24h)
        idempotencyStore.put(idempotencyKey, result, Duration.ofHours(24));

        return ResponseEntity.ok(result);
    } finally {
        lockService.unlock("pay:" + idempotencyKey);
    }
}

Key design decisions:

  • Redis for the idempotency store — fast, supports TTL
  • Distributed lock prevents race condition where two identical

requests arrive simultaneously

  • Double-check pattern (check → lock → check again) handles

the edge case where the lock acquisition takes time

  • 24-hour TTL — long enough for retry storms, short enough

to not fill Redis

  • Store the RESULT, not just a flag — retries get the same

response (status code, payment ID, everything)

👨‍💻 Alex: Solid. What happens if the service crashes between step 4 (charge succeeded) and step 5 (storing the result)?

👨 Khanh: That's the hardest edge case. The payment went through but we have no record. If the client retries, we'd charge them again.

Solutions, from simple to complex:

Option 1: Transactional outbox. Write the payment result to our database AND the idempotency record in the SAME database transaction. If either fails, both fail. Then a separate process syncs to Redis for fast lookups.

Option 2: Payment gateway idempotency. If the payment gateway (Stripe, Adyen) supports idempotency keys, we pass our key through. The gateway itself prevents double charging. This is the safest approach because the idempotency guarantee is at the money layer.

Option 3: Reconciliation. Have a batch job that reconciles our payment records with the gateway's records periodically. Flag discrepancies for manual review. This is a safety net, not the primary mechanism.

In practice, I'd use Option 2 + Option 3. Gateway-level idempotency as the primary safety, with reconciliation as the backup.

👨‍💻 Alex: Excellent answer. You anticipated the failure mode before I even asked. That's exactly the thinking we need for payments.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Payment Processing Pipeline (40 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: OK, the main event. Here's the design problem:

*"Design a payment processing system for Grab. When a rider completes a trip, we need to charge the passenger's payment method (credit card, GrabPay wallet, bank transfer) and credit the driver. Requirements: 20K transactions/second at peak, exactly-once processing, support for multiple payment methods, real-time settlement status updates to both rider and driver."*

Take 2 minutes to think, then walk us through it.

👨 Khanh: [Takes 90 seconds to think and sketch]

Let me start with clarifying requirements, then architecture.

Functional Requirements:

  • Trip completion triggers payment
  • Charge rider (multiple payment methods)
  • Credit driver (net of commission)
  • Real-time status updates to rider and driver apps
  • Support refunds

Non-Functional:

  • 20K TPS at peak, so average ~5K TPS
  • Exactly-once payment semantics (can't charge twice, can't miss)
  • Low latency: rider should see "payment successful" within 3 seconds
  • High availability: payment system down = entire Grab is down
  • Audit trail for every transaction (financial compliance)

Estimations:

20K TPS × 86,400 sec/day ≈ 1.7B transactions/day (peak)
Each transaction record ≈ 1KB
Daily storage: 1.7TB (peak), realistic avg: ~500GB/day

High-Level Architecture:

Trip Service ──► Payment API Gateway (rate limit, auth)
                      │
                      ▼
              ┌──────────────┐
              │ Payment      │
              │ Orchestrator │ (Saga coordinator)
              └──────┬───────┘
                     │
            ┌────────┼────────┐
            ▼        ▼        ▼
      ┌─────────┐ ┌───────┐ ┌──────────┐
      │ Charge  │ │ Wallet│ │ Driver   │
      │ Service │ │Service│ │ Credit   │
      │ (cards) │ │(grab  │ │ Service  │
      │         │ │ pay)  │ │          │
      └────┬────┘ └───┬───┘ └─────┬────┘
           │          │           │
           ▼          ▼           ▼
      ┌─────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌────────┐
      │ Stripe/ │ │Internal│ │ Driver │
      │ Adyen   │ │ Ledger │ │ Ledger │
      │ Gateway │ │        │ │        │
      └─────────┘ └────────┘ └────────┘

Payment Orchestrator — this is the brain. It runs a Saga:

Step 1: Validate payment → check rider balance/card validity
Step 2: CHARGE rider → call appropriate payment method
Step 3: CREDIT driver → calculate net amount after commission
Step 4: UPDATE ledger → record transaction for accounting
Step 5: NOTIFY → push status to rider + driver apps

If Step 2 fails → no further steps (rider not charged)
If Step 3 fails → COMPENSATE: refund rider
If Step 4 fails → retry (ledger is append-only, idempotent)

👩‍💻 Linh: Let me stop you here. Why a Saga orchestrator instead of choreography with events?

👨 Khanh: For payments, orchestration is better for three reasons:

  1. Visibility. I need to know the exact state of every payment at any

moment — "charged but not credited," "pending refund," etc. With choreography, the state is scattered across event logs. With an orchestrator, the saga has an explicit state machine.

  1. Compensating transactions. If charging succeeds but driver credit

fails, I need a guaranteed refund. An orchestrator can enforce this sequentially. With choreography, I'm relying on eventual consistency which is riskier for money.

  1. Debugging. When a payment is stuck, I can query the orchestrator

DB: "show me all payments in CHARGE_COMPLETED_CREDIT_PENDING state." With choreography, I'd have to reconstruct state from event logs.

However, I'd use choreography via Kafka for non-critical downstream consumers: analytics, fraud detection, notifications. They subscribe to payment events but don't affect the core payment flow.

👨‍💻 Alex: Good trade-off analysis. Continue with the database design.

👨 Khanh: Database Strategy:

Data Storage Why
Transaction records Aurora PostgreSQL ACID, complex queries, financial compliance
Idempotency keys Redis (TTL 24h) Fast lookup, auto-expiration
Saga state Aurora (same DB as transactions) Transactional consistency with payment data
Real-time status Redis pub/sub → WebSocket Push updates to apps
Event log Kafka Async consumers, audit trail
Analytics Data warehouse (BigQuery/Redshift) Batch analytics, reporting

Transaction table schema:

CREATE TABLE payments (
    id              UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    idempotency_key VARCHAR(64) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    rider_id        VARCHAR(36) NOT NULL,
    driver_id       VARCHAR(36) NOT NULL,
    amount          DECIMAL(12,2) NOT NULL,
    currency        VARCHAR(3) NOT NULL,
    payment_method  VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,  -- CARD, GRABPAY, BANK
    status          VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,  -- PENDING, CHARGED, COMPLETED, FAILED, REFUNDED
    gateway_ref     VARCHAR(128),
    created_at      TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    updated_at      TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    version         INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0  -- optimistic locking
);

CREATE INDEX idx_payments_rider ON payments(rider_id, created_at DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_payments_status ON payments(status) WHERE status IN ('PENDING', 'CHARGED');

Exactly-once guarantee:

Three layers working together:

  1. Idempotency key at API level (Redis) — prevents duplicate submission
  2. Database unique constraint on idempotency_key — prevents duplicate records
  3. Payment gateway idempotency — prevents duplicate charges at the money layer

Scaling to 20K TPS:

  • Payment Orchestrator is stateless → horizontally scale behind LB
  • Aurora PostgreSQL: write to primary, read from replicas
  • For 20K TPS writes to PostgreSQL — we might need partitioning

by rider_id or sharding. Aurora handles up to ~30K writes/sec with proper instance sizing (db.r6g.4xlarge).

  • Kafka: 20K events/sec is comfortable with 12+ partitions
  • Redis: cluster mode, easily handles 100K+ ops/sec

Real-time notifications:

Payment Orchestrator
    │
    ├──► Kafka topic: "payment.status.updates"
    │
    ▼
Notification Consumer
    │
    ├──► Firebase Cloud Messaging (push to rider app)
    └──► Firebase Cloud Messaging (push to driver app)

Monitoring — specific to payments:

Metric Alert Threshold Why
Payment success rate < 98% Drop means gateway or service issue
Charge-to-credit latency > 5s P99 Driver should see payment fast
Stuck sagas (PENDING > 5 min) > 10 count Something is failing silently
Refund rate > 2% May indicate fraud or system bugs
Reconciliation mismatch Any Gateway records ≠ our records = money problem

👨‍💻 Alex: Very thorough. One challenge: what if the payment gateway (Stripe) is having an outage? How does your system behave?

👨 Khanh: Circuit breaker on the Charge Service → Stripe connection. When the circuit opens:

  1. New payment attempts return "payment processing delayed" to the rider
  2. The saga moves to a PENDING_RETRY state
  3. A scheduler retries pending payments every 30 seconds with

exponential backoff

  1. After 10 retries (5 minutes), the payment moves to FAILED

and the rider is notified to retry manually

For GrabPay wallet payments — those go through our internal ledger, not Stripe. So wallet payments continue working even during a card gateway outage. This is a graceful degradation strategy — we can nudge users toward GrabPay during gateway outages.

👩‍💻 Linh: Smart. Using GrabPay as a fallback during gateway outages. That's product thinking, not just engineering.

PART C — LIVE CODING (30 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: Good design. Let's code.

"Design and implement an in-memory rate limiter. It should support: 1. boolean allowRequest(String clientId) — returns true if allowed 2. Each client gets a maximum of N requests per time window 3. Use the sliding window algorithm"

Example: limit = 5 requests per 60 seconds.

👨 Khanh: Let me think about the approach first.

Sliding window — I'll track the timestamp of each request per client. When a new request comes in, I remove timestamps older than the window, then check if the count is below the limit.

Time complexity: O(n) where n = number of requests in window. Space: O(clients × limit) worst case.

For a production system, I'd use Redis sorted sets, but for this in-memory implementation, I'll use a ConcurrentHashMap with a Deque of timestamps per client. 

import java.util.concurrent.*;
import java.util.*;

public class SlidingWindowRateLimiter {
    private final int maxRequests;
    private final long windowMillis;
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deque<Long>> clientWindows;

    public SlidingWindowRateLimiter(int maxRequests, long windowSeconds) {
        this.maxRequests = maxRequests;
        this.windowMillis = windowSeconds * 1000;
        this.clientWindows = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    }

    public boolean allowRequest(String clientId) {
        long now = System.currentTimeMillis();
        long windowStart = now - windowMillis;

        // Get or create the deque for this client
        Deque<Long> timestamps = clientWindows.computeIfAbsent(
            clientId, k -> new ConcurrentLinkedDeque<>()
        );

        // Remove expired timestamps from the front
        while (!timestamps.isEmpty() && timestamps.peekFirst() <= windowStart) {
            timestamps.pollFirst();
        }

        // Check if under limit
        if (timestamps.size() < maxRequests) {
            timestamps.addLast(now);
            return true;
        }

        return false; // rate limited
    }
}

Let me trace through an example. Limit: 3 requests per 10 seconds.

Time 0s:  allowRequest("user1") → timestamps: [0], size=1 < 3 → true
Time 2s:  allowRequest("user1") → timestamps: [0,2], size=2 < 3 → true
Time 4s:  allowRequest("user1") → timestamps: [0,2,4], size=3 = 3 → true
Time 6s:  allowRequest("user1") → timestamps: [0,2,4], size=3 → false
Time 11s: allowRequest("user1") → evict 0 → [2,4], size=2 < 3 → add [2,4,11] → true

Edge cases:

  • First request ever → new deque, always allowed
  • Client never seen → computeIfAbsent handles it
  • Requests exactly at window boundary → <= in eviction handles it
  • Different clients → isolated deques

Thread safety concern: There's a race condition between the size() check and addLast(). Two threads could both see size=2, both add, resulting in size=4 (exceeding limit of 3).

Fix with synchronized per client:

public boolean allowRequest(String clientId) {
    long now = System.currentTimeMillis();
    long windowStart = now - windowMillis;

    Deque<Long> timestamps = clientWindows.computeIfAbsent(
        clientId, k -> new LinkedList<>()
    );

    synchronized (timestamps) {  // lock per client, not global
        while (!timestamps.isEmpty() && timestamps.peekFirst() <= windowStart) {
            timestamps.pollFirst();
        }
        if (timestamps.size() < maxRequests) {
            timestamps.addLast(now);
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
}

I used synchronized(timestamps) — per-client locking, so different clients don't block each other. Only concurrent requests from the SAME client are serialized, which is the correct behavior for rate limiting.

👨‍💻 Alex: Great catch on the race condition — most candidates miss that. Follow-up: how would you adapt this for a distributed system with multiple service instances?

👨 Khanh: Redis sorted set. Each member is a unique request ID (or timestamp with nanos), score is the timestamp.

MULTI
  ZREMRANGEBYSCORE rate:{clientId} 0 {windowStart}
  ZADD rate:{clientId} {now} {requestId}
  ZCARD rate:{clientId}
  EXPIRE rate:{clientId} {windowSeconds}
EXEC

If ZCARD result > maxRequests, reject (and remove the just-added member).

Using Redis MULTI ensures atomicity across instances. All service instances share the same Redis — so the rate limit is global, not per-instance. This is exactly how I'd implement it for Grab's payment API rate limiting.

👩‍💻 Linh: Perfect answer — from in-memory to distributed, with thread safety addressed. That's the complete picture.

👨‍💻 Alex: Outstanding session, Khanh. Any questions for us?

👨 Khanh: Thanks Alex and Linh. Two questions.

First — what does the tech stack look like today? I want to make sure I'm not making wrong assumptions.

👨‍💻 Alex: Java 17, Spring Boot 3.2, Kafka for events, Aurora PostgreSQL for transactional data, Redis for caching and locking, DynamoDB for some high-throughput lookup tables, deployed on EKS. Very similar to what you described in your design actually.

👨 Khanh: Great — that's almost identical to my Sony stack. Second — Linh, what's the most interesting engineering problem you've personally worked on at Grab?

👩‍💻 Linh: The payment reconciliation system. We process billions of dollars, and every cent has to match between our records and the payment gateways. Building a system that can reconcile millions of transactions daily and flag discrepancies within minutes — that was the most challenging and rewarding thing I've built here.

👨 Khanh: That sounds incredible. Thanks for sharing!

🛡️ ROUND 4 — BAR RAISER (Video Call, 45 min)

Setting: Friday, 11 AM. A Principal Engineer from a different team.

👨‍💻 Jun (Principal Eng, Maps team): Hi Khanh, I'm Jun from the Maps Platform team. I haven't seen any feedback from your other rounds — I'm a fresh evaluator. My job is to make sure we maintain our hiring bar.

This round is 50% technical depth, 50% leadership and judgment. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Ready.

👨‍💻 Jun: You have both AWS SA Professional and GCP PCA. I have a specific question: When would you recommend GCP over AWS?

👨 Khanh: Good question. Here's my honest assessment based on using both:

Use Case Winner Why
Ad-hoc analytics GCP BigQuery Serverless, fast, SQL on petabytes. Athena is slower.
ML/AI workloads GCP Vertex AI More integrated ML pipeline, better TPU access
Kubernetes GCP GKE Born from Borg, auto-upgrade, more polished than EKS
Enterprise integration AWS More services, broader ecosystem, IAM is more mature
Database options AWS Aurora, DynamoDB, ElastiCache — more managed options
Global networking Tie Both excellent. GCP's premium tier is slightly better for latency
Cost for compute GCP Sustained use discounts auto-apply (no commitment needed)

For Grab specifically — if you're already on AWS, the migration cost wouldn't justify switching. But for specific workloads like ML model serving or data analytics, a multi-cloud approach using GCP BigQuery for analytics while keeping the core platform on AWS makes strategic sense.

👨‍💻 Jun: Practical answer. Last question — the most important one.

**You're on-call. At 2 AM, you get paged: payment success rate has dropped from 99.5% to 92%. What do you do? Walk me through minute by minute.**

👨 Khanh:

Minute 0–2: Assess severity and communicate. - Check dashboard: is this affecting all payment methods or specific ones? - Check: is it one region or global? - Post in incident Slack channel: "Investigating payment success rate drop. 92% from 99.5%. Assessing scope." Minute 2–5: Narrow the blast radius. - If it's ONE payment method (e.g., credit cards) → likely gateway issue - If it's ALL methods → likely our service issue - Check recent deployments (last 24h) — did anything change? - Check Kafka consumer lag — are payments stuck in queue? Minute 5–10: Identify root cause. - Scenario: credit card failures only, Stripe returning 500s - Confirm: check Stripe status page and our circuit breaker metrics - If Stripe is degraded → our system is working correctly by failing fast Minute 10–15: Mitigate. - If Stripe outage: enable "payment pending" mode → accept ride, defer charging - Nudge users to GrabPay wallet (internal, unaffected) - Update incident channel with findings and ETA Minute 15–30: Monitor recovery. - Watch success rate as Stripe recovers - Process deferred payments when gateway is back - Verify reconciliation shows no lost or double charges Next day: Post-mortem. - Timeline, root cause, impact (how many riders affected, total failed amount) - Action items: improve circuit breaker sensitivity, add Stripe health pre-check before routing payments, consider backup gateway

👨‍💻 Jun: The "accept ride, defer charging" is key — that's the business-aware decision that separates senior from mid. You're optimizing for the rider experience, not just the technical system.

Strong performance. Thanks Khanh.

📊 FULL INTERVIEW SCORECARD

Round 3 — Technical ✅

Category Score Notes
High concurrency config 10/10 Specific numbers (thread pool, HikariCP formula), not generic
WebFlux trade-off 10/10 Three reasons, pragmatic, showed ecosystem awareness
Idempotent API design 10/10 Full code, crash scenario handled, gateway-level idempotency
System Design: Payments 10/10 Saga orchestrator with clear trade-off vs choreography
DB schema + exactly-once 10/10 Three-layer idempotency guarantee, partial index on status
Gateway outage handling 10/10 GrabPay fallback = product thinking
Coding: Rate Limiter 10/10 Working code, trace, race condition caught, Redis extension
Questions asked 9/10 "Most interesting problem you've worked on" — builds rapport

Round 4 — Bar Raiser ✅

Category Score Notes
Multi-cloud expertise 10/10 Honest comparison with specific use cases, not fanboy for either
Incident response 10/10 Minute-by-minute structure, business-aware decisions
Communication 10/10 Clear, structured, confident without arrogance

🏆 OVERALL VERDICT: STRONG HIRE — SENIOR ENGINEER

🔑 Unique Techniques in This Mock (#7 vs #2–#6)

What's Different Why It Matters
Idempotent API with code Payment-specific pattern never covered in previous mocks
Saga orchestrator vs choreography trade-off Deeper than previous "just use Saga" answers
WebFlux rejection rationale Shows you can say NO to trendy tech with reasoning
Per-client locking in rate limiter Caught race condition + fixed it — shows concurrency depth
2 AM incident response minute-by-minute Bar raiser scenario — tests real-world judgment under pressure
"Accept ride, defer charging" Business-aware engineering decision — senior-level thinking
Multi-cloud honest comparison Leverages both certifications with practical, non-biased analysis

📊 Complete 7-Interview Master Map

Mock # Company Domain System Design Coding Problem
2 LogiTech Logistics Real-time shipment tracking Group Anagrams
3 CloudMart E-Commerce Flash sale checkout LRU Cache
4 VietBank Banking Notification system Valid Parentheses
5 TravelNow Travel/OTA Hotel booking Two Sum variants

🎭 Full Mock Interview #8 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: ShopNow (SEA Marketplace, 6000+ engineers, IPO'd)

Context: ShopNow is a marketplace like Shopee/Lazada — connecting 5M+ sellers with 200M+ buyers across 7 SEA countries. 150M+ product listings, 3M+ orders/day. The Product Catalog & Search team owns the platform that powers product discovery — search, filtering, ranking, and recommendations. Based in Ho Chi Minh City. Why different from Mock #3 (CloudMart): CloudMart was a single-seller B2C e-commerce. ShopNow is a multi-seller marketplace — fundamentally different challenges: catalog scale (150M products), seller management, search ranking, and cross-seller order splitting.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREENING (Video Call, 25 min)

👩 Lisa (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! I'm Lisa from ShopNow engineering talent. I see you're currently a Senior Engineer at FPT working on the Sony Global project. Let me tell you about our opportunity first.

We're hiring for the Product Catalog team. This team owns the backbone of ShopNow — every product listing, every search query, every filter click flows through our systems. 150 million products, 50,000 search queries per second at peak, sub-200ms response time requirement.

The team is 15 engineers. We need someone who can handle distributed data systems and has strong Java fundamentals. Sound interesting?

👨 Khanh: Very much. Product search at that scale — 50K QPS with sub-200ms latency across 150M products — is an incredible engineering challenge. My experience with high-availability systems at Sony and Elasticsearch at Axon Active makes me particularly drawn to this role.

👩 Lisa: You mentioned Elasticsearch — that's actually our core search technology. Tell me about your background.

👨 Khanh: I've spent 4.5 years in backend engineering with a deliberate progression in scale and complexity.

At TMA Solutions, I built healthcare microservices — strict compliance, Kubernetes deployments, CI/CD pipelines. This was my foundation in production-grade engineering.

At Axon Active, I worked on Valiant, a banking platform migration from monolith to microservices. Crucially, this is where I worked with Elasticsearch — we integrated it for account and transaction search. I learned how to model search indices, handle data sync between PostgreSQL and ES, and tune relevance scoring.

Now at FPT/Sony Global, I design cloud-native microservices on AWS serving global users. I own system reliability — monitoring, incident response, and performance optimization under strict SLAs.

The combination of my ES experience, cloud-native architecture skills, and reliability ownership maps directly to what your catalog team needs.

👩 Lisa: That's a strong match. Why leave your current role?

👨 Khanh: Two reasons. First, I want to work on consumer-facing products where I can directly impact millions of users. At FPT, I'm building enterprise systems for Sony — excellent engineering, but the user impact is indirect.

Second, ShopNow's scale is a meaningful step up. 50K search QPS is 10x anything I've handled. I want that challenge, and I want to learn from engineers who've solved it.

👩 Lisa: Our process is 3 rounds: this call, behavioral with EM, then a technical deep-dive. Can you do all three this week?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely.

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL (Video Call, 40 min)

Setting: Wednesday, 3 PM. Google Meet. Engineering Manager.

👨‍💼 David (EM): Hey Khanh. I'm David, EM for Product Catalog. Today I want to understand how you deal with ambiguity, influence, and ownership. These are critical because our team makes decisions that affect the entire shopping experience for 200 million users.

👨‍💼 David: First question. Tell me about a time you had to make a significant technical decision with incomplete information. You didn't have all the data you wanted, but you had to move forward.

👨 Khanh: This happened at Sony, about 4 months ago.

[Situation] We needed to choose a caching strategy for a new service that served user preferences. The challenge: we didn't have clear traffic patterns yet — the service was being launched for the first time. We didn't know the read:write ratio, the cache hit rate, or the hot key distribution. But we had a deadline — the launch was in 3 weeks.

[Task] I needed to design a caching layer that would work well enough for launch, with the ability to optimize once we had real data.

[Action] Instead of trying to predict the perfect strategy, I designed for observability first, optimization later.

I implemented cache-aside with Redis and added extensive instrumentation: cache hit/miss ratio, key access frequency distribution, TTL effectiveness, and eviction rate. All exposed as CloudWatch custom metrics with Grafana dashboards.

For the initial configuration, I made conservative choices:

  • TTL of 5 minutes (short enough that stale data isn't a big problem)
  • allkeys-lru eviction (safe default)
  • Redis cluster with enough memory for 2x our estimated working set
