2. balance_after in ledger entries. At any point, I can reconstruct a wallet's balance history by reading the ledger. The wallets.balance is a materialized view for fast reads.

3. Optimistic locking on wallets:

UPDATE wallets
SET balance = balance - 100, version = version + 1
WHERE user_id = 'A' AND version = 5 AND balance >= 100;
-- If 0 rows affected → concurrent modification → retry

Transfer Service code:

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
public TransferResult transfer(TransferRequest req) {
    // 1. Idempotency check
    if (idempotencyRepo.existsByKey(req.getIdempotencyKey())) {
        return idempotencyRepo.findByKey(req.getIdempotencyKey()).getResult();
    }

    // 2. Lock wallets in consistent order (prevent deadlock)
    String first = min(req.getFrom(), req.getTo());
    String second = max(req.getFrom(), req.getTo());
    Wallet w1 = walletRepo.findForUpdate(first);  // SELECT FOR UPDATE
    Wallet w2 = walletRepo.findForUpdate(second);

    Wallet sender = req.getFrom().equals(first) ? w1 : w2;
    Wallet receiver = req.getFrom().equals(first) ? w2 : w1;

    // 3. Balance check
    if (sender.getBalance().compareTo(req.getAmount()) < 0) {
        throw new InsufficientBalanceException();
    }

    // 4. Debit + Credit
    UUID transferId = UUID.randomUUID();
    sender.debit(req.getAmount());
    receiver.credit(req.getAmount());

    // 5. Ledger entries
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.debit(transferId, sender, req.getAmount()));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.credit(transferId, receiver, req.getAmount()));

    // 6. Save idempotency
    idempotencyRepo.save(req.getIdempotencyKey(), transferId);

    // 7. Publish event (after commit via @TransactionalEventListener)
    events.publishEvent(new TransferCompleted(transferId, req));

    return TransferResult.success(transferId);
}

Critical detail: Lock ordering (line: min/max). If transfer A→B and B→A happen simultaneously, both lock wallets in the SAME order (alphabetically). This prevents deadlock.

👨‍💻 Tùng: That lock ordering detail is crucial — most candidates miss it. What about scaling beyond a single PostgreSQL?

👨 Khanh: At 200 TPS peak, single Aurora handles it easily. When we hit 2,000+ TPS:

Option 1: Partition by user ID. Users whose IDs hash to shard 1 have their wallet on DB shard 1. Same-shard transfers stay atomic. Cross-shard transfers need a Saga (less common — most transfers are within the same region).

Option 2: CQRS. Keep writes on Aurora (source of truth). Read balances from Redis cache (updated after each transaction). Most "check balance" requests hit Redis, not the DB. This reduces DB read load by 90%.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Highlight
Phone BigDecimal, idempotency knowledge 10/10 "Never UPDATE, always INSERT"
Coding Cycle detection (fraud) 10/10 DFS 3-color + production optimization ideas
Behavioral Speed vs quality 10/10 Parallel implementation + feature flag rollout
Behavioral Underperforming teammate 10/10 Private conversation + checklist + pair programming
Behavioral Financial on-call 10/10 "Fail closed" principle for money systems
Design P2P money transfer 10/10 Double-entry ledger, deadlock prevention via lock ordering

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

🎭 Full Mock Interview #12 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: StreamBox (Video Streaming Platform, 100M users, Tokyo/HCMC)

Context: StreamBox is a video streaming platform (like Netflix/YouTube). 100M monthly users, 500K concurrent viewers at peak, 50TB of new video uploaded daily. Hiring for the Video Processing Pipeline team in HCMC. Why unique: First media/streaming mock. Covers video transcoding pipelines, CDN delivery, adaptive bitrate streaming — completely different architecture patterns from all previous mocks.

📞 ROUND 1 — BEHAVIORAL + CODING (55 min)

👨‍💻 Kenji (EM, Tokyo): Hi Khanh! I'm Kenji, EM for Video Platform. I see you work on the Sony Global project — we're actually a Sony subsidiary! Let's do 20 min behavioral, 35 min coding.

Behavioral (20 min)

👨‍💻 Kenji: *"Tell me about a time you had to make a system significantly more efficient. What was the bottleneck and how did you solve it?"*

👨 Khanh: At Sony, 3 months ago.

[Situation] Our user profile API had P99 latency of 800ms — well above our 500ms SLA. The service was handling 3K RPS and the latency was slowly degrading week over week.

[Action] I investigated layer by layer:

Step 1: Identify the bottleneck. Added timing logs at each layer. Found that 650ms of the 800ms was spent in two database queries that were called sequentially — one for user preferences, one for user settings.

Step 2: Parallelize. These queries were independent. I refactored them to run concurrently using CompletableFuture.allOf():

CompletableFuture<Prefs> prefs = supplyAsync(() -> prefsRepo.find(id));
CompletableFuture<Settings> settings = supplyAsync(() -> settingsRepo.find(id));
CompletableFuture.allOf(prefs, settings).join();

This dropped latency from 800ms to 450ms — both queries running simultaneously instead of sequentially.

Step 3: Cache the hot path. 80% of profile reads were for the same 10K active users (power users). Added Redis cache with 5-minute TTL for preferences (rarely change). Cache hit rate reached 85%.

Step 4: Optimize the queries. The settings query was doing a full table scan on a 50M row table. Added a composite index on (user_id, setting_type). Query went from 350ms to 8ms.

[Result] P99 latency dropped from 800ms to 120ms — 6.5x improvement. The fix was deployed in stages over 2 sprints. I wrote an internal tech blog post documenting the investigation methodology so the team could apply it to other slow endpoints.

👨‍💻 Kenji: *"Describe a situation where you had to work with a team that had very different engineering practices than yours."*

👨 Khanh: When I joined FPT for the Sony project, coming from Axon Active.

[Situation] Axon Active used Quarkus, trunk-based development, and weekly releases. Sony/FPT used Spring Boot, long-lived feature branches (sometimes 3-4 weeks), and monthly releases with heavy change advisory board (CAB) approval processes.

[Honest first reaction] I thought the long branches were wrong. I'd experienced merge conflicts, stale code, and integration pain from long branches before.

[Action] Instead of complaining, I took time to understand WHY. I talked to the tech lead and the Sony client. The reason: Sony operates under strict change management policies. Every production deployment requires documentation, security review, and client approval. Monthly releases with CAB weren't inefficiency — they were a contractual requirement for a global consumer electronics company.

I adapted my workflow: I broke my work into smaller, reviewable chunks within the feature branch. I rebased against main daily to minimize merge conflicts. I proposed (and got approved) adding a staging environment where we could validate features continuously, even if production deployment was monthly.

[Result] My staging proposal reduced our production deployment failures by 40% because features were validated earlier. I earned the team's trust by adapting to their process first, then improving it from within — not by fighting the process from day one.

[Key lesson] Engineering practices exist in a business context. What seems "wrong" often makes sense when you understand the constraints the team operates under.

Coding: Meeting Room Scheduler (35 min)

👨‍💻 Kenji: Coding time. This is relevant to our scheduling systems for video encoding jobs.

"Design a MeetingScheduler class:

  • book(int start, int end) — books a meeting in [start, end). Returns true if no conflict.
  • cancel(int start, int end) — cancels exactly this meeting.
  • getNextAvailable(int earliest, int duration) — returns the earliest start time ≥ earliest where a meeting of duration length can fit." 
book(1, 5)   → true
book(3, 8)   → false (overlaps with [1,5))
book(6, 10)  → true
getNextAvailable(0, 3) → 10  (slots [0,1) too short, [5,6) too short, [10,∞) works)
getNextAvailable(0, 1) → 0   ([0,1) is free and long enough)

👨 Khanh: I need a data structure that:

  1. Stores non-overlapping intervals
  2. Efficiently checks for overlap
  3. Finds gaps between intervals

A TreeMap is perfect here. Key = start time, Value = end time. TreeMap keeps intervals sorted, and floorEntry/ceilingEntry let me find neighbors in O(log n).

import java.util.TreeMap;

public class MeetingScheduler {
    // Key: start time, Value: end time
    private final TreeMap<Integer, Integer> meetings = new TreeMap<>();

    public boolean book(int start, int end) {
        // Check overlap with the meeting starting before or at 'start'
        var prev = meetings.floorEntry(start);
        if (prev != null && prev.getValue() > start) {
            return false; // previous meeting extends past our start
        }

        // Check overlap with the meeting starting after 'start'
        var next = meetings.ceilingEntry(start);
        if (next != null && next.getKey() < end) {
            return false; // next meeting starts before our end
        }

        meetings.put(start, end);
        return true;
    }

    public boolean cancel(int start, int end) {
        Integer existing = meetings.get(start);
        if (existing != null && existing == end) {
            meetings.remove(start);
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }

    public int getNextAvailable(int earliest, int duration) {
        // Start searching from 'earliest'
        int candidate = earliest;

        // Check if 'earliest' falls inside an existing meeting
        var prev = meetings.floorEntry(candidate);
        if (prev != null && prev.getValue() > candidate) {
            candidate = prev.getValue(); // jump past this meeting
        }

        // Now iterate through meetings to find a gap >= duration
        while (true) {
            var next = meetings.ceilingEntry(candidate);
            if (next == null) {
                // No more meetings — infinite free space
                return candidate;
            }
            if (next.getKey() - candidate >= duration) {
                // Gap between candidate and next meeting is big enough
                return candidate;
            }
            // Gap too small — jump past this meeting
            candidate = next.getValue();
        }
    }
}

Trace:

book(1, 5) → prev=null(ok), next=null(ok) → {1:5} → true
book(3, 8) → prev={1:5}, 5 > 3 → overlap → false
book(6, 10) → prev={1:5}, 5 ≤ 6(ok). next=null(ok) → {1:5, 6:10} → true

getNextAvailable(0, 3):
  candidate=0, prev=null, next={1:5}
  gap = 1 - 0 = 1 < 3 → too small
  candidate = 5
  next = {6:10}, gap = 6 - 5 = 1 < 3 → too small
  candidate = 10
  next = null → return 10 ✓

getNextAvailable(0, 1):
  candidate=0, prev=null, next={1:5}
  gap = 1 - 0 = 1 >= 1 → return 0 ✓

Complexity:

  • book(): O(log n)
  • cancel(): O(log n)
  • getNextAvailable(): O(n) worst case (scan all meetings), O(log n) average

👨‍💻 Kenji: How would you make getNextAvailable O(log n)?

👨 Khanh: Augment the tree: at each node, store the maximum gap size in its subtree. Then I can binary search for the first node with a gap ≥ duration. This is an interval tree / augmented BST. Java's TreeMap doesn't support custom augmentation, so I'd need a custom balanced BST or use a skip list.

For our video job scheduler, if we have millions of encoding jobs, this optimization would matter. For meeting rooms (thousands of bookings), O(n) scan is fast enough.

👨‍💻 Kenji: Good pragmatic answer.

💻 ROUND 2 — SYSTEM DESIGN: Video Upload & Streaming (45 min)

👩‍💻 Yuki (Staff Eng): Design StreamBox's **video upload and streaming pipeline.** A creator uploads a video. Viewers watch it on any device (phone, tablet, TV, browser) with adaptive quality.

Scale: 50TB new video/day, 500K concurrent viewers.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • Upload: accept videos up to 10GB, any common format
  • Process: transcode to multiple resolutions and formats
  • Store: durable, globally distributed
  • Stream: adaptive bitrate, low start-up latency
  • Scale: 50TB uploads/day, 500K concurrent streams

High-Level Architecture:

Creator                                    Viewer
  │                                          │
  │ Upload                          Stream   │
  ▼                                          ▼
┌────────────┐                    ┌────────────┐
│ Upload API │                    │  CDN       │ (CloudFront)
│            │                    │  (edge)    │
└─────┬──────┘                    └─────┬──────┘
      │                                 │ cache miss
      ▼                                 ▼
┌────────────┐                    ┌────────────┐
│ S3 (raw)   │                    │ S3 (processed)│
│ input      │                    │ output       │
│ bucket     │                    │ bucket       │
└─────┬──────┘                    └──────────────┘
      │ S3 event                        ▲
      ▼                                 │
┌────────────┐    ┌─────────────┐       │
│ Transcode  │───►│ Transcode   │───────┘
│ Orchestrator│   │ Workers     │
│ (SQS/Step  │   │ (FFmpeg on  │
│  Functions)│   │  ECS Spot)  │
└─────┬──────┘   └─────────────┘
      │
      ▼
┌────────────┐
│ Metadata   │
│ DB (Aurora)│
│ video info,│
│ status,    │
│ thumbnails │
└────────────┘

Upload Flow:

1. Client requests upload URL → API returns pre-signed S3 URL
2. Client uploads directly to S3 (bypasses our servers — handles 10GB files)
3. S3 triggers event → SQS → Transcode Orchestrator
4. Orchestrator creates transcoding jobs for each resolution:
   - 1080p (H.264, 5 Mbps)
   - 720p  (H.264, 2.5 Mbps)
   - 480p  (H.264, 1 Mbps)
   - 360p  (H.264, 0.5 Mbps)
5. Each job runs on ECS Spot instances (cost: 60-90% cheaper)
   - FFmpeg transcodes the video
   - Generates HLS segments (.m3u8 manifest + .ts chunks)
   - Uploads to S3 output bucket
6. Orchestrator marks video as "ready" in metadata DB
7. Creator gets notification: "Video is live!"

Why pre-signed S3 upload? Our servers never touch the raw video file. S3 handles the 10GB upload with multipart upload, checksums, and retry. We save bandwidth and compute.

Why ECS Spot instances for transcoding? Transcoding is CPU-heavy, fault-tolerant (can restart), and bursty. Spot instances give 60-90% cost savings. If a Spot instance is reclaimed, SQS visibility timeout ensures the job is retried on another instance.

Streaming Flow (Adaptive Bitrate):

1. Viewer opens video → client requests manifest: /video/123/master.m3u8
2. CDN serves manifest (cached). Contains pointers to quality variants:
   #EXT-X-STREAM-INF:BANDWIDTH=5000000,RESOLUTION=1920x1080
   1080p/index.m3u8
   #EXT-X-STREAM-INF:BANDWIDTH=2500000,RESOLUTION=1280x720
   720p/index.m3u8
   ...

3. Client estimates bandwidth → picks appropriate quality
4. Requests 10-second video segments: /video/123/1080p/segment_001.ts
5. CDN serves from cache (hit rate ~95% for popular content)
6. Client continuously monitors bandwidth:
   - Bandwidth drops → switch to 720p (next segment)
   - Bandwidth recovers → switch back to 1080p
   This is HLS Adaptive Bitrate Streaming (ABR)

CDN Strategy:

Content Cache Policy
Manifests (.m3u8) Cache 10s (short — might change for live)
Video segments (.ts) Cache 30 days (immutable content)
Thumbnails Cache 7 days
API responses No cache (dynamic)

Popular videos (top 1%) = 90% of traffic. CDN cache hit rate ~95%.

Monitoring:

Metric Target Alert
Upload-to-ready time < 30 min for 1h video > 60 min
Transcoding failure rate < 1% > 5%
Video start time (viewer) < 2s > 5s
Rebuffering ratio < 0.5% > 2%
CDN cache hit rate > 90% < 80%

👩‍💻 Yuki: How do you handle a viral video — suddenly 100K concurrent viewers in 5 minutes?

👨 Khanh: The CDN handles this naturally. Once the first viewer fetches a segment, it's cached at the edge. All subsequent viewers get the cached copy — our origin S3 bucket sees almost no load.

For a truly viral video (millions of viewers), I'd:

  1. Pre-warm CDN: Push segments to edge locations proactively

(CloudFront supports origin shield — single cache layer before origin)

  1. S3 Transfer Acceleration: For viewers in regions far from our

S3 region

  1. Multiple CDN providers: Use a multi-CDN strategy (CloudFront +

Akamai) with DNS-based routing. If one CDN is saturated, route to the other.

👩‍💻 Yuki: Excellent. What about copyright / content moderation?

👨 Khanh: After upload, before making the video "ready":

  1. Automated scan: AWS Rekognition for NSFW content detection
  2. Audio fingerprinting: Compare against a database of copyrighted

music (like YouTube's Content ID)

  1. Manual review queue: Flagged content goes to human moderators
  2. Only after passing all checks → video status changes to "published"

This is implemented as additional steps in the Orchestrator pipeline, running in parallel with transcoding.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Highlight
Behavioral Performance optimization 10/10 Layer-by-layer investigation: parallel, cache, index
Behavioral Different practices 10/10 Adapted first, improved from within, staging proposal
Coding Meeting scheduler 10/10 TreeMap with floor/ceiling, clean gap-finding
Design Video pipeline 10/10 Pre-signed S3, Spot transcoding, HLS ABR, CDN strategy
Viral handling CDN + multi-CDN 10/10 Origin shield, pre-warming, multi-CDN failover

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

🎭 Full Mock Interview #13 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: QuickBite (Food Delivery, 80M orders/month, SEA)

Context: QuickBite is a food delivery platform (like GrabFood/ShopeeFood). 80M orders/month, 500K drivers, 300K restaurants across SEA. Hiring for the Dispatch & Matching team — the algorithm that assigns drivers to orders in real-time. Why unique: First food delivery / real-time matching mock. Covers geospatial algorithms, driver-order matching, ETA estimation — fundamentally different from all previous system designs.

📞 ROUND 1 — BEHAVIORAL + TECH SCREEN (50 min)

👩 Mai (EM): Hi Khanh! 15 min behavioral, 35 min coding.

Behavioral (15 min)

👩 Mai: *"Tell me about a time you improved a process that everyone else accepted as 'just how things are.'"*

👨 Khanh: At Sony/FPT — our deployment process.

[Situation] Every production deployment took 4 hours. The process: build locally, create a ticket, wait for CAB approval (1-2 hours), manually trigger CodePipeline, watch logs for 30 minutes, verify in production, update the ticket. Everyone accepted this as normal because "Sony requires it."

[Action] I analyzed where the 4 hours actually went:

  • 1.5 hours: waiting for CAB email approval
  • 30 min: manual build steps
  • 1 hour: watching deployment logs for errors
  • 1 hour: manual smoke testing in production

I couldn't change the CAB process (contractual), but I could automate everything else:

  1. Pre-approved deployment windows. I negotiated with the Sony

PM to pre-approve Tuesday and Thursday as deployment days. CAB approval became a checkbox, not an email chain.

  1. Automated smoke tests. Wrote a suite of 15 API health checks

that ran automatically post-deployment. Slack notification: "✅ All health checks passed" or "❌ /api/users returning 500."

  1. Single-click deployment. Created a Slack bot: /deploy staging

or /deploy production triggered the full pipeline with proper parameters. No manual CodePipeline clicking.

[Result] Deployment time: 4 hours → 45 minutes. Team velocity increased because engineers no longer dreaded deployments. We went from deploying once a week to twice a week within the same CAB constraints. The Sony PM praised the improvement.

👩 Mai: *"How do you handle disagreements between team members during code reviews?"*

👨 Khanh: I have a simple rule: **if the debate takes more than 3 comments, move it to a 10-minute call.**

Written code review comments lose tone and create defensiveness. I've seen 20-comment threads that could've been resolved in 5 minutes face-to-face.

Real example: at TMA, two engineers debated for 2 days in a PR about whether to use inheritance vs composition for a new feature. I pulled them into a 15-minute whiteboard session. We listed the trade-offs, agreed on composition (more flexible for our use case), and documented the decision as an ADR (Architecture Decision Record) so we wouldn't rehave the debate later.

My principles:

  • Style disagreements → follow the linter/team convention. Not worth debating.
  • Design disagreements → whoever has data wins. No data? Prototype both.
  • Fundamental disagreements → escalate to tech lead for a tiebreaker. No shame in this.

Coding: Find K Nearest Drivers (35 min)

👩 Mai: *"Given a list of driver locations (id, lat, lng) and a restaurant location (lat, lng), find the K nearest available drivers. Distance is Euclidean for simplicity."*

Drivers: [(D1, 1.0, 1.0), (D2, 3.0, 3.0), (D3, 0.5, 0.5), (D4, 10.0, 10.0)]
Restaurant: (0.0, 0.0), K=2
Output: [D3, D1]  (closest two)

👨 Khanh: This is the K-closest-points problem. I'll use a max-heap of size K — it keeps the K smallest distances by evicting the largest whenever the heap exceeds K.

public List<String> findNearestDrivers(
        List<Driver> drivers, double restLat, double restLng, int k) {

    // Max-heap by distance (largest distance at top)
    PriorityQueue<Driver> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> Double.compare(
            distance(b, restLat, restLng),
            distance(a, restLat, restLng)
        )
    );

    for (Driver driver : drivers) {
        maxHeap.offer(driver);
        if (maxHeap.size() > k) {
            maxHeap.poll(); // remove the farthest
        }
    }

    // Extract results (closest first)
    List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
        result.add(maxHeap.poll().id);
    }
    Collections.reverse(result);
    return result;
}

private double distance(Driver d, double lat, double lng) {
    double dx = d.lat - lat;
    double dy = d.lng - lng;
    return dx * dx + dy * dy; // skip sqrt — comparing relative distances
}

Key optimization: I use dxdx + dydy instead of sqrt(dxdx + dydy). Since sqrt is monotonic, the ordering is the same without the expensive square root operation.

Trace:

D1(1,1): dist²=1+1=2, heap=[D1(2)]
D2(3,3): dist²=9+9=18, heap=[D2(18), D1(2)]
D3(0.5,0.5): dist²=.25+.25=.5, heap=[D2(18),D1(2),D3(.5)], size=3>2
  → poll D2(18, farthest) → heap=[D1(2), D3(.5)]
D4(10,10): dist²=200, heap=[D4(200),D1(2),D3(.5)], size=3>2
  → poll D4(200) → heap=[D1(2), D3(.5)]

Extract: D1, D3 → reverse → [D3, D1] ✓

Complexity: O(n log k) time, O(k) space. For 500K drivers and K=10, this is very efficient.

👩 Mai: Good. Follow-up — in production with 500K drivers and thousands of orders per minute, how would you optimize this?

👨 Khanh: At production scale, scanning all 500K drivers per order is wasteful. Spatial indexing:

1. Geohash grid. Divide the map into geohash cells (e.g., 1km²). Store drivers in Redis by geohash:

GEOADD drivers:online <lng> <lat> "D1"
GEORADIUS drivers:online <rest_lng> <rest_lat> 5 km COUNT 20 ASC

Redis GEORADIUS returns the nearest 20 drivers within 5km — O(log n + k).

2. Expanding radius search. Start with 2km radius. If < K results, expand to 5km, then 10km. Most urban orders find drivers within 2km, so we rarely need to expand.

3. Pre-filter by status. Only search among AVAILABLE drivers. Maintain a separate Redis set drivers:available and intersect with geo results.

👩 Mai: Perfect production answer.

💻 ROUND 2 — SYSTEM DESIGN: Order Dispatch System (45 min)

👨‍💻 Dũng (Staff Eng): Design QuickBite's **real-time order dispatch system.** When a customer places an order, we need to match it with the best available driver and dispatch them to the restaurant. 3K orders/minute at peak, 500K drivers, real-time.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • Customer places order → system assigns a driver within 30 seconds
  • Driver gets notification → accepts/declines within 15 seconds
  • If declined/timeout → reassign to next best driver
  • Optimize for: delivery time, driver utilization, fairness
  • 3K orders/min = 50 orders/second

The Matching Problem:

This isn't just "find nearest driver." A good dispatch system considers:

  1. Distance to restaurant (ETA)
  2. Driver direction — a driver heading toward the restaurant is better
  3. Current load — driver already carrying an order?
  4. Fairness — driver who's been idle longest should get priority
  5. Restaurant prep time — no point dispatching a driver for

a meal that takes 20 min to cook

Architecture:

┌──────────┐     ┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────────┐
│ Order    │────►│ Dispatch │────►│  Driver Matching  │
│ Service  │     │ Queue    │     │  Engine           │
│          │     │ (Kafka)  │     │                   │
└──────────┘     └──────────┘     └─────┬──────┬──────┘
                                        │      │
                                  ┌─────▼──┐ ┌─▼──────────┐
                                  │ Driver │ │ Redis       │
                                  │ Location│ │ (driver     │
                                  │ Service│ │  state,     │
                                  │ (Redis │ │  geohash)   │
                                  │  Geo)  │ └─────────────┘
                                  └────────┘
                                        │
                                  ┌─────▼──────┐
                                  │ Notification│
                                  │ Service     │
                                  │ (FCM push)  │
                                  └─────────────┘

Dispatch Flow:

1. Order placed → Order Service publishes to Kafka "orders.new"

2. Matching Engine consumes the order:
   a. Query Redis GEORADIUS: find 20 nearest available drivers
   b. Score each driver:
      score = w1 × (1/distance) + w2 × (1/idle_time) + w3 × direction_bonus
   c. Sort by score, pick top candidate

3. Send dispatch offer to Driver #1 via push notification
   - Start 15-second countdown timer (Redis key with TTL)

4. Driver responds:
   - ACCEPT → assign order, update driver status to BUSY
   - DECLINE or TIMEOUT → try Driver #2, then #3...
   - After 5 attempts → widen search radius, recalculate

5. Once assigned:
   - Send driver ETA to customer (via WebSocket)
   - Track driver location updates (GPS every 10s)
   - Update order status: ASSIGNED → PICKED_UP → DELIVERED

Driver Location Tracking:

// Driver app sends location every 10 seconds
@PostMapping("/drivers/{id}/location")
public void updateLocation(@PathVariable String id,
                           @RequestBody LocationUpdate loc) {
    // 1. Update in Redis Geo
    redis.geoAdd("drivers:online", loc.getLng(), loc.getLat(), id);

    // 2. Update driver heading (for direction bonus)
    redis.hset("driver:" + id, Map.of(
        "lat", String.valueOf(loc.getLat()),
        "lng", String.valueOf(loc.getLng()),
        "heading", String.valueOf(loc.getHeading()),
        "speed", String.valueOf(loc.getSpeed()),
        "updated_at", String.valueOf(System.currentTimeMillis())
    ));

    // 3. Publish for live tracking
    kafka.send("driver.location." + id, loc);
}

Handling Peak Hours (lunch/dinner rush):

During peak, demand > supply. Strategies:

  1. Batch matching. Instead of matching orders instantly, collect

orders for 5-second windows and run a batch optimization:

  • Hungarian algorithm or min-cost bipartite matching
  • Globally optimizes driver-order assignments instead of greedy
  1. Stacking orders. Assign a second order to a driver who's

already delivering, if the new restaurant is on their route.

  1. Dynamic pricing. Increase delivery fee to reduce demand /

attract more drivers (business decision, not engineering).

Database:

Data Storage Why
Driver real-time location Redis Geo O(log n) geospatial queries
Driver state (available/busy) Redis Hash Fast state checks
Order records Aurora PostgreSQL ACID, complex queries for analytics
Dispatch events Kafka Event sourcing for debugging dispatch decisions
Historical locations TimescaleDB Time-series for route optimization ML

Key Metric: Dispatch Rate

Metric Target Alert
Order-to-dispatch time < 30s (P95) > 60s
Driver acceptance rate > 80% < 60%
Failed dispatches (no driver) < 2% > 5%
ETA accuracy ±3 min ±10 min

👨‍💻 Dũng: What if a driver's app crashes after accepting but before picking up?

👨 Khanh: The system monitors driver location updates. If a driver stops sending GPS pings for > 2 minutes after accepting:

  1. Mark driver as UNREACHABLE
  2. Auto-reassign the order to the next best driver
  3. Notify the customer: "We're reassigning your order. New ETA: X"
  4. When the original driver's app comes back online, notify them

the order was reassigned

We also track "driver at restaurant" — if the driver hasn't arrived at the restaurant within 2× the estimated ETA, we proactively reassign. Better to reassign early than have a cold meal.

👨‍💻 Dũng: Excellent — the proactive reassignment shows you think about the customer experience, not just the technical system.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Highlight
Behavioral Process improvement 10/10 4h→45min deploys, automated smoke tests, Slack bot
Behavioral Code review conflicts 10/10 "3-comment rule → call", ADR for decisions
Coding K-nearest drivers 10/10 Max-heap, skip sqrt, Redis GEORADIUS for production
Design Dispatch system 10/10 Multi-factor scoring, batch matching for peak hours
Failure handling Driver crash recovery 10/10 GPS monitoring, proactive reassignment

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

3 CloudMart E-Commerce Flash sale LRU Cache Redis atomic
4 VietBank Banking Notifications Valid Parentheses Spring Security
5 TravelNow Travel Hotel booking Two Sum variant Design Patterns
6 HealthSync HealthTech Rate limiter Merge Intervals Testing
7 GrabTech Payments Payment pipeline Rate Limiter Idempotency, Saga
8 ShopNow Marketplace Product search TopK Frequent Elasticsearch
9 Amazon Cloud Task scheduler DAG scheduling Leadership Principles
10 Google Messaging Group chat TimeMap+Anagrams Cassandra
11 PayWave Fintech P2P transfer Fraud detection Double-entry ledger
12 StreamBox Streaming Video pipeline Meeting scheduler HLS, CDN, FFmpeg
13 QuickBite Food Delivery Order dispatch K-nearest drivers Geospatial, batch matching

🎭 Full Mock Interview #14 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: BuzzNet (Social Media Platform, 200M MAU, Singapore)

Context: BuzzNet is a social media platform (like Twitter/Instagram). 200M monthly active users, 50K posts/second at peak, 500K feed refreshes/second. Hiring for the Feed & Timeline team. Why unique: First social media mock. Covers news feed generation (fan-out on write vs read), social graph, content ranking — the most classic system design topic not yet covered.

📞 ROUND 1 — BEHAVIORAL + CODING (50 min)

👨‍💻 Raj (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh! 15 min behavioral, 35 min coding.

Behavioral (15 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: *"Tell me about a time you had to make a trade-off between building something the 'right' way vs shipping fast. How did you decide?"*

👨 Khanh: At TMA, Humana healthcare project.

[Situation] We needed to integrate with a third-party insurance verification API for patient eligibility checks. The "right" way: build an abstraction layer, handle all error codes, add retries, circuit breaker, and comprehensive logging. Estimated: 2 weeks.

The PM needed it in 4 days for a demo to the client.

[Decision framework] I asked myself three questions:

  1. Who sees it? Demo audience only, not production users.
  2. What's the blast radius of failure? Zero — demo environment.
  3. Can I upgrade it later? Yes, if the client approves the feature.

[Action] I built a "demo-grade" integration in 3 days:

  • Direct HTTP client call (no abstraction layer)
  • Basic error handling (try-catch with generic error message)
  • Happy-path logging only

BUT — I did one thing that saved future me: I defined a clean InsuranceVerificationService interface. The demo implementation was quick-and-dirty BEHIND the interface. When we later built the production version, we swapped the implementation without changing any calling code.

[Result] Demo succeeded. Client approved. I then spent 2 weeks building the production implementation with full resilience patterns. The interface I defined on day 1 made the upgrade seamless.

[Key principle] **Decide speed vs quality based on blast radius, not ego.** Demo code can be messy. Production code cannot. But always leave a clean seam for the upgrade.

👨‍💻 Raj: "How do you onboard to a new, large codebase quickly?"

👨 Khanh: I've done this three times — TMA, Axon Active, and Sony. My system:

Day 1-2: Understand the WHY. Read the product documentation, not the code. What does this system do? Who are the users? What are the SLAs? I can't understand code without understanding its purpose.

Day 3-5: Map the architecture. Run the system locally. Follow one request end-to-end: HTTP entry → controller → service → database. Draw the dependency graph. Identify the 3-5 most important classes/services.

Day 6-10: Contribute with small PRs. I pick 2-3 small bugs or improvements. This forces me to navigate the codebase, set up the dev environment, and go through the PR process. I learn more from fixing one bug than from reading code for a week.

Ongoing: Read every PR. For the first month, I review or at least read every PR from the team. This shows me the active areas, the team's coding style, and the current priorities.

Secret weapon: I write a personal "architecture notes" document as I learn. Key services, their responsibilities, where the tricky parts are. This becomes invaluable 3 months later when I've forgotten the details.

Coding: Merge K Sorted Feeds (35 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: *"You're building a social media timeline. Each user you follow has a sorted feed (newest first). Merge K sorted feeds into one unified timeline, returning the N most recent posts."*

Feed A: [Post(t=10), Post(t=7), Post(t=3)]
Feed B: [Post(t=9), Post(t=5)]
Feed C: [Post(t=8), Post(t=6), Post(t=1)]

mergeFeeds(feeds, N=5) →
  [Post(t=10), Post(t=9), Post(t=8), Post(t=7), Post(t=6)]

👨 Khanh: This is the classic "merge K sorted lists" problem — same pattern as merging K sorted linked lists.

I'll use a max-heap since we want the most recent (largest timestamp) first. Put the first element of each feed into the heap. Pop the max, then push the next element from that same feed.

public List<Post> mergeFeeds(List<List<Post>> feeds, int n) {
    List<Post> result = new ArrayList<>();

    // Max-heap: {feedIndex, postIndex, post}
    // Sorted by timestamp descending
    PriorityQueue<int[]> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
        (a, b) -> Long.compare(
            feeds.get(b[0]).get(b[1]).timestamp,
            feeds.get(a[0]).get(a[1]).timestamp
        )
    );

    // Initialize: add first post from each feed
    for (int i = 0; i < feeds.size(); i++) {
        if (!feeds.get(i).isEmpty()) {
            maxHeap.offer(new int[]{i, 0}); // {feedIndex, postIndex}
        }
    }

    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty() && result.size() < n) {
        int[] top = maxHeap.poll();
        int feedIdx = top[0];
        int postIdx = top[1];

        result.add(feeds.get(feedIdx).get(postIdx));

        // If this feed has more posts, add next one
        if (postIdx + 1 < feeds.get(feedIdx).size()) {
            maxHeap.offer(new int[]{feedIdx, postIdx + 1});
        }
    }

    return result;
}

Trace:

Init heap: [A:0(t=10), B:0(t=9), C:0(t=8)]

Pop A:0(t=10) → result=[10]. Push A:1(t=7). Heap: [B:0(9),C:0(8),A:1(7)]
Pop B:0(t=9)  → result=[10,9]. Push B:1(t=5). Heap: [C:0(8),A:1(7),B:1(5)]
Pop C:0(t=8)  → result=[10,9,8]. Push C:1(t=6). Heap: [A:1(7),C:1(6),B:1(5)]
Pop A:1(t=7)  → result=[10,9,8,7]. Push A:2(t=3). Heap: [C:1(6),B:1(5),A:2(3)]
Pop C:1(t=6)  → result=[10,9,8,7,6]. size=5=n → STOP

Result: [10, 9, 8, 7, 6] ✓

Complexity: O(n log k) where k = number of feeds, n = posts needed. Space: O(k) for the heap.

Edge cases:

  • Empty feeds → skip in initialization
  • All feeds from same user → just return first N of that feed
  • N > total posts → return all posts
  • K = 1 → just return first N of the single feed (heap unnecessary but works)

👨‍💻 Raj: Clean. How would you handle this when a user follows 100K people and each has thousands of posts?

👨 Khanh: Two optimizations:

1. Don't merge all 100K feeds. Pre-compute a "relevant follows" list — the 500 accounts this user interacts with most (likes, comments, clicks). Only merge feeds from these 500.

2. Fan-out on write (pre-computed timeline). When a user posts, push the post ID into each follower's timeline cache (Redis list):

LPUSH timeline:{followerId} postId
LTRIM timeline:{followerId} 0 999  // keep last 1000

When the user opens their feed, the timeline is already assembled — just fetch from Redis. No merge needed at read time.

The trade-off: fan-out on write is expensive for users with millions of followers (celebrities). For those, use hybrid fan-out: push to active followers only, merge on read for inactive ones.

💻 ROUND 2 — SYSTEM DESIGN: News Feed System (45 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: Expand on what you just described. Design BuzzNet's news feed system end-to-end. 200M MAU, 50K posts/second, 500K feed refreshes/second.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • User posts content (text, image, video)
  • User follows other users
  • Feed shows posts from followed users, ranked by relevance
  • Feed refresh must be < 500ms
  • New posts should appear in followers' feeds within 30 seconds

Estimations:

200M MAU, ~50M DAU
50K new posts/second
500K feed reads/second (10:1 read-heavy)
Average user follows 200 accounts
Top 1% "celebrity" users have 1M+ followers

High-Level Architecture:

┌──────────┐      ┌──────────────┐      ┌──────────────┐
│ Post     │─────►│ Post Service │─────►│ Fan-out      │
│ Creation │      │ (validate,   │      │ Service      │
│          │      │  store)      │      │ (write to    │
└──────────┘      └──────┬───────┘      │  timelines)  │
                         │              └──────┬───────┘
                         ▼                     │
                  ┌──────────────┐             ▼
                  │ Post DB      │      ┌──────────────┐
                  │ (Aurora)     │      │ Timeline     │
                  └──────────────┘      │ Cache (Redis)│
                                        └──────┬───────┘
                                               │
┌──────────┐      ┌──────────────┐             │
│ Feed     │─────►│ Feed Service │◄────────────┘
│ Read     │      │ (assemble,   │
│          │      │  rank, serve)│
└──────────┘      └──────────────┘

The Fan-Out Decision — Hybrid Approach:

User Type Followers Strategy Why
Normal (99%) < 10K Fan-out on WRITE Pre-compute timeline, fast reads
Celebrity (1%) > 10K Fan-out on READ Writing to 1M timelines is too slow

Fan-out on Write (normal users):

User A posts → Fan-out Service:
  1. Get A's follower list (from Social Graph Service)
  2. For each follower F:
     LPUSH timeline:{F} {postId}
     LTRIM timeline:{F} 0 799  // keep last 800 posts
  3. If follower count < 10K, do this synchronously via Kafka consumers

Fan-out on Read (celebrities):

User opens feed → Feed Service:
  1. Fetch pre-computed timeline from Redis (normal users' posts)
  2. Fetch latest posts from celebrity accounts user follows
  3. Merge both lists (same merge-K-sorted algorithm from coding!)
  4. Rank by ML model
  5. Return top 20 posts (first page)

Data Storage:

Data Storage Why
Posts Aurora PostgreSQL ACID, rich queries
Post media S3 + CloudFront CDN Large files, global delivery
Social graph (follows) Redis + Aurora Redis for fast follower lookup, Aurora for persistence
Pre-computed timelines Redis Lists Fast reads, auto-trim
Feed ranking features Feature Store (Redis) ML model input: user engagement history

Post Table:

CREATE TABLE posts (
    id          BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,     -- Snowflake ID
    user_id     BIGINT NOT NULL,
    content     TEXT,
    media_urls  JSONB,
    created_at  TIMESTAMP NOT NULL,
    like_count  INT DEFAULT 0,
    reply_count INT DEFAULT 0
);
CREATE INDEX idx_posts_user ON posts(user_id, created_at DESC);

Feed Ranking:

Raw timeline order (chronological) isn't enough. We re-rank using:

score = w1 × recency
      + w2 × engagement_prediction (ML)
      + w3 × relationship_strength (interaction frequency)
      + w4 × content_type_preference
      - w5 × seen_penalty (posts already scrolled past)

The ML model predicts P(user engages with this post) based on:

  • Historical interactions between this user and the author
  • Post type (image, video, text) vs user's preference
  • Time of day
  • Content similarity to user's recent likes

Scaling to 500K feed reads/second:

  1. Redis cluster for timelines — easily handles 500K reads/sec
  2. Timeline already pre-computed — no complex query at read time
  3. Pagination with cursor — LRANGE timeline:{userId} cursor cursor+19
  4. Celebrity posts cached separately — shared across all their

followers, not duplicated per timeline

Post Delivery Latency:

Post created (t=0)
  → Kafka "posts.new" topic (t=50ms)
  → Fan-out consumer reads follower list (t=100ms)
  → Writes to Redis timelines (t=200ms for 5K followers)
  → Follower refreshes feed (t=next read)

Total: post appears in feeds within ~1 second for normal users
Celebrity posts: available on next feed refresh (read-time merge)

Monitoring:

Metric Target Alert
Feed latency P99 < 500ms > 800ms
Fan-out lag < 5s > 30s
Timeline cache hit rate > 95% < 85%
Post storage success > 99.99% < 99.9%

👨‍💻 Raj: What about deleting a post? How does that propagate?

👨 Khanh: Two levels:

Immediate: Soft-delete in Post DB (deleted_at = NOW()). The Feed Service filters out deleted posts at read time. Even if the post ID is still in Redis timelines, it won't render.

Async cleanup: A background job scans timelines and removes deleted post IDs. This is best-effort and not urgent — the soft-delete filter already prevents display.

Why not remove from all timelines immediately? If a post was fanned out to 10K timelines, removing from all of them synchronously is slow and unnecessary. The read-time filter is instant.

👨‍💻 Raj: Smart — lazy cleanup with a filter at read time. Clean.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Highlight
Behavioral Speed vs quality 10/10 "Decide based on blast radius, not ego" + clean interface seam
Behavioral Onboarding to codebase 10/10 System: WHY → architecture → small PRs → read all PRs
Coding Merge K sorted feeds 10/10 Max-heap with feed/post index, correct trace
Coding follow-up Fan-out on write 10/10 Redis LPUSH+LTRIM, hybrid for celebrities
Design News feed system 10/10 Hybrid fan-out, ML ranking formula, lazy delete

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE
