🎭 Full Mock Interview #15 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: CloudCRM (Multi-Tenant SaaS, B2B, Series D, Singapore)

Context: CloudCRM is a B2B SaaS CRM platform (like Salesforce/HubSpot). 15,000 tenant companies, 2M end users, 100M records across all tenants. Hiring for the Platform Infrastructure team that owns multi-tenancy, data isolation, and API platform. Why unique: First SaaS/multi-tenant mock. Covers tenant isolation, data partitioning strategies, noisy neighbor prevention — fundamentally different architecture from consumer-facing systems.

📞 ROUND 1 — BEHAVIORAL + CODING (50 min)

👩 Chen (EM): Hi Khanh! 15 min behavioral, 35 min coding.

Behavioral (15 min)

👩 Chen: *"Tell me about a time you had to design something that needed to serve very different customer needs with the same codebase."*

👨 Khanh: At Axon Active, Valiant banking platform.

[Situation] Valiant served both retail banking (individual customers) and private banking (wealth management) clients. Same backend, but radically different requirements: retail needed high-throughput simple transactions, private banking needed complex portfolio operations with multi-level approval workflows.

[Action] I designed a configuration-driven architecture:

  1. Feature flags per client type. Instead of if (clientType == RETAIL),

we used a config map: features.approval.workflow.enabled = true/false. The code path was identical — the behavior changed based on config.

  1. Strategy pattern for business rules. Transaction validation

had a ValidationStrategy interface. Retail used SimpleValidation (balance check only). Private banking used MultiLevelValidation (balance + compliance + advisor approval). New client types could add new strategies without touching existing code.

  1. Shared data model with optional extensions. Core Account

entity was shared. Private banking fields (risk profile, advisor) were in a separate AccountExtension table joined only when needed. Retail queries never touched the extension table — no performance penalty.

[Result] One codebase served both client types. Adding corporate banking later took 2 weeks instead of the estimated 2 months because we just added new strategies and config values.

👩 Chen: *"How do you handle a situation where you're asked to estimate something you've never built before?"*

👨 Khanh: I use a decomposition + uncertainty multiplier approach.

Step 1: Decompose. Break the unknown into smaller knowns. Even if I've never built "a real-time analytics dashboard," I've built APIs, database queries, and WebSocket connections. Those pieces I can estimate.

Step 2: Spike the unknowns. For the part I genuinely can't estimate (e.g., integrating a new charting library), I timebox a spike: "Give me 4 hours to prototype this one piece, then I'll give you a real estimate."

Step 3: Uncertainty multiplier.

  • Built something similar before → 1.2x my estimate
  • New tech but familiar pattern → 1.5x
  • Completely new domain → 2x
  • Plus 20% buffer for code review, testing, and "things I didn't

think of"

Step 4: Communicate ranges, not points. "I estimate 8-12 days" is more honest and useful than "10 days." I tell the PM: "8 days if everything goes smoothly, 12 if we hit unknowns. I'll update you at day 4 with a refined estimate."

This approach has kept my estimates within 20% accuracy across the last 2 years.

Coding: Autocomplete with Trie (35 min)

👩 Chen: *"Build an autocomplete system. Implement a Trie that supports:*

  • insert(word) — add a word
  • search(prefix) — return all words starting with the prefix
  • topK(prefix, k) — return the K most-searched words with that prefix"

Track how many times each word has been searched (call frequency).

👨 Khanh: I'll build a Trie where each node stores a character and leaf nodes store word frequency. For topK, I'll collect all words under the prefix and use a min-heap.

public class AutoComplete {

    private static class TrieNode {
        TrieNode[] children = new TrieNode[26];
        String word = null;      // non-null at end of a word
        int frequency = 0;       // search frequency
    }

    private final TrieNode root = new TrieNode();

    public void insert(String word) {
        TrieNode node = root;
        for (char c : word.toCharArray()) {
            int idx = c - 'a';
            if (node.children[idx] == null) {
                node.children[idx] = new TrieNode();
            }
            node = node.children[idx];
        }
        node.word = word;
        node.frequency++; // track insertions as frequency
    }

    public List<String> search(String prefix) {
        TrieNode node = findNode(prefix);
        if (node == null) return List.of();

        List<String> results = new ArrayList<>();
        collectWords(node, results);
        return results;
    }

    public List<String> topK(String prefix, int k) {
        TrieNode node = findNode(prefix);
        if (node == null) return List.of();

        // Min-heap of size K (smallest freq at top)
        PriorityQueue<TrieNode> heap = new PriorityQueue<>(
            Comparator.comparingInt(n -> n.frequency)
        );

        collectForTopK(node, heap, k);

        // Extract in descending frequency order
        List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
        while (!heap.isEmpty()) {
            result.add(heap.poll().word);
        }
        Collections.reverse(result);
        return result;
    }

    private TrieNode findNode(String prefix) {
        TrieNode node = root;
        for (char c : prefix.toCharArray()) {
            int idx = c - 'a';
            if (node.children[idx] == null) return null;
            node = node.children[idx];
        }
        return node;
    }

    private void collectWords(TrieNode node, List<String> results) {
        if (node.word != null) results.add(node.word);
        for (TrieNode child : node.children) {
            if (child != null) collectWords(child, results);
        }
    }

    private void collectForTopK(TrieNode node,
                                 PriorityQueue<TrieNode> heap, int k) {
        if (node.word != null) {
            heap.offer(node);
            if (heap.size() > k) heap.poll();
        }
        for (TrieNode child : node.children) {
            if (child != null) collectForTopK(child, heap, k);
        }
    }
}

Trace:

insert("search"), insert("search"), insert("senior"), insert("server")
insert("send"), insert("send"), insert("send")

Trie structure:
  s → e → a → r → c → h  (word="search", freq=2)
       ↘ n → i → o → r    (word="senior", freq=1)
          ↘ d              (word="send", freq=3)
       ↘ r → v → e → r    (word="server", freq=1)

topK("se", 2):
  findNode("se") → node at 'e' under 's'
  Collect all words: search(2), senior(1), send(3), server(1)
  Min-heap(size 2): [search(2), send(3)]
  Result reversed: ["send", "search"] ✓ (top 2 by frequency)

Complexity:

  • insert(): O(L) where L = word length
  • search(): O(P + W) where P = prefix length, W = total matching words
  • topK(): O(P + N log K) where N = words under prefix

👩 Chen: How would you handle this at scale for a search engine with billions of queries?

👨 Khanh: Two key changes:

1. Pre-compute top-K at each Trie node. Instead of traversing the subtree on every query, store the top-10 words directly at each prefix node. Update asynchronously when frequencies change. This makes topK() O(prefix length) — instant.

2. Distributed Trie. Partition by first 2 characters:

  • Shard "aa-am" → Server 1
  • Shard "an-az" → Server 2
  • etc.

API Gateway routes the prefix to the correct shard. Each shard fits in memory (26² = 676 shards for uniform distribution).

3. Frequency updates via streaming. Every search query → Kafka event → consumer updates frequency counters and periodically rebuilds the top-K cache at each node.

💻 ROUND 2 — SYSTEM DESIGN: Multi-Tenant SaaS Platform (45 min)

👨‍💻 Anil (Staff Eng): Design CloudCRM's **multi-tenant data platform.** 15,000 tenants sharing the same infrastructure. Requirements: data isolation between tenants, no tenant can see another's data, one large tenant can't degrade performance for others, and it must be cost-efficient.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Requirements:

  • 15K tenants, 2M users total
  • Strict data isolation (tenant A never sees tenant B's data)
  • Noisy neighbor prevention (one tenant's heavy query can't slow others)
  • Cost-efficient (can't run 15K separate databases)
  • Tenant-level customization (custom fields, workflows)

Multi-Tenancy Models — The Key Decision:

Model How Pros Cons
Separate DB per tenant Each tenant gets own database Perfect isolation Expensive, 15K databases impossible
Separate schema per tenant Shared DB, separate schemas Good isolation Connection pool per schema, migration pain
Shared tables, tenant_id column All data in same tables Cost-efficient, simple ops Must enforce isolation in every query

My recommendation: Shared tables with tenant_id for most tenants, dedicated databases for enterprise tier (top 20 largest customers).

Architecture:

┌──────────────┐
│  API Gateway │  ← extracts tenant_id from JWT
└──────┬───────┘
       │ tenant_id in request context
       ▼
┌──────────────┐
│  CRM API     │  ← TenantContext ThreadLocal
│  Service     │
└──────┬───────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
│           Tenant Router                  │
│                                          │
│  Standard tenants → Shared DB Pool       │
│  Enterprise tenants → Dedicated DB       │
└──────────────────────────────────────────┘
       │                    │
       ▼                    ▼
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│ Shared Aurora │    │ Dedicated    │
│ (14,980      │    │ Aurora       │
│  tenants)    │    │ (20 large    │
│              │    │  tenants)    │
└──────────────┘    └──────────────┘

Tenant Isolation — Defense in Depth:

Layer 1: API Gateway. Extract tenant_id from JWT token. Every request is stamped with tenant identity at the edge.

Layer 2: Application — TenantContext.

public class TenantContext {
    private static final ThreadLocal<String> currentTenant =
        new ThreadLocal<>();

    public static void set(String tenantId) { currentTenant.set(tenantId); }
    public static String get() { return currentTenant.get(); }
    public static void clear() { currentTenant.remove(); }
}

// Filter sets tenant at the start of every request
@Component
public class TenantFilter extends OncePerRequestFilter {
    @Override
    protected void doFilterInternal(HttpServletRequest req, ...) {
        String tenantId = extractFromJwt(req);
        TenantContext.set(tenantId);
        try {
            filterChain.doFilter(req, res);
        } finally {
            TenantContext.clear(); // CRITICAL — prevent leaks
        }
    }
}

Layer 3: Hibernate Filter — Automatic query modification.

@Entity
@FilterDef(name = "tenantFilter",
           parameters = @ParamDef(name = "tenantId", type = String.class))
@Filter(name = "tenantFilter", condition = "tenant_id = :tenantId")
public class Contact {
    @Column(nullable = false)
    private String tenantId;
    // ... other fields
}

// Every query automatically gets: WHERE tenant_id = 'xxx'
// Developers CAN'T forget to add tenant_id filter

Layer 4: Database — Row Level Security (PostgreSQL).

ALTER TABLE contacts ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

CREATE POLICY tenant_isolation ON contacts
    USING (tenant_id = current_setting('app.current_tenant'));

-- Even if application has a bug, the DB enforces isolation

Four layers: JWT → ThreadLocal → Hibernate Filter → DB RLS. If ANY single layer fails, the others still prevent data leakage.

Noisy Neighbor Prevention:

Problem Solution
One tenant runs expensive query Per-tenant query timeout: 10s. Kill queries exceeding limit.
One tenant consumes all API quota Per-tenant rate limiting: 1000 req/min default, configurable
One tenant's bulk import eats all DB connections Per-tenant connection pool limit: max 5 connections from shared pool of 100
One tenant's data skews indexes Partition large tables by tenant_id range

Rate Limiter implementation:

@Aspect
@Component
public class TenantRateLimiter {
    private final Map<String, RateLimiter> limiters = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    @Around("@annotation(RateLimited)")
    public Object limit(ProceedingJoinPoint pjp) throws Throwable {
        String tenant = TenantContext.get();
        RateLimiter limiter = limiters.computeIfAbsent(
            tenant, k -> RateLimiter.create(getTenantLimit(k))
        );

        if (!limiter.tryAcquire(1, TimeUnit.SECONDS)) {
            throw new TooManyRequestsException(
                "Rate limit exceeded for tenant " + tenant
            );
        }
        return pjp.proceed();
    }
}

Tenant-Specific Customization (Custom Fields):

CRM customers need custom fields. Two approaches:

Approach How Pros Cons
EAV (Entity-Attribute-Value) Separate table: (entity_id, field_name, field_value) Unlimited flexibility Complex queries, poor performance
JSONB column custom_fields JSONB on main table PostgreSQL indexes JSONB, simple queries Max document size, can't enforce types

My choice: JSONB for simplicity with GIN index:

ALTER TABLE contacts ADD COLUMN custom_fields JSONB DEFAULT '{}';
CREATE INDEX idx_contacts_custom ON contacts USING GIN(custom_fields);

-- Query custom field:
SELECT * FROM contacts
WHERE tenant_id = 'xxx'
  AND custom_fields->>'industry' = 'tech';

Monitoring:

Metric Per-Tenant Alert
API request count Yes > 2x normal for that tenant
DB query time P99 Yes > 5s
Storage usage Yes > 80% of plan limit
Connection pool usage Yes > 80% of tenant limit

👨‍💻 Anil: How do you handle schema migrations across 15K tenants?

👨 Khanh: Since we use shared tables (not schema-per-tenant), migrations run once on the shared database. Standard Flyway migration.

The risk: a migration on a table with 100M rows (all tenants combined) could lock the table. Mitigation:

  1. Online DDL: ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN in PostgreSQL is

instant for nullable columns (no table rewrite).

  1. Large migrations: Use pg_repack or create new table + backfill

for operations that rewrite data.

  1. Feature flags: Deploy code that handles both old and new

schema, run migration, then remove old-schema code path.

For enterprise tenants on dedicated databases, we run the same Flyway migration independently — automated via a migration orchestrator that tracks each tenant's schema version.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Highlight
Behavioral Serving different needs 10/10 Config-driven + Strategy pattern + optional extensions
Behavioral Estimation framework 10/10 Decompose + spike + uncertainty multiplier + ranges
Coding Autocomplete Trie 10/10 Trie + min-heap topK, distributed scaling approach
Design Multi-tenant SaaS 10/10 4-layer isolation, noisy neighbor prevention, JSONB custom fields

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete 15-Interview Master Map

# Company Domain System Design Coding Unique Focus
2 LogiTech Logistics Shipment tracking Group Anagrams WebSocket
3 CloudMart E-Commerce Flash sale LRU Cache Redis atomic
4 VietBank Banking Notifications Valid Parentheses Spring Security
5 TravelNow Travel Hotel booking Two Sum variant Design Patterns
6 HealthSync HealthTech Rate limiter Merge Intervals Testing
7 GrabTech Payments Payment pipeline Rate Limiter Idempotency, Saga
8 ShopNow Marketplace Product search TopK Frequent Elasticsearch
9 Amazon Cloud Task scheduler DAG scheduling Leadership Principles
10 Google Messaging Group chat TimeMap+Anagrams Cassandra
11 PayWave Fintech P2P transfer Fraud detection Double-entry ledger
12 StreamBox Streaming Video pipeline Meeting scheduler HLS, CDN
13 QuickBite Food Delivery Order dispatch K-nearest drivers Geospatial
14 BuzzNet Social Media News feed Merge K feeds Fan-out hybrid
15 CloudCRM SaaS/CRM Multi-tenant platform Trie autocomplete Tenant isolation, JSONB, RLS

🎭 Full Mock Interview #16 — Part 1: HR + Behavioral

Company: AdPulse (Real-Time Advertising Platform, $2B valuation, Singapore)

Context: AdPulse is a Demand-Side Platform (DSP) in the programmatic advertising space. When you see an ad on a website, AdPulse's system decided which ad to show — in under 100ms. They process **3 million bid requests per second** globally. The engineering team is 250 people. Hiring for the Bidding Engine team — the core system that receives ad requests, runs ML models to predict click probability, calculates bid prices, and responds within 100ms. Based in Ho Chi Minh City. Why this is the hardest mock so far: AdTech has the most extreme latency and throughput requirements of any domain. Every millisecond of latency = lost revenue. This interview tests performance engineering, data pipeline thinking, and decision-making under extreme constraints.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREENING (Video Call, 30 min)

Setting: Monday, 10 AM. Google Meet. Senior Talent Partner.

👩 Nadia (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! I'm Nadia from AdPulse. Thanks for your time. I'll tell you about the role, then we'll chat about your background.

AdPulse is a DSP — Demand-Side Platform. Here's the simplified flow:

User visits website
    → Publisher's ad exchange sends "bid request" to DSPs
    → AdPulse has 100ms to decide:
        1. Should we bid on this ad slot?
        2. For which advertiser?
        3. How much should we bid?
    → We respond with our bid (or "no bid")
    → Exchange runs auction, highest bidder's ad is shown
    → User sees the ad → maybe clicks → advertiser pays us

We process 3 million of these bid requests every second. Our bidding engine must respond in under 50ms (leaving 50ms for network). Every millisecond of added latency means we time out on more auctions and lose revenue.

The team is 8 senior engineers. We work on the bidding engine itself, the ML model serving layer, and the real-time budget management system. The tech stack is Java 21, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, and we deploy on bare-metal servers (not cloud — latency-sensitive).

Does this sound interesting?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. 3 million bid requests per second with sub-50ms response time — that's an order of magnitude beyond anything I've worked on. But I find the challenge fascinating.

At Sony, I've optimized Spring Boot services for P99 latency under strict SLAs, and I understand the principles: minimize allocations, reduce GC pressure, cache aggressively, and eliminate unnecessary IO. Applying those principles at AdPulse's scale would be an incredible learning opportunity and challenge.

I'm also curious about one thing — you mentioned bare-metal servers. That tells me latency matters so much that even the virtualization overhead of cloud instances is too expensive. That level of performance engineering is exactly what excites me.

👩 Nadia: You picked up on the bare-metal thing — most candidates don't ask about that. You're right, we benchmarked AWS vs bare-metal and the consistent tail latency on bare-metal was 30% better.

Tell me about your career trajectory.

👨 Khanh: I'll frame it around the skills most relevant to AdPulse.

Foundation (TMA Solutions / Humana): Built healthcare microservices with strict compliance requirements. This taught me disciplined engineering — code reviews, test coverage, and production monitoring. Healthcare has zero tolerance for bugs, which I see paralleling AdTech's zero tolerance for latency.

Architecture growth (Axon Active / Valiant banking): Led a monolith-to-microservices migration for a banking platform. Here I developed deep skills in system decomposition, Elasticsearch integration, and performance-sensitive data processing. Financial systems have similar requirements to bidding engines — you process monetary transactions where errors mean real money lost.

Scale and reliability (FPT / Sony Global): Designing cloud-native services serving global users under strict SLAs. I own production monitoring, incident response, and performance optimization. Key achievement: reduced API P99 latency from 800ms to 120ms through systematic bottleneck identification — parallelizing independent queries, adding strategic caching, and database index optimization.

The thread: Each role increased my performance engineering sophistication. TMA gave me discipline, Axon gave me architecture skills, Sony gave me production-scale optimization experience. AdPulse would be the next level — where performance isn't just important, it's the entire product.

👩 Nadia: That 800ms → 120ms optimization story is exactly the kind of work you'd do here, but at much higher stakes.

Why leave Sony/FPT now?

👨 Khanh: Three reasons, ordered by importance.

First: Technical ceiling. At Sony, I've mastered our scale and our problems. The services are stable, the architecture is sound. I'm not growing as fast as I want. AdPulse's scale (3M QPS, sub-50ms) would push me into territory where I'd be learning intensely again — JVM tuning, lock-free data structures, cache-line optimization. That discomfort is where growth happens.

Second: Product fascination. Advertising technology combines ML, systems engineering, and economics in a unique way. Every bid decision is a real-time prediction — "will this user click this ad?" — and the answer must come in microseconds. That fusion of ML and low-latency systems is unlike anything I've encountered.

Third: Direct impact. At FPT, I build for a client's internal systems. At AdPulse, the bidding engine IS the product. If I make it 5ms faster, that directly translates to more auctions won and more revenue. I can measure my impact in dollars per millisecond.

👩 Nadia: "Dollars per millisecond" — I've never heard anyone frame it that way. That shows you understand our business.

Compensation expectations?

👨 Khanh: For a senior role at a company with AdPulse's technical depth and scale, I'm looking at competitive total compensation in the Singapore tech market range. But honestly, the technical challenge of this specific role is what drives my decision. I'd love to understand what the band looks like for this position.

👩 Nadia: We're competitive with Grab, Shopee, and Google for senior roles in HCMC. Equity is significant given our growth.

Process: 3 rounds after this.

  1. Behavioral deep-dive with EM (45 min)
  2. System design + coding (90 min)
  3. Culture fit with VP Engineering (30 min)

Questions for me?

👨 Khanh: Two questions.

First — what's the team's approach to testing? At 3M QPS, a bug in the bidding engine affects millions of auctions instantly. How do you safely deploy changes?

👩 Nadia: Great question. We use a "shadow bidding" approach — new code runs in parallel with production, processing the same requests but not actually submitting bids. We compare output for a week before promoting to production. Plus canary deployments to 1% of traffic first.

👨 Khanh: Shadow bidding — that's brilliant. The cost of a bad deployment is so high that you essentially run A/B tests on your own infrastructure before going live. Love it.

Second question — what does the on-call look like?

👩 Nadia: Weekly rotation, 8 engineers. When the bidding engine goes down, we lose approximately $50K per minute. So on-call is high-pressure but we've invested heavily in auto-remediation and runbooks. Most incidents are auto-resolved.

👨 Khanh: $50K per minute makes the importance very tangible. Thanks Nadia!

🎭 ROUND 2 — BEHAVIORAL DEEP-DIVE (45 min)

Setting: Wednesday, 2 PM. Zoom. Engineering Manager + Senior Engineer.

👨‍💼 James (EM, Singapore): Hey Khanh. I'm James, EM for the Bidding Engine team. And this is Linh, one of our senior engineers in HCMC.

👩‍💻 Linh (Sr Eng, HCMC): Hi!

👨‍💼 James: This round is 100% behavioral. At AdPulse, we care as much about how you work as what you know technically. Five questions, deep follow-ups. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Ready.

👨‍💼 James: Question 1. *"Tell me about the most technically complex project you've led or contributed significantly to. Not just 'built a feature' — I want something where you had to solve genuinely hard engineering problems."*

👨 Khanh: The Valiant banking platform migration at Axon Active.

[Situation] Valiant Bank had a 15-year-old Java monolith — 500K lines of code, single Oracle database, 400+ database tables with deeply intertwined foreign keys. The system processed all retail banking transactions. The mandate: decompose it into microservices without any downtime.

[Why it was hard — three specific challenges]

Challenge 1: Data decomposition. The monolith had a single database where tables had foreign keys across what should be different domains. accounts referenced customers referenced transactions referenced audit_logs. You couldn't just split the database — you'd break referential integrity.

My contribution: I mapped every table to a domain boundary using Domain-Driven Design. I identified 6 bounded contexts: Customer, Account, Transaction, Compliance, Notification, Reporting. For cross-domain references, I introduced domain events: when a Transaction was created, it published an event that the Reporting service consumed to build its read model.

Challenge 2: Strangler Fig pattern execution. We couldn't rewrite everything at once. I designed an API gateway that routed requests either to the monolith or the new microservice, depending on which features had been migrated.

Request → API Gateway
    ├── /api/customers/* → New Customer Service (migrated)
    ├── /api/accounts/*  → New Account Service (migrated)
    └── /api/legacy/*    → Old Monolith (not yet migrated)

We migrated one bounded context at a time, over 8 months. At any point, if a new service had issues, we could reroute back to the monolith within seconds.

Challenge 3: Data consistency during migration. During the transition, some data lived in the old Oracle DB and some in the new PostgreSQL databases. I built a dual-write adapter that wrote to both systems during migration, with the old system as the source of truth. A reconciliation job verified consistency every hour. Once we were confident the new service was correct for 2 weeks, we cut over.

[Result] Migrated 3 of 6 bounded contexts during my time there. Zero downtime during migration. Transaction processing latency improved 40% in the new architecture (smaller services, optimized queries). The pattern I established was followed for the remaining 3 contexts after I left.

👨‍💼 James: That's genuinely complex. Follow-up — what's the one thing you'd do differently if you did it again?

👨 Khanh: I would have invested in contract testing from day one. We caught several API incompatibilities between the new services during integration testing that contract tests would have caught earlier and cheaper. We added Spring Cloud Contract later, but by then we'd already spent debugging time we could have saved.

👩‍💻 Linh: Question 2. *"Tell me about a time you had to optimize something for performance. I want specifics — numbers, tools you used, what worked and what didn't."*

👨 Khanh: The Sony API P99 latency optimization. Let me go deeper than my previous summary.

[Starting state] User profile API, P99 = 800ms, SLA = 500ms. 3K RPS. Trending upward — we were going to breach SLA within a month.

[Tools I used]

  • Micrometer + CloudWatch: custom metrics per layer (controller,

service, repository). Each method annotated with @Timed.

  • Async Profiler: sampled CPU and allocation profiles in staging

under load.

  • Aurora Performance Insights: identified slow queries with

execution plans.

  • JFR (Java Flight Recorder): captured GC pauses and lock

contention in production.

[Investigation — layer by layer]

Finding 1: Sequential DB queries (650ms of 800ms). Two independent queries ran sequentially. Fixed with CompletableFuture.allOf(). Impact: 800ms → 450ms.

Finding 2: Missing composite index. The settings query did a sequential scan on 50M rows. Added index on (user_id, setting_type). Impact: 450ms → 250ms.

Finding 3: Jackson serialization overhead. Profiler showed 40ms spent in Jackson serializing the response. The response object had nested collections with lazy-loaded Hibernate proxies. Jackson triggered N+1 queries during serialization. Fixed by using DTOs with explicit projections instead of serializing entities directly. Impact: 250ms → 180ms.

Finding 4: GC pauses. JFR showed G1GC pauses of 30-50ms every 10 seconds. The young generation was sized too small, causing frequent minor GCs. Tuned: -XX:NewRatio=2 -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=20. Impact: 180ms → 120ms.

[What DIDN'T work] I initially tried adding a Redis cache before optimizing queries. The cache helped for hot data but masked the underlying problem. When cache misses occurred, latency was still 800ms. I learned: optimize the source first, cache second. Caching a slow query just means your cache misses are still slow.

[Result] P99: 800ms → 120ms. Each optimization was deployed separately with A/B metrics comparison. Documented the entire investigation as a "performance playbook" for the team.

👩‍💻 Linh: The Jackson N+1 during serialization — that's a subtle one that most people miss. And the lesson about optimizing before caching is exactly right.

👨‍💼 James: Question 3. *"Describe a production incident that taught you the most. Not just what happened — tell me how it changed the way you build systems."*

👨 Khanh: The database index lock incident at Axon Active that I've mentioned before taught me the mechanics. But the incident that changed my philosophy happened at Sony, 2 months ago.

[Incident] A seemingly harmless config change — increasing the Tomcat max thread pool from 200 to 400 — caused cascading failures.

[What happened] We increased threads because we were seeing thread pool exhaustion during traffic spikes. The fix seemed obvious: more threads = more capacity. We deployed it, and within 30 minutes, CPU spiked to 100%, response times went from 200ms to 5 seconds, and our health checks started failing. ECS tried to restart tasks, but new tasks also immediately overloaded.

[Root cause] 400 threads all making database queries simultaneously overwhelmed our Aurora connection pool (max 50 connections). HikariCP's queue filled up, threads blocked waiting for connections, context switching between 400 blocked threads consumed all CPU. We didn't get more throughput — we got less.

The real bottleneck was the database, not the thread pool. Adding more threads just moved the bottleneck and made it catastrophic.

[How it changed how I build systems]

Principle 1: Understand the full pipeline capacity. Before changing any resource limit, I now map the entire chain:

Client → Load Balancer → Tomcat threads → Service logic
→ HikariCP connections → Aurora → Response

Each component has a maximum throughput. The system's throughput = min(all components). Increasing one beyond the weakest link causes queuing and cascading failure.

Principle 2: Little's Law. L = λ × W (concurrent requests = arrival rate × latency). If latency increases (DB slow), concurrent requests increase, which fills thread pools, which increases latency further. It's a vicious cycle. The fix isn't more threads — it's reducing latency or rejecting excess traffic.

Principle 3: Load test before changing resource limits. We now have a staging environment with production-like load testing (using Gatling). Any configuration change is tested under load before deploying to production.

Principle 4: Circuit breaker on connection pools. I added Resilience4j circuit breaker wrapping the database calls. When connection wait time exceeds 3 seconds, the circuit opens and returns a fallback response. This prevents cascading failure — the system degrades gracefully instead of collapsing.

[Result] We reverted to 200 threads, added connection pool monitoring with alerts at 80% utilization, and implemented the circuit breaker. The system now handles traffic spikes by shedding load gracefully (429 responses) instead of collapsing.

👨‍💼 James: Little's Law and understanding the full pipeline capacity — that's Staff Engineer level thinking. Most people treat configuration changes as safe. You understand why they're not.

👩‍💻 Linh: Question 4. *"How do you approach technical disagreements when you're confident you're right but can't convince the other person with words alone?"*

👨 Khanh: I let the code and data speak.

Most recent example: at Sony, a colleague insisted on using synchronized blocks for a shared cache in a high-traffic service. I believed ConcurrentHashMap was sufficient and that synchronized would create a contention bottleneck.

Instead of arguing, I wrote a benchmark:

@BenchmarkMode(Mode.Throughput)
@Fork(2) @Warmup(iterations = 3) @Measurement(iterations = 5)
public class CacheBenchmark {

    @Benchmark
    public void synchronizedMap(Blackhole bh) {
        synchronized (syncMap) {
            bh.consume(syncMap.get(randomKey()));
        }
    }

    @Benchmark
    public void concurrentHashMap(Blackhole bh) {
        bh.consume(concurrentMap.get(randomKey()));
    }
}

Results at 16 threads:

  • synchronized: 2.3M ops/sec
  • ConcurrentHashMap: 45M ops/sec — 20x faster

I shared the results without any "I told you so." I said: "I ran this benchmark to help us both decide. Here are the numbers."

My colleague reviewed the benchmark, confirmed it was fair, and agreed to use ConcurrentHashMap. No ego bruised, and we had a documented decision with evidence.

My principle: benchmarks > opinions. If a disagreement can be resolved with data, get the data. Save persuasion for decisions that can't be benchmarked (like architecture choices or team processes).

👨‍💼 James: Question 5. *"What's your approach to learning a new domain — not just a new technology, but an entirely new business domain you know nothing about?"*

👨 Khanh: I've done this three times: healthcare (TMA), banking (Axon Active), and consumer electronics (Sony). My method:

Week 1: Become a user. Before reading any code, I use the product as a customer would. At TMA, I actually scheduled mock appointments through the patient portal. At Axon Active, I studied retail banking flows — opening accounts, making transfers. This gave me intuition about what the system SHOULD do before I saw how it DOES it.

Week 2: Learn the vocabulary. Every domain has jargon. In healthcare: "eligibility verification," "prior authorization," "claim adjudication." In banking: "double-entry bookkeeping," "settlement," "T+2." I keep a personal glossary and actively use domain terms in conversations — it builds credibility with domain experts.

Week 3-4: Map the domain to engineering concepts. I translate domain rules into technical patterns:

  • "A patient can't book two appointments at the same time" → unique

constraint with conflict detection

  • "Every financial transaction must have two entries" → double-entry

ledger pattern

  • "Ad bid must respond within 100ms" → latency budget decomposition

Ongoing: Find a domain expert and learn from them. At every company, I identify the person who understands the business best and buy them coffee. Fifteen minutes with a domain expert saves me 15 hours of reading documentation.

If I joined AdPulse, I'd spend my first week understanding programmatic advertising: what's an impression, what's a bid request, what's a second-price auction, what's eCPM. Before touching any code.

👨‍💼 James: That's mature. Most engineers skip the domain understanding and go straight to the code. Then they build technically correct but business-wrong systems.

Any questions for us?

👨 Khanh: Yes, two detailed ones.

First — James, what's the biggest risk to the Bidding Engine team in the next 12 months?

👨‍💼 James: Privacy regulations. Cookies are dying, and we rely on cookie-based user identification for targeting. We need to rebuild our targeting system to work with first-party data and contextual signals instead. It's a fundamental architecture change.

👨 Khanh: That's a fascinating challenge — moving from identity-based to context-based targeting while maintaining bid accuracy. My experience with architecture migrations at Axon Active would be directly relevant.

Second — Linh, what's the most surprising thing about working on a system that processes 3M requests per second?

👩‍💻 Linh: That the boring things matter most. It's not the algorithm that makes or breaks latency — it's things like object allocation patterns, GC tuning, TCP socket reuse, and how you structure your logs. A single String.format() in the hot path can add 5μs per request. At 3M QPS, that's 15 seconds of CPU time wasted per second. You develop an obsession with allocation.

👨 Khanh: "Obsession with allocation" — that resonates with my GC tuning work at Sony. Thank you both!

📊 ROUND 1–2 SCORECARD

Round 1 — Recruiter ✅

Category Score Notes
Career narrative 10/10 Framed as "performance engineering progression"
Why this role 10/10 "Dollars per millisecond" shows business understanding
Technical curiosity 10/10 Caught bare-metal detail, asked about shadow bidding
Questions asked 10/10 Testing strategy + on-call = operational maturity

Round 2 — Behavioral ✅

Category Score Notes
Complex project 10/10 Strangler Fig + dual-write + reconciliation. Real migration
Performance optimization 10/10 4 layers of optimization with specific tools and numbers
Incident → philosophy change 10/10 Little's Law, pipeline capacity, load testing principle
Technical disagreements 10/10 JMH benchmark resolved synchronized vs ConcurrentHashMap
Domain learning 10/10 "Become a user first" — practical, proven method

🔑 New Techniques in This Mock

Technique Why It's Elite
"Dollars per millisecond" Shows you think in business impact, not just technical metrics
Little's Law reference Mathematical framework for capacity planning — Staff-level knowledge
JMH benchmark for disagreements Data > opinions. Professional conflict resolution
"Optimize source first, cache second" Counter-intuitive for many engineers who reach for cache first
Jackson N+1 during serialization Subtle performance trap most miss — serializing Hibernate entities
Shadow bidding appreciation Understanding deployment strategies for high-stakes systems

Next: See [Part 2](file:///Users/tuongkhanh/Desktop/Mock%20Interview/mock_interview_16_part2.md) for the 90-minute Technical Round — System Design (real-time bidding engine) + Live Coding (implement a budget-aware rate limiter).

🎭 Full Mock Interview #16 — Part 2: Technical Round

Company: AdPulse | Role: Senior Java Engineer, Bidding Engine

💻 ROUND 3 — TECHNICAL DEEP-DIVE (90 min)

Setting: Friday, 10 AM. Zoom + CoderPad. Two Staff Engineers.

👨‍💻 Marcus (Staff Eng, Singapore): Hey Khanh, I'm Marcus — Staff Engineer, Bidding Engine. And this is Hanh from the ML Serving team.

👩‍💻 Hanh (Staff Eng, HCMC): Hey!

👨‍💻 Marcus: Three parts:

  1. Java performance deep-dive (25 min)
  2. System design: real-time bidding engine (40 min)
  3. Live coding (25 min)

At AdPulse, every microsecond counts. I'll push you on performance details. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Let's go!

PART A — Java Performance Deep-Dive (25 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: At 3M QPS, JVM performance IS the product. Let me test your depth.

**Question 1: Explain how G1GC works and how you'd tune it for a low-latency application processing 3M requests/second.**

👨 Khanh: G1GC divides the heap into equal-sized regions (typically 2-32MB each). Each region can be Eden, Survivor, or Old.

How G1GC works:

Heap Layout:
┌─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┬─────┐
│ Eden│ Eden│ Surv│ Old │ Old │ Eden│ Old │ Free│
└─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┴─────┘

Young GC (minor): Copies live objects from Eden → Survivor → Old
  - Stop-the-world, but only processes young regions
  - Duration: 5-20ms typically

Mixed GC: Collects young + some old regions
  - G1 picks old regions with most garbage ("garbage first" → name)
  - Concurrent marking phase identifies garbage

Full GC: Last resort — compacts entire heap
  - VERY slow (100ms+). Must be avoided.

Tuning for 3M QPS low-latency:

java \
  -Xms8g -Xmx8g              # Fixed heap — no resize pauses
  -XX:+UseG1GC                # G1 is default since Java 9
  -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=10     # Target 10ms max pause
  -XX:G1NewSizePercent=40     # Larger young gen — fewer minor GCs
  -XX:G1MaxNewSizePercent=50  # Cap young gen at 50%
  -XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35  # Start marking early
  -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=4m     # 4MB regions for 8GB heap
  -XX:+ParallelRefProcEnabled # Parallel reference processing
  -XX:+AlwaysPreTouch         # Pre-touch pages — no page faults later
  -Xlog:gc*:file=gc.log:time  # Detailed GC logging

Key tuning decisions:

Setting Value Why
-Xms = -Xmx 8g = 8g Prevent heap resize pauses
MaxGCPauseMillis 10ms Our latency budget allows only 10ms for GC
G1NewSizePercent 40% At 3M QPS, we allocate fast. Large young gen = fewer minor GCs
InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent 35% Start concurrent marking early to avoid Full GC
AlwaysPreTouch enabled Pre-allocates memory pages at startup — no OS page faults during serving

What to monitor:

  • GC pause time distribution (P99 should be < 15ms)
  • Allocation rate (MB/sec) — if increasing, we're creating too many objects
  • Promotion rate — objects moving to old gen too fast = problem
  • Full GC count — should be ZERO in steady state

👨‍💻 Marcus: Good. Have you considered ZGC for ultra-low latency?

👨 Khanh: Yes. ZGC (Java 15+) promises sub-1ms pauses regardless of heap size. It uses colored pointers and load barriers instead of stop-the-world pauses.

For a bidding engine, ZGC would be ideal IF:

  • We're on Java 17+ (we are — Java 21)
  • Our workload is allocation-heavy but long-lived objects are manageable
  • We can accept ~5% throughput reduction (ZGC's barrier overhead)

The trade-off: G1GC has better raw throughput, ZGC has better latency. For a bidding engine where latency matters more than throughput (we have horizontal scaling for throughput), ZGC is likely the better choice.

I'd benchmark both under production-like load and compare P99 pause times. If ZGC gives us sub-1ms pauses vs G1's 10-15ms, that's 10ms saved per request — enormous at our scale.

# ZGC configuration
java \
  -XX:+UseZGC \
  -XX:+ZGenerational \     # Generational ZGC (Java 21)
  -Xms8g -Xmx8g \
  -XX:SoftMaxHeapSize=6g   # Target 6GB, use 8GB only under pressure

👩‍💻 Hanh: **Question 2: How do you minimize object allocation in a hot path? Give specific Java patterns.**

👨 Khanh: At 3M QPS, every allocation triggers eventual GC. Key patterns:

1. Object pooling for expensive objects:

// BAD — 3M new byte arrays per second
public BidResponse processBid(BidRequest req) {
    byte[] buffer = new byte[4096]; // allocated every call
    // ...
}

// GOOD — thread-local reuse
private static final ThreadLocal<byte[]> BUFFER =
    ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new byte[4096]);

public BidResponse processBid(BidRequest req) {
    byte[] buffer = BUFFER.get(); // reused, zero allocation
    // ...
}

2. Avoid autoboxing in hot paths:

// BAD — Integer.valueOf() on every call
Map<Integer, Double> scores = new HashMap<>(); // boxing int→Integer, double→Double

// GOOD — primitive-specialized collections (Eclipse Collections or custom)
IntDoubleHashMap scores = new IntDoubleHashMap(); // no boxing

3. Pre-size collections:

// BAD — ArrayList resizes multiple times
List<String> results = new ArrayList<>();

// GOOD — pre-sized
List<String> results = new ArrayList<>(expectedSize);

4. StringBuilder reuse:

// BAD — String concatenation in loop creates garbage
String result = "";
for (String s : parts) result += s; // O(n²) allocations

// GOOD — ThreadLocal StringBuilder
private static final ThreadLocal<StringBuilder> SB =
    ThreadLocal.withInitial(() -> new StringBuilder(256));

public String buildKey(String... parts) {
    StringBuilder sb = SB.get();
    sb.setLength(0); // reset without deallocating
    for (String s : parts) sb.append(s);
    return sb.toString();
}

5. Avoid lambda captures in hot paths:

// BAD — lambda captures 'threshold', creates anonymous class instance each time
bidRequests.stream()
    .filter(r -> r.getFloorPrice() > threshold) // captures threshold
    .collect(Collectors.toList());

// GOOD — old-school loop, zero allocation
for (BidRequest r : bidRequests) {
    if (r.getFloorPrice() > threshold) {
        results.add(r);
    }
}

6. Flyweight pattern for frequently created value objects:

// Cache frequently used AdSize objects
public class AdSize {
    private static final Map<String, AdSize> CACHE = Map.of(
        "300x250", new AdSize(300, 250),
        "728x90",  new AdSize(728, 90),
        "320x50",  new AdSize(320, 50)
    );

    public static AdSize of(int w, int h) {
        return CACHE.getOrDefault(w + "x" + h, new AdSize(w, h));
    }
}

How I verify: Java Flight Recorder (JFR) allocation profiling in staging under load. Sort by allocation count — the top allocating call sites are your optimization targets.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Excellent depth. The ThreadLocal buffer reuse and autoboxing avoidance are exactly what we do here.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Real-Time Bidding Engine (40 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: The main challenge. Design AdPulse's bidding engine.

*"You receive bid requests from ad exchanges. For each request, you must decide: which ad to show and how much to bid. You have 50ms to respond. Scale: 3 million requests/second. Each advertiser has a daily budget that must not be overspent."*

👨 Khanh: [Takes 2 minutes to think]

What's in a bid request:

{
  "request_id": "abc-123",
  "user_id": "cookie-xyz",       // anonymized user
  "site": "news-site.com",
  "ad_slot": { "width": 300, "height": 250 },
  "floor_price": 0.50,           // minimum bid in USD CPM
  "geo": { "country": "VN", "city": "HCMC" },
  "device": { "type": "mobile", "os": "android" },
  "timestamp": 1700000000
}

What we must respond with (within 50ms):

{
  "request_id": "abc-123",
  "bid_price": 1.25,             // USD CPM
  "ad_creative_id": "creative-456",
  "advertiser_id": "adv-789"
}

Latency Budget Decomposition:

Total: 50ms
  ├── Network (exchange → us → exchange): ~15ms
  ├── Request parsing + validation: ~2ms
  ├── Campaign filtering: ~5ms
  ├── ML prediction (P(click)): ~10ms
  ├── Bid price calculation: ~3ms
  ├── Budget check: ~5ms
  ├── Response serialization: ~2ms
  └── Buffer: ~8ms

Every component must hit its latency target. No room for database queries or remote service calls in the hot path.

High-Level Architecture:

Ad Exchanges (Google, OpenRTB)
         │
         │ HTTP POST (bid requests)
         ▼
┌────────────────────────────────┐
│       Load Balancer            │  (L4, no TLS termination — too slow)
└────────────┬───────────────────┘
             │
    ┌────────▼────────┐
    │  Bidding Server  │  ×100 instances (bare-metal)
    │                  │
    │  ┌────────────┐  │
    │  │ 1. Parse   │  │
    │  │ 2. Filter  │  │  ALL in-process, no remote calls
    │  │ 3. Predict │  │
    │  │ 4. Price   │  │
    │  │ 5. Budget  │  │
    │  └────────────┘  │
    └────────┬─────────┘
             │ async (after response sent)
             ▼
    ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐
    │   Kafka      │────►│  Budget      │
    │  (bid log)   │     │  Aggregator  │
    └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘
                              │
                         ┌────▼─────┐
                         │  Budget  │
                         │  DB      │
                         └──────────┘

Key Principle: EVERYTHING is in-memory on the bidding server. No database calls, no remote service calls during bid processing.

Step 1: Campaign Data — Pre-loaded in Memory

// Loaded from DB every 30 seconds by background thread
public class CampaignCache {
    // All active campaigns, indexed for fast lookup
    private volatile CampaignIndex index;

    // Background refresh
    @Scheduled(fixedRate = 30_000)
    public void refresh() {
        List<Campaign> campaigns = campaignDb.findAllActive();
        CampaignIndex newIndex = CampaignIndex.build(campaigns);
        this.index = newIndex; // volatile swap — atomic, no lock
    }

    // Called in hot path — O(1) lookup, zero allocation
    public List<Campaign> match(BidRequest req) {
        return index.findMatching(
            req.getGeo(), req.getDevice(), req.getAdSlot()
        );
    }
}

The CampaignIndex uses pre-built lookup maps:

geo → device_type → ad_size → List<Campaign>

Nested hash maps for O(1) filtering. No iteration over all campaigns.

Step 2: ML Prediction — In-Process Model

// ONNX Runtime for in-process ML inference (~5ms)
public class ClickPredictor {
    private final OrtSession session;

    public ClickPredictor() {
        OrtEnvironment env = OrtEnvironment.getEnvironment();
        this.session = env.createSession("click_model.onnx");
    }

    public float predictClickProbability(BidRequest req, Campaign camp) {
        // Feature extraction — pre-computed, no allocation
        float[] features = featureExtractor.extract(req, camp);

        // Run inference in-process — no network call
        OnnxTensor input = OnnxTensor.createTensor(env, new float[][]{features});
        OrtSession.Result result = session.run(Map.of("input", input));

        return ((float[][]) result.get(0).getValue())[0][0];
    }
}

Why ONNX in-process vs ML service? A network call to an ML service would add 10-20ms. ONNX Runtime runs the model directly in the JVM process in ~5ms. The model file (~50MB) is loaded at startup and shared across all threads.

Step 3: Bid Price Calculation

// Second-price auction: bid slightly above expected value
public double calculateBid(float pClick, Campaign campaign, BidRequest req) {
    double expectedValue = pClick * campaign.getPayPerClick();

    // Bid shading: don't bid our full value (second-price auction)
    double shadedBid = expectedValue * 0.7; // keep 30% margin

    // Floor price check
    if (shadedBid < req.getFloorPrice()) {
        return 0; // no bid — not profitable
    }

    return shadedBid;
}

Step 4: Budget Management — The Hardest Part

The challenge: 100 servers process bids concurrently. Each server can't call a central DB to check budget — too slow. But we can't overspend the advertiser's daily budget.

Solution: Distributed Budget with Token Buckets

Budget Aggregator (central, single source of truth)
    │
    │ Every 10 seconds: allocate budget tokens to each server
    │
    ├──► Server 1: "You can spend $50 in the next 10 seconds"
    ├──► Server 2: "You can spend $50 in the next 10 seconds"
    └──► Server 100: "You can spend $50 in the next 10 seconds"

Each server has a LOCAL budget counter:
  - Bid wins → decrement local counter
  - Counter reaches 0 → stop bidding for that advertiser
  - Every 10 seconds → request new token allocation
public class BudgetManager {
    // Per-advertiser local budget (in microdollars to avoid floating point)
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, AtomicLong> localBudgets =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public boolean trySpend(String advertiserId, long microDollars) {
        AtomicLong budget = localBudgets.get(advertiserId);
        if (budget == null) return false;

        long remaining = budget.addAndGet(-microDollars);
        if (remaining < 0) {
            budget.addAndGet(microDollars); // rollback
            return false; // budget exhausted on this server
        }
        return true;
    }

    // Called by background thread every 10 seconds
    public void refreshBudgets(Map<String, Long> allocations) {
        allocations.forEach((advId, amount) ->
            localBudgets.computeIfAbsent(advId, k -> new AtomicLong())
                        .addAndGet(amount)
        );
    }
}

Why this works:

  • Hot path: AtomicLong.addAndGet() — lock-free, nanoseconds
  • No remote calls during bid processing
  • Budget accuracy: ±$50 per 10-second window (acceptable for $10K daily budgets)
  • If a server crashes, its unspent allocation is reclaimed by the aggregator

👩‍💻 Hanh: What if an advertiser's budget runs out mid-day but they want to spread it evenly across 24 hours?

👨 Khanh: Pacing algorithm. Instead of spending the full daily budget as fast as possible, the Budget Aggregator implements pacing:

Hourly allocation = (remaining_budget / remaining_hours) × pacing_factor

pacing_factor adjusts based on actual vs expected spend rate:
  - Underspending → pacing_factor > 1.0 (bid more aggressively)
  - Overspending → pacing_factor < 1.0 (reduce bid frequency)

In practice, this means some bid requests get a "no bid" response even when the budget isn't exhausted — we're throttling intentionally to spread the budget evenly. The pacing is recalculated every minute.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Perfect. That's exactly how our pacing works.

PART C — LIVE CODING (25 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: Code time. Related to what we just discussed:

*"Implement a TokenBucketLimiter that supports per-key rate limiting with configurable refill rates. This simulates our budget allocation."*

Interface:
  boolean tryConsume(String key, int tokens)
  void addTokens(String key, int tokens)

Behavior:
  - Each key starts with 0 tokens
  - addTokens() adds tokens (called by the budget aggregator)
  - tryConsume() atomically removes tokens if available
  - Must be thread-safe for concurrent access

👨 Khanh: This is a concurrent data structure problem. I need atomic operations on per-key counters.

import java.util.concurrent.ConcurrentHashMap;
import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicLong;

public class TokenBucketLimiter {
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, AtomicLong> buckets =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
    private final long maxTokens; // cap per key

    public TokenBucketLimiter(long maxTokens) {
        this.maxTokens = maxTokens;
    }

    public boolean tryConsume(String key, int tokens) {
        AtomicLong bucket = buckets.get(key);
        if (bucket == null) return false;

        // CAS loop for atomic consume
        while (true) {
            long current = bucket.get();
            if (current < tokens) return false; // insufficient

            if (bucket.compareAndSet(current, current - tokens)) {
                return true; // successfully consumed
            }
            // CAS failed — another thread modified — retry
        }
    }

    public void addTokens(String key, int tokens) {
        buckets.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new AtomicLong(0))
               .updateAndGet(current -> Math.min(current + tokens, maxTokens));
    }

    // Utility: check remaining tokens
    public long getRemaining(String key) {
        AtomicLong bucket = buckets.get(key);
        return bucket == null ? 0 : bucket.get();
    }

    // Cleanup: remove keys with zero budget (called periodically)
    public void removeExhausted() {
        buckets.entrySet().removeIf(e -> e.getValue().get() <= 0);
    }
}

Trace:

addTokens("adv-1", 100) → buckets: {"adv-1": 100}
addTokens("adv-2", 50)  → buckets: {"adv-1": 100, "adv-2": 50}

tryConsume("adv-1", 30) → CAS(100, 70) ✓ → buckets: {"adv-1": 70}
tryConsume("adv-1", 30) → CAS(70, 40) ✓ → buckets: {"adv-1": 40}
tryConsume("adv-1", 50) → 40 < 50 → false (insufficient)
tryConsume("adv-3", 10) → bucket=null → false (unknown key)

addTokens("adv-1", 200) → min(40+200, maxTokens) → capped at maxTokens

Why CAS loop instead of synchronized?

  • synchronized: acquires monitor lock, forces memory barrier,

risk of contention under 3M QPS

  • CAS loop: lock-free, only retries if actual contention occurs.

In practice, contention on a single advertiser's bucket is low because requests are distributed across 100 servers.

Thread safety analysis:

  • ConcurrentHashMap.get(): thread-safe, O(1)
  • AtomicLong.compareAndSet(): hardware-level atomic (CPU CAS instruction)
  • computeIfAbsent(): atomic insertion
  • No locks held during the entire operation

Complexity: O(1) amortized for both operations.

👩‍💻 Hanh: What if we need to support time-based auto-refill (X tokens per second automatically)?

👨 Khanh: Instead of storing a raw count, store the last refill timestamp and compute tokens on-the-fly:

public boolean tryConsume(String key, int tokens) {
    BucketState state = buckets.get(key);
    if (state == null) return false;

    synchronized (state) { // per-key lock, fine-grained
        long now = System.nanoTime();
        long elapsed = now - state.lastRefill;
        long newTokens = (elapsed / 1_000_000_000L) * state.refillRate;

        state.tokens = Math.min(state.tokens + newTokens, maxTokens);
        state.lastRefill = now;

        if (state.tokens >= tokens) {
            state.tokens -= tokens;
            return true;
        }
        return false;
    }
}

This is the lazy refill pattern — tokens are calculated at access time, not by a background timer. Zero overhead when a key isn't accessed.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Perfect. CAS for the simple version, lazy refill for the time-based version. Both are production patterns we use.

Outstanding session. Questions?

👨 Khanh: Yes — Marcus, what's the biggest latency optimization the team has ever made? What was the "aha" moment?

👨‍💻 Marcus: Switching from JSON to Protocol Buffers for bid request parsing. JSON parsing was consuming 8ms per request. Protobuf brought it to 0.5ms. We saved 7.5ms — which at our scale was worth about $2M per year in additional auctions won. The aha: serialization format is not a boring choice. It's a business decision.

👨 Khanh: "$2M per year from changing serialization format." That's the ultimate "dollars per millisecond" story. Thank you!

🤝 ROUND 4 — CULTURE FIT + VP (30 min)

👨‍💼 Tan (VP Engineering): Khanh, one question. This is the most important one.

*"At AdPulse, we operate at extreme scale. We lose $50K per minute of downtime. We process money. Engineers here carry enormous responsibility. Tell me — when have you broken something in production, and how do you carry that weight?"*

👨 Khanh: [Pauses thoughtfully]

I've broken production twice in my career. The index lock at Axon Active and the thread pool change at Sony.

The weight is real. After the Axon incident, I couldn't sleep that night. I kept replaying what I should have done differently.

But here's what I've learned about carrying that weight productively:

First, I converted guilt into systems. Every mistake I've made has resulted in a checklist, a monitoring rule, or an automated test that prevents it from happening again. The weight doesn't go away — it transforms into defensive engineering habits.

Second, I became the "pre-mortem" person. Before every major deployment, I ask: "What could go wrong? What's our rollback plan? What's the blast radius?" My team sometimes finds this annoying. But they've also never had a repeat of the incidents I've experienced.

**Third, I accept that in a system processing millions of requests, perfection is impossible.** What matters is: detection time (how fast do we know?), mitigation time (how fast do we act?), and learning (do we prevent the next one?). A 5-minute incident with a thorough post-mortem is infinitely better than a "perfect" system that nobody understands.

If I join AdPulse and manage a system where downtime costs $50K/minute, that weight would be heavy. But it's also clarifying — it forces you to build the best monitoring, the best runbooks, the best team processes. The highest-stakes systems I've worked on have also been my best-engineered systems, precisely because of that weight.

👨‍💼 Tan: That's the answer I was looking for. Welcome to AdPulse.

📊 FULL INTERVIEW SCORECARD

Category Score Notes
G1GC tuning 10/10 Full flag set with rationale, ZGC comparison
Allocation optimization 10/10 6 patterns with code: ThreadLocal, autoboxing, flyweight
Bidding engine design 10/10 In-memory everything, ONNX for ML, latency budget decomposition
Budget management 10/10 Distributed token bucket with pacing algorithm
Coding: TokenBucket 10/10 CAS loop, lazy refill follow-up, thread safety analysis

Round 4 — Culture Fit ✅

Category Score Notes
Vulnerability + maturity 10/10 "Converted guilt into systems" — profound and practical

🏆 OVERALL VERDICT: STRONG HIRE — SENIOR ENGINEER

📊 Complete 16-Interview Master Map

# Company Domain System Design Coding Unique Focus
2 LogiTech Logistics Shipment tracking Group Anagrams WebSocket
3 CloudMart E-Commerce Flash sale LRU Cache Redis atomic
4 VietBank Banking Notifications Valid Parentheses Spring Security
5 TravelNow Travel Hotel booking Two Sum variant Design Patterns
6 HealthSync HealthTech Rate limiter Merge Intervals Testing
7 GrabTech Payments Payment pipeline Rate Limiter Idempotency, Saga
8 ShopNow Marketplace Product search TopK Frequent Elasticsearch

11 PayWave Fintech P2P transfer Fraud detection Double-entry ledger

🎭 Mock Interview #17 — Senior Java Engineer (Outsourcing)

Company: KMS Technology (IT Outsourcing, 2000+ engineers, HCMC/Danang)

Context: KMS Technology is a top-tier Vietnamese IT outsourcing company. They work with US/EU clients building SaaS products. The client for this role is a US healthcare insurance company building a claims processing platform. Team: 8 developers in Vietnam + 3 in the US. Why this matters: This is how 80% of senior Java interviews actually work in Vietnam's outsourcing market. Practical questions, no LeetCode hard, focus on Spring Boot mastery, client communication, and Agile. Very different from FAANG — and MORE relevant for your job search.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (Video Call, 20 min)

👩 Hồng (HR): Chào anh Khanh! Em là Hồng, HR của KMS. Mình nói tiếng Việt nha, round technical sẽ bằng tiếng Anh. Anh ổn không?

👨 Khanh: Chào Hồng, ok em!

👩 Hồng: Anh giới thiệu ngắn về bản thân đi ạ.

👨 Khanh: Anh là Tuấn Khanh, gần 5 năm kinh nghiệm Java Backend. Anh đang làm Senior Engineer ở FPT Software, project Sony Global — thiết kế microservices trên AWS. Trước đó anh ở Axon Active làm banking platform với Quarkus, và TMA Solutions làm healthcare cho Humana. Anh có AWS SA Pro và GCP PCA.

👩 Hồng: Project hiện tại anh làm bao nhiêu người?

👨 Khanh: Team khoảng 8-10 dev, anh làm Senior — review code, thiết kế technical, và communicate trực tiếp với client Nhật.

👩 Hồng: Anh nói tiếng Anh communication với client ra sao?

👨 Khanh: Hàng ngày anh join standup với team Nhật, viết technical document bằng tiếng Anh, và present design proposal cho stakeholder. Anh tự tin communication technical bằng tiếng Anh.

👩 Hồng: Lý do anh muốn chuyển?

👨 Khanh: Hai lý do chính. Một là anh muốn làm product có impact trực tiếp đến end-user — healthcare thì mỗi feature ảnh hưởng đến bệnh nhân thật. Hai là anh muốn career path rõ ràng hơn — ở FPT thì path lên architect hoặc tech lead hơi limited do phụ thuộc vào project.

👩 Hồng: Mức lương anh expect bao nhiêu?

👨 Khanh: Với vị trí Senior và kinh nghiệm của anh, anh mong muốn mức cạnh tranh với market cho senior role ở các công ty outsourcing top-tier. Anh flexible về con số cụ thể, quan trọng là opportunity phù hợp. Em có thể share range của position này không?

👩 Hồng: Range cho vị trí này là 30-45 triệu gross tùy level. Với kinh nghiệm của anh thì khoảng 38-45. Anh ok không?

👨 Khanh: Anh thấy reasonable. Mình có thể discuss chi tiết hơn nếu qua technical round ạ.

👩 Hồng: OK anh. Round tiếp theo là technical interview bằng tiếng Anh với Tech Lead và client representative. Khoảng 60 phút.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL INTERVIEW (60 min, English)

Setting: Thursday, 2 PM. MS Teams. Tech Lead (VN) + Client Tech Lead (US).

👨‍💻 Minh (Tech Lead, KMS): Hi Khanh! I'm Minh, Tech Lead on the healthcare claims project. And this is David, our client-side Tech Lead from the US.

👨‍💼 David (Client TL, US): Hey Khanh, nice to meet you!

👨‍💻 Minh: Today we'll cover:

  1. Java & Spring Boot practical questions (20 min)
  2. Project experience walkthrough (15 min)
  3. Scenario-based discussion (15 min)
  4. Small coding exercise (10 min)

No trick questions. We want to see if you can build real production software and communicate clearly with the team.

PART A — Java & Spring Boot Practical (20 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: Let's start simple. **Explain the difference between @RestController and @Controller. When do you use each?**

👨 Khanh: @Controller is for MVC applications — it returns view names (like Thymeleaf templates). You need @ResponseBody on each method to return data directly.

@RestController combines @Controller + @ResponseBody. Every method automatically serializes the return value to JSON. I use @RestController for all REST APIs, which is 99% of what we build in microservices.

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/claims")
public class ClaimController {

    @GetMapping("/{id}")
    public ClaimResponse getClaim(@PathVariable Long id) {
        return claimService.findById(id); // auto-serialized to JSON
    }
}

👨‍💻 Minh: Good. **How do you handle exceptions in a Spring Boot REST API? Show me your approach.**

👨 Khanh: I use @ControllerAdvice with @ExceptionHandler — one central place to handle all exceptions consistently.

@RestControllerAdvice
public class GlobalExceptionHandler {

    @ExceptionHandler(ResourceNotFoundException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleNotFound(ResourceNotFoundException ex) {
        ErrorResponse error = new ErrorResponse(
            HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND.value(),
            ex.getMessage(),
            LocalDateTime.now()
        );
        return ResponseEntity.status(404).body(error);
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(MethodArgumentNotValidException.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleValidation(MethodArgumentNotValidException ex) {
        String message = ex.getBindingResult().getFieldErrors().stream()
            .map(e -> e.getField() + ": " + e.getDefaultMessage())
            .collect(Collectors.joining(", "));

        return ResponseEntity.badRequest()
            .body(new ErrorResponse(400, message, LocalDateTime.now()));
    }

    @ExceptionHandler(Exception.class)
    public ResponseEntity<ErrorResponse> handleGeneral(Exception ex) {
        log.error("Unexpected error", ex);
        return ResponseEntity.status(500)
            .body(new ErrorResponse(500, "Internal server error", LocalDateTime.now()));
    }
}

Key points: all APIs return the same error format, validation errors show which fields failed, and unexpected exceptions are logged but don't leak stack traces to the client.

👨‍💻 Minh: **How do you manage database migrations in your projects?**

👨 Khanh: I use Flyway with version-numbered SQL scripts:

src/main/resources/db/migration/
  V1__create_claims_table.sql
  V2__add_status_column.sql
  V3__create_audit_table.sql

Rules I follow:

  1. Never modify a migration that's been applied. Create a new one.
  2. Each migration is small and focused — one table change per file.
  3. Always test on a copy of production data before deploying.
  4. Rollback plan: I always write a corresponding rollback SQL

(even if Flyway doesn't enforce it).

# application.yml
spring:
  flyway:
    enabled: true
    locations: classpath:db/migration
    baseline-on-migrate: true

At Sony, we have a strict rule: no ALTER TABLE that locks the table for more than 1 second. If it would, we use the expand-and- contract pattern.

👨‍💼 David: **How do you handle authentication and authorization in your APIs?**

👨 Khanh: Spring Security with JWT tokens. Typical setup:

@Configuration
@EnableWebSecurity
public class SecurityConfig {

    @Bean
    public SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
        return http
            .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()) // REST API, no CSRF needed
            .sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(STATELESS))
            .authorizeHttpRequests(auth -> auth
                .requestMatchers("/api/auth/**").permitAll()
                .requestMatchers("/api/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
                .requestMatchers("/api/claims/**").hasAnyRole("USER", "ADMIN")
                .anyRequest().authenticated()
            )
            .addFilterBefore(jwtFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter.class)
            .build();
    }
}

The JWT filter extracts the token from the Authorization header, validates it, and sets the SecurityContext. Each request is stateless — no server-side session.

For role-based access, I use @PreAuthorize on sensitive endpoints:

@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') or #userId == authentication.principal.id")
@GetMapping("/users/{userId}/claims")
public List<Claim> getUserClaims(@PathVariable Long userId) { ... }

This ensures a user can only see their own claims unless they're admin.

👨‍💻 Minh: **What's the difference between @Transactional with REQUIRED vs REQUIRES_NEW propagation?**

👨 Khanh: REQUIRED (default): joins the existing transaction if one exists, or creates a new one. If the calling method already started a transaction, the called method runs within the same transaction.

REQUIRES_NEW: always creates a new, independent transaction. Suspends the current one. If the inner transaction fails, the outer transaction is not affected.

Practical example where REQUIRES_NEW matters:

@Service
public class ClaimService {

    @Transactional
    public void processClaim(Claim claim) {
        claimRepository.save(claim);           // part of main transaction
        auditService.logAudit(claim, "PROCESSED"); // should ALWAYS log
        externalService.notify(claim);         // might fail
    }
}

@Service
public class AuditService {
    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void logAudit(Claim claim, String action) {
        auditRepository.save(new AuditLog(claim.getId(), action));
        // This commits even if the outer transaction rolls back
    }
}

If externalService.notify() throws and rolls back the main transaction, the audit log is still saved because it ran in its own transaction. Audit logs should never be lost.

Common pitfall: calling @Transactional methods within the same class doesn't work — Spring's proxy-based AOP only intercepts calls from outside the class. That's why auditService is a separate bean.

👨‍💼 David: That self-invocation pitfall — we've had bugs from that. Good that you mentioned it.

PART B — Project Experience Walkthrough (15 min)

👨‍💼 David: Walk me through your healthcare project at TMA. We're building healthcare software too, so this is directly relevant.

👨 Khanh: Sure. At TMA, I worked on a system for Humana — one of the largest health insurance companies in the US.

What we built: A patient appointment and health records platform used by clinics. Patients schedule appointments, doctors access medical history, and the system handles insurance eligibility verification.

My responsibilities:

  • Backend microservices with Spring Boot — appointment scheduling,

patient records, and insurance verification APIs

  • Kubernetes deployments on AWS EKS
  • CI/CD pipelines with GitHub Actions and Jenkins
  • Integration with third-party APIs (insurance providers)

Architecture:

React Frontend → API Gateway (Spring Cloud Gateway)
    ├── Patient Service (Spring Boot, PostgreSQL)
    ├── Appointment Service (Spring Boot, PostgreSQL)
    ├── Insurance Verification Service (calls external APIs)
    └── Notification Service (email/SMS via SES/SNS)

Challenges and how I solved them:

1. HIPAA compliance. Healthcare data requires encryption at rest and in transit, audit logging of all data access, and role-based access control. I implemented field-level encryption for sensitive fields (SSN, DOB) using AES-256, and created an audit interceptor that logs every database query with the requesting user's identity.

2. Third-party API unreliability. Insurance verification APIs had 2-3% failure rate and sometimes took 10+ seconds. I added:

  • Circuit breaker (Resilience4j) to fail-fast
  • Retry with exponential backoff (max 3 retries)
  • Cached verification results for 24 hours (insurance status

rarely changes within a day)

3. Appointment conflict detection. Two patients booking the same time slot. Solved with database-level constraints:

ALTER TABLE appointments ADD CONSTRAINT unique_slot
    UNIQUE (doctor_id, appointment_date, time_slot);

Plus optimistic locking in the application to handle concurrent requests gracefully with a user-friendly error message.

👨‍💼 David: That's very relevant to our claims project. How did you handle the communication with the US team?

👨 Khanh: Daily standup at 9 PM Vietnam time (8 AM US Eastern). We used Jira for task tracking, Confluence for documentation, and Slack for async communication. I wrote all technical documentation in English — design docs, API specs, and deployment runbooks.

When requirements were unclear, I learned to ask specific questions rather than making assumptions. For example, instead of asking "how should eligibility work?", I'd write: "I propose we cache eligibility results for 24 hours. If the patient's insurance changes within 24 hours, the cached result would be stale. Is this acceptable, or do we need real-time verification?"

Specific questions get specific answers. Vague questions get ignored or delayed.

👨‍💼 David: That communication style is exactly what I need from the Vietnam team. Proactive, specific, documented.

PART C — Scenario-Based Discussion (15 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: Let's talk about scenarios you'd face on this project.

Scenario 1: *"We have a REST API that processes insurance claims. The processing takes 30-60 seconds (calling external providers). How would you design this so the API doesn't timeout?"*

👨 Khanh: Don't process synchronously. Use the async job pattern:

// Step 1: Accept the claim, return immediately
@PostMapping("/claims")
public ResponseEntity<ClaimResponse> submitClaim(@RequestBody ClaimRequest req) {
    Claim claim = claimService.create(req);  // save with status=PENDING
    messageQueue.send("claims.process", claim.getId()); // async

    return ResponseEntity.accepted()  // 202 Accepted
        .body(new ClaimResponse(claim.getId(), "PENDING",
              "/api/claims/" + claim.getId() + "/status"));
}

// Step 2: Background worker processes the claim
@KafkaListener(topics = "claims.process")
public void processClaim(String claimId) {
    Claim claim = claimRepository.findById(claimId);
    try {
        ExternalResult result = externalProvider.verify(claim); // 30-60 sec
        claim.setStatus("APPROVED");
        claim.setResult(result);
    } catch (Exception e) {
        claim.setStatus("FAILED");
        claim.setErrorMessage(e.getMessage());
    }
    claimRepository.save(claim);
    notificationService.notify(claim.getUserId(), "Claim " + claim.getStatus());
}

// Step 3: Client polls for status
@GetMapping("/claims/{id}/status")
public ClaimStatusResponse getStatus(@PathVariable String id) {
    Claim claim = claimRepository.findById(id);
    return new ClaimStatusResponse(claim.getStatus(), claim.getResult());
}

The client gets an immediate response with a status URL. They can poll every 5 seconds, or we can push a notification when done.

👨‍💼 David: Scenario 2: *"A client reports that the claims search page is slow — takes 5+ seconds. You check the database and the table has 10 million rows. How do you investigate and fix?"*

👨 Khanh: Step-by-step investigation:

Step 1: Get the slow query. Enable slow query logging in PostgreSQL:

SET log_min_duration_statement = 1000; -- log queries > 1 second

Step 2: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the query.

EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM claims
WHERE status = 'PENDING' AND created_at > '2024-01-01'
ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;

Look for: Seq Scan (bad — means no index), Sort on large datasets, and actual vs estimated row counts.

Step 3: Common fixes I'd check:

Problem Fix
Missing index CREATE INDEX idx_claims_status_date ON claims(status, created_at DESC)
SELECT * fetching all columns Select only needed columns: SELECT id, status, amount, created_at
No pagination Add LIMIT 20 OFFSET 0, or better: keyset pagination
N+1 query from ORM Use @EntityGraph or JOIN FETCH in the repository query

Step 4: Keyset pagination (better than OFFSET for large tables):

-- Instead of OFFSET (slow for page 500):
SELECT * FROM claims WHERE status = 'PENDING'
  ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20 OFFSET 10000; -- scans 10000 rows!

-- Keyset pagination (fast for any page):
SELECT * FROM claims WHERE status = 'PENDING'
  AND created_at < '2024-03-15T10:30:00'  -- last item from previous page
  ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20;       -- index seek, fast!

Step 5: If table is very hot and write-heavy, consider a read replica. Route all search queries to the replica, writes to primary. Spring Boot supports this with @Transactional(readOnly = true) routing to the read replica datasource.

👨‍💻 Minh: Scenario 3: *"You join the project and discover the code has no unit tests. The team says 'we don't have time for tests.' How do you introduce testing culture?"*

👨 Khanh: I would NOT try to add tests to all existing code at once. That's overwhelming and the team will resist.

My approach — gradual adoption:

Week 1-2: Lead by example. I add tests to MY code. Every PR I submit includes tests. The team sees it, sees it's not that much extra work, and sees the confidence it provides.

Week 3: Add a testing rule for new code only. "From now on, every new feature or bug fix must include at least one test." Not retroactive — we don't test old code. This is psychologically easier to accept.

Week 4: Show the value. When a test catches a bug before it reaches QA, I share it in Slack: "This test saved us a QA cycle. The bug would have taken 2 hours to debug in staging." Concrete value, not theoretical arguments.

Ongoing: Create test templates. Lower the barrier to writing tests. I create templates for common patterns:

// Template: testing a service with mocked repository
@ExtendWith(MockitoExtension.class)
class ClaimServiceTest {
    @Mock private ClaimRepository claimRepo;
    @InjectMocks private ClaimService claimService;

    @Test
    void shouldCreateClaimWithPendingStatus() {
        // Given
        ClaimRequest request = new ClaimRequest("patient-1", 500.0);

        // When
        claimService.create(request);

        // Then
        verify(claimRepo).save(argThat(claim ->
            claim.getStatus().equals("PENDING")
        ));
    }
}

I copy-paste this template and say: "Just change the class names. It takes 5 minutes." Once it's easy, people do it.

👨‍💼 David: That's exactly the right approach. We tried forcing 80% coverage once — the team wrote bad tests just to hit the number. Your gradual approach is much better.

PART D — Small Coding Exercise (10 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: Last part. Simple practical exercise — not LeetCode.

*"Write a method that validates a claim request. Return a list of validation errors. Requirements:*

  • Amount must be positive and ≤ $1,000,000
  • Patient ID must not be empty
  • Claim type must be one of: MEDICAL, DENTAL, VISION
  • Service date must not be in the future"

👨 Khanh: Clean validation with clear error messages:

public class ClaimValidator {

    private static final Set<String> VALID_TYPES =
        Set.of("MEDICAL", "DENTAL", "VISION");
    private static final BigDecimal MAX_AMOUNT =
        new BigDecimal("1000000");

    public List<String> validate(ClaimRequest request) {
        List<String> errors = new ArrayList<>();

        // Patient ID
        if (request.getPatientId() == null || request.getPatientId().isBlank()) {
            errors.add("Patient ID is required");
        }

        // Amount
        if (request.getAmount() == null) {
            errors.add("Amount is required");
        } else if (request.getAmount().compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) <= 0) {
            errors.add("Amount must be positive");
        } else if (request.getAmount().compareTo(MAX_AMOUNT) > 0) {
