            errors.add("Amount must not exceed $1,000,000");
        }

        // Claim type
        if (request.getClaimType() == null || request.getClaimType().isBlank()) {
            errors.add("Claim type is required");
        } else if (!VALID_TYPES.contains(request.getClaimType().toUpperCase())) {
            errors.add("Claim type must be one of: " + VALID_TYPES);
        }

        // Service date
        if (request.getServiceDate() == null) {
            errors.add("Service date is required");
        } else if (request.getServiceDate().isAfter(LocalDate.now())) {
            errors.add("Service date must not be in the future");
        }

        return errors;
    }
}

And the corresponding test:

@Test
void shouldRejectFutureServiceDate() {
    ClaimRequest req = new ClaimRequest("P-1", new BigDecimal("100"),
        "MEDICAL", LocalDate.now().plusDays(1));

    List<String> errors = validator.validate(req);

    assertThat(errors).containsExactly("Service date must not be in the future");
}

@Test
void shouldAcceptValidClaim() {
    ClaimRequest req = new ClaimRequest("P-1", new BigDecimal("500"),
        "DENTAL", LocalDate.now().minusDays(5));

    List<String> errors = validator.validate(req);

    assertThat(errors).isEmpty();
}

👨‍💻 Minh: Clean, readable, testable. I'd approve this PR.

👨‍💼 David: Good code. I like that you used BigDecimal for money — many candidates use double. And you wrote the test without being asked. That shows good habits.

👨 Khanh: Questions for you both?

First — David, what's the biggest challenge working with the Vietnam team currently?

👨‍💼 David: Honestly, timezone communication. Sometimes I write a question at 5 PM US time, and I don't get a response until my next morning because the Vietnam team has already gone home. I need someone senior in Vietnam who can make reasonable decisions without waiting for me.

👨 Khanh: That resonates with my Sony experience. I've developed a practice of writing "decision documents" — when I face a decision that would normally require the client, I document the options with my recommendation and send it with: "I'll proceed with Option A unless you disagree by tomorrow." This keeps progress moving across timezones.

👨‍💼 David: That's exactly what I need. "Proceed unless you disagree" — that's senior-level autonomy.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR Self-intro (VN) 9/10 Clear, concise, relevant
HR Salary negotiation 9/10 Flexible but not desperate
Technical Spring Boot basics 10/10 Exception handling, security, transactions — production-grade
Technical Flyway migrations 10/10 Rules + expand-contract pattern
Project walkthrough Healthcare at TMA 10/10 HIPAA, circuit breaker, conflict detection
Client communication English clarity 10/10 Specific questions > vague questions
Scenario Async claim processing 10/10 202 Accepted + background worker + status polling
Scenario Slow query debugging 10/10 EXPLAIN ANALYZE + keyset pagination + read replica
Scenario Testing culture 10/10 Gradual adoption, lead by example, templates
Coding Claim validator 10/10 BigDecimal, clean errors, wrote tests voluntarily
Questions "Proceed unless disagree" 10/10 Shows timezone autonomy

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE — SENIOR ENGINEER

🔑 Outsourcing Interview vs FAANG — Key Differences

Aspect FAANG Outsourcing (KMS, FPT, TMA)
Coding LeetCode Medium/Hard Practical validation, CRUD, real scenarios
System Design 1M+ QPS theoretical Practical architecture for real projects
Behavioral Leadership Principles Client communication, timezone management
Language English only Vietnamese HR + English technical
Focus Algorithm mastery Spring Boot production skills
Salary talk "What's your expectation?" Direct range discussion (Vietnamese style)
Unique question "Design Twitter" "Client reports page is slow — fix it"

💡 Top Tips for Outsourcing Interviews

  1. Show client communication skills. "Proceed unless you disagree" > waiting for approval
  2. Emphasize practical experience. Real projects > theoretical knowledge
  3. Mention specific tools. Flyway, Resilience4j, Testcontainers — not just concepts
  4. Healthcare/Finance experience is gold. These are the most common outsourcing domains
  5. Show testing habits. Write tests in the coding exercise without being asked
  6. Salary: know the market. Senior in HCMC: 30-50M gross at top outsourcing companies
  7. Ask about the client. Shows you care about the work, not just the paycheck

🎭 Mock Interview #18 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Employment Hero (HR Tech SaaS, Australia, HCMC office ~400 engineers)

Context: Employment Hero builds HR/payroll/benefits software for SMBs in Australia, NZ, UK, Singapore, and Malaysia. 300K+ businesses use the platform. HCMC is their main engineering hub. They use Java for backend services, Ruby on Rails for legacy, React for frontend. Hiring for the Payroll Engine team — calculates salaries, tax, superannuation, leave balances for 1M+ employees across 5 countries, each with different tax rules.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR + CULTURE FIT (25 min, English)

👩 Amy (People Partner): Hi Khanh! I'm Amy from People & Culture. At Employment Hero we're a product company, not outsourcing — so this round focuses on culture fit and growth mindset. We value ownership, transparency, and "heroes" who make others better.

Tell me about yourself and what attracted you to Employment Hero.

👨 Khanh: Hi Amy! I'm Khanh, nearly 5 years in Java backend. Currently Senior Engineer at FPT/Sony Global.

What attracted me: two things. First, Employment Hero is a product company — I want to own features end-to-end and see the impact on real users. In outsourcing, I build what the client specifies. At Employment Hero, I'd be part of shaping the product.

Second, the payroll domain. Payroll is deceptively complex — tax rules change annually, each country has different regulations, and accuracy is non-negotiable. Getting someone's pay wrong is personal. That combination of technical complexity and human impact motivates me.

👩 Amy: Love that. What's your working style?

👨 Khanh: I'm async-first — I write things down rather than schedule meetings. I document my decisions, share context proactively, and prefer pull requests with detailed descriptions over quick chats.

But I'm not a hermit — I pair-program when solving hard problems, and I believe code reviews are learning opportunities, not gatekeeping.

👩 Amy: How do you handle feedback?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, my first reaction to critical feedback is defensive — that's human. But I've trained myself to say "thank you, let me think about that" and process it before responding.

Real example: at Axon Active, a colleague told me my design documents were "too long — nobody reads 10 pages." My first thought was "but they need the detail!" After reflecting, he was right. I started writing 2-page summaries with a link to the full document for those who wanted depth. My docs actually got read after that.

👩 Amy: Great self-awareness. Salary expectations?

👨 Khanh: I understand Employment Hero's range for senior engineers in HCMC. I'm looking for something competitive with the product company market here — I know product companies typically offer better than outsourcing rates. I'm happy to discuss specific numbers after the technical round.

👩 Amy: Fair. Technical round is next — with our Engineering Manager and a Senior Engineer from the Payroll team. About 60 minutes.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (60 min, English)

Setting: Same day, 3 PM. Google Meet. EM + Senior Engineer.

👨‍💼 Tom (EM, Melbourne): Hey Khanh! I'm Tom, EM for the Payroll Engine team. I'm in Melbourne. This is Trang, Senior Engineer in HCMC.

👩‍💻 Trang (Sr Eng, HCMC): Hi!

👨‍💼 Tom: Three parts:

  1. Java & Spring deep-dive (15 min)
  2. Design discussion — payroll scenario (25 min)
  3. Coding exercise — practical (20 min)

No LeetCode. We want to see how you'd build real features.

PART A — Java & Spring (15 min)

👩‍💻 Trang: **How do you structure a Spring Boot project? What's your package structure and why?**

👨 Khanh: I use a feature-based package structure, not layer-based: 

// ❌ Layer-based (common but bad at scale)
com.company.app.controllers/
com.company.app.services/
com.company.app.repositories/

// ✅ Feature-based (what I use)
com.company.payroll/
  ├── employee/
  │   ├── EmployeeController.java
  │   ├── EmployeeService.java
  │   ├── EmployeeRepository.java
  │   ├── Employee.java (entity)
  │   └── EmployeeDTO.java
  ├── tax/
  │   ├── TaxCalculationService.java
  │   ├── TaxRuleRepository.java
  │   └── TaxRule.java
  ├── leave/
  │   ├── LeaveController.java
  │   ├── LeaveService.java
  │   └── ...
  └── shared/
      ├── config/
      ├── exception/
      └── security/

Why feature-based:

  • When I work on "tax calculation," all related files are in one folder
  • Each feature package could become a microservice later — natural boundaries
  • New team members find code faster — "where's the leave logic?" → leave/
  • Layer-based forces you to jump between 5 folders for one feature

👩‍💻 Trang: **How do you write a REST API with proper validation, pagination, and error handling? Show me the full stack.**

👨 Khanh: Here's how I'd build an employee search endpoint:

Controller:

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/v1/employees")
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class EmployeeController {

    private final EmployeeService employeeService;

    @GetMapping
    public Page<EmployeeDTO> searchEmployees(
            @RequestParam(required = false) String name,
            @RequestParam(required = false) String department,
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "0") int page,
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "20") int size,
            @RequestParam(defaultValue = "name,asc") String sort) {

        Pageable pageable = PageRequest.of(page, size, Sort.by(
            sort.endsWith("desc") ? Sort.Direction.DESC : Sort.Direction.ASC,
            sort.split(",")[0]
        ));

        return employeeService.search(name, department, pageable);
    }

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<EmployeeDTO> createEmployee(
            @Valid @RequestBody CreateEmployeeRequest request) {
        EmployeeDTO created = employeeService.create(request);
        return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED).body(created);
    }
}

Request DTO with validation:

public record CreateEmployeeRequest(
    @NotBlank(message = "Name is required")
    String name,

    @Email(message = "Invalid email format")
    @NotBlank(message = "Email is required")
    String email,

    @NotNull(message = "Start date is required")
    @PastOrPresent(message = "Start date cannot be in the future")
    LocalDate startDate,

    @Positive(message = "Salary must be positive")
    BigDecimal annualSalary
) {}

Service with Specification for dynamic filtering:

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class EmployeeService {

    private final EmployeeRepository repo;

    public Page<EmployeeDTO> search(String name, String dept, Pageable pageable) {
        Specification<Employee> spec = Specification.where(null);
        if (name != null) spec = spec.and(nameLike(name));
        if (dept != null) spec = spec.and(departmentEquals(dept));

        return repo.findAll(spec, pageable).map(this::toDTO);
    }

    private Specification<Employee> nameLike(String name) {
        return (root, query, cb) ->
            cb.like(cb.lower(root.get("name")), "%" + name.toLowerCase() + "%");
    }
}

Key decisions:

  • Page<EmployeeDTO> returns total count, page info — frontend needs this
  • Specification pattern for dynamic filters — much cleaner than if-else chains
  • record for DTOs (Java 16+) — immutable, less boilerplate
  • Bean Validation annotations — declarative, no manual if-checks

👨‍💼 Tom: Clean. I like the Specification pattern — we actually use that in our codebase. Good match.

👨‍💼 Tom: **Quick one: explain Optional and how you use it in service layer vs controller layer.**

👨 Khanh: Optional signals "this value might not exist."

Repository layer: Spring Data returns Optional automatically:

Optional<Employee> findByEmail(String email);

Service layer: I handle the Optional here — not in the controller:

public EmployeeDTO getByEmail(String email) {
    return repo.findByEmail(email)
        .map(this::toDTO)
        .orElseThrow(() -> new ResourceNotFoundException(
            "Employee not found with email: " + email
        ));
}

Controller layer: Never receives Optional. It gets either a DTO or an exception (which @ControllerAdvice handles).

Rules I follow:

  • ✅ Return Optional from repository/query methods
  • ✅ Use map(), orElseThrow(), orElse() — functional style
  • ❌ Never use Optional as method parameter
  • ❌ Never use Optional as entity field
  • ❌ Never call .get() without checking — defeats the purpose

PART B — Design Discussion: Payroll Scenario (25 min)

👨‍💼 Tom: Here's a real problem we face. I want to hear how you'd approach it.

*"Employment Hero calculates payroll for 5 countries: Australia, NZ, UK, Singapore, Malaysia. Each country has completely different tax rules. Australia has tax brackets + Medicare levy + HECS debt. UK has PAYE + National Insurance. How would you design the tax calculation engine to support all countries without a mess of if-else statements?"*

👨 Khanh: This is a classic case for the Strategy Pattern combined with a factory.

Design:

// Strategy interface — one per country
public interface TaxCalculator {
    TaxResult calculate(EmployeeTaxInput input);
    String getCountryCode();
}

// Australia implementation
@Component
public class AustraliaTaxCalculator implements TaxCalculator {

    @Override
    public String getCountryCode() { return "AU"; }

    @Override
    public TaxResult calculate(EmployeeTaxInput input) {
        BigDecimal gross = input.getGrossPay();
        BigDecimal incomeTax = calculateBrackets(gross, AU_BRACKETS);
        BigDecimal medicarelevy = gross.multiply(new BigDecimal("0.02"));
        BigDecimal superannuation = gross.multiply(new BigDecimal("0.115")); // 11.5%
        BigDecimal hecs = input.hasHecsDebt() ? calculateHecs(gross) : ZERO;

        return TaxResult.builder()
            .incomeTax(incomeTax)
            .medicareLevy(medicarelevy)
            .superannuation(superannuation)
            .hecsRepayment(hecs)
            .netPay(gross.subtract(incomeTax).subtract(medicarelevy).subtract(hecs))
            .build();
    }

    private BigDecimal calculateBrackets(BigDecimal annual, List<TaxBracket> brackets) {
        BigDecimal totalTax = ZERO;
        BigDecimal remaining = annual;
        for (TaxBracket bracket : brackets) {
            BigDecimal taxable = remaining.min(bracket.width());
            totalTax = totalTax.add(taxable.multiply(bracket.rate()));
            remaining = remaining.subtract(taxable);
            if (remaining.compareTo(ZERO) <= 0) break;
        }
        return totalTax;
    }
}

// UK implementation
@Component
public class UKTaxCalculator implements TaxCalculator {
    @Override
    public String getCountryCode() { return "UK"; }

    @Override
    public TaxResult calculate(EmployeeTaxInput input) {
        // PAYE brackets, National Insurance, student loan
        // Completely different logic — but same interface
    }
}

// Factory — auto-discovers all implementations
@Component
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class TaxCalculatorFactory {

    private final Map<String, TaxCalculator> calculators;

    // Spring auto-injects all TaxCalculator beans into a map!
    public TaxCalculatorFactory(List<TaxCalculator> calculatorList) {
        this.calculators = calculatorList.stream()
            .collect(Collectors.toMap(TaxCalculator::getCountryCode, c -> c));
    }

    public TaxCalculator getCalculator(String countryCode) {
        TaxCalculator calc = calculators.get(countryCode);
        if (calc == null) {
            throw new UnsupportedCountryException(
                "Tax calculation not supported for: " + countryCode);
        }
        return calc;
    }
}

// Usage in payroll service — clean, no if-else
@Service
public class PayrollService {
    private final TaxCalculatorFactory taxFactory;

    public PaySlip generatePaySlip(Employee employee, PayPeriod period) {
        TaxCalculator calculator = taxFactory.getCalculator(employee.getCountry());
        TaxResult tax = calculator.calculate(buildInput(employee, period));
        return new PaySlip(employee, period, tax);
    }
}

Why this design works:

  1. Adding a new country = add one new class. No existing code changes.

Open/Closed Principle in practice.

  1. Tax rules are testable independently.

```java @Test void australiaTax_bracket2() { var calc = new AustraliaTaxCalculator(); var input = EmployeeTaxInput.of(grossPay(80000)); var result = calc.calculate(input); assertThat(result.getIncomeTax()).isEqualByComparingTo("16467"); } ```

  1. Tax rules change annually. Each country's calculator can

have versioned rules (2024 brackets vs 2025 brackets) loaded from a database or config file, not hardcoded.

For the tax brackets data:

// Load from DB — updated when tax laws change, no code deploy needed
@Entity
@Table(name = "tax_brackets")
public class TaxBracket {
    private String countryCode;
    private int fiscalYear;
    private BigDecimal lowerBound;
    private BigDecimal upperBound;
    private BigDecimal rate;
}

👨‍💼 Tom: This is almost exactly how our tax engine works. The Strategy + Factory + database-driven brackets is the right architecture. How would you handle a payroll run for 50,000 employees in one company? Performance-wise.

👨 Khanh: Payroll runs are batch operations — naturally parallelizable because each employee's calculation is independent.

@Async
public CompletableFuture<PaySlip> generatePaySlipAsync(Employee emp, PayPeriod period) {
    return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(generatePaySlip(emp, period));
}

// Or use parallel stream for batch
public List<PaySlip> runPayroll(List<Employee> employees, PayPeriod period) {
    return employees.parallelStream()
        .map(emp -> generatePaySlip(emp, period))
        .toList();
}

For 50K employees:

  • Partition into batches of 1000
  • Process batches with a thread pool (sized to DB connection pool)
  • Use @Transactional per batch, not per employee (reduce DB round trips)
  • Bulk insert pay slips using saveAll() with batch sizing:

```yaml spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size: 50 ```

Expected time: 50K employees ÷ 1000 batch × ~2s per batch = ~100 seconds. Acceptable for a scheduled overnight job.

👨‍💼 Tom: Practical and correct. Last part.

PART C — Coding Exercise (20 min)

👩‍💻 Trang: Practical coding. No algorithms.

*"Write a leave balance calculator. An employee has 20 annual leave days per year. They accrue leave monthly (20/12 ≈ 1.67 days/month). Given their start date and a list of leave requests (approved), calculate their current available leave balance."*

Employee started: 2024-01-15
Today: 2024-07-15 (6 months)
Accrued: 6 × 1.6667 = 10 days
Taken: 3 days (one leave request: Mar 4-6)
Balance: 10 - 3 = 7 days

👨 Khanh:

public class LeaveCalculator {

    private static final BigDecimal ANNUAL_LEAVE_DAYS = new BigDecimal("20");
    private static final BigDecimal MONTHS_PER_YEAR = new BigDecimal("12");

    public BigDecimal calculateBalance(LocalDate startDate,
                                        LocalDate asOfDate,
                                        List<LeaveRequest> approvedLeaves) {
        // 1. Calculate months of service
        long monthsWorked = ChronoUnit.MONTHS.between(startDate, asOfDate);
        if (monthsWorked < 0) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Start date is in the future");
        }

        // 2. Calculate accrued leave
        BigDecimal accrued = ANNUAL_LEAVE_DAYS
            .divide(MONTHS_PER_YEAR, 4, RoundingMode.HALF_UP)
            .multiply(BigDecimal.valueOf(monthsWorked));

        // Cap at annual maximum (can't accrue more than 20 in year 1)
        accrued = accrued.min(ANNUAL_LEAVE_DAYS);

        // 3. Calculate total leave taken
        BigDecimal taken = BigDecimal.ZERO;
        for (LeaveRequest leave : approvedLeaves) {
            // Only count leave taken before asOfDate
            if (!leave.getStartDate().isAfter(asOfDate)) {
                long days = ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(
                    leave.getStartDate(), leave.getEndDate()) + 1; // inclusive
                taken = taken.add(BigDecimal.valueOf(days));
            }
        }

        // 4. Balance = accrued - taken
        BigDecimal balance = accrued.subtract(taken);

        return balance.setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);
    }
}

Test:

@Test
void shouldCalculateCorrectBalance() {
    LocalDate start = LocalDate.of(2024, 1, 15);
    LocalDate today = LocalDate.of(2024, 7, 15);
    List<LeaveRequest> leaves = List.of(
        new LeaveRequest(LocalDate.of(2024, 3, 4), LocalDate.of(2024, 3, 6)) // 3 days
    );

    BigDecimal balance = calculator.calculateBalance(start, today, leaves);

    // 6 months × 1.6667 = 10.00, minus 3 taken = 7.00
    assertThat(balance).isEqualByComparingTo("7.00");
}

@Test
void shouldHandleNewEmployee() {
    LocalDate start = LocalDate.of(2024, 7, 1);
    LocalDate today = LocalDate.of(2024, 7, 15);

    BigDecimal balance = calculator.calculateBalance(start, today, List.of());

    // 0 full months worked = 0 accrued
    assertThat(balance).isEqualByComparingTo("0.00");
}

Edge cases handled:

  • New employee (0 months) → 0 balance
  • Leave taken in the future (after asOfDate) → not counted
  • Multiple leave requests → summed correctly
  • Fractional days → rounded to 2 decimal places with BigDecimal

👩‍💻 Trang: Clean, readable, handles edge cases. I'd ship this.

👨‍💼 Tom: Great session. You'd fit well on the team. We'll be in touch within a week.

📊 SCORECARD

Category Score Notes
Culture fit 10/10 Async-first, feedback self-awareness, product motivation
Project structure 10/10 Feature-based packages with clear reasoning
REST API full stack 10/10 Validation, pagination, Specification pattern, DTOs
Optional usage 10/10 Clear rules: where to use, where not to
Tax engine design 10/10 Strategy + Factory + DB-driven brackets
Batch payroll 10/10 Parallel + bulk insert + batch sizing
Leave calculator 10/10 BigDecimal, edge cases, tests included
Communication 10/10 Clear English, structured answers

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Employment Hero / Product Company Interview Tips

  1. Show product thinking. "How does this affect the end user?" not just "how to build it"
  2. Culture fit matters more than FAANG. They ask about feedback, collaboration, ownership
  3. Domain knowledge is a plus. Payroll, HR, tax — show you can learn complex domains
  4. Clean code > clever code. They review your coding style, not algorithm speed
  5. Feature-based packaging is what modern product teams use — mention it
  6. BigDecimal for money — always. They deal with salaries, never use double

🎭 Mock Interview #19 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: NAB Vietnam (National Australia Bank, Tech Hub HCMC, 1500+ engineers)

Context: NAB is one of Australia's Big 4 banks. Their HCMC tech hub builds core banking systems — payments, lending, customer onboarding. They hire senior Java engineers at competitive rates (often higher than outsourcing). Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, PostgreSQL, AWS. Why relevant for Khanh: Your Axon Active banking + AWS experience is a perfect match. NAB pays well and has strong career growth.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Lisa (HR): Hi Khanh! NAB is a product bank — you'd be building the bank's own systems, not outsourcing. The team is Customer Onboarding — KYC, identity verification, account opening. Tell me about yourself.

👨 Khanh: Hi Lisa! I'm a Senior Java Engineer with nearly 5 years experience. Most relevantly, I spent over a year at Axon Active building Valiant — a banking platform. I understand financial domain concepts: KYC, transaction processing, regulatory compliance, and the engineering discipline required for systems that handle money.

I'm attracted to NAB because it's a real bank — not outsourcing for a bank, but building the actual banking infrastructure. The scale and responsibility are what I'm looking for.

👩 Lisa: Why leave FPT?

👨 Khanh: At FPT I'm building for Sony — great engineering but it's indirect impact. At NAB, the code I write directly serves millions of bank customers. Also, NAB's tech hub has a clear career ladder for engineers, which is important to me.

👩 Lisa: Technical round is 75 minutes with two engineers.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (75 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer + Senior Engineer.

👨‍💻 Phong (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. Three parts: Java/Spring (20 min), banking design scenario (30 min), coding (25 min). We focus on production quality and banking domain awareness.

PART A — Java & Spring (20 min)

👨‍💻 Phong: **You're building a REST API for account opening. The API must validate the request, check for duplicate accounts, create the account, and send a welcome notification. Show me how you'd structure this with proper transaction management.**

👨 Khanh:

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/v1/accounts")
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class AccountController {
    private final AccountService accountService;

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<AccountResponse> openAccount(
            @Valid @RequestBody OpenAccountRequest request) {
        AccountResponse response = accountService.openAccount(request);
        return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.CREATED).body(response);
    }
}

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class AccountService {
    private final AccountRepository accountRepo;
    private final KycService kycService;
    private final ApplicationEventPublisher eventPublisher;

    @Transactional
    public AccountResponse openAccount(OpenAccountRequest request) {
        // 1. Check duplicate
        if (accountRepo.existsByEmail(request.email())) {
            throw new DuplicateAccountException("Account with this email already exists");
        }

        // 2. KYC verification
        KycResult kyc = kycService.verify(request.toKycInput());
        if (!kyc.isApproved()) {
            throw new KycFailedException("Identity verification failed: " + kyc.getReason());
        }

        // 3. Create account
        Account account = Account.builder()
            .email(request.email())
            .fullName(request.fullName())
            .status(AccountStatus.ACTIVE)
            .kycStatus(KycStatus.VERIFIED)
            .build();

        account = accountRepo.save(account);

        // 4. Publish event (notification sent AFTER transaction commits)
        eventPublisher.publishEvent(new AccountOpenedEvent(account.getId()));

        return AccountResponse.from(account);
    }
}

// Event listener — runs AFTER the transaction commits successfully
@Component
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class AccountEventListener {
    private final NotificationService notificationService;

    @TransactionalEventListener(phase = TransactionPhase.AFTER_COMMIT)
    public void onAccountOpened(AccountOpenedEvent event) {
        notificationService.sendWelcomeEmail(event.getAccountId());
    }
}

Key decisions:

  1. @TransactionalEventListener(AFTER_COMMIT) — the notification

is sent only AFTER the account is successfully created. If the transaction rolls back, no email is sent. This prevents sending "Welcome!" to a customer whose account creation actually failed.

  1. KYC check inside the transaction. If KYC fails, nothing is saved.

Clean rollback.

  1. existsByEmail before save. Application-level check is fast,

but I'd also add a UNIQUE constraint on the email column as defense in depth — handles race conditions between two concurrent requests.

  1. No notification logic in the service. Event-driven decoupling.

The service doesn't know or care about emails. Other listeners could react to the same event (analytics, audit, etc.).

👩‍💻 Hà (Sr Eng): **In banking, audit logging is critical. How do you implement automatic audit logging for all entity changes?**

👨 Khanh: Two approaches I've used:

Approach 1: Hibernate Envers (what I used at Axon Active):

@Entity
@Audited  // Hibernate Envers — auto-creates audit table
public class Account {
    @Id
    private Long id;
    private String email;
    private AccountStatus status;
    // Every change is automatically tracked in account_aud table
}

Envers creates account_aud table with every version of the record, who changed it, and when. Query audit history:

AuditReader reader = AuditReaderFactory.get(entityManager);
List<Number> revisions = reader.getRevisions(Account.class, accountId);
Account oldVersion = reader.find(Account.class, accountId, revisions.get(0));

Approach 2: Spring Data JPA Auditing (lighter — just who/when):

@MappedSuperclass
@EntityListeners(AuditingEntityListener.class)
public abstract class BaseEntity {

    @CreatedBy
    private String createdBy;

    @CreatedDate
    private LocalDateTime createdAt;

    @LastModifiedBy
    private String updatedBy;

    @LastModifiedDate
    private LocalDateTime updatedAt;
}

Combined with an AuditorAware bean that reads the current user from SecurityContext.

For NAB's requirements, I'd use Envers for financially sensitive entities (accounts, transactions) where we need full change history, and Spring Data Auditing for everything else (less overhead).

PART B — Banking Design Scenario (30 min)

👨‍💻 Phong: *"Design the customer onboarding flow. A new customer wants to open a bank account. Steps: collect personal info, verify identity (KYC), credit check, create account, issue debit card. Some steps take seconds, others take minutes (external API calls). How do you design this?"*

👨 Khanh: This is a long-running workflow — some steps are instant, some are async, and the whole flow might take 2-30 minutes.

State Machine approach:

STARTED → PERSONAL_INFO_COLLECTED → KYC_IN_PROGRESS → KYC_VERIFIED
    → CREDIT_CHECK_IN_PROGRESS → CREDIT_CHECK_PASSED
    → ACCOUNT_CREATED → CARD_REQUESTED → COMPLETED

Failure states:
    → KYC_FAILED → REJECTED
    → CREDIT_CHECK_FAILED → REJECTED

Architecture:

Frontend → Onboarding API
              │
              ▼
        ┌──────────────┐
        │  Onboarding  │ (Spring State Machine or custom)
        │  Orchestrator │
        └──────┬───────┘
               │
     ┌─────────┼─────────┬──────────┐
     ▼         ▼         ▼          ▼
  KYC API   Credit    Account    Card
 (external) Check API  Service   Service
  (async)   (async)   (internal) (async)

Implementation:

@Entity
public class OnboardingApplication {
    @Id
    private UUID id;
    private Long customerId;

    @Enumerated(EnumType.STRING)
    private OnboardingStatus status;

    private String kycReference;    // external provider reference
    private String creditReference;
    private LocalDateTime startedAt;
    private LocalDateTime completedAt;

    @Version
    private int version; // optimistic locking
}

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class OnboardingService {
    private final OnboardingRepository repo;
    private final KycClient kycClient;
    private final CreditCheckClient creditClient;
    private final AccountService accountService;

    @Transactional
    public OnboardingApplication startOnboarding(CustomerInfo info) {
        OnboardingApplication app = OnboardingApplication.builder()
            .id(UUID.randomUUID())
            .status(PERSONAL_INFO_COLLECTED)
            .startedAt(LocalDateTime.now())
            .build();
        repo.save(app);

        // Initiate KYC async
        String kycRef = kycClient.submitVerification(info);
        app.setKycReference(kycRef);
        app.setStatus(KYC_IN_PROGRESS);
        repo.save(app);

        return app;
    }

    // Called via webhook when KYC provider responds
    @Transactional
    public void handleKycResult(String kycRef, KycResult result) {
        OnboardingApplication app = repo.findByKycReference(kycRef)
            .orElseThrow();

        if (result.isApproved()) {
            app.setStatus(KYC_VERIFIED);
            // Trigger credit check
            String creditRef = creditClient.submitCheck(app.getCustomerId());
            app.setCreditReference(creditRef);
            app.setStatus(CREDIT_CHECK_IN_PROGRESS);
        } else {
            app.setStatus(REJECTED);
        }
        repo.save(app);
    }

    // Called via webhook when credit check responds
    @Transactional
    public void handleCreditResult(String creditRef, CreditResult result) {
        OnboardingApplication app = repo.findByCreditReference(creditRef)
            .orElseThrow();

        if (result.isPassed()) {
            app.setStatus(CREDIT_CHECK_PASSED);
            // Create account synchronously (internal service)
            Account account = accountService.create(app.getCustomerId());
            app.setStatus(ACCOUNT_CREATED);
            // Request card async
            cardService.requestCard(account.getId());
            app.setStatus(CARD_REQUESTED);
            app.setCompletedAt(LocalDateTime.now());
            app.setStatus(COMPLETED);
        } else {
            app.setStatus(REJECTED);
        }
        repo.save(app);
    }
}

Frontend status polling:

@GetMapping("/onboarding/{id}/status")
public OnboardingStatusResponse getStatus(@PathVariable UUID id) {
    OnboardingApplication app = repo.findById(id).orElseThrow();
    return new OnboardingStatusResponse(
        app.getStatus(),
        getProgressPercentage(app.getStatus()),
        getNextStepMessage(app.getStatus())
    );
}

The customer sees: "Verifying your identity... (40%)" → "Checking credit... (60%)" → "Creating your account... (80%)" → "Welcome! ✅"

Key design decisions:

  1. Every state transition is persisted. If the server crashes

mid-flow, we resume from the last saved state.

  1. External calls are async with webhooks. KYC might take 5 minutes.

We don't hold HTTP connections or threads — we save the reference ID and wait for the callback.

  1. Optimistic locking prevents concurrent webhook processing

from corrupting the application state.

  1. Timeout handling: A scheduled job checks for applications

stuck in *_IN_PROGRESS for more than 30 minutes and marks them for manual review.

👨‍💻 Phong: Solid. This is close to how our real onboarding works. The webhook + state persistence pattern is exactly right for long-running banking workflows.

PART C — Coding Exercise (25 min)

👩‍💻 Hà: Practical banking problem:

*"Implement a transaction history service. Given a list of account transactions, write a method that returns a monthly statement: group transactions by month, calculate the opening balance, closing balance, total credits, and total debits for each month."*

👨 Khanh:

public class StatementService {

    public List<MonthlyStatement> generateStatements(
            BigDecimal initialBalance,
            List<Transaction> transactions) {

        // Sort by date
        List<Transaction> sorted = transactions.stream()
            .sorted(Comparator.comparing(Transaction::getDate))
            .toList();

        // Group by year-month
        Map<YearMonth, List<Transaction>> grouped = sorted.stream()
            .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(
                t -> YearMonth.from(t.getDate()),
                LinkedHashMap::new,  // preserve insertion order
                Collectors.toList()
            ));

        List<MonthlyStatement> statements = new ArrayList<>();
        BigDecimal runningBalance = initialBalance;

        for (var entry : grouped.entrySet()) {
            YearMonth month = entry.getKey();
            List<Transaction> monthTxns = entry.getValue();

            BigDecimal openingBalance = runningBalance;

            BigDecimal totalCredits = monthTxns.stream()
                .filter(t -> t.getType() == TransactionType.CREDIT)
                .map(Transaction::getAmount)
                .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);

            BigDecimal totalDebits = monthTxns.stream()
                .filter(t -> t.getType() == TransactionType.DEBIT)
                .map(Transaction::getAmount)
                .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);

            BigDecimal closingBalance = openingBalance.add(totalCredits).subtract(totalDebits);

            statements.add(new MonthlyStatement(
                month, openingBalance, closingBalance,
                totalCredits, totalDebits, monthTxns.size()
            ));

            runningBalance = closingBalance; // next month's opening
        }

        return statements;
    }
}

// Test
@Test
void shouldGenerateMonthlyStatements() {
    BigDecimal initial = new BigDecimal("1000.00");
    List<Transaction> txns = List.of(
        new Transaction(LocalDate.of(2024, 1, 5), CREDIT, new BigDecimal("500")),
        new Transaction(LocalDate.of(2024, 1, 15), DEBIT, new BigDecimal("200")),
        new Transaction(LocalDate.of(2024, 2, 3), CREDIT, new BigDecimal("1000")),
        new Transaction(LocalDate.of(2024, 2, 20), DEBIT, new BigDecimal("300"))
    );

    List<MonthlyStatement> result = service.generateStatements(initial, txns);

    // January: open=1000, credits=500, debits=200, close=1300
    assertThat(result.get(0).getOpeningBalance()).isEqualByComparingTo("1000");
    assertThat(result.get(0).getClosingBalance()).isEqualByComparingTo("1300");

    // February: open=1300, credits=1000, debits=300, close=2000
    assertThat(result.get(1).getOpeningBalance()).isEqualByComparingTo("1300");
    assertThat(result.get(1).getClosingBalance()).isEqualByComparingTo("2000");
}

👨‍💻 Phong: Clean code. LinkedHashMap for preserving month order is a nice detail. BigDecimal throughout — correct for banking.

📊 SCORECARD

Category Score Notes
Account opening flow 10/10 TransactionalEventListener, UNIQUE constraint, event-driven
Audit logging 10/10 Envers for financial, JPA Auditing for general
Onboarding design 10/10 State machine, webhook, timeout handling
Statement coding 10/10 BigDecimal, running balance, ordered grouping, tested
Banking domain 10/10 KYC, credit check, compliance awareness

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 NAB / Banking Tech Hub Interview Tips

  1. Banking domain knowledge wins. Mention KYC, AML, ACID, audit trails
  2. Every entity change must be auditable. Mention Envers or CDC
  3. Long-running workflows are common. State machine > synchronous API
  4. Always use BigDecimal. They WILL check
  5. TransactionalEventListener — show you understand commit boundaries
  6. NAB pays well. Senior in HCMC: 40-60M+ gross. Know your worth

🎭 Mock Interview #20 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: NashTech Vietnam (UK-based, 1500+ engineers in HCMC/Hanoi)

Context: NashTech builds software for UK/EU clients. Current opening is for a UK retail client — building an order management system (OMS) for a fashion e-commerce company. Team: 6 devs in Vietnam + Product Owner and QA in London. Agile/Scrum, 2-week sprints. Interview format: NashTech interviews are practical and Agile-focused. They test: can you build production features? Can you work independently with a UK client? Do you understand Scrum ceremonies?

📞 ROUND 1 — HR (15 min, Vietnamese + English check)

👩 Thảo (HR): Chào anh Khanh! Mình sẽ nói tiếng Việt trước, sau đó em check English nha.

Anh giới thiệu ngắn gọn kinh nghiệm đi ạ.

👨 Khanh: Anh có gần 5 năm Java Backend. Hiện tại ở FPT/Sony Global làm microservices trên AWS. Trước đó anh ở Axon Active làm banking platform — cũng là model tương tự NashTech, team Việt Nam làm việc với client Thụy Sĩ. Và TMA Solutions làm healthcare cho client Mỹ.

👩 Thảo: OK, giờ mình switch sang English nha. Can you tell me about your experience working with foreign clients?

👨 Khanh: Sure. I have direct experience with three foreign clients. At TMA, I worked with Humana — a US healthcare company. Daily standups at 9 PM Vietnam time. At Axon Active, I worked with Valiant Bank in Switzerland. And currently at Sony, I communicate with Japanese stakeholders.

Each client has a different communication style. American clients are direct — they tell you exactly what they want. Swiss clients are precise — they expect detailed documentation. Japanese clients value consensus — decisions take longer but are more stable.

I'm comfortable adapting to any client communication style.

👩 Thảo: Perfect English. Round 2 is technical, 60 minutes.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (60 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer + Technical Architect from NashTech.

👨‍💻 Duy (Sr Eng): Hi Khanh! I'm Duy, I lead the OMS project. And this is Tuấn, our Technical Architect.

👨‍💻 Tuấn (Architect): Hey!

👨‍💻 Duy: Four parts: microservices (15 min), Docker/K8s (10 min), design scenario (20 min), coding (15 min). Practical questions only.

PART A — Microservices Practical (15 min)

👨‍💻 Duy: **You're building an order management system with microservices. How do you decide the service boundaries? What services would you create?**

👨 Khanh: I use Domain-Driven Design to find boundaries. For an e-commerce OMS, I'd identify these bounded contexts:

1. Product Catalog Service — products, categories, pricing
2. Inventory Service — stock levels, warehouse locations
3. Order Service — order lifecycle (created → paid → shipped → delivered)
4. Payment Service — payment processing, refunds
5. Shipping Service — carrier integration, tracking
6. Customer Service — profiles, addresses, preferences
7. Notification Service — email, SMS, push notifications

How I decide boundaries: Each service owns its data and can be deployed independently. The test: "Can this service function if other services are temporarily down?" If yes, good boundary.

For example, Order Service can accept an order even if Notification Service is down — notifications can be sent later. But Order Service needs Inventory Service to check stock — so that's a synchronous dependency I'd handle with a circuit breaker.

Inter-service communication:

  • Synchronous (REST/gRPC): Order → Inventory (stock check).

Use when the caller NEEDS the response to continue.

  • Asynchronous (Kafka events): Order → Notification ("order

confirmed" event). Use when the caller doesn't need to wait.

Order placed → sync call to Inventory (reserve stock)
             → sync call to Payment (charge customer)
             → async event to Notification (send confirmation email)
             → async event to Shipping (prepare shipment)

👨‍💻 Tuấn: **How do you handle the situation where Order Service calls Payment Service and it's slow? The customer is waiting.**

👨 Khanh: Three layers of protection:

1. Timeout. Set a hard timeout on the HTTP client:

@Bean
public RestClient paymentClient() {
    return RestClient.builder()
        .baseUrl("http://payment-service")
        .requestFactory(new HttpComponentsClientHttpRequestFactory(
            HttpClientBuilder.create()
                .setConnectionManager(PoolingHttpClientConnectionManagerBuilder.create()
                    .setMaxConnTotal(50)
                    .setMaxConnPerRoute(20)
                    .build())
                .build()))
        .defaultHeaders(h -> h.setContentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON))
        .build();
}
// With timeout configured via properties:
// spring.web.client.connect-timeout=3000
// spring.web.client.read-timeout=5000

2. Circuit Breaker (Resilience4j):

@CircuitBreaker(name = "payment", fallbackMethod = "paymentFallback")
public PaymentResult processPayment(PaymentRequest req) {
    return paymentClient.post()
        .uri("/payments")
        .body(req)
        .retrieve()
        .body(PaymentResult.class);
}

private PaymentResult paymentFallback(PaymentRequest req, Exception ex) {
    log.warn("Payment service unavailable, queueing for retry", ex);
    paymentRetryQueue.send(req);
    return PaymentResult.pending("Payment will be processed shortly");
}

3. User experience. Return "processing" status immediately, show the customer a "payment processing" screen, and update them via WebSocket or email when payment completes. Don't make them stare at a loading spinner for 30 seconds.

PART B — Docker & Kubernetes (10 min)

👨‍💻 Tuấn: **Show me a basic Dockerfile for a Spring Boot application. What best practices do you follow?**

👨 Khanh:

# Multi-stage build — smaller final image
FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jdk AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY pom.xml .
COPY src ./src
# Cache Maven dependencies (re-downloaded only when pom.xml changes)
RUN mvn dependency:go-offline -B
RUN mvn package -DskipTests -B

# Runtime stage — JRE only, no JDK, no source code
FROM eclipse-temurin:17-jre-alpine
WORKDIR /app

# Non-root user for security
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup
USER appuser

COPY --from=build /app/target/*.jar app.jar

EXPOSE 8080

# JVM tuning for containers
ENTRYPOINT ["java", \
  "-XX:+UseContainerSupport", \
  "-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0", \
  "-jar", "app.jar"]

Best practices I follow:

Practice Why
Multi-stage build Final image has no JDK, Maven, source code — 300MB vs 800MB
eclipse-temurin base Official, well-maintained, security patched
Alpine variant for runtime Smaller image (~100MB smaller)
Non-root user Security — container doesn't run as root
UseContainerSupport JVM respects container memory limits (not host)
MaxRAMPercentage=75 Leave 25% for non-heap (threads, native memory)
Cache Maven deps Docker layer caching — only re-download when pom.xml changes

👨‍💻 Tuấn: **You deploy on Kubernetes. How do you configure health checks?**

👨 Khanh: Spring Boot Actuator + K8s probes:

# application.yml
management:
  endpoints:
    web.exposure.include: health,info,prometheus
  health:
    show-details: always
  endpoint:
    health:
      probes:
        enabled: true

# K8s deployment.yml
spec:
  containers:
    - name: order-service
      image: order-service:1.0
      ports:
        - containerPort: 8080
      livenessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /actuator/health/liveness
          port: 8080
        initialDelaySeconds: 30   # wait for app startup
        periodSeconds: 10
        failureThreshold: 3       # restart after 3 failures
      readinessProbe:
        httpGet:
          path: /actuator/health/readiness
          port: 8080
        initialDelaySeconds: 10
        periodSeconds: 5
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: "512Mi"
          cpu: "250m"
        limits:
          memory: "1Gi"
          cpu: "500m"

The difference:

  • Liveness: "Is the app alive?" If NO → K8s restarts the pod.

Should check: JVM is running, not deadlocked.

  • Readiness: "Can the app serve traffic?" If NO → K8s removes

from load balancer. Should check: DB connection OK, cache connected.

Common mistake: Making liveness probe depend on external services (like DB). If DB is down, all pods restart — makes things worse. Liveness should only check the app itself.

PART C — Design Scenario: Order Management (20 min)

👨‍💻 Duy: *"Design the order lifecycle for our fashion e-commerce client. An order goes through: Created → Paid → Processing → Shipped → Delivered. Also support: Cancelled and Refunded states. How do you model this?"*

👨 Khanh: State machine with strict transition rules:

                    ┌─────────────┐
                    │   CREATED   │
                    └──────┬──────┘
                           │ payment received
                    ┌──────▼──────┐        ┌───────────┐
                    │    PAID     │───────►│ CANCELLED │
                    └──────┬──────┘  cancel └───────────┘
                           │ warehouse picks items
                    ┌──────▼──────┐
                    │ PROCESSING  │
                    └──────┬──────┘
                           │ shipped with carrier
                    ┌──────▼──────┐
                    │   SHIPPED   │
                    └──────┬──────┘
                           │ delivery confirmed
                    ┌──────▼──────┐        ┌──────────┐
                    │  DELIVERED  │───────►│ REFUNDED │
                    └─────────────┘ refund  └──────────┘

Implementation:

public enum OrderStatus {
    CREATED, PAID, PROCESSING, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED, REFUNDED;

    // Define valid transitions
    private static final Map<OrderStatus, Set<OrderStatus>> TRANSITIONS = Map.of(
        CREATED,    Set.of(PAID, CANCELLED),
        PAID,       Set.of(PROCESSING, CANCELLED),
        PROCESSING, Set.of(SHIPPED),
        SHIPPED,    Set.of(DELIVERED),
        DELIVERED,  Set.of(REFUNDED),
        CANCELLED,  Set.of(),  // terminal state
        REFUNDED,   Set.of()   // terminal state
    );

    public boolean canTransitionTo(OrderStatus next) {
        return TRANSITIONS.getOrDefault(this, Set.of()).contains(next);
    }
}

@Entity
public class Order {
    @Id
    private UUID id;

    @Enumerated(EnumType.STRING)
    private OrderStatus status;

    private BigDecimal totalAmount;
    private String customerId;
    private LocalDateTime createdAt;
    private LocalDateTime updatedAt;

    @OneToMany(cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
    private List<OrderItem> items;

    @OneToMany(cascade = CascadeType.ALL)
    private List<OrderStatusHistory> statusHistory;

    public void transitionTo(OrderStatus newStatus, String reason) {
        if (!this.status.canTransitionTo(newStatus)) {
            throw new InvalidStatusTransitionException(
                "Cannot transition from " + this.status + " to " + newStatus
            );
        }
        this.statusHistory.add(new OrderStatusHistory(
            this.status, newStatus, reason, LocalDateTime.now()
        ));
        this.status = newStatus;
        this.updatedAt = LocalDateTime.now();
    }
}

@Service
public class OrderService {

    @Transactional
    public Order cancelOrder(UUID orderId, String reason) {
        Order order = orderRepo.findById(orderId).orElseThrow();
        order.transitionTo(OrderStatus.CANCELLED, reason);

        if (order.getStatus() == PAID) {
            // Trigger refund if already paid
            paymentService.refund(order.getPaymentId());
            inventoryService.releaseStock(order.getItems());
        }

        eventPublisher.publishEvent(new OrderCancelledEvent(order));
        return orderRepo.save(order);
    }
}

Key decisions:

  1. Transition validation in the enum. Invalid transitions throw

exceptions — impossible to go from CREATED to DELIVERED directly.

  1. Status history. Every transition is recorded with timestamp

and reason. The client can see: "Order was cancelled by customer at 3:15 PM, reason: changed mind."

  1. Side effects on transition. Cancelling a paid order triggers

a refund and stock release. These are handled in the service, not the entity.

  1. Events on state change. Other services react to events:

cancellation → notification to customer, refund to payment, stock release to inventory.

👨‍💻 Duy: Clean design. The transition map in the enum is elegant. How would you handle partial refunds?

👨 Khanh: Add a PARTIALLY_REFUNDED status. The refund() method accepts a list of items to refund (not the whole order). Each refund creates a RefundRecord linked to the order. The order status becomes PARTIALLY_REFUNDED after the first partial refund, and REFUNDED when all items are refunded.

PART D — Coding Exercise (15 min)

👨‍💻 Duy: Quick practical coding:

"Write a discount calculator for the fashion store. Rules:

  • Orders over $100 get 10% off
  • Orders over $200 get 15% off
  • VIP customers get an extra 5% off (applied after tier discount)
  • Maximum total discount is 25%"

👨 Khanh:

public class DiscountCalculator {

    private static final BigDecimal TIER1_THRESHOLD = new BigDecimal("100");
    private static final BigDecimal TIER2_THRESHOLD = new BigDecimal("200");
    private static final BigDecimal TIER1_RATE = new BigDecimal("0.10");
    private static final BigDecimal TIER2_RATE = new BigDecimal("0.15");
    private static final BigDecimal VIP_RATE = new BigDecimal("0.05");
    private static final BigDecimal MAX_DISCOUNT_RATE = new BigDecimal("0.25");

    public DiscountResult calculate(BigDecimal orderTotal, boolean isVip) {
        // Tier discount
        BigDecimal tierRate = BigDecimal.ZERO;
        if (orderTotal.compareTo(TIER2_THRESHOLD) >= 0) {
            tierRate = TIER2_RATE;
        } else if (orderTotal.compareTo(TIER1_THRESHOLD) >= 0) {
            tierRate = TIER1_RATE;
        }

        // VIP discount (additive)
        BigDecimal totalRate = tierRate;
        if (isVip) {
            totalRate = totalRate.add(VIP_RATE);
        }

        // Cap at maximum
        totalRate = totalRate.min(MAX_DISCOUNT_RATE);

        BigDecimal discountAmount = orderTotal.multiply(totalRate)
            .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);
        BigDecimal finalPrice = orderTotal.subtract(discountAmount);

        return new DiscountResult(orderTotal, discountAmount, finalPrice, totalRate);
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void noDiscount_under100() {
    var result = calculator.calculate(new BigDecimal("50"), false);
    assertThat(result.discountAmount()).isEqualByComparingTo("0.00");
    assertThat(result.finalPrice()).isEqualByComparingTo("50.00");
}

@Test
void tier1_over100() {
    var result = calculator.calculate(new BigDecimal("150"), false);
    assertThat(result.discountAmount()).isEqualByComparingTo("15.00"); // 10%
}

@Test
void tier2_over200() {
    var result = calculator.calculate(new BigDecimal("250"), false);
    assertThat(result.discountAmount()).isEqualByComparingTo("37.50"); // 15%
}

@Test
void vip_tier2_capped() {
    var result = calculator.calculate(new BigDecimal("300"), true);
    // 15% + 5% = 20%, under cap of 25%
    assertThat(result.discountAmount()).isEqualByComparingTo("60.00"); // 20%
}

@Test
void vip_tier2_atBoundary() {
    var result = calculator.calculate(new BigDecimal("200"), true);
    // 15% + 5% = 20%
    assertThat(result.finalPrice()).isEqualByComparingTo("160.00");
}

👨‍💻 Duy: Clean, tested, BigDecimal. You'd fit right in.

👨 Khanh: Two questions. First — what's the sprint velocity like? How many story points per sprint?

👨‍💻 Duy: We average 35-40 points per sprint with 6 devs. Stories are 2-5 points typically. We do sprint planning Monday, standup daily at 5:30 PM (9:30 AM London), and retro every other Friday.

👨 Khanh: Second — how do you handle timezone overlap with the UK client?

👨‍💻 Duy: Our overlap is 2-6 PM Vietnam time (7-11 AM London). We keep that window for meetings and real-time communication. All other work is async — Jira comments, Confluence docs, Slack. The PO trusts us to make implementation decisions without checking every detail.

👨 Khanh: That's a healthy setup. Thanks!

📊 SCORECARD

Category Score Notes
English communication 10/10 Clear, professional, adapted to context
Service boundaries 10/10 DDD-driven, sync vs async criteria
Circuit breaker + fallback 10/10 Full Resilience4j with queue-based fallback
Dockerfile 10/10 Multi-stage, non-root, container-aware JVM
K8s health checks 10/10 Liveness vs readiness distinction, common mistakes
Order state machine 10/10 Enum transitions, history tracking, side effects
Discount calculator 10/10 BigDecimal, cap logic, comprehensive tests
Client experience 10/10 3 different foreign clients, adapted style each time

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 NashTech / UK-Client Interview Tips

  1. Agile vocabulary matters. Know: sprint planning, retro, story points, velocity, PO, SM
  2. Timezone management. Show you know how to maximize the overlap window
  3. Docker + K8s is expected. Practical knowledge, not just theory
  4. State machines are common in e-commerce. Order lifecycle is a classic question
  5. Write tests in coding exercises. Shows professionalism
  6. Show client adaptability. Different clients = different communication styles
  7. NashTech salary range. Senior in HCMC: 28-42M gross. Negotiate based on client and domain

🎭 Mock Interview #21 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: EPAM Systems Vietnam (Global IT, 50K+ engineers, HCMC office)

Context: EPAM is a global software engineering company (NASDAQ: EPAM). Unlike typical outsourcing, EPAM positions itself as a "product engineering consultancy" — they embed senior engineers directly into client product teams. Current opening: US FinTech client building an investment portfolio platform. Team: 4 EPAM engineers in Vietnam, working directly with the US client's internal engineering team. What makes EPAM different: They hire only senior engineers. The technical bar is higher than typical outsourcing. They test architectural thinking, Spring ecosystem depth, and code quality. English must be strong. EPAM pays well — senior roles in HCMC often 40-55M+ gross.

📞 ROUND 1 — TECHNICAL SCREENING (45 min, English)

Setting: Google Meet. Senior Technical Recruiter + one engineer. EPAM combines HR and tech screening into one round.

👩 Sarah (Tech Recruiter): Hi Khanh! At EPAM, we skip the traditional HR round — our recruiters are technical. I'll assess both your experience and technical depth in 45 minutes.

Tell me about your most recent architecture decision that had significant production impact.

👨 Khanh: At Sony, I designed the caching strategy for our user profile service — 3K RPS, P99 was 800ms against a 500ms SLA.

The architecture decision: I recommended **cache-aside with short TTL** (5 minutes) instead of write-through. The trade-off: write- through guarantees strong consistency but adds latency on every write. Cache-aside accepts eventual consistency (5-minute staleness window) but keeps writes fast.

For a user profile (which rarely changes), 5 minutes of stale data is acceptable. If a user updates their profile, they might see the old version for up to 5 minutes — that's fine. If they change their bank account, that's a different service with stronger consistency requirements.

The result: P99 dropped from 800ms to 120ms. Cache hit rate was 85%. The decision was driven by the specific data access pattern, not a blanket "always cache everything."

👩 Sarah: Good. Tell me your understanding of the CAP theorem and how it influenced a real design decision.

👨 Khanh: CAP says a distributed system can guarantee at most 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. Since network partitions are inevitable in distributed systems, we effectively choose between CP and AP.

Real decision at Axon Active: the banking ledger needed CP — during a network partition, we'd rather return an error than return potentially stale or incorrect balance data. Money amounts must be correct. We configured our Aurora cluster to refuse reads from potentially stale replicas during partition events.

At Sony, the user profile service was AP — during a partition, we serve stale cached profiles rather than returning errors. A user seeing their old avatar for a minute is far better than the entire service returning 503.

The skill is matching CP vs AP to the specific business requirement — not picking one for the whole system.

👩 Sarah: Strong answer. Last question before I hand to the engineer: rate yourself 1-10 on Spring Boot and explain your rating.

👨 Khanh: 8 out of 10. I'm confident in: core Spring context, AOP, Spring Security, Spring Data JPA, Spring Batch, Spring Cloud (Gateway, Config, Feign). I've tuned production services, handled complex transaction management, and built multi-module projects.

I rate myself 8 not 10 because I haven't built a Spring Framework custom starter from scratch, and my knowledge of Spring's reactive stack (WebFlux, R2DBC) is theoretical — I've read the documentation but haven't built production reactive services. I'd rate myself 7 on reactive Spring specifically.

👩 Sarah: Honest, calibrated self-assessment. Handing over to Hiếu now.

👨‍💻 Hiếu (Sr Eng, EPAM): Hi Khanh! I'm Hiếu, Senior Engineer on the fintech project. Let's go deeper on Spring and design.

Spring Ecosystem Deep-Dive

👨‍💻 Hiếu: **Explain Spring AOP. When do you use it and what are its limitations?**

👨 Khanh: AOP — Aspect-Oriented Programming — lets you apply cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, transactions, metrics) to methods without polluting business code.

How it works in Spring: Spring creates a proxy around your bean. When you call a method, the proxy intercepts it, runs the advice (your cross-cutting logic), then calls the real method.

// The aspect
@Aspect
@Component
public class ExecutionTimeAspect {

    @Around("@annotation(Timed)")
    public Object measureTime(ProceedingJoinPoint pjp) throws Throwable {
        long start = System.currentTimeMillis();
        try {
            return pjp.proceed();
        } finally {
            long duration = System.currentTimeMillis() - start;
            log.info("{} took {}ms",
                pjp.getSignature().toShortString(), duration);
        }
    }
}

// Usage — clean business code
@Service
public class PortfolioService {
    @Timed  // AOP handles logging, no logging code here
    public PortfolioSummary calculate(String userId) {
        // pure business logic
    }
}

When I use AOP:

  • Logging method execution time (@Timed)
  • Audit logging for sensitive operations
  • Retry logic (@Retryable — Spring Retry uses AOP)
  • Rate limiting per user/role
  • Caching (@Cacheable is AOP under the hood)

Limitations — critical to know:

1. Self-invocation doesn't work:

@Service
public class OrderService {
    @Transactional  // This works — called from outside
    public void createOrder() {
        this.updateInventory(); // This @Transactional is IGNORED
    }

    @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW) // NEVER triggered!
    public void updateInventory() { ... }
}

Spring's proxy intercepts calls FROM OUTSIDE the class only. Solution: inject self (@Autowired OrderService self) or split into two beans.

2. Only works on Spring-managed beans. If you new MyService(), no proxy, no AOP.

3. Only works on public methods (for JDK proxies, the default).

4. Performance overhead. Each proxied call has a small overhead from proxy creation and advice execution. Not noticeable for service methods, but avoid on very hot paths called millions of times/second.

👨‍💻 Hiếu: **How do you implement caching in Spring Boot? Show me the full setup with a real example.**

👨 Khanh: Spring Cache abstraction with Redis:

# application.yml
spring:
  cache:
    type: redis
  data:
    redis:
      host: ${REDIS_HOST:localhost}
      port: 6379
  cache:
    redis:
      time-to-live: 300000  # 5 minutes default TTL
@Configuration
@EnableCaching
public class CacheConfig {

    @Bean
    public RedisCacheManagerBuilderCustomizer cacheManagerCustomizer() {
        return builder -> builder
            .withCacheConfiguration("portfolios",
                RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig()
                    .entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(5))
                    .disableCachingNullValues())
            .withCacheConfiguration("marketPrices",
                RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig()
                    .entryTtl(Duration.ofSeconds(30))  // prices change fast
                    .disableCachingNullValues());
    }
}

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class PortfolioService {

    @Cacheable(
        value = "portfolios",
        key = "#userId",
        unless = "#result == null"  // don't cache null results
    )
    public PortfolioSummary getPortfolio(String userId) {
        return portfolioRepo.calculateSummary(userId); // DB call only on miss
    }

    @CachePut(value = "portfolios", key = "#result.userId")
    @Transactional
    public PortfolioSummary updatePortfolio(UpdatePortfolioRequest req) {
        Portfolio updated = portfolioRepo.save(from(req));
        return PortfolioSummary.from(updated); // updates cache with new value
    }

    @CacheEvict(value = "portfolios", key = "#userId")
    @Transactional
    public void deletePortfolio(String userId) {
        portfolioRepo.deleteByUserId(userId);
    }

    // Evict ALL entries (e.g., after market data update)
    @CacheEvict(value = "marketPrices", allEntries = true)
    @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
    public void refreshMarketPrices() {
        // Called by scheduler — evicts all market price cache entries
        // Next read will re-fetch fresh prices
    }
}

Cache annotations:

Annotation When to use
@Cacheable Read — return cached value if exists, compute and cache if not
@CachePut Always compute AND update the cache (for write-through)
@CacheEvict Remove from cache on update/delete

Testing caches:

@SpringBootTest
class PortfolioServiceCacheTest {

    @Autowired PortfolioService portfolioService;
    @MockBean PortfolioRepository portfolioRepo;

    @Test
    void shouldCacheOnSecondCall() {
        when(portfolioRepo.calculateSummary("user1"))
            .thenReturn(new PortfolioSummary("user1", BigDecimal.TEN));

        portfolioService.getPortfolio("user1");
        portfolioService.getPortfolio("user1"); // second call — should use cache

        // Repository called only once, despite 2 service calls
        verify(portfolioRepo, times(1)).calculateSummary("user1");
    }
}

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP-DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Two Staff Engineers. Full design + coding round.

👨‍💻 Minh (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. I'm Minh, Staff Engineer. Design + coding today. The client is an investment platform.

Design: Investment Portfolio Service (30 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: *"Design an API for managing investment portfolios. Users have portfolios containing stocks. The API needs to:*

  1. Add/remove stocks from a portfolio
  2. Get current portfolio value (real-time stock prices)
  3. Get portfolio performance history (how value changed over time)
  4. Support 50K users, each with up to 50 stocks"

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

Domain model:

User (1) → Portfolio (many) → Position (stock + quantity)
                ↓
         PortfolioSnapshot (daily value history)

Database schema:

CREATE TABLE portfolios (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    user_id     UUID NOT NULL,
    name        VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL,
    currency    CHAR(3) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'USD',
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
);

CREATE TABLE positions (
    id              UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    portfolio_id    UUID REFERENCES portfolios(id),
    ticker          VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL,  -- e.g., 'AAPL', 'GOOGL'
    quantity        DECIMAL(15,6) NOT NULL,  -- fractional shares
    average_cost    DECIMAL(15,4) NOT NULL,  -- cost basis per share
    added_at        TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    UNIQUE(portfolio_id, ticker)
);

-- Historical daily snapshots (for performance history)
CREATE TABLE portfolio_snapshots (
    portfolio_id    UUID REFERENCES portfolios(id),
    snapshot_date   DATE NOT NULL,
    total_value     DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL,
    total_cost      DECIMAL(15,2) NOT NULL,
    PRIMARY KEY (portfolio_id, snapshot_date)
);
CREATE INDEX idx_snapshots_date ON portfolio_snapshots(snapshot_date);

API Design:

GET  /api/v1/portfolios                       → list user's portfolios
POST /api/v1/portfolios                       → create portfolio
GET  /api/v1/portfolios/{id}                  → get with current value
PUT  /api/v1/portfolios/{id}/positions        → add/update position
DELETE /api/v1/portfolios/{id}/positions/{ticker} → remove position
GET  /api/v1/portfolios/{id}/performance?from=&to= → history

Current Value Calculation — the key challenge:

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class PortfolioValueService {

    private final PositionRepository positionRepo;
    private final MarketDataService marketDataService;

    public PortfolioValuation getCurrentValue(UUID portfolioId) {
        List<Position> positions = positionRepo.findByPortfolioId(portfolioId);

        if (positions.isEmpty()) {
            return PortfolioValuation.empty(portfolioId);
        }

        // Fetch all prices in ONE call (not N calls)
        Set<String> tickers = positions.stream()
            .map(Position::getTicker).collect(toSet());
        Map<String, BigDecimal> prices = marketDataService.getPrices(tickers);

        BigDecimal totalValue = BigDecimal.ZERO;
        BigDecimal totalCost = BigDecimal.ZERO;
        List<PositionValuation> positionValues = new ArrayList<>();

        for (Position pos : positions) {
            BigDecimal price = prices.getOrDefault(pos.getTicker(), BigDecimal.ZERO);
            BigDecimal marketValue = price.multiply(pos.getQuantity());
            BigDecimal cost = pos.getAverageCost().multiply(pos.getQuantity());
            BigDecimal gainLoss = marketValue.subtract(cost);

            positionValues.add(new PositionValuation(
                pos.getTicker(), pos.getQuantity(), price,
                marketValue, gainLoss
            ));

            totalValue = totalValue.add(marketValue);
            totalCost = totalCost.add(cost);
        }

        return new PortfolioValuation(portfolioId, totalValue, totalCost,
            totalValue.subtract(totalCost), positionValues);
    }
}

Key: batch price fetch. One call to getPrices(Set<String>) for all tickers — not N individual calls. If a user has 50 stocks, we'd make 50 API calls without batching. With batching: 1 call.

Market Data Caching:

@Service
public class MarketDataService {

    @Cacheable(value = "marketPrices", key = "#tickers.toString()")
    public Map<String, BigDecimal> getPrices(Set<String> tickers) {
        return marketDataClient.getBatchPrices(tickers);  // external API
    }
}

Price cache TTL: 30 seconds (stock prices need to be fairly fresh).

Performance History:

A daily scheduled job:

@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 20 * * MON-FRI", zone = "America/New_York")
// 8 PM ET = market close
public void takePortfolioSnapshots() {
    portfolioRepo.findAllActiveIds().forEach(portfolioId -> {
        PortfolioValuation val = getCurrentValue(portfolioId);
        portfolioSnapshotRepo.save(new PortfolioSnapshot(
            portfolioId, LocalDate.now(), val.getTotalValue(), val.getTotalCost()
        ));
    });
}

Performance history query:

@Cacheable("performance")
public List<PortfolioSnapshot> getPerformance(UUID portfolioId,
                                               LocalDate from, LocalDate to) {
    return snapshotRepo.findByPortfolioIdAndDateBetween(portfolioId, from, to);
}

👨‍💻 Minh: Scale question — 50K users × 50 stocks each = 2.5M positions. How does the nightly snapshot job handle this?

👨 Khanh: Process in parallel batches:

@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 20 * * MON-FRI", zone = "America/New_York")
public void takePortfolioSnapshots() {
    List<UUID> allIds = portfolioRepo.findAllActiveIds();

    // Partition into batches of 500
    Lists.partition(allIds, 500).forEach(batch ->
        executor.submit(() -> processBatch(batch))
    );
}

With 50K portfolios, 100 batches of 500, processed with an executor pool of 20 threads: 5 batches concurrently. At ~200ms per batch (single price fetch covers all 500 portfolios' tickers if they overlap): total time ~5-10 minutes. Well within the off-hours window.

Live Coding: Portfolio Gain/Loss Report (30 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: *"Write a method that takes a list of positions and a map of current prices, and returns a sorted report of all positions by gain/loss percentage (highest gain first). Include total portfolio gain/loss at the end."*

👨 Khanh:

public class GainLossReport {
    public record PositionReport(
        String ticker,
        BigDecimal quantity,
        BigDecimal avgCost,
        BigDecimal currentPrice,
        BigDecimal marketValue,
        BigDecimal gainLossAmount,
        BigDecimal gainLossPercent
    ) {}

    public record PortfolioReport(
        List<PositionReport> positions,
        BigDecimal totalMarketValue,
        BigDecimal totalCostBasis,
        BigDecimal totalGainLoss,
        BigDecimal totalGainLossPercent
    ) {}

    public PortfolioReport generate(
            List<Position> positions,
            Map<String, BigDecimal> currentPrices) {

        List<PositionReport> reports = positions.stream()
            .filter(p -> currentPrices.containsKey(p.getTicker()))
            .map(p -> {
                BigDecimal price = currentPrices.get(p.getTicker());
                BigDecimal marketValue = price.multiply(p.getQuantity());
                BigDecimal costBasis = p.getAverageCost().multiply(p.getQuantity());
                BigDecimal gainLoss = marketValue.subtract(costBasis);

                // Avoid division by zero
                BigDecimal gainLossPercent = costBasis.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) == 0
                    ? BigDecimal.ZERO
                    : gainLoss.divide(costBasis, 4, RoundingMode.HALF_UP)
                        .multiply(new BigDecimal("100"))
                        .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);

                return new PositionReport(
                    p.getTicker(), p.getQuantity(), p.getAverageCost(),
                    price, marketValue, gainLoss, gainLossPercent
                );
            })
            // Sort: highest gain% first, losses at bottom
            .sorted(Comparator.comparing(
                PositionReport::gainLossPercent).reversed())
            .toList();

        // Portfolio totals
        BigDecimal totalMarket = reports.stream()
            .map(PositionReport::marketValue)
            .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);

        BigDecimal totalCost = positions.stream()
            .filter(p -> currentPrices.containsKey(p.getTicker()))
            .map(p -> p.getAverageCost().multiply(p.getQuantity()))
            .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);

        BigDecimal totalGain = totalMarket.subtract(totalCost);

        BigDecimal totalPercent = totalCost.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) == 0
            ? BigDecimal.ZERO
            : totalGain.divide(totalCost, 4, RoundingMode.HALF_UP)
                .multiply(new BigDecimal("100"))
                .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);

        return new PortfolioReport(reports, totalMarket, totalCost,
            totalGain, totalPercent);
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void shouldSortByGainPercentDescending() {
    List<Position> positions = List.of(
        new Position("AAPL", new BigDecimal("10"), new BigDecimal("150")), // cost=1500
        new Position("TSLA", new BigDecimal("5"),  new BigDecimal("200"))  // cost=1000
    );
    Map<String, BigDecimal> prices = Map.of(
        "AAPL", new BigDecimal("180"),  // +20%
        "TSLA", new BigDecimal("160")   // -20%
    );

    var report = generator.generate(positions, prices);

    assertThat(report.positions().get(0).ticker()).isEqualTo("AAPL");  // highest gain first
    assertThat(report.positions().get(0).gainLossPercent()).isEqualByComparingTo("20.00");
    assertThat(report.positions().get(1).ticker()).isEqualTo("TSLA");
    assertThat(report.positions().get(1).gainLossPercent()).isEqualByComparingTo("-20.00");
}

@Test
void shouldCalculateTotalCorrectly() {
    List<Position> positions = List.of(
        new Position("AAPL", new BigDecimal("10"), new BigDecimal("100"))
    );
    Map<String, BigDecimal> prices = Map.of("AAPL", new BigDecimal("120"));

    var report = generator.generate(positions, prices);

    assertThat(report.totalMarketValue()).isEqualByComparingTo("1200");
    assertThat(report.totalCostBasis()).isEqualByComparingTo("1000");
    assertThat(report.totalGainLoss()).isEqualByComparingTo("200");
    assertThat(report.totalGainLossPercent()).isEqualByComparingTo("20.00");
}

@Test
void shouldSkipPositionsWithNoPrice() {
    List<Position> positions = List.of(
        new Position("AAPL", BigDecimal.TEN, new BigDecimal("100")),
        new Position("UNKNOWN", BigDecimal.ONE, new BigDecimal("50"))
    );
    Map<String, BigDecimal> prices = Map.of("AAPL", new BigDecimal("110"));

    var report = generator.generate(positions, prices);

    assertThat(report.positions()).hasSize(1);
    assertThat(report.positions().get(0).ticker()).isEqualTo("AAPL");
}

👨‍💻 Minh: Excellent code. Java record for DTOs, BigDecimal throughout, zero-division guard, comprehensive tests including the "skip missing prices" edge case. This is production quality.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Screening Cache-aside trade-off 10/10 Justified by data access pattern, not "always cache"
Screening CAP theorem applied 10/10 CP for ledger, AP for profiles — concrete examples
Screening Self-rating 10/10 Honest 8/10, identified WebFlux gap — shows maturity
Deep-dive AOP + self-invocation pitfall 10/10 Explained proxy mechanism, the #1 gotcha
Deep-dive Spring Cache full setup 10/10 Config, annotations, testing cache behavior
Design Portfolio service 10/10 Batch price fetch, snapshot job with parallel batches
Coding Gain/loss report 10/10 Records, BigDecimal, sorted, 3 comprehensive tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 EPAM Interview Tips

  1. EPAM tests architectural thinking, not just coding. CAP, trade-offs, justification
  2. Self-rating questions. Give an honest number with specific gaps — never say "10/10"
  3. AOP self-invocation pitfall. EPAM specifically tests this — very common question
  4. Spring Cache. Know all 3 annotations and when to use each
  5. Java records (Java 16+). Use them for DTOs — shows modern Java knowledge
  6. Batch operations. Any "N items" scenario → think batching + parallel processing
  7. EPAM salary. Senior in HCMC: 40-55M gross. They pay above market
  8. EPAM cares about English. More than typical outsourcing — they embed with US/EU teams

🎭 Mock Interview #22 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: VNG Corporation (Vietnam's #1 Tech Unicorn, HCMC)

Context: VNG is Vietnam's largest and most prestigious tech company — the only Vietnamese tech unicorn ($1B+ valuation). Products: Zalo (70M users, Vietnam's #1 messaging app), ZaloPay (digital wallet), ZingMP3, ZingPlay (gaming). Engineering team: 2000+ engineers. This is the Google of Vietnam. Hiring for the ZaloPay Core Platform team — the backbone that handles millions of payment transactions daily. Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, MySQL/TiDB, K8s on-premise. Why this is top-tier: VNG has the highest engineering bar in Vietnam. They compete with global tech companies for talent. Senior engineers here earn 50-80M+ gross. The interview tests deep Java knowledge, system design at Vietnamese scale, and product thinking.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR + CULTURE (25 min, Vietnamese → English)

👩 Phương (HR, VNG): Chào anh Khanh! Em là Phương bên Talent Acquisition của VNG. Mình sẽ bắt đầu bằng tiếng Việt, sau đó switch sang tiếng Anh cho phần sau nha.

Anh biết đến VNG qua đâu và tại sao anh muốn join?

👨 Khanh: VNG thì ai làm tech ở Việt Nam cũng biết anh ơi! Em biết VNG vì Zalo — em dùng Zalo hàng ngày, và mỗi lần dùng em hay tự hỏi backend họ handle thế nào với 70M user mà vẫn mượt.

Lý do muốn join cụ thể là ZaloPay. Em có background banking (Axon Active - Valiant Bank) và payments (Sony Global). ZaloPay đang scale mạnh — 30M+ users, competitive với MoMo. Bài toán kỹ thuật ở đây — high-throughput payments, fraud detection, real-time balance — là những gì em thực sự hứng thú.

Và thành thật mà nói, VNG là nơi em muốn "level up" thực sự. Đây là tech company thật sự của Việt Nam, không phải outsource.

👩 Phương: Anh đang làm ở FPT/Sony. Lý do anh muốn rời?

👨 Khanh: FPT là outsourcing — em build cho Sony, không phải build product của mình. Em muốn ownership thực sự: em design một feature, nó go-live, 30M người dùng ZaloPay sử dụng. Cái impact đó khác hẳn.

👩 Phương: Expected salary của anh?
