👨 Khanh: Em tìm hiểu market thì senior engineer ở VNG dao động 50-80M gross tùy level và domain. Với 5 năm kinh nghiệm, background payments và banking, và AWS certifications, em expect khoảng 55-65M. Nhưng em open to discuss sau khi qua technical rounds.

👩 Phương: Fair. VNG có 4 technical rounds. Khá demanding đấy. Anh sẵn sàng không?

👨 Khanh: Sẵn sàng — em coi đó là cơ hội học hỏi dù kết quả thế nào.

💻 ROUND 2 — JAVA CORE DEEP-DIVE (45 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer. Tests deep Java internals.

👨‍💻 Long (Principal Eng): Hi Khanh. At VNG, we work with millions of concurrent users. I'll test your Java internals depth because at our scale, the wrong data structure choice can bring down a service.

👨‍💻 Long: **Explain Java memory model — heap, stack, metaspace. What goes where and why does it matter for a payment service?**

👨 Khanh: Java memory has three main regions:

Stack — per-thread, fast. Stores:

  • Local variables (primitives and references)
  • Method call frames
  • Very fast allocation/deallocation (just move a pointer)
  • Size: ~512KB-1MB per thread. At 200 threads → 200MB just for stacks.

Heap — shared across all threads. Stores:

  • All objects (new Object(), all collections, all entities)
  • Divided into Young Gen (Eden + Survivor) and Old Gen
  • GC manages this space — allocation and deallocation are slower

Metaspace (Java 8+, replaced PermGen). Stores:

  • Class metadata, method bytecode, static fields
  • Lives in native memory (not heap)
  • Grows dynamically — metaspace leak = OOM native memory

Why it matters for ZaloPay:

// Payment service processing 5K TPS

// PROBLEM 1: Thread stack exhaustion
@RestController
public class PaymentController {
    // If we create 1000 threads (bad config), stack memory = 1GB
    // Better: use async (Project Reactor / CompletableFuture)
    // so fewer threads handle more requests
}

// PROBLEM 2: Heap pressure from temporary objects
public BigDecimal calculateFee(PaymentRequest req) {
    // Each calculation creates: BigDecimal objects, String concatenations
    // At 5K TPS → 5K × (number of objects) allocations/sec
    // This is why we minimize allocations in hot paths
    return req.getAmount()
        .multiply(FEE_RATE)  // creates new BigDecimal
        .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);  // creates another
    // These are short-lived → Young Gen → fast GC → fine
}

// PROBLEM 3: Metaspace leak
// Happens with dynamic class loading (e.g., generating classes with CGLIB)
// Groovy scripts evaluated at runtime, some serialization frameworks
// Symptom: java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Metaspace
// Fix: -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=256m to cap and alert early

Monitoring in production:

# JVM metrics via Micrometer → CloudWatch/Grafana
jvm.memory.used{area=heap}
jvm.memory.used{area=nonheap}  # Metaspace is here
jvm.gc.pause
jvm.threads.live

👨‍💻 Long: **HashMap vs ConcurrentHashMap vs Hashtable. In a payment service with concurrent requests, which do you use and why?**

👨 Khanh: Three very different tools:

HashMap:

  • Not thread-safe. Multiple threads reading and writing → race conditions,

potential infinite loop in Java 7 (HashMap resize bug).

  • Use: single-threaded code, local variables, inside synchronized blocks.

Hashtable:

  • Thread-safe via synchronized on every method — the entire map is locked.
  • At 5K TPS: 5K threads competing for one lock → severe contention.
  • Use: never. Legacy class. No reason to use it today.

ConcurrentHashMap:

  • Thread-safe with fine-grained locking — locks individual buckets (segments),

not the whole map. Multiple threads can read/write simultaneously as long as they hit different buckets.

  • get() is completely lock-free (uses volatile reads).
  • Use: always in multi-threaded payment code.
// ZaloPay payment service — concurrent request handling

// ❌ WRONG — race condition, possible data corruption
private final Map<String, PaymentSession> sessions = new HashMap<>();

// ✅ CORRECT — thread-safe without sacrificing performance
private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, PaymentSession> sessions =
    new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

// Also use atomic operations:
// putIfAbsent() — atomic "only add if not exists"
// computeIfAbsent() — atomic "get or compute"
sessions.computeIfAbsent(sessionId, k -> new PaymentSession(k));
// This is atomic — only one thread creates the session even under heavy concurrency

Advanced: ConcurrentHashMap at VNG scale:

At 5K TPS, if multiple threads update the same key simultaneously:

// ❌ Not atomic — read-modify-write race condition
sessions.get(key).incrementRetryCount();

// ✅ Atomic — use compute()
sessions.compute(key, (k, v) -> {
    if (v == null) return new PaymentSession(k);
    v.incrementRetryCount();
    return v;
});

👨‍💻 Long: Explain volatile keyword. When do you need it?

👨 Khanh: volatile guarantees two things:

  1. Visibility: A write to a volatile variable is immediately

visible to all threads. Without volatile, each CPU core may cache the variable — one thread's write isn't seen by other cores.

  1. Ordering: No instruction reordering around volatile reads/writes.

Classic use case — feature flag:

public class PaymentService {
    // Without volatile: Thread A sets to true, Thread B might still see false
    private volatile boolean maintenanceMode = false;

    // Background config refresh thread updates this
    public void setMaintenanceMode(boolean mode) {
        this.maintenanceMode = mode; // written once, read by 1000 request threads
    }

    public PaymentResult process(PaymentRequest req) {
        if (maintenanceMode) {  // read by many threads
            throw new ServiceUnavailableException("System under maintenance");
        }
        // ...
    }
}

Another use case — lazy singleton (double-checked locking):

public class PaymentConfig {
    private static volatile PaymentConfig instance; // MUST be volatile

    public static PaymentConfig getInstance() {
        if (instance == null) {               // first check (no lock)
            synchronized (PaymentConfig.class) {
                if (instance == null) {       // second check (with lock)
                    instance = new PaymentConfig(); // volatile write
                }
            }
        }
        return instance; // volatile read
    }
}
// Without volatile, instruction reordering could cause another thread
// to see a partially constructed object

When NOT to use volatile:

  • When you need atomic compound operations (read-modify-write) → use AtomicLong
  • When multiple fields must be updated together → use synchronized
  • volatile only makes sense for simple reads/writes of a single variable.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer. ZaloPay scale system design.

👨‍💻 Hùng (Staff Eng): Design ZaloPay's QR payment flow. A user opens ZaloPay, scans a merchant's QR code, and pays. The merchant's terminal shows "Payment received" within 3 seconds. Scale: 10M transactions/day, peak 200 TPS. Vietnam-specific context: unreliable network, offline-capable terminal preferred.

👨 Khanh: [2 minutes to think]

QR Payment Types:

  • Static QR (merchant's fixed QR): same QR for all transactions,

amount entered by customer

  • Dynamic QR (generated per transaction): amount pre-filled,

unique per payment

I'll design for dynamic QR (more secure, more common for larger amounts).

Flow:

Merchant creates QR:
  1. Merchant terminal → ZaloPay Gateway: POST /qr/generate {amount, merchantId}
  2. Gateway → QR Service: creates a unique payment token (UUID), stores in Redis
  3. Returns QR code containing: zalopay://pay?token=abc123&amount=150000

Customer scans:
  4. ZaloPay app reads QR → extracts token
  5. App → ZaloPay Gateway: POST /payments {token, customerId}
  6. Gateway → Payment Service:
     a. Validate token (exists? not expired? not already used?)
     b. Check customer balance
     c. Debit customer, credit merchant (atomic)
     d. Mark token as USED
  7. Return success

Merchant notification:
  8. Payment Service → Kafka: "payment.completed" event
  9. Merchant Notification Service consumes event
  10. Push to merchant terminal via WebSocket: "Payment received ✓"

Architecture:

Customer App          Merchant Terminal
     │                      │
     │ scan QR              │ WebSocket
     │                      │
     ▼                      ▼
┌────────────────────────────────┐
│         API Gateway            │
│    (rate limiting, auth)       │
└───────┬──────────┬─────────────┘
        │          │
┌───────▼──┐  ┌────▼────────────┐
│  QR      │  │ Payment Service  │
│ Service  │  │ (core logic)     │
│ (Redis)  │  └────┬────────────┘
└──────────┘       │
                   │ Kafka event
                   ▼
              ┌────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
              │  Payment   │    │  Merchant    │
              │  DB        │    │  Notification│
              │  (TiDB)    │    │  (WebSocket) │
              └────────────┘    └──────────────┘

Token Design (Redis):

Key: "qr:token:{token_uuid}"
Value: {
  merchantId: "merchant-123",
  amount: 150000,
  currency: "VND",
  status: "PENDING",  → USED after payment
  expiresAt: 1700000300  (5-minute TTL)
}
TTL: 300 seconds (5 minutes — QR expires)

Idempotency — critical for payments:

@Transactional
public PaymentResult processPayment(PaymentRequest req) {
    // 1. Check if token already used (idempotency)
    String tokenKey = "qr:token:" + req.getToken();
    PaymentToken token = redisTemplate.opsForValue().get(tokenKey);

    if (token == null) throw new TokenExpiredException();
    if ("USED".equals(token.getStatus())) {
        // Return the original result (already processed)
        return paymentRepo.findByToken(req.getToken())
            .map(PaymentResult::from)
            .orElseThrow(() -> new PaymentNotFoundException());
    }

    // 2. Atomic balance debit with Redis + DB
    // Use Redis for fast balance check, DB as source of truth
    boolean reserved = redisTemplate.execute(DEBIT_SCRIPT,
        List.of("balance:" + req.getCustomerId()),
        token.getAmount().toString()
    );  // Lua script: atomic check-and-debit

    if (!reserved) throw new InsufficientBalanceException();

    // 3. Persist to DB
    Payment payment = Payment.builder()
        .token(req.getToken())
        .customerId(req.getCustomerId())
        .merchantId(token.getMerchantId())
        .amount(token.getAmount())
        .status("SUCCESS")
        .build();
    paymentRepo.save(payment);

    // 4. Mark token as used
    token.setStatus("USED");
    redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(tokenKey, token, Duration.ofMinutes(5));

    // 5. Publish event for merchant notification
    kafkaTemplate.send("payment.completed", payment.getId().toString(),
        new PaymentCompletedEvent(payment));

    return PaymentResult.from(payment);
}

Why Redis Lua script for balance debit?

-- Atomic: check balance AND debit in one operation
local balance = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]))
local amount = tonumber(ARGV[1])
if balance >= amount then
    redis.call('DECRBY', KEYS[1], amount)
    return 1  -- success
else
    return 0  -- insufficient balance
end

This prevents race condition where two simultaneous payments both pass the balance check but together overdraw the account.

Handling Vietnam's unreliable network:

The merchant terminal might not receive the WebSocket notification (network drop). Solution: polling fallback: 

// Merchant terminal polls if WebSocket notification not received in 5 seconds
GET /api/payments/status?token={token}&merchantId={merchantId}
// Returns PENDING, SUCCESS, or FAILED
// Terminal polls every 2 seconds for up to 30 seconds

The DB query is fast (indexed by token). No heavy lifting.

👨‍💻 Hùng: Sharp design. The Lua script for atomic balance check is exactly what we do. How do you handle the case where the DB write succeeds but Kafka publish fails?

👨 Khanh: Outbox pattern:

Instead of publishing directly to Kafka, write the event to an outbox table within the same DB transaction:

CREATE TABLE payment_outbox (
    id          UUID PRIMARY KEY,
    payment_id  UUID NOT NULL,
    event_type  VARCHAR(50),
    payload     JSONB,
    published   BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);
@Transactional
public PaymentResult processPayment(PaymentRequest req) {
    // ... existing logic ...

    // Same transaction: save payment + outbox event
    paymentRepo.save(payment);
    outboxRepo.save(new OutboxEvent("payment.completed", payment));
    // If EITHER fails, both roll back. Atomic.

    return PaymentResult.from(payment);
}

// Background job reads unpublished outbox events and sends to Kafka
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 1000)
public void publishOutboxEvents() {
    outboxRepo.findUnpublished().forEach(event -> {
        kafkaTemplate.send("payment.completed", event.getPayload());
        event.setPublished(true);
        outboxRepo.save(event);
    });
}

This guarantees at-least-once delivery to Kafka. The consumer must be idempotent (use paymentId as Kafka message key so Kafka ensures ordering, and consumer deduplicates by paymentId).

💻 ROUND 4 — LIVE CODING (40 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer. LeetCode medium-level but practical.

👨‍💻 Hùng: This is a real problem from ZaloPay's fraud system.

*"You receive a stream of payment events. Each event has: userId, amount, timestamp. Detect users who make more than 5 payments in any 60-second sliding window. Return the list of flagged userIds."* 

Events (sorted by time):
  user1, $50,  t=0
  user1, $30,  t=10
  user1, $20,  t=20
  user1, $100, t=30
  user1, $50,  t=40
  user1, $10,  t=50  ← 6th payment within [0,60) → FLAG user1
  user2, $200, t=55  ← only 1 payment → OK

👨 Khanh: Sliding window per user. For each user, I maintain a queue of timestamps. When a new payment comes in, I:

  1. Remove timestamps older than current - 60
  2. Add the new timestamp
  3. If queue size > 5, flag the user
public Set<String> detectFraudulentUsers(List<PaymentEvent> events) {
    // userId → deque of timestamps (sorted, oldest first)
    Map<String, Deque<Long>> userWindows = new HashMap<>();
    Set<String> flagged = new HashSet<>();

    for (PaymentEvent event : events) {
        String userId = event.getUserId();
        long timestamp = event.getTimestamp();
        long windowStart = timestamp - 60;

        // Get or create window for this user
        Deque<Long> window = userWindows.computeIfAbsent(
            userId, k -> new ArrayDeque<>()
        );

        // Remove events outside the 60-second window
        while (!window.isEmpty() && window.peekFirst() <= windowStart) {
            window.pollFirst();
        }

        // Add current event
        window.addLast(timestamp);

        // Check threshold
        if (window.size() > 5) {
            flagged.add(userId);
        }
    }

    return flagged;
}

Trace:

user1, t=0:  window=[0], size=1, OK
user1, t=10: window=[0,10], size=2, OK
user1, t=20: window=[0,10,20], size=3, OK
user1, t=30: window=[0,10,20,30], size=4, OK
user1, t=40: window=[0,10,20,30,40], size=5, OK
user1, t=50: windowStart=50-60=-10, nothing removed
             window=[0,10,20,30,40,50], size=6 > 5 → FLAG user1 ✓
user2, t=55: window=[55], size=1, OK

Result: {user1} ✓

Complexity:

  • Time: O(n) total — each event is added once, removed at most once
  • Space: O(u × w) where u = unique users, w = max payments in any window

Edge cases:

  • Same timestamp: <= in remove condition (not <) handles events

at exactly windowStart correctly

  • Single user, all payments at same time: window never shrinks,

flagged after 6th payment

  • Empty events list: returns empty set

Follow-up: Real-time with Kafka Streams:

// For production streaming (not batch)
KStream<String, PaymentEvent> payments = builder.stream("payments");

payments
    .groupByKey()  // group by userId
    .windowedBy(SlidingWindows.ofTimeDifferenceWithNoGrace(Duration.ofSeconds(60)))
    .count()
    .filter((windowedKey, count) -> count > 5)
    .toStream()
    .map((windowedKey, count) -> KeyValue.pair(windowedKey.key(), new FraudAlert()))
    .to("fraud.alerts");

Kafka Streams handles the sliding window natively — no need to maintain the Deque per user manually. More scalable for millions of users.

👨‍💻 Hùng: Clean solution. The Kafka Streams follow-up shows you think beyond the algorithm to production-scale implementation.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR Motivation (Vietnamese) 10/10 Authentic — Zalo user, product ownership, clear growth reason
HR Salary negotiation 10/10 Market-researched range, confident
Java Core Memory model (Heap/Stack/Metaspace) 10/10 Applied to payment service context
Java Core ConcurrentHashMap vs Hashtable 10/10 computeIfAbsent atomic operation
Java Core volatile keyword 10/10 Visibility, ordering, double-checked locking
System Design QR payment flow 10/10 Lua script atomic debit, outbox pattern for Kafka
Coding Sliding window fraud detection 10/10 O(n) solution + Kafka Streams production follow-up

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 VNG Interview Tips

  1. VNG is the hardest technical bar in Vietnam. Expect Java internals, not just Spring Boot
  2. They ask about concurrency deeply. volatile, ConcurrentHashMap, atomic operations
  3. Payments domain knowledge. QR payment, idempotency, atomic balance operations
  4. Vietnamese HR round. Be authentic about why VNG — mention Zalo/ZaloPay as a user
  5. Salary: know VNG pays top market. Don't undersell. 50-80M for senior is realistic
  6. Outbox pattern is expected. Shows you understand distributed system guarantees
  7. Lua scripts in Redis. This is how top companies do atomic operations
  8. Kafka Streams for real-time. Sliding window problems → show streaming solution

🎭 Mock Interview #23 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: MoMo (M_Service, Vietnam's #1 Digital Wallet, HCMC)

Context: MoMo is Vietnam's leading digital wallet — 30M+ users, 50K+ merchant partners, 300K+ transactions/day. Backed by Warburg Pincus and Goldman Sachs. Engineering team: 800+ engineers in HCMC. Valuation: ~$2B. This is one of the top 3 most desired tech employers in Vietnam after VNG and Sea Group. Hiring for the Merchant Platform team — APIs used by 50K merchants to accept MoMo payments, manage settlements, and view analytics. Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, MySQL, Redis, Kafka, AWS. Interview format: MoMo has 3 technical rounds. More product-focused than VNG — they want engineers who understand merchant/business problems, not just algorithms. Good English required but Vietnamese is fine for HR.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, Vietnamese)

👩 Ngọc (HR): Chào anh Khanh! Em là Ngọc bên People team MoMo. Anh có hay dùng MoMo không?

👨 Khanh: Có chứ em! Anh dùng MoMo gần như hàng tuần — nạp tiền điện, thanh toán grab, và mua trên Shopee. Thật ra anh hay để ý UX của MoMo và so sánh với ZaloPay — cả hai đều tốt nhưng MoMo có merchant network rộng hơn trong daily life.

👩 Ngọc: Tại sao anh muốn join MoMo cụ thể?

👨 Khanh: Anh có background payments ở Sony và banking ở Axon Active. MoMo Merchant Platform là điểm giao giữa hai cái đó — vừa là payments processing, vừa là B2B product cho merchants. Bài toán kỹ thuật rất thú vị: 50K merchants với traffic profile rất khác nhau — một cái cửa hàng bán lẻ nhỏ vs. Shopee vs. CGV. Design một platform serve all of them well là challenge thật sự.

👩 Ngọc: Anh expect gì về career growth ở MoMo?

👨 Khanh: Anh muốn grow thành Tech Lead trong 2-3 năm — không chỉ code mà còn mentor team và drive technical decisions. MoMo đang scale nhanh, nên opportunity cho senior engineers trở thành tech lead khá rõ ràng hơn so với outsourcing.

👩 Ngọc: Expected salary?

👨 Khanh: Với vị trí Senior và domain payments của anh, anh expect 45-60M gross. Anh biết MoMo có equity program — anh cũng quan tâm đến phần đó.

👩 Ngọc: OK anh. Technical rounds sẽ có 3 vòng — Spring deep dive, system design, và coding. Good luck nha!

💻 ROUND 2 — SPRING BOOT & DATABASE (50 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer. Practical Spring Boot + DB focus.

👨‍💻 Đức (Sr Eng): Hi Khanh! I'm Đức, Senior Engineer on Merchant Platform. Our tech is very standard Spring Boot + MySQL — no exotic stuff. But we need depth on production-ready patterns.

Spring Boot Production Patterns (25 min)

👨‍💻 Đức: **In our merchant API, we have endpoints that call multiple downstream services. How do you implement proper timeout, retry, and circuit breaker in Spring Boot?**

👨 Khanh: I use Resilience4j — it integrates cleanly with Spring Boot via annotations and auto-configuration.

Dependency + config:

# application.yml
resilience4j:
  circuitbreaker:
    instances:
      paymentGateway:
        slidingWindowSize: 10
        failureRateThreshold: 50        # open if 50% of last 10 calls fail
        waitDurationInOpenState: 30s    # stay open 30 seconds then try
        permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState: 3
  retry:
    instances:
      paymentGateway:
        maxAttempts: 3
        waitDuration: 500ms
        exponentialBackoffMultiplier: 2 # 500ms, 1s, 2s
        retryExceptions:
          - java.net.ConnectException
          - java.net.SocketTimeoutException
        ignoreExceptions:
          - com.momo.exception.InvalidRequestException  # don't retry client errors
  timelimiter:
    instances:
      paymentGateway:
        timeoutDuration: 3s

Service implementation:

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class MerchantSettlementService {

    private final PaymentGatewayClient gatewayClient;
    private final SettlementRepository settlementRepo;

    @CircuitBreaker(name = "paymentGateway", fallbackMethod = "settleFallback")
    @Retry(name = "paymentGateway")
    @TimeLimiter(name = "paymentGateway")
    public CompletableFuture<SettlementResult> initiateSettlement(
            String merchantId, BigDecimal amount) {

        return CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() ->
            gatewayClient.settle(merchantId, amount)
        );
    }

    // Fallback: queue for manual settlement if gateway is down
    public CompletableFuture<SettlementResult> settleFallback(
            String merchantId, BigDecimal amount, Exception ex) {

        log.warn("Gateway unavailable for merchant {}, queuing: {}", merchantId, ex.getMessage());

        PendingSettlement pending = new PendingSettlement(merchantId, amount, LocalDateTime.now());
        settlementRepo.save(pending);

        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(
            SettlementResult.queued("Settlement queued — will process when gateway recovers")
        );
    }
}

Annotation order matters: @TimeLimiter → @CircuitBreaker → @Retry (outermost to innermost). This means: retry happens first, then circuit breaker tracks failures, then timeout applies to the whole chain.

Monitoring circuit breaker state:

// Expose via Actuator + Micrometer
// Metrics auto-published: resilience4j.circuitbreaker.state
// Alert when state changes to OPEN

👨‍💻 Đức: **Merchant dashboard shows a list of recent transactions. The query needs: filter by date range, status, amount range. With 100M transaction rows, how do you keep this fast?**

👨 Khanh: Four layers of optimization:

Layer 1: Composite index:

-- The query pattern: WHERE merchant_id = ? AND created_at BETWEEN ? AND ?
-- AND status = ? AND amount BETWEEN ? AND ?

-- Covering index — includes all filtered and selected columns
CREATE INDEX idx_txn_merchant_date ON transactions
    (merchant_id, created_at DESC, status, amount);

-- MySQL can satisfy the entire query from this index alone (no table access)
EXPLAIN SELECT id, amount, status, created_at
FROM transactions
WHERE merchant_id = 'M123'
  AND created_at BETWEEN '2024-01-01' AND '2024-03-31'
  AND status = 'SUCCESS';
-- Should show: "Using index" → fast

Layer 2: Partition by date (for 100M+ rows):

-- Partition transactions table by month
CREATE TABLE transactions (
    id          BIGINT,
    merchant_id VARCHAR(50),
    amount      DECIMAL(15,2),
    status      VARCHAR(20),
    created_at  DATETIME,
    PRIMARY KEY (id, created_at)  -- partition key must be in PK
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (YEAR(created_at) * 100 + MONTH(created_at)) (
    PARTITION p202401 VALUES LESS THAN (202402),
    PARTITION p202402 VALUES LESS THAN (202403),
    PARTITION p202403 VALUES LESS THAN (202404),
    PARTITION p202404 VALUES LESS THAN (202405)
    -- ...
);
-- Date range queries only scan relevant partitions

Layer 3: Keyset pagination (not OFFSET):

// ❌ Slow for large pages
// SELECT * FROM transactions WHERE merchant_id=? LIMIT 20 OFFSET 10000
// MySQL still reads 10020 rows and discards 10000

// ✅ Fast keyset pagination
@Query("""
    SELECT t FROM Transaction t
    WHERE t.merchantId = :merchantId
    AND t.createdAt < :beforeDate
    AND (:lastId IS NULL OR t.id < :lastId)
    ORDER BY t.createdAt DESC, t.id DESC
    LIMIT 20
    """)
List<Transaction> findPage(
    @Param("merchantId") String merchantId,
    @Param("beforeDate") LocalDateTime beforeDate,
    @Param("lastId") Long lastId  // cursor from last item of previous page
);

Layer 4: Read replica for dashboard queries:

// Route read-only dashboard queries to MySQL read replica
@Transactional(readOnly = true)
@DS("replica")  // Shyiko dynamic datasource annotation
public Page<TransactionDTO> getMerchantTransactions(MerchantFilter filter, Pageable page) {
    // Hits read replica, not primary
}

This removes dashboard load from the primary DB that handles writes.

Database Design (25 min)

👨‍💻 Đức: **Design the database schema for MoMo Merchant Platform. A merchant has: profile, multiple bank accounts for settlement, stores/branches, and transactions. Show me your schema.**

👨 Khanh:

-- Core merchant profile
CREATE TABLE merchants (
    id              VARCHAR(20) PRIMARY KEY,    -- 'MCH-20240001'
    business_name   VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    tax_code        VARCHAR(20) UNIQUE,         -- Vietnam tax code
    business_type   ENUM('INDIVIDUAL','COMPANY','ENTERPRISE'),
    status          ENUM('PENDING','ACTIVE','SUSPENDED','CLOSED'),
    tier            ENUM('BASIC','STANDARD','PREMIUM'),
    momo_rate       DECIMAL(5,4) NOT NULL,      -- transaction fee rate (e.g., 0.0150 = 1.5%)
    created_at      DATETIME NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at      DATETIME NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW() ON UPDATE NOW(),
    INDEX idx_status (status),
    INDEX idx_tier (tier)
);

-- Multiple bank accounts per merchant (for settlement)
CREATE TABLE merchant_bank_accounts (
    id              BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    merchant_id     VARCHAR(20) REFERENCES merchants(id),
    bank_code       VARCHAR(10) NOT NULL,       -- 'VCB', 'TCB', 'ACB'
    account_number  VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
    account_name    VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    is_default      BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE,
    verified_at     DATETIME,
    INDEX idx_merchant (merchant_id)
);

-- Physical stores / branches
CREATE TABLE merchant_stores (
    id              BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    merchant_id     VARCHAR(20) REFERENCES merchants(id),
    store_name      VARCHAR(200) NOT NULL,
    address         TEXT,
    city            VARCHAR(100),
    qr_code         VARCHAR(500),               -- static QR for this store
    is_active       BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE,
    INDEX idx_merchant (merchant_id)
);

-- Transactions (high-volume — 300K/day)
CREATE TABLE transactions (
    id              BIGINT PRIMARY KEY,         -- Snowflake ID
    merchant_id     VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    store_id        BIGINT,
    user_id         VARCHAR(50),                -- MoMo user
    amount          DECIMAL(15,0) NOT NULL,     -- VND, no decimals needed
    fee             DECIMAL(15,0) NOT NULL,     -- MoMo fee
    net_amount      DECIMAL(15,0) NOT NULL,     -- amount - fee
    status          ENUM('PENDING','SUCCESS','FAILED','REFUNDED'),
    payment_method  ENUM('WALLET','QR','POS','ONLINE'),
    ref_code        VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE,         -- external reference
    created_at      DATETIME NOT NULL,
    INDEX idx_merchant_date (merchant_id, created_at DESC),
    INDEX idx_status (status),
    INDEX idx_ref_code (ref_code)
) PARTITION BY RANGE (TO_DAYS(created_at)) (...);

-- Daily settlement summary (pre-aggregated)
CREATE TABLE merchant_settlements (
    id              BIGINT PRIMARY KEY AUTO_INCREMENT,
    merchant_id     VARCHAR(20) REFERENCES merchants(id),
    settlement_date DATE NOT NULL,
    total_transactions INT NOT NULL,
    gross_amount    DECIMAL(15,0) NOT NULL,
    total_fee       DECIMAL(15,0) NOT NULL,
    net_amount      DECIMAL(15,0) NOT NULL,     -- amount transferred to merchant
    bank_account_id BIGINT REFERENCES merchant_bank_accounts(id),
    status          ENUM('PENDING','PROCESSING','COMPLETED','FAILED'),
    transferred_at  DATETIME,
    UNIQUE KEY uk_merchant_date (merchant_id, settlement_date)
);

Key design decisions:

Decision Reason
VND as integer (no decimals) VND has no sub-currency unit — simplifies math
net_amount stored separately Audit trail — don't recalculate from amount - fee
Snowflake ID for transactions Globally unique, time-ordered, sortable — better than UUID
ref_code unique index Idempotency — same external reference = same transaction
Pre-aggregated daily settlements Dashboard "total this month" is instant — no SUM() over millions of rows
Partition transactions by date Range queries are fast — most queries are "last 30 days"

👨‍💻 Đức: VND as integer, pre-aggregated settlements, and Snowflake IDs — all three show production payment system experience. Good schema.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN + CODING (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer.

👨‍💻 Hải (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. I'm Hải, Staff Engineer. Design + coding today.

Design: Merchant Settlement System (30 min)

👨‍💻 Hải: *"Design MoMo's daily settlement system. Every day at 10 PM, we calculate what each merchant earned (transactions minus fees), then transfer that amount to their bank account. 50K active merchants, 300K transactions/day. The transfer must be accurate — merchants get paid exactly what they earned. Transfers happen via bank API which sometimes fails."*

👨 Khanh:

Settlement Flow:

Daily Batch (10 PM):
  1. Aggregate: SUM transactions by merchant for the day
  2. Calculate net (gross - fees)
  3. Create settlement records (one per merchant)
  4. Queue bank transfers
  5. Execute bank transfers via API
  6. Confirm and notify merchants

Architecture:

10 PM trigger (Scheduler)
    │
    ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│  Settlement Job │  (Spring Batch)
│  - Step 1: Agg  │  → MySQL → SettlementRecords
│  - Step 2: Queue│  → Kafka topic "settlements.pending"
└─────────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐      ┌─────────────┐
│  Transfer       │─────►│ Bank API    │
│  Service        │      │ (external)  │
│  (Kafka consumer│◄─────│             │
└─────────────────┘      └─────────────┘
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│  Notification   │  → SMS/email to merchant
│  Service        │
└─────────────────┘

Step 1 — Aggregation (Spring Batch):

@Configuration
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class SettlementBatchConfig {

    @Bean
    public Job settlementJob(Step aggregateStep, Step queueTransferStep) {
        return jobBuilderFactory.get("settlementJob")
            .start(aggregateStep)
            .next(queueTransferStep)
            .build();
    }

    @Bean
    @StepScope
    public JdbcCursorItemReader<MerchantDaySummary> aggregateReader(
            @Value("#{jobParameters['settlementDate']}") String date) {
        return new JdbcCursorItemReaderBuilder<MerchantDaySummary>()
            .name("aggregateReader")
            .dataSource(dataSource)
            .sql("""
                SELECT merchant_id,
                       COUNT(*) as transaction_count,
                       SUM(amount) as gross_amount,
                       SUM(fee) as total_fee,
                       SUM(net_amount) as net_amount
                FROM transactions
                WHERE DATE(created_at) = ?
                  AND status = 'SUCCESS'
                GROUP BY merchant_id
                """)
            .preparedStatementSetter(ps -> ps.setString(1, date))
            .rowMapper(new MerchantDaySummaryRowMapper())
            .build();
    }

    @Bean
    public ItemWriter<MerchantDaySummary> settlementWriter() {
        return summaries -> {
            // Write settlement records to DB
            settlementRepo.saveAll(summaries.stream()
                .map(s -> MerchantSettlement.from(s, LocalDate.now()))
                .toList());
        };
    }
}

Handling bank transfer failures:

@KafkaListener(topics = "settlements.pending", groupId = "transfer-service")
@Transactional
public void processSettlement(SettlementMessage msg) {
    MerchantSettlement settlement = settlementRepo.findById(msg.getSettlementId())
        .orElseThrow();

    if (!settlement.getStatus().equals("PENDING")) {
        log.warn("Settlement {} already processed, skipping", msg.getSettlementId());
        return; // Idempotent — safe to receive duplicate message
    }

    try {
        BankTransferResult result = bankApiClient.transfer(
            settlement.getMerchantBankAccountId(),
            settlement.getNetAmount(),
            buildReference(settlement)
        );

        settlement.setStatus("COMPLETED");
        settlement.setTransferredAt(LocalDateTime.now());
        settlement.setBankReference(result.getReference());

    } catch (BankApiTemporaryException ex) {
        // Temporary failure — retry
        settlement.setRetryCount(settlement.getRetryCount() + 1);
        if (settlement.getRetryCount() >= 3) {
            settlement.setStatus("FAILED_NEEDS_REVIEW");
            alertOpsTeam(settlement); // Manual review needed
        } else {
            // Re-queue with delay (Kafka doesn't support delay natively)
            retryQueue.scheduleRetry(msg, Duration.ofMinutes(15));
        }

    } catch (BankApiPermanentException ex) {
        // Wrong account number, etc. — don't retry
        settlement.setStatus("FAILED_INVALID_ACCOUNT");
        notifyMerchant(settlement, "Settlement failed: " + ex.getMessage());
    }

    settlementRepo.save(settlement);
}

Accuracy guarantee:

  1. Reconciliation job (daily 6 AM):
  • Compare: SUM(transactions.net_amount) by merchant by date
  • vs. SUM(settlements.net_amount) by merchant by date
  • Any mismatch → alert + manual review
  1. Idempotent transfers using ref_code:
  • Each settlement has unique ref_code = "SETTLE-{merchantId}-{date}"
  • Bank API receives this as idempotency key
  • If transfer is retried, bank uses same reference → no double transfer

👨‍💻 Hải: Sharp. The reconciliation job and idempotent bank transfers are exactly the safeguards we have. What if the batch job runs but some merchants have no transactions that day?

👨 Khanh: The GROUP BY query naturally excludes merchants with zero transactions — they simply don't appear in the result set. We only process and transfer where net_amount > 0. This is also correct behavior — no transfer needed if they earned nothing.

Live Coding: Merchant Fee Calculator (30 min)

👨‍💻 Hải: Practical problem from our actual system.

*"MoMo charges merchants different fee rates based on their tier and transaction amount. Implement a fee calculator:*

  • BASIC tier: flat 2% on all transactions
  • STANDARD tier: 1.5% for amounts ≤ 1M VND, 1% for amounts > 1M VND
  • PREMIUM tier: 0.8% on all amounts, but minimum fee is 1,000 VND
  • All fees rounded to nearest 100 VND (Vietnamese custom)
  • Maximum fee is 50,000 VND per transaction regardless of tier"

👨 Khanh:

public class MerchantFeeCalculator {

    // VND amounts as long (no decimals)
    private static final long MAX_FEE = 50_000L;
    private static final long MIN_FEE_PREMIUM = 1_000L;
    private static final long STANDARD_THRESHOLD = 1_000_000L;
    private static final long ROUNDING_UNIT = 100L;

    private static final BigDecimal BASIC_RATE = new BigDecimal("0.020");
    private static final BigDecimal STANDARD_RATE_LOW = new BigDecimal("0.015");
    private static final BigDecimal STANDARD_RATE_HIGH = new BigDecimal("0.010");
    private static final BigDecimal PREMIUM_RATE = new BigDecimal("0.008");

    public long calculateFee(long amountVnd, MerchantTier tier) {
        if (amountVnd <= 0) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Amount must be positive");
        }

        long rawFee = switch (tier) {
            case BASIC -> applyRate(amountVnd, BASIC_RATE);
            case STANDARD -> amountVnd <= STANDARD_THRESHOLD
                ? applyRate(amountVnd, STANDARD_RATE_LOW)
                : applyRate(amountVnd, STANDARD_RATE_HIGH);
            case PREMIUM -> Math.max(
                applyRate(amountVnd, PREMIUM_RATE),
                MIN_FEE_PREMIUM
            );
        };

        // Apply maximum cap
        long capped = Math.min(rawFee, MAX_FEE);

        // Round to nearest 100 VND
        return roundToNearest(capped, ROUNDING_UNIT);
    }

    private long applyRate(long amount, BigDecimal rate) {
        return BigDecimal.valueOf(amount)
            .multiply(rate)
            .setScale(0, RoundingMode.HALF_UP)
            .longValue();
    }

    private long roundToNearest(long value, long unit) {
        return ((value + unit / 2) / unit) * unit;
    }
}

// Tests
class MerchantFeeCalculatorTest {

    MerchantFeeCalculator calc = new MerchantFeeCalculator();

    @Test
    void basic_2percent() {
        // 500,000 × 2% = 10,000 → rounds to 10,000
        assertThat(calc.calculateFee(500_000L, BASIC)).isEqualTo(10_000L);
    }

    @Test
    void standard_belowThreshold() {
        // 500,000 × 1.5% = 7,500 → rounds to 7,500
        assertThat(calc.calculateFee(500_000L, STANDARD)).isEqualTo(7_500L);
    }

    @Test
    void standard_aboveThreshold() {
        // 2,000,000 × 1% = 20,000
        assertThat(calc.calculateFee(2_000_000L, STANDARD)).isEqualTo(20_000L);
    }

    @Test
    void premium_minimumFee() {
        // 100,000 × 0.8% = 800 → below min 1,000 → returns 1,000
        assertThat(calc.calculateFee(100_000L, PREMIUM)).isEqualTo(1_000L);
    }

    @Test
    void premium_aboveMinimum() {
        // 1,000,000 × 0.8% = 8,000 → rounds to 8,000
        assertThat(calc.calculateFee(1_000_000L, PREMIUM)).isEqualTo(8_000L);
    }

    @Test
    void maximumFeeCapApplied() {
        // 5,000,000 × 2% = 100,000 → capped at 50,000
        assertThat(calc.calculateFee(5_000_000L, BASIC)).isEqualTo(50_000L);
    }

    @Test
    void roundingToNearest100() {
        // 333,333 × 2% = 6,667 → rounds to 6,700
        assertThat(calc.calculateFee(333_333L, BASIC)).isEqualTo(6_700L);
    }

    @Test
    void zeroAmountThrowsException() {
        assertThatThrownBy(() -> calc.calculateFee(0L, BASIC))
            .isInstanceOf(IllegalArgumentException.class);
    }
}

Design decisions:

  • long for VND amounts — VND never has decimals, long is faster

than BigDecimal and avoids floating-point precision issues

  • Only use BigDecimal for the rate multiplication — that's the

only place we need decimal precision

  • switch expression (Java 14+) — exhaustive, clean, no fall-through
  • 7 test cases — covers all tiers, boundary conditions, cap, rounding, error

👨‍💻 Hải: Excellent. long for VND instead of BigDecimal everywhere — smart because the multiplication still uses BigDecimal for accuracy but the final value is a whole VND amount. Most candidates use BigDecimal throughout which is slower in a hot path.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR (VN) Motivation + salary 10/10 Daily MoMo user, merchant platform insight, equity awareness
Spring Resilience4j full setup 10/10 Correct annotation order, fallback to queue pattern
DB Query optimization 10/10 Composite index, partition, keyset pagination, read replica
DB Schema design 10/10 VND as integer, Snowflake ID, pre-aggregated settlements
Design Settlement system 10/10 Spring Batch, idempotent bank transfer, reconciliation job
Coding Fee calculator 10/10 long for VND, switch expression, 8 comprehensive tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 MoMo Interview Tips

  1. Be a real MoMo user. Know their products — wallet, bill pay, QR, merchant portal
  2. VND = integer, not BigDecimal. Shows fintech domain knowledge
  3. Reconciliation is non-negotiable. Any payment system needs it — mention proactively
  4. Pre-aggregated data for analytics. Never SUM() over millions of rows at query time
  5. Snowflake ID over UUID. Time-ordered, shorter, sortable — payment systems love it
  6. Spring Batch for bulk jobs. MoMo does daily settlement, batch jobs are core
  7. Annotation order in Resilience4j. Many candidates get it wrong
  8. MoMo salary range. Senior: 45-65M gross. Equity is meaningful at their valuation

🎭 Mock Interview #24 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Tiki (Vietnam E-Commerce, Top 3 in VN, HCMC)

Context: Tiki is Vietnam's third-largest e-commerce platform after Shopee and Lazada. Unlike Shopee (Singapore-owned), Tiki is proudly Vietnamese — founded in HCMC in 2010. 20M+ customers, 100K+ sellers, 1M+ products. Known for TikiNOW same-day delivery. Engineering team: 600+. Hiring for the Order & Fulfillment team — the system that takes an order from "placed" to "delivered to customer's door." Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, PostgreSQL, Redis, AWS EKS. Why Tiki is worth targeting: Genuine product company, strong engineering culture, equity program, and growing fast. Senior salary: 40-60M+ gross. Also tests real Agile/product collaboration skills.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR + CULTURE (20 min, Vietnamese)

👩 Linh (HR): Chào anh Khanh! Em là Linh, HR của Tiki. Anh có hay mua hàng trên Tiki không?

👨 Khanh: Có ạ, anh dùng Tiki khá nhiều — đặc biệt là TikiNOW vì giao hàng nhanh, đặt tối hôm nay sáng mai có hàng. Anh hay so sánh với Shopee và thấy Tiki UX bên checkout flow và tracking order khá tốt. Điều đó thực ra là lý do anh quan tâm — cái experience đó cần backend rất solid ở phía sau.

👩 Linh: Tại sao anh muốn join team Order & Fulfillment?

👨 Khanh: Order fulfillment là "spine" của e-commerce — mọi thứ khác đều phục vụ cho cái moment khách hàng nhận được hàng. Anh thấy bài toán kỹ thuật ở đây rất phong phú: xử lý concurrent orders, routing đến warehouse đúng nhất, handle partial fulfillment khi hết hàng, và real-time tracking. Anh có background event-driven architecture ở Sony và banking transactions ở Axon Active — apply vào fulfillment workflow là fit tự nhiên.

👩 Linh: Culture của Tiki là "Tikier" — ownership, move fast, collaborate. Anh nghĩ anh fit không?

👨 Khanh: Anh nghĩ fit. "Move fast" không có nghĩa là careless — ở Sony anh vừa deploy nhanh (2 lần/tuần) vừa maintain quality. "Ownership" thì anh có kinh nghiệm — anh đã từng tự take over CI/CD pipeline khi DevOps engineer nghỉ đột xuất và không để team bị block. Collaborate thì càng tốt — anh thích pair programming và design review hơn là code một mình.

💻 ROUND 2 — JAVA & SPRING (45 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer. Practical focus.

👨‍💻 Minh (Sr Eng): Hi Khanh! Tiki's Order service is high traffic — Black Friday we hit 5K orders/min. I'll test how well you handle concurrency and Spring Boot edge cases.

Java Concurrency & Spring (20 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: **What's the difference between @Async and using CompletableFuture directly? When do you use each?**

👨 Khanh: Both achieve async execution but at different levels of abstraction.

@Async — Spring-managed, annotation-driven:

@Service
public class NotificationService {

    @Async("notificationExecutor")  // runs on a specific thread pool
    public CompletableFuture<Void> sendOrderConfirmation(Order order) {
        emailClient.send(order.getEmail(), buildEmailTemplate(order));
        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(null);
    }
}

// In the calling service:
@Service
public class OrderService {
    private final NotificationService notificationService;

    @Transactional
    public Order placeOrder(OrderRequest req) {
        Order order = orderRepo.save(Order.from(req));
        notificationService.sendOrderConfirmation(order); // fire and forget
        return order;
    }
}

// Thread pool config:
@Bean("notificationExecutor")
public Executor notificationExecutor() {
    ThreadPoolTaskExecutor executor = new ThreadPoolTaskExecutor();
    executor.setCorePoolSize(5);
    executor.setMaxPoolSize(20);
    executor.setQueueCapacity(100);
    executor.setThreadNamePrefix("notification-");
    executor.setRejectedExecutionHandler(new CallerRunsPolicy()); // don't drop tasks
    executor.initialize();
    return executor;
}

CompletableFuture directly — more control, no Spring context needed: 

// When you need to orchestrate multiple async operations
public OrderSummary getOrderSummary(String orderId) {
    CompletableFuture<Order> orderFuture =
        CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> orderRepo.findById(orderId));
    CompletableFuture<List<TrackingEvent>> trackingFuture =
        CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> trackingService.getEvents(orderId));
    CompletableFuture<PaymentStatus> paymentFuture =
        CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> paymentService.getStatus(orderId));

    // Wait for ALL three concurrently
    CompletableFuture.allOf(orderFuture, trackingFuture, paymentFuture).join();

    return new OrderSummary(
        orderFuture.join(),
        trackingFuture.join(),
        paymentFuture.join()
    );
}

When to use each:

Scenario Use
Fire-and-forget (send email, push notification) @Async
Parallel fetch of independent data CompletableFuture.allOf()
Chain of async operations with transformation CompletableFuture.thenApply()/.thenCompose()
Need Spring transaction context in async thread @Async (Spring propagates context)
Non-Spring code (utility class, library) CompletableFuture

Common pitfall with @Async: Same bean self-invocation doesn't work (proxy is bypassed). And the method must be public, on a different bean.

👨‍💻 Minh: **On Black Friday, the order service gets 5K orders/min. How do you protect it from overload while giving the best possible user experience?**

👨 Khanh: Three layers — from edge to database:

Layer 1: Rate limiting at API Gateway.

# AWS API Gateway / Kong rate limit
rate_limit:
  per_user: 10 requests/minute   # prevent bot orders
  per_ip: 30 requests/minute
  global: 10000 requests/minute  # service-level cap

Layer 2: Queue-based order processing.

HTTP Request → Accept immediately → Return orderId
                    │
                    ▼
              Kafka "orders.new"
                    │
                    ▼
            Order Processor (consumers)
            - Validates stock
            - Creates order
            - Publishes "order.confirmed"

This decouples acceptance rate (10K/min possible) from processing rate (limited by DB/downstream). Users get immediate feedback, processing catches up asynchronously.

@PostMapping("/orders")
public ResponseEntity<OrderAcceptedResponse> placeOrder(
        @Valid @RequestBody OrderRequest req,
        @AuthenticationPrincipal UserPrincipal user) {

    String trackingId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();
    // Publish to Kafka — lightweight, fast
    kafkaTemplate.send("orders.new",
        new OrderMessage(trackingId, req, user.getId()));

    return ResponseEntity.accepted()  // 202
        .body(new OrderAcceptedResponse(trackingId,
              "/orders/track/" + trackingId));
}

Layer 3: Database connection pool protection.

spring:
  datasource:
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 20
      connection-timeout: 3000    # 3s timeout — fail fast instead of queue forever
      max-lifetime: 600000

When the pool is full and new requests wait > 3 seconds → SQLTimeoutException → circuit breaker opens → return "Service busy, try again in 30 seconds." Better to fail fast than queue thousands of requests and have everyone wait 2 minutes.

Layer 4: Graceful degradation.

@CircuitBreaker(name = "orderService", fallbackMethod = "orderFallback")
public OrderResult processOrder(OrderMessage msg) {
    return orderProcessor.process(msg);
}

public OrderResult orderFallback(OrderMessage msg, Exception ex) {
    // Move to retry queue — process when load drops
    retryQueue.add(msg, Priority.HIGH);
    return OrderResult.queued("Order received — processing shortly");
}

Real Scenario: Concurrent Stock Reserve (25 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: Real Black Friday problem. *"10,000 users click 'Buy' for the same product that has 100 units in stock. How do you prevent overselling while keeping the checkout fast?"*

👨 Khanh: This is the classic inventory reservation problem. Three approaches with different trade-offs:

Option 1: Database optimistic locking:

-- Check and decrement atomically
UPDATE inventory
SET quantity = quantity - 1,
    version = version + 1
WHERE product_id = 'P123'
  AND quantity >= 1
  AND version = :expectedVersion;
-- If 0 rows affected → version mismatch → retry

Pros: Simple, no external dependency. Cons: At 10K concurrent requests, high retry rate (99% will retry multiple times). For Black Friday, this creates a thundering herd.

Option 2: Redis atomic decrement (what I'd use for Tiki):

// Pre-load stock to Redis at start of Black Friday sale
// Key: "stock:{productId}", Value: 100

private static final String RESERVE_SCRIPT = """
    local stock = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]))
    if stock == nil then return -1 end  -- product not in Redis
    if stock <= 0 then return 0 end     -- out of stock
    redis.call('DECR', KEYS[1])
    return 1  -- success
""";

public ReservationResult reserveStock(String productId, String userId) {
    Long result = redisTemplate.execute(
        new DefaultRedisScript<>(RESERVE_SCRIPT, Long.class),
        List.of("stock:" + productId)
    );

    return switch (result.intValue()) {
        case 1  -> ReservationResult.success();
        case 0  -> ReservationResult.outOfStock();
        case -1 -> ReservationResult.fallbackToDb(); // Redis miss
        default -> throw new IllegalStateException();
    };
}

Redis handles 100K operations/second — far more than the DB can handle. The Lua script is atomic — no race condition possible.

Reconciliation: Every 30 seconds, sync Redis counter with the DB:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
public void syncStockToDb() {
    // Read Redis stock, update DB if different
    // Redis is source of truth during flash sale
    // DB is source of truth at end of day
}

Option 3: Hybrid — Redis for fast path, DB for accuracy:

Request → Check Redis stock (fast, ~1ms)
    ├── Stock available in Redis:
    │       Decrement Redis, add to "pending confirmations"
    │       → Async: verify DB stock, confirm or rollback
    └── Stock = 0 in Redis → Immediately return "Sold out" (no DB hit)

For Tiki's Black Friday, I'd go with Option 2 — Redis atomic Lua script for the hot path. The Lua script prevents overselling. Any discrepancy between Redis and DB is caught by the reconciliation job and handled by inventory ops.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer.

👨‍💻 Việt (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. Design Tiki's **order tracking system.** When a customer places an order, they should see real-time updates: "Order confirmed" → "Packed" → "Picked up by shipper" → "Out for delivery" → "Delivered." 5M orders/day, customers check tracking every few minutes.

👨 Khanh:

Status update sources:

  • Internal events (Tiki warehouse): order picked, packed, dispatched
  • External shipper APIs (GiaoHangNhanh, GiaoHangTietKiem, Ahamove):

webhooks when status changes

  • Delivery driver app (TikiNOW): GPS updates every 30 seconds

Architecture:

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  EVENT SOURCES                                   │
│                                                  │
│  Tiki Warehouse → Kafka "order.warehouse.events" │
│  GHN Webhook   → Tracking API → Kafka            │
│  GHTK Webhook  → Tracking API → Kafka            │
│  Driver App    → Tracking API → Kafka            │
└──────────────────┬───────────────────────────────┘
                   │
                   ▼
         ┌─────────────────┐
         │ Tracking Service │ (consumes all Kafka topics)
         │ - Normalizes     │   → Unified TrackingEvent
         │ - Stores state   │
         └────┬────────┬───┘
              │        │
    ┌─────────▼──┐  ┌──▼──────────────┐
    │  Tracking  │  │  Real-time Push  │
    │  DB        │  │  (WebSocket /    │
    │(PostgreSQL)│  │   SSE / Push)   │
    └────────────┘  └─────────────────┘

Normalized TrackingEvent:

public record TrackingEvent(
    String orderId,
    String shipperId,           // "GHN", "GHTK", "TIKI_NOW"
    TrackingStatus status,      // unified enum
    String rawStatus,           // original status from shipper
    String description,         // human-readable: "Đang giao hàng"
    GeoLocation location,       // nullable — driver GPS
    Instant eventTime
) {}

public enum TrackingStatus {
    ORDER_CONFIRMED,
    WAREHOUSE_PROCESSING,
    PACKED,
    PICKED_UP_BY_SHIPPER,
    IN_TRANSIT,
    OUT_FOR_DELIVERY,
    DELIVERY_ATTEMPTED,         // no one home
    DELIVERED,
    RETURNED                    // failed delivery
}

Each shipper uses different status codes. The Tracking Service normalizes them:

@Component
public class GHNStatusMapper {
    private static final Map<String, TrackingStatus> MAPPING = Map.of(
        "ready_to_pick", PICKED_UP_BY_SHIPPER,
        "picking",       IN_TRANSIT,
        "delivering",    OUT_FOR_DELIVERY,
        "delivered",     DELIVERED,
        "return",        RETURNED
    );

    public TrackingStatus map(String ghnStatus) {
        return MAPPING.getOrDefault(ghnStatus, IN_TRANSIT);
    }
}

Database Schema:

CREATE TABLE order_tracking (
    order_id    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    status      VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,
    description TEXT,
    location    JSONB,
    event_time  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    PRIMARY KEY (order_id, event_time)  -- time-ordered per order
);
CREATE INDEX idx_order_latest ON order_tracking(order_id, event_time DESC);

Real-time Delivery to Customers:

Three options:

Method Best for Latency
SSE (Server-Sent Events) Tracking page updates ~1s
WebSocket Bidirectional (driver map) ~100ms
Push notification Background / mobile ~3s

For the tracking page, I'd use SSE:

@GetMapping(value = "/orders/{id}/tracking/stream",
            produces = MediaType.TEXT_EVENT_STREAM_VALUE)
public SseEmitter streamTracking(@PathVariable String id) {
    SseEmitter emitter = new SseEmitter(300_000L); // 5-minute timeout

    sseRegistry.register(id, emitter);

    // Send current status immediately on connect
    TrackingStatus current = trackingService.getLatest(id);
    emitter.send(SseEmitter.event().data(current));

    return emitter;
}

// When new tracking event comes in:
@EventListener
public void onTrackingUpdate(TrackingEvent event) {
    sseRegistry.getEmitters(event.getOrderId())
        .forEach(emitter -> {
            try {
                emitter.send(SseEmitter.event().data(event));
            } catch (IOException e) {
                sseRegistry.remove(event.getOrderId(), emitter);
            }
        });
}

Scaling SSE connections:

5M orders/day. Assume 20% check tracking actively = 1M concurrent SSE.

  • Each SSE holds one HTTP connection (thread or async)
  • With Spring WebFlux (reactive), one thread handles many SSE connections
  • Or: use sticky sessions (K8s) + Redis pub/sub for cross-pod SSE delivery

Poll fallback for mobile:

// For customers on bad network, polling is more reliable
@GetMapping("/orders/{id}/tracking")
public List<TrackingEvent> getTracking(
        @PathVariable String id,
        @RequestParam(required = false) Instant since) {
    if (since != null) {
        return trackingRepo.findByOrderIdAfter(id, since);
    }
    return trackingRepo.findByOrderId(id); // full history
}

👨‍💻 Việt: Good design. The SSE + Redis pub/sub for cross-pod is exactly what we built. How do you handle out-of-order events from the shipper?

👨 Khanh: Shippers sometimes deliver events out of order — "Delivered" arrives before "Out for delivery." Two rules:

  1. Store all events with their event_time — the table is an

immutable log. Never update, always insert.

  1. Status display uses the event with the LATEST event_time,

not the latest insertion time. The UI shows the correct logical sequence even if events arrived out of order.

  1. Never downgrade status via the API. If we've already shown

"Delivered," we don't regress to "Out for delivery" even if a delayed event arrives:

public void processEvent(TrackingEvent event) {
    TrackingStatus current = trackingRepo.getLatestStatus(event.getOrderId());
    if (current != null && current.ordinal() > event.getStatus().ordinal()) {
        log.warn("Out-of-order event for order {} — storing but not updating display",
            event.getOrderId());
    }
    trackingRepo.save(event); // always store
    // Push to SSE only if it advances the status
    if (current == null || event.getStatus().ordinal() > current.ordinal()) {
        sseRegistry.push(event.getOrderId(), event);
    }
}

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR (VN) Motivation + Tiki product 10/10 TikiNOW user story, fulfillment spine analogy
Spring @Async vs CompletableFuture 10/10 Clear use cases, thread pool config, pitfalls
Concurrency Black Friday overload protection 10/10 4 layers: rate limit, queue, pool, circuit breaker
Inventory Redis Lua atomic reserve 10/10 Lua script prevents oversell, Redis → DB reconciliation
Design Order tracking system 10/10 SSE, status normalization, out-of-order handling

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

📊 Complete Vietnam Top-Tier Map

# Company Type Salary Domain
19 NAB Vietnam Banking Hub 40-60M Account/KYC
22 VNG/ZaloPay Unicorn 50-80M Payments
23 MoMo FinTech 45-65M Merchant Settlement
24 Tiki E-Commerce 40-60M Order Fulfillment

💡 Tiki Interview Tips

  1. Be a Tiki user. TikiNOW, flash sales, seller portal — know the product
  2. Black Friday scenarios. They test concurrent inventory, queue patterns
  3. Redis Lua scripts. Essential for high-concurrency inventory operations
  4. SSE vs WebSocket vs polling. Know when to use each for real-time
  5. Event normalization. Multiple shippers → unified event model
  6. Out-of-order event handling. Common in distributed systems — have an answer
  7. @Async thread pool sizing. Not just "add @Async" — configure the executor

🎭 Mock Interview #25 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Techcombank (Vietnam's Top Digital Bank, HCMC/Hanoi)

Context: Techcombank (Techcombank - TCB) is consistently ranked Vietnam's #1 or #2 bank by profit and digital innovation. Unlike NAB Vietnam (which builds for an Australian bank), Techcombank IS the Vietnamese bank — 13M+ customers, 11M active digital banking users, $5B+ market cap. They've invested $500M+ in technology since 2018 and built a world-class tech team. Hiring for the Digital Banking Platform team — the core banking APIs powering Techcombank app, internet banking, and partner integrations. Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Oracle DB (legacy) + PostgreSQL (new services), Kafka, Redis, OpenShift/K8s. Why top-tier: Techcombank pays competitively, has real product ownership (not outsourcing), and your banking background from Axon Active maps perfectly here. Senior salary: 45-70M+ gross.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR (20 min, Vietnamese)

👩 Thủy (HR): Chào anh Khanh! Em là Thủy bên Human Capital của Techcombank. Anh có tài khoản Techcombank không?

👨 Khanh: Có ạ! Anh dùng app Techcombank — VNPAY QR, chuyển tiền, và gần đây thử tính năng mở tài khoản online. Anh ấn tượng với flow KYC bằng CCCD và video call — cả process dưới 10 phút. So với một số ngân hàng khác, UX của TCB app khá smooth.

👩 Thủy: Tại sao anh muốn join Techcombank, không phải một fintech hay product company khác như MoMo hay VNG?

👨 Khanh: Anh nghĩ banking và fintech là hai thứ khác nhau. MoMo và ZaloPay về cơ bản là payment intermediary — họ move money. Còn Techcombank là nơi tiền "sống" — savings, loans, investments, insurance. Scope của bài toán kỹ thuật rộng hơn nhiều.

Anh có background banking từ Axon Active — làm banking platform cho Valiant Bank Thụy Sĩ. Những gì anh học được ở đó: KYC/AML, double-entry ledger, regulatory compliance, transaction atomicity — apply trực tiếp vào Techcombank. Đây là lý do fit tự nhiên nhất.

Và thành thật mà nói, Techcombank đang build technology thật — không phải outsource. Đây là ngân hàng của Việt Nam, serving người Việt Nam. Impact thực sự.

👩 Thủy: Anh expect gì?

👨 Khanh: Với banking domain knowledge và 5 năm kinh nghiệm, anh expect 50-65M gross. Anh cũng quan tâm đến chính sách phát triển career — Techcombank có tech lead path rõ ràng không?

👩 Thủy: Có, TCB có career ladder từ Senior → Lead → Principal → Architect. Anh pass technical thì mình discuss chi tiết hơn.

💻 ROUND 2 — BANKING DOMAIN + JAVA (55 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer + Domain Architect. Banking-specific.

👨‍💻 Khoa (Sr Eng): Hi Khanh! I'm Khoa. At Techcombank, we care about banking domain knowledge as much as Java skills.

👨‍💼 An (Domain Architect): Hi! I'll focus on architectural thinking for financial systems.

Banking Domain Knowledge (20 min)

👨‍💼 An: **Explain double-entry bookkeeping and why it matters for a banking system. How does it translate to code?**

👨 Khanh: Double-entry bookkeeping is the foundation of all accounting — every financial transaction affects at least two accounts, with total debits always equaling total credits.

The core principle:

Customer transfers 5,000,000 VND from Savings to Checking:

DEBIT  Savings Account (asset decreases):     -5,000,000 VND
CREDIT Checking Account (asset increases):    +5,000,000 VND
Sum: 0 ← debits = credits, always balanced

Why it matters for banking:

  1. Audit completeness — every VND is accounted for, no money

appears or disappears without a matching entry

  1. Error detection — if debits ≠ credits, there's a bug or corruption
  2. Regulatory requirement — Vietnam banking regulations (SBV)

mandate proper ledger accounting

Code implementation:

@Entity
@Table(name = "journal_entries")
public class JournalEntry {
    @Id
    private UUID id;

    private UUID transactionId;  // groups related entries

    private String accountId;    // which account

    @Enumerated(EnumType.STRING)
    private EntryType type;      // DEBIT or CREDIT

    private BigDecimal amount;   // always positive

    private BigDecimal balanceAfter; // account balance after this entry

    private String description;

    private Instant createdAt;
}

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class LedgerService {

    private final JournalEntryRepository journalRepo;
    private final AccountRepository accountRepo;

    @Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
    public UUID recordTransfer(String fromAccountId,
                               String toAccountId,
                               BigDecimal amount,
                               String description) {
        UUID txnId = UUID.randomUUID();

        // Lock accounts in consistent order (prevent deadlock)
        String firstId = fromAccountId.compareTo(toAccountId) < 0
            ? fromAccountId : toAccountId;
        String secondId = fromAccountId.equals(firstId)
            ? toAccountId : fromAccountId;

        Account fromAcc = accountRepo.findByIdForUpdate(firstId.equals(fromAccountId)
            ? fromAccountId : toAccountId);
        Account toAcc = accountRepo.findByIdForUpdate(firstId.equals(fromAccountId)
            ? toAccountId : fromAccountId);

        // Validate
        if (fromAcc.getBalance().compareTo(amount) < 0) {
            throw new InsufficientFundsException(fromAccountId, amount);
        }

        // Debit source
        fromAcc.debit(amount);
        BigDecimal fromBalance = fromAcc.getBalance();

        // Credit destination
        toAcc.credit(amount);
        BigDecimal toBalance = toAcc.getBalance();

        // Record both journal entries
        journalRepo.save(new JournalEntry(txnId, fromAccountId,
            DEBIT, amount, fromBalance, description));
        journalRepo.save(new JournalEntry(txnId, toAccountId,
            CREDIT, amount, toBalance, description));

        // Verify: total debits = total credits for this transaction
        assertBalanced(txnId);

        accountRepo.saveAll(List.of(fromAcc, toAcc));
        return txnId;
    }

    private void assertBalanced(UUID txnId) {
        List<JournalEntry> entries = journalRepo.findByTransactionId(txnId);
        BigDecimal debits = entries.stream()
            .filter(e -> e.getType() == DEBIT)
            .map(JournalEntry::getAmount)
            .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);
        BigDecimal credits = entries.stream()
            .filter(e -> e.getType() == CREDIT)
            .map(JournalEntry::getAmount)
            .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);

        if (debits.compareTo(credits) != 0) {
            throw new LedgerImbalanceException(txnId, debits, credits);
        }
    }
}

Database-level protection:

-- Balance can never go negative (for regular accounts)
ALTER TABLE accounts ADD CONSTRAINT chk_positive_balance
    CHECK (balance >= 0);

-- Reconciliation query (runs nightly)
-- Total of all DEBIT entries must equal total of all CREDIT entries
SELECT
    SUM(CASE WHEN type='DEBIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_debits,
    SUM(CASE WHEN type='CREDIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS total_credits,
    SUM(CASE WHEN type='DEBIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) -
    SUM(CASE WHEN type='CREDIT' THEN amount ELSE 0 END) AS imbalance
FROM journal_entries
WHERE DATE(created_at) = CURRENT_DATE;
-- imbalance must always = 0

👨‍💼 An: That lock ordering for deadlock prevention — most candidates miss that detail. What's the Isolation.SERIALIZABLE for?

👨 Khanh: SERIALIZABLE is the strictest isolation level. It prevents phantom reads — where a transaction reads a set of rows, another transaction inserts a row that would match, and the first transaction would see a different result if it re-read.

For a bank transfer, REPEATABLE_READ (MySQL default) is usually sufficient, but for complex multi-account operations (like batch payroll disbursement), SERIALIZABLE ensures total isolation.

The trade-off: SERIALIZABLE has higher lock contention. For simple 2-account transfers, REPEATABLE_READ + SELECT FOR UPDATE is often better for performance.

👨‍💻 Khoa: **Techcombank has a legacy Oracle database and new PostgreSQL services. How do you migrate data from Oracle to Postgres without downtime?**

👨 Khanh: Expand-and-Contract pattern (Strangler Fig for data):

Phase 1: Expand — dual-write:

@Service
public class AccountService {

    private final OracleAccountRepo oracleRepo;   // legacy
    private final PostgresAccountRepo postgresRepo; // new
    private final FeatureFlags flags;

    @Transactional
    public Account updateAccount(Account account) {
        // Write to Oracle (source of truth during migration)
        Account saved = oracleRepo.save(account);

        // Also write to Postgres (best-effort, catch errors)
        try {
            postgresRepo.save(account);
        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("Postgres dual-write failed for account {}", account.getId(), e);
            // Don't fail the request — Oracle succeeded
        }

        return saved;
    }

    public Account getAccount(String id) {
        if (flags.isEnabled("USE_POSTGRES_READ")) {
            // Shadow read: compare Oracle vs Postgres for validation
            Account oracle = oracleRepo.findById(id);
            Account postgres = postgresRepo.findById(id);
            if (!oracle.equals(postgres)) {
                log.warn("Data mismatch for account {}", id);
                metricsService.recordMismatch();
            }
            return oracle; // still return Oracle during validation phase
        }
        return oracleRepo.findById(id);
    }
}

Phase 2: Validate — shadow reads:

  • Run both reads, compare results, log discrepancies
  • Fix any sync bugs until mismatch rate = 0% for 2 weeks

Phase 3: Contract — cut over reads:

  • Enable USE_POSTGRES_READ feature flag (100% of reads from Postgres)
  • Keep dual-writes for 1 more month (rollback capability)

Phase 4: Remove — drop Oracle dependency:

  • Remove dual-write code
  • Archive Oracle data

Tools: Debezium CDC (Change Data Capture) to replicate Oracle changes to Postgres in real-time during migration — more reliable than dual-write for high-volume tables.

Java Production Patterns (35 min)

👨‍💻 Khoa: **Show me how you implement proper connection pool management in a banking service. What metrics do you monitor?**

👨 Khanh:

# application.yml — HikariCP for PostgreSQL
spring:
  datasource:
    url: jdbc:postgresql://primary-db:5432/tcb_banking
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 20           # max DB connections
      minimum-idle: 5                 # always-warm connections
      connection-timeout: 3000        # fail fast if no connection in 3s
      idle-timeout: 600000            # release idle connections after 10min
      max-lifetime: 1800000           # replace connections after 30min
      keepalive-time: 60000           # heartbeat every 60s (prevents firewall drops)
      pool-name: TCB-Primary-Pool
      # Critical for banking: validate connection before use
      connection-test-query: SELECT 1
      validation-timeout: 1000

Monitoring in production:

@Configuration
public class PoolMetricsConfig {

    @Bean
    public MeterRegistryCustomizer<MeterRegistry> hikariMetrics(DataSource dataSource) {
        return registry -> {
            HikariDataSource hikari = dataSource.unwrap(HikariDataSource.class);
            new HikariCPMetrics(hikari.getHikariPoolMXBean())
                .bindTo(registry);
        };
    }
}

// Key metrics to alert on:
// hikaricp.connections.active > 18/20 (90% pool used) → scale up or optimize
// hikaricp.connections.pending > 0 → threads waiting for connections
// hikaricp.connections.timeout → connections failing to acquire → P1 alert
// hikaricp.connections.creation.timeout → DB not reachable

Connection pool sizing rule:

Pool size = (core_count × 2) + effective_spindle_count

For a 4-core app server connecting to cloud DB:
  Pool size ≈ (4 × 2) + 1 = 9 → round to 10

More connections beyond the formula causes:
  - DB CPU wasted on connection management
  - Lock contention increases
  - Latency gets WORSE (counter-intuitive but true)

Read/Write split for banking:

// Route read-heavy queries to replica
@Configuration
public class DataSourceConfig {

    @Bean
    @Primary
    public DataSource routingDataSource(
            @Qualifier("primaryDs") DataSource primary,
            @Qualifier("replicaDs") DataSource replica) {

        Map<Object, Object> dataSources = Map.of(
            "primary", primary,
            "replica", replica
        );

        AbstractRoutingDataSource routing = new AbstractRoutingDataSource() {
            @Override
            protected Object determineCurrentLookupKey() {
                // Route read-only transactions to replica
                return TransactionSynchronizationManager.isCurrentTransactionReadOnly()
                    ? "replica" : "primary";
            }
        };
        routing.setTargetDataSources(dataSources);
        routing.setDefaultTargetDataSource(primary);
        return routing;
    }
}

// Usage: @Transactional(readOnly = true) → automatically routes to replica
@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public AccountStatement getStatement(String accountId, YearMonth month) {
    // Hits replica — no impact on primary writes
}

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer.

👨‍💻 Minh (Principal Eng): Design Techcombank's **fund transfer service.** Support: intrabank (between TCB accounts) and interbank (to other banks via NAPAS, Vietnam's interbank network). Scale: 2M transfers/day, 99.99% uptime SLA, T+0 for intrabank, T+0 or T+1 for interbank depending on amount and time.

👨 Khanh:

Two transfer modes:

INTRABANK (A → B, both TCB accounts):
  Atomic ledger update → instant (T+0)
  No external dependency

INTERBANK (TCB account → VietcomBank account):
  TCB debit → NAPAS → Destination Bank credit
  NAPAS response time: 1-30 seconds during business hours
  Weekend/after-hours: batched T+1

Architecture:

┌──────────────┐
│ Transfer API │  (validates, rate limits, deduplicates)
└──────┬───────┘
       │
       ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Transfer Orchestrator                │
│                                              │
│  IF intrabank → LedgerService (atomic)       │
│  IF interbank → NAPAS Gateway + Saga         │
└──────┬───────────────────┬────────────────────┘
       │                   │
┌──────▼──────┐     ┌──────▼──────┐
│  Ledger DB  │     │ NAPAS Client│
│ (PostgreSQL)│     │ (external)  │
└─────────────┘     └──────┬──────┘
                           │ async response
                    ┌──────▼──────┐
                    │  Transfer   │
                    │  State DB   │ (tracks interbank status)
                    └─────────────┘

Intrabank Transfer (simple + fast):

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.REPEATABLE_READ)
public TransferResult intraBankTransfer(TransferRequest req) {
    // 1. Idempotency check
    Optional<Transfer> existing = transferRepo.findByRequestId(req.getRequestId());
    if (existing.isPresent()) return TransferResult.from(existing.get());

    // 2. Debit/Credit (with deadlock prevention via account ID ordering)
    ledgerService.recordTransfer(req.getFromAccount(), req.getToAccount(),
        req.getAmount(), req.getDescription());

    // 3. Save transfer record
    Transfer transfer = transferRepo.save(Transfer.intrabank(req, SUCCESS));

    // 4. Async notification
    eventPublisher.publishEvent(new TransferCompletedEvent(transfer));

    return TransferResult.success(transfer);
}

Interbank Transfer (NAPAS, Saga pattern):

// Saga: Debit TCB → Send to NAPAS → Confirm
@Service
public class InterBankTransferSaga {

    @Transactional
    public TransferResult start(TransferRequest req) {
        // Step 1: Reserve funds (debit with RESERVED status)
        Transfer transfer = Transfer.interbank(req, FUNDS_RESERVED);
        ledgerService.debit(req.getFromAccount(), req.getAmount(), transfer.getId());
        transferRepo.save(transfer);

        // Step 2: Send to NAPAS asynchronously
        String napasRef = napasGateway.initiate(NapasRequest.from(req, transfer.getId()));
        transfer.setNapasReference(napasRef);
        transfer.setStatus(NAPAS_PENDING);
        transferRepo.save(transfer);

        return TransferResult.pending(transfer.getId(), "Processing with NAPAS");
    }

    // Called by NAPAS webhook when result comes back
    @Transactional
    public void handleNapasCallback(NapasCallback callback) {
        Transfer transfer = transferRepo.findByNapasReference(callback.getRef())
            .orElseThrow();

        if (callback.isSuccess()) {
            transfer.setStatus(COMPLETED);
            // Credit destination bank account
            ledgerService.creditExternal(transfer, callback.getDestinationConfirmation());
            notificationService.notifySuccess(transfer);
        } else {
            // Compensating transaction: reverse the debit
            transfer.setStatus(FAILED);
            ledgerService.credit(transfer.getFromAccount(),
                transfer.getAmount(), "Reversal: " + callback.getFailureReason());
            notificationService.notifyFailure(transfer, callback.getFailureReason());
        }

        transferRepo.save(transfer);
    }
}

Handling NAPAS timeout (no callback within 30s):

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 60_000)
public void checkPendingNapasTransfers() {
    LocalDateTime cutoff = LocalDateTime.now().minusMinutes(5);
    List<Transfer> stuck = transferRepo.findByStatusAndCreatedAtBefore(
        NAPAS_PENDING, cutoff
    );

    for (Transfer t : stuck) {
        NapasStatusResponse status = napasGateway.queryStatus(t.getNapasReference());
        if (status.isCompleted()) {
            handleNapasCallback(NapasCallback.success(t.getNapasReference(), status));
        } else if (status.isFailed()) {
            handleNapasCallback(NapasCallback.failed(t.getNapasReference(), status));
        }
        // Still pending: leave for next check cycle
    }
}

High Availability (99.99% SLA = 52 min downtime/year):

Component HA Strategy
Transfer API 3+ pods, circuit breaker
PostgreSQL Aurora with Multi-AZ
NAPAS Gateway Primary + backup NAPAS connection
Kafka (events) 3-broker cluster, replication factor 3
Redis Redis Sentinel or Cluster

99.99% checklist:

  • Zero-downtime deployments (rolling updates)
  • Database failover < 30s (Aurora automatic)
  • Circuit breaker on NAPAS calls (open → use queue, process when recovered)
  • Active health checks with auto-remediation

👨‍💻 Minh: Strong architecture. What's your approach to the regulatory requirement that every transfer must be auditable for 7 years?

👨 Khanh: Three layers:

  1. Immutable journal entries — journal_entries table has

no UPDATE or DELETE operations. Only INSERTs. DB-level policy (revoke UPDATE/DELETE privileges from the application user).

  1. Cold storage archival — transfers older than 2 years move

to S3 (Parquet format) via a nightly job. Retained 7+ years. Queryable via Athena for compliance queries.

  1. Audit log table — separate from journal entries. Logs EVERY

API call that touches a transfer: who requested, what changed, from which IP, at what time. Even failed attempts are logged.

CREATE TABLE transfer_audit_log (
    id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    transfer_id UUID,
    action      VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,  -- 'INITIATE', 'NAPAS_CALLBACK', 'QUERY'
    actor_id    VARCHAR(50),           -- user or system making the action
    actor_ip    INET,
    request_payload  JSONB,
    response_payload JSONB,
    created_at  TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW()
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);    -- partition by month for query performance
-- NEVER delete from this table. Archive to S3 after 2 years.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR (VN) Banking domain motivation 10/10 "Where money lives" vs payment intermediary
Banking Double-entry bookkeeping 10/10 Code implementation + assertBalanced + reconciliation
Banking Oracle → Postgres migration 10/10 Expand-and-contract + Debezium CDC
Java Connection pool management 10/10 Hikari config, sizing formula, read/write routing
Design Fund transfer service 10/10 Intrabank simple, interbank Saga, NAPAS timeout handler
Compliance 7-year audit trail 10/10 Immutable journal, cold storage, audit log table

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Techcombank Interview Tips

  1. Banking domain is required. Double-entry, KYC, AML, NAPAS — know the vocabulary
  2. "Where money lives" — frame yourself as a banker, not just a Java engineer
  3. Oracle is still present. They have legacy Oracle — don't assume everything is modern
  4. 7-year audit requirement. Vietnam SBV regulation — always mention immutable ledger
  5. NAPAS = Vietnam's SWIFT. Know that interbank transfers go through NAPAS
  6. Saga for interbank. Intrabank is one transaction. Interbank is distributed — needs Saga
  7. Connection pool sizing formula. Shows you understand DB performance, not just code
  8. Techcombank salary. Senior: 45-70M gross. They match top-tier market

🎭 Mock Interview #26 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Sea Group — Shopee Vietnam (Singapore HQ, HCMC Engineering Hub)
