Context: Sea Group (NYSE: SE) is Southeast Asia's largest tech company — Shopee (e-commerce), SeaMoney (digital finance), Garena (gaming). Market cap $30B+. HCMC engineering hub has 1000+ engineers. This is the highest-paying tech employer in Vietnam after Google/Meta offices. Hiring for the Shopee Platform Engineering team — core infrastructure services used by all Shopee product teams: service mesh, internal tooling, developer platforms, observability. This is a platform/infra-leaning role, not a product feature team. Why Sea Group is the hardest Vietnam interview: - They interview like a mid-tier FAANG — system design is at real scale - Strong emphasis on observability, reliability, and platform thinking - Engineers report to Singapore — English must be excellent - Salary: 60-100M+ gross for senior (highest in Vietnam market) - The technical bar is closest to Google/Amazon of all Vietnam employers

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (25 min, English)

👩 Rachel (Recruiter, Singapore): Hi Khanh! I'm Rachel from Sea Group Talent. This role is on the Platform Engineering team in HCMC, reporting to a Staff Engineer in Singapore. Everything is in English. Tell me about yourself.

👨 Khanh: Hi Rachel! I'm Khanh, Senior Java Backend Engineer with nearly 5 years experience. My focus areas:

Architecturally: I design and build microservices on AWS — Spring Boot, Kafka, PostgreSQL, Redis, K8s. I've led migrations from monolith to microservices, built event-driven payment systems, and optimized services from 800ms to 120ms P99.

Domain-wise: I have depth in fintech and e-commerce adjacent systems — payments at Sony, banking at Axon Active, healthcare at TMA.

What draws me to Sea Group specifically: Platform Engineering is different from product feature work. You build the foundation that 100 other engineers build on top of. If I improve the internal service framework by 10%, every team gets that 10%. That multiplier effect is what excites me about platform roles.

👩 Rachel: What's your experience with observability and monitoring?

👨 Khanh: At Sony, I own the observability stack for our services. We use Micrometer + CloudWatch for metrics, CloudWatch Logs with structured JSON logging, and X-Ray for distributed tracing. I've set up the golden signals monitoring — latency, error rate, traffic, saturation — with alerting thresholds per service.

I've also gone beyond tooling: I wrote our team's observability runbook, created Grafana dashboards for on-call engineers, and introduced structured logging standards so log queries are consistent across services.

One real impact: I added a custom metric payment.processing.duration with percentile buckets. That metric caught a P99 regression 30 minutes before users started complaining, and we rolled back before it became a customer-visible incident.

👩 Rachel: Salary expectation?

👨 Khanh: I've done market research — Sea Group is the top-paying employer in HCMC for senior engineers. For a platform role with my background, I'm targeting 70-85M gross as a starting point. I'm open to discussing the full package including equity and benefits.

👩 Rachel: That's in range. Four technical rounds — 2 coding, 1 system design, 1 with VP. This is a demanding process. Still interested?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. The high bar means the team is exceptional. That's exactly where I want to work.

💻 ROUND 2 — CODING ROUND 1 (45 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer. CoderPad.

👨‍💻 Wei (Sr Eng, Singapore): Hi Khanh. Two problems today. Practical engineering problems, not pure algorithms.

Problem 1: Implement a Consistent Hash Ring (25 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: *"In Shopee's service mesh, we distribute requests across backend instances using consistent hashing. Implement a ConsistentHashRing that:*

  • addNode(String node) — add a server node
  • removeNode(String node) — remove a server node
  • getNode(String key) — return which node handles this key
  • When a node is added/removed, only ~1/n of keys remapped
  • Use virtual nodes (150 per physical node) for even distribution"

👨 Khanh: Consistent hashing maps both nodes and keys to a circular ring using hash functions. For a given key, we find the nearest node clockwise on the ring.

Virtual nodes (vnodes) place each physical node at multiple points on the ring — this makes load distribution much more uniform.

public class ConsistentHashRing {

    private final TreeMap<Long, String> ring = new TreeMap<>();
    private final int virtualNodes;
    private final MessageDigest md5;

    public ConsistentHashRing(int virtualNodes) {
        this.virtualNodes = virtualNodes;
        try {
            this.md5 = MessageDigest.getInstance("MD5");
        } catch (NoSuchAlgorithmException e) {
            throw new RuntimeException(e);
        }
    }

    public synchronized void addNode(String node) {
        for (int i = 0; i < virtualNodes; i++) {
            long hash = hash(node + "#vnode-" + i);
            ring.put(hash, node);
        }
    }

    public synchronized void removeNode(String node) {
        for (int i = 0; i < virtualNodes; i++) {
            long hash = hash(node + "#vnode-" + i);
            ring.remove(hash);
        }
    }

    public synchronized String getNode(String key) {
        if (ring.isEmpty()) {
            throw new IllegalStateException("No nodes in the ring");
        }
        long hash = hash(key);

        // Find the first node with hash >= key's hash (clockwise)
        Map.Entry<Long, String> entry = ring.ceilingEntry(hash);

        // Wrap around: if no node found clockwise, use the first node
        if (entry == null) {
            entry = ring.firstEntry();
        }

        return entry.getValue();
    }

    private long hash(String key) {
        byte[] digest = md5.digest(key.getBytes(StandardCharsets.UTF_8));
        // Use first 8 bytes as long
        long result = 0;
        for (int i = 0; i < 8; i++) {
            result = (result << 8) | (digest[i] & 0xFF);
        }
        return result;
    }

    // Utility: show distribution across nodes
    public Map<String, Long> getDistribution() {
        return ring.values().stream()
            .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(v -> v, Collectors.counting()));
    }
}

Trace:

addNode("server1") → places 150 vnodes for server1 on ring
addNode("server2") → places 150 vnodes for server2 on ring
addNode("server3") → places 150 vnodes for server3 on ring

getNode("user-123") → hash("user-123") = 5432...
  → ring.ceilingEntry(5432) → "server2" (nearest clockwise)

removeNode("server2"):
  → removes all 150 server2 vnodes
  → requests that were going to server2 now go to server3 (next clockwise)
  → server1 and server3 requests unaffected ✓

Why 150 virtual nodes? With 150 vnodes per physical node and 3 servers:

  • 450 total points on the ring
  • Statistical distribution becomes very uniform (σ² is low)
  • Too few vnodes → uneven load (some nodes get 2x traffic of others)
  • Too many → memory overhead

Why synchronized? Ring operations must be atomic — concurrent addNode + getNode could cause race conditions on the TreeMap. For production, use ReadWriteLock (multiple concurrent reads, exclusive writes): 

private final ReadWriteLock lock = new ReentrantReadWriteLock();

public String getNode(String key) {
    lock.readLock().lock();
    try {
        // ... read operation
    } finally {
        lock.readLock().unlock();
    }
}

public void addNode(String node) {
    lock.writeLock().lock();
    try {
        // ... write operation
    } finally {
        lock.writeLock().unlock();
    }
}

Problem 2: Design a Rate Limiter for Internal APIs (20 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: *"Shopee's internal service mesh needs per-service rate limiting. Implement a SlidingWindowRateLimiter that limits requests per service per second. Thread-safe, no external dependencies."*

Limit: 100 requests/second per serviceId
isAllowed("payment-service") → true/false

👨 Khanh: I'll use a sliding window with a circular buffer — O(1) time and space per check.

public class SlidingWindowRateLimiter {

    private final int maxRequests;
    private final long windowMs;

    // Per-service tracking
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, ServiceWindow> windows =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public SlidingWindowRateLimiter(int maxRequests, long windowMs) {
        this.maxRequests = maxRequests;
        this.windowMs = windowMs;
    }

    public boolean isAllowed(String serviceId) {
        ServiceWindow window = windows.computeIfAbsent(
            serviceId, k -> new ServiceWindow(maxRequests)
        );
        return window.tryAcquire(windowMs);
    }

    private static class ServiceWindow {
        private final long[] timestamps; // circular buffer
        private int head = 0;            // oldest entry
        private int count = 0;           // entries in window
        private final int capacity;
        private final Object lock = new Object();

        ServiceWindow(int capacity) {
            this.capacity = capacity;
            this.timestamps = new long[capacity];
        }

        boolean tryAcquire(long windowMs) {
            synchronized (lock) {
                long now = System.currentTimeMillis();
                long windowStart = now - windowMs;

                // Remove expired entries from the front
                while (count > 0 && timestamps[head] <= windowStart) {
                    head = (head + 1) % capacity;
                    count--;
                }

                if (count >= capacity) {
                    return false; // window full — rate limit exceeded
                }

                // Add current timestamp at tail
                int tail = (head + count) % capacity;
                timestamps[tail] = now;
                count++;
                return true;
            }
        }
    }
}

Test:

@Test
void shouldAllowUpToLimit() {
    var limiter = new SlidingWindowRateLimiter(5, 1000);
    for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) {
        assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-A")).isTrue();
    }
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-A")).isFalse(); // 6th denied
}

@Test
void shouldAllowAfterWindowExpires() throws InterruptedException {
    var limiter = new SlidingWindowRateLimiter(2, 100); // 2 req per 100ms
    limiter.isAllowed("svc");
    limiter.isAllowed("svc");
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc")).isFalse();

    Thread.sleep(150); // wait for window to expire
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc")).isTrue(); // window reset
}

@Test
void shouldIsolatePerService() {
    var limiter = new SlidingWindowRateLimiter(1, 1000);
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-A")).isTrue();
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-A")).isFalse();
    assertThat(limiter.isAllowed("svc-B")).isTrue(); // different service
}

👨‍💻 Wei: Clean. The circular buffer avoids list allocations. Strong solutions for both problems.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Platform team.

👨‍💻 Ryan (Staff Eng, Singapore): Design Shopee's **internal observability platform.** Every service (500+) must emit metrics, logs, and traces. Platform team provides the infrastructure. 50K metrics/second, 1M log lines/second, engineers must be able to debug any incident in < 5 minutes.

👨 Khanh:

Three Pillars of Observability:

METRICS   → "Is the system healthy?"  (numbers over time)
LOGS      → "What happened?"          (events with context)
TRACES    → "Where did time go?"      (request flow across services)

Architecture — each pillar has its own pipeline:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Services (500+)                                        │
│  Java agent (auto-instruments): Micrometer + OTel SDK  │
└───┬─────────────────┬──────────────────────┬────────────┘
    │ METRICS          │ LOGS                 │ TRACES
    ▼                  ▼                      ▼
┌───────────┐   ┌─────────────┐       ┌─────────────┐
│Prometheus │   │ Kafka       │       │ Kafka       │
│Pushgateway│   │"logs.raw"   │       │"traces.raw" │
│or OTel    │   └──────┬──────┘       └──────┬──────┘
│Collector  │          │                      │
└─────┬─────┘          ▼                      ▼
      │         ┌─────────────┐       ┌─────────────┐
      ▼         │ Log         │       │ Trace       │
┌──────────┐    │ Processor   │       │ Processor   │
│ Thanos   │    │(parse, tag, │       │(sample,     │
│(long-term│    │ enrich)     │       │ store)      │
│ metrics  │    └──────┬──────┘       └──────┬──────┘
│ storage) │           │                     │
└──────────┘           ▼                     ▼
      │         ┌─────────────┐       ┌─────────────┐
      │         │Elasticsearch│       │  Jaeger /   │
      │         │(log storage │       │  Tempo      │
      │         │& search)    │       │  (trace DB) │
      │         └─────────────┘       └─────────────┘
      │                 │                     │
      └─────────────────┴─────────────────────┘
                        │
                   ┌────▼────┐
                   │ Grafana │  (unified dashboard: metrics + logs + traces)
                   └─────────┘

Metrics Pipeline (50K/sec):

// Service-side: auto-instrumented with Micrometer
@Configuration
public class MetricsConfig {
    @Bean
    MeterRegistryCustomizer<MeterRegistry> commonTags(
            @Value("${spring.application.name}") String appName) {
        return r -> r.config()
            .commonTags("service", appName, "env", "prod");
        // Auto-adds service + env to every metric
    }
}

// Custom business metric example
@Service
public class OrderService {
    private final Counter orderCounter;
    private final Timer orderProcessingTime;

    public OrderService(MeterRegistry registry) {
        orderCounter = Counter.builder("shopee.orders.total")
            .tag("status", "created")
            .register(registry);
        orderProcessingTime = Timer.builder("shopee.orders.processing.time")
            .percentiles(0.5, 0.95, 0.99)
            .register(registry);
    }
}

Log Pipeline (1M lines/sec):

Key design: structured JSON logging — every log line is parseable.

// logback-spring.xml — structured JSON output
// {"timestamp":"2024-01-15T10:30:00Z","level":"INFO",
//  "service":"order-service","traceId":"abc123","message":"Order created",
//  "orderId":"ORD-456","userId":"USR-789"}

@Slf4j
@Service
public class OrderService {
    public Order createOrder(OrderRequest req) {
        log.info("Order created",
            // Structured fields via MDC or SLF4J key-value
            kv("orderId", order.getId()),
            kv("userId", req.getUserId()),
            kv("amount", req.getAmount())
        );
    }
}

Kafka as the log transport: services → Kafka → Logstash processor → Elasticsearch. Kafka decouples: if Elasticsearch is slow, services don't block. Kafka retains logs for reprocessing if needed.

Trace Pipeline:

// Auto-instrumented via OpenTelemetry Java agent
// No code changes needed — agent instruments Spring Boot, Kafka, HTTP clients

// Just add to JVM args:
// -javaagent:opentelemetry-javaagent.jar
// -Dotel.service.name=order-service
// -Dotel.exporter.otlp.endpoint=http://collector:4317

Sampling strategy:

  • 100% sampling for errors
  • 10% sampling for normal requests (reduce storage)
  • 100% for requests > 1s (slow requests always traced)

Correlating the three pillars:

Every request gets a traceId (from OTel).
traceId is added to:
  - All log entries (via MDC: MDC.put("traceId", span.getTraceId()))
  - All metrics tags (tag: "traceId" on error metrics)
  - The trace itself

In Grafana:
  Engineer sees a spike in error rate (METRIC)
  → Clicks to see error logs for that time window (LOG)
  → Picks a specific error log → clicks traceId (TRACE)
  → Sees the full request journey across 5 services
  → Identifies which service and which line failed
  Total time: < 5 minutes ✓

Scaling to 500 services:

Concern Solution
Kafka ingestion 20-partition topics, consumer groups per region
Elasticsearch 30-node cluster, index per day, hot-warm-cold tiers
Thanos metrics Object storage (S3) for long-term, Prometheus for recent
Grafana Separate dashboard per team, shared platform dashboards
Alert fatigue Alert on symptoms (user-visible), not causes (CPU)

Onboarding new services:

# New service just needs these in their K8s deployment:
annotations:
  prometheus.io/scrape: "true"
  prometheus.io/port: "8080"
  prometheus.io/path: "/actuator/prometheus"
# OTel agent auto-attached via K8s mutating webhook
# Logs auto-collected by Fluentd DaemonSet
# Zero code changes needed from service teams

👨‍💻 Ryan: That Grafana correlation flow — metric → log → trace in < 5 minutes — is exactly our goal. The auto-instrumentation via K8s webhook means service teams don't have to do anything. Strong.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR Platform role motivation 10/10 "Multiplier effect" framing — platform thinking
HR Observability experience 10/10 Custom metric caught regression 30 min early
Coding 1 Consistent hash ring 10/10 Virtual nodes, circular ring, ReadWriteLock
Coding 2 Sliding window rate limiter 10/10 Circular buffer O(1), per-service isolation
Design Observability platform 10/10 3 pillars, structured logs, trace correlation
Auto-instrumentation K8s webhook zero-config 10/10 Service teams do nothing — platform handles it

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Sea Group / Shopee Interview Tips

  1. Platform thinking. "My change helps 100 teams" > "my feature helps 10K users"
  2. Observability is core. They expect golden signals, trace correlation, alert design
  3. Consistent hashing is a classic platform interview question — know it cold
  4. Structured logging. JSON logs with traceId/spanId — not free-text log.info()
  5. OpenTelemetry — the standard. Know: metrics (Micrometer), logs (Logback), traces (OTel SDK)
  6. Sea Group is English-only. Communication quality matters as much as technical
  7. Salary negotiation. 60-100M range — anchor high (70-85M), they expect negotiation
  8. 4 rounds. Prepare for the long process — they're thorough

📊 FINAL: Complete Vietnam Top-Tier Mock Map

# Company Type Salary Key Topics
19 NAB Vietnam Banking Hub 40-60M KYC, Envers, state machine
22 VNG/ZaloPay Unicorn 50-80M QR payment, Lua, Java concurrency
23 MoMo FinTech 45-65M Spring Batch settlement, fee calc
24 Tiki E-Commerce 40-60M Inventory reserve, SSE tracking
25 Techcombank Digital Bank 45-70M Double-entry, NAPAS, HikariCP
26 Sea Group FAANG-tier 60-100M Consistent hash, observability

🎭 Mock Interview #27 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Got It (EdTech, Vietnamese Startup, HCMC/Hanoi/US)

Context: Got It is Vietnam's most successful EdTech startup. Products: Got It Study (tutoring platform connecting 5M students with 50K tutors in the US/Vietnam), Trada (visual commerce AI for e-commerce). Backed by $25M+ funding. Offices in HCMC, Hanoi, and San Francisco. Hiring for the Core Platform team — the matching engine that connects students to available tutors in real-time, handles session management, and manages payments across USD (US students) and VND (Vietnam students). Why Got It is interesting: - True cross-border product (US + Vietnam users simultaneously) - Real-time matching is a technically interesting problem - Multi-currency payments (USD + VND in same platform) - Strong engineering culture — CTO is ex-Amazon - Senior salary: 40-60M + equity with potential upside - English-first (US market is primary)

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Linh (HR): Hi Khanh! I'm Linh, People Operations. Got It is English-first — our US team works directly with the Vietnam team. How's your English communication in a fast-paced startup?

👨 Khanh: Very comfortable — at Sony, I've been communicating directly with Japanese stakeholders in English daily for 2 years. I write technical documentation, run design reviews, and have async conversations with engineers in different time zones. Startup pace is something I actively want — I find structured corporate processes slow me down. I prefer shipping and iterating.

👩 Linh: Got It's culture is "builder mindset" — you own the feature from design to deployment. No hand-offs. What does that mean to you?

👨 Khanh: To me, ownership means I can't say "that's not my job." If a feature I shipped breaks at 2 AM, it's my incident. If the database schema I designed makes queries slow 6 months later, I own fixing it.

At Sony, I once took ownership of our entire CI/CD pipeline when our DevOps engineer left — not because I was asked to, but because the team was blocked and I had enough knowledge to help. That's the builder mentality I operate by.

👩 Linh: Why EdTech? You have fintech background.

👨 Khanh: Honest answer: the technical problems are what attract me, not just the domain. Real-time matching at scale, multi-currency payments, live tutoring session management — these are genuinely hard distributed systems problems.

And EdTech has a clear social impact angle. In Vietnam, access to quality education is often limited by geography or cost. Got It removes those barriers digitally. That mission matters to me.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer (ex-Amazon).

👨‍💻 David (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh. I'm David, ex-Amazon. No fluff today — practical engineering problems for our actual system.

Spring & Java Production (25 min)

👨‍💻 David: **Got It's matching engine receives 1K+ session requests per minute at peak (US school dismissal time ~3-6 PM ET). How do you design a Spring Boot service that's non-blocking during matching — which takes 200-2000ms depending on availability?**

👨 Khanh: Classic async request handling. The client shouldn't wait 2 seconds for a match — they should get an immediate response and receive the match result asynchronously.

// Request-accept pattern with polling or WebSocket callback
@RestController
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class MatchingController {

    private final MatchingService matchingService;

    // Step 1: Accept request immediately
    @PostMapping("/sessions/request")
    public ResponseEntity<SessionRequestResponse> requestSession(
            @Valid @RequestBody SessionRequest req,
            @AuthenticationPrincipal StudentPrincipal student) {

        String sessionId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

        // Start matching asynchronously — don't block HTTP thread
        matchingService.startMatchingAsync(sessionId, req, student.getId());

        return ResponseEntity.accepted()
            .body(new SessionRequestResponse(
                sessionId,
                "FINDING_TUTOR",
                "/sessions/" + sessionId + "/status"
            ));
    }

    // Step 2: Client polls for result (or gets WebSocket push)
    @GetMapping("/sessions/{id}/status")
    public SessionStatusResponse getStatus(@PathVariable String id) {
        return matchingService.getStatus(id);
    }
}

@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
public class MatchingService {

    private final TutorAvailabilityCache tutorCache;
    private final SessionRepository sessionRepo;
    private final NotificationService notificationService;
    private final ApplicationEventPublisher eventPublisher;

    @Async("matchingExecutor")
    public CompletableFuture<Void> startMatchingAsync(
            String sessionId, SessionRequest req, String studentId) {

        try {
            // Create session record
            Session session = Session.requested(sessionId, studentId, req.getSubject());
            sessionRepo.save(session);

            // Run matching algorithm (200-2000ms)
            Optional<Tutor> match = findBestMatch(req);

            if (match.isPresent()) {
                session.assignTutor(match.get().getId());
                sessionRepo.save(session);

                // Notify student AND tutor
                notificationService.notifyStudent(studentId, session);
                notificationService.notifyTutor(match.get().getId(), session);
            } else {
                session.setStatus(NO_TUTOR_AVAILABLE);
                sessionRepo.save(session);
                notificationService.notifyNoTutor(studentId, session);
            }

        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("Matching failed for session {}", sessionId, e);
            sessionRepo.updateStatus(sessionId, MATCHING_FAILED);
        }

        return CompletableFuture.completedFuture(null);
    }

    private Optional<Tutor> findBestMatch(SessionRequest req) {
        // Check Redis for available tutors by subject
        List<Tutor> available = tutorCache.getAvailableBySubject(req.getSubject());
        return available.stream()
            .filter(t -> t.getRating() >= req.getMinRating())
            .max(Comparator.comparing(Tutor::getMatchScore));
    }
}

// Thread pool sized for matching workload
@Bean("matchingExecutor")
public Executor matchingExecutor() {
    ThreadPoolTaskExecutor exec = new ThreadPoolTaskExecutor();
    exec.setCorePoolSize(20);
    exec.setMaxPoolSize(50);
    exec.setQueueCapacity(200);
    exec.setThreadNamePrefix("matching-");
    exec.setRejectedExecutionHandler(new ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy());
    exec.initialize();
    return exec;
}

Real-time push via WebSocket (better than polling):

@EventListener
public void onSessionMatched(SessionMatchedEvent event) {
    // Push update to student's browser in real-time
    messagingTemplate.convertAndSendToUser(
        event.getStudentId(),
        "/queue/session-updates",
        new SessionUpdateMessage(event.getSessionId(), "TUTOR_FOUND",
            event.getTutorName(), event.getEstimatedStartTime())
    );
}

👨‍💻 David: **Multi-currency is tricky. How do you store and process payments in both USD (US students) and VND (Vietnam students)?**

👨 Khanh: Three key decisions:

1. Store currency alongside every monetary amount:

@Embeddable
public record Money(
    @Column(name = "amount") BigDecimal amount,
    @Column(name = "currency") String currency  // "USD" or "VND"
) {
    public static Money usd(BigDecimal amount) { return new Money(amount, "USD"); }
    public static Money vnd(long amount) { return new Money(BigDecimal.valueOf(amount), "VND"); }

    // Never compare amounts across currencies directly
    public Money add(Money other) {
        if (!this.currency.equals(other.currency)) {
            throw new CurrencyMismatchException(this.currency, other.currency);
        }
        return new Money(this.amount.add(other.amount), this.currency);
    }
}

@Entity
public class Session {
    @Embedded
    @AttributeOverrides({
        @AttributeOverride(name = "amount",   column = @Column(name = "price_amount")),
        @AttributeOverride(name = "currency", column = @Column(name = "price_currency"))
    })
    private Money price; // e.g., USD 15.00 or VND 200,000
}

2. Decimal places per currency:

public enum CurrencyConfig {
    USD(2, BigDecimal.class),   // $15.99 — 2 decimal places
    VND(0, Long.class);         // 200,000 — no decimal places

    private final int decimalPlaces;

    public BigDecimal round(BigDecimal amount) {
        return amount.setScale(decimalPlaces, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);
    }
}

3. Never convert currencies implicitly:

// ❌ BAD — implicit conversion hides bugs
BigDecimal usdAmount = session.getPrice(); // is this USD or VND?

// ✅ GOOD — always explicit
Money sessionPrice = session.getPrice();
if (sessionPrice.getCurrency().equals("VND")) {
    // Handle VND-specific logic
}

Payment processing:

  • US students → Stripe (USD, credit card)
  • VN students → MoMo or ZaloPay SDK (VND, local wallet)

Each payment processor is behind an interface:

public interface PaymentProcessor {
    PaymentResult charge(Money amount, PaymentMethod method);
    String getSupportedCurrency();
}

@Component
public class PaymentProcessorRouter {
    private final Map<String, PaymentProcessor> processors;

    public PaymentResult process(Money amount, PaymentMethod method) {
        PaymentProcessor processor = processors.get(amount.getCurrency());
        if (processor == null) {
            throw new UnsupportedCurrencyException(amount.getCurrency());
        }
        return processor.charge(amount, method);
    }
}

Design: Tutor Availability System (35 min)

👨‍💻 David: Got It's core challenge: 50K tutors, each with their own weekly schedule. When a student requests a session NOW, we need to find available tutors in < 500ms. How do you design the tutor availability system?

👨 Khanh:

Two types of availability:

  1. Scheduled availability — tutor sets recurring hours: "I'm

available Mon-Fri 6-10 PM ET"

  1. Online now — tutor is actively online and accepting requests

Architecture:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  TUTOR APP                                      │
│  "Go Online" button → real-time availability    │
└──────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┘
                   │
         ┌─────────▼──────────┐
         │  Availability API  │
         └─────────┬──────────┘
                   │
         ┌─────────▼──────────┐      ┌──────────────────┐
         │  Redis              │      │ PostgreSQL        │
         │  (online now)       │      │ (scheduled hours) │
         │  SET online:tutors  │      │ tutor_schedules   │
         │  TTL: 60 seconds    │      │                  │
         └─────────────────────┘      └──────────────────┘

"Online Now" via Redis with heartbeat:

// Tutor app sends heartbeat every 30 seconds
@PostMapping("/tutors/{id}/heartbeat")
public void heartbeat(@PathVariable String id) {
    // Add to Redis sorted set with current timestamp as score
    redisTemplate.opsForZSet().add(
        "tutors:online:" + getSubjects(id),  // one set per subject
        id,
        System.currentTimeMillis()
    );
    // TTL handled by scheduled cleanup (see below)
}

// Remove tutors who haven't sent heartbeat in 90 seconds
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
public void cleanupOfflineTutors() {
    long cutoff = System.currentTimeMillis() - 90_000;
    redisTemplate.opsForZSet().removeRangeByScore(
        "tutors:online:math",   // one per subject
        0, cutoff
    );
}

// Matching: get available tutors for a subject
public List<String> getAvailableTutors(String subject) {
    long cutoff = System.currentTimeMillis() - 90_000;
    // Get tutors active in the last 90 seconds
    return new ArrayList<>(redisTemplate.opsForZSet()
        .rangeByScore("tutors:online:" + subject, cutoff, Double.MAX_VALUE));
}

Scheduled availability in PostgreSQL:

CREATE TABLE tutor_schedules (
    id          BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    tutor_id    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    day_of_week INT NOT NULL,       -- 1=Mon, 7=Sun
    start_time  TIME NOT NULL,      -- in tutor's timezone
    end_time    TIME NOT NULL,
    timezone    VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    subjects    TEXT[] NOT NULL,    -- array of subjects: {'math','physics'}
    is_active   BOOLEAN DEFAULT TRUE
);

-- Query: find tutors scheduled NOW for 'math'
SELECT DISTINCT tutor_id FROM tutor_schedules
WHERE is_active = TRUE
  AND day_of_week = EXTRACT(DOW FROM NOW() AT TIME ZONE timezone)
  AND start_time <= (NOW() AT TIME ZONE timezone)::TIME
  AND end_time >= (NOW() AT TIME ZONE timezone)::TIME
  AND 'math' = ANY(subjects);

Combined matching strategy:

public List<Tutor> findAvailableTutors(String subject, Instant requestTime) {
    // Priority 1: Online NOW (real-time, fastest match)
    List<String> onlineTutorIds = getAvailableTutors(subject);

    if (onlineTutorIds.size() >= 5) {
        // Enough online tutors — no need for schedule query
        return enrichAndRank(onlineTutorIds);
    }

    // Priority 2: Scheduled (should be available now, may need to ping)
    List<String> scheduledIds = scheduleRepo.findAvailableNow(subject, requestTime);

    // Combine, deduplicate, rank by: rating, response time, match history
    return Stream.concat(onlineTutorIds.stream(), scheduledIds.stream())
        .distinct()
        .map(this::enrich)
        .sorted(Comparator.comparing(Tutor::getMatchScore).reversed())
        .limit(10)
        .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

👨‍💻 David: The Redis sorted set with timestamp score for heartbeat tracking — that's clean. How do you handle timezone complexity for scheduled tutors?

👨 Khanh: Store ALL times in UTC in the database. The timezone column on tutor_schedules is for display and input conversion only.

When a tutor in EST sets "I'm available 6-10 PM," we convert to UTC (11 PM - 3 AM UTC) before storing. When querying, we compare against current UTC time.

public void setSchedule(String tutorId, ScheduleRequest req) {
    ZoneId tutorZone = ZoneId.of(req.getTimezone()); // e.g., "America/New_York"

    LocalTime startLocal = req.getStartTime();  // 18:00 in EST
    LocalTime endLocal = req.getEndTime();       // 22:00 in EST

    // Convert to UTC for storage
    ZonedDateTime startUtc = startLocal.atDate(LocalDate.now())
        .atZone(tutorZone)
        .withZoneSameInstant(ZoneOffset.UTC);

    scheduleRepo.save(TutorSchedule.builder()
        .tutorId(tutorId)
        .startTimeUtc(startUtc.toLocalTime())
        .dayOfWeekUtc(startUtc.getDayOfWeek().getValue())
        .timezone(req.getTimezone())  // keep for display
        .build());
}

Edge case: US Daylight Saving Time shifts. By storing in UTC, we're immune — 6 PM EST in summer is 10 PM UTC, in winter is 11 PM UTC. The schedule automatically adjusts.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR Builder mindset 10/10 CI/CD ownership story, startup mentality authentic
Spring Async matching (202+polling) 10/10 Executor config, WebSocket push, CallerRunsPolicy
Multi-currency Money record + processor router 10/10 Currency-aware type, no implicit conversion
Design Tutor availability system 10/10 Redis sorted set heartbeat, scheduled availability, combined matching
Timezone UTC storage, display conversion 10/10 DST immune, practical implementation

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Got It Interview Tips

  1. English fluency is essential — US market primary, CTO is ex-Amazon
  2. Builder mindset — show you own things end-to-end, no hand-offs
  3. Real-time matching is the core product — understand async patterns deeply
  4. Multi-currency — Money value object, never bare BigDecimal
  5. Timezone handling — always store UTC, convert at boundaries
  6. Redis sorted set — elegant for "online now" with TTL via score
  7. Got It salary — 40-60M + meaningful equity (startup, not listed)
  8. EdTech is growing — online tutoring market post-COVID is massive

🎭 Mock Interview #28 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Grab Vietnam (Super-App, Singapore HQ, HCMC Hub, 800+ engineers VN)

Context: Grab is Southeast Asia's #1 super-app — GrabCar, GrabFood, GrabPay, GrabMart. Revenue $2.4B, 35M monthly active users across 8 countries. HCMC engineering hub is their second-largest after Singapore. Role: Marketplace Platform team — the engine that matches drivers/merchants to riders/customers in real-time. Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, PostgreSQL, K8s on AWS. Salary range: 55-90M+ gross. One of the top 3 employers in VN.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Priya (Recruiter, Singapore): Hi Khanh! Tell me about yourself and why Grab.

👨 Khanh: Hi Priya! Senior Java Engineer, 5 years, currently at FPT/Sony. My most relevant experience for Grab: at Sony I designed high-throughput event-driven services on AWS at 3K RPS with strict SLAs. At Axon Active, I led distributed system design for a banking platform.

Why Grab specifically: the Marketplace platform is one of the hardest real-time matching problems in Southeast Asia. Matching 50K+ active drivers to 2M+ daily ride requests, with dynamic pricing and live GPS tracking — that's a genuinely hard distributed systems problem. I want to work on systems at this scale and complexity.

Also — I use Grab daily. That direct connection between what I build and what I experience as a user keeps engineering grounded in real impact.

👩 Priya: Grab moves fast — we ship multiple times per day. How do you balance speed and quality?

👨 Khanh: Feature flags and incremental rollouts are my answer. At Sony, every risky change ships behind a feature flag to 1% of traffic first. We measure latency and error rates for 30 minutes, then promote to 10%, 50%, 100%. That way we move fast without big bang risk.

For quality: I write tests for the happy path and the two most likely failure modes. Not 100% coverage — pragmatic coverage. A well-tested core with fast iteration beats a perfectly tested system that ships slowly.

👩 Priya: Expected compensation?

👨 Khanh: I've researched Grab's HCMC senior range — 55-90M. With my background in distributed systems and payments, I'm targeting 70-80M gross as a starting point. I'm also interested in the RSU structure.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (75 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer from Singapore + Senior Engineer HCMC.

👨‍💻 Kai (Staff Eng, SG): Hi Khanh. Three parts: Java/distributed systems (20 min), system design (35 min), coding (20 min).

PART A — Java & Distributed Systems (20 min)

👨‍💻 Kai: **Explain the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking. For a ride booking system where two users might book the same driver simultaneously, which do you use and why?**

👨 Khanh: Two fundamentally different strategies for concurrent access:

Pessimistic locking: Lock the resource before reading it. No other transaction can modify it until you release the lock.

// SELECT ... FOR UPDATE — locks the row
@Query("SELECT d FROM Driver d WHERE d.id = :id")
@Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
Driver findByIdForUpdate(@Param("id") String id);

// Usage
@Transactional
public Booking bookDriver(String driverId, String riderId) {
    Driver driver = driverRepo.findByIdForUpdate(driverId); // LOCKED
    if (!driver.isAvailable()) throw new DriverUnavailableException();
    driver.setStatus(BUSY);
    driverRepo.save(driver);
    return bookingRepo.save(new Booking(driverId, riderId));
    // Lock released on commit
}

Optimistic locking: No lock taken. Use a version column. If two transactions read the same version and both try to update, the second one fails with OptimisticLockException. 

@Entity
public class Driver {
    @Id private String id;
    private DriverStatus status;

    @Version
    private Long version; // auto-incremented on every update
}

// Service — no explicit lock
@Transactional
public Booking bookDriver(String driverId, String riderId) {
    Driver driver = driverRepo.findById(driverId).orElseThrow();
    if (!driver.isAvailable()) throw new DriverUnavailableException();
    driver.setStatus(BUSY); // if concurrent update happened → OptimisticLockException
    driverRepo.save(driver);
    return bookingRepo.save(new Booking(driverId, riderId));
}

For Grab's ride booking — I use pessimistic:

Reason: at Grab's scale, a driver gets booking requests from potentially hundreds of nearby riders simultaneously during peak hours. Optimistic locking would cause a thundering herd of retries — every concurrent booking except one would fail and retry. With pessimistic locking, only one succeeds and others fail fast with a clear "Driver unavailable."

However, locking duration must be minimal. The entire book-driver transaction completes in < 100ms so lock contention is brief.

Alternative at true Grab scale: Use Redis atomic operations instead of DB locks — much faster:

// Atomic: mark driver as busy only if currently available
Boolean reserved = redisTemplate.execute(
    RESERVE_DRIVER_SCRIPT,
    List.of("driver:status:" + driverId),
    "AVAILABLE", "BUSY"
);
// Lua: if GET key == AVAILABLE, SET key = BUSY, return 1; else return 0
if (!Boolean.TRUE.equals(reserved)) {
    throw new DriverUnavailableException();
}

Redis operations are 10x faster than DB SELECT FOR UPDATE at scale.

👨‍💻 Kai: **What is eventual consistency? Give a concrete example of where Grab would accept it and where we would not.**

👨 Khanh: Eventual consistency means that after all updates propagate, all nodes converge to the same state — but there's a window where different nodes may return different values.

Where Grab ACCEPTS eventual consistency:

Driver location updates (GPS every 5 seconds):

Driver moves → GPS update → Redis (primary updated)
→ Redis replica might show old location for 100ms
→ Nearby riders see slightly outdated driver position on map

100ms stale position for a car moving 40 km/h = 1.1 meter error. Completely acceptable for map display.

Surge pricing calculation:

Supply/demand ratio calculated every 30 seconds
→ Different app servers might use pricing calculated 10-30 sec ago
→ Rider might see 1.2x but pay 1.3x (or vice versa)

Acceptable — close enough for dynamic pricing.

Where Grab does NOT accept eventual consistency:

Ride booking — must be strongly consistent:

WRONG: Driver marked BUSY on primary DB
       Replica still shows AVAILABLE
       Second rider books same driver from replica read
→ Two riders in same car — unacceptable

Payment deduction:

WRONG: Rider's balance deducted on primary
       Replica shows old (higher) balance
       Rider books another ride using stale balance
→ Overdraft / double-spending — unacceptable

Rule: User-visible money and exclusive resource assignment → strong consistency. Analytics, location display, recommendations → eventual consistency is fine.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Ride Matching (35 min)

👨‍💻 Kai: *"Design Grab's real-time driver-rider matching. A rider requests a ride from point A to B. Find the best available driver within 3km. Respond in < 2 seconds. Scale: 2M rides/day, peak 500 ride requests/second in HCMC alone."*

👨 Khanh: [2 min thinking]

Core Problem:

  • 50K+ active drivers in HCMC at peak, each with changing GPS position
  • 500 ride requests/second — each needs nearest available driver found < 2s
  • Driver availability changes every second (accepting/completing rides)

Key Insight: This is a geospatial + availability problem. Two data structures needed:

  1. Where is each driver? (geospatial index)
  2. Is this driver available? (availability state)

Architecture:

Rider App → Matching API
               │
    ┌──────────▼────────────┐
    │   Matching Service     │
    │   1. Geo search        │──► Redis GEO (driver locations)
    │   2. Availability check│──► Redis HASH (driver status)
    │   3. Score & rank      │
    │   4. Send offer        │──► Kafka "driver.offer"
    └────────────────────────┘
               │
    ┌──────────▼────────────┐
    │   Driver App           │ (accept/reject within 15s)
    └────────────────────────┘

Driver Location in Redis GEO:

// Driver sends GPS every 5 seconds
@PostMapping("/drivers/{id}/location")
public void updateLocation(@PathVariable String id,
                           @RequestBody GpsUpdate gps) {
    // Redis GEO — stores lat/lng as geohash, enables radius search
    redisTemplate.opsForGeo().add(
        "drivers:locations",
        new Point(gps.getLongitude(), gps.getLatitude()),
        id
    );
    // Also update last-seen timestamp
    redisTemplate.opsForHash().put("drivers:lastseen", id,
        String.valueOf(System.currentTimeMillis()));
}

// Find drivers within 3km of pickup point
public List<String> findNearbyDrivers(double lat, double lng) {
    Circle searchArea = new Circle(
        new Point(lng, lat),
        new Distance(3, Metrics.KILOMETERS)
    );
    GeoResults<GeoLocation<String>> results =
        redisTemplate.opsForGeo()
            .radius("drivers:locations", searchArea,
                GeoRadiusCommandArgs.newGeoRadiusArgs()
                    .includeDistance()
                    .sortAscending()
                    .limit(20)); // top 20 nearest

    return results.getContent().stream()
        .map(r -> r.getContent().getName())
        .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

Driver Availability Filter:

public List<String> getAvailableDrivers(List<String> nearbyIds) {
    // Batch check availability in Redis HASH
    List<Object> statuses = redisTemplate.opsForHash()
        .multiGet("drivers:status", new ArrayList<>(nearbyIds));

    List<String> available = new ArrayList<>();
    for (int i = 0; i < nearbyIds.size(); i++) {
        if ("AVAILABLE".equals(statuses.get(i))) {
            available.add(nearbyIds.get(i));
        }
    }
    return available;
}

Matching + Scoring:

public MatchResult matchDriver(RideRequest req) {
    // 1. Find nearby drivers (Redis GEO)
    List<String> nearby = findNearbyDrivers(req.getPickupLat(), req.getPickupLng());

    // 2. Filter available (Redis HASH multiGet — single round trip)
    List<String> available = getAvailableDrivers(nearby);

    if (available.isEmpty()) return MatchResult.noDriver();

    // 3. Score drivers
    Driver best = available.stream()
        .map(driverCache::get)  // local in-memory cache of driver profiles
        .max(Comparator.comparingDouble(d ->
            scoreDriver(d, req)))
        .orElseThrow();

    // 4. Tentatively reserve driver (atomic Redis)
    boolean reserved = reserveDriver(best.getId());
    if (!reserved) {
        // Driver just became unavailable — retry with next best
        available.remove(best.getId());
        // ... retry logic
    }

    return MatchResult.matched(best, req);
}

private double scoreDriver(Driver d, RideRequest req) {
    double distanceScore = 1.0 / (d.getDistanceKm() + 0.1);  // closer = better
    double ratingScore = d.getRating() / 5.0;
    double acceptanceScore = d.getAcceptanceRate();
    // Weighted composite score
    return 0.5 * distanceScore + 0.3 * ratingScore + 0.2 * acceptanceScore;
}

Surge Pricing:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
public void recalculateSurge() {
    // For each city zone: calculate supply/demand ratio
    cityZones.forEach(zone -> {
        long activeDrivers = countAvailableDrivers(zone);
        long pendingRequests = countPendingRequests(zone);

        double ratio = (double) pendingRequests / Math.max(activeDrivers, 1);
        double surgeMultiplier = calculateSurge(ratio);
        // Store in Redis for fast read by Matching Service
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "surge:" + zone.getId(),
            String.valueOf(surgeMultiplier),
            Duration.ofSeconds(60)
        );
    });
}

private double calculateSurge(double demandSupplyRatio) {
    if (ratio < 0.5) return 1.0;       // plenty of drivers
    if (ratio < 1.0) return 1.2;       // slight shortage
    if (ratio < 2.0) return 1.5;       // moderate shortage
    if (ratio < 3.0) return 2.0;       // high demand
    return 2.5;                         // cap surge at 2.5x
}

Offer + Timeout:

// Send offer to driver via push notification / WebSocket
kafkaTemplate.send("driver.offers", driverId,
    new DriverOffer(rideRequest, surgeMultiplier, offerExpiresAt));

// If driver doesn't accept in 15 seconds → try next driver
scheduledExecutor.schedule(
    () -> handleOfferTimeout(rideRequest, driverId),
    15, TimeUnit.SECONDS
);

👩‍💻 Anh (Sr Eng, HCMC): What if all nearby drivers reject?

👨 Khanh: Expand the search radius progressively:

  • Round 1: 3km, top 3 drivers, 15s timeout each
  • Round 2: 5km, next 5 drivers, 15s timeout
  • Round 3: 8km, next 10 drivers
  • After 3 minutes total → notify rider "No driver available"

Each round runs sequentially, expanding outward. We don't blast all 50 nearby drivers at once — that creates a poor driver experience (many accept a ride that's already booked).

PART C — LIVE CODING (20 min)

👨‍💻 Kai: *"Implement a function that, given a list of ride events (REQUESTED, MATCHED, STARTED, COMPLETED, CANCELLED), calculates metrics per driver: total rides completed, total rides cancelled, cancellation rate, average ride duration (minutes)."*

Input events:
  {driverId: "D1", type: MATCHED,   rideId: "R1", timestamp: t=0}
  {driverId: "D1", type: STARTED,   rideId: "R1", timestamp: t=5}
  {driverId: "D1", type: COMPLETED, rideId: "R1", timestamp: t=25}
  {driverId: "D1", type: MATCHED,   rideId: "R2", timestamp: t=30}
  {driverId: "D1", type: CANCELLED, rideId: "R2", timestamp: t=35}

👨 Khanh:

public record RideEvent(
    String driverId,
    String rideId,
    EventType type,
    long timestamp  // epoch seconds
) {}

public record DriverMetrics(
    String driverId,
    int totalCompleted,
    int totalCancelled,
    double cancellationRate,        // cancelled / (completed + cancelled)
    double avgRideDurationMinutes
) {}

public List<DriverMetrics> calculateMetrics(List<RideEvent> events) {
    // Group events by driverId
    Map<String, List<RideEvent>> byDriver = events.stream()
        .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(RideEvent::driverId));

    return byDriver.entrySet().stream()
        .map(e -> computeDriverMetrics(e.getKey(), e.getValue()))
        .collect(Collectors.toList());
}

private DriverMetrics computeDriverMetrics(String driverId,
                                            List<RideEvent> events) {
    // Group events by rideId to process each ride
    Map<String, List<RideEvent>> byRide = events.stream()
        .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(RideEvent::rideId));

    int completed = 0;
    int cancelled = 0;
    long totalDurationSeconds = 0;

    for (Map.Entry<String, List<RideEvent>> rideEntry : byRide.entrySet()) {
        List<RideEvent> rideEvents = rideEntry.getValue();

        boolean isCompleted = rideEvents.stream()
            .anyMatch(e -> e.type() == EventType.COMPLETED);
        boolean isCancelled = rideEvents.stream()
            .anyMatch(e -> e.type() == EventType.CANCELLED);

        if (isCompleted) {
            completed++;
            // Duration = COMPLETED timestamp - STARTED timestamp
            OptionalLong startTime = rideEvents.stream()
                .filter(e -> e.type() == EventType.STARTED)
                .mapToLong(RideEvent::timestamp).findFirst();
            OptionalLong endTime = rideEvents.stream()
                .filter(e -> e.type() == EventType.COMPLETED)
                .mapToLong(RideEvent::timestamp).findFirst();

            if (startTime.isPresent() && endTime.isPresent()) {
                totalDurationSeconds += endTime.getAsLong() - startTime.getAsLong();
            }
        } else if (isCancelled) {
            cancelled++;
        }
    }

    int totalRides = completed + cancelled;
    double cancellationRate = totalRides == 0 ? 0.0
        : (double) cancelled / totalRides * 100;
    double avgDuration = completed == 0 ? 0.0
        : (totalDurationSeconds / 60.0) / completed;

    return new DriverMetrics(driverId, completed, cancelled,
        Math.round(cancellationRate * 100.0) / 100.0,
        Math.round(avgDuration * 100.0) / 100.0);
}

Trace for D1:

R1: MATCHED(t=0), STARTED(t=5), COMPLETED(t=25) → duration=20s → completed++
R2: MATCHED(t=30), CANCELLED(t=35) → cancelled++

Result: completed=1, cancelled=1
cancellationRate = 1/2 * 100 = 50.00%
avgDuration = (20/60) / 1 = 0.33 minutes

Tests:

@Test
void singleCompletedRide() {
    var events = List.of(
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", MATCHED,   0),
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", STARTED,   300),
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", COMPLETED, 1500)
    );
    var metrics = calculator.calculateMetrics(events);
    assertThat(metrics).hasSize(1);
    assertThat(metrics.get(0).totalCompleted()).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(metrics.get(0).cancellationRate()).isEqualTo(0.0);
    assertThat(metrics.get(0).avgRideDurationMinutes()).isEqualTo(20.0);
}

@Test
void mixedCompletedAndCancelled() {
    var events = List.of(
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", MATCHED,   0),
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", STARTED,   10),
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", COMPLETED, 310),
        new RideEvent("D1","R2", MATCHED,   400),
        new RideEvent("D1","R2", CANCELLED, 500)
    );
    var metrics = calculator.calculateMetrics(events).get(0);
    assertThat(metrics.totalCompleted()).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(metrics.totalCancelled()).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(metrics.cancellationRate()).isEqualTo(50.0);
}

@Test
void multipleDrivers() {
    var events = List.of(
        new RideEvent("D1","R1", COMPLETED, 0),
        new RideEvent("D2","R2", CANCELLED, 0)
    );
    var metrics = calculator.calculateMetrics(events);
    assertThat(metrics).hasSize(2);
}

👨‍💻 Kai: Java records for immutable event data, clean grouping logic, separate concerns (ride-level vs driver-level), comprehensive tests. Strong solution.

🤝 ROUND 3 — CULTURE FIT + VP (25 min, English)

👨‍💼 Marcus (VP Eng): Khanh, Grab operates in 8 countries with different regulations, user behaviors, and infrastructure reliability. How do you design systems that work across markets with very different conditions?

👨 Khanh: Three principles I've developed:

1. Design for the worst connection, not the average. Vietnam's 4G is inconsistent. If my API assumes a stable 100ms network, it breaks in rural areas. I design all APIs with:

  • Idempotency keys (retries are safe)
  • Short-lived tokens that can be refreshed (not long-lived sessions)
  • Compressed payloads (Protobuf or gzipped JSON)
  • Aggressive client-side caching of reference data

2. Feature flags per market. Not all markets have the same payment options, legal requirements, or user preferences. I use feature flags keyed by countryCode so the same codebase serves different behaviors:

if (featureFlags.isEnabled("CASH_PAYMENT", country)) {
    // Only Vietnam, Thailand enable cash rides
}

No separate codebases per country — one platform, configurable behavior.

3. Graceful degradation over hard failures. If surge pricing service is down, don't reject the ride request — default to 1.0x multiplier and log for reconciliation. If driver rating service is slow, use cached rating. The core flow (rider gets a driver) must work even when non-critical services fail.

👨‍💼 Marcus: "Design for the worst connection" — that's a real production lesson most engineers learn the hard way. Welcome to Grab.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR Why Grab + salary 10/10 Daily user, distributed systems motivation, market-researched range
Java Pessimistic vs optimistic locking 10/10 Context-dependent choice + Redis alternative at scale
Java Eventual consistency 10/10 Location/surge = AP, booking/payment = CP. Clear examples
Design Driver-rider matching 10/10 Redis GEO + HASH, progressive radius, surge calc, offer timeout
Coding Driver metrics calculator 10/10 Records, grouping, 3 comprehensive tests
Culture Multi-market design 10/10 Feature flags per country, worst-connection design, graceful degradation

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Grab Interview Tips

  1. Redis GEO commands — GEOADD, GEORADIUS — know the API cold
  2. Pessimistic vs optimistic — always frame with "at Grab's scale" context
  3. Eventual vs strong consistency — have concrete examples ready
  4. Multi-market thinking — Grab operates in 8 countries, show global design sense
  5. Progressive radius expansion — don't blast all drivers at once
  6. Surge pricing — scheduled recalculation, zone-based, capped multiplier
  7. Grab salary — 55-90M gross. Strong negotiation position with your background
  8. English is mandatory — SG team leads most design discussions

🎭 Mock Interview #29 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Agoda (Booking Holdings, Travel Tech, Bangkok HQ, HCMC office)

Context: Agoda is one of the world's largest online travel platforms. Owned by Booking Holdings ($15B revenue). 2.5M+ hotels, 400M+ users. Engineering hubs in Bangkok, Singapore, HCMC, and Bangalore. HCMC office: 300+ engineers, strong growth. Role: Hotel Availability & Pricing team — the system that shows "3 rooms left at $89/night" in real-time to 400M users worldwide. Tech stack: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, Redis, Cassandra, Elasticsearch, AWS. Why Agoda stands out: International culture (English-first, 70+ nationalities), travel domain is highly seasonal with 10x traffic spikes during holidays, and pricing algorithms are genuinely complex (currency, taxes, promotions). Salary: 50-80M+ gross in HCMC.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Siri (Recruiter, Bangkok): Hi Khanh! Tell me about yourself and why Agoda.

👨 Khanh: Hi Siri! I'm Khanh, Senior Java Engineer with nearly 5 years building high-throughput microservices. Key background for Agoda:

At Sony, I optimized a service from 800ms to 120ms P99 at 3K RPS — the kind of performance engineering Agoda's search and pricing systems demand. At Axon Active, I built banking systems that required absolute accuracy in financial calculations — directly relevant to Agoda's multi-currency pricing.

Why Agoda: Travel pricing is one of the most complex pricing problems in tech — real-time inventory from thousands of hotel partners, dynamic pricing based on demand, promotions stacked on top, taxes varying by country, and currency conversions. That complexity combined with global scale is the kind of challenge I want to work on.

I also travel frequently and use Agoda myself, which gives me a user perspective on the product.

👩 Siri: Agoda is extremely data-driven — every engineering decision is backed by metrics. How do you approach measuring the impact of technical improvements?

👨 Khanh: I define success metrics before writing code. For every significant change I make, I answer three questions upfront:

  1. What is the current baseline? (measure first)
  2. What metric will improve? (latency, error rate, throughput, cost)
  3. How will I know the change worked? (A/B test, canary deployment with metrics comparison)

At Sony, before my latency optimization project, I established:

  • Baseline: P99 = 800ms, P50 = 200ms
  • Target: P99 < 500ms
  • Method: Deploy to 10% traffic, compare P99 between old and new using CloudWatch
  • Success criteria: P99 drops, error rate unchanged, CPU usage not significantly higher

I only declare success when data confirms it — not when the code looks right.

👩 Siri: Good. Three technical rounds — two with engineers, one with Director of Engineering. Process takes about 2 weeks.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (70 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer from Bangkok + Senior Engineer HCMC.

👨‍💻 Tom (Staff Eng, Bangkok): Hi Khanh! Two parts: Java/Spring deep-dive and a hotel availability system design.

PART A — Java & Spring Deep-Dive (25 min)

👨‍💻 Tom: **Explain Spring's bean lifecycle. When would you use @PostConstruct, InitializingBean, and SmartLifecycle?**

👨 Khanh: Spring beans go through this lifecycle:

1. Instantiate (constructor called)
2. Populate properties (dependency injection)
3. BeanNameAware, BeanFactoryAware callbacks
4. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization()
5. @PostConstruct / InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet()
6. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization()
7. Bean is ready to use
8. ... (in use) ...
9. @PreDestroy / DisposableBean.destroy()

@PostConstruct — runs after DI, before the bean is used. Most common choice. Simple, annotation-driven.

@Component
public class HotelPriceCache {
    private Map<String, BigDecimal> cache;

    @PostConstruct
    public void initialize() {
        // Load initial price data from DB into memory cache
        // DI is complete here — can use @Autowired fields
        cache = priceRepository.loadAllActivePrices();
        log.info("Loaded {} hotel prices into cache", cache.size());
    }
}

InitializingBean — same timing as @PostConstruct but interface-based. Use when writing framework code (no Spring annotations in library code):

// Library code — no Spring dependency in the interface
public class ConnectionPoolManager implements InitializingBean {
    @Override
    public void afterPropertiesSet() {
        // Initialize connection pool
    }
}

SmartLifecycle — for beans that need to start/stop with the application context lifecycle. Critical difference: runs AFTER all beans are fully initialized, and supports graceful shutdown ordering:

@Component
public class KafkaConsumerService implements SmartLifecycle {
    private boolean running = false;

    @Override
    public void start() {
        // Start consuming — called after ALL Spring beans ready
        // Important: Kafka consumer should start AFTER DB, cache, etc. are ready
        consumer.subscribe(List.of("hotel.availability.updates"));
        running = true;
    }

    @Override
    public void stop() {
        // Graceful shutdown — finish processing in-flight messages
        consumer.close(Duration.ofSeconds(30));
        running = false;
    }

    @Override
    public int getPhase() { return 100; } // higher = starts later, stops sooner
}

When to use which:

Scenario Use
Load cache data after DI @PostConstruct
Framework/library initialization InitializingBean
Start background threads/consumers SmartLifecycle
Anything needing graceful stop ordering SmartLifecycle

👩‍💻 Minh (Sr Eng, HCMC): **How do you implement pagination for Elasticsearch in Spring? What's the difference between from/size and search-after pagination?**

👨 Khanh: Two fundamentally different mechanisms:

from/size — offset-based (simple but limited):

@Repository
public class HotelSearchRepository {

    public Page<Hotel> search(HotelSearchQuery query, Pageable pageable) {
        NativeQuery searchQuery = NativeQuery.builder()
            .withQuery(buildQuery(query))
            .withSort(Sort.by(Sort.Direction.ASC, "price"))
            .withPageable(pageable) // from = page*size, size = pageSize
            .build();

        SearchHits<Hotel> hits = elasticsearchTemplate.search(searchQuery, Hotel.class);

        return SearchHitSupport.searchPageFor(hits, pageable).map(SearchHit::getContent);
    }
}

// Simple but: ES rejects from+size > 10,000
// Deep pagination causes performance issues (ES must fetch and discard early results)

search_after — cursor-based (production choice for deep pagination): 

public SearchAfterResult<Hotel> searchWithCursor(
        HotelSearchQuery query, Object[] searchAfterValues, int size) {

    NativeQueryBuilder queryBuilder = NativeQuery.builder()
        .withQuery(buildQuery(query))
        .withSort(Sort.by(Sort.Direction.ASC, "price"))
        .withSort(Sort.by(Sort.Direction.ASC, "_id"))  // tie-breaker (unique)
        .withMaxResults(size);

    if (searchAfterValues != null) {
        // Continue from last item of previous page
        queryBuilder.withSearchAfter(searchAfterValues);
    }

    SearchHits<Hotel> hits = elasticsearchTemplate.search(
        queryBuilder.build(), Hotel.class);

    // Extract sort values from last hit → use as cursor for next page
    Object[] nextCursor = null;
    if (!hits.isEmpty()) {
        SearchHit<Hotel> lastHit = hits.getSearchHit((int) hits.getTotalHits() - 1);
        nextCursor = lastHit.getSortValues().toArray();
    }

    return new SearchAfterResult<>(
        hits.stream().map(SearchHit::getContent).toList(),
        nextCursor,
        hits.getTotalHits()
    );
}

Key differences:

Aspect from/size search_after
Max depth 10K results Unlimited
Performance Degrades with depth Consistent O(log N)
Random access ✅ Can jump to page 5 ❌ Must traverse sequentially
Real-time results ✅ ⚠️ Use PIT (Point In Time) for consistent pages
Use case First 10 pages Infinite scroll, export

For Agoda's hotel search (users rarely go past page 5), from/size is fine. For a "export all hotels" admin feature, search_after is required.

PART B — SYSTEM DESIGN: Hotel Availability & Pricing (45 min)

👨‍💻 Tom: *"Design Agoda's hotel availability system. Hotels push inventory updates (room availability + price) via API. Users search for 'Bangkok, check-in Mar 15, 2 nights, 2 adults.' Return available hotels sorted by price. Scale: 2.5M hotels, 500M search requests/day (peak: 50K searches/second), inventory updates from hotels: 1M updates/minute."*

👨 Khanh: This is a read-heavy (searches >> updates) system with complex filtering and strict freshness requirements.

Data Flow:

Hotel Partners → Inventory API → Kafka → Availability Processor
                                                  │
                                    ┌─────────────┴──────────────┐
                                    │                             │
                               Redis Cache              Elasticsearch
                               (exact date             (search & filter)
                                availability)
                                    │                             │
                                    └─────────────┬──────────────┘
                                                  │
                                     Search API ◄─┘
                                          │
                                       Users (50K QPS)

Inventory Update Pipeline:

// Hotel partner pushes availability update
@PostMapping("/inventory/update")
public ResponseEntity<Void> receiveInventoryUpdate(
        @RequestBody InventoryUpdate update,
        @RequestHeader("X-Hotel-API-Key") String apiKey) {

    // Validate API key, basic schema check
    inventoryValidator.validate(update, apiKey);

    // Publish to Kafka — accept immediately, process async
    kafkaTemplate.send("inventory.updates", update.getHotelId(), update);

    return ResponseEntity.accepted().build();
}

// Kafka consumer processes updates
@KafkaListener(topics = "inventory.updates", concurrency = "20")
public void processInventoryUpdate(InventoryUpdate update) {
    // Update Redis for exact date availability (fast lookup)
    updateRedisAvailability(update);

    // Update Elasticsearch for search indexing
    updateElasticsearchIndex(update);
}

Redis Availability Structure:

Key: "avail:{hotelId}:{roomTypeId}:{date}"
Value: {
  "available": 3,        -- rooms remaining
  "price": 89.00,
  "currency": "USD",
  "updatedAt": 1700000000
}
TTL: 24 hours (stale after a day if no update)
private void updateRedisAvailability(InventoryUpdate update) {
    for (AvailabilityRecord record : update.getRecords()) {
        String key = String.format("avail:%s:%s:%s",
            update.getHotelId(), record.getRoomTypeId(), record.getDate());

        Map<String, String> fields = Map.of(
            "available", String.valueOf(record.getRoomsAvailable()),
            "price",     record.getPrice().toPlainString(),
            "currency",  record.getCurrency(),
            "updatedAt", String.valueOf(Instant.now().getEpochSecond())
        );
        redisTemplate.opsForHash().putAll(key, fields);
        redisTemplate.expire(key, Duration.ofHours(24));
    }
}

Search Flow:

@Service
public class HotelSearchService {

    public List<HotelSearchResult> search(SearchQuery query) {
        // Step 1: Find hotels matching destination + amenities in Elasticsearch
        List<String> candidateHotelIds = elasticSearch.findHotels(
            query.getCity(), query.getAmenities(), query.getStarRating()
        );  // Returns ~1000 candidate hotels quickly

        // Step 2: Check Redis for actual availability on requested dates
        List<AvailableRoom> available = checkAvailabilityInRedis(
            candidateHotelIds, query.getCheckIn(), query.getCheckOut()
        );

        // Step 3: Calculate total price (multi-night, taxes, promotions)
        List<HotelSearchResult> results = available.stream()
            .map(room -> calculateTotalPrice(room, query))
            .filter(r -> r.getTotalPrice().compareTo(query.getMaxPrice()) <= 0)
            .sorted(Comparator.comparing(HotelSearchResult::getTotalPrice))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());

        return results;
    }

    private List<AvailableRoom> checkAvailabilityInRedis(
            List<String> hotelIds, LocalDate checkIn, LocalDate checkOut) {

        // Generate all dates in the stay
        List<LocalDate> nights = checkIn.datesUntil(checkOut).toList();

        List<AvailableRoom> available = new ArrayList<>();
        for (String hotelId : hotelIds) {
            // Check all nights in one Redis pipeline (single round-trip)
            List<Object> results = redisTemplate.executePipelined(connection -> {
                for (LocalDate night : nights) {
                    String key = "avail:" + hotelId + ":STANDARD:" + night;
                    connection.hashCommands().hGetAll(key.getBytes());
                }
                return null;
            });

            // Hotel is available only if ALL nights have rooms
            boolean allNightsAvailable = results.stream()
                .noneMatch(r -> r == null || ((Map<?, ?>) r).isEmpty() ||
                    Integer.parseInt(((Map<?, ?>) r).get("available").toString()) == 0);

            if (allNightsAvailable) {
                available.add(buildAvailableRoom(hotelId, results));
            }
        }
        return available;
    }
}

Multi-Currency Price Calculation:

public HotelSearchResult calculateTotalPrice(AvailableRoom room, SearchQuery query) {
    // Base price in hotel's native currency
    BigDecimal baseNightlyRate = room.getPrice();
    String hotelCurrency = room.getCurrency();

    // Total for all nights
    long nights = ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(query.getCheckIn(), query.getCheckOut());
    BigDecimal subtotal = baseNightlyRate.multiply(BigDecimal.valueOf(nights));

    // Apply promotions (member discount, early-bird, etc.)
    BigDecimal discount = promotionEngine.calculate(room, query.getUserTier());
    BigDecimal afterDiscount = subtotal.subtract(discount);

    // Add taxes (varies by country)
    BigDecimal taxRate = taxService.getRate(room.getCountry(), room.getCity());
    BigDecimal tax = afterDiscount.multiply(taxRate);
    BigDecimal totalInHotelCurrency = afterDiscount.add(tax);

    // Convert to user's preferred display currency
    BigDecimal exchangeRate = fxService.getRate(hotelCurrency, query.getDisplayCurrency());
    BigDecimal displayPrice = totalInHotelCurrency.multiply(exchangeRate)
        .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);

    return HotelSearchResult.builder()
        .hotelId(room.getHotelId())
        .totalPrice(displayPrice)
        .displayCurrency(query.getDisplayCurrency())
        .priceBreakdown(new PriceBreakdown(subtotal, discount, tax, exchangeRate))
        .build();
}

Handling 50K QPS Search:

Layer Strategy
CDN Cache search results for popular queries (city + date) for 60s
API Gateway Rate limit per user: 10 searches/second
Search Service Horizontal scale: 50 pods × 1K QPS each
Redis Cluster mode: 20 shards, pipeline for multi-date checks
Elasticsearch 30-node cluster, dedicated search nodes

Holiday Traffic (10x spike):

  • Pre-warm cache for predictable high-demand destinations (Đà Nẵng before Tết)
  • Auto-scaling: K8s HPA triggers on CPU > 70%
  • Circuit breaker: if Elasticsearch is slow → serve cached results with 5-minute TTL
  • Shed load gracefully: rate limit anonymous users more aggressively during peak

👨‍💻 Tom: The Redis pipeline for multi-date availability check — single round-trip instead of N round-trips — that's the key optimization here. Most candidates miss this. How do you handle a hotel that updates prices 10 times per minute?

👨 Khanh: Kafka naturally handles this — if a hotel sends 10 updates in a minute, all 10 are in the topic. The consumer processes them in order (Kafka preserves per-key order). Only the latest update matters in Redis — each update overwrites the previous. No data loss, no inconsistency.

For Elasticsearch, if updates come faster than the indexer can process, I'd use Kafka consumer lag monitoring. If lag exceeds 5 minutes, scale up consumers. The ES index might be 5 minutes stale during a burst — acceptable for search (price might be slightly off), but Redis (used for booking confirmation) gets the latest price directly from the hotel's last update.

PART C — LIVE CODING (20 min)

👩‍💻 Minh: *"Write a method that finds the cheapest hotel room for a multi-night stay. Given a map of {date → {hotelId → price}}, return the hotel with the lowest TOTAL price across all nights. Only include hotels available for ALL requested nights."* 

checkIn=Mar15, checkOut=Mar17 (2 nights: Mar15, Mar16)
prices = {
  Mar15: {H1: $80, H2: $90, H3: $70},
  Mar16: {H1: $85, H2: $88, H3: null}  ← H3 unavailable Mar16
}
Expected: H1 with total $165 (H2 = $178, H3 excluded)

👨 Khanh:

public Optional<HotelPriceSummary> findCheapestAvailableHotel(
        LocalDate checkIn,
        LocalDate checkOut,
        Map<LocalDate, Map<String, BigDecimal>> pricesByDate) {

    // Generate all nights in the stay
    List<LocalDate> nights = checkIn.datesUntil(checkOut).toList();

    if (nights.isEmpty()) return Optional.empty();

    // Find hotels available on the first night — then verify all others
    Map<String, BigDecimal> firstNightPrices = pricesByDate.getOrDefault(
        nights.get(0), Collections.emptyMap()
    );

    Map<String, BigDecimal> totalPrices = new HashMap<>();

    for (String hotelId : firstNightPrices.keySet()) {
        BigDecimal total = BigDecimal.ZERO;
        boolean availableAllNights = true;

        for (LocalDate night : nights) {
            Map<String, BigDecimal> nightPrices =
                pricesByDate.getOrDefault(night, Collections.emptyMap());

            BigDecimal nightPrice = nightPrices.get(hotelId);
            if (nightPrice == null) {
                availableAllNights = false;
                break; // skip this hotel
            }
            total = total.add(nightPrice);
        }

        if (availableAllNights) {
            totalPrices.put(hotelId, total);
        }
    }

    return totalPrices.entrySet().stream()
        .min(Map.Entry.comparingByValue())
        .map(e -> new HotelPriceSummary(e.getKey(), e.getValue(), nights.size()));
}

public record HotelPriceSummary(
    String hotelId,
    BigDecimal totalPrice,
    int nights
) {}

Trace:

nights = [Mar15, Mar16]

H1: Mar15=$80 ✓, Mar16=$85 ✓ → total = $165
H2: Mar15=$90 ✓, Mar16=$88 ✓ → total = $178
H3: Mar15=$70 ✓, Mar16=null → skip (unavailable)

min(H1=$165, H2=$178) → H1 ✓

Tests:

@Test
void findsLowestTotalExcludingUnavailable() {
    var prices = Map.of(
        MAR_15, Map.of("H1", bd(80), "H2", bd(90), "H3", bd(70)),
        MAR_16, Map.of("H1", bd(85), "H2", bd(88))
    );
    var result = service.findCheapestAvailableHotel(MAR_15, MAR_17, prices);

    assertThat(result).isPresent();
    assertThat(result.get().hotelId()).isEqualTo("H1");
    assertThat(result.get().totalPrice()).isEqualByComparingTo("165");
}

@Test
void returnsEmptyWhenNoHotelAvailableAllNights() {
    var prices = Map.of(
        MAR_15, Map.of("H1", bd(80)),
        MAR_16, Collections.emptyMap()  // no hotels Mar16
    );
    var result = service.findCheapestAvailableHotel(MAR_15, MAR_17, prices);
    assertThat(result).isEmpty();
}

@Test
void singleNightStay() {
    var prices = Map.of(MAR_15, Map.of("H1", bd(80), "H2", bd(60)));
    var result = service.findCheapestAvailableHotel(MAR_15, MAR_16, prices);

    assertThat(result.get().hotelId()).isEqualTo("H2");
    assertThat(result.get().totalPrice()).isEqualByComparingTo("60");
}

👨‍💻 Tom: Clean. datesUntil() is a nice Java 9+ API that most candidates don't know. Early break on unavailable night avoids unnecessary iterations. Tests cover the exact edge cases we care about.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR Data-driven mindset 10/10 Define metrics BEFORE coding — baseline, target, method
Java Bean lifecycle 10/10 PostConstruct vs InitializingBean vs SmartLifecycle with real use cases
Java ES pagination 10/10 from/size limitations, search_after with PIT for production
Design Hotel availability system 10/10 Redis pipeline multi-date check, Kafka ordering, multi-currency
Coding Cheapest available hotel 10/10 datesUntil(), early break, 3 comprehensive tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Agoda Interview Tips

  1. Travel domain vocabulary. Check-in/check-out dates, room types, rate plans, OTA
  2. SmartLifecycle — Agoda has many background processors; know when to use it
  3. Elasticsearch search_after — they use ES heavily for hotel search
  4. Redis pipeline — multi-date availability check in single round trip
  5. Multi-currency with BigDecimal — critical; exchange rates + taxes + promotions
  6. Holiday traffic spikes — 10x is common in travel; show you've thought about it
  7. Data-driven culture — always measure before and after changes
  8. Agoda salary — 50-80M gross, English-first, international culture

🎭 Mock Interview #30 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Lazada Vietnam (Alibaba Group E-Commerce, HCMC, 1200+ engineers)

Context: Lazada is Southeast Asia's leading e-commerce platform, fully owned by Alibaba Group since 2018. Operating in 6 countries: Vietnam, Thailand, Philippines, Malaysia, Indonesia, Singapore. 160M+ annual shoppers. After Alibaba acquisition, Lazada migrated to Alibaba's technology stack: **AliCloud, RocketMQ (not Kafka), Dubbo (not REST), Canal (CDC from MySQL), AHAS (circuit breaker), and Nacos (config/discovery)**. Understanding this stack is what separates candidates who know Lazada from those who don't. Role: Logistics & Fulfillment Platform team — the system that routes packages from seller → Lazada warehouse → last-mile delivery → buyer. 3M+ parcels/day across 6 countries. Salary: 45-70M+ gross. Working language: English (SG/ID teams).

📞 ROUND 1 — HR SCREENING (20 min, English)

👩 Fiona (Recruiter, Singapore): Hi Khanh! Lazada uses Alibaba's tech stack — AliCloud, RocketMQ, Dubbo. Do you have experience with these?

👨 Khanh: Honest answer: I've worked with Kafka and Spring Cloud, not RocketMQ and Dubbo directly. But here's my perspective on why that's manageable:

RocketMQ and Kafka solve the same fundamental problem — distributed message queue — with different trade-offs. RocketMQ has stronger transaction message support, which I understand conceptually from my work with distributed transactions at Axon Active. Switching from Kafka to RocketMQ is a 2-3 week learning curve, not a 6-month one.

Similarly, Dubbo is a Java RPC framework. I've used Feign for service-to-service calls. The patterns are the same: service discovery, load balancing, circuit breaking. Different API, same concepts.

What I bring is deep understanding of the why behind distributed systems — consistency, availability, failure modes — that transfers across any specific technology.

👩 Fiona: Fair answer. Lazada is fast-paced — we ship 10+ times daily. How do you handle production incidents?

👨 Khanh: My incident response follows four steps:

  1. Mitigate first, diagnose second. If a feature flag can disable

the broken feature, do it immediately. Don't spend 20 minutes finding root cause while users are impacted.

  1. Communicate proactively. I post updates to the incident channel

every 10 minutes — "Still investigating," "Found the cause, fixing now," "Deployed fix, monitoring." Silence is the worst thing in a production incident.

  1. Document everything in real-time. I keep a running timeline

in the incident doc: "10:03 — alerts fired. 10:07 — identified memory spike. 10:12 — rolled back deployment."

  1. Post-mortem within 48 hours. Blameless — focus on system

gaps, not people. Every incident produces at least one preventive action.

👩 Fiona: Good. Technical round next — two engineers.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (70 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer + Senior Engineer.

👨‍💻 Wei (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh! We'll test both core Java knowledge and logistics domain thinking.

PART A — Java Core + Distributed Systems (25 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: **Explain RocketMQ's transaction message feature. How is it different from Kafka? When would you use it?**

👨 Khanh: This is a key differentiator of RocketMQ.

The problem: In distributed systems, you often need to do a local DB transaction AND send a message atomically. Classic example:
