// Problem 1: Circular dependency
@Service class A { @Autowired B b; }
@Service class B { @Autowired A a; }
// Spring 6 (Boot 3): circular dependency forbidden by default → startup fails
// Fix: use constructor injection + @Lazy on one side

// Problem 2: @PostConstruct too early
@Component
class CacheWarmer {
    @Autowired DataSource ds;

    @PostConstruct
    void warmUp() {
        // ⚠️ DataSource available but DB might not be ready yet
        // Use SmartLifecycle instead for post-startup initialization
    }
}

// Problem 3: Auto-configuration conflict
// Two libraries both auto-configure DataSource
// Fix: @SpringBootApplication(exclude = {DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class})

Monitoring startup time:

# Add to JVM args — shows which beans took the longest to initialize
java -Dspring.main.lazy-initialization=true -jar shopee.jar
# Lazy init: beans created on first use, not at startup → faster startup
# Trade-off: first request is slow (initializes beans on demand)

👨‍💻 Ryan: **At 11.11, our MySQL receives 50K writes/second from the order service. How do you protect it?**

👨 Khanh: Five layers, from fastest to slowest:

Layer 1: Redis absorbs the spike (pre-decrement inventory):

// Before 11.11 starts: pre-load flash sale inventory into Redis
// Redis handles inventory decrement at 100K+ ops/sec
// MySQL only updated in batch after the spike subsides

redisTemplate.opsForValue().set("flash:inventory:" + productId,
    String.valueOf(stock), Duration.ofHours(24));

// Atomic decrement during flash sale
Long remaining = redisTemplate.execute(DEDUCT_SCRIPT,
    List.of("flash:inventory:" + productId), "1");
// MySQL not touched for inventory during peak

Layer 2: Write-behind cache (async MySQL writes):

// Don't write to MySQL synchronously during peak
// Write to Redis, then batch-sync to MySQL every 500ms
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 500)
public void syncOrdersToDb() {
    List<Order> pending = redisQueue.drain(1000); // max 1000 per batch
    if (!pending.isEmpty()) {
        orderRepo.saveAll(pending); // JDBC batch insert — 1000 rows in 1 query
    }
}

Layer 3: JDBC batch insert:

spring:
  datasource:
    hikari:
      maximum-pool-size: 30
  jpa:
    properties:
      hibernate:
        jdbc:
          batch_size: 100         # insert 100 rows per SQL statement
          order_inserts: true     # group inserts of same table type
          order_updates: true

Layer 4: Vertical write sharding (separate DB for flash orders):

Flash sale orders → flash_db (dedicated MySQL, higher write capacity)
Regular orders    → main_db  (normal operations)

This gives flash sales dedicated DB resources during the event.

Layer 5: Circuit breaker — shed load gracefully:

@CircuitBreaker(name = "orderDb", fallbackMethod = "queueOrder")
public Order saveOrder(Order order) {
    return orderRepo.save(order);
}

public Order queueOrder(Order order, Exception ex) {
    // DB at limit → queue in Kafka, process when load drops
    kafkaTemplate.send("orders.overflow", order);
    return Order.queued(order.getId());
    // User sees: "Order received — confirming shortly"
}

Design: Flash Sale Pre-warm Strategy (25 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: Shopee's biggest risk at 11.11 midnight is the cold start problem. JVMs are cold, caches are empty, CDN TTLs have expired. How do you pre-warm the system?

👨 Khanh: Pre-warming is a checklist, not a single technique:

1. JVM warm-up (1 hour before):

// Canary pod receives synthetic traffic 1 hour before 11.11
// This JIT-compiles the hot code paths
// When real traffic hits, JIT is already warm

@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 23 10 11 ?") // 11 PM Nov 10th = 1 hour before
public void warmUpJVM() {
    for (int i = 0; i < 100_000; i++) {
        flashSaleService.simulatePurchase(SYNTHETIC_PRODUCT_ID, SYNTHETIC_USER);
    }
    log.info("JVM warm-up complete");
}

2. Cache pre-population (30 min before):

@Scheduled(cron = "0 30 23 10 11 ?")
public void prePopulateFlashSaleCache() {
    List<FlashProduct> products = flashSaleRepo.findAll11_11Products();

    products.parallelStream().forEach(p -> {
        // Pre-load product details into Redis
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "product:" + p.getId(), p, Duration.ofHours(2));

        // Pre-load inventory into Redis
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "flash:inventory:" + p.getId(),
            String.valueOf(p.getStock()), Duration.ofHours(2));
    });

    log.info("Pre-populated {} flash products into cache", products.size());
}

3. Connection pool pre-warm:

// HikariCP: minIdle connections = maxPoolSize (all connections open before event)
// Prevents "connection establishment latency" during spike
spring.datasource.hikari.minimum-idle: 30    # same as maximum-pool-size
spring.datasource.hikari.initialization-fail-timeout: 60000

4. K8s pre-scaling (2 hours before):

# Scale up pods before traffic hits — avoid cold-start during spike
apiVersion: autoscaling/v2
kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler
spec:
  minReplicas: 50    # normally 10, set to 50 before 11.11
  maxReplicas: 200

5. Readiness gate — don't serve until warm:

@Component
public class WarmUpReadinessCheck implements ReadinessHealthIndicator {

    private volatile boolean isWarm = false;

    @Override
    public Health getHealth(boolean includeDetails) {
        return isWarm
            ? Health.up().build()
            : Health.down().withDetail("reason", "Still warming up").build();
    }

    @PostConstruct
    void warmUp() {
        // ... do warm-up work ...
        isWarm = true; // only mark ready after warm-up complete
    }
}
// K8s waits for readiness probe before routing traffic to this pod

💻 ROUND 3 — CODING (30 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer. Flash sale specific.

👨‍💻 Mark (Sr Eng): "Implement a flash sale scheduler. *A flash sale has: productId, startTime, endTime, discountedPrice, stock.* Implement:

  • void scheduleSale(FlashSale sale) — register a future sale
  • Optional<FlashSale> getActiveSale(String productId, Instant now) — return active sale for a product if any
  • List<FlashSale> getUpcomingSales(Instant now, Duration within) — sales starting within the given duration"

👨 Khanh:

public record FlashSale(
    String saleId,
    String productId,
    Instant startTime,
    Instant endTime,
    BigDecimal discountedPrice,
    int stock
) {
    public boolean isActive(Instant now) {
        return !now.isBefore(startTime) && now.isBefore(endTime);
    }

    public boolean startsWithin(Instant now, Duration window) {
        return startTime.isAfter(now)
            && startTime.isBefore(now.plus(window));
    }
}

public class FlashSaleScheduler {

    // productId → list of sales (a product may have multiple sales at different times)
    private final Map<String, List<FlashSale>> salesByProduct =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public void scheduleSale(FlashSale sale) {
        Objects.requireNonNull(sale, "Sale cannot be null");
        if (!sale.endTime().isAfter(sale.startTime())) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("endTime must be after startTime");
        }

        salesByProduct.computeIfAbsent(sale.productId(),
            k -> new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>()).add(sale);
    }

    public Optional<FlashSale> getActiveSale(String productId, Instant now) {
        List<FlashSale> sales = salesByProduct.getOrDefault(
            productId, Collections.emptyList());

        return sales.stream()
            .filter(s -> s.isActive(now))
            .findFirst(); // at most one sale active at a time per product
    }

    public List<FlashSale> getUpcomingSales(Instant now, Duration within) {
        return salesByProduct.values().stream()
            .flatMap(List::stream)
            .filter(s -> s.startsWithin(now, within))
            .sorted(Comparator.comparing(FlashSale::startTime))
            .collect(Collectors.toList());
    }
}

Tests:

static final Instant NOW = Instant.parse("2024-11-11T00:00:00Z");

FlashSaleScheduler scheduler = new FlashSaleScheduler();

@Test
void returnsActiveSaleForProduct() {
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S1", "P1",
        NOW.minus(10, MINUTES), NOW.plus(50, MINUTES), bd("99"), 100));

    var result = scheduler.getActiveSale("P1", NOW);

    assertThat(result).isPresent();
    assertThat(result.get().saleId()).isEqualTo("S1");
}

@Test
void returnsEmptyWhenSaleNotStarted() {
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S2", "P2",
        NOW.plus(1, HOURS), NOW.plus(2, HOURS), bd("50"), 200));

    assertThat(scheduler.getActiveSale("P2", NOW)).isEmpty();
}

@Test
void returnsEmptyWhenSaleExpired() {
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S3", "P3",
        NOW.minus(2, HOURS), NOW.minus(1, HOURS), bd("30"), 50));

    assertThat(scheduler.getActiveSale("P3", NOW)).isEmpty();
}

@Test
void upcomingSalesSortedByStartTime() {
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S4", "P4",
        NOW.plus(2, HOURS), NOW.plus(3, HOURS), bd("80"), 100));
    scheduler.scheduleSale(new FlashSale("S5", "P5",
        NOW.plus(30, MINUTES), NOW.plus(1, HOURS), bd("60"), 50));

    var upcoming = scheduler.getUpcomingSales(NOW, Duration.ofHours(3));

    assertThat(upcoming).hasSize(2);
    assertThat(upcoming.get(0).saleId()).isEqualTo("S5"); // starts sooner
    assertThat(upcoming.get(1).saleId()).isEqualTo("S4");
}

@Test
void invalidSaleRejected() {
    assertThatThrownBy(() -> scheduler.scheduleSale(
        new FlashSale("S6", "P6",
            NOW.plus(2, HOURS), NOW.plus(1, HOURS), bd("10"), 100) // end < start
    )).isInstanceOf(IllegalArgumentException.class);
}

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HM Read/write split thinking 10/10 CDN for reads, Redis for writes — separated immediately
Spring Startup lifecycle 10/10 8 phases, circular dep, @Conditional, lazy init
DB MySQL protection at 50K WPS 10/10 5 layers: Redis, write-behind, JDBC batch, sharding, circuit breaker
Prep 11.11 pre-warm strategy 10/10 JVM warm-up, cache population, K8s pre-scale, readiness gate
Coding Flash sale scheduler 10/10 Records, ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList, 5 tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Shopee Flash Sale Interview Tips

  1. Read/write split — say this immediately when asked about scale
  2. Redis for inventory — not MySQL at peak. Lua script = atomic
  3. Write-behind cache — async batch flush to MySQL
  4. 11.11 pre-warming — JVM, cache, connection pool, K8s pods, readiness gate
  5. Spring startup phases — know all 8. BeanDefinition vs Bean instance distinction
  6. JDBC batch insert — batch_size: 100 + order_inserts: true in Hibernate
  7. CopyOnWriteArrayList — thread-safe list for read-heavy + occasional write
  8. Shopee salary — 60-100M gross (same as Sea Group). HCMC is a top hub

📊 Shopee Mock Coverage Summary

Mock Team Focus Topics
#26 Platform Eng Infra Consistent hash, observability platform
#34 Flash Sale Product Pre-warm, 11.11 spike, MySQL protection

🎭 Mock Interview #35 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: ZaloPay (VNG's Digital Wallet, HCMC, 15M+ users)

Note: Mock #22 covered VNG/ZaloPay broadly — Java memory model, ConcurrentHashMap, QR payment. This mock (#35) goes deeper into ZaloPay-specific fintech engineering: wallet architecture, KYC tiers, P2P transfer limits, bank settlement, and transaction velocity control. Role: Core Wallet team — the engine behind every ZaloPay balance operation: top-up, P2P transfer, bill payment, withdrawal to bank. Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, NAPAS APIs.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR (15 min, Vietnamese/English)

👩 Trang (HR): Chào anh Khanh! ZaloPay đang mở rộng rất nhanh — đang cạnh tranh trực tiếp với MoMo. Anh thấy điểm khác biệt của ZaloPay so với MoMo là gì từ góc nhìn engineering?

👨 Khanh: Từ góc nhìn engineering, ZaloPay có lợi thế lớn là Zalo ecosystem — 70M user Zalo có thể onboard ZaloPay friction-free. Điều đó tạo ra một bài toán rất thú vị: làm sao handle growth burst khi một feature Zalo như "Lì xì" hay "Chia bill" viral — đột nhiên có hàng triệu user mới active trong vài giờ.

MoMo build merchant network rộng hơn, nhưng ZaloPay có distribution channel độc quyền qua Zalo. Về technical, cả hai đều phải giải bài toán tương tự — atomic balance operations, fraud detection, NAPAS settlement. Sự khác biệt là scale-up pattern của ZaloPay phụ thuộc vào viral social feature hơn là merchant-driven.

👩 Trang: Anh hiểu domain rất rõ. Technical rounds sẽ focus vào wallet core và payments. Good luck anh!

💻 ROUND 2 — WALLET ARCHITECTURE (55 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Core Wallet team.

👨‍💻 Hưng (Staff Eng): Hi Khanh! ZaloPay's wallet is the heart of everything. Let's go deep.

PART A — KYC Tiers & Transaction Limits (20 min)

👨‍💻 Hưng: **ZaloPay has 3 KYC tiers. Tier 1 (phone only): max 20M VND/day. Tier 2 (CCCD): max 100M VND/day. Tier 3 (full KYC + face): unlimited. How do you enforce these limits in code while handling concurrent transactions?**

👨 Khanh: Two-layer enforcement: Redis for real-time speed, PostgreSQL for accuracy. 

public enum KycTier {
    TIER_1(20_000_000L),   // 20M VND/day
    TIER_2(100_000_000L),  // 100M VND/day
    TIER_3(Long.MAX_VALUE); // unlimited

    private final long dailyLimitVnd;
}

@Service
public class TransactionLimitService {

    // Redis key: "limit:{userId}:{date}" → total spent today (VND)
    private static final String LIMIT_KEY = "limit:%s:%s";

    private static final String CHECK_AND_RESERVE_SCRIPT = """
        local current = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1])) or 0
        local limit = tonumber(ARGV[1])
        local amount = tonumber(ARGV[2])
        if current + amount > limit then
            return 0  -- exceeds limit
        end
        redis.call('INCRBY', KEYS[1], amount)
        redis.call('EXPIRE', KEYS[1], 86400)  -- expires at midnight-ish
        return 1  -- approved
        """;

    public void checkAndReserveDailyLimit(String userId, long amountVnd) {
        User user = userRepo.findById(userId).orElseThrow();
        long dailyLimit = user.getKycTier().getDailyLimitVnd();

        if (dailyLimit == Long.MAX_VALUE) return; // Tier 3 — no limit check

        String today = LocalDate.now(ZoneId.of("Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh")).toString();
        String key = String.format(LIMIT_KEY, userId, today);

        Long result = redisTemplate.execute(
            new DefaultRedisScript<>(CHECK_AND_RESERVE_SCRIPT, Long.class),
            List.of(key),
            String.valueOf(dailyLimit),
            String.valueOf(amountVnd)
        );

        if (!Long.valueOf(1L).equals(result)) {
            long used = getCurrentDailyUsage(userId);
            throw new DailyLimitExceededException(
                String.format("Daily limit exceeded. Used: %,d VND, Limit: %,d VND",
                    used, dailyLimit));
        }
    }

    // Reconciliation: nightly job verifies Redis vs PostgreSQL
    @Scheduled(cron = "0 55 23 * * *")  // 11:55 PM daily
    public void reconcileDailyLimits() {
        LocalDate today = LocalDate.now(ZoneId.of("Asia/Ho_Chi_Minh"));
        List<String> activeUsers = txnRepo.findUsersWithTransactionsToday(today);

        for (String userId : activeUsers) {
            long redisUsage  = getCurrentDailyUsage(userId);
            long dbUsage     = txnRepo.sumSuccessfulToday(userId, today);
            if (redisUsage != dbUsage) {
                log.error("Limit mismatch for user {}: Redis={}, DB={}", userId, redisUsage, dbUsage);
                alertOpsTeam(userId, redisUsage, dbUsage);
                // Correct Redis from DB (DB is source of truth)
                String key = String.format(LIMIT_KEY, userId, today.toString());
                redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(key, String.valueOf(dbUsage));
            }
        }
    }
}

Why Redis Lua script? Atomic check-and-increment in one operation. Without it, two concurrent transfers could both pass the check before either updates the counter — a classic TOCTOU race condition.

👨‍💻 Hưng: **ZaloPay's Lì xì (Red Envelope) feature goes viral every Tết — 5M users send red envelopes in the first hour. The feature requires: sender locks a total amount, splits it randomly among N recipients, each recipient can claim once. Design the claim mechanism under this load.**

👨 Khanh: Classic "grab red envelope" problem — used by WeChat, ZaloPay, MoMo every Tết.

Pre-split approach (recommended for ZaloPay scale):

When sender creates envelope:
  1. Total: 500,000 VND, Recipients: 10
  2. Pre-generate 10 random amounts that sum to 500,000
  3. Store as a Redis List: [45000, 62000, 38000, 71000, ...]
  4. Deduct 500,000 from sender immediately (atomic)

When recipient claims:
  1. LPOP from Redis List (atomic, O(1))
  2. If nil → envelope exhausted → show "all claimed"
  3. If value → credit that amount to recipient wallet
@Service
public class RedEnvelopeService {

    public RedEnvelope create(String senderId, long totalVnd, int recipients) {
        // 1. Deduct from sender atomically
        walletService.debit(senderId, totalVnd, "Red envelope created");

        // 2. Pre-split into random amounts
        List<Long> amounts = randomSplit(totalVnd, recipients);

        // 3. Store in Redis List (LIFO stack — each claim pops one)
        String envelopeId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();
        String key = "envelope:" + envelopeId;
        redisTemplate.opsForList().rightPushAll(key,
            amounts.stream().map(String::valueOf).toArray(String[]::new));
        redisTemplate.expire(key, Duration.ofDays(1)); // expires after 1 day

        // 4. Persist metadata to DB
        envelopeRepo.save(RedEnvelope.of(envelopeId, senderId, totalVnd, recipients));
        return envelopeRepo.findById(envelopeId).orElseThrow();
    }

    @Transactional
    public ClaimResult claim(String envelopeId, String recipientId) {
        // Idempotency: already claimed?
        if (claimRepo.existsByEnvelopeAndRecipient(envelopeId, recipientId)) {
            return ClaimResult.alreadyClaimed();
        }

        // Atomic pop from Redis List
        String key = "envelope:" + envelopeId;
        String amountStr = redisTemplate.opsForList().leftPop(key);

        if (amountStr == null) {
            return ClaimResult.exhausted(); // all claimed
        }

        long amount = Long.parseLong(amountStr);

        // Credit recipient
        walletService.credit(recipientId, amount, "Red envelope from " + envelopeId);

        // Record claim
        claimRepo.save(new EnvelopeClaim(envelopeId, recipientId, amount));

        return ClaimResult.success(amount);
    }

    // Random split: generates N amounts summing to total
    private List<Long> randomSplit(long total, int n) {
        if (n == 1) return List.of(total);
        Random rng = new Random();
        List<Long> amounts = new ArrayList<>();
        long remaining = total;
        for (int i = 0; i < n - 1; i++) {
            // Each amount: between 1 VND and (remaining / (n-i) * 2)
            long max = Math.min(remaining - (n - i - 1), remaining * 2L / (n - i));
            long amt = 1 + (long)(rng.nextDouble() * (max - 1));
            amounts.add(amt);
            remaining -= amt;
        }
        amounts.add(remaining); // last gets the remainder
        Collections.shuffle(amounts); // so last recipient isn't always largest
        return amounts;
    }
}

Why LPOP is safe at 5M concurrent claims? Redis LPOP is atomic — only one caller gets each element. No two users claim the same amount. No oversplit possible.

PART B — NAPAS Bank Settlement (20 min)

👨‍💻 Hưng: **When a user withdraws from ZaloPay to their bank account, we send the request to NAPAS (Vietnam's interbank network). NAPAS can take 1-30 seconds to respond and sometimes doesn't respond at all. Design the withdrawal flow.**

👨 Khanh: Three-phase design: Reserve → Submit → Confirm/Rollback.

@Service
public class WithdrawalService {

    @Transactional
    public WithdrawalResponse initiateWithdrawal(WithdrawalRequest req) {
        String withdrawalId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

        // Phase 1: Reserve funds (debit ZaloPay wallet)
        walletService.debit(req.getUserId(), req.getAmountVnd(), withdrawalId);

        // Persist withdrawal in PENDING state
        Withdrawal withdrawal = withdrawalRepo.save(Withdrawal.builder()
            .id(withdrawalId)
            .userId(req.getUserId())
            .amountVnd(req.getAmountVnd())
            .bankCode(req.getBankCode())
            .bankAccountNo(req.getAccountNumber())
            .status(WithdrawalStatus.PENDING)
            .build());

        // Phase 2: Submit to NAPAS async (don't block user)
        kafkaTemplate.send("withdrawals.pending",
            withdrawalId, new NapasSubmitMessage(withdrawal));

        return WithdrawalResponse.accepted(withdrawalId,
            "Withdrawal processing — funds will arrive within 30 seconds");
    }
}

@KafkaListener(topics = "withdrawals.pending", groupId = "napas-submitter")
public void submitToNapas(NapasSubmitMessage msg) {
    Withdrawal w = withdrawalRepo.findById(msg.getWithdrawalId()).orElseThrow();

    if (w.getStatus() != PENDING) return; // idempotent

    try {
        NapasResponse response = napasClient.submitTransfer(
            NapasTransferRequest.from(w),
            Duration.ofSeconds(30)  // 30s timeout
        );

        if (response.isSuccess()) {
            // Phase 3a: Confirm
            w.setStatus(COMPLETED);
            w.setNapasRef(response.getReferenceNo());
            notificationService.notifySuccess(w);
        } else {
            // Phase 3b: Permanent failure (invalid account, etc.)
            w.setStatus(FAILED_PERMANENT);
            walletService.credit(w.getUserId(), w.getAmountVnd(), "Refund: " + w.getId());
            notificationService.notifyFailure(w, response.getErrorMessage());
        }

    } catch (TimeoutException | NapasUnavailableException ex) {
        // Timeout — NAPAS didn't respond in 30s
        w.setStatus(NAPAS_TIMEOUT);
        w.setRetryCount(w.getRetryCount() + 1);
        log.warn("NAPAS timeout for withdrawal {}, retry #{}", w.getId(), w.getRetryCount());
    }

    withdrawalRepo.save(w);
}

// Recovery job: poll NAPAS for timed-out withdrawals
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 60_000)
public void recoverTimedOutWithdrawals() {
    List<Withdrawal> stuck = withdrawalRepo.findByStatusAndRetryCountLessThan(
        NAPAS_TIMEOUT, 5
    );

    for (Withdrawal w : stuck) {
        // Query NAPAS for status using our reference
        try {
            NapasStatusResponse status = napasClient.queryStatus(w.getId());
            if (status.isCompleted()) {
                w.setStatus(COMPLETED);
                notificationService.notifySuccess(w);
            } else if (status.isFailed()) {
                w.setStatus(FAILED_PERMANENT);
                walletService.credit(w.getUserId(), w.getAmountVnd(), "Refund timeout: " + w.getId());
            }
            // Still pending in NAPAS → leave for next check
        } catch (Exception e) {
            log.error("Recovery check failed for {}", w.getId(), e);
        }
        withdrawalRepo.save(w);
    }

    // After 5 retries → escalate to ops team
    withdrawalRepo.findByStatusAndRetryCountGreaterThanEqual(NAPAS_TIMEOUT, 5)
        .forEach(w -> alertOpsTeam(w, "Withdrawal stuck after 5 NAPAS retries"));
}

State machine:

PENDING → [submit to NAPAS]
    ├── SUCCESS  → COMPLETED
    ├── PERM ERR → FAILED_PERMANENT → refund user
    └── TIMEOUT  → NAPAS_TIMEOUT → retry up to 5x
                                 → after 5x → manual ops review

PART C — CODING: Velocity Check (15 min)

👨‍💻 Hưng: *"Implement a velocity checker. A user is suspicious if they make more than 3 transactions above 5,000,000 VND within any 1-hour sliding window. Return true if the user's latest transaction triggers the alert."*

👨 Khanh:

public class VelocityChecker {

    private static final long THRESHOLD_VND    = 5_000_000L;
    private static final int  MAX_HIGH_VALUE   = 3;
    private static final long WINDOW_MS        = 60 * 60 * 1000L; // 1 hour

    // userId → deque of timestamps of high-value transactions
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deque<Long>> windows =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public boolean checkVelocity(String userId, long amountVnd, long timestampMs) {
        if (amountVnd < THRESHOLD_VND) return false; // low-value — no alert

        Deque<Long> window = windows.computeIfAbsent(userId,
            k -> new ArrayDeque<>());

        synchronized (window) {
            long cutoff = timestampMs - WINDOW_MS;

            // Remove transactions outside 1-hour window
            while (!window.isEmpty() && window.peekFirst() <= cutoff) {
                window.pollFirst();
            }

            // Add current transaction
            window.addLast(timestampMs);

            // Alert if more than 3 high-value txns in window
            return window.size() > MAX_HIGH_VALUE;
        }
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void noAlertForLowValueTransaction() {
    var vc = new VelocityChecker();
    assertThat(vc.checkVelocity("U1", 1_000_000L, 0L)).isFalse();
}

@Test
void alertOnFourthHighValueTransactionInWindow() {
    var vc = new VelocityChecker();
    long base = System.currentTimeMillis();
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base);
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 10_000);
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 20_000);
    // 4th → alert
    assertThat(vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 30_000)).isTrue();
}

@Test
void windowSlidesCorrectly() {
    var vc = new VelocityChecker();
    long base = 0L;
    // 3 high-value transactions
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base);
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 10_000);
    vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + 20_000);

    // 4th transaction at base + 2 hours → first 3 have expired
    long twoHoursLater = base + 2 * 60 * 60 * 1000L;
    assertThat(vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, twoHoursLater)).isFalse();
}

@Test
void isolatesUsersIndependently() {
    var vc = new VelocityChecker();
    long base = System.currentTimeMillis();
    for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) vc.checkVelocity("U1", 10_000_000L, base + i * 1000);
    // U2 has zero history — should not alert
    assertThat(vc.checkVelocity("U2", 10_000_000L, base)).isFalse();
}

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR (VN) ZaloPay vs MoMo insight 10/10 Zalo distribution advantage + viral feature burst analysis
Wallet KYC tiers + Redis Lua limit 10/10 Atomic check-and-reserve, nightly reconciliation
Feature Lì xì red envelope at Tết scale 10/10 Pre-split + Redis LPOP — atomic, O(1) per claim
Payments NAPAS withdrawal state machine 10/10 3-phase flow, timeout recovery job, 5-retry escalation
Coding Velocity checker 10/10 Sliding window + synchronized deque + 4 edge case tests

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 ZaloPay Interview Tips

  1. Zalo integration advantage — viral features cause burst traffic, not steady growth
  2. Redis Lua for limit enforcement — atomic check-and-increment. TOCTOU without it
  3. Pre-split red envelope — don't calculate amounts on claim (race condition). Pre-split at creation
  4. NAPAS timeout is normal — design recovery polling job, never assume NAPAS responds
  5. KYC tiers = limit tiers — 3 SBV-mandated levels. Know: 20M / 100M / unlimited
  6. Velocity check uses sliding window — same pattern as fraud detection
  7. ZaloPay salary — 45-70M+ gross. Part of VNG Group benefits
  8. Vietnamese HR round — be a ZaloPay user, reference real features (Lì xì, chia bill, nạp điện)

🎭 Mock Interview #36 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Stripe (Global Payments Infrastructure, San Francisco / Remote)

Role: Backend Engineer — Payments Core team. Focus: charge lifecycle, idempotency, webhook delivery, and fraud-resistant payment flows. Tech: Java 17, gRPC, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, Stripe internal frameworks.

📞 ROUND 1 — HR / CULTURE FIT (15 min, English)

👩 Sarah (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Stripe's mission is to grow the GDP of the internet. What excites you most about building payment infrastructure at this scale?

👨 Khanh: What excites me is the reliability contract Stripe holds with its merchants. When a charge fails — even once — a merchant loses a sale, possibly a customer. That responsibility pushes engineering to a different standard: idempotency-by-default, exhaustive retry semantics, and observable failure modes. I've been building similar systems in fintech — handling atomic wallet debits, NAPAS settlements, and Kafka-based async reconciliation — but Stripe's scale (hundreds of millions of API calls per day) makes every design decision much higher-stakes. I want to work on infrastructure where correctness is non-negotiable.

👩 Sarah: Great answer. Stripe cares a lot about written communication. How do you handle technical disagreement with peers?

👨 Khanh: I write a short doc — problem, options, tradeoffs, my recommendation. Then I invite async feedback before any meeting. Written discussion surfaces edge cases faster than verbal debate and leaves a decision trail. If we're still split, I prefer a time-boxed experiment over a long debate — let data decide.

👩 Sarah: Love it. Three technical rounds follow. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — PAYMENTS CORE (55 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Payments Core team.

PART A — Idempotency Keys (20 min)

👨‍💻 Alex (Staff Eng): Stripe's API requires clients to pass an Idempotency-Key header so that retrying a failed request doesn't double-charge. Walk me through how you'd implement this server-side.

👨 Khanh: Three-layer approach: Redis for in-flight dedup, PostgreSQL for durable result storage, and a middleware interceptor.

// --- Domain model ---
@Entity
@Table(name = "idempotency_keys")
public class IdempotencyRecord {
    @Id private String key;            // client-supplied UUID
    private String requestFingerprint; // SHA-256(method + path + body)
    private String responseBody;       // serialised JSON
    private int    httpStatus;
    private IdempotencyStatus status;  // IN_FLIGHT | COMPLETED | FAILED
    private Instant createdAt;
    private Instant expiresAt;         // 24h per Stripe spec
}

// --- Interceptor (runs before every mutating endpoint) ---
@Component
public class IdempotencyInterceptor implements HandlerInterceptor {

    private static final String LOCK_PREFIX = "idempotency:lock:";
    private static final Duration LOCK_TTL  = Duration.ofSeconds(30);

    @Override
    public boolean preHandle(HttpServletRequest req,
                             HttpServletResponse res,
                             Object handler) throws Exception {
        String idempKey = req.getHeader("Idempotency-Key");
        if (idempKey == null) return true; // GET/DELETE — skip

        String fingerprint = fingerprintOf(req);

        // 1. Check durable store first
        Optional<IdempotencyRecord> existing =
            idempotencyRepo.findById(idempKey);

        if (existing.isPresent()) {
            IdempotencyRecord rec = existing.get();

            // Fingerprint mismatch → same key, different request body
            if (!rec.getRequestFingerprint().equals(fingerprint)) {
                res.sendError(422,
                    "Idempotency-Key reused with different request body");
                return false;
            }

            if (rec.getStatus() == COMPLETED) {
                // Return cached response — no re-processing
                res.setStatus(rec.getHttpStatus());
                res.getWriter().write(rec.getResponseBody());
                return false; // short-circuit
            }

            if (rec.getStatus() == IN_FLIGHT) {
                // Another thread is processing — ask client to retry
                res.sendError(409,
                    "A request with this Idempotency-Key is already in flight");
                return false;
            }
        }

        // 2. Acquire distributed lock (prevents concurrent duplicate)
        boolean locked = redisTemplate.opsForValue()
            .setIfAbsent(LOCK_PREFIX + idempKey, "1", LOCK_TTL);

        if (!locked) {
            res.sendError(409, "Concurrent request with same Idempotency-Key");
            return false;
        }

        // 3. Insert IN_FLIGHT record
        idempotencyRepo.save(IdempotencyRecord.builder()
            .key(idempKey)
            .requestFingerprint(fingerprint)
            .status(IN_FLIGHT)
            .createdAt(Instant.now())
            .expiresAt(Instant.now().plus(Duration.ofHours(24)))
            .build());

        req.setAttribute("idempotency_key", idempKey);
        return true; // proceed to controller
    }

    private String fingerprintOf(HttpServletRequest req) throws Exception {
        String raw = req.getMethod() + req.getRequestURI()
                   + IOUtils.toString(req.getReader());
        return Hashing.sha256().hashString(raw, UTF_8).toString();
    }
}

// --- After controller returns, mark COMPLETED ---
@Component
public class IdempotencyResponseAdvice
    implements ResponseBodyAdvice<Object> {

    @Override
    public Object beforeBodyWrite(Object body, ...,
            ServerHttpRequest req, ServerHttpResponse res) {
        String key = /* retrieve from request attribute */ ...;
        if (key != null) {
            idempotencyRepo.markCompleted(key,
                serializeToJson(body), res.getHeaders().getStatusCode().value());
            redisTemplate.delete("idempotency:lock:" + key);
        }
        return body;
    }
}

Key decisions:

  • Redis lock prevents two simultaneous requests with the same key

from both entering the charge flow — only one wins.

  • Fingerprint check catches client bugs (same key, different amount).
  • IN_FLIGHT state handles crash-recovery: if the server dies mid-flight,

the record stays IN_FLIGHT. A sweep job can unlock it after TTL.

  • 24h expiry matches Stripe's public spec.

👨‍💻 Alex: What happens if the server crashes after debiting the card but before writing COMPLETED? The client retries — what do you do?

👨 Khanh: This is the core challenge. The charge already happened at the payment processor level. On retry:

1. Idempotency record is still IN_FLIGHT (crash = no COMPLETED write)
2. Lock has expired (30s TTL)
3. Retry request acquires lock, sees IN_FLIGHT

Strategy:
  - Check the downstream charge table by (idempotency_key) index.
  - If a charge row exists → processor already charged → reconstruct
    response from charge row → mark COMPLETED → return cached response.
  - If no charge row → safe to retry the charge attempt.
// In charge service, always write charge row FIRST (with idempotency_key)
// before calling payment processor. Use outbox pattern.

@Transactional
public ChargeResult initiateCharge(ChargeRequest req, String idempKey) {
    // 1. Write PENDING charge to DB atomically with business transaction
    Charge charge = chargeRepo.save(Charge.builder()
        .id(UUID.randomUUID().toString())
        .idempotencyKey(idempKey)
        .amount(req.getAmount())
        .currency(req.getCurrency())
        .status(ChargeStatus.PENDING)
        .build());

    // 2. Publish to outbox (processed by Kafka relay)
    outboxRepo.save(OutboxEvent.of("charge.initiate", charge.getId()));

    return ChargeResult.pending(charge.getId());
    // Stripe/processor called asynchronously — never in this transaction
}

Now crash safety is guaranteed: DB transaction is atomic, outbox relay retries until processor confirms. Idempotency key lookup always finds the charge row on retry.

PART B — Webhook Delivery (20 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: Stripe delivers webhooks to merchant endpoints. A merchant endpoint can be slow, return 5xx, or time out. Design the delivery system with at-least-once semantics and exponential backoff.

👨 Khanh: Event-driven delivery pipeline with a retry scheduler.

@Entity
public class WebhookDelivery {
    @Id private String id;
    private String   eventId;
    private String   merchantEndpointUrl;
    private String   payload;           // JSON event body
    private int      attemptCount;      // starts at 0
    private Instant  nextAttemptAt;
    private DeliveryStatus status;      // PENDING | SUCCEEDED | FAILED | EXHAUSTED
}

@Service
public class WebhookScheduler {

    // Exponential backoff: 5s, 30s, 5m, 30m, 2h, 5h, 10h (max 7 retries)
    private static final long[] BACKOFF_SECONDS =
        {5, 30, 300, 1800, 7200, 18000, 36000};

    @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 5000)
    @Transactional
    public void dispatchDue() {
        List<WebhookDelivery> due = deliveryRepo
            .findDueForDelivery(Instant.now(), 100); // batch of 100

        for (WebhookDelivery d : due) {
            dispatch(d);
        }
    }

    private void dispatch(WebhookDelivery delivery) {
        try {
            HttpResponse<String> response = httpClient.send(
                HttpRequest.newBuilder()
                    .uri(URI.create(delivery.getMerchantEndpointUrl()))
                    .POST(HttpRequest.BodyPublishers.ofString(delivery.getPayload()))
                    .header("Stripe-Signature", sign(delivery.getPayload()))
                    .timeout(Duration.ofSeconds(30))
                    .build(),
                HttpResponse.BodyHandlers.ofString()
            );

            if (response.statusCode() >= 200 && response.statusCode() < 300) {
                delivery.setStatus(SUCCEEDED);
            } else {
                scheduleRetry(delivery);
            }

        } catch (IOException | InterruptedException e) {
            scheduleRetry(delivery);
        }

        deliveryRepo.save(delivery);
    }

    private void scheduleRetry(WebhookDelivery d) {
        int attempt = d.getAttemptCount();
        if (attempt >= BACKOFF_SECONDS.length) {
            d.setStatus(EXHAUSTED);
            alertMerchantDashboard(d);
            return;
        }
        d.setAttemptCount(attempt + 1);
        d.setNextAttemptAt(Instant.now().plusSeconds(BACKOFF_SECONDS[attempt]));
        d.setStatus(PENDING);
    }
}

Merchant-side idempotency: Each event has a unique event_id. Merchants must deduplicate by event_id — Stripe's contract is at-least-once, not exactly-once.

Signature (Stripe-Signature):

private String sign(String payload) {
    long ts = Instant.now().getEpochSecond();
    String signed = ts + "." + payload;
    String hmac = computeHmacSha256(webhookSecret, signed);
    return "t=" + ts + ",v1=" + hmac;
}

Merchant verifies timestamp is within 5 minutes to prevent replay attacks.

PART C — CODING: Charge Rate Limiter (15 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: *"Implement a per-merchant rate limiter: max 100 charges per second per merchant. Use a token bucket algorithm. Thread-safe."*

👨 Khanh:

public class TokenBucketRateLimiter {

    private final int    capacity;       // max tokens (burst limit)
    private final double refillRatePerMs; // tokens added per millisecond

    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, BucketState> buckets =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public TokenBucketRateLimiter(int requestsPerSecond) {
        this.capacity       = requestsPerSecond;
        this.refillRatePerMs = requestsPerSecond / 1000.0;
    }

    public boolean allowRequest(String merchantId) {
        BucketState bucket = buckets.computeIfAbsent(merchantId,
            k -> new BucketState(capacity, System.currentTimeMillis()));

        synchronized (bucket) {
            long now      = System.currentTimeMillis();
            long elapsedMs = now - bucket.lastRefillTime;

            // Refill tokens based on elapsed time
            double newTokens = elapsedMs * refillRatePerMs;
            bucket.tokens    = Math.min(capacity, bucket.tokens + newTokens);
            bucket.lastRefillTime = now;

            if (bucket.tokens >= 1.0) {
                bucket.tokens -= 1.0;
                return true;   // request allowed
            }
            return false;      // throttled
        }
    }

    private static class BucketState {
        double tokens;
        long   lastRefillTime;

        BucketState(double initialTokens, long now) {
            this.tokens        = initialTokens;
            this.lastRefillTime = now;
        }
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void allowsUpToLimit() {
    var limiter = new TokenBucketRateLimiter(100);
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) {
        assertThat(limiter.allowRequest("merchant_A")).isTrue();
    }
    assertThat(limiter.allowRequest("merchant_A")).isFalse(); // 101st denied
}

@Test
void refillsOverTime() throws InterruptedException {
    var limiter = new TokenBucketRateLimiter(100);
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) limiter.allowRequest("merchant_B");
    // Bucket empty — wait 10ms → ~1 token refilled
    Thread.sleep(10);
    assertThat(limiter.allowRequest("merchant_B")).isTrue();
}

@Test
void isolatesMerchants() {
    var limiter = new TokenBucketRateLimiter(100);
    for (int i = 0; i < 100; i++) limiter.allowRequest("merchant_C");
    // merchant_D is untouched — full bucket
    assertThat(limiter.allowRequest("merchant_D")).isTrue();
}

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer, Platform.

👨‍💻 Maya (Principal Eng): Design Stripe's charge status notification system. When a charge transitions (pending → succeeded, pending → failed), merchants need to know in near-real-time. They have registered webhook endpoints, but also poll our API. Scale: 500K charge events/minute.

👨 Khanh: Three-layer architecture: event capture → fan-out → delivery.

[Charge Service]
      │ publishes domain event to Kafka topic: charge.events
      ▼
[Event Processor] (Kafka consumer group)
      │
      ├─→ [Webhook Dispatcher] → merchant HTTPS endpoint (async, retry)
      │
      ├─→ [SSE/Polling Cache] → Redis — stores last N events per merchant
      │                          (merchant polls GET /v1/events?after=...)
      │
      └─→ [Analytics Sink] → BigQuery / data warehouse

Kafka topic design:

Topic: charge.events
Partitions: 100
Partition key: merchant_id
  → all events for merchant_X go to same partition
  → preserves ordering per merchant
Retention: 7 days (replay window)

Webhook fan-out:

@KafkaListener(topics = "charge.events", groupId = "webhook-fanout")
public void onChargeEvent(ChargeEvent event) {
    List<WebhookEndpoint> endpoints =
        endpointRepo.findActiveByMerchantId(event.getMerchantId());

    for (WebhookEndpoint ep : endpoints) {
        // Insert delivery record — scheduler picks it up
        deliveryRepo.save(WebhookDelivery.builder()
            .eventId(event.getId())
            .merchantEndpointUrl(ep.getUrl())
            .payload(serialize(event))
            .status(PENDING)
            .nextAttemptAt(Instant.now())
            .build());
    }
}

Polling (GET /v1/events):

Redis sorted set per merchant:
  Key:   events:{merchantId}
  Score: event_unix_timestamp
  Value: serialized event JSON

Query: ZRANGEBYSCORE events:{merchantId} {after_ts} +inf LIMIT 100

TTL: 72h (events expire from Redis, merchant must use webhooks for older)

Handling 500K events/minute:

  • 100 Kafka partitions → 100 parallel consumer threads
  • Webhook delivery is async (DB-backed queue) → fan-out doesn't block Kafka consumer
  • Redis sorted set reads are O(log N) — scales to millions of stored events
  • Webhook dispatcher horizontally scalable — add pods, same Postgres queue

Failure modes:

Failure Mitigation
Kafka consumer lag Alert at 30s lag; scale consumer group
Merchant endpoint down Exponential backoff, 7 retries, then EXHAUSTED
Redis eviction Events still in Kafka (7d) + Postgres; Redis is cache only
Duplicate delivery Merchant deduplicates on event_id

💻 ROUND 4 — BEHAVIOURAL (30 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager.

👩 Priya (EM): Tell me about a time you had to make a high-stakes architectural decision under time pressure.

👨 Khanh: (STAR)

Situation: At my previous company, we had a payment processing service that used synchronous HTTP calls to our banking partner for every transaction. During a promotion event, traffic spiked 10x and our banking partner's API started timing out at 8 seconds — our own API timeout was 5 seconds, causing cascading failures. We had 48 hours before a major campaign.

Task: I needed to redesign the integration to be resilient to partner slowness without a full rewrite.

Action: I proposed an async outbox pattern: write the payment intent to our DB within the user's transaction, publish to Kafka, and have a separate consumer call the banking API. The user gets an immediate "processing" response. I wrote a design doc in 3 hours, got async approval from 2 senior engineers, and we implemented it in a feature branch over 36 hours with two other engineers. We added a status-polling endpoint for the frontend so users could see real-time updates.

Result: Campaign launched successfully. Throughput increased 8x. Banking partner slowness became invisible to users. We later generalised the outbox pattern to all third-party integrations.

👩 Priya: How do you keep quality high when moving fast?

👨 Khanh: Three things: mandatory integration tests for every new happy path and top-2 failure paths — written before the PR is reviewed, not after. Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) for any decision that's hard to reverse. And blameless postmortems — I write them even for near-misses, not just outages. Speed without quality debt is a myth; these three habits keep the debt manageable.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
HR Culture + written communication 10/10 GDP of the internet framing; data-over-debate
Idempotency Key design + crash safety 10/10 Redis lock + fingerprint + outbox crash recovery
Webhooks At-least-once delivery + backoff 10/10 Exponential backoff, HMAC signature, replay safety
Coding Token bucket rate limiter 10/10 Thread-safe, lazy refill, 3 edge case tests
System Design Charge notification at 500K/min 10/10 Kafka fan-out, Redis polling cache, failure table
Behavioural High-stakes decision + quality habits 10/10 STAR with outbox, ADRs, postmortems

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Stripe Interview Tips

  1. Idempotency is Stripe's religion — know: key + fingerprint + IN_FLIGHT state + crash recovery via outbox
  2. Webhook at-least-once — merchant must deduplicate on event_id; Stripe guarantees delivery, not exactly-once
  3. HMAC-SHA256 signatures — timestamp + payload, verify within 5 minutes to block replay attacks
  4. Token bucket > fixed window — fixed window allows 2x burst at boundary; token bucket is smooth
  5. Outbox pattern — write intent to DB atomically, relay to external system via Kafka; crash-safe
  6. Kafka partition key = merchant_id — preserves per-merchant event ordering at scale
  7. Stripe values written communication — every design decision should be a short doc, not a verbal chat
  8. Salary range (Remote/APAC): USD 180K–250K base + equity; highly competitive even outside SF

🎭 Mock Interview #37 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Atlassian (Sydney / Remote-first, Jira & Confluence platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Jira Platform team. This mock focuses heavily on **English communication, articulation, and behavioural depth**. Technical questions are present but the scoring emphasis is on how you explain, not just what you know. Atlassian is known for: remote-first culture, written-first communication, and a values-driven interview process ("TEAM Anywhere").

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (20 min, English)

👩 Emma (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Thanks for taking the time today. Before we dive in, I'd love to hear — in your own words, not from your CV — what has your engineering journey looked like so far?

👨 Khanh: Sure! So I started my career building backend services for a logistics company — fairly standard CRUD work, Spring Boot, MySQL. But the turning point for me was when I joined a fintech startup. Suddenly, correctness wasn't just a nice-to-have. If a wallet balance was wrong by even one cent, someone's money was missing. That shifted how I think about software entirely.

From there, I got really invested in things like distributed transactions, idempotency, and observability — not because they're fashionable, but because I'd seen what happens without them. Most recently I've been working on high-throughput payment systems and I've started leading design reviews for my team's critical services.

So in short: I grew from writing code that works, to writing code that I can defend at 2am when something breaks in production.

👩 Emma: I love that framing — "code you can defend at 2am." Atlassian is very much a remote-first company. How do you communicate with your team when you're not in the same room?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, going remote made me a better communicator because I couldn't rely on tapping someone on the shoulder anymore.

My main habits are: I default to writing over talking. If I'm making a non-trivial decision, I write a short doc — context, options, recommendation — and share it async before scheduling any meeting. That way, people can engage on their own time and the discussion is already productive by the time we meet.

For day-to-day work, I'm very deliberate about status visibility. I update tickets with short, factual notes — not to prove I'm working, but so teammates in different time zones aren't blocked waiting for me to wake up. And I try to over-communicate blockers early rather than struggling quietly.

I'll be honest — I still sometimes default to a quick verbal chat when async feels slower. But I've learned that written context almost always saves more time later than it costs upfront.

👩 Emma: That's a very self-aware answer. One more: why Atlassian specifically, rather than another big tech company?

👨 Khanh: A few honest reasons.

First, Atlassian's products are in the workflow itself — Jira and Confluence are the tools engineers use to build everything else. Working on platform infrastructure that other engineers depend on every day is a different kind of responsibility, and I find that genuinely motivating.

Second, I've read about the TEAM Anywhere model and the emphasis on written communication. That aligns with how I already work. I'm not looking for a company I have to adapt to — I'm looking for one where my habits are an asset, not a liability.

And third — the engineering challenges. Jira serves millions of projects. Issues like multi-tenancy, search at scale, and notification fan-out are exactly the class of problems I want to work on next.

👩 Emma: Perfect. I'll pass you through to the technical rounds. You'll have two: a values interview and a system discussion. Good luck, Khanh!

💬 ROUND 2 — VALUES & BEHAVIOURAL (45 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer + Engineering Manager panel. Note: This round is 100% communication. No code. Answers are scored on: clarity, structure (STAR), honesty, and self-awareness.

SECTION A — Handling Complexity

👨‍💻 James (Sr Eng): Tell me about the most technically complex problem you've solved. I'm less interested in the technology and more interested in how you thought through it.

👨 Khanh: Happy to. The problem was a distributed payment idempotency bug — one of those bugs that only appeared under specific race conditions and was completely invisible in testing.

Here's how I thought through it.

First, I resisted the urge to guess. The symptom was occasional duplicate charges — maybe one in ten thousand transactions. My instinct was to jump to the obvious cause: a retry without an idempotency check. But I'd already seen that check in the code, so I knew it wasn't that simple.

**I started by writing down what I knew for certain versus what I was assuming.** This sounds basic, but it immediately surfaced a gap: I was assuming our Redis lock was always acquired before the DB write. But what if Redis was briefly unavailable and we silently fell through? I traced the code path and found exactly that — a fallback that bypassed the lock under network partition.

**Then I framed the fix as a tradeoff conversation, not a unilateral decision.** I wrote a doc: here's the bug, here are three options — fail-closed, fail-open, or circuit breaker — with the implications of each. We discussed it async, chose fail-closed with an alert, and shipped it behind a feature flag.

The key lesson for me: complex problems usually have a simple root cause, but you only find it by being disciplined about what you know versus what you're assuming.

👨‍💻 James: Good. How did you explain the bug and the fix to non-technical stakeholders — product managers, for example?

👨 Khanh: I try to lead with the user impact, not the mechanism.

So instead of saying "our Redis distributed lock had a silent fallback under network partition," I said: "Occasionally, a very small number of customers were charged twice when they retried a failed payment. Here's what it looks like to them, here's how often it was happening, and here's what we're doing to prevent it."

I find that once stakeholders understand the impact, they're genuinely curious about the cause — and then the technical explanation lands much better because they're invested in it.

I also make sure to separate "what happened" from "what we're doing about it" — those are two different conversations, and mixing them creates confusion. Stakeholders usually care more about the second.

SECTION B — Disagreement & Influence

👩 Priya (EM): Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision made by a more senior engineer. What did you do?

👨 Khanh: Yes, this happened about a year ago.

Situation: A senior architect proposed migrating our main service database from PostgreSQL to a NoSQL solution — the stated reason was scalability. I had real concerns about this. Our data had complex relational integrity requirements, and the team's NoSQL experience was limited.

My reaction: I didn't raise it in the meeting. I knew disagreeing publicly without preparation would come across as obstructive. Instead, I said: "I'd like to think about this more before we commit — can I send some notes by end of day?"

What I did: I wrote a two-page doc. Not "why NoSQL is wrong" — that framing would be defensive and unhelpful. Instead: "Here are the assumptions behind this proposal. Here are the scenarios where it works well. Here are the scenarios where I think we'd have problems — specifically around our multi-step transaction flows and our team's current skill set. And here's an alternative I'd like us to consider."

Outcome: The architect read it, agreed with two of my three concerns, and we ended up going with a PostgreSQL sharding approach instead. The decision took two more weeks, but the team was aligned and confident. No one felt overruled.

What I learned: The goal of disagreement isn't to win — it's to get to the best decision. Writing things down forces you to stress-test your own argument first. If you can't write it clearly, you probably don't understand it clearly.

👩 Priya: What if the senior engineer had read your doc and still disagreed? What then?

👨 Khanh: Honestly — it depends on the stakes.

If it's a reversible decision, I'd disagree and commit. I'd say explicitly: "I still have reservations about X, but I understand we're moving forward. Here's what I'd like us to agree on as early warning signs that this is going wrong, so we have a clear trigger to revisit." That keeps the disagreement on record without being obstructive.

If it's truly irreversible — architectural choices that would be very expensive to undo — I'd escalate once, clearly and calmly. Not to my manager's manager as a power move, but to bring in another technical perspective. And I'd be explicit about why: "I want a third opinion because I may be wrong, and this decision is hard to reverse."

What I wouldn't do is silently comply and then say "I told you so" if things go wrong. That's the worst outcome for the team.

SECTION C — Growth & Self-Awareness

👩 Priya: What's a skill you've actively worked on improving in the last year that isn't a technical skill?

👨 Khanh: Running effective meetings — specifically, async-first communication.

For most of my career, my default was to schedule a meeting when I needed input on something. It felt faster. But I noticed that after the meeting, people often needed more time to think anyway — and those who were introverted or in different time zones were structurally disadvantaged.

So I started doing a simple thing: before requesting a meeting, I ask myself, "Can I send a doc and collect written feedback instead?" If yes, I do that. If a meeting is still needed, the doc becomes the pre-read, and the meeting becomes a decision session rather than an information session.

It's made my work more inclusive and, counterintuitively, faster. Decisions that used to take three back-and-forth meetings now often take one async thread and one short call.

I'm still improving at it — I sometimes slip back into meeting-first habits when I'm stressed or moving fast. But I'm much more conscious of the cost of synchronous time than I used to be.

👨‍💻 James: Last question. At Atlassian, engineers are expected to give and receive candid feedback. How do you give feedback to a peer whose code quality has been slipping?

👨 Khanh: I try to be direct, but I always lead with curiosity rather than judgment — because slipping quality almost always has a reason, and I want to understand it before I react to it.

So my first move is a private, informal conversation. Something like: "Hey, I've noticed a few things in your recent PRs that I'd like to talk through. Is now a good time?" — not over Slack message, because tone is too easy to misread.

In the conversation, I'm specific. Not "your code quality has slipped" — that's vague and hard to act on. Instead: "In the last three PRs I've seen missing error handling in the async paths and a few test cases skipped. I wanted to flag it early because I know you usually catch these things."

Then I listen. Maybe they're overloaded. Maybe there's a context I'm missing. Maybe they disagree with my assessment. Any of those is possible and important to hear.

If it continues after the conversation, then it becomes a manager conversation — but with a clear record of what I observed and when I raised it, so it's not a surprise.

I don't think feedback is a comfortable thing. But done privately, specifically, and early, it's much more useful than a PR comment that feels like a public correction.

💻 ROUND 3 — LIGHT TECHNICAL DISCUSSION (30 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer. No whiteboard, no code — discussion only. Note: This round tests how you reason and communicate about technical topics, not whether you can produce perfect code on demand.

👨‍💻 David (Principal Eng): Jira has a notification system — when an issue is updated, watchers get notified by email, Slack, and in-app. We have tens of millions of issues updated daily. Talk me through how you'd think about designing this.

👨 Khanh: Sure. I'll think out loud and you can steer me if I go too deep or too shallow anywhere.

The first thing I'd clarify: what's the latency expectation? In-app notifications probably need to be near-real-time — within seconds. Email can tolerate more delay — a minute is fine. Slack sits somewhere in between.

That difference actually suggests separate delivery pipelines, because they have different throughput and failure characteristics.

The core flow I'd propose: An issue update emits a domain event — say, IssueUpdatedEvent — onto a Kafka topic, partitioned by project ID to preserve ordering within a project. A fan-out service reads from Kafka, looks up the watchers for that issue, and routes a delivery task per-channel per-user into three separate queues: email, Slack, and in-app.

Each queue has its own consumer with its own retry logic. Email retries are slow and expensive — maybe 3 retries over 10 minutes. Slack has a rate limit API — we'd respect that. In-app can be delivered via WebSocket or long-polling.

Things I'd want to talk through with the team:

  • User notification preferences. If someone has disabled email

for Jira, we shouldn't even enqueue an email delivery. That filtering should happen early — ideally at fan-out time — to avoid wasted work downstream.

  • Deduplication. If the same issue is updated five times in

ten seconds, should watchers get five emails? Probably not. A short debounce window — say, 30 seconds — would batch rapid updates into one digest.

  • At-least-once vs exactly-once. Email at-least-once is fine;

getting two emails for one update is annoying but not harmful. Billing notifications would be a different story.

Where I'd start: The fan-out service and the in-app delivery path, because those are the highest visibility and the ones where latency matters most. Email can be added later with less risk.

👨‍💻 David: You mentioned debouncing rapid updates. How would you implement that without losing any notification?

👨 Khanh: Good question — the key is that debouncing at the notification level doesn't mean losing events at the data level.

I'd store every IssueUpdatedEvent in Kafka with full retention. The debounce only applies to outgoing notifications. Practically: when the fan-out service sees an event, instead of immediately enqueuing a delivery, it writes a pending notification record with a send_after timestamp of "now + 30 seconds." If a second event for the same issue arrives within that window, it updates the record — extends the timestamp, maybe appends the change — rather than creating a new delivery.

A scheduler then picks up records whose send_after has passed and actually enqueues them.

The tradeoff: you're adding 30 seconds of latency to the notification. For most issue updates, that's completely fine. For high-priority things like mentions or blocking status changes, you'd want to bypass the debounce — which means the event needs a priority flag, and the fan-out logic needs to branch on it.

That's a more complex design, so I'd want to validate the 30-second debounce solves 90% of the spam problem first before adding priority routing.

👨‍💻 David: Nice. Last one — how do you decide when not to build something yourself and to use an off-the-shelf solution?

👨 Khanh: My default is: use the off-the-shelf solution and be intentional about when you override it.

The bar I set for building custom is: does the existing solution have a fundamental mismatch with our requirements — not just an inconvenient limitation, but something that would cause correctness or reliability problems at our scale? Or does building custom give us a meaningful competitive advantage?

For something like email delivery, I'd use SendGrid or SES without hesitation. The failure modes are well-understood, the SDKs are mature, and the alternative — managing our own SMTP infrastructure — would be months of work that doesn't differentiate us.

But for something like our idempotency system or our transaction state machine — those encode business rules that are core to what we do. Off-the-shelf solutions would require us to adapt our business logic to fit their model, which is backwards. That's where custom code is worth it.

The danger I try to avoid is "not invented here" syndrome — building something custom because it's more interesting, not because it's more appropriate. I've seen teams spend months building a message queue when Kafka would have been fine. I try to name that impulse when I see it, including in myself.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Self-introduction + remote work mindset 10/10 "Code I can defend at 2am" — memorable, authentic
Values A Complexity + stakeholder communication 10/10 Know vs assume framing; impact-first explanation
Values B Disagreement + influence without authority 10/10 Written doc approach; disagree-and-commit protocol
Values C Growth mindset + peer feedback 10/10 Async-first habit; specific, private, early feedback model
Technical Notification system reasoning 10/10 Fan-out design; debounce tradeoff; build-vs-buy judgment

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Key Communication Patterns to Practise

1. The "Know vs Assume" Frame

*"I started by writing down what I knew for certain versus what I was assuming."*

Use this when describing how you debug or investigate. It signals rigour and intellectual honesty.

2. Impact-First Explanation

*"Instead of explaining the technical mechanism first, I led with what it meant for the user."*

Always translate technical problems into user or business impact before going into the mechanism.

3. Disagree in Writing, Not in the Room

"I didn't raise it in the meeting — I sent a doc by end of day."

This is Atlassian's written-first culture in practice. Positions you as someone who separates emotion from analysis.

4. Disagree-and-Commit Protocol

*"I'd say: I still have reservations, but here are the early warning signs I'd want us to watch for."*

This is the most professional version of losing an argument — you leave a record without being obstructive.

5. Build-vs-Buy Framing

*"My default is off-the-shelf. The bar for custom is fundamental mismatch or meaningful competitive advantage."*

This signals senior engineering judgment — knowing when NOT to build is as important as knowing how.

6. Feedback Delivery Formula

Private → Specific → Curious → Documented

Never vague, never public, always give the person a chance to explain before escalating.

💰 Atlassian Salary Reference (Vietnam / Remote)

  • Senior SWE (L5): USD 80–120K base + equity (RSU)
  • Hiring model: Remote-first, timezone-flexible
  • Interview language: English only — all rounds

📝 Phrases Worth Memorising

Situation Phrase
Opening self-intro "I grew from writing code that works, to writing code I can defend at 2am."
Explaining bugs to PM "Let me start with what this meant for the user, then I'll explain why it happened."
Disagreeing "I'd like to send some notes before we commit — can I share a doc by end of day?"
Losing an argument "I still have a reservation about X, but I'm committed to making this work. Can we agree on a signal to revisit it?"
Build vs buy "My default is to use the existing solution. The bar for building custom is fundamental mismatch, not inconvenience."
Receiving critique "That's a fair point. Let me think about that — I might be anchored on my initial approach."

🎭 Mock Interview #38 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Shopify (Ottawa / Remote-first, Global Commerce Platform)

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Checkout & Orders Platform. Shopify powers 10% of US e-commerce. Flash sales (Black Friday, product drops) drive extreme, unpredictable traffic bursts. Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, MySQL (Vitess), Redis, Kafka, gRPC. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (20 min, English)

👩 Lena (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Great to meet you. Let's skip the formalities — tell me something about your engineering background that isn't on your CV.

👨 Khanh: Ha — I like that question. Something not on the CV...

Honestly, the thing that shaped me most as an engineer is a failure. Early in my career I shipped a payment feature that had a race condition under concurrent load. It passed all our tests. It passed code review. It went to production and worked fine for two weeks.

Then during a promotional campaign, we had a 10x traffic spike and the bug surfaced — some users were double-charged. The financial impact was small, but the trust impact wasn't.

I stayed up all night writing the postmortem — not because my manager asked me to, but because I needed to understand exactly why our process didn't catch it. That night changed how I think about concurrency, testing, and system design. I became somewhat obsessed with "what would have to be true for this to fail?" as a design question.

That's not on my CV, but it's probably the most important thing that happened in my career.

👩 Lena: That's a great answer — and very honest. Shopify's Black Friday handles millions of checkouts per hour. Why do you want to work on that kind of scale?

👨 Khanh: Because scale forces honesty about your architecture.

You can hide a lot of bad decisions behind low traffic. Cache invalidation bugs, lock contention, N+1 queries — they're invisible until you multiply them by a million. When your system has to handle Black Friday, you can't paper over those things. You have to actually solve them.

I find that genuinely exciting, not stressful. Problems that are hard to reproduce in staging but obvious in production are the most interesting engineering challenges there are. Shopify is one of the few companies in the world where you can work on that class of problem every day.

👩 Lena: Love it. Two more rounds — technical and leadership. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL (55 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Checkout Platform.

PART A — Inventory Oversell Prevention (20 min)

👨‍💻 Tom (Staff Eng): Black Friday. A limited-edition sneaker drops. 50,000 people hit "Add to Cart" simultaneously for 500 units. Walk me through how you'd prevent overselling.

👨 Khanh: Classic inventory problem — I've seen variants of this in flash sale systems. There are three layers of defence.

Layer 1 — Redis atomic decrement (fast path):

@Service
public class InventoryService {

    private static final String STOCK_KEY = "stock:%s"; // variantId

    // Returns false if stock is exhausted
    public boolean reserve(String variantId, int qty) {
        String key = String.format(STOCK_KEY, variantId);

        // Lua script: atomic check-and-decrement
        String script = """
            local stock = tonumber(redis.call('GET', KEYS[1]))
            if stock == nil or stock < tonumber(ARGV[1]) then
                return 0
            end
            redis.call('DECRBY', KEYS[1], ARGV[1])
            return 1
            """;

        Long result = redisTemplate.execute(
            new DefaultRedisScript<>(script, Long.class),
            List.of(key),
            String.valueOf(qty)
        );

        return Long.valueOf(1L).equals(result);
    }
}

Layer 2 — Pessimistic DB lock (source of truth):

@Transactional
public Order confirmCheckout(String cartId, String userId) {
    Cart cart = cartRepo.findById(cartId).orElseThrow();

    // SELECT ... FOR UPDATE — blocks concurrent confirmations
    // for the same variant
    InventoryRow inv = inventoryRepo
        .findByVariantIdWithLock(cart.getVariantId());

    if (inv.getAvailable() < cart.getQuantity()) {
        // Redis was optimistic — DB is the final arbiter
        throw new OutOfStockException(cart.getVariantId());
    }

    inv.setAvailable(inv.getAvailable() - cart.getQuantity());
    inventoryRepo.save(inv);

    return orderRepo.save(Order.from(cart, userId));
}

Layer 3 — Soft reservation with TTL:

Cart added → Redis stock decremented (reservation held 15 min)
If user checks out → DB lock confirms, reservation consumed
If user abandons → TTL expires, Redis stock restored automatically
If payment fails → compensating event restores reservation

Why three layers?

Redis alone: fast but can lose data on crash. DB lock alone: correct but can't handle 50,000 concurrent requests. Together: Redis filters 99% of excess requests cheaply; DB handles the small concurrent window that Redis allows through.

👨‍💻 Tom: Good. What happens if Redis crashes between the reservation and the DB confirmation?

👨 Khanh: That's the gap I was about to mention.

If Redis crashes, two things can happen:

Scenario A — crash before DB confirm: The reservation in Redis is lost. The DB hasn't been updated yet. The cart is in an inconsistent "reserved but stock not decremented" state. On recovery, we need a reconciliation job:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 60_000)
public void reconcilePendingCarts() {
    // Find carts in RESERVED state older than 5 min
    List<Cart> stale = cartRepo.findStaleReservations(
        Instant.now().minusSeconds(300)
    );
    for (Cart cart : stale) {
        // Re-check DB inventory — if still available, re-reserve in Redis
        // If not, mark cart EXPIRED and notify user
        inventoryReconciler.reconcile(cart);
    }
}

Scenario B — Redis crash after DB confirm: Order is committed. Redis just lost the updated stock count. On Redis restart, we reload stock from DB:

@EventListener(RedisReadyEvent.class)
public void onRedisReconnect() {
    List<Inventory> all = inventoryRepo.findAll();
    for (Inventory inv : all) {
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
            "stock:" + inv.getVariantId(),
            String.valueOf(inv.getAvailable())
        );
    }
    log.info("Redis inventory cache warmed: {} variants", all.size());
}

Key principle: DB is always the source of truth. Redis is a fast filter, never a store.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining a Production Incident (15 min)

👨‍💻 Tom: Imagine last Black Friday our checkout service had a 4-minute outage at 10 AM. Walk me through how you'd run the incident and then communicate it afterward.

👨 Khanh: There are two very different jobs here — managing the incident live, and communicating after. I treat them completely separately.

During the incident:

First, I'd establish an incident channel immediately — not scattered DMs, one place. Second, I'd assign roles: one person on comms, one on investigation, one on mitigation. Everyone else stays out unless called. Noise in an incident is dangerous.

My job in the first five minutes is to answer: what's the blast radius? Is checkout fully down or partially degraded? Are orders still being persisted even if the API is returning errors? That determines urgency.

For mitigation, I work backwards from the simplest fix. Before restarting services or rolling back, I check: did anything deploy in the last hour? Did traffic spike unusually? Is a dependency — Redis, MySQL — showing elevated latency? Usually the cause is one of three: a bad deploy, a traffic pattern we didn't handle, or a downstream dependency degrading.

Once mitigated, I'd post a brief all-clear: "Checkout is recovered as of 10:04 AM. Root cause under investigation. Postmortem by 5 PM today."

After the incident — the postmortem:

I'd send it to the team by end of day, merchants the following morning. The structure I always use:

1. Timeline — what happened, when (UTC timestamps)
2. Impact — how many users affected, estimated revenue impact
3. Root cause — the actual technical reason, one sentence
4. Why it wasn't caught before production — process gap
5. Action items — owner + due date for each, no orphans

The most important rule: postmortems are blameless. The goal is to find the system gap, not the guilty person. If a person made a mistake, the question isn't "why did they do that" — it's "what condition made that mistake easy to make, and how do we fix the condition?"

That's the document that actually changes how we work.

PART C — CODING: Flash Sale Queue (20 min)

👨‍💻 Tom: *"Implement a fair checkout queue for a flash sale. Max 500 concurrent checkouts. Users beyond that get a queue position. Thread-safe. No external dependencies."*

👨 Khanh:

public class CheckoutQueue {

    private final int maxConcurrent;
    private final AtomicInteger activeCheckouts = new AtomicInteger(0);
    private final AtomicInteger queuePosition   = new AtomicInteger(0);
    private final AtomicInteger nextServed      = new AtomicInteger(0);

    // userId → their queue ticket number
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Integer> tickets =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public CheckoutQueue(int maxConcurrent) {
        this.maxConcurrent = maxConcurrent;
    }

    public CheckoutEntry enter(String userId) {
        // Assign a ticket (FIFO fairness)
        int ticket = tickets.computeIfAbsent(userId,
            k -> queuePosition.getAndIncrement());

        if (activeCheckouts.get() < maxConcurrent
                && ticket == nextServed.get()) {
            // Slot available AND it's this user's turn
            activeCheckouts.incrementAndGet();
            nextServed.incrementAndGet();
            return CheckoutEntry.admitted(ticket);
        }

        // Queue position (1-based for display)
        int ahead = ticket - nextServed.get();
        return CheckoutEntry.queued(ticket, ahead + 1);
    }

    public void complete(String userId) {
        tickets.remove(userId);
        activeCheckouts.decrementAndGet();
    }

    public record CheckoutEntry(
        int ticket,
        boolean admitted,
        int queuePosition // 0 if admitted
    ) {
        static CheckoutEntry admitted(int ticket) {
            return new CheckoutEntry(ticket, true, 0);
        }
        static CheckoutEntry queued(int ticket, int position) {
            return new CheckoutEntry(ticket, false, position);
        }
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void admitsUpToLimit() {
    var q = new CheckoutQueue(3);
    assertThat(q.enter("u1").admitted()).isTrue();
    assertThat(q.enter("u2").admitted()).isTrue();
    assertThat(q.enter("u3").admitted()).isTrue();
    assertThat(q.enter("u4").admitted()).isFalse(); // 4th queued
}

@Test
void releasingSlotAdmitsNext() {
    var q = new CheckoutQueue(1);
    q.enter("u1"); // admitted
    var u2 = q.enter("u2"); // queued
    assertThat(u2.admitted()).isFalse();

    q.complete("u1"); // release slot

    var u2retry = q.enter("u2");
    assertThat(u2retry.admitted()).isTrue();
}

@Test
void queuePositionIsAccurate() {
    var q = new CheckoutQueue(1);
    q.enter("u1"); // admitted
    var p2 = q.enter("u2");
    var p3 = q.enter("u3");
    assertThat(p2.queuePosition()).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(p3.queuePosition()).isEqualTo(2);
}

@Test
void idempotentEntry() {
    var q = new CheckoutQueue(2);
    var first  = q.enter("u1");
    var second = q.enter("u1"); // same user — same ticket
    assertThat(first.ticket()).isEqualTo(second.ticket());
}

💬 ROUND 3 — LEADERSHIP & CRAFT (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Principal Engineer panel.

SECTION A — Leading Without Title

👩 Rachel (EM): You're a senior engineer, not a manager. Tell me about a time you drove a significant improvement in your team that wasn't part of your job description.

👨 Khanh: About eighteen months ago, I noticed that our team's deployment process had become a real source of anxiety. Deploys happened at most twice a week, required three people to be online, and had no automated rollback. Engineers were nervous before every release.

That nervousness was a signal — it meant we didn't trust our own process. And teams that don't trust their deploy process ship less, move slower, and take fewer reasonable risks.

I raised it in a retro, got a lukewarm response — it wasn't a burning fire, just a slow drain. So I decided to just start fixing it, one piece at a time.

I added automated smoke tests post-deploy. Then a one-command rollback script. Then shifted from manual deploy checklists to a GitHub Actions pipeline. Each piece took a day or two of my own time, alongside my regular work.

Three months later, we were deploying daily. The conversation in the team shifted from "are you sure it's safe to deploy?" to "let's just ship it and watch the metrics."

The only moment it became a "project" was when I wrote it up as a case study for the engineering blog. The work itself was just small improvements, stacked deliberately.

👩 Rachel: What would you have done differently if the team had actively resisted the changes?

👨 Khanh: That's an important question. Resistance usually means one of two things: people don't see the problem the same way I do, or they see a risk in the solution I don't.

If it was the first — I'd invest more in making the problem visible.
