Not "deploys are bad" as an opinion, but data: here are the deploy incidents we've had in the last six months, here's the average time to recover, here's what other teams with daily deploys look like. Data is harder to argue with than feelings.

If it was the second — I'd genuinely want to understand the concern. Maybe there's a class of change where daily deploys really are risky for us and I hadn't thought it through. In that case, the right answer is to design around the concern, not steamroll it.

The one thing I wouldn't do is build the thing in isolation and present it as a fait accompli. That builds resentment even when the outcome is good. Buy-in matters more than speed when you're changing how a team works.

SECTION B — On Technical Craft

👨‍💻 Daniel (Principal Eng): What does "good code" mean to you? I'm not looking for a textbook answer.

👨 Khanh: My honest answer has changed over the years.

When I was junior, good code meant clever code — concise, elegant, using every language feature available. I was proud of one-liners and lambdas-in-lambdas.

Then I had to maintain my own "clever" code six months later and couldn't understand it. That was humbling.

Now, good code to me means: the next engineer who reads this — who might be me on a bad day, or a new teammate at midnight during an incident — can understand what this does, why it does it this way, and what would break if they changed it.

Practically, that means: method names that say why, not just what. Error paths that are as readable as happy paths. Comments that explain decisions, not descriptions. Tests that serve as documentation. And honest complexity — if something is inherently hard, don't hide it behind an abstraction that lies about how simple it is.

The bar I use: would I be comfortable explaining every line of this in a postmortem? If yes, it's good code.

👨‍💻 Daniel: How do you think about technical debt? When do you pay it down versus live with it?

👨 Khanh: I think of technical debt in three categories, because "technical debt" is overloaded to the point of being useless as a term.

Category 1 — Deliberate shortcuts. We knew it was wrong when we wrote it, did it anyway to meet a deadline, and left a clear comment or ticket. This is fine. It's a conscious tradeoff. Pay it down when the feature stabilises and you have breathing room.

Category 2 — Discovered complexity. The system grew into a shape we didn't anticipate. The abstraction that made sense for three use cases breaks down at ten. This is the most common type. Pay it down incrementally — don't wait for a "refactoring sprint" that never comes. The rule I follow: leave the code slightly better than you found it. Boy Scout Rule. Not a rewrite, just a nudge.

Category 3 — Foundational rot. Core decisions that are now actively causing production incidents, slowing every feature, or creating security risk. This is the kind of debt that needs to be treated like a project — scoped, prioritised, and resourced properly. Living with it has a compounding cost.

The mistake I see most often is treating Category 1 like Category 3 — panicking about a known shortcut and demanding a rewrite when incremental improvement would do. The second mistake is treating Category 3 like Category 1 — saying "we'll deal with it later" when "later" has already arrived.

SECTION C — Communication Under Pressure

👩 Rachel: You're in the middle of a complex feature implementation when your manager tells you a high-priority bug just landed and needs you specifically. How do you respond?

👨 Khanh: First — I don't just drop what I'm doing. Context switching has a real cost, and the cost isn't just mine — it's the feature that loses momentum.

So my first question is: what's the actual urgency? "High priority" can mean "production is down right now" or it can mean "a customer complained and a VP noticed." Those require very different responses.

If it's a live production issue, I hand off clearly. I'd spend five minutes writing a brief note in the feature branch: where I am, what's been tested, what the next step is. Then I switch. The five minutes costs almost nothing compared to the hours it saves the next person who picks it up.

If it's urgent-but-not-on-fire, I'd negotiate. "I can start on this in two hours when I hit a natural stopping point. Does that work, or is there something I'm not understanding about the urgency?" That's not resistance — it's responsible prioritisation. Managers generally appreciate it when you ask rather than assume.

Either way, I'd close the loop after: "I'm on the bug now. Here's my current status on the feature. Let me know if the priority changes again." Keeping visibility high prevents surprises in both directions.

👩 Rachel: Last question. Where do you want to be in three years, and how does this role get you there?

👨 Khanh: Three years from now, I want to be the kind of engineer other engineers come to not just for technical answers, but for help thinking through hard problems — architectural decisions, tradeoffs, incident post-mortems. That's what I associate with genuine seniority: not knowing more, but being able to help others think more clearly.

This role gets me there because of the scale. Working on systems that handle Black Friday traffic isn't glamorous every day, but it's the kind of environment where your assumptions get stress-tested constantly. You can't fake good architectural judgment at Shopify's scale — either the system holds or it doesn't.

I also want to improve at cross-functional communication — working with product managers and merchant partners, not just engineers. Shopify's model, where engineers are close to merchants and business outcomes, is exactly the context I want to grow in.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Authenticity + motivation 10/10 Failure story as opener — memorable and honest
Tech A Inventory oversell — 3-layer defence 10/10 Redis Lua + DB lock + soft reservation + crash recovery
Tech B Incident communication 10/10 Live incident roles; blameless postmortem structure
Tech C Coding: flash sale queue 10/10 FIFO ticket, idempotent entry, 4 tests
Leadership A Leading without title 10/10 Bottom-up improvement with data, without steamrolling
Craft Good code + tech debt taxonomy 10/10 3-category debt model; postmortem readability bar
Pressure Context switching under urgency 10/10 Negotiate > assume; hand-off note practice

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Shopify Interview Tips

  1. Black Friday = your design constraint — always qualify answers with "at flash sale scale"
  2. Redis is a filter, DB is the truth — never let Redis be your inventory source of truth
  3. Lua scripts for atomicity — check-and-decrement in one round trip, no TOCTOU
  4. Soft reservation with TTL — cart reservation expires automatically; no manual cleanup needed
  5. Incident comms = two jobs — live management (roles, blast radius) vs. postmortem (blameless, structured)
  6. Technical debt taxonomy — deliberate shortcut / discovered complexity / foundational rot
  7. Leadership without title — small improvements stacked deliberately beat one big "project"
  8. Shopify salaries (Remote/APAC): USD 120–160K base + RSU; Shopify is generous with equity

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Opener "The thing that shaped me most as an engineer is a failure."
Scale motivation "Scale forces honesty about your architecture."
Incident "Noise in an incident is dangerous."
Postmortem "What condition made that mistake easy to make?"
Resistance "Buy-in matters more than speed when you're changing how a team works."
Good code "Would I be comfortable explaining every line of this in a postmortem?"
Priority conflict "That's not resistance — it's responsible prioritisation."
Career goal "Not knowing more, but helping others think more clearly."

🎭 Mock Interview #39 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Airbnb (San Francisco / Remote, Global Travel Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Payments & Trust team. Airbnb processes $10B+ in payments annually across 190+ countries, multiple currencies, and complex host-guest payout flows. Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, gRPC, MySQL, Kafka, Redis, Temporal. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (20 min, English)

👩 Claire (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Tell me about yourself — but specifically, what kind of engineering problems get you genuinely excited?

👨 Khanh: The problems that excite me most are the ones where correctness and scale are in tension.

Most engineering problems are either hard to get right or hard to scale. The interesting ones are both. Payment flows are a perfect example — a single payout to a host involves currency conversion, fraud scoring, bank routing, tax withholding, and a reconciliation trail. Every step can fail. The system has to be correct across all of them, and it has to do it for millions of transactions per day.

I love that class of problem because it forces you to think carefully about failure modes, state machines, and eventual consistency — not as academic topics but as real design constraints with real consequences.

👩 Claire: Airbnb is very mission-driven — "belong anywhere." How does that kind of mission influence how you think about the engineering work?

👨 Khanh: It shapes the error handling, honestly.

When Airbnb's payout to a host fails silently, it's not just a bug — it's someone waiting for money they've already earned. That's a very different emotional weight than a 500 error on a dashboard widget. Mission-driven context makes me more serious about: surfacing failures clearly to users instead of hiding them behind generic error messages, designing retry and recovery paths so failures are temporary not permanent, and monitoring the user experience of failure, not just the system metrics.

Engineers who are connected to the mission tend to care about those things. Engineers who aren't, tend to mark the ticket "done" when the happy path works.

👩 Claire: Well said. Moving to technical rounds — good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Payments Core.

PART A — Host Payout State Machine (25 min)

👨‍💻 Raj (Staff Eng): When a guest checks out, Airbnb releases the host payout 24 hours later. The flow involves: check-out confirmation, fraud hold (optional), currency conversion, bank transfer, and reconciliation. Design the state machine and make it resilient to partial failures.

👨 Khanh: This is a classic long-running transaction problem. I'd model it as a saga — a sequence of compensable steps — orchestrated by a state machine persisted in the database.

State machine:

INITIATED
  → FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING
      → FRAUD_HOLD (manual review required)
      → FRAUD_CLEARED
          → CURRENCY_CONVERTING
              → BANK_TRANSFER_PENDING
                  → COMPLETED
                  → BANK_TRANSFER_FAILED → RETRY or MANUAL_REVIEW
          → FRAUD_REJECTED → CANCELLED (payout blocked)
  → CANCELLED

Implementation — persisted state machine:

@Entity
public class HostPayout {
    @Id private String id;
    private String reservationId;
    private String hostId;
    private BigDecimal amount;
    private String currency;
    private PayoutStatus status;
    private int retryCount;
    private String failureReason;
    private Instant scheduledAt;    // 24h after checkout
    private Instant lastUpdatedAt;
}

public enum PayoutStatus {
    INITIATED, FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING, FRAUD_HOLD,
    FRAUD_CLEARED, FRAUD_REJECTED,
    CURRENCY_CONVERTING, BANK_TRANSFER_PENDING,
    COMPLETED, BANK_TRANSFER_FAILED,
    CANCELLED, MANUAL_REVIEW
}

Saga orchestrator — each step is idempotent:

@Service
public class PayoutOrchestrator {

    @Transactional
    public void advance(String payoutId) {
        HostPayout payout = payoutRepo.findById(payoutId)
            .orElseThrow();

        switch (payout.getStatus()) {
            case INITIATED -> submitFraudCheck(payout);
            case FRAUD_CLEARED -> startCurrencyConversion(payout);
            case CURRENCY_CONVERTING -> submitBankTransfer(payout);
            case BANK_TRANSFER_FAILED -> handleTransferFailure(payout);
            default -> log.warn("No transition for status: {}",
                payout.getStatus());
        }
    }

    private void submitFraudCheck(HostPayout payout) {
        transition(payout, FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING);
        kafkaTemplate.send("payout.fraud-check",
            payout.getId(), new FraudCheckRequest(payout));
    }

    private void startCurrencyConversion(HostPayout payout) {
        transition(payout, CURRENCY_CONVERTING);
        BigDecimal converted = fxService.convert(
            payout.getAmount(), payout.getCurrency(), "USD");
        payout.setAmountUsd(converted);
        transition(payout, BANK_TRANSFER_PENDING); // sync in this case
        kafkaTemplate.send("payout.bank-transfer",
            payout.getId(), new BankTransferRequest(payout));
    }

    private void handleTransferFailure(HostPayout payout) {
        if (payout.getRetryCount() < 3) {
            payout.setRetryCount(payout.getRetryCount() + 1);
            // Schedule retry with exponential backoff
            Instant retryAt = Instant.now().plusSeconds(
                (long) Math.pow(2, payout.getRetryCount()) * 60);
            payout.setScheduledAt(retryAt);
            transition(payout, BANK_TRANSFER_PENDING);
        } else {
            transition(payout, MANUAL_REVIEW);
            alertOpsTeam(payout, "Bank transfer failed after 3 retries");
        }
    }

    private void transition(HostPayout payout, PayoutStatus next) {
        payout.setStatus(next);
        payout.setLastUpdatedAt(Instant.now());
        payoutRepo.save(payout);
        // Audit log every transition
        auditLog.record(payout.getId(), next);
    }
}

Resilience mechanisms:

  • Every transition is persisted before the async action —

crash-safe by design.

  • Kafka consumers are idempotent: they check current status

before processing to avoid double-advancing.

  • A recovery job polls for payouts stuck in *_PENDING

states for more than 30 minutes and re-advances them.

👨‍💻 Raj: What if the fraud check service is down for an hour? How does the host payout flow behave?

👨 Khanh: The payout stays in FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING state — which is exactly what we want. The state machine doesn't advance until the fraud service responds.

The Kafka message for the fraud check has already been sent. When the fraud service recovers, it will process the message and publish a result event — FraudCheckResult — which our consumer picks up and advances the state machine.

Two safety nets:

// Safety net 1: Recovery sweep for stuck payouts
@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 900_000) // every 15 min
public void recoverStuckPayouts() {
    Instant cutoff = Instant.now().minusSeconds(3600); // 1 hour
    List<HostPayout> stuck = payoutRepo
        .findByStatusAndLastUpdatedAtBefore(FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING, cutoff);

    for (HostPayout p : stuck) {
        log.warn("Payout {} stuck in FRAUD_CHECK_PENDING for >1h", p.getId());
        // Re-publish fraud check request (idempotent — fraud service deduplicates)
        kafkaTemplate.send("payout.fraud-check",
            p.getId(), new FraudCheckRequest(p));
    }
}

// Safety net 2: Dead Letter Queue monitoring
// If fraud-check messages land in DLQ → alert ops

The host experience: they see "Payout processing" — a deliberately vague status that doesn't mention fraud. If the hold exceeds 24 additional hours, we send a support ticket automatically.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Delivering Bad News (15 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: Imagine a bug in the payout system caused 2,000 hosts to receive the wrong payout amount — some overpaid, some underpaid. You discovered it. Walk me through what you do next.

👨 Khanh: This is a financial correctness incident — I treat it with the same urgency as a production outage, maybe more.

Step 1 — Stop the bleeding first. Before I tell anyone, I need to know: is this bug still running? If yes, I'd coordinate an immediate disable of the payout batch job — not a rollback yet, just a pause — while we understand the blast radius.

Step 2 — Quantify accurately before communicating. The worst thing I can do is send a message saying "some hosts were affected" and then need to correct the numbers twice. I'd spend 20–30 minutes running exact queries:

  • How many hosts?
  • Total amount overpaid vs underpaid?
  • Date range affected?
  • Is the error consistent (formula bug) or random?

Step 3 — Tell the right people, in the right order. First my manager and the on-call lead — before Slack, before email. Then a short, factual incident brief:

"We have a confirmed payout calculation error affecting 2,000 hosts across [date range]. ~1,200 were underpaid (total: $X), ~800 were overpaid (total: $Y). The batch job is paused. Root cause is [one sentence]. Fix ETA: [time]. We will reconcile all affected payouts by [date]."

Step 4 — The hardest part: communication to hosts. I'd push for proactive outreach over waiting for complaints. Hosts who discover the error themselves before we tell them lose trust permanently. A message that says "We found an error and we're correcting it" is much better than one that says "We noticed you contacted support."

The message should be: specific amount, clear correction timeline, and a direct apology — no corporate language, no passive voice. Hosts are small business owners. They deserve a straight answer.

Step 5 — Postmortem. Blameless, within 48 hours, with reconciliation as a tracked action item until every cent is corrected.

PART C — CODING: Currency Conversion Cache (20 min)

👨‍💻 Raj: *"FX rates are fetched from an external API. Implement a simple in-memory cache with a 5-minute TTL per currency pair. Thread-safe. Handle expired entries correctly."*

👨 Khanh:

public class FxRateCache {

    private record CachedRate(BigDecimal rate, Instant expiresAt) {
        boolean isExpired() {
            return Instant.now().isAfter(expiresAt);
        }
    }

    private final Duration ttl;
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, CachedRate> cache =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    public FxRateCache(Duration ttl) {
        this.ttl = ttl;
    }

    // Returns cached rate if valid, else fetches fresh and caches it
    public BigDecimal getRate(String from, String to,
                              Supplier<BigDecimal> fetcher) {
        String key = from + "_" + to;

        CachedRate cached = cache.get(key);
        if (cached != null && !cached.isExpired()) {
            return cached.rate(); // cache hit
        }

        // Expired or absent — fetch fresh rate
        // Use compute to avoid duplicate fetches under concurrent access
        CachedRate[] result = new CachedRate[1];
        cache.compute(key, (k, existing) -> {
            // Double-check inside compute — another thread may have refreshed
            if (existing != null && !existing.isExpired()) {
                result[0] = existing;
                return existing; // no-op
            }
            BigDecimal fresh = fetcher.get();
            CachedRate newEntry = new CachedRate(
                fresh, Instant.now().plus(ttl));
            result[0] = newEntry;
            return newEntry;
        });

        return result[0].rate();
    }

    // Explicit eviction (e.g., on rate-update webhook)
    public void invalidate(String from, String to) {
        cache.remove(from + "_" + to);
    }

    public int size() { return cache.size(); }
}

// Tests
@Test
void returnsCachedRateOnHit() {
    var cache = new FxRateCache(Duration.ofMinutes(5));
    AtomicInteger fetchCount = new AtomicInteger(0);

    Supplier<BigDecimal> fetcher = () -> {
        fetchCount.incrementAndGet();
        return new BigDecimal("1.08");
    };

    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", fetcher);
    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", fetcher); // should hit cache

    assertThat(fetchCount.get()).isEqualTo(1);
}

@Test
void fetchesFreshRateAfterExpiry() throws InterruptedException {
    var cache = new FxRateCache(Duration.ofMillis(50)); // 50ms TTL
    AtomicInteger fetchCount = new AtomicInteger(0);

    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", () -> {
        fetchCount.incrementAndGet();
        return BigDecimal.ONE;
    });
    Thread.sleep(60); // expire the entry

    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", () -> {
        fetchCount.incrementAndGet();
        return BigDecimal.ONE;
    });

    assertThat(fetchCount.get()).isEqualTo(2);
}

@Test
void invalidationForcesRefetch() {
    var cache = new FxRateCache(Duration.ofMinutes(5));
    AtomicInteger fetchCount = new AtomicInteger(0);

    Supplier<BigDecimal> f = () -> {
        fetchCount.incrementAndGet();
        return BigDecimal.TEN;
    };

    cache.getRate("GBP", "USD", f);
    cache.invalidate("GBP", "USD");
    cache.getRate("GBP", "USD", f);

    assertThat(fetchCount.get()).isEqualTo(2);
}

@Test
void isolatesCurrencyPairs() {
    var cache = new FxRateCache(Duration.ofMinutes(5));
    AtomicInteger calls = new AtomicInteger(0);

    cache.getRate("USD", "EUR", () -> { calls.incrementAndGet(); return BigDecimal.ONE; });
    cache.getRate("USD", "GBP", () -> { calls.incrementAndGet(); return BigDecimal.TWO; });

    assertThat(calls.get()).isEqualTo(2); // different pairs — both fetched
    assertThat(cache.size()).isEqualTo(2);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — CULTURE & GROWTH (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager, Trust & Payments.

👩 Maya (EM): Airbnb had a rough period during COVID — the company had to lay off 25% of its workforce and then rebuild. Engineering culture shifted a lot. How do you stay motivated when the environment around you is turbulent?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, I think about this one carefully because I've seen two failure modes in turbulent environments.

The first is people who completely disconnect — they show up, do the minimum, and wait for stability to return before re-engaging. The second is people who try to fix everything and burn out because they can't.

The version I try to practise is: find what I can control and do that exceptionally well. In uncertain periods, the most stabilising thing a senior engineer can do is be the person their immediate team can rely on. Not heroics — just consistency. Showing up to reviews, keeping documentation current, being patient with people who are more anxious than you. That kind of reliability is disproportionately valuable when everything else feels unstable.

The thing that keeps me personally motivated is focusing on the craft, not the company's quarterly metrics. The code I write today either handles failure gracefully or it doesn't — and that's true regardless of stock price or headcount. I find that focusing on that level keeps me grounded.

👩 Maya: Tell me about a time you had to earn trust on a new team. What did you do?

👨 Khanh: When I joined my most recent team, I made a deliberate decision not to have strong opinions for the first four weeks.

That sounds passive, but it wasn't. I was intensely listening and reading — code, Slack history, postmortems, ADRs. I was building a map of what the team valued, what frustrated them, and where they felt stuck. I'd ask questions in one-on-ones rather than in group discussions, so people could be honest without performing.

At the four-week mark, I'd identified one thing I could contribute that was clearly useful and clearly within my ability: improving the test coverage on the payment reconciliation service, which everyone knew was fragile but no one had time to fix. I did it quietly, put up a PR, and asked for a review.

The PR itself was less important than the signal it sent: I understand what matters to you, I do what I say I'll do, and I'm not here to redesign everything in my first month.

Trust, in my experience, comes from predictability more than brilliance. I'd rather be the person who ships what they commit to, every time, than the person who occasionally produces something brilliant and is otherwise a variable.

👩 Maya: What do you think is the hardest part of being a senior engineer — not the technical part?

👨 Khanh: Knowing when to let someone struggle.

When you're a junior engineer, the most helpful thing a senior can do is often just give you the answer. It saves time, unblocks you, and you learn something.

But as a senior engineer, always giving answers is actually harmful to the team. People stop thinking through problems because they know the shortcut is available. And you become a bottleneck — nothing moves without your input.

The harder skill is staying quiet long enough to let someone work through something, and then asking a question that helps them find the answer themselves. That's not satisfying in the moment — it's slower, and it requires real restraint when you can see the solution clearly. But it's the only way a team actually develops.

I'm still learning this. I catch myself jumping to answers more often than I'd like. But I've gotten better at pausing and asking "what have you tried so far?" instead.

👩 Maya: Final question. What question do you wish interviewers would ask you that they never do?

👨 Khanh: What have you changed your mind about recently?

I think that question tells you more about an engineer than almost any technical question. It shows whether they're still learning or whether they've calcified into a set of positions they just defend.

My honest answer right now: I used to believe microservices were almost always the right architectural choice for scalable systems. I've changed my mind. The operational overhead — distributed tracing, network latency, deployment complexity — is significant, and I've seen too many teams treat microservices as a scalability solution when their actual bottleneck was the database or the algorithm. A well-structured monolith with clear module boundaries is often the better starting point. Migrate to services when you have a specific reason, not as a default.

That's a position I would not have held five years ago.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Mission alignment + motivation 10/10 "Error handling shaped by mission" — original and sharp
Tech A Payout state machine + saga 10/10 Full state enum, idempotent transitions, recovery sweep
Tech B Incident: financial error comms 10/10 Stop → quantify → escalate → proactive host outreach
Tech C Coding: FX rate cache 10/10 compute() for double-check, 4 edge-case tests
Culture A Resilience in turbulence 10/10 Craft over metrics; reliability as stability signal
Culture B Earning trust on a new team 10/10 4-week listening period; predictability over brilliance
Craft Senior engineer's hardest skill 10/10 Knowing when to let someone struggle

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Airbnb Interview Tips

  1. Mission shapes error handling — connect engineering decisions to host/guest experience, not just metrics
  2. Saga pattern for long-running flows — payout spans hours; each step must be compensable and idempotent
  3. Persist state before async action — crash-safety requires writing the transition before sending the Kafka message
  4. Financial incident = highest urgency — stop → quantify exactly → communicate proactively
  5. Trust = predictability — Airbnb values engineers who ship what they commit to, consistently
  6. FX cache: use compute() — prevents duplicate external calls under concurrent access (vs putIfAbsent)
  7. "What have you changed your mind about?" — signals intellectual honesty; prepare a genuine answer
  8. Airbnb salaries (Remote/APAC): USD 150–200K base + RSU; top-tier benefits globally

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Motivation "Correctness and scale are in tension — that's where the interesting problems are."
Mission "Mission-driven context makes me more serious about the user experience of failure."
Incident "The worst thing I can do is communicate before I have accurate numbers."
Proactive comms "Hosts who discover the error themselves before we tell them lose trust permanently."
Turbulence "The most stabilising thing a senior engineer can do is be consistent."
Trust "Trust comes from predictability more than brilliance."
Senior skill "Knowing when to let someone struggle — and staying quiet long enough for them to do it."
Growth "I'm still learning this. I catch myself jumping to answers more than I'd like."

🎭 Mock Interview #40 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Uber (San Francisco / Remote, Global Mobility & Delivery Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Marketplace & Pricing team. Uber processes 25 million trips per day across 70+ countries. Real-time surge pricing, driver matching, and trip lifecycle are the core engineering challenges. Tech: Java 17, gRPC, Kafka, Redis, Cassandra, MySQL, Cadence/Temporal. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Nina (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Quick question to start — if you had to describe your engineering style in three words, what would they be and why?

👨 Khanh: Deliberate, honest, and recoverable.

Deliberate — I try not to write code on autopilot. Every design choice is a tradeoff, and I want to be conscious of what I'm trading.

Honest — I don't hide complexity behind clean-looking abstractions. If something is hard, the code should communicate that, not pretend it isn't.

Recoverable — I design for failure. Not because I'm pessimistic, but because in distributed systems failure is not the exception, it's the schedule. Every system I build, I ask: when this breaks, can we recover without losing data and without waking someone up at 3am?

👩 Nina: I love "recoverable" — very Uber. We deal with real-time systems where even 200ms latency matters. What's your experience with that kind of constraint?

👨 Khanh: My most direct experience is from payment systems where users abandon flows after about 3 seconds — so every hop in the chain matters. I learned to treat latency as a first-class feature, not an afterthought.

Practically that meant: profiling before optimising — never guess where the slowness is. Pushing slow work async wherever the business logic allowed it. Being very deliberate about what had to be synchronous versus what could be fire-and-forget. And monitoring p99 latency, not just average — because averages hide the tail where users actually churn.

Real-time matching is a different scale of that problem, but the thinking is the same.

👩 Nina: Great. Technical rounds are next. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Marketplace Platform.

PART A — Surge Pricing (20 min)

👨‍💻 Wei (Staff Eng): Surge pricing kicks in when driver supply is low relative to rider demand in a geographic zone. Walk me through how you'd design the surge calculation and apply it in real time.

👨 Khanh: Two parts: computing the surge multiplier, and applying it safely at fare calculation time.

Supply/demand ratio per hex zone (H3 grid):

@Service
public class SurgeCalculator {

    // H3 hex index → current surge multiplier
    private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Double> surgeCache =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    // Triggered every 60 seconds by Kafka consumer
    // consuming driver-location and ride-request events
    public void recalculate(String hexId,
                            int activeDrivers,
                            int pendingRequests) {
        double ratio = (pendingRequests == 0 || activeDrivers == 0)
            ? (pendingRequests > 0 ? 3.0 : 1.0)
            : (double) pendingRequests / activeDrivers;

        double multiplier = computeMultiplier(ratio);
        surgeCache.put(hexId, multiplier);

        // Publish for downstream services (fare calc, driver app)
        kafkaTemplate.send("surge.updates",
            hexId, new SurgeUpdate(hexId, multiplier, Instant.now()));
    }

    private double computeMultiplier(double demandSupplyRatio) {
        // Piecewise linear scaling, capped at 5x
        if (demandSupplyRatio <= 1.0) return 1.0;
        if (demandSupplyRatio <= 2.0) return 1.0 + (demandSupplyRatio - 1.0) * 0.5;
        if (demandSupplyRatio <= 4.0) return 1.5 + (demandSupplyRatio - 2.0) * 0.5;
        return Math.min(5.0, 2.5 + (demandSupplyRatio - 4.0) * 0.25);
    }

    public double getSurge(String hexId) {
        return surgeCache.getOrDefault(hexId, 1.0);
    }
}

Applying surge at fare estimation (before rider confirms):

@Service
public class FareCalculator {

    public FareEstimate estimate(RideRequest request) {
        String hexId = h3Service.latLngToHex(
            request.getPickupLat(), request.getPickupLng());

        double surge      = surgeCalculator.getSurge(hexId);
        double baseFare   = calculateBaseFare(request);
        double totalFare  = baseFare * surge;

        // Lock the surge for this request for 2 minutes
        // So fare doesn't change between estimate and confirmation
        String lockKey = "fare-lock:" + request.getRequestId();
        redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(lockKey,
            String.valueOf(surge), Duration.ofMinutes(2));

        return FareEstimate.builder()
            .baseFare(baseFare)
            .surgeMultiplier(surge)
            .totalFare(totalFare)
            .surgeHexId(hexId)
            .validUntil(Instant.now().plusSeconds(120))
            .build();
    }

    public ConfirmedFare confirmFare(String requestId) {
        String lockKey = "fare-lock:" + requestId;
        String lockedSurge = redisTemplate.opsForValue().get(lockKey);

        if (lockedSurge == null) {
            // Lock expired — recalculate with current surge
            throw new FareLockExpiredException(
                "Fare lock expired. Please request a new estimate.");
        }

        // Use the locked surge — not the current one
        double surge = Double.parseDouble(lockedSurge);
        return buildConfirmedFare(requestId, surge);
    }
}

Why lock the surge per request?

Without this, a rider sees "1.2x surge," confirms the ride, but by the time the backend processes the confirmation, surge has jumped to 2.5x. The rider is charged more than they agreed to. The lock guarantees price consistency between estimate and confirmation — a basic consumer trust requirement.

👨‍💻 Wei: What happens in a major event — a concert ends and 50,000 people request rides in two minutes. How does your system behave?

👨 Khanh: This is where the design is deliberately intentional about load shedding and graceful degradation.

Three failure zones and how I'd handle each:

1 — Surge calculator lag: If the driver-location Kafka topic is suddenly flooded with 50,000 events per minute, consumers may lag. Surge values in surgeCache become stale.

Mitigation: the surge cache has a computedAt timestamp. If a value is older than 90 seconds, fare estimation returns the stale value but adds a warning flag and bumps it conservatively upward. Stale-but-conservative is safer than stale-and-too-low.

public double getSurge(String hexId) {
    SurgeEntry entry = surgeCache.get(hexId);
    if (entry == null) return 1.5; // conservative default
    if (Duration.between(entry.computedAt(), Instant.now())
            .toSeconds() > 90) {
        return Math.min(5.0, entry.multiplier() * 1.2); // stale bump
    }
    return entry.multiplier();
}

2 — Redis overload (fare lock writes): Circuit breaker on Redis writes. If Redis is unavailable, skip the fare lock, log it, and allow re-estimation at confirmation. Slightly worse UX, but trips still work.

3 — Fare service CPU saturation: Rate limit at the API gateway: max 500 fare estimation requests per second per geographic region. Excess requests get a 429 with a retry-after header and a message telling users to try again in 10 seconds. This prevents the service from collapsing entirely under write storm.

Overall principle: degrade gracefully. A slightly stale surge is better than an error. A 10-second retry is better than a crash.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining Surge to a Non-Technical Audience (10 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: Imagine you're presenting the surge pricing system to Uber's public policy team — lawyers and government relations staff — who are preparing for a regulatory meeting. They've heard complaints that surge pricing is "price gouging." How do you explain what the system actually does?

👨 Khanh: I'd start by acknowledging the concern is legitimate — because it is. "Prices went up during an emergency" is a bad experience, and dismissing that feeling with engineering jargon makes things worse.

Then I'd explain it in plain terms, without the word "algorithm":

*"When a concert ends, five thousand people open Uber at the same moment. There are maybe three hundred drivers nearby. If we charged the normal price, all five thousand people would request a ride — and the first three hundred would get one. The other 4,700 would wait, potentially for hours, with no visibility on when a car would arrive.*

*Higher prices during that period do two things: they reduce demand to the level drivers can actually serve, and they signal to drivers further away that it's worth coming. Within twenty minutes, more drivers arrive, prices come down, and the situation normalises.*

*We also have a hard cap — prices never exceed 5x the normal fare, regardless of demand. And we never apply surge pricing during declared emergencies."*

Then I'd say: we know this is uncomfortable, and we take the concern seriously. That's why we send a clear notification when surge is active, show the exact multiplier before you confirm the ride, and you can always cancel before you're charged.

The goal of this explanation is not to win an argument but to give the policy team accurate information they can work with. Their job is harder than mine, and I should make it easier, not defend the system.

PART C — CODING: Driver Match Score (20 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: *"Given a list of available drivers and a rider's pickup location, implement a function that ranks drivers by a match score: 70% weight on distance, 30% weight on driver rating. Return the top 3 drivers. Use clean, readable Java."*

👨 Khanh:

public record Location(double lat, double lng) {}

public record Driver(
    String id,
    Location location,
    double rating    // 1.0 – 5.0
) {}

public record DriverMatch(Driver driver, double score) {}

public class DriverMatcher {

    private static final double DISTANCE_WEIGHT = 0.70;
    private static final double RATING_WEIGHT   = 0.30;
    private static final int    TOP_N           = 3;

    // Max expected search radius: 10 km = 10,000 m
    private static final double MAX_DISTANCE_M  = 10_000.0;
    private static final double MAX_RATING      = 5.0;

    public List<DriverMatch> topMatches(List<Driver> drivers,
                                        Location pickup) {
        return drivers.stream()
            .map(driver -> {
                double distanceM  = haversineMeters(
                    pickup, driver.location());
                double distScore  = normaliseDistance(distanceM);
                double ratingScore = driver.rating() / MAX_RATING;
                double score = DISTANCE_WEIGHT * distScore
                             + RATING_WEIGHT   * ratingScore;
                return new DriverMatch(driver, score);
            })
            .sorted(Comparator.comparingDouble(
                DriverMatch::score).reversed())
            .limit(TOP_N)
            .toList();
    }

    // Closer = higher score: score = 1 - (distance / max)
    // Clamped to [0, 1]
    private double normaliseDistance(double distanceM) {
        return Math.max(0.0,
            1.0 - (distanceM / MAX_DISTANCE_M));
    }

    // Haversine formula — straight-line distance in metres
    private double haversineMeters(Location a, Location b) {
        final double R = 6_371_000; // Earth radius in metres
        double dLat = Math.toRadians(b.lat() - a.lat());
        double dLng = Math.toRadians(b.lng() - a.lng());
        double sinDLat = Math.sin(dLat / 2);
        double sinDLng = Math.sin(dLng / 2);
        double h = sinDLat * sinDLat
                 + Math.cos(Math.toRadians(a.lat()))
                 * Math.cos(Math.toRadians(b.lat()))
                 * sinDLng * sinDLng;
        return 2 * R * Math.asin(Math.sqrt(h));
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void closerDriverRanksHigher() {
    var matcher = new DriverMatcher();
    Location pickup = new Location(10.0, 106.0);

    Driver near = new Driver("d1", new Location(10.001, 106.001), 4.5);
    Driver far  = new Driver("d2", new Location(10.05,  106.05),  4.5);

    var results = matcher.topMatches(List.of(near, far), pickup);
    assertThat(results.get(0).driver().id()).isEqualTo("d1");
}

@Test
void higherRatingBreaksTieAtSameDistance() {
    var matcher = new DriverMatcher();
    Location pickup = new Location(10.0, 106.0);
    Location same   = new Location(10.001, 106.001);

    Driver lowRated  = new Driver("d1", same, 3.0);
    Driver highRated = new Driver("d2", same, 5.0);

    var results = matcher.topMatches(List.of(lowRated, highRated), pickup);
    assertThat(results.get(0).driver().id()).isEqualTo("d2");
}

@Test
void returnsAtMostThreeDrivers() {
    var matcher = new DriverMatcher();
    Location pickup = new Location(10.0, 106.0);
    List<Driver> drivers = IntStream.range(0, 10)
        .mapToObj(i -> new Driver("d" + i,
            new Location(10.0 + i * 0.001, 106.0), 4.0))
        .toList();

    assertThat(matcher.topMatches(drivers, pickup)).hasSize(3);
}

@Test
void handlesEmptyDriverList() {
    var matcher = new DriverMatcher();
    assertThat(matcher.topMatches(List.of(),
        new Location(10.0, 106.0))).isEmpty();
}

💬 ROUND 3 — LEADERSHIP & CULTURE (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager, Marketplace.

👩 Lisa (EM): Uber moves fast. Sometimes faster than the quality would ideally allow. How do you personally maintain standards without becoming a bottleneck?

👨 Khanh: This tension is real, and I've seen it handled badly in both directions.

The anti-pattern I try to avoid is being the engineer who blocks every PR with exhaustive comments — "this variable name is suboptimal," "I'd have structured this differently." That's not maintaining standards; that's ego in code review clothes.

The pattern I try to follow is: reserve my strongest opinions for things that are genuinely hard to fix later. Naming, formatting, minor structure — I'll mention it once, note it's non-blocking, and move on. But if I see a missing error path in a payment flow, or a race condition in a concurrent operation, or a test that only covers the happy path — those I will hold the line on, because they will cost us much more to fix in production.

I also try to pair review with mentoring. If someone makes the same structural mistake twice, I don't just comment — I ask if they have 20 minutes to walk through it together. That's faster overall than two cycles of async comments, and it actually changes how the person thinks, not just what they ship this time.

The goal is to be a quality partner, not a quality gatekeeper.

👩 Lisa: Tell me about a time you failed on a project. Not a small thing — a real failure.

👨 Khanh: The failure I think about most happened about two years ago.

I was leading the technical design of a service migration — moving a legacy monolith component to a standalone microservice. I had a clear technical plan, a timeline, and buy-in from my team. What I didn't do well was keep the adjacent teams — the API gateway team and the mobile client team — aligned on the rollout schedule.

I assumed our internal communication was sufficient. I sent one update email and thought that was enough.

It wasn't. When we deployed the new service, the API gateway team hadn't updated their routing rules and the mobile clients were still calling the old endpoint. We had a 45-minute partial outage — some users were affected, some weren't, which is actually harder to debug than a full outage.

The technical fix was straightforward. The harder lesson was about communication. I had treated the migration as a technical project when it was actually a coordination project. The code was 40% of the work; the alignment was 60%. I'd only planned for the 40%.

Since then, I create a stakeholder map at the start of any cross-team project — who needs to know what, by when, and in what format. And I follow up. One email is not communication. One email is a notification.

👩 Lisa: If you joined Uber, what would your first 90 days look like?

👨 Khanh: I break it into thirds.

First 30 days — listen and map. I'd resist the urge to contribute code or have opinions. My goal is to understand: what does the team consider its most important work right now, and why? What are the ongoing pain points that everyone knows about but nobody has time to fix? Where does the team feel proud, and where do they feel embarrassed? I learn more from those conversations than from the codebase.

I'd also shadow on-call for at least one rotation — nothing tells you more about a system's actual state than what breaks at 2am and why.

Days 31–60 — make one meaningful contribution. Based on what I've learned, I'd pick one thing I can improve that clearly matters to the team — not the most glamorous thing, just the most useful one. Ship it, get it reviewed, iterate. This is about demonstrating that I can execute in the new environment, not just that I have good ideas.

Days 61–90 — start forming opinions. By this point I have enough context to have informed views. I'd share them carefully — in writing, with reasoning — and see how they land. If I've been listening well, they'll be relevant. If I've been assuming too much, the feedback will teach me something.

The thing I'd explicitly avoid is arriving with a list of things I'd change based on how my last company did it. That's the fastest way to lose credibility on a new team.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Engineering style + latency mindset 10/10 "Recoverable" framing; p99 over average — sharp
Tech A Surge pricing + fare lock 10/10 H3 grid, piecewise multiplier, 2-min Redis lock for price consistency
Tech A+ Concert event degradation 10/10 Stale-bump strategy, circuit breaker, rate limiting at gateway
Tech B Surge comms to policy team 10/10 Lead with empathy, plain language, no jargon, hard cap facts
Tech C Coding: driver match score 10/10 Haversine, weighted score, normalised, 4 tests
Leadership Quality without bottleneck 10/10 Quality partner vs gatekeeper; pair review over comment cycles
Failure Real failure story 10/10 Technical vs coordination project distinction — genuinely insightful
90 days Onboarding plan 10/10 Listen → contribute → form opinions; shadow on-call

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Uber Interview Tips

  1. H3 hexagonal grid — Uber's standard for geospatial zoning; know what it is conceptually
  2. Fare lock in Redis — price consistency between estimate and confirmation is a trust requirement
  3. Stale-but-conservative — in real-time systems, a stale cached value with a safety bump beats an error
  4. Surge = supply/demand signal, not gouging — have a clean, non-defensive explanation ready
  5. Haversine formula — know it exists for lat/lng distance; doesn't need to be memorised perfectly
  6. Failure story = coordination failure — "1 email is a notification, not communication" is memorable
  7. 90-day plan — always mention: shadow on-call, pick one meaningful contribution, form opinions last
  8. Uber salaries (Remote/APAC): USD 150–210K base + significant RSU; strong L5 compensation

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Style "I design for failure — not because I'm pessimistic, but because failure is not the exception, it's the schedule."
Latency "Treat latency as a first-class feature, not an afterthought."
Degradation "A slightly stale surge is better than an error. A 10-second retry is better than a crash."
Policy comms "The goal is not to win an argument but to give them accurate information they can work with."
Code review "Quality partner, not quality gatekeeper."
Failure "One email is not communication. One email is a notification."
Onboarding "Nothing tells you more about a system's actual state than what breaks at 2am and why."

🎭 Mock Interview #41 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Netflix (Los Gatos / Remote, Global Streaming Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Streaming Platform team. Netflix serves 270M subscribers across 190 countries, delivering 15% of global internet traffic. Engineering culture is famous for radical candour, freedom & responsibility, and chaos engineering. Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, gRPC, Kafka, Cassandra, EVCache (Redis), Zuul, Hystrix/Resilience4j, Temporal. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Mia (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Netflix is unusual in how we hire — we don't just look for skill, we look for judgment. What does good judgment look like to you in engineering?

👨 Khanh: Good judgment means making the right call with incomplete information — and being honest about the uncertainty.

Technical judgment specifically, for me, is about reversibility. When I'm making a decision under uncertainty, I ask: if I'm wrong, how expensive is it to undo? If the answer is "very expensive," I slow down, gather more data, and write down the reasoning before committing. If the answer is "cheap," I make the call, ship it, and learn from what happens.

The failure mode I try to avoid is false precision — spending two weeks analysing a decision that should take two days, because the analysis feels safer than the commitment. Sometimes the most valuable thing you can do is make a reasonable call, observe the result, and iterate. That's not recklessness; it's calibrated confidence.

👩 Mia: Netflix has a famous "Keeper Test" — managers ask themselves whether they'd fight to keep someone. What makes you someone a team would fight to keep?

👨 Khanh: I think it's the combination of two things that individually aren't rare, but together are.

The first is technical depth that's actually useful — not just knowing things, but being the person who can work through a novel production problem methodically, under pressure, without guessing.

The second is making people around me more effective. I ask the question in code review that helps the author think differently, not just fix this PR. I write the postmortem that changes the process, not just documents the incident. I give the new engineer the context they need instead of just the answer.

Either one alone is a solid senior engineer. Together, they're what I'd fight to keep.

👩 Mia: Great. Four technical and behavioural rounds follow. Good luck, Khanh!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Streaming Platform.

PART A — Video Streaming Resilience (25 min)

👨‍💻 Carlos (Staff Eng): Netflix pioneered chaos engineering. The idea is: if we don't deliberately break things, we won't know they're breakable until a real outage. As a backend engineer, how do you design a service that degrades gracefully when its dependencies fail?

👨 Khanh: Graceful degradation is about having a deliberate answer to every failure question — not hoping dependencies stay up.

The pattern I use is layered fallbacks with circuit breakers. Let me walk through it with a concrete example: the recommendation service that decides what shows to display on the homepage.

Three dependency levels, three fallback strategies:

@Service
public class HomepageService {

    // Primary: personalised recommendations (ML model, 50ms p99)
    // Fallback 1: cached recommendations from last call
    // Fallback 2: trending content (no personalisation, but always available)
    // Fallback 3: hardcoded editorial picks (zero dependencies)

    @CircuitBreaker(name = "recommendations",
                    fallbackMethod = "cachedRecommendations")
    public List<Title> getPersonalised(String userId) {
        return recommendationClient.fetch(userId);
    }

    public List<Title> cachedRecommendations(String userId,
                                              Throwable ex) {
        log.warn("Recommendations circuit open for user {}: {}",
            userId, ex.getMessage());
        // Try Redis cache (last successful response, up to 1 hour old)
        List<Title> cached = cacheClient.get("recs:" + userId);
        if (cached != null && !cached.isEmpty()) return cached;
        return trendingFallback(userId, ex);
    }

    public List<Title> trendingFallback(String userId,
                                         Throwable ex) {
        log.warn("Cache miss for user {} — serving trending", userId);
        try {
            return trendingService.getGlobalTrending();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            return editorialFallback(userId, e);
        }
    }

    public List<Title> editorialFallback(String userId,
                                          Throwable ex) {
        log.error("All recommendation sources failed for user {}", userId);
        // Hardcoded — zero network calls, never fails
        return EditorialContent.TOP_PICKS;
    }
}

Circuit breaker configuration:

// In application.yml
resilience4j:
  circuitbreaker:
    instances:
      recommendations:
        slidingWindowSize: 20          # last 20 calls
        failureRateThreshold: 50       # open if >50% fail
        waitDurationInOpenState: 10s   # try again after 10s
        permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState: 3

The discipline behind this: Each fallback is pre-built and tested — not written during an incident. Chaos testing verifies each level works before it's needed in production. 

@Test
void fallsBackToCacheWhenRecommendationsFail() {
    when(recommendationClient.fetch("u1"))
        .thenThrow(new RuntimeException("service down"));
    when(cacheClient.get("recs:u1"))
        .thenReturn(List.of(new Title("Stranger Things")));

    var result = homepageService.getPersonalised("u1");
    assertThat(result).extracting(Title::name)
        .contains("Stranger Things");
}

@Test
void fallsBackToEditorialWhenAllSourcesFail() {
    when(recommendationClient.fetch("u1"))
        .thenThrow(new RuntimeException());
    when(cacheClient.get("recs:u1")).thenReturn(null);
    when(trendingService.getGlobalTrending())
        .thenThrow(new RuntimeException());

    var result = homepageService.getPersonalised("u1");
    assertThat(result).isEqualTo(EditorialContent.TOP_PICKS);
}

👨‍💻 Carlos: You mentioned chaos testing. Practically, how would you introduce chaos into a staging environment for this service?

👨 Khanh: Three categories of chaos I'd inject, each testing a different failure mode.

1 — Latency injection (tests timeout handling):

// Chaos proxy wraps the recommendation client in staging
public class ChaosRecommendationClient implements RecommendationClient {

    private final RecommendationClient delegate;
    private final ChaosConfig config; // toggled per environment

    @Override
    public List<Title> fetch(String userId) {
        if (config.isLatencyEnabled()) {
            int delayMs = config.getLatencyMs(); // e.g., 3000ms
            Uninterruptibles.sleepUninterruptibly(
                delayMs, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
        }
        return delegate.fetch(userId);
    }
}

2 — Error injection (tests circuit breaker):

@Override
public List<Title> fetch(String userId) {
    if (config.isErrorEnabled()
            && Math.random() < config.getErrorRate()) { // e.g., 0.6
        throw new RuntimeException("Chaos: simulated failure");
    }
    return delegate.fetch(userId);
}

3 — Network partition (tests fallback completeness): In staging, use tc (Linux traffic control) or Toxiproxy to block the network path to the recommendation service entirely. This catches the "we have a fallback" assumption that turns out to only work when the service is slow, not when it's completely unreachable.

How I'd run it:

  • Start with 10% error rate in staging, verify cache fallback
  • Escalate to 60% — verify circuit breaker opens
  • Full block — verify editorial fallback is returned
  • Run in canary production with 0.1% traffic, monitor p99

The key principle: chaos tests should run automatically in CI/CD, not just manually. If a fallback breaks silently in a code change, you want to know before the release.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Radical Candour in Practice (15 min)

👨‍💻 Carlos: Netflix's culture requires direct, honest feedback. Tell me about a time you gave feedback that was genuinely hard to deliver — and how you did it.

👨 Khanh: About a year ago, a colleague — someone I respected and had worked well with for months — was consistently missing the design and reliability bar on a critical service we were co-owning. Two incidents in six weeks were traceable to their code. The team was aware but nobody had said anything directly.

I found that more troubling than the incidents themselves. Silence in those situations isn't kindness — it's avoidance that compounds over time.

I asked for 30 minutes with them privately. I'd thought carefully about how to open it — not with "I have concerns about your work" which would have triggered defensiveness — but with: *"I want to talk about the last two incidents because I think they point to something important, and I'd rather talk about it with you directly than have it become a manager conversation."*

I was specific. I described both incidents, what the code did, and what the production impact was. I did not generalise to "your quality has been low." Generalisation is unkind and unhelpful — it gives someone nothing to act on.

Then I listened. And the thing I discovered was important: they were dealing with a personal situation that had affected their concentration for two months. They hadn't flagged it to anyone. The workload we'd given them was genuinely more than they could absorb at that time.

So the conversation shifted. I helped them reprioritise, took one of their critical components back into my review scope temporarily, and checked in weekly.

Two months later, they were back to full capacity. The feedback mattered — but so did the curiosity that came after it.

The lesson I took: direct feedback and genuine care are not opposites. Radical candour without care is just bluntness. The care is what makes the candour land.

PART C — CODING: Rate-Aware Retry (20 min)

👨‍💻 Carlos: *"Implement a retry utility with exponential backoff and jitter. Max 4 retries. Jitter prevents thundering herd when many clients retry simultaneously. Clean, testable Java."*

👨 Khanh:

public class RetryWithBackoff {

    private final int    maxRetries;
    private final long   baseDelayMs;
    private final long   maxDelayMs;
    private final Random random;

    public RetryWithBackoff(int maxRetries,
                            long baseDelayMs,
                            long maxDelayMs) {
        this.maxRetries  = maxRetries;
        this.baseDelayMs = baseDelayMs;
        this.maxDelayMs  = maxDelayMs;
        this.random      = new Random();
    }

    // Execute a supplier with retry; throws last exception if all fail
    public <T> T execute(Supplier<T> action) throws Exception {
        Exception lastException = null;

        for (int attempt = 0; attempt <= maxRetries; attempt++) {
            try {
                return action.get();
            } catch (Exception ex) {
                lastException = ex;

                if (attempt == maxRetries) break; // no more retries

                long delay = computeDelay(attempt);
                log.warn("Attempt {} failed ({}). Retrying in {}ms",
                    attempt + 1, ex.getMessage(), delay);
                Thread.sleep(delay);
            }
        }

        throw new RetryExhaustedException(
            "Failed after " + maxRetries + " retries", lastException);
    }

    // Exponential backoff + full jitter
    // delay = random(0, min(maxDelay, base * 2^attempt))
    private long computeDelay(int attempt) {
        long exponential = baseDelayMs * (1L << attempt); // base * 2^attempt
        long capped      = Math.min(exponential, maxDelayMs);
        return (long) (random.nextDouble() * capped); // full jitter
    }
}

// --- Tests (inject a deterministic clock / mock sleeper in prod) ---
@Test
void succeedsOnFirstAttempt() throws Exception {
    var retry = new RetryWithBackoff(4, 100, 5000);
    AtomicInteger calls = new AtomicInteger(0);

    String result = retry.execute(() -> {
        calls.incrementAndGet();
        return "ok";
    });

    assertThat(result).isEqualTo("ok");
    assertThat(calls.get()).isEqualTo(1);
}

@Test
void retriesUntilSuccess() throws Exception {
    var retry = new RetryWithBackoff(4, 1, 10); // tiny delays for testing
    AtomicInteger calls = new AtomicInteger(0);

    String result = retry.execute(() -> {
        if (calls.incrementAndGet() < 3) {
            throw new RuntimeException("transient");
        }
        return "recovered";
    });

    assertThat(result).isEqualTo("recovered");
    assertThat(calls.get()).isEqualTo(3);
}

@Test
void throwsAfterMaxRetries() {
    var retry = new RetryWithBackoff(3, 1, 10);

    assertThatThrownBy(() ->
        retry.execute(() -> { throw new RuntimeException("always fails"); })
    ).isInstanceOf(RetryExhaustedException.class)
     .hasMessageContaining("3 retries");
}

@Test
void jitterKeepsDelayWithinBounds() {
    var retry = new RetryWithBackoff(4, 100, 1000);
    // Attempt 0: delay in [0, 100)
    // Attempt 1: delay in [0, 200)
    // Attempt 2: delay in [0, 400)
    // Attempt 3: delay in [0, 800)
    // All capped at 1000ms
    for (int attempt = 0; attempt < 4; attempt++) {
        long delay = retry.computeDelay(attempt); // expose for testing
        assertThat(delay).isBetween(0L, Math.min(1000L,
            100L * (1L << attempt)));
    }
}

Why full jitter matters:

Without jitter, if 10,000 clients all fail at the same moment and all retry with the same exponential delay, they all retry simultaneously — creating the thundering herd that caused the first failure. Full jitter spreads retries randomly across the window, reducing peak load by up to 90%.

💬 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (30 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer, Platform.

👨‍💻 Elena (Principal Eng): Design Netflix's play history service — when a user watches a show, record the position so they can resume. 270M users, each watching daily. Focus on the write path and the resume read path.

👨 Khanh: Before I design anything, I want to clarify the requirements — because the right design depends heavily on two questions.

First: what's the consistency requirement? If a user watches 10 minutes on their TV, switches to their phone, and the phone shows they're at 8 minutes — is that acceptable? I'd expect the answer is "yes, within a few seconds is fine," but I want to confirm.

Second: what's the write frequency? Players typically emit a position heartbeat every 10–30 seconds. At 270M users, if even 10% are watching simultaneously, that's 27M writes every 10–30 seconds — roughly 1M–2.7M writes per minute. That shapes the storage choice.

Assuming: eventual consistency acceptable, 30-second heartbeat interval.

Write path — high-throughput, low-latency:

Player client → API Gateway
  → Position Service (Spring Boot, stateless, 50+ pods)
    → Kafka topic: playback.positions
      (key = userId, ensures ordering per user)
  → Consumer group: position-persister
    → Batched upsert to Cassandra every 5 seconds

Why Kafka buffer?

Direct writes to Cassandra at 1M+ writes/minute is feasible but creates hot partitions and makes the API synchronously coupled to DB write throughput. Kafka absorbs the burst, consumers batch-write, and the player gets an immediate 200 OK.

Cassandra schema:

Table: playback_positions
Partition key: user_id
Clustering key: content_id
Columns: position_seconds, updated_at, device_id

Query: SELECT * FROM playback_positions
       WHERE user_id = ? AND content_id = ?
→ Single partition read, O(1), <5ms p99

Resume read path:

GET /v1/resume?contentId=123
  → Position Service
    → EVCache (Redis-like) lookup: "pos:{userId}:{contentId}"
      HIT → return immediately (~1ms)
      MISS → read from Cassandra → populate cache (TTL 1h)
              → return to client

Cache strategy: write-through on every Kafka consume. When the consumer writes to Cassandra, it also updates EVCache. Cache is always warm for active watchers.

Numbers:

  • Write throughput: 1–3M writes/min via Kafka → absorbed
  • Resume read: <5ms p99 from EVCache, <20ms from Cassandra
  • Storage: 270M users × avg 500 shows watched = 135B rows.

Cassandra handles this comfortably with proper partitioning.

What I deliberately left simple: I didn't add a real-time sync protocol between devices. That's a separate problem — and adding it now would complicate the design without evidence it's needed.

👨‍💻 Elena: The Kafka consumer is batching writes every 5 seconds. A user watches, then immediately closes the app and reopens on a different device within 3 seconds. What happens?

👨 Khanh: Exactly the race condition worth calling out.

What happens: the first position write is still in Kafka, not yet in Cassandra. The second device reads EVCache — also not yet updated — and gets the position from the previous session, potentially off by up to 30 seconds of viewing.

Acceptable or not? For Netflix specifically, I'd argue it's acceptable. Resuming 30 seconds early is a minor inconvenience. Resuming 30 seconds late is slightly worse. Neither is data loss.

If it's not acceptable, two options:

Option A — Synchronous write on app close: When the player app detects a close event, it fires one synchronous write directly to Cassandra, bypassing the Kafka buffer. Most writes are async; final-position write is sync. Adds latency only on close.

Option B — Read from Kafka offset before reading DB: The resume read queries the Kafka consumer's in-memory position buffer before falling back to Cassandra. More complex, but achieves near-real-time consistency without synchronous writes.

I'd recommend Option A — simpler, lower risk, solves the specific problem without changing the write path for the 99.9% case.

💬 ROUND 4 — CULTURE & VALUES (25 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer + EM panel.

👩 Priya (EM): Netflix gives engineers a lot of autonomy. How do you avoid making bad decisions when there's no one checking your work?

👨 Khanh: I've thought about this, and my honest answer is: autonomy works when you internalise the accountability that usually comes externally.

Practically, I have three self-imposed checks.

First, I write down my reasoning before I commit to a significant decision. Not to share it — just to pressure test it. Writing forces a level of precision that thinking doesn't. If I can't write it clearly, I probably don't understand it clearly enough to decide.

Second, I find one person to tell my plan to — not for approval, but to hear myself say it out loud. I call this "saying it to a rubber duck with opinions." Often I catch the flaw myself mid-explanation. And occasionally they catch something I missed.

Third, I define upfront what would tell me I was wrong. What metric, what user signal, what failure mode would indicate this decision was bad? Having a pre-agreed failure signal makes it much easier to change course without it feeling like an admission of defeat.

Autonomy isn't "do whatever you want." It's being able to answer "what would change my mind?" before you commit.

👩 Priya: Netflix has a no-blame culture around failures. Do you think no-blame can ever go too far?

👨 Khanh: Yes — and this is a nuanced area I care about.

Blameless culture at its best means: when something goes wrong, we look for the system condition that made the mistake easy to make, rather than punishing the individual. That is genuinely better. It leads to process improvements instead of scapegoating, and it means people surface problems early rather than hiding them.

But "blameless" can tip into "no accountability" — and those are very different. If an engineer repeatedly makes the same class of mistake after it's been raised directly, that's a performance issue. Blameless culture should protect people from unfair punishment, not shield them from honest feedback.

The version I try to practise is: *blameless about the system, accountable about the behaviour.* We don't blame the person for the incident — but if someone isn't growing or isn't meeting the expectations of the role, that needs to be named directly and managed appropriately.

The most dysfunctional thing I've seen in engineering teams is when "blameless culture" becomes the reason nobody gives honest feedback. That's not psychological safety — that's avoidance with a good PR name.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Judgment + Keeper Test 10/10 Reversibility framing; depth + making others better
Tech A Graceful degradation + circuit breaker 10/10 4-level fallback, Resilience4j config, chaos test suite
Tech A+ Chaos engineering in practice 10/10 Latency / error / partition injection; CI chaos tests
Behavioural Radical candour delivery 10/10 Specific not general; curiosity after feedback; care + candour
Tech C Coding: retry with jitter 10/10 Full jitter formula, 4 tests, thundering herd explanation
System Design Play history at 270M scale 10/10 Kafka buffer, Cassandra, EVCache write-through, race condition analysis
Culture A Autonomy + self-accountability 10/10 Write reasoning, rubber duck with opinions, pre-agree failure signal
Culture B Blameless culture nuance 10/10 Blameless about system, accountable about behaviour — sophisticated take

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Netflix Interview Tips

  1. Freedom & Responsibility — autonomy is earned by demonstrating good judgment, not given by default
  2. Layered fallbacks — build and test every level before production; chaos tests should run in CI
  3. Full jitter — not just capped exponential; random spread across the window prevents thundering herd
  4. Cassandra for write-heavy, partition-based reads — user_id as partition key = O(1) reads
  5. Kafka buffer for burst writes — decouples API latency from DB write throughput
  6. Blameless ≠ no accountability — "blameless about the system, accountable about the behaviour"
  7. Radical candour — specific not general; curiosity after delivery is what makes feedback land
  8. Netflix salaries (Senior/L5): USD 200–300K base + significant RSU; one of the highest in tech

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Judgment "If I'm wrong, how expensive is it to undo?"
Judgment "False precision — analysing for two weeks what should take two days."
Keeper Test "Making people around me more effective — not just being effective myself."
Degradation "Each fallback is pre-built and tested — not written during an incident."
Candour "Radical candour without care is just bluntness. The care is what makes it land."
Jitter "Full jitter spreads retries randomly, reducing peak load by up to 90%."
Autonomy "Autonomy works when you internalise the accountability that usually comes externally."
Blameless "Blameless about the system. Accountable about the behaviour."

🎭 Mock Interview #42 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Google (Mountain View / Remote, Cloud & Infrastructure)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Google Cloud Pub/Sub team. Google Cloud serves millions of developers. Pub/Sub alone processes hundreds of billions of messages per day with global delivery guarantees. Tech: Java 17, Protocol Buffers, gRPC, Spanner, Bigtable, internal frameworks. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. Note: Google interviews have a distinctive style — they value "Googleyness" (intellectual humility, collaboration, comfort with ambiguity) as much as technical skill. This mock reflects that.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER / HIRING COMMITTEE PREP (15 min, English)

👩 Sarah (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Google is competitive. Tell me — what's the hardest technical interview you've ever had, and what did you take away from it?

👨 Khanh: The hardest one was for a role I didn't get — and that's probably why it was the most valuable.

The interviewer asked me to design a distributed rate limiter at global scale. I had a reasonable solution — token buckets, Redis, consistent hashing. But halfway through, they asked: "What happens if two data centres disagree on the count?" and I froze. I'd built a solution that assumed a single source of truth without ever questioning that assumption.

I didn't get the role. But the question stayed with me for weeks. I went home and read about CRDTs, about eventual consistency in distributed counters, about the CAP theorem in the context of rate limiting. I rebuilt my mental model completely.

Two things I took away. First: the most revealing interview questions are the follow-ups, not the opening problem — so now I try to ask myself the follow-up before the interviewer does. Second: not getting a role isn't failure if you leave knowing something you didn't know before.

👩 Sarah: Great self-awareness. Google values "Googleyness" — intellectual humility, comfort with ambiguity, collaboration. How do you handle being wrong in public?

👨 Khanh: My honest answer is: I've had to practice this.

My natural instinct when I'm challenged is to defend my position, because I've usually thought about it more than the person challenging me in that moment. That instinct is wrong more often than it's right.

What I've trained myself to do is pause and ask: "What would have to be true for their point to be correct?" That question is not rhetorical — I actually try to answer it. If I can construct a scenario where they're right and I'm wrong, I say so immediately. The faster you say "you're right, I hadn't considered that," the more trust you build and the faster the team moves.

Being wrong in public is only embarrassing if you treat it as a loss. I try to treat it as information.

👩 Sarah: Five technical rounds follow. Prepare for ambiguity. Good luck, Khanh!

💻 ROUND 2 — ALGORITHMS & PROBLEM SOLVING (45 min, English)

Setting: Senior Software Engineer. No framework preference — pure logic.

👨‍💻 David (Sr SWE): Let's start with something practical.

*"You have a stream of log lines arriving in real time. Each line has a timestamp (not guaranteed ordered) and a severity level: INFO, WARN, ERROR. Implement a sliding-window alert: fire an alert if more than 10 ERROR lines appear within any 60-second window. Optimise for memory — the stream is infinite."*

👨 Khanh: Let me think through this out loud before coding.

The key constraint is "any 60-second window" — that's a sliding window, not a tumbling window. And "infinite stream" means I can't store everything. So I need a data structure that stores only what's relevant: ERROR timestamps within the last 60 seconds.

A deque is the right structure — I slide the left boundary forward as time advances, and add to the right.

public class ErrorAlertDetector {

    private static final int    ALERT_THRESHOLD = 10;
    private static final long   WINDOW_SECONDS  = 60L;

    // Stores epoch-seconds timestamps of recent ERROR lines
    private final Deque<Long> errorWindow = new ArrayDeque<>();

    public record LogLine(long timestampSeconds, String severity) {}

    /**
     * Process one log line. Returns true if alert should fire.
     * Thread-safety note: caller must synchronise if multi-threaded.
     */
    public boolean process(LogLine line) {
        if (!"ERROR".equals(line.severity())) return false;

        long cutoff = line.timestampSeconds() - WINDOW_SECONDS;

        // Evict timestamps outside the 60-second window
        while (!errorWindow.isEmpty()
                && errorWindow.peekFirst() <= cutoff) {
            errorWindow.pollFirst();
        }

        errorWindow.addLast(line.timestampSeconds());

        return errorWindow.size() > ALERT_THRESHOLD;
    }

    public int currentWindowSize() { return errorWindow.size(); }
}

Complexity:

  • Time: amortised O(1) per line — each timestamp enters

and exits the deque exactly once.

  • Space: O(k) where k = max ERROR lines in any 60s window.

For a well-behaved system, k is small. Worst case is bounded by the maximum ERROR rate.

// Tests
@Test
void noAlertBelowThreshold() {
    var detector = new ErrorAlertDetector();
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
        assertThat(detector.process(
            new LogLine(1000L + i, "ERROR"))).isFalse();
    }
}

@Test
void alertOnEleventhError() {
    var detector = new ErrorAlertDetector();
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
        detector.process(new LogLine(1000L + i, "ERROR"));
    }
    assertThat(detector.process(new LogLine(1059L, "ERROR"))).isTrue();
}

@Test
void windowSlidesCorrectly() {
    var detector = new ErrorAlertDetector();
    // 10 errors at t=0..9
    for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {
        detector.process(new LogLine(i, "ERROR"));
    }
    // At t=70, all previous errors are outside 60s window
    assertThat(detector.process(new LogLine(70L, "ERROR"))).isFalse();
}

@Test
void ignoresNonErrorLines() {
    var detector = new ErrorAlertDetector();
    for (int i = 0; i < 20; i++) {
        assertThat(detector.process(
            new LogLine(1000L + i, "INFO"))).isFalse();
    }
    assertThat(detector.currentWindowSize()).isEqualTo(0);
}

👨‍💻 David: Good. Follow-up: the stream is now multi-threaded — multiple threads call process() concurrently. What changes?

👨 Khanh: The ArrayDeque isn't thread-safe, so concurrent process() calls would corrupt the deque state — lost updates, or peekFirst() returning a value that's been concurrently removed.

Two options:

Option A — synchronized method (simplest):

public synchronized boolean process(LogLine line) { ... }

Correct, low overhead for moderate concurrency. All threads serialise through one lock.

Option B — Separate per-key deques with finer granularity: If the alert is per-service or per-host, use a ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deque<Long>> where each key has its own lock:

private final ConcurrentHashMap<String, Deque<Long>> windowsBySource =
    new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

public boolean process(String sourceId, LogLine line) {
    Deque<Long> window = windowsBySource.computeIfAbsent(
        sourceId, k -> new ArrayDeque<>());
    synchronized (window) {
        // same sliding logic
    }
}

This scales linearly with the number of distinct sources rather than being a single global bottleneck.

For a single global counter, Option A is fine. For per-source counters at high throughput, Option B is better.

💻 ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer, Cloud Infrastructure.

👨‍💻 Lin (Principal Eng): Design a simplified version of Google Pub/Sub: a publish-subscribe messaging system. Publishers send messages to topics. Subscribers pull messages from subscriptions. At-least-once delivery. Handle millions of messages per second globally.

Take a moment to clarify requirements before you start.

👨 Khanh: Thank you — I do want to clarify a few things before designing.

Clarifying questions:

  1. Message ordering — do subscribers need messages in publish order?

Or is best-effort ordering acceptable?

  1. Message size — is there a max? (Pub/Sub is 10MB — I'll assume that.)
  2. Retention — how long do unacknowledged messages live? 7 days?
  3. Pull vs push delivery — are we supporting both, or pull only for now?
  4. Global? — do messages need to be readable from multiple regions?

*Assuming: ordering not guaranteed globally, 7-day retention, pull delivery for now, multi-region read.*

High-level architecture:
