[Publisher] → Ingestion API (gRPC) → Message Store → [Subscriber pulls]
                                           ↓
                                    Delivery Tracker (ack management)

Core data model:

Topic: { topicId, name, retentionDays }
Subscription: { subscriptionId, topicId, ackDeadlineSeconds }
Message: { messageId, topicId, payload, publishedAt, attributes }
AckRecord: { subscriptionId, messageId, ackDeadline, ackedAt }

Write path — Publish:

@GrpcService
public class PublisherService extends PublisherGrpc.PublisherImplBase {

    @Override
    public void publish(PublishRequest request,
                        StreamObserver<PublishResponse> observer) {
        String topicId = request.getTopic();
        List<String> messageIds = new ArrayList<>();

        for (PubsubMessage msg : request.getMessagesList()) {
            String msgId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

            // Write to Bigtable — row key: topicId#timestamp#msgId
            // Ensures range scans by topic+time are fast
            bigtableClient.write(Cell.of(
                topicId + "#" + epochMicros() + "#" + msgId,
                msg.getData(),
                msg.getAttributesMap()
            ));

            messageIds.add(msgId);
        }

        // Notify subscription dispatchers via Kafka
        kafkaTemplate.send("new-messages", topicId,
            new NewMessageEvent(topicId, messageIds));

        observer.onNext(PublishResponse.newBuilder()
            .addAllMessageIds(messageIds).build());
        observer.onCompleted();
    }
}

Read path — Pull:

@GrpcService
public class SubscriberService extends SubscriberGrpc.SubscriberImplBase {

    @Override
    public void pull(PullRequest request,
                     StreamObserver<PullResponse> observer) {
        String subId    = request.getSubscription();
        int    maxMsgs  = Math.min(request.getMaxMessages(), 1000);

        // Find undelivered messages for this subscription
        List<Message> messages = deliveryTracker
            .getUndelivered(subId, maxMsgs);

        // Set ack deadline (start the clock)
        List<ReceivedMessage> received = messages.stream()
            .map(msg -> {
                String ackId = deliveryTracker.lease(subId,
                    msg.getId(), subscriptionRepo
                        .getAckDeadline(subId)); // e.g., 30s
                return ReceivedMessage.of(msg, ackId);
            })
            .toList();

        observer.onNext(PullResponse.newBuilder()
            .addAllReceivedMessages(received).build());
        observer.onCompleted();
    }

    @Override
    public void acknowledge(AcknowledgeRequest request,
                            StreamObserver<Empty> observer) {
        // Mark ack records as acknowledged — won't be redelivered
        deliveryTracker.acknowledge(
            request.getSubscription(), request.getAckIdsList());
        observer.onNext(Empty.getDefaultInstance());
        observer.onCompleted();
    }
}

At-least-once delivery — ack deadline redelivery:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 5000)
public void redeliverExpired() {
    // Find messages whose ack deadline has passed without ack
    List<AckRecord> expired = deliveryTracker.findExpired(Instant.now());

    for (AckRecord rec : expired) {
        log.info("Redelivering message {} on sub {} (deadline expired)",
            rec.messageId(), rec.subscriptionId());
        // Reset deadline — will be returned on next Pull call
        deliveryTracker.resetLease(rec);
    }
}

Scaling to millions of messages/second:

Layer Scale mechanism
Ingestion API Stateless pods, horizontal scale, gRPC streaming
Message store Bigtable — row key includes timestamp for ordered scans, auto-sharded
Delivery tracker Spanner — global transactions for ack state
Kafka Topic-per-region fan-out to subscription dispatchers

👨‍💻 Lin: Good. How do you handle a slow subscriber — one that pulls messages but never acknowledges them?

👨 Khanh: Two problems here: wasted redelivery loops, and eventually the subscription falling so far behind that the topic's retention window passes and messages are lost.

Short-term — ack deadline extension: Subscribers that are doing heavy processing should call ModifyAckDeadline to extend their lease before it expires. The client library does this automatically for long-running processing. We don't redeliver while the lease is valid.

Medium-term — dead letter topic: After N redelivery attempts (configurable, e.g., 5), the message is moved to a dead letter topic rather than retried indefinitely. Operators can inspect or reprocess from there.

private void redeliverExpired() {
    for (AckRecord rec : expired) {
        if (rec.deliveryAttempts() >= maxDeliveryAttempts) {
            // Move to dead letter topic
            deadLetterPublisher.publish(
                subscription.getDeadLetterTopic(), rec.message());
            deliveryTracker.discard(rec);
        } else {
            deliveryTracker.resetLease(rec);
        }
    }
}

Long-term — subscription backlog alert: If a subscription's unacked backlog exceeds a threshold (e.g., 7-day retention × expected rate), we alert the subscriber team. If messages expire before ack, they are simply lost — which is the trade-off the subscriber accepted when they chose a retention window.

💬 ROUND 4 — GOOGLEYNESS & BEHAVIOURAL (30 min, English)

Setting: Two interviewers assessing culture, leadership, and comfort with ambiguity.

👨‍💻 Kai: Tell me about a time you had to work on something genuinely ambiguous — no clear requirements, no clear owner. What did you do?

👨 Khanh: About eighteen months ago, we had a recurring alert that fired every few weeks — always at different times, always for different reasons on the surface. It wasn't owned by any single team. Each time it fired, whoever was on-call investigated, patched the immediate issue, and moved on.

I noticed the pattern after the fourth occurrence and claimed it informally — not because I was asked to, but because I was tired of seeing it come back.

The ambiguity was real: there were no requirements for what "fixed" looked like, no stakeholder with a deadline, and no clear technical root cause since each surface symptom was different.

My approach was to start with observation, not solution. I spent three days reading through the postmortems for all four incidents, mapping what they had in common. The pattern that emerged was subtle: every incident happened when a specific upstream data pipeline was late by more than 20 minutes. Our service had no awareness of that latency — it assumed data freshness.

I wrote a two-page doc: here's what I observed, here's my hypothesis, here's the smallest test I can run to validate it. I shared it with three engineers in adjacent teams and asked if my hypothesis matched their mental model. Two of them added context I'd missed; one knew about an upstream config change six months ago that was probably the root cause.

We fixed it in a week. It hasn't recurred.

The lesson: ambiguous problems often look ambiguous because no single person has assembled all the pieces. The act of writing a hypothesis and sharing it is often enough to surface the missing context.

👩 Amy: How do you approach problems where you might not be the smartest person in the room?

👨 Khanh: Honestly — that's my preferred situation.

If I'm not the most knowledgeable person in the room, it means I'm in a position to learn something significant. I treat that as a feature, not a threat.

Practically, I try to contribute what I do have — which might be a different perspective, a clarifying question, or the ability to synthesise what experts are saying into a conclusion. Not every valuable contribution in a room of experts is another expert. Sometimes the most valuable person is the one who asks "Can you say that in one sentence?" or *"So what does that mean we should do?"*

I'm also careful not to perform competence I don't have. If I don't know something, I say so clearly — *"I'm not familiar with this area, but let me make sure I understand correctly..."* — rather than speaking with false confidence and wasting everyone's time.

The engineers I've learned the most from were genuinely comfortable saying "I don't know." That's what made them trustworthy.

👩 Amy: Last question. If you could redesign any part of how software teams work — not the technology, the process — what would you change?

👨 Khanh: I'd change how we do planning.

Most planning I've seen is an estimation exercise dressed up as a commitment exercise. Engineers estimate in points or days, those estimates get treated as promises, and then when reality doesn't match the estimate — which it almost never does — someone is accountable for the gap.

What I'd replace it with is: planning as risk surfacing. Instead of "how long will this take?" the primary question is *"what would make this take much longer than expected, and how confident are we about each of those unknowns?"* Then the plan becomes a list of unknowns with assigned owners and a date by which each unknown will be resolved.

This shifts the conversation from blame to learning. When something takes longer, it's because one of the unknowns turned out to be real — and we can discuss whether we should have known that sooner, rather than whether someone "failed to deliver."

I've done this informally in my own work for two years. It's not perfect, but it's more honest than point estimates, and it makes scope negotiations much easier because everyone sees the actual uncertainty.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Judgment + Googleyness 10/10 Failed interview as learning story; "being wrong is information"
Coding Sliding window log alert 10/10 Amortised O(1), memory-efficient, 4 tests, concurrency follow-up
System Design Pub/Sub at scale 10/10 Bigtable row key design, ack deadline, dead letter, backlog alert
Googleyness A Ambiguity — pattern across incidents 10/10 Observe first, write hypothesis, share for missing context
Googleyness B Not the smartest in the room 10/10 "I don't know" as trustworthy signal; contribute synthesis not performance
Googleyness C Process redesign 10/10 Planning as risk surfacing — unknowns with owners vs point estimates

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Google Interview Tips

  1. Clarify before designing — Google interviewers expect you to ask scope, consistency, and scale questions upfront
  2. Think out loud — Google values reasoning transparency over arriving at the answer silently
  3. Amortised complexity — know how to explain O(1) amortised for sliding window / deque operations
  4. Bigtable row key design — composite key (topicId#timestamp#msgId) enables efficient range scans
  5. Dead letter topic — Google Pub/Sub has this natively; shows domain knowledge to mention it
  6. Googleyness = intellectual humility + curiosity — "being wrong is information" is the right mindset
  7. Planning as risk surfacing — unique, thoughtful process answer that stands out from generic answers
  8. Google salaries (L5/Senior): USD 180–250K base + significant bonus + substantial RSU refreshes

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Failed interview "Not getting a role isn't failure if you leave knowing something you didn't know before."
Being challenged "What would have to be true for their point to be correct?"
Being wrong "Being wrong in public is only embarrassing if you treat it as a loss."
Ambiguity "Ambiguous problems look ambiguous because no single person has assembled all the pieces."
Not the expert "I don't perform competence I don't have."
Not the expert "Sometimes the most valuable person is the one who asks: Can you say that in one sentence?"
Planning "Planning as risk surfacing — not estimation dressed up as commitment."
Honesty "The engineers I've learned most from were genuinely comfortable saying I don't know."

🎭 Mock Interview #43 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Meta (Menlo Park / Remote, Social & Messaging Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Messaging Infrastructure team. Meta Messenger handles 100 billion messages per day across Messenger, WhatsApp, and Instagram DMs. Real-time delivery, message ordering, read receipts, and offline storage are the core engineering challenges. Tech: Java 17, Apache Thrift/gRPC, MySQL (MyRocks), HBase, Memcached, ZooKeeper, Kafka, internal Async framework. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. Note: Meta interviews focus heavily on concrete impact, data-driven decisions, and moving fast without breaking things. They want engineers who take ownership and make things happen.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Jessica (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Meta moves fast. Tell me about a time you had to make a significant decision quickly with limited information. What happened?

👨 Khanh: About a year ago, we had a production incident at peak traffic — our payment processing service was returning elevated error rates. Within the first five minutes I could see two possible causes in the logs: a database connection pool exhaustion, or a downstream API timing out and holding threads. I didn't have time to investigate fully — we were losing transactions every second.

I made a fast call: restart the service with a reduced connection pool ceiling and a shorter timeout on the external API. It wasn't the perfect fix — it was the reversible fix that had the highest chance of buying us time. Transactions recovered within two minutes.

The root cause turned out to be the external API — my second hypothesis. The connection pool change was unnecessary and I reverted it during the postmortem. But the service recovered, and I had time to do proper diagnosis after the bleeding stopped.

What I took from it: fast decisions under pressure should be reversible decisions. When you're losing money every second, "good enough and undoable" beats "perfect and slow" every time. The key is knowing the difference upfront.

👩 Jessica: Meta values ownership — engineers who see a problem and fix it without being told. How do you decide what to own that isn't assigned to you?

👨 Khanh: I use a simple filter: is this problem causing repeated pain, and does it have a clear owner?

If it's causing repeated pain and has no clear owner — that's the gap I try to step into. Not permanently, not heroically, but enough to either fix it or find the right owner and transfer it with context.

What I avoid is owning things performatively — picking up visible problems to look good rather than because I can actually help. That's a waste of everyone's time and mine. I prefer to own things that are invisible and annoying, because that's where the compound value is.

The honest discipline behind it: ownership without follow-through is worse than not owning it at all. So I only claim something if I have a realistic plan to actually move it forward.

👩 Jessica: Great mindset. Four technical rounds next. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL: MESSAGING SYSTEM (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Messaging Infrastructure.

PART A — Message Delivery & Ordering (20 min)

👨‍💻 Amir (Staff Eng): WhatsApp sends 100 billion messages per day. Messages in a conversation must be displayed in order. But users can be offline, switch devices, and reconnect. Design the message delivery and ordering system.

👨 Khanh: Three problems to solve: storage, ordering, and delivery to potentially offline recipients. Let me address each.

Ordering — monotonic sequence number per conversation:

// Each conversation has a Redis counter for message sequence
// Atomic increment ensures strict ordering even under concurrency

@Service
public class MessageSequencer {

    // "seq:{conversationId}" → current sequence number
    public long nextSequence(String conversationId) {
        return redisTemplate.opsForValue()
            .increment("seq:" + conversationId);
        // If Redis is unavailable, fall back to DB sequence:
        // conversationRepo.incrementAndGetSequence(conversationId)
    }
}

Storage — write path:

@Entity
public class Message {
    @Id     private String messageId;      // UUID
    private String conversationId;
    private String senderId;
    private long   sequenceNumber;         // monotonically increasing per conversation
    private String content;               // encrypted client-side
    private MessageStatus status;         // SENT | DELIVERED | READ
    private Instant sentAt;
    private Instant deliveredAt;
    private Instant readAt;
}

@Transactional
public MessageResult send(SendMessageRequest req) {
    long seq = sequencer.nextSequence(req.getConversationId());

    Message msg = Message.builder()
        .messageId(UUID.randomUUID().toString())
        .conversationId(req.getConversationId())
        .senderId(req.getSenderId())
        .sequenceNumber(seq)
        .content(req.getEncryptedContent())
        .status(SENT)
        .sentAt(Instant.now())
        .build();

    messageRepo.save(msg);  // durable write first

    // Attempt real-time delivery
    deliveryService.deliver(msg);

    return MessageResult.of(msg.getMessageId(), seq);
}

Delivery — online vs offline handling:

@Service
public class DeliveryService {

    public void deliver(Message msg) {
        List<String> recipients = conversationRepo
            .getParticipants(msg.getConversationId())
            .stream()
            .filter(uid -> !uid.equals(msg.getSenderId()))
            .toList();

        for (String recipientId : recipients) {
            boolean online = presenceService.isOnline(recipientId);

            if (online) {
                // Push via WebSocket connection
                boolean pushed = wsGateway.push(recipientId, msg);
                if (pushed) {
                    updateStatus(msg, recipientId, DELIVERED);
                    return;
                }
            }

            // Offline or push failed → queue for later delivery
            offlineQueue.enqueue(recipientId, msg.getMessageId());
        }
    }

    // Called when user reconnects
    public void deliverPending(String userId) {
        List<String> pending = offlineQueue.drain(userId);
        // Fetch messages in sequence order and push in bulk
        List<Message> messages = messageRepo
            .findByIdsOrderBySequence(pending);
        wsGateway.pushBatch(userId, messages);
        messages.forEach(m -> updateStatus(m, userId, DELIVERED));
    }
}

Read receipts — async, not blocking:

@KafkaListener(topics = "message.read-receipts")
public void onReadReceipt(ReadReceiptEvent event) {
    // Mark message READ in DB
    messageRepo.updateStatus(
        event.getMessageId(), event.getReaderId(), READ);
    // Notify sender via WebSocket
    wsGateway.push(event.getSenderId(),
        new ReadReceiptNotification(event.getMessageId()));
}

Why sequence numbers matter for ordering: Without server-assigned sequence numbers, clients sort by device clock — which can drift. Two messages sent within milliseconds on different devices could display in wrong order. Server sequence is the single source of ordering truth.

👨‍💻 Amir: What if two users send messages to the same conversation simultaneously? How does sequencing hold up?

👨 Khanh: Redis INCR is atomic — even two simultaneous calls will get strictly different values. One gets N, the other gets N+1. The order between them is determined by which INCR executes first, which is fair given they arrived simultaneously.

The subtle problem is: the user who got N+1 sees their message appear after the other person's even if they tapped send first from their perspective. This is inherent in any system with network latency — you can't have both "first tap wins" and "server assigns sequence." We accept server time as the tie-breaker.

If Redis fails, the fallback is a DB-level sequence using SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on the conversation row:

// In DB fallback:
@Transactional
public long incrementSequence(String convId) {
    Conversation conv = convRepo.findByIdWithLock(convId);
    long next = conv.getLastSequence() + 1;
    conv.setLastSequence(next);
    convRepo.save(conv);
    return next;
}

Slower, but correct. Redis is the fast path, DB is the reliable fallback.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Handling a Data Privacy Concern (15 min)

👨‍💻 Amir: A product manager comes to you and says: *"We want to add a feature that reads users' message content to power better ad targeting. Engineering needs to make it happen."* How do you respond?

👨 Khanh: I'd take a breath before responding, because this is a decision that has legal, ethical, and trust implications well beyond what either of us can decide in a conversation.

My response to the PM would be direct but not dismissive:

*"I want to engage with this seriously because it's a real business question. But I have significant concerns before we go further. WhatsApp and Messenger are end-to-end encrypted in many contexts — reading message content for ad targeting would require either breaking that encryption or fundamentally changing our privacy promise to users. Both of those are decisions that have to involve Legal, Privacy, and executive leadership — not just Engineering and Product."*

I'd then ask a clarifying question: *"Is this a directional idea you want feedback on, or is this a decision that's already been made above us?"*

If it's directional, I'd offer to help frame the tradeoffs in writing — what's technically possible, what the legal risk is, what user trust impact looks like — so leadership has an honest picture.

If it's already decided at a level above mine, I'd say: *"I'll implement whatever we're asked to implement once I understand the legal and ethical review that's happened. But I'd want that review documented before we start, because this is exactly the kind of decision that comes back to Engineering if something goes wrong."*

What I wouldn't do is either immediately say yes — because the blast radius is too large — or immediately refuse, because I'm not the decision-maker here. My job is to surface the tradeoffs clearly and make sure the right people are in the room.

PART C — CODING: Conversation Unread Count (20 min)

👨‍💻 Amir: *"Implement a service that tracks the unread message count per user per conversation. When a message is sent, increment the recipient's count. When they read, reset to zero. Must handle concurrent sends correctly."*

👨 Khanh:

@Service
public class UnreadCountService {

    // Redis key: "unread:{userId}:{conversationId}" → count
    private static final String KEY = "unread:%s:%s";

    // Called when a message is sent to a conversation
    public void onMessageSent(String conversationId,
                              String senderId,
                              List<String> participantIds) {
        for (String recipientId : participantIds) {
            if (recipientId.equals(senderId)) continue;

            String key = String.format(KEY, recipientId, conversationId);
            redisTemplate.opsForValue().increment(key);
            // No TTL — counts persist until explicitly reset
        }
    }

    // Called when user opens and reads the conversation
    public void onConversationRead(String userId,
                                   String conversationId) {
        String key = String.format(KEY, userId, conversationId);
        redisTemplate.delete(key); // reset to zero
    }

    // Get unread count for one conversation
    public long getUnreadCount(String userId, String conversationId) {
        String key = String.format(KEY, userId, conversationId);
        String val = redisTemplate.opsForValue().get(key);
        return val == null ? 0L : Long.parseLong(val);
    }

    // Get total unread across ALL conversations (badge count)
    // Uses Redis SCAN — don't use KEYS in production (blocking)
    public long getTotalUnread(String userId) {
        String pattern = String.format(KEY, userId, "*");
        long total = 0L;

        ScanOptions opts = ScanOptions.scanOptions()
            .match(pattern).count(200).build();

        try (Cursor<byte[]> cursor = redisTemplate
                .getConnectionFactory()
                .getConnection()
                .scan(opts)) {

            while (cursor.hasNext()) {
                String k = new String(cursor.next());
                String v = redisTemplate.opsForValue().get(k);
                if (v != null) total += Long.parseLong(v);
            }
        }
        return total;
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void incrementsForRecipient() {
    service.onMessageSent("conv1", "alice", List.of("alice", "bob"));
    assertThat(service.getUnreadCount("bob", "conv1")).isEqualTo(1L);
    assertThat(service.getUnreadCount("alice", "conv1")).isEqualTo(0L);
}

@Test
void resetsOnRead() {
    service.onMessageSent("conv1", "alice", List.of("alice", "bob"));
    service.onMessageSent("conv1", "alice", List.of("alice", "bob"));
    assertThat(service.getUnreadCount("bob", "conv1")).isEqualTo(2L);

    service.onConversationRead("bob", "conv1");
    assertThat(service.getUnreadCount("bob", "conv1")).isEqualTo(0L);
}

@Test
void concurrentSendsAreAccurate() throws InterruptedException {
    int threads = 20;
    ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(threads);
    CountDownLatch latch = new CountDownLatch(threads);

    for (int i = 0; i < threads; i++) {
        pool.submit(() -> {
            service.onMessageSent("conv2", "alice",
                List.of("alice", "bob"));
            latch.countDown();
        });
    }
    latch.await();
    assertThat(service.getUnreadCount("bob", "conv2"))
        .isEqualTo(20L); // Redis INCR is atomic
}

@Test
void totalUnreadSumsAcrossConversations() {
    service.onMessageSent("convA", "alice", List.of("alice", "bob"));
    service.onMessageSent("convA", "alice", List.of("alice", "bob"));
    service.onMessageSent("convB", "carol", List.of("carol", "bob"));

    assertThat(service.getTotalUnread("bob")).isEqualTo(3L);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — LEADERSHIP & IMPACT (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Senior Engineer.

👩 Rachel (EM): Meta expects engineers to have impact beyond their immediate team. Tell me about a time you made a measurable impact that you're genuinely proud of.

👨 Khanh: I'll give you one with a specific before-and-after.

Our team had a reconciliation job that ran nightly to catch mismatches between our transaction ledger and the bank's settlement files. It had been running for years and had a simple, known problem: it was O(n²) — it loaded all transactions into memory and compared every pair. At low volumes, fine. As the business grew, it started running for 6–7 hours and occasionally timing out, meaning the next day's reconciliation started before the previous one finished.

I wasn't assigned to fix it. I noticed it because I was on-call twice when the timeout alert fired. After the second time, I pulled the code, understood the problem, and estimated the fix would take three days.

I rewrote the algorithm to use sorted merge — both data sets sorted by transaction ID, then a single linear pass to identify mismatches. O(n log n) total instead of O(n²), and memory-safe because it streamed data in chunks.

Before: 6–7 hours, occasional timeout, 2 on-call pages per month. After: 22 minutes, zero timeouts, zero on-call pages in the eight months since.

The part I'm proud of isn't the algorithm — that's standard. It's that I saw a recurring problem, estimated the cost of fixing it versus living with it, and made the judgment call to fix it without being asked. The company got roughly 180 engineering-hours back per month from the on-call reduction alone.

👩 Rachel: How do you think about measuring your own impact?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, this is something I've had to be deliberate about because my instinct is to not measure it — to just do the work and assume people notice.

That instinct is wrong, and it's professionally self-defeating. Impact that isn't articulated is invisible, and invisible impact doesn't get you into rooms where bigger decisions are made.

So I now keep a simple running doc — what I built, what the measurable outcome was, and roughly when. Not for political reasons, but because six months later I genuinely can't remember the details, and I need those details for performance reviews, promotion discussions, and honestly just for my own sense of progress.

The discipline I try to apply: for every significant piece of work, I ask at the end — *"How is this system or this team better now than it was before I touched it, and can I express that in a number?"* Sometimes the answer is "I can't measure it well yet" — and that's okay, as long as I know when I'll be able to.

👨‍💻 Ben (Sr Eng): You've been on a team for two years. A new engineer joins who is technically strong but struggles with the team's communication norms — they go silent in design reviews, write very terse PR descriptions, and miss key Slack threads. How do you handle it?

👨 Khanh: I'd approach this early and privately, because waiting for it to become a pattern makes the conversation harder for everyone.

I'd start not with feedback but with curiosity. Something like: *"Hey, I've noticed you don't say much in design reviews — I want to make sure that's a preference and not because something about the dynamic isn't working for you."*

That question matters because the answer changes what I do. If they say *"I prefer to review async, I'll always send notes after"* — that's a style difference, not a problem, and I'd make sure the team knows to look for their async input. If they say *"Honestly, the reviews move so fast I can't get a word in"* — that's a team process issue, not a personal one, and I'd raise it at a retro.

On the PR descriptions — I'd share an example of a well-written one and explain what purpose it serves for the reviewer: *"This context helps me understand what you're trying to accomplish before I look at the diff, which makes my review faster and more useful to you."* Frame it as helping them, not correcting them.

On missed Slack threads — I'd ask if the notification setup is working for them, and share how I personally configure channel priorities. Tools issue, not a character issue.

The framing I try to maintain throughout: I'm not trying to change who they are, I'm trying to help them be effective in this specific environment. Those are very different things.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Fast decisions + ownership 10/10 Reversible-first decision under pressure; invisible ownership principle
Tech A Messaging: delivery + ordering 10/10 Sequence numbers, offline queue, read receipts async, DB fallback
Tech A+ Concurrent sequencing 10/10 Redis INCR atomicity, DB fallback with SELECT FOR UPDATE
Tech B Privacy scenario response 10/10 Direct but not dismissive; surfaces tradeoffs; escalates to right people
Tech C Coding: unread counts 10/10 Redis INCR, atomic reset, SCAN for totals, concurrency test
Leadership A Measurable impact story 10/10 O(n²) → O(n log n) with concrete before/after numbers
Leadership B Measuring own impact 10/10 Running doc habit; "how is this better, can I express it as a number?"
Leadership C Coaching communication norms 10/10 Curiosity first; style vs problem distinction; framing as helping

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Meta Interview Tips

  1. Concrete impact numbers — Meta wants before/after metrics. "It got faster" is weak. "6h → 22min" is strong
  2. Ownership without assignment — describe problems you fixed that nobody asked you to fix
  3. Privacy is a first-class concern — never immediately say yes to a feature that has privacy implications
  4. Redis INCR for counters — atomic increment handles concurrent message sends safely
  5. Message ordering — server-assigned sequence number, not client clock; Redis INCR is the fast path
  6. Offline delivery queue — drain on reconnect in sequence order; don't assume online delivery
  7. Reversible decisions under pressure — fast calls should be undoable calls; fix the bleeding, then investigate
  8. Meta salaries (E5/Senior): USD 180–280K base + bonus + RSU; total comp often USD 400K+ with equity

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Fast decision "Good enough and undoable beats perfect and slow when you're losing money every second."
Ownership "I prefer to own things that are invisible and annoying — that's where the compound value is."
Privacy "This is exactly the kind of decision that comes back to Engineering if something goes wrong."
Privacy "I'll implement what we're asked once I understand the legal and ethical review that's happened."
Impact "How is this system better now than before I touched it, and can I express that as a number?"
Coaching "I'm not trying to change who they are — I'm helping them be effective in this environment."
Visibility "Impact that isn't articulated is invisible. Invisible impact doesn't get you into bigger rooms."
Running doc "Six months later I genuinely can't remember the details — and I need those details."

🎭 Mock Interview #44 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Amazon / AWS (Seattle / Remote, Cloud & E-Commerce)

Role: Senior Software Development Engineer — AWS DynamoDB team. DynamoDB handles 10+ trillion requests per year, serving as the backbone for Amazon.com, Prime, and thousands of AWS customers. Tech: Java 17, gRPC, internal distributed systems frameworks, DynamoDB internals, Kinesis, S3, Lambda. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. ⚠️ Amazon-specific: Every round includes Leadership Principle (LP) questions. Amazon interviewers score LP answers separately from technical answers. Each LP story must be STAR format and data-driven. Having 8–10 unique LP stories prepared is essential.

📞 ROUND 1 — PHONE SCREEN (30 min, English)

Setting: Senior SDE screens for LP fit and basic technical depth.

👨‍💻 Mark (Sr SDE): Hi Khanh! Amazon has 16 Leadership Principles. Let's start with Customer Obsession. Tell me about a time you went significantly beyond what was asked to deliver a better outcome for a customer — internal or external.

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Customer Obsession)

Situation: I was building a batch reconciliation feature for our payment operations team — an internal customer. The requirement was simple: generate a daily CSV report of mismatched transactions. I built it, it worked, ticket closed.

Task: But when I shared it with the operations team for UAT, I noticed something. They had to download the CSV, open Excel, apply three manual filters, and cross-reference with another tool before they could act on anything. The report technically met the spec. But the actual workflow had five manual steps we'd never discussed.

Action: I wasn't asked to do anything more. But I spent one extra day adding: a server-side filter API so they could query by mismatch type and date range directly, a summary row at the top of the CSV with total counts and total VND in dispute, and an optional email delivery so they didn't have to log in to download it each morning.

I presented the additions to my manager as a fast-follow and got them approved. Total extra effort: one day.

Result: The operations team's daily reconciliation workflow went from 40 minutes to 8 minutes. They later told me it was the most useful internal tool shipped that quarter. And they trusted our team enough to bring us into the design of their next tool — which became a much bigger project. One extra day of effort opened a year-long relationship.

👨‍💻 Mark: Strong. Now a quick technical one to calibrate. *"DynamoDB stores items in partitions. If 80% of your reads hit the same partition key, what happens and how do you fix it?"*

👨 Khanh: That's a hot partition problem — one of the most common DynamoDB anti-patterns.

What happens: DynamoDB allocates read/write capacity uniformly across partitions. If 80% of traffic hits one partition, that partition gets throttled while others sit idle. You see ProvisionedThroughputExceededException even though your overall table capacity looks fine.

Three mitigation strategies, in order of preference:

1 — Redesign the partition key (best long-term): The root cause is almost always a poor key choice. If you're partitioning by countryCode and 80% of users are from Vietnam, that's the problem. Add a shard suffix: instead of VN, use VN_0 through VN_9, and select the shard by userId % 10. Reads need to fan out across 10 shards, but writes distribute evenly. 

public String shardedKey(String country, String userId) {
    int shard = Math.abs(userId.hashCode() % 10);
    return country + "_" + shard;
}

// Read: query all 10 shards in parallel, merge results
public List<Item> readAll(String country) {
    return IntStream.range(0, 10).parallel()
        .mapToObj(i -> dynamodb.query(country + "_" + i))
        .flatMap(Collection::stream)
        .toList();
}

2 — DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator) for hot-read items: A fully managed in-memory cache in front of DynamoDB. Microsecond reads for the hottest items. No code change to the read path — just change the endpoint.

3 — Write sharding with read aggregation: For counters or leaderboards, write to userId_0..userId_N, aggregate on read. Same pattern as #1 but specifically for high-write scenarios.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Principal Engineer + Staff Engineer panel.

PART A — Distributed Key-Value Store Design (25 min)

👨‍💻 Priya (Principal Eng): Let's go deeper on distributed storage. Design a simplified key-value store — like DynamoDB — that supports: put(key, value), get(key), eventual consistency, and horizontal scaling. Walk through your design.

👨 Khanh: I'll clarify one thing first: eventual consistency implies we accept that reads might return stale data for a brief window after a write. That's the tradeoff I'll design around.

Core architecture — consistent hashing for distribution:

[Client]
    ↓
[Coordinator Node] — receives all reads/writes
    ↓ hashes key → determines responsible nodes
[Replica Nodes] — N=3 replicas per key (configurable)
    ↓
[Storage Engine] — per-node LSM tree (like LevelDB)

Consistent hashing — assigning keys to nodes:

public class ConsistentHashRing {

    private final TreeMap<Long, String> ring = new TreeMap<>();
    private final int virtualNodes; // e.g., 150 per physical node

    public void addNode(String nodeId) {
        for (int i = 0; i < virtualNodes; i++) {
            long hash = hash(nodeId + "#" + i);
            ring.put(hash, nodeId);
        }
    }

    // Returns N distinct nodes responsible for a key
    public List<String> getNodes(String key, int replicationFactor) {
        long keyHash = hash(key);
        Set<String> nodes = new LinkedHashSet<>();

        // Walk clockwise from keyHash position
        NavigableMap<Long, String> tail = ring.tailMap(keyHash);
        for (String node : Iterables.concat(
                tail.values(), ring.values())) {
            nodes.add(node);
            if (nodes.size() == replicationFactor) break;
        }
        return new ArrayList<>(nodes);
    }

    private long hash(String input) {
        return Hashing.murmur3_128().hashString(
            input, UTF_8).asLong();
    }
}

Write path — quorum writes (W=2 of N=3):

@Service
public class KvCoordinator {

    private static final int N = 3; // replicas
    private static final int W = 2; // write quorum
    private static final int R = 2; // read quorum

    public void put(String key, String value) {
        List<String> nodes = hashRing.getNodes(key, N);
        String versionedValue = vectorClock.stamp(value);

        // Send to all N replicas in parallel
        List<CompletableFuture<Boolean>> writes = nodes.stream()
            .map(node -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(
                () -> replicaClient.put(node, key, versionedValue)))
            .toList();

        // Wait for W acknowledgements
        long successes = writes.stream()
            .map(f -> f.orTimeout(500, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS))
            .filter(f -> {
                try { return f.get(); }
                catch (Exception e) { return false; }
            })
            .count();

        if (successes < W) {
            throw new WriteQuorumException(
                "Only " + successes + "/" + W + " replicas acked");
        }
        // W-1 lagging replicas will catch up via anti-entropy
    }

    // Read path — quorum reads (R=2 of N=3)
    public String get(String key) {
        List<String> nodes = hashRing.getNodes(key, N);

        List<CompletableFuture<VersionedValue>> reads = nodes.stream()
            .map(node -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(
                () -> replicaClient.get(node, key)))
            .toList();

        // Collect R responses, return highest vector clock version
        return reads.stream()
            .map(f -> {
                try { return f.orTimeout(300, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS).get(); }
                catch (Exception e) { return null; }
            })
            .filter(Objects::nonNull)
            .limit(R)
            .max(Comparator.comparing(VersionedValue::clockVersion))
            .map(VersionedValue::value)
            .orElseThrow(() -> new ReadQuorumException("Not enough replicas responded"));
    }
}

Anti-entropy — background repair for lagging replicas:

@Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
public void antiEntropy() {
    // Merkle tree comparison between replica pairs
    // Identifies divergent key ranges, syncs them
    // This is how eventual consistency "eventual" resolves
    merkleRepairService.syncAll();
}

Why W + R > N for strong consistency?

With N=3, W=2, R=2: any read quorum of 2 must overlap with any write quorum of 2 by at least 1 node. That node has the latest write. So even eventual consistency can be tuned toward strong consistency by raising W and R.

PART B — LP ROUND: Dive Deep + Disagree & Commit (20 min)

👨‍💻 Priya: Dive Deep LP. Tell me about a time you discovered a problem that nobody else on your team had noticed. How did you find it and what did you do?

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Dive Deep)

Situation: About 18 months ago, our team had a Java microservice that was experiencing periodic GC pauses — roughly every 6 hours, latency spiked for 3–4 seconds. The on-call team had been restarting the service every time it happened. Nobody had investigated the root cause because "restart fixes it" had become the accepted response.

Task: I wasn't on-call during most of these incidents, but I noticed the pattern in our metrics dashboard during a routine review. The interval was too regular — exactly every 5.5 to 6.5 hours — which suggested a periodic accumulation, not random load.

Action: I added heap profiling with async-profiler during the next expected window and let it run for two hours before the pause. What I found: a static Map being used as a cache with no eviction policy. Every API request added an entry. After ~6 hours of traffic the map hit 2GB, triggered a full GC, and paused the world.

The fix was one afternoon: replace the unbounded map with a Caffeine cache with a 10,000 entry limit and 1-hour TTL. Zero GC pauses in the 4 months since.

Result: Before: restart every 6 hours, 3–4 second user impact each time, 2 on-call engineer-hours per week. After: zero restarts, zero pauses, zero pages.

What struck me was how long this had been accepted as normal. The culture of "restart fixes it" had made an invisible problem invisible. Diving into the metrics with fresh eyes surfaced it in two hours.

👨‍💻 Priya: Disagree & Commit LP. Tell me about a time you strongly disagreed with a decision, but committed to it anyway. What was the outcome?

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Disagree & Commit)

Situation: My team decided to migrate our primary database from MySQL to a NoSQL solution to handle growing write volume. I had serious reservations — our data model had complex relational integrity requirements and our team had limited NoSQL operational experience. I believed the migration risk outweighed the scaling benefit, at least at our current volume.

Task: I had one opportunity to influence the decision before it was finalised: the architecture review meeting.

Action: I came prepared with a written doc — not a rebuttal, but a structured risk analysis. I presented: the specific relational constraints I was concerned about, the estimated migration effort, an alternative PostgreSQL sharding approach with projected capacity headroom, and the key question I wanted us to answer before deciding — "At what write volume does NoSQL become unavoidable?"

The team listened. The tech lead acknowledged two of my three concerns. But the decision held — the organisation had strategic reasons for the migration beyond our team's scope that I hadn't been fully briefed on.

How I committed: I said explicitly in the meeting: *"I still have reservations about the migration timeline and our NoSQL operational readiness. I want those concerns on record. And I am fully committed to making this succeed."*

Then I volunteered to lead the migration runbook and own the rollback plan — because if I believed the risk was real, I should be the one managing it, not hoping someone else would.

Result: The migration completed successfully. My rollback plan was used once during a staging rehearsal — which validated the concern about operational readiness — but not in production. The tech lead later said my concern about the timeline was correct, and we should have taken an extra three weeks. That conversation opened the door to more influence in future architectural decisions.

PART C — CODING: LRU Cache (15 min)

👨‍💻 Priya: *"Implement an LRU (Least Recently Used) cache with get and put in O(1) time. Thread-safe."*

👨 Khanh: Classic — doubly linked list + HashMap.

public class LruCache<K, V> {

    private final int capacity;

    // Node in doubly-linked list
    private static class Node<K, V> {
        K key; V value;
        Node<K, V> prev, next;
        Node(K k, V v) { this.key = k; this.value = v; }
    }

    // Sentinel head (MRU end) and tail (LRU end)
    private final Node<K, V> head = new Node<>(null, null);
    private final Node<K, V> tail = new Node<>(null, null);

    private final Map<K, Node<K, V>> map = new HashMap<>();

    private final ReadWriteLock lock = new ReentrantReadWriteLock();

    public LruCache(int capacity) {
        this.capacity = capacity;
        head.next = tail;
        tail.prev = head;
    }

    public V get(K key) {
        lock.readLock().lock();
        try {
            Node<K, V> node = map.get(key);
            if (node == null) return null;
            // Promote to MRU under write lock
            lock.readLock().unlock();
            lock.writeLock().lock();
            try {
                moveToFront(node);
                return node.value;
            } finally {
                lock.readLock().lock();
                lock.writeLock().unlock();
            }
        } finally {
            lock.readLock().unlock();
        }
    }

    public void put(K key, V value) {
        lock.writeLock().lock();
        try {
            Node<K, V> existing = map.get(key);
            if (existing != null) {
                existing.value = value;
                moveToFront(existing);
                return;
            }

            Node<K, V> node = new Node<>(key, value);
            map.put(key, node);
            addToFront(node);

            if (map.size() > capacity) {
                Node<K, V> lru = removeTail();
                map.remove(lru.key);
            }
        } finally {
            lock.writeLock().unlock();
        }
    }

    private void addToFront(Node<K, V> node) {
        node.next = head.next;
        node.prev = head;
        head.next.prev = node;
        head.next = node;
    }

    private void moveToFront(Node<K, V> node) {
        removeNode(node);
        addToFront(node);
    }

    private void removeNode(Node<K, V> node) {
        node.prev.next = node.next;
        node.next.prev = node.prev;
    }

    private Node<K, V> removeTail() {
        Node<K, V> lru = tail.prev;
        removeNode(lru);
        return lru;
    }

    public int size() {
        lock.readLock().lock();
        try { return map.size(); }
        finally { lock.readLock().unlock(); }
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void returnsNullForMissingKey() {
    var cache = new LruCache<String, Integer>(3);
    assertThat(cache.get("missing")).isNull();
}

@Test
void evictsLeastRecentlyUsed() {
    var cache = new LruCache<String, Integer>(2);
    cache.put("a", 1);
    cache.put("b", 2);
    cache.put("c", 3); // evicts "a" (LRU)
    assertThat(cache.get("a")).isNull();
    assertThat(cache.get("b")).isEqualTo(2);
    assertThat(cache.get("c")).isEqualTo(3);
}

@Test
void accessPromotesToMru() {
    var cache = new LruCache<String, Integer>(2);
    cache.put("a", 1);
    cache.put("b", 2);
    cache.get("a"); // promote "a"
    cache.put("c", 3); // should evict "b", not "a"
    assertThat(cache.get("a")).isEqualTo(1);
    assertThat(cache.get("b")).isNull();
}

@Test
void updatesExistingKey() {
    var cache = new LruCache<String, Integer>(2);
    cache.put("a", 1);
    cache.put("a", 99); // update
    assertThat(cache.get("a")).isEqualTo(99);
    assertThat(cache.size()).isEqualTo(1);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — BAR RAISER (40 min, English)

Setting: Bar Raiser — a senior engineer from a different team who has veto power regardless of team preference. Their job is to evaluate against the full SDE bar, not fit.

👨‍💻 Omar (Bar Raiser): Earn Trust LP. Tell me about a time you made a mistake that affected others. What did you do?

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Earn Trust)

Situation: About two years ago, I merged a configuration change to our production Kafka consumer — adjusting the max.poll.records setting — during a low-traffic window on a Friday afternoon. I'd tested it in staging and believed the change was safe.

What went wrong: I hadn't accounted for the interaction between max.poll.records and our session timeout config. With the new setting, our consumers were polling more records per batch than they could process within the session timeout window. Kafka started rebalancing consumers every few minutes. The result: message processing throughput dropped by 60% for 3 hours before I was paged. Several downstream services backed up significantly.

Task: Fix the immediate problem and make sure the team understood what happened and why.

Action: I reverted the config change immediately — 5 minutes from being paged to recovery. Then I wrote a thorough postmortem that evening, even though it wasn't a required process for configuration changes at the time.

The postmortem was honest about two things I got wrong: the technical gap — I hadn't read the Kafka documentation carefully enough about the interaction between these two settings — and the process gap — I had merged and deployed without asking anyone to review the change, even informally.

I sent the postmortem to the whole team, including my manager, the next morning with a message that said: *"I made this mistake. Here's exactly what happened, here's why I didn't catch it, and here are the two changes I'm making to my own process to ensure it doesn't happen again."*

Result: No one on the team was surprised or angry — the postmortem had already named everything. What they were was appreciative. Two engineers told me privately that they'd made similar config changes without review and were going to start doing peer review too.

My manager later cited the postmortem as an example of how to handle mistakes on the team. The trust I built by being direct about it was worth more than the trust I would have lost by hiding it.

What I changed: I now always get a second pair of eyes on any config change that touches consumer or producer settings, even in staging. Thirty minutes of review has saved us from several near-misses since.

👨‍💻 Omar: Think Big LP. Most engineers optimise within the existing system. Have you ever proposed something that fundamentally changed the direction of a project?

👨 Khanh: (STAR — Think Big)

Situation: My team was planning a major feature: a rule engine that operations staff could configure to auto-flag suspicious transactions. The plan was a traditional if-then rule builder — a UI, a rule DSL, a rule evaluation engine. The project was scoped at four months.

My observation: I looked at how the operations team actually worked. They didn't think in rules — they thought in examples. They'd say: *"Flag transactions like this one"* and point to a specific suspicious transaction. They weren't going to write rules in a DSL; they'd tried something similar before and abandoned it.

What I proposed: Instead of a rule engine, build a similarity-based flagging system. Operations staff mark known-suspicious transactions. The system finds new transactions that are similar — same amount range, same merchant category, same device fingerprint cluster — and flags them automatically. No rules to write. The UI is: "Mark this as suspicious" or "This is fine."

This was a fundamentally different direction. It reduced the engineering scope (no DSL, no rule UI), but required us to build a lightweight feature-similarity pipeline — something the team had never done.

Action: I wrote a two-page proposal with a prototype I'd built in two days using a simple cosine similarity approach over a feature vector. I presented it to the PM and tech lead, showed it finding real suspicious patterns in synthetic data, and quantified the comparison: *"Rule engine: 4 months, requires operations training, adoption risk. Similarity approach: 6 weeks, zero learning curve, self-improving as they mark more transactions."*

Result: The PM and tech lead adopted the proposal. We built it in 7 weeks. Operations adoption was immediate — no training required. It caught 34 suspicious transaction patterns in the first month that the old manual process had missed. The project became a reference case for how to scope features by starting from user behaviour rather than the engineering solution.

👨‍💻 Omar: Last question. Why Amazon specifically — and why DynamoDB?

👨 Khanh: Amazon because of the scale and the standards.

AWS infrastructure underpins a significant fraction of the internet. When DynamoDB has a bad day, thousands of applications have a bad day. That level of responsibility requires engineering discipline that I find genuinely motivating — not stressful, motivating. You can't get away with weak failure handling or vague monitoring at that scale. The bar is set externally by the stakes, not internally by culture alone.

DynamoDB specifically because it's a system I've used extensively as a consumer and have deep respect for. The design decisions — eventual consistency as a first-class choice, partition-key-based sharding, the consistency model tradeoffs — are things I've thought about from the outside. I want to understand them from the inside.

And the Leadership Principles honestly resonate with me — particularly Customer Obsession and Dive Deep. Those aren't cultural slogans I'm saying because they're on the website. They describe habits I already have. This role would let me practise them at a scale I haven't had access to before.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Phone Screen Customer Obsession LP 10/10 40 min → 8 min ops workflow; trust opened a year-long relationship
Phone Screen Hot partition problem 10/10 Sharded key design + DAX + write aggregation — all three options
Tech A KV store design 10/10 Consistent hashing, quorum W+R>N, anti-entropy repair, vector clocks
LP — Dive Deep GC pause root cause 10/10 Profiling → unbounded map → Caffeine fix; before/after metrics
LP — Disagree & Commit NoSQL migration 10/10 Written risk analysis, named concerns publicly, owned rollback plan
Coding LRU Cache O(1) 10/10 DLL + HashMap, ReadWriteLock, 4 tests
LP — Earn Trust Kafka config mistake 10/10 Immediate revert, honest postmortem, personal process change
LP — Think Big Similarity engine proposal 10/10 Rethought feature from user behaviour; 4 months → 7 weeks

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Amazon / AWS Interview Tips

  1. Prepare 8–10 LP stories — each unique, each with data. You'll need them across 5 rounds
  2. STAR is mandatory — Situation, Task, Action, Result. Interviewers take notes on each section
  3. "I" not "We" — Amazon wants YOUR specific contribution. "We built..." tells them nothing
  4. Hot partition = sharded key — add _0 to _N suffix, fan-out reads in parallel
  5. LRU cache is a classic Amazon coding question — O(1) get/put with DLL + HashMap
  6. Disagree & Commit — must show genuine disagreement, written escalation, then full commitment
  7. Think Big — don't describe an optimisation; describe a directional change with evidence
  8. Amazon/AWS salaries (SDE II/SDE III): USD 150–220K base; significant RSU (vest over 4 years, back-heavy)

📝 Leadership Principle Quick Reference

LP One-line signal
Customer Obsession "I went beyond the spec because the workflow wasn't right for the customer."
Dive Deep "I noticed the pattern no one else had. I profiled, not guessed."
Disagree & Commit "I raised it in writing, named my concern publicly, then committed fully."
Earn Trust "I wrote the postmortem the same evening, sent it to the whole team."
Think Big "I proposed a different direction, not an optimisation of the existing one."
Deliver Results "Before: 6 hours. After: 22 minutes. Zero pages since."

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Fast decision "Good enough and undoable beats perfect and slow when you're losing transactions every second."
Ownership "I only claim something if I have a realistic plan to move it forward."
Postmortem "I made this mistake. Here's exactly what happened, and here's what I'm changing."
Think Big "I started from user behaviour, not from the engineering solution."
Why Amazon "The bar is set externally by the stakes, not internally by culture alone."
LPs genuine "Those aren't cultural slogans I'm saying because they're on the website."

🎭 Mock Interview #45 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Revolut (London / Remote-first, Global Neobank)

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Core Banking team. Revolut serves 45M+ customers across 35+ countries, offering multi-currency accounts, instant transfers, stock trading, and crypto. It processes 500M+ transactions per year. Revolut is known for an intense, high-ownership engineering culture — move fast, break nothing, own your service end to end. Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, gRPC, internal ledger framework. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. What makes Revolut interviews distinct: They test real production thinking — messy problems, incomplete specs, and a strong preference for engineers who ask "what could go wrong?" before "what should I build?"

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER / VALUES SCREEN (20 min, English)

👩 Elena (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Revolut moves very fast — sometimes too fast, honestly. How do you maintain quality when the pace around you is high?

👨 Khanh: That's a question I've had to think about practically, not theoretically — because I've worked in environments where "move fast" became a reason to skip things that shouldn't be skipped.

My answer is: I protect a small set of non-negotiables and stay flexible on everything else.

The non-negotiables for me are: error paths must be handled explicitly — not swallowed or logged and forgotten; any data mutation that can fail halfway needs a recovery story; and anything going to production needs at least one test that would have caught the last real bug in that component.

Everything else — naming conventions, architecture elegance, refactoring opportunities — I'll defer, document, and pick up when the pace allows.

The discipline is knowing which category something falls into. When I'm moving fast, I still ask: *"If this fails in the worst possible way, what happens to the user's money or data?"* If the answer is alarming, I slow down. If it's recoverable and observable, I ship it.

👩 Elena: Revolut engineers own their services fully — deployment, monitoring, on-call. No handoffs. Does that appeal to you or concern you?

👨 Khanh: It appeals to me, but I want to be honest about why — because I think the appeal can be naive if you haven't actually done it.

Full ownership is motivating because the feedback loop is direct. When something I built breaks at 2am, I'm the one who fixes it — which means I'm also the one most motivated to make sure it doesn't break at 2am. That alignment of incentives produces better software than any process I've seen.

The concern — and it's real — is that full ownership without adequate tooling is just full exhaustion. The question I'd want to answer in the first month is: what does the on-call experience actually look like? How mature is the observability stack? Can I diagnose an issue in minutes from dashboards, or do I need to SSH into servers and read raw logs?

If the tooling is good, full ownership is one of the best engineering environments you can be in. If it isn't, it's burnout with extra steps. I've learned to ask that question early.

👩 Elena: Very self-aware. Moving to three technical rounds. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Core Banking.

PART A — Multi-Currency Ledger (25 min)

👨‍💻 Ivan (Staff Eng): Revolut's core product is a multi-currency wallet. A user can hold GBP, EUR, USD, and VND simultaneously. They can transfer between currencies instantly. Design the ledger for this.

👨 Khanh: Before I design, one clarification: when a user converts GBP to EUR, do they get a live FX rate locked at the moment of the transfer, or do they select a rate and confirm separately? This affects whether the FX conversion is part of the same atomic ledger transaction.

Assuming: rate is locked at transfer time, single atomic operation.

Core principle — double-entry bookkeeping:

Every money movement has two sides. A debit from one account must equal a credit to another. The ledger never creates or destroys money — it only moves it.

@Entity
public class LedgerEntry {
    @Id     private String entryId;
    private String accountId;       // which account (user+currency)
    private String transactionId;   // groups debit + credit entries
    private EntryType type;         // DEBIT | CREDIT
    private BigDecimal amount;      // always positive
    private String currency;        // ISO 4217: GBP, EUR, USD, VND
    private String description;
    private Instant createdAt;
}

// Each user has one account per currency
@Entity
public class Account {
    @Id     private String accountId;    // userId + currency
    private String userId;
    private String currency;
    private BigDecimal balance;          // current balance
    private BigDecimal reservedBalance;  // held for pending ops
}

Atomic transfer — same currency (e.g., GBP → GBP):

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
public TransferResult transfer(TransferRequest req) {
    // Lock both accounts in consistent order to prevent deadlock
    String fromId = accountId(req.getSenderId(), req.getCurrency());
    String toId   = accountId(req.getRecipientId(), req.getCurrency());

    // Always lock lower ID first
    List<String> lockOrder = Stream.of(fromId, toId)
        .sorted().toList();

    Account a1 = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock(lockOrder.get(0));
    Account a2 = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock(lockOrder.get(1));

    Account from = a1.getId().equals(fromId) ? a1 : a2;
    Account to   = a1.getId().equals(toId)   ? a1 : a2;

    if (from.getBalance().compareTo(req.getAmount()) < 0) {
        throw new InsufficientFundsException(from.getId());
    }

    String txnId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

    // Debit sender
    from.setBalance(from.getBalance().subtract(req.getAmount()));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.debit(txnId, from, req.getAmount()));

    // Credit recipient
    to.setBalance(to.getBalance().add(req.getAmount()));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.credit(txnId, to, req.getAmount()));

    accountRepo.saveAll(List.of(from, to));

    return TransferResult.success(txnId, req.getAmount(), req.getCurrency());
}

Cross-currency transfer (GBP → EUR):

@Transactional(isolation = Isolation.SERIALIZABLE)
public TransferResult crossCurrencyTransfer(CrossCurrencyRequest req) {
    // 1. Lock FX rate (provided by FX service at request time)
    BigDecimal rate       = req.getLockedRate();       // e.g., 1.18
    BigDecimal eurAmount  = req.getGbpAmount().multiply(rate)
        .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);

    String txnId = UUID.randomUUID().toString();

    // 2. Debit GBP from sender
    Account gbpAccount = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock(
        accountId(req.getSenderId(), "GBP"));
    if (gbpAccount.getBalance().compareTo(req.getGbpAmount()) < 0) {
        throw new InsufficientFundsException(gbpAccount.getId());
    }
    gbpAccount.setBalance(gbpAccount.getBalance()
        .subtract(req.getGbpAmount()));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.debit(txnId, gbpAccount,
        req.getGbpAmount(), "GBP→EUR FX debit"));

    // 3. Credit EUR to recipient
    Account eurAccount = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock(
        accountId(req.getRecipientId(), "EUR"));
    eurAccount.setBalance(eurAccount.getBalance().add(eurAmount));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.credit(txnId, eurAccount,
        eurAmount, "GBP→EUR FX credit"));

    // 4. Record FX spread to Revolut's revenue account
    BigDecimal spread   = req.getGbpAmount()
        .multiply(req.getSpreadRate()); // e.g., 0.005 = 0.5%
    Account    fxRevenue = accountRepo.findByIdWithLock("revolut_fx_revenue_GBP");
    fxRevenue.setBalance(fxRevenue.getBalance().add(spread));
    ledgerRepo.save(LedgerEntry.credit(txnId, fxRevenue, spread, "FX spread"));

    accountRepo.saveAll(List.of(gbpAccount, eurAccount, fxRevenue));

    return TransferResult.success(txnId, eurAmount, "EUR");
}

Why SERIALIZABLE isolation?

Lower isolation levels allow phantom reads — two concurrent transfers from the same account could both pass the balance check before either deducts, causing the balance to go negative. Serializable prevents this at the DB level. For very high-volume accounts, we'd layer Redis pre-checks on top to avoid hitting the DB for obviously insufficient-funds cases.

👨‍💻 Ivan: What happens if the transaction commits the GBP debit but then crashes before writing the EUR credit?

👨 Khanh: That's the partial write problem — the most dangerous failure mode in a ledger.

With SERIALIZABLE and a single DB, this can't happen — the entire transaction either commits or rolls back atomically. If the server crashes mid-transaction, PostgreSQL rolls back on restart.

The harder case is cross-service or cross-database:

If GBP and EUR accounts live in different database shards — which they might at Revolut's scale — then a single DB transaction doesn't span both. Here I'd use the outbox + saga pattern:

Phase 1: Debit GBP → write debit entry + "pending EUR credit" outbox event
         → commit to GBP shard
Phase 2: Outbox relay publishes event to Kafka
Phase 3: EUR shard consumer processes → writes credit entry
         → publishes "EUR credit confirmed" event
Phase 4: GBP shard marks the transaction COMPLETED

On failure between phases:
  → If Phase 1 committed but Phase 3 never completes →
    compensation job detects pending outbox events older
    than 5 minutes → alerts ops or auto-reverses the GBP debit

Invariant we maintain: every txnId must have exactly one DEBIT and one CREDIT entry at the end. A sweep job runs every minute checking for orphaned single-entry transactions and flags them for manual review.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining a Compliance Risk (15 min)

👨‍💻 Ivan: A PM wants to ship a new peer-to-peer transfer feature by end of the month. You realise it doesn't have any AML (Anti-Money Laundering) transaction monitoring. How do you handle this conversation?

👨 Khanh: I want to handle this carefully because the natural instinct — just saying "we can't ship without AML" — often triggers a negotiation I shouldn't be having.

AML monitoring isn't an engineering best practice we're trading off against speed. It's a legal requirement in every jurisdiction Revolut operates in. Shipping a P2P transfer feature without it isn't a calculated risk — it's a regulatory violation.

So my first move is to understand whether this is a knowledge gap or a deliberate decision. I'd go to the PM privately and say: *"Before we finalise the timeline, I want to make sure compliance has signed off on the AML monitoring plan for this feature. Have we looped them in?"*

If the answer is no — they didn't know it was required — that's a knowledge gap I can help close. I'd offer to set up a 30-minute call with the PM, the compliance team, and our engineering lead to align on what's needed and whether there's a phased approach: for example, launch with basic rule-based transaction screening and add ML-based monitoring in a follow-up sprint.

If the answer is "we know, but we're going to ship anyway and handle it later" — that's a different conversation. I'd be direct: *"I can't support shipping this without AML monitoring in place. That's not a quality bar I'm setting — it's a legal requirement. I need us to escalate this to our Head of Compliance before any timeline is confirmed."*

What I wouldn't do is write a ticket, add a comment, and let it become someone else's problem. If this ships incorrectly and Revolut gets fined, "I flagged it in Jira" is not an adequate defence — for the company or for me professionally.

PART C — CODING: Balance Snapshot (20 min)

👨‍💻 Ivan: *"Given a list of ledger entries (debits and credits) in no particular order, implement a function that returns the current balance per currency for a given user. Handle multiple currencies. Efficient and clean."*

👨 Khanh:

public record LedgerEntry(
    String userId,
    String currency,     // "GBP", "EUR", "USD"
    EntryType type,      // DEBIT | CREDIT
    BigDecimal amount    // always positive
) {}

public enum EntryType { DEBIT, CREDIT }

public class BalanceCalculator {

    /**
     * Returns current balance per currency for a user.
     * Balance = sum(CREDIT) - sum(DEBIT) per currency.
     * Result may be negative — caller decides if that's valid.
     */
    public Map<String, BigDecimal> calculateBalances(
            String userId,
            List<LedgerEntry> entries) {

        return entries.stream()
            .filter(e -> e.userId().equals(userId))
            .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(
                LedgerEntry::currency,
                Collectors.reducing(
                    BigDecimal.ZERO,
                    e -> e.type() == EntryType.CREDIT
                        ? e.amount()
                        : e.amount().negate(),
                    BigDecimal::add
                )
            ));
    }

    /**
     * Balance at a specific point in time — same as above
     * but entries pre-filtered by timestamp externally.
     * (Clean separation of concerns — this function doesn't
     *  know about time; the caller filters the entry list.)
     */
    public BigDecimal balanceForCurrency(
            String userId,
            String currency,
            List<LedgerEntry> entries) {

        return calculateBalances(userId, entries)
            .getOrDefault(currency, BigDecimal.ZERO);
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void singleCurrencyBalance() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    var entries = List.of(
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("100.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", DEBIT,  new BigDecimal("30.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("50.00"))
    );
    assertThat(calc.balanceForCurrency("u1", "GBP", entries))
        .isEqualByComparingTo("120.00");
}

@Test
void multiCurrencyBalance() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    var entries = List.of(
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("100.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "EUR", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("200.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "EUR", DEBIT,  new BigDecimal("50.00"))
    );
    var balances = calc.calculateBalances("u1", entries);
    assertThat(balances.get("GBP")).isEqualByComparingTo("100.00");
    assertThat(balances.get("EUR")).isEqualByComparingTo("150.00");
}

@Test
void ignoresOtherUsers() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    var entries = List.of(
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "USD", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("500.00")),
        new LedgerEntry("u2", "USD", CREDIT, new BigDecimal("999.00"))
    );
    assertThat(calc.balanceForCurrency("u1", "USD", entries))
        .isEqualByComparingTo("500.00");
}

@Test
void returnsZeroForUnknownCurrency() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    assertThat(calc.balanceForCurrency("u1", "JPY", List.of()))
        .isEqualByComparingTo("0.00");
}

@Test
void handlesNegativeBalance() {
    var calc = new BalanceCalculator();
    var entries = List.of(
        new LedgerEntry("u1", "GBP", DEBIT, new BigDecimal("200.00"))
        // No credit — negative balance possible during ledger repair
    );
    assertThat(calc.balanceForCurrency("u1", "GBP", entries))
        .isEqualByComparingTo("-200.00");
}

💬 ROUND 3 — ENGINEERING CULTURE & JUDGMENT (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Senior Engineer.

👩 Anya (EM): Revolut has had public incidents in the past — briefly blocking transactions due to a fraud system bug in 2018. If you were on the team that shipped that bug, what would you want the culture to look like in the aftermath?

👨 Khanh: I'd want three things from the culture — and they have to exist together, because each one is hollow without the others.

First: a fast, honest postmortem with no blame. Within 24 hours. Every step of the failure timeline documented with facts, not narratives. The goal of that document is to identify the system condition that made the bug easy to ship — not to find who wrote the line of code. If the culture punishes the person, people start hiding problems instead of surfacing them. That's how small incidents become large ones.

Second: engineering changes, not promises. The postmortem must produce specific, assignable action items with owners and due dates. Not "we will be more careful." That's meaningless. Specific: *"We will add integration tests that simulate this fraud rule input. Owner: X. Due: two weeks."* I've seen postmortems that were beautifully written and led to zero change. That's worse than no postmortem.

Third: executive visibility without witch-hunting. Leadership needs to know what happened — that's legitimate. But the communication to leadership should come from the team, in writing, and should focus on the systemic fix, not on individual accountability. If leadership uses the incident as an opportunity to find someone to blame, you will lose your best engineers, because the best engineers take risks. Risk-taking and blameless culture have to coexist.

Revolut's 2018 incident — from what's publicly known — was handled reasonably well. They communicated quickly to users, restored service fast, and were transparent. That's the bar. The internal culture in the aftermath determines whether the lesson sticks or the same incident happens again in 18 months.

👩 Anya: Revolut moves into new markets quickly — a new country every few weeks at peak expansion. Engineers need to build features that work globally by default. How do you think about internationalisation in backend engineering?

👨 Khanh: Internationalisation in backend is mostly about resisting assumptions — and the assumptions are sneaky because they feel like facts.

The ones I've been burned by personally, and now check explicitly:

Currency and arithmetic: never use double or float for money. Always BigDecimal, always explicit rounding mode, always store amounts as integers in the smallest unit (cents, pence, đồng) and convert for display only.

Time zones: UTC everywhere in the system — storage, logs, Kafka messages, API payloads. Convert to local time only at the presentation layer. One timezone assumption buried in a scheduled job caused a reconciliation bug for our Vietnamese customers that took two days to find.

Phone numbers: E.164 format from day one. Never store phone numbers as int or without country code. I've seen a system that stored Vietnamese numbers without the country code break the moment they added international SMS.

Names and addresses: don't assume first name / last name order, don't assume single-line addresses, don't assume ASCII characters. Vietnam has Nguyễn Thị Thanh — accented characters, family name first. A form that strips diacritics or reverses name order breaks KYC for an entire country.

Regulatory per-market: transaction limits, KYC tiers, and reporting thresholds differ by country. These shouldn't be hardcoded — they should be configuration, ideally per-market config in a database that compliance can update without a code deploy.

The discipline I try to install in the team: before shipping any feature, ask — *"What assumption in this code is specific to one country?"* If you can find one, you've found your next internationalisation bug.

👨‍💻 Dmitri (Sr Eng): Last one. You discover during a code review that a colleague has implemented a financial calculation using double arithmetic. There's a subtle precision bug that won't show up in normal usage but will cause rounding errors on large amounts. How do you handle the review?

👨 Khanh: This is a case where being direct in the review comment is both kind and necessary.

My comment would be specific and educational, not just corrective:

*"This calculation uses double, which has binary floating-point precision issues. For example, 0.1 + 0.2 in Java returns 0.30000000000000004, not 0.3. For financial calculations, this causes rounding errors that accumulate over large volumes or large amounts. Please replace with BigDecimal and specify an explicit RoundingMode — HALF_UP is standard for financial rounding. I can share a reference or pair on this if helpful."*

I flag it as a blocking comment — not because I want to be difficult, but because a precision bug in a financial calculation is a correctness issue, not a style issue. It will cause real money to be wrong. That's the line.

What I wouldn't do is fix it myself without telling them — that removes their learning opportunity. And I wouldn't be vague about why it matters — "use BigDecimal instead of double" without explanation is dismissive. I want them to understand the reason so they make the right choice next time without needing the review to catch it.

If this is a pattern — double arithmetic appearing in multiple PRs from multiple engineers — I'd raise it in a team session and add a BigDecimal standard to our code review checklist. One-on-one feedback for one PR; systemic change for a systemic pattern.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Quality under speed + ownership 10/10 Non-negotiable filter; tooling question before accepting on-call
Tech A Multi-currency ledger + double-entry 10/10 Lock ordering for deadlock prevention, SERIALIZABLE, FX spread entry
Tech A+ Crash between debit/credit 10/10 Outbox + saga for cross-shard; orphan sweep job
Tech B AML compliance conversation 10/10 Knowledge gap vs wilful non-compliance — handled differently
Tech C Coding: balance from ledger entries 10/10 Stream + groupingBy + reducing, 5 edge-case tests
Culture A Post-incident culture 10/10 Postmortem + engineering changes + exec visibility without witch-hunt
