Culture B Internationalisation 10/10 BigDecimal, UTC, E.164, diacritics, per-market config — specific and practical
Culture C Blocking code review 10/10 Educational comment, blocking flag, systemic vs individual response

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Revolut Interview Tips

  1. Double-entry bookkeeping — every transfer has a debit and a credit; total must always balance
  2. BigDecimal not double — the most important financial rule; bring it up proactively
  3. Lock ordering to prevent deadlock — always lock accounts in sorted ID order in DB transactions
  4. SERIALIZABLE isolation — required for concurrent balance checks; know the tradeoff (performance)
  5. AML = legal, not optional — Revolut operates under FCA, NBB, and other regulators; this is binary
  6. UTC everywhere — convert to local time only in the presentation layer
  7. E.164 for phone numbers — Revolut is in 35+ countries; phone number assumptions break at border
  8. Revolut salaries (Senior/Staff): GBP 90–140K base + competitive equity; remote-first since 2020

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Quality vs speed "I protect a small set of non-negotiables and stay flexible on everything else."
On-call ownership "Full ownership without adequate tooling is just full exhaustion."
AML concern "AML monitoring isn't a best practice we trade off against speed — it's a legal requirement."
Compliance "'I flagged it in Jira' is not an adequate defence — for the company or for me professionally."
Post-incident "If leadership uses the incident to find someone to blame, you will lose your best engineers."
i18n "Before shipping any feature, ask: what assumption in this code is specific to one country?"
Code review "A precision bug in a financial calculation is a correctness issue, not a style issue."
Pattern vs instance "One-on-one feedback for one PR. Systemic change for a systemic pattern."

🎭 Mock Interview #46 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: LinkedIn (Sunnyvale / Remote, Professional Network)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Feed & Notifications team. LinkedIn has 1 billion members. The feed processes billions of events daily — posts, likes, comments, job changes, endorsements. Feed ranking, notification fan-out, and relevance at scale are the core engineering challenges. Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, Kafka, Venice (internal feature store), Espresso (distributed NoSQL), MySQL, Voldemort, Pinot (OLAP). Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. What makes LinkedIn interviews distinct: Strong emphasis on large-scale distributed systems, data pipelines, and — uniquely — how engineering decisions affect member experience and professional trust. Culture is collaborative and values engineers who balance speed with member safety.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Priya (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! LinkedIn's mission is to create economic opportunity for every member of the global workforce. How does engineering connect to that mission for you personally?

👨 Khanh: It connects more directly than most products.

LinkedIn is one of the few platforms where the quality of the engineering directly affects someone's ability to find a job, grow their career, or connect with the right person at the right time. A job recommendation algorithm that surfaces irrelevant roles wastes a candidate's time. A notification system that's too noisy trains people to ignore it — so the one notification that matters gets missed.

That's a different kind of engineering responsibility than making a feed slightly more engaging. It's about whether the product actually fulfills its purpose for the people who need it most. I find that motivating in a way that's hard to manufacture — it's either there or it isn't.

👩 Priya: Well said. One culture question: LinkedIn values "members first." Have you ever pushed back on a business or product requirement because you believed it wasn't in users' best interest? What happened?

👨 Khanh: Yes — and I want to give you the honest version, not the sanitised one.

A product manager wanted to increase the frequency of notification emails to drive engagement metrics. The proposed change would triple the number of emails a user received per week. The metrics rationale was reasonable — open rates on the first email of the week were high, so "more emails = more engagement."

I raised a concern: open rate on email one is high because it's the first. Email three of the week is where users hit unsubscribe. We'd be optimising for a short-term metric that would erode long-term trust.

I didn't just say no. I proposed an alternative: personalised notification frequency based on observed behaviour — users who open daily get more; users who open weekly get fewer. Same engineering effort, aligned incentives.

The PM was initially resistant because it was more complex than "just increase the frequency." I wrote a short doc with the expected churn curve based on benchmark data from similar products, and shared it. That moved the conversation.

We shipped the personalised approach. Unsubscribe rates dropped 18%. Engagement among high-intent users improved. The metric the PM cared about ended up being better, not worse. But it took me being willing to be temporarily unpopular to get there.

👩 Priya: Perfect. Technical rounds are next. Good luck!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Feed & Notifications.

PART A — Notification Fan-Out at Scale (25 min)

👨‍💻 Wei (Staff Eng): When someone with 500,000 followers posts on LinkedIn, all 500,000 followers potentially need a notification. We call this the "celebrity problem." Design the notification fan-out system.

👨 Khanh: The celebrity problem is a classic fan-out challenge. Let me start with a clarifying question: do all 500,000 followers need real-time notification, or is near-real-time (within a few minutes) acceptable for most users? And does notification relevance filter reduce the actual fan-out — e.g., only followers who have engaged with this person recently get notified?

*Assuming: near-real-time acceptable, relevance filter reduces effective fan-out to ~20% of followers.*

Two fan-out strategies — push vs pull:

Push fan-out (write to each follower on post): Pre-compute and store a notification for each follower. Fast reads. But 500K writes per celebrity post is expensive.

Pull fan-out (read following list on load): No pre-computation. On feed load, merge posts from all followed accounts. Scales read side cheaply. But complex at read time for large follow graphs.

Hybrid (LinkedIn's actual approach):

  • Normal users (< 5K followers): push fan-out
  • Celebrities (> 5K followers): pull fan-out at read time
  • Notification specifically: push to an async queue,

batch and filter before delivery

@Service
public class NotificationFanOutService {

    private static final int CELEBRITY_THRESHOLD = 5_000;

    public void fanOut(Post post) {
        int followerCount = socialGraphService
            .getFollowerCount(post.getAuthorId());

        if (followerCount <= CELEBRITY_THRESHOLD) {
            pushFanOut(post);    // write to each follower's queue
        } else {
            deferredFanOut(post); // log event; pull at read time
        }
    }

    private void pushFanOut(Post post) {
        // Paginate followers to avoid memory explosion
        int pageSize = 500;
        int page     = 0;
        List<String> followers;

        do {
            followers = socialGraphService.getFollowers(
                post.getAuthorId(), page++, pageSize);

            List<NotificationTask> tasks = followers.stream()
                .filter(uid -> relevanceFilter.shouldNotify(
                    uid, post)) // engagement-based filter
                .map(uid -> NotificationTask.of(uid, post))
                .toList();

            // Bulk publish to Kafka — consumers handle delivery
            kafkaTemplate.send("notifications.pending",
                new BulkNotificationEvent(tasks));

        } while (followers.size() == pageSize);
    }

    private void deferredFanOut(Post post) {
        // Just record the post event — no pre-fan-out
        // Feed service pulls celebrity posts at read time
        kafkaTemplate.send("posts.celebrity",
            post.getAuthorId(), new CelebrityPostEvent(post));
    }
}

Notification delivery pipeline (Kafka consumers):

@KafkaListener(topics = "notifications.pending",
               groupId = "notification-delivery",
               concurrency = "20")
public void deliver(BulkNotificationEvent event) {
    for (NotificationTask task : event.getTasks()) {
        // Check user's notification preferences
        NotificationPreferences prefs = prefsCache.get(task.getUserId());
        if (!prefs.isEnabled(task.getType())) continue;

        // Batch: in-app notification (immediate)
        inAppStore.upsert(task.getUserId(), task.toInAppNotification());

        // Email: only if user hasn't received one in last 24h for this type
        if (prefs.isEmailEnabled() &&
                !recentEmailTracker.sentRecently(task.getUserId(),
                    task.getType(), Duration.ofHours(24))) {
            emailQueue.enqueue(task.toEmailNotification());
        }
    }
}

Deduplication: If the same event triggers multiple notifications (e.g., 5 people like your post within a minute), batch into one digest notification rather than sending 5. Controlled by a 5-minute dedup window per (userId, notificationType, targetId) in Redis.

👨‍💻 Wei: The relevance filter decides whether to notify a follower. How would you design that filter without making it a black-box ML model that nobody can explain?

👨 Khanh: I'd design it as a transparent rule engine with observable inputs and explainable outputs — not because ML is wrong, but because trust requires explainability for something that affects whether members see important career content.

Tiered rule engine:

public class RelevanceFilter {

    public boolean shouldNotify(String followerId, Post post) {
        double score = computeRelevanceScore(followerId, post);
        // Threshold tuned by experimentation (e.g., 0.3)
        return score >= NOTIFY_THRESHOLD;
    }

    private double computeRelevanceScore(String uid, Post post) {
        // Each signal is observable and logged separately
        double engagementSignal = recentEngagement(uid,
            post.getAuthorId()); // 0.0–1.0: did they interact recently?
        double recencySignal    = recencyScore(post.getAuthorId(),
            uid);               // 0.0–1.0: how fresh is their connection?
        double topicSignal      = topicOverlap(uid,
            post.getTopics());  // 0.0–1.0: do their interests match?
        double activitySignal   = memberActivity(uid); // are they active?

        // Weighted sum — weights are tunable config, not hardcoded
        return weights.engagement() * engagementSignal
             + weights.recency()    * recencySignal
             + weights.topic()      * topicSignal
             + weights.activity()   * activitySignal;
    }
}

Why this approach over a pure ML model:

  • Every notification decision can be audited: *"User X

wasn't notified because their engagement score was 0.1 and topic overlap was 0.0."*

  • Product and trust teams can tune weights without an

ML deployment.

  • A/B testing is clean — swap weights, measure outcomes.
  • If a member complains "why didn't I see this?" — we

can answer specifically.

ML can be layered on top later to learn the optimal weights. But the structure remains explainable.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Presenting a System Design Decision (15 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: Imagine you've designed the notification fan-out system and you need to present it to a group of four stakeholders: a product manager, a data scientist, a mobile engineer, and a senior backend engineer. How do you structure that presentation?

👨 Khanh: Four different people with four different contexts. A presentation that works for one will lose another. So I structure it in layers.

Layer 1 — the shared opening (2 minutes): "Here's the problem we're solving and why it matters." Everyone needs this, regardless of role. I frame it in member terms: *"When someone with 500K followers posts, how do we make sure the right followers see it promptly without crashing our systems?"* That's relatable to everyone in the room.

Layer 2 — the key decision and rationale (5 minutes): The hybrid push/pull model. I explain this in plain language — no code, no jargon. For the PM: what the user experience looks like. For the data scientist: where the relevance filter plugs in. For the mobile engineer: what the notification payload looks like and when it arrives. For the backend engineer: the high-level architecture diagram.

This is the core. Everyone needs to understand the decision even if they don't understand every implementation detail.

Layer 3 — the deep dives (optional, per audience): After the shared layer, I ask: *"Does anyone want to go deeper on a specific area?"* The backend engineer will want to talk about Kafka partition design and failure modes. The data scientist will want to talk about the relevance filter signals. The mobile engineer will want to talk about the push notification contract. The PM probably doesn't need either.

What I never do: Start with the technical architecture diagram and expect everyone to follow. That's presenting to the room you wish you had, not the room you're in.

PART C — CODING: Feed Merge (20 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: *"Given N sorted streams of posts (sorted by timestamp, newest first), merge them into a single sorted stream. This is the celebrity pull fan-out read path — merging posts from multiple followed accounts. Handle it efficiently."*

👨 Khanh: Classic K-way merge — priority queue, O(N log K) where K is the number of streams.

public record Post(String postId, String authorId, Instant publishedAt,
                   String content) {}

public class FeedMerger {

    /**
     * Merges K sorted (newest-first) post streams into one sorted stream.
     * Uses a max-heap on publishedAt — newest post wins each poll.
     * Time: O(N log K), Space: O(K) where K = number of streams.
     */
    public List<Post> merge(List<Iterator<Post>> streams, int limit) {
        // Max-heap: compare by publishedAt descending (newest first)
        PriorityQueue<HeapEntry> heap = new PriorityQueue<>(
            Comparator.comparing((HeapEntry e) -> e.post().publishedAt())
                      .reversed()
        );

        // Seed the heap with the first post from each stream
        for (int i = 0; i < streams.size(); i++) {
            Iterator<Post> stream = streams.get(i);
            if (stream.hasNext()) {
                heap.offer(new HeapEntry(stream.next(), stream, i));
            }
        }

        List<Post> result = new ArrayList<>();

        while (!heap.isEmpty() && result.size() < limit) {
            HeapEntry entry = heap.poll();
            result.add(entry.post());

            // Advance that stream and push its next post into the heap
            if (entry.stream().hasNext()) {
                heap.offer(new HeapEntry(
                    entry.stream().next(), entry.stream(), entry.streamIndex()));
            }
        }

        return result;
    }

    private record HeapEntry(Post post, Iterator<Post> stream, int streamIndex) {}
}

// Tests
@Test
void mergesTwoStreams() {
    var merger = new FeedMerger();
    Instant now = Instant.now();

    List<Post> streamA = List.of(
        new Post("a1", "userA", now.minusSeconds(1), "A newest"),
        new Post("a2", "userA", now.minusSeconds(10), "A older")
    );
    List<Post> streamB = List.of(
        new Post("b1", "userB", now.minusSeconds(5), "B middle"),
        new Post("b2", "userB", now.minusSeconds(20), "B oldest")
    );

    var result = merger.merge(
        List.of(streamA.iterator(), streamB.iterator()), 10);

    assertThat(result).extracting(Post::postId)
        .containsExactly("a1", "b1", "a2", "b2");
}

@Test
void respectsLimit() {
    var merger = new FeedMerger();
    Instant now = Instant.now();

    List<Post> stream = IntStream.range(0, 100)
        .mapToObj(i -> new Post("p" + i, "u1",
            now.minusSeconds(i), "post " + i))
        .toList();

    var result = merger.merge(List.of(stream.iterator()), 10);
    assertThat(result).hasSize(10);
}

@Test
void handlesEmptyStreams() {
    var merger = new FeedMerger();
    Instant now = Instant.now();

    List<Post> nonEmpty = List.of(
        new Post("p1", "u1", now, "only post"));

    var result = merger.merge(
        List.of(Collections.<Post>emptyList().iterator(),
                nonEmpty.iterator()), 10);

    assertThat(result).extracting(Post::postId).containsExactly("p1");
}

@Test
void handlesAllEmptyStreams() {
    var merger = new FeedMerger();
    var result = merger.merge(
        List.of(Collections.<Post>emptyList().iterator()), 10);
    assertThat(result).isEmpty();
}

💬 ROUND 3 — LEADERSHIP & MEMBER TRUST (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Staff Engineer.

👩 Lisa (EM): LinkedIn is unusual in that a bad engineering decision can affect someone's career. Has that kind of responsibility ever made you slow down and think differently about a technical choice?

👨 Khanh: Yes — and there's a specific instance I think about.

I was working on a job recommendation feature. We were optimising the ranking model for click-through rate — a standard engagement metric. During a review, a data scientist on the team pointed out something I wouldn't have thought to check: our model was systematically ranking senior roles lower for profiles with career gaps, and lower-paying roles higher for certain geographic regions.

The model wasn't wrong by its own metric. It was learning patterns in historical data that reflected real-world bias. But "accurate to historical patterns" and "fair" are not the same thing. A model that learns that people with career gaps don't get senior roles will ensure that people with career gaps don't get senior roles.

We paused the rollout. That was an uncomfortable decision — we had a timeline and the click-through numbers were good. But shipping a model that systematically disadvantaged certain groups of job seekers wasn't a risk I could rationalise as an acceptable tradeoff.

We spent three weeks auditing the training data for demographic proxies, added fairness constraints to the model objective, and retrained. The final model had slightly lower click-through but significantly better fairness across career gap and geography segments.

The lesson I carry from it: when you're building something that affects people's economic opportunities, the responsibility check isn't "does the metric look good?" It's "who does this work for, and who does it not work for?"

👩 Lisa: How do you handle it when your engineering team is moving fast and the quality of collaboration starts to drop — people skip reviews, documentation gets sparse, on-call gets noisy?

👨 Khanh: I've been in that situation, and the instinct I've learned to resist is addressing it with more process.

The temptation is: people are skipping reviews, so mandate reviews. Documentation is sparse, so require documentation. On-call is noisy, so add an alert review meeting.

That almost never works. Engineers skip reviews because they're overloaded or because the reviews aren't adding value. Adding a rule that says "you must review" doesn't change the underlying condition. It just adds friction and resentment.

What I do instead is start with a conversation — not a retrospective where everyone has to say something, but one-on-one conversations with three or four people to understand what's actually happening. Is it overload? Is it unclear expectations? Is it that reviews are slow and blocking their work?

Then I address the underlying thing. If it's overload, the question is: can we reduce scope? If it's reviews being slow, can we establish a 24-hour SLA? If it's on-call noise, can we dedicate one sprint to silence the top five flapping alerts?

The principle is: treat process problems the same way you treat technical problems — find the root cause, don't just add more monitoring to the symptom.

👨‍💻 Ryan (Staff Eng): Last question. LinkedIn has been around for 20+ years and has significant technical debt in its core systems. How do you stay motivated working on old systems that need modernisation, rather than greenfield projects?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, I find old systems more interesting than greenfield, not less.

A greenfield project starts with a blank slate and a set of assumptions. An old system tells you what actually happened — which assumptions held up and which didn't, where the original design hit limits, what the real usage patterns turned out to be. That's information you can't manufacture.

When I work on an old system, I try to understand it before I change it. Not superficially — I mean reading the original design docs if they exist, talking to engineers who built it if they're still around, reading the incident history to understand what has broken and why. That context makes every improvement more targeted and less likely to introduce new problems.

The other thing that motivates me is the compounding value. Improving a system that processes billions of requests per day by 10% is worth more than building a new system that processes a million. The impact is a function of scale, not novelty.

I also think greenfield envy — the feeling that the interesting work is always the new thing — is a sign of underestimating the complexity of what exists. The LinkedIn feed has been running for 20 years under production load. Understanding it well enough to change it safely is genuinely hard. I find hard things interesting.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Mission alignment + members first 10/10 Job recommendations affecting career; pushed back on notification frequency
Tech A Notification fan-out + celebrity problem 10/10 Hybrid push/pull, relevance filter, dedup window, paginated fan-out
Tech A+ Explainable relevance filter 10/10 Weighted rule engine with observable inputs; ML-ready but auditable
Tech B Multi-audience presentation 10/10 Layered structure: shared opening → key decision → optional deep dives
Tech C Coding: K-way merge 10/10 Max-heap, O(N log K), 4 edge-case tests
Culture A Fairness in job recommendation 10/10 Paused rollout; fairness constraint added; "accurate ≠ fair" framing
Culture B Collaboration quality drop 10/10 Find root cause, not add more process; one-on-one before retrospective
Culture C Working on legacy systems 10/10 Old systems reveal what actually happened; compounding scale value

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 LinkedIn Interview Tips

  1. Celebrity problem = hybrid push/pull — push for normal users, pull fan-out for high-follower accounts
  2. Notification deduplication — dedup window per (userId, notificationType, targetId) in Redis
  3. Explainability matters at LinkedIn — member trust requires auditable, not black-box, ranking decisions
  4. K-way merge = priority queue — O(N log K); know it cold for feed, search result, timeline merging
  5. Fairness is a first-class engineering concern — LinkedIn interview will test if you think about who a model disadvantages
  6. Multi-audience presentations — layer: shared context → key decision → optional deep dives per role
  7. Legacy > greenfield — LinkedIn values engineers who can modernise complex existing systems
  8. LinkedIn salaries (Senior/Staff, SWE): USD 160–230K base + RSU; total comp USD 250K–400K+

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Mission "Whether the product actually fulfills its purpose for the people who need it most."
Pushback "It took me being willing to be temporarily unpopular to get there."
Fairness "'Accurate to historical patterns' and 'fair' are not the same thing."
Fairness "Who does this work for — and who does it not work for?"
Process "Treat process problems the same way you treat technical problems — find the root cause."
Presentation "Presenting to the room you wish you had, not the room you're in."
Legacy "Old systems tell you what actually happened — which assumptions held and which didn't."
Scale "Improving a system that processes billions of requests by 10% is worth more than building a new one."

🎭 Mock Interview #47 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Coinbase (San Francisco / Remote, Crypto Exchange)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Trading & Order Management team. Coinbase is the largest US crypto exchange, processing billions of dollars in trades daily across 250+ crypto assets. Engineers face challenges around order book management, blockchain confirmation latency, wallet security, and regulatory compliance in 100+ countries. Tech: Java 17, gRPC, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, Go (some services), internal blockchain integration layer. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. What makes Coinbase interviews distinct: They test deep understanding of financial systems with crypto-specific nuance — blockchain finality, wallet custody, trade settlement. Communication emphasis is on clarity under complexity and explaining highly technical concepts to non-technical audiences.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Rachel (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Crypto is a polarising space — some engineers love it, some are sceptical. Where do you stand, and why does that bring you to Coinbase?

👨 Khanh: Honest answer: I'm interested in the engineering problems more than the asset class itself.

Crypto creates a genuinely novel class of technical challenges that don't exist in traditional finance. Blockchain confirmation latency — where a transaction is "pending" for minutes or hours before it's final — creates state management problems that have no clean precedent in bank-to-bank transfers. Wallet custody at scale, where losing a private key means losing money permanently with no recourse, sets a different reliability bar than any system I've worked on before.

I also think financial inclusion is a real use case — sending money across borders cheaply and quickly matters to hundreds of millions of people, and crypto infrastructure is part of that story whether or not you believe in any particular token's long-term value.

What brings me to Coinbase specifically: you operate in highly regulated markets, which means you're building crypto infrastructure with the same compliance rigour as traditional finance. That's a harder problem and a more interesting one than unregulated exchanges.

👩 Rachel: Coinbase has had some well-publicised incidents — outages during market spikes, briefly incorrect prices. How do you think about building reliable systems in a domain where the asset price can move 30% in an hour?

👨 Khanh: Price volatility and system reliability are actually correlated in the worst possible way — the moments when your system is under the most load are exactly the moments when it must be most reliable, because that's when your users need it most urgently.

The design principle that follows from this: never let traffic volume determine your error rate. A system that works at 1,000 requests per second but falls over at 10,000 isn't reliable — it's just lightly loaded. Load shedding, circuit breakers, and graceful degradation need to be designed in, not bolted on when the first spike hits.

The second principle: during a market spike, users can tolerate slightly stale prices or a 500ms order submission latency. What they cannot tolerate is submitting an order and not knowing whether it was accepted. The worst UX in a volatile market is uncertainty. So your first reliability investment should be in clear, accurate order status communication — even if the underlying system is under stress.

👩 Rachel: Perfect framing. Three technical rounds next. Good luck, Khanh!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Trading Platform.

PART A — Order Book & Trade Matching (25 min)

👨‍💻 Sanjay (Staff Eng): An order book holds buy orders (bids) and sell orders (asks) for a trading pair like BTC/USD. When a new order arrives, it tries to match against existing orders at a suitable price. Design the data structure and matching engine.

👨 Khanh: Let me clarify the order types first — this shapes the matching logic significantly.

*Assuming: limit orders (price specified) and market orders (fill at best available price). No stop orders for now.*

Order book data structure:

// Bid side: sorted by price DESC (highest bid gets matched first)
// Ask side: sorted by price ASC  (lowest ask gets matched first)
// Within same price: FIFO by arrival time (time priority)

public class OrderBook {

    // Key: price, Value: deque of orders at that price (FIFO)
    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> bids =
        new TreeMap<>(Comparator.reverseOrder()); // highest first

    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> asks =
        new TreeMap<>(); // lowest first

    private final Map<String, Order> ordersById = new HashMap<>();

    public void addOrder(Order order) {
        ordersById.put(order.getId(), order);
        TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> side =
            order.getSide() == Side.BUY ? bids : asks;
        side.computeIfAbsent(order.getPrice(),
            p -> new ArrayDeque<>()).addLast(order);
    }

    public BigDecimal bestBid() {
        return bids.isEmpty() ? null : bids.firstKey();
    }

    public BigDecimal bestAsk() {
        return asks.isEmpty() ? null : asks.firstKey();
    }

    public void removeOrder(String orderId) {
        Order order = ordersById.remove(orderId);
        if (order == null) return;
        TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> side =
            order.getSide() == Side.BUY ? bids : asks;
        Deque<Order> q = side.get(order.getPrice());
        if (q != null) {
            q.remove(order);
            if (q.isEmpty()) side.remove(order.getPrice());
        }
    }
}

Matching engine — price-time priority:

@Service
public class MatchingEngine {

    @Transactional
    public List<Trade> match(Order incoming) {
        List<Trade> trades = new ArrayList<>();
        TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> opposingSide =
            incoming.getSide() == Side.BUY
                ? orderBook.getAsks()
                : orderBook.getBids();

        BigDecimal remaining = incoming.getQuantity();

        while (remaining.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) > 0
                && !opposingSide.isEmpty()) {

            BigDecimal bestPrice = opposingSide.firstKey();

            // Check price compatibility
            boolean priceMatches = incoming.getSide() == Side.BUY
                ? incoming.getPrice().compareTo(bestPrice) >= 0  // bid >= ask
                : incoming.getPrice().compareTo(bestPrice) <= 0; // ask <= bid

            // Market orders always match (no price check)
            if (!incoming.isMarketOrder() && !priceMatches) break;

            Deque<Order> ordersAtPrice = opposingSide.get(bestPrice);
            Order resting = ordersAtPrice.peekFirst();

            BigDecimal fillQty = remaining.min(resting.getRemainingQty());
            BigDecimal fillPrice = resting.getPrice(); // resting order's price

            // Record trade
            trades.add(Trade.of(incoming.getId(), resting.getId(),
                fillQty, fillPrice));

            // Update quantities
            remaining = remaining.subtract(fillQty);
            resting.fill(fillQty);

            if (resting.isFullyFilled()) {
                ordersAtPrice.pollFirst();
                if (ordersAtPrice.isEmpty()) opposingSide.remove(bestPrice);
            }
        }

        // Any unfilled remainder: add to book (limit) or reject (market)
        if (remaining.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) > 0) {
            if (incoming.isMarketOrder()) {
                incoming.setStatus(OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED_CANCELLED);
            } else {
                incoming.setRemainingQty(remaining);
                orderBook.addOrder(incoming);
            }
        } else {
            incoming.setStatus(OrderStatus.FULLY_FILLED);
        }

        return trades;
    }
}

Persistence after matching:

// Every match produces an immutable audit trail
// Never update trades — only append
@Entity
public class Trade {
    @Id     private String tradeId;
    private String makerOrderId;  // resting order
    private String takerOrderId;  // incoming order
    private BigDecimal quantity;
    private BigDecimal price;
    private String tradingPair;   // "BTC/USD"
    private Instant executedAt;
}

Why TreeMap for order book? O(log n) for insert, remove, and find-best-price. The FIFO deque at each price level gives time priority within the same price — ensuring fairness.

👨‍💻 Sanjay: When a user buys Bitcoin, they expect to receive it in their wallet. But Bitcoin confirmation takes 10–60 minutes on-chain. How do you design the settlement flow?

👨 Khanh: This is where crypto differs fundamentally from traditional equity trading.

In equities, settlement is T+2 — two business days after the trade. The latency is known and contractual. In crypto, blockchain confirmation is probabilistic — you have confirmations (each adding security) but no guaranteed finality time.

Two-phase settlement model:

Phase 1 — Ledger Credit (immediate, internal):
  Trade executes → user's internal Coinbase ledger
  is credited instantly. User can see their balance,
  can trade that BTC immediately within Coinbase.
  This is "custodial" balance — Coinbase holds the keys.

Phase 2 — On-chain Settlement (async, blockchain-dependent):
  If user requests external withdrawal, we broadcast
  a blockchain transaction. We wait for N confirmations
  (N=3 for BTC by default) before marking withdrawal final.
@Service
public class WithdrawalSettlementService {

    // Polling job: checks confirmation count every 30 seconds
    @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 30_000)
    public void checkPendingWithdrawals() {
        List<Withdrawal> pending = withdrawalRepo
            .findByStatus(WithdrawalStatus.BROADCAST);

        for (Withdrawal w : pending) {
            try {
                int confirmations = blockchainClient
                    .getConfirmations(w.getTxHash(), w.getNetwork());

                int required = confirmationPolicy
                    .requiredFor(w.getNetwork(), w.getAmountUsd());
                // Larger amounts require more confirmations for security

                if (confirmations >= required) {
                    w.setStatus(WithdrawalStatus.CONFIRMED);
                    notifyUser(w, "Your withdrawal of "
                        + w.getAmount() + " BTC is confirmed.");
                } else {
                    log.debug("Withdrawal {} has {}/{} confirmations",
                        w.getId(), confirmations, required);
                }
            } catch (BlockchainNodeException e) {
                log.warn("Node unavailable for withdrawal {}", w.getId(), e);
                // Try again next poll cycle — don't fail the withdrawal
            }
            withdrawalRepo.save(w);
        }
    }
}

// Confirmation requirements (risk-based):
// Small amounts (<$100): 1 confirmation
// Medium ($100-$10K): 3 confirmations
// Large (>$10K): 6 confirmations (BTC standard for large sums)

What if the transaction never confirms (dropped from mempool)?

// After 24 hours unconfirmed: re-broadcast with higher fee
// After 72 hours: escalate to ops, notify user, offer to re-initiate
@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 * * * *") // hourly
public void handleStuckWithdrawals() {
    Instant cutoff24h = Instant.now().minusSeconds(86400);
    withdrawalRepo.findBroadcastBefore(cutoff24h)
        .forEach(w -> {
            log.warn("Withdrawal {} stuck > 24h — re-broadcasting", w.getId());
            blockchainClient.rebroadcastWithBump(w.getTxHash(),
                w.getNetwork()); // fee bump (RBF for Bitcoin)
        });
}

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining Crypto Risk to Non-Technical Executives (15 min)

👨‍💻 Sanjay: Your VP of Engineering asks you: *"We're seeing engineers want to store hot wallet private keys in our standard secrets manager, the same way we store database passwords. What's wrong with that, and what should we do instead?"*

How do you explain this to a VP who understands software but not cryptography?

👨 Khanh: I'd start by making the stakes concrete, because "private key security" sounds like a normal security problem until you understand what makes it different.

*"A database password that gets compromised is serious — but we can rotate it. We change the password, the attacker loses access, and we're back to normal. A Bitcoin private key that gets compromised is not like that. Whoever has the key controls the funds — permanently, irrevocably. There is no 'change the password.' There is no 'contact your bank.' The blockchain doesn't have a fraud department. If an attacker steals a private key managing $10M in assets, that $10M is gone.*

*So the question isn't 'is our secrets manager secure?' Our secrets manager is good. The question is: does it meet the bar for something where the consequence of compromise is permanent, irreversible loss of potentially hundreds of millions of dollars?*

*A standard secrets manager is designed for operational credentials — things you rotate regularly, things where compromise is detectable and reversible. It's not designed for custody of bearer assets where possession equals ownership.*

*What we should use instead is a Hardware Security Module — an HSM. An HSM is a physical device that generates and stores private keys internally. The key never leaves the device in plaintext. Even an engineer with full access to our systems cannot extract the raw key. To sign a transaction, you request the HSM to sign it — the key stays inside.*

*The cost is higher. The operational complexity is higher. But for a hot wallet holding hundreds of millions of dollars, this is not optional."*

That framing — irreversible loss, bearer asset, possession equals ownership — usually lands immediately with executives. The technical solution follows naturally once they understand why this is categorically different from a database credential.

PART C — CODING: Order Status State Machine (20 min)

👨‍💻 Sanjay: *"Implement a clean state machine for an order's lifecycle: PENDING → OPEN → PARTIALLY_FILLED → FILLED. An order can also be CANCELLED from PENDING or OPEN. Validate transitions — invalid transitions should throw. Clean, extensible Java."*

👨 Khanh:

public enum OrderStatus {
    PENDING, OPEN, PARTIALLY_FILLED, FILLED, CANCELLED
}

public class OrderStateMachine {

    // Defines all valid (from → to) transitions
    private static final Map<OrderStatus, Set<OrderStatus>> TRANSITIONS =
        Map.of(
            OrderStatus.PENDING,          Set.of(OrderStatus.OPEN,
                                                  OrderStatus.CANCELLED),
            OrderStatus.OPEN,             Set.of(OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED,
                                                  OrderStatus.FILLED,
                                                  OrderStatus.CANCELLED),
            OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED, Set.of(OrderStatus.FILLED,
                                                  OrderStatus.CANCELLED),
            OrderStatus.FILLED,           Set.of(),  // terminal
            OrderStatus.CANCELLED,        Set.of()   // terminal
        );

    private OrderStatus current;

    public OrderStateMachine(OrderStatus initial) {
        this.current = initial;
    }

    public void transition(OrderStatus next) {
        Set<OrderStatus> allowed = TRANSITIONS.getOrDefault(
            current, Set.of());

        if (!allowed.contains(next)) {
            throw new InvalidTransitionException(
                String.format("Invalid transition: %s → %s. Allowed: %s",
                    current, next, allowed));
        }
        this.current = next;
    }

    public OrderStatus current() { return current; }

    public boolean isTerminal() {
        return TRANSITIONS.get(current).isEmpty();
    }
}

public class InvalidTransitionException extends RuntimeException {
    public InvalidTransitionException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void happyPath() {
    var sm = new OrderStateMachine(OrderStatus.PENDING);
    sm.transition(OrderStatus.OPEN);
    sm.transition(OrderStatus.PARTIALLY_FILLED);
    sm.transition(OrderStatus.FILLED);
    assertThat(sm.current()).isEqualTo(OrderStatus.FILLED);
    assertThat(sm.isTerminal()).isTrue();
}

@Test
void cancelFromPending() {
    var sm = new OrderStateMachine(OrderStatus.PENDING);
    sm.transition(OrderStatus.CANCELLED);
    assertThat(sm.current()).isEqualTo(OrderStatus.CANCELLED);
    assertThat(sm.isTerminal()).isTrue();
}

@Test
void cancelFromOpen() {
    var sm = new OrderStateMachine(OrderStatus.OPEN);
    sm.transition(OrderStatus.CANCELLED);
    assertThat(sm.current()).isEqualTo(OrderStatus.CANCELLED);
}

@Test
void rejectsInvalidTransition() {
    var sm = new OrderStateMachine(OrderStatus.FILLED);
    assertThatThrownBy(() -> sm.transition(OrderStatus.OPEN))
        .isInstanceOf(InvalidTransitionException.class)
        .hasMessageContaining("FILLED → OPEN");
}

@Test
void rejectsTransitionFromTerminalState() {
    var sm = new OrderStateMachine(OrderStatus.CANCELLED);
    assertThatThrownBy(() -> sm.transition(OrderStatus.PENDING))
        .isInstanceOf(InvalidTransitionException.class);
}

@Test
void directFillWithoutPartial() {
    // Small orders can go OPEN → FILLED directly
    var sm = new OrderStateMachine(OrderStatus.OPEN);
    sm.transition(OrderStatus.FILLED);
    assertThat(sm.current()).isEqualTo(OrderStatus.FILLED);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — JUDGMENT & CULTURE (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Principal Engineer.

👩 Maya (EM): Crypto companies have faced significant trust crises — FTX collapse, exchange hacks, rug pulls. How do you think about your personal responsibility as an engineer building financial infrastructure in this space?

👨 Khanh: I think about it seriously — and I want to give you an honest answer rather than a rehearsed one.

The FTX collapse wasn't primarily an engineering failure — it was fraud. But engineering enabled it. Systems can be built in ways that make fraud possible, auditable, or invisible. An engineer who builds a system without asking "could this be used to misappropriate funds?" is not absolved of responsibility by the fact that a human made the fraudulent decision.

My personal responsibility, as I see it, is threefold.

First: build auditability in. Every financial system I touch should have an immutable audit trail where every balance change, every transfer, every permission change is logged permanently with who authorised it. This isn't a nice-to-have — it's the minimum that separates a trustworthy exchange from an opaque one.

Second: refuse to build systems I can't explain to a regulator. If a PM asks me to build a feature and I can't answer the question *"how would we explain this to our compliance team or to a regulator?"* — that's a signal I need to raise, not suppress.

Third: acknowledge uncertainty. Crypto is evolving faster than the regulatory frameworks around it. That means we're often building in grey areas. In grey areas, my default is toward more transparency and more caution, not less. The cost of being too conservative is missed features. The cost of being too permissive is user harm at scale and potentially criminal liability.

I want to build things I'm proud to put my name on. That's not a lofty statement — it's a practical filter for the decisions I make every day.

👩 Maya: Coinbase operates in a space with extraordinary regulatory scrutiny — SEC, CFTC, FinCEN, and others. Engineers sometimes feel constrained by compliance requirements. How do you stay motivated when legal and compliance add friction?

👨 Khanh: I reframe the friction, which helps.

When compliance says "you can't do this without these controls," my first instinct now — after years of working in fintech — is not "this is slowing us down." It's "what is this control protecting against, and do I understand the threat model well enough to implement it correctly?"

Compliance requirements are usually encoding a real risk that someone else learned the hard way. KYC requirements exist because money laundering is real. Transaction limits exist because fraud is real. Travel Rule requirements exist because terrorist financing is real. These aren't bureaucratic inventions — they're responses to actual harms.

When I understand the threat model, I can implement the control correctly rather than technically. The difference matters: a technically correct implementation passes the audit but fails in production. A correctly-understood implementation actually prevents the harm.

The place I push back is when a compliance requirement is framed so broadly that it would prevent legitimate user behaviour without meaningfully reducing risk. I push back through the right channel — writing a doc, asking for a call with the compliance team, asking for the specific regulatory reference — not by implementing something deliberately narrow without disclosing it.

Compliance isn't my adversary. It's a different form of engineering constraint — one with legal consequences instead of performance consequences.

👨‍💻 Omar (Principal Eng): Last question. What's the most important thing a new engineer joining Coinbase's trading team should know that they won't find in any documentation?

👨 Khanh: The thing I always wish someone had told me when joining a financial systems team: the difference between "the system says it worked" and "it actually worked."

In most software, those are the same thing. In financial systems, they're not. A transaction can return HTTP 200, update your database, and still fail — because the external settlement leg timed out, because a blockchain transaction was dropped from the mempool, because a bank declined after their 30-second async response window.

New engineers often build happy-path systems that look correct in staging and fail silently in production. The discipline I'd share is: for every state transition your service creates, ask — *"What confirms that the external world agrees with my internal state?"*

In a trading system, that question is the job. The order book can say a trade executed. The settlement system can say funds moved. But until the blockchain confirms or the bank settles, the user's money is in a state of uncertain truth — and the engineer's job is to manage that uncertainty honestly rather than hiding it behind a false success status.

That mindset is the single most important thing. Everything else — the Kafka patterns, the state machines, the confirmation logic — follows from understanding that the world is asynchronous and your system's view of it is always provisional.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Crypto motivation + reliability mindset 10/10 Engineering problems not asset class; uncertainty = worst UX framing
Tech A Order book + matching engine 10/10 TreeMap + FIFO deque, price-time priority, FIFO fairness
Tech A+ Blockchain settlement 10/10 Two-phase ledger+on-chain, confirmation policy, fee bump for stuck txns
Tech B HSM explanation to VP 10/10 Irreversible loss → bearer asset → possession = ownership → HSM
Tech C Coding: order state machine 10/10 Transition map, terminal states, 6 tests including invalid transitions
Culture A Personal responsibility in crypto 10/10 Auditability, regulator explainability, conservative in grey areas
Culture B Compliance as constraint 10/10 Understand the threat model; compliance = legal engineering constraint
Culture C Advice for new engineers 10/10 "System says worked ≠ actually worked" — provisional truth mindset

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Coinbase Interview Tips

  1. Order book = TreeMap + FIFO deque — price-time priority; know it from first principles
  2. Blockchain finality is probabilistic — N confirmations required; amount determines N
  3. Hot wallet private keys ≠ database passwords — irreversible loss; HSM is the answer
  4. Two-phase settlement — internal ledger credit (instant) vs on-chain confirmation (async)
  5. Fee bump / RBF — Replace-By-Fee for stuck Bitcoin transactions; know it exists
  6. Auditability is non-negotiable — immutable audit trail for every state change
  7. "System says worked ≠ actually worked" — the crypto engineer's core mental model
  8. Coinbase salaries (Senior/E5): USD 180–250K base + significant equity; strong benefits

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Reliability "Never let traffic volume determine your error rate."
Reliability "The worst UX in a volatile market is uncertainty."
HSM "Possession equals ownership — the blockchain has no fraud department."
Responsibility "Build systems I'm proud to put my name on — that's a practical filter, not a lofty statement."
Compliance "Compliance requirements encode a real risk someone learned the hard way."
Compliance "Compliance isn't my adversary. It's engineering with legal consequences."
Advice "The world is asynchronous and your system's view of it is always provisional."
Settlement "What confirms that the external world agrees with my internal state?"

🎭 Mock Interview #48 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: DoorDash (San Francisco / Remote, Food Delivery Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Dispatch & Logistics team. DoorDash completes 2 billion orders per year across 37 countries. Real-time dasher assignment, ETA prediction, and restaurant capacity management are the core engineering challenges. Every second of assignment delay costs customer satisfaction and dasher earnings. Tech: Java 17, Kotlin, gRPC, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, Flink, internal Geo service (H3 grid). Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. What makes DoorDash interviews distinct: Strong emphasis on real-time systems, multi-party coordination (customer + restaurant + dasher), and making decisions under uncertainty with incomplete information. They want engineers who think about the last-mile edge case, not just the happy path.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Kelly (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! DoorDash's mission is to grow and empower local economies. What draws you to a logistics and delivery platform versus other tech verticals?

👨 Khanh: What draws me is that the system's output is physical — a meal arriving at someone's door. That makes the feedback loop immediate and tangible in a way that most software doesn't provide.

In a payment system, if latency spikes, a transaction takes longer. That's bad. In a food delivery system, if the dispatch algorithm assigns the wrong dasher, the food arrives cold or late — and the restaurant, the dasher, and the customer all have a worse day. The multi-party impact of every engineering decision forces you to think at a systems level, not just a service level.

I'm also drawn to the real-time constraint. A dasher assignment algorithm that takes 30 seconds is useless — the optimal assignment window has closed and conditions have changed. Building systems that make good decisions quickly under uncertainty is one of the harder and more interesting classes of engineering problems.

👩 Kelly: DoorDash has three stakeholders in every order — customer, restaurant, dasher. Engineers sometimes say that makes every product decision complicated because optimising for one can hurt another. How do you think about that?

👨 Khanh: That tension is real and I think it's worth naming clearly rather than pretending it doesn't exist.

My mental model: the three parties are in a value chain, not in competition. A dasher who earns well becomes more reliable and available — which improves customer experience. A restaurant that receives accurate ETAs can time preparation correctly — which reduces wait time for dashers and freshness for customers. The system works best when all three parties are served reasonably well, not when one is optimised at another's expense.

That said, when there's a genuine conflict — and there will be — I think the right approach is to be explicit about who's being prioritised and why, rather than pretending the decision is neutral. An algorithm that favours shorter Dasher drive times might slightly increase restaurant wait times. That's a conscious product decision that engineering should surface, not quietly encode.

👩 Kelly: Great framing. Technical rounds are next. Good luck, Khanh!

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Dispatch & Logistics.

PART A — Dasher Assignment Algorithm (25 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus (Staff Eng): An order just came in. There are 20 available dashers in the vicinity. Design the system that selects and assigns the best dasher. What signals matter, how do you score them, and how do you handle the assignment atomically?

👨 Khanh: Let me start with what "best" means in this context — because the right assignment depends on what we're optimising for.

*Assuming: optimise for food freshness (minimise total delivery time) while keeping dasher utilisation above a minimum threshold.*

Signals that matter for dasher selection:

public record DasherCandidate(
    String dasherId,
    Location currentLocation,
    double rating,            // 1.0–5.0
    int activeOrders,         // current concurrent orders
    boolean isOnShift,        // within scheduled hours
    VehicleType vehicleType   // BICYCLE | SCOOTER | CAR
) {}

public class DasherScorer {

    public double score(DasherCandidate dasher,
                        Restaurant restaurant,
                        Order order) {

        // 1. Drive time to restaurant (most important signal)
        double driveMinutes = geoService.estimateDriveTime(
            dasher.currentLocation(),
            restaurant.getLocation(),
            dasher.vehicleType());

        // Normalise to [0,1]: 0 min = 1.0, 15+ min = 0.0
        double proximityScore = Math.max(0.0,
            1.0 - driveMinutes / 15.0);

        // 2. Dasher load — prefer dashers with fewer active orders
        double loadScore = Math.max(0.0,
            1.0 - dasher.activeOrders() / 3.0); // max 3 concurrent orders

        // 3. Rating — prefer reliable dashers for large orders
        double ratingScore = (dasher.rating() - 1.0) / 4.0; // normalise to [0,1]

        // 4. Vehicle type — for large orders, prefer car over bicycle
        double vehicleScore = vehicleMatchScore(
            dasher.vehicleType(), order.getEstimatedWeightKg());

        // Weighted composite score
        return 0.50 * proximityScore   // proximity is dominant
             + 0.25 * loadScore
             + 0.15 * ratingScore
             + 0.10 * vehicleScore;
    }

    private double vehicleMatchScore(VehicleType type,
                                     double weightKg) {
        if (weightKg > 5.0 && type == VehicleType.BICYCLE) return 0.3;
        if (weightKg > 10.0 && type == VehicleType.SCOOTER)  return 0.5;
        return 1.0;
    }
}

Assignment — preventing double-assignment with Redis lock:

@Service
public class DispatchService {

    private static final Duration LOCK_TTL = Duration.ofSeconds(10);

    public AssignmentResult assignBestDasher(Order order,
                                              Restaurant restaurant) {
        // Fetch available dashers in search radius (H3 hex ring)
        List<DasherCandidate> candidates = geoService
            .getAvailableDashers(restaurant.getLocation(), radiusKm(2));

        if (candidates.isEmpty()) {
            // Widen search radius up to 10km
            candidates = geoService.getAvailableDashers(
                restaurant.getLocation(), radiusKm(10));
        }

        // Score and rank
        List<DasherCandidate> ranked = candidates.stream()
            .sorted(Comparator.comparingDouble(
                d -> -scorer.score(d, restaurant, order)))
            .toList();

        // Try to assign in rank order — first lock that succeeds wins
        for (DasherCandidate dasher : ranked) {
            String lockKey = "dasher:lock:" + dasher.dasherId();

            boolean locked = redisTemplate.opsForValue()
                .setIfAbsent(lockKey, order.getId(), LOCK_TTL);

            if (!locked) continue; // dasher being assigned to another order

            try {
                // Atomic DB update
                boolean assigned = dasherRepo
                    .assignIfAvailable(dasher.dasherId(), order.getId());

                if (assigned) {
                    notifyDasher(dasher.dasherId(), order);
                    return AssignmentResult.success(dasher.dasherId());
                }
            } finally {
                // Release lock if DB assignment failed
                // Keep lock if successful (expires after 10s naturally)
                if (!dasherRepo.isAssignedTo(
                        dasher.dasherId(), order.getId())) {
                    redisTemplate.delete(lockKey);
                }
            }
        }

        return AssignmentResult.noAvailableDasher();
    }
}

What happens when no dasher is available?

// Enqueue for retry with backoff — don't block the thread
public void handleNoAvailableDasher(Order order) {
    int retryCount = order.getAssignmentRetries();

    if (retryCount < 5) {
        // Retry in 30s — conditions change as dashers complete orders
        long retryDelayMs = 30_000 * (retryCount + 1);
        scheduledExecutor.schedule(
            () -> dispatchService.assignBestDasher(order,
                restaurantRepo.findById(order.getRestaurantId())
                    .orElseThrow()),
            retryDelayMs, TimeUnit.MILLISECONDS);
        order.setAssignmentRetries(retryCount + 1);
    } else {
        // After 5 retries (2.5 minutes) → cancel order, refund customer
        order.setStatus(OrderStatus.CANCELLED_NO_DASHER);
        refundService.refund(order.getId());
        notifyCustomer(order, "We couldn't find a Dasher for your order.");
    }
    orderRepo.save(order);
}

👨‍💻 Marcus: What if the restaurant says the food won't be ready for 20 minutes? Should you assign a dasher now or wait?

👨 Khanh: This is a classic explore-exploit problem under uncertainty, and the right answer depends on supply conditions.

If dasher supply is abundant (many available nearby): Wait 15 minutes before assigning. There's no urgency — a fresh dasher will likely be available when food is ready, and you avoid the dasher waiting at the restaurant burning time and becoming frustrated.

If dasher supply is tight (few available, peak hours): Assign now, but with a "staged dispatch" — the dasher is notified but instructed not to leave for pickup until a food-ready signal arrives from the restaurant:

public enum DasherInstruction {
    PICK_UP_NOW,         // food is ready
    WAIT_FOR_SIGNAL,     // assigned but hold — restaurant not ready
    PROCEED_TO_PICKUP    // restaurant signalled ready
}

// Restaurant tablet sends: POST /orders/{id}/ready
@PostMapping("/orders/{orderId}/ready")
public void markFoodReady(@PathVariable String orderId) {
    Order order = orderRepo.findById(orderId).orElseThrow();
    order.setFoodReadyAt(Instant.now());

    if (order.getDasherId() != null) {
        // Dasher was pre-assigned — signal them to proceed
        notifyDasher(order.getDasherId(), DasherInstruction.PROCEED_TO_PICKUP);
    } else {
        // No dasher yet — trigger assignment now
        dispatchService.assignBestDasher(order,
            restaurantRepo.findById(order.getRestaurantId()).orElseThrow());
    }
    orderRepo.save(order);
}

Decision logic:

public void scheduleDasherAssignment(Order order, int prepTimeMinutes) {
    int dashersAvailable = geoService
        .countAvailableDashers(order.getPickupLocation(), radiusKm(3));

    if (dashersAvailable > 10 || prepTimeMinutes < 5) {
        // Plenty of supply or food almost ready — wait or assign now
        long waitMs = Math.max(0, (prepTimeMinutes - 5) * 60_000L);
        scheduledExecutor.schedule(
            () -> assignBestDasher(order), waitMs, MILLISECONDS);
    } else {
        // Tight supply — pre-assign with staged dispatch
        assignBestDasher(order); // assigns in WAIT_FOR_SIGNAL mode
    }
}

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining an ETA Miss to a Customer (15 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: A customer tweets: *"DoorDash said my food would arrive in 25 minutes. It's been 55 minutes. Your app still says 5 minutes away. This is broken."*

You're the engineer on call. The tweet goes semi-viral. Your Head of Communications asks you to draft a technical explanation of what likely went wrong and what you're doing about it. How do you respond?

👨 Khanh: First — I separate two jobs that are easy to conflate. The immediate customer response is Communications' job. My job is to give them accurate information to work with.

What I'd send to the Head of Communications (internal):

*"Here's what likely happened technically, in plain English. Our ETA is a prediction based on: restaurant prep time, dasher pickup time, and driving time to delivery address. It's updated every 2 minutes using real-time signals.*

*The 55-minute actual vs 25-minute predicted gap is large. Most likely causes, in order of probability:*

*1. The restaurant took significantly longer than estimated to prepare the order — this is the most common cause of large ETA misses and the one we have least control over.*

*2. The dasher was assigned to a concurrent order and arrived at the restaurant late — our app shows time to delivery address, not to restaurant, so the customer wouldn't see the delay building.*

*3. Our map routing underestimated drive time — traffic conditions changed after the estimate.*

*What makes this case worse: the app still showed '5 minutes away' at the 55-minute mark. That's a stale prediction — the model should have updated more aggressively. I'm looking at whether our ETA refresh interval failed or whether we suppressed updates to avoid alarming customers (a product decision that backfired here).*

*What I'm checking in the next hour: the dasher's GPS track for this order, the restaurant's confirmed-ready timestamp, and whether the ETA update pipeline showed any anomalies in this window.*

I'll have a clearer picture in 2 hours."

For the public tweet, I'd recommend: *"We're sorry your order was delayed and we understand how frustrating that is. We're looking into what happened with your specific order and will follow up directly. We're also investigating why the app showed an inaccurate ETA — that's not acceptable and we're working on improving it."*

Short, honest, specific about the ETA problem, no excuses.

PART C — CODING: ETA Tracker (20 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: *"Implement a simple ETA tracker. A Dasher reports their GPS location every 30 seconds. Given a history of locations and the delivery address, calculate the estimated remaining time in minutes. Use straight-line distance as a proxy — assume 20 km/h average speed."*

👨 Khanh:

public record GpsPoint(double lat, double lng, Instant recordedAt) {}

public class EtaTracker {

    private static final double AVG_SPEED_KMH = 20.0;
    private static final double EARTH_RADIUS_KM = 6371.0;

    private final Deque<GpsPoint> locationHistory = new ArrayDeque<>();
    private final GpsPoint deliveryAddress;

    public EtaTracker(GpsPoint deliveryAddress) {
        this.deliveryAddress = deliveryAddress;
    }

    public void recordLocation(GpsPoint point) {
        locationHistory.addLast(point);
        // Keep only last 10 readings (5 minutes of history)
        while (locationHistory.size() > 10) {
            locationHistory.pollFirst();
        }
    }

    /**
     * Returns estimated remaining delivery time in minutes.
     * Uses last known location and straight-line distance.
     */
    public double estimateRemainingMinutes() {
        if (locationHistory.isEmpty()) {
            throw new IllegalStateException(
                "No location data available for ETA calculation");
        }

        GpsPoint last = locationHistory.peekLast();
        double distanceKm = haversineKm(last, deliveryAddress);

        return (distanceKm / AVG_SPEED_KMH) * 60.0; // minutes
    }

    /**
     * Calculates actual speed (km/h) from recent location history.
     * Returns empty if insufficient data.
     */
    public OptionalDouble actualSpeedKmh() {
        if (locationHistory.size() < 2) return OptionalDouble.empty();

        GpsPoint first = locationHistory.peekFirst();
        GpsPoint last  = locationHistory.peekLast();

        double distKm = haversineKm(first, last);
        double hours  = Duration.between(first.recordedAt(),
            last.recordedAt()).toSeconds() / 3600.0;

        if (hours == 0) return OptionalDouble.empty();
        return OptionalDouble.of(distKm / hours);
    }

    /**
     * ETA using actual measured speed (more accurate when moving).
     * Falls back to default speed if insufficient history.
     */
    public double smartEtaMinutes() {
        OptionalDouble actualSpeed = actualSpeedKmh();
        double speed = actualSpeed.orElse(AVG_SPEED_KMH);

        // Guard: if speed is unrealistically high or zero, use default
        if (speed < 0.5 || speed > 120.0) speed = AVG_SPEED_KMH;

        GpsPoint last = locationHistory.peekLast();
        double distKm = haversineKm(last, deliveryAddress);
        return (distKm / speed) * 60.0;
    }

    // Haversine distance in km
    private double haversineKm(GpsPoint a, GpsPoint b) {
        double dLat = Math.toRadians(b.lat() - a.lat());
        double dLng = Math.toRadians(b.lng() - a.lng());
        double h = Math.sin(dLat / 2) * Math.sin(dLat / 2)
                 + Math.cos(Math.toRadians(a.lat()))
                 * Math.cos(Math.toRadians(b.lat()))
                 * Math.sin(dLng / 2) * Math.sin(dLng / 2);
        return 2 * EARTH_RADIUS_KM * Math.asin(Math.sqrt(h));
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void estimatesEtaFromDistance() {
    // Delivery is ~1 km away at 20 km/h = 3 minutes
    var tracker = new EtaTracker(new GpsPoint(10.0, 106.001, Instant.now()));
    tracker.recordLocation(new GpsPoint(10.0, 106.0, Instant.now()));

    double eta = tracker.estimateRemainingMinutes();
    assertThat(eta).isBetween(2.5, 3.5); // ~3 minutes
}

@Test
void throwsWhenNoLocationRecorded() {
    var tracker = new EtaTracker(new GpsPoint(10.0, 106.0, Instant.now()));
    assertThatThrownBy(tracker::estimateRemainingMinutes)
        .isInstanceOf(IllegalStateException.class);
}

@Test
void keepsOnlyLastTenReadings() {
    var tracker = new EtaTracker(new GpsPoint(10.0, 106.0, Instant.now()));
    for (int i = 0; i < 15; i++) {
        tracker.recordLocation(
            new GpsPoint(10.0 + i * 0.001, 106.0, Instant.now()));
    }
    assertThat(tracker.locationHistory).hasSize(10);
}

@Test
void smartEtaUsesActualSpeed() {
    Instant now = Instant.now();
    // Dasher moved 2km in 10 min = 12 km/h (slower than default 20)
    var tracker = new EtaTracker(new GpsPoint(10.05, 106.0, now));
    tracker.recordLocation(new GpsPoint(10.0, 106.0,
        now.minusSeconds(600)));
    tracker.recordLocation(new GpsPoint(10.02, 106.0, now));

    // Smart ETA should use ~12 km/h, not 20
    double smartEta   = tracker.smartEtaMinutes();
    double defaultEta = tracker.estimateRemainingMinutes();

    // Slower actual speed → longer ETA than default
    assertThat(smartEta).isGreaterThan(defaultEta * 0.9);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — LEADERSHIP & OPERATIONS (35 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager + Staff Engineer.

👩 Sarah (EM): Peak hours at DoorDash are predictable — Friday and Saturday evenings. How do you prepare a distributed system for a load pattern you can predict?

👨 Khanh: Predictable load is a gift in distributed systems — it means you can prepare instead of react. I think about preparation in four layers.

First — capacity headroom: We know Friday dinner will be roughly 3x baseline. That number should live in a runbook, validated quarterly against actual peak metrics. Before peak seasons, I'd verify that auto-scaling policies are configured to handle the ramp — not just the peak — because the ramp from 5pm to 7pm is actually harder on services than the sustained peak.

Second — pre-warming: Caches that are cold on startup create a thundering herd when traffic arrives. For predictable peaks, I'd pre-warm critical caches — restaurant menus, dasher availability zones, common address geolocation results — starting 30 minutes before the expected ramp.

Third — degradation playbook: Every service should have a documented degradation mode. For dispatch: if the recommendation scoring service is slow, fall back to nearest-dasher-only assignment. If geolocation is degraded, use cached zone boundaries. Each fallback should be a feature flag I can toggle in under two minutes — not a code change, not a deployment.

Fourth — game day: Two weeks before a major peak (Super Bowl, Valentine's Day), I'd run a game day — a controlled drill where we activate our degradation playbooks on a small traffic slice and verify they work. The worst time to discover that your fallback has a bug is during the actual peak.

The mindset I try to install: peak hours aren't emergencies to survive — they're scheduled events to prepare for. If a predictable load pattern surprises your system, that's a planning failure, not a technical failure.

👩 Sarah: You've been working on an important feature for six weeks. Two days before release, QA finds a serious bug that will require at least a week to fix properly. The business has been marketing this release date. What do you do?

👨 Khanh: I tell the right people immediately — and I mean immediately, not after I've tried to find a shortcut.

The instinct to delay telling people bad news is understandable but almost always makes things worse. Every hour I spend trying to "find a solution first" is an hour that Marketing, Sales, and leadership spend making commitments based on a release date that's no longer valid.

So my first move is to go to my manager and be direct: *"We have a serious bug. I believe it will take one week to fix properly. The release date needs to move."*

Then I frame the options clearly:

  1. Delay one week — fix it properly. Clean release.
  2. Ship with a known workaround — document the limitation,

communicate it to affected users, fix in next sprint.

  1. Partial release — ship the parts of the feature that

work, hold the broken component.

Which option is right depends on the bug's impact on users and the business cost of the delay. That's a product decision, not an engineering decision — but engineering needs to give an honest input on the cost and risk of each.

What I won't do is estimate "maybe three days" when I think it's a week, to soften the news. That trades one missed deadline for two, and burns credibility each time. If I say a week, I mean a week — and I'll flag immediately if the estimate changes.

The other thing I'd check: how did a serious bug get to QA two days before release? That's a process question worth a postmortem regardless of which option we choose.

👨‍💻 James (Staff Eng): Last question. DoorDash operates in cities where conditions are completely different — New York on a rainy Friday, Phoenix in summer heat, rural Kansas with three dashers in the area. How do you build algorithms that work well across such different contexts?

👨 Khanh: This is one of the most interesting challenges in marketplace engineering — your algorithm is a single codebase that has to make reasonable decisions in wildly different operating environments.

The first principle I'd apply: separate policy from mechanism.

The matching algorithm's mechanism — score candidates, pick the best, assign atomically — should be consistent everywhere. What changes is the policy: the scoring weights, the search radius, the retry thresholds.

// Policy is configuration, not hardcoded logic
public record DispatchPolicy(
    double proximityWeight,   // Phoenix: 0.6 (supply rich)
    double loadWeight,        // NYC: 0.3 (many concurrent orders)
    double ratingWeight,
    int initialSearchRadiusKm,
    int maxSearchRadiusKm,
    int retryCount,
    int retryIntervalSeconds
) {}

// Loaded from config per market — tunable without deployment
// Markets: NYC, SF, PHX, RURAL, DEFAULT
DispatchPolicy policy = policyService.forMarket(order.getMarket());

Second principle: model the supply differently per context.

In rural Kansas with three dashers, the assignment algorithm needs to be much more conservative — don't reject a dasher because their rating is 4.2 instead of 4.5, because there's no one else. Low-supply markets should have lower minimum thresholds and wider initial search radii.

// Dynamic threshold adjustment based on supply level
double ratingThreshold = supplyLevel < 3
    ? MINIMUM_ACCEPTABLE_RATING     // 4.0 → relaxed to 3.5
    : STANDARD_RATING_THRESHOLD;    // 4.0 normal

Third: measure separately, improve separately.

An algorithm that performs well on average across all markets might be great in NYC and terrible in rural areas. Market-level dashboards — assignment rate, ETA accuracy, cancellation rate — let you see where the algorithm is actually underperforming, instead of having good average metrics hide local failures.

The general lesson: one algorithm, many policies. Tune the policies with data. Never encode city-specific logic directly into the algorithm — that path leads to an unmaintainable if-else chain and a codebase nobody wants to touch.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Multi-party systems + real-time 10/10 Physical feedback loop; algorithmic decisions affect dashers' livelihoods
Tech A Dasher scoring + assignment 10/10 Weighted composite score, Redis lock for double-assignment, staged dispatch
Tech A+ Prep time vs pre-assignment 10/10 Supply-aware staging; food-ready webhook triggers dispatch
Tech B ETA miss communications 10/10 Internal technical brief vs external response; stale ETA root cause
Tech C Coding: ETA tracker 10/10 Haversine, sliding history, smart ETA from actual speed, 4 tests
Ops A Predictable peak preparation 10/10 Capacity + pre-warm + degradation playbook + game day
Ops B Bug before release deadline 10/10 Immediate escalation; three options framed; no padding estimates
Ops C Multi-market algorithm design 10/10 Separate policy from mechanism; per-market config; market-level metrics

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 DoorDash Interview Tips

  1. Three-party marketplace — every algorithm decision affects customer, restaurant, AND dasher
  2. Redis lock for assignment — SET NX EX prevents two concurrent assignment processes picking the same dasher
  3. Staged dispatch — assign dasher early in low-supply markets; delay in high-supply markets
  4. Separate policy from mechanism — algorithm logic is universal; weights and thresholds are per-market config
  5. Game day before peaks — predictable load = scheduled event to prepare for, not emergency to survive
  6. ETA accuracy = trust — stale ETA is worse than a slightly longer accurate one
  7. Bad news immediately — delay only compounds the problem; frame options, don't hide problems
  8. DoorDash salaries (Senior/E5): USD 170–230K base + RSU; total comp USD 300K–450K with equity

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Multi-party "The system works best when all three parties are served reasonably well — not when one is optimised at another's expense."
Peak load "Peak hours aren't emergencies to survive — they're scheduled events to prepare for."
Bad news "Every hour I spend trying to find a solution first is an hour that others spend on false assumptions."
Estimation "If I say a week, I mean a week — and I'll flag immediately if that changes."
Algorithm "One algorithm, many policies. Tune the policies with data."
Market fairness "Good average metrics can hide local failures — market-level dashboards prevent that."
ETA miss "Stale ETA is worse than a slightly longer accurate one."
Supply-aware "In rural Kansas with three dashers, you can't reject someone for a 4.2 rating."

🎭 Mock Interview #49 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Twilio (San Francisco / Remote, Communications API Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Messaging Infrastructure team. Twilio sends 200+ billion messages per year across SMS, WhatsApp, and email. Every API call is a developer's promise to their user. Reliability, global routing, and webhook delivery are the core challenges. Tech: Java 17, Spring Boot, gRPC, PostgreSQL, Redis, Kafka, AWS. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Amy (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Twilio is developer-first. Our customers are engineers. How does building for developers change how you think about API design and reliability?

👨 Khanh: Building for developers is humbling in a specific way — your users will read your error messages, inspect your HTTP headers, and call your API from a debugger. They'll notice everything.

That changes three things for me.

First, error messages become a first-class feature. A vague 400 Bad Request is useless to a developer at 2am debugging their integration. A clear `"Invalid 'To' number format: +849... — Vietnam numbers must start with +84 followed by 9 digits"` is the difference between a 10-minute fix and a 2-hour debugging session.

Second, idempotency is non-negotiable. Developers retry failed requests. If my API doesn't handle that gracefully — if a retry sends a second SMS — I've created a bug in their application through my API design.

Third, reliability needs to be observable. Developers need to know whether their message was delivered, and why it wasn't if it failed. A status callback with a clear error code is part of the product, not an afterthought.

👩 Amy: Twilio guarantees at-least-once SMS delivery. What does that mean to you as an engineer, and what does it cost?

👨 Khanh: At-least-once means we commit to delivering every message — accepting that very occasionally a message may be delivered more than once rather than risk dropping it.

The cost is: recipients may get duplicates in rare failure scenarios. For most SMS use cases — OTP codes, alerts, notifications — that's acceptable. For transactional messages like "your order is confirmed," it's annoying but manageable if the message content is idempotent.

The engineering cost is a persistent retry queue with deduplication at the carrier interface, and a reconciliation mechanism to detect messages that were accepted by the carrier but never delivered. Neither is trivial at 200 billion messages per year.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Messaging Platform.

PART A — SMS Routing Pipeline (25 min)

👨‍💻 Dan (Staff Eng): Walk me through how you'd design the pipeline from a developer's API call POST /messages to an SMS appearing on the recipient's phone.

👨 Khanh: Five stages: intake, validation, carrier selection, submission, and status tracking.

Stage 1 — Intake & Idempotency:

@PostMapping("/2010-04-01/Accounts/{accountSid}/Messages")
public MessageResponse sendMessage(@RequestBody MessageRequest req,
                                   @RequestHeader(value = "X-Twilio-Idempotency-Token",
                                                  required = false) String idempToken) {
    // Idempotency check — prevents duplicate sends on retry
    if (idempToken != null) {
        Optional<MessageResponse> existing =
