                "This transaction couldn't be processed",
                DeclineReason.FRAUD_BLOCK); // don't tell them why
        }
        if (!credit.isApproved()) {
            return CreditDecision.declined(
                credit.getExplanation(), // GDPR-compliant explanation
                DeclineReason.CREDIT_FAIL);
        }

        // Schedule instalment plan
        InstalmentPlan plan = instalmentScheduler.create(
            req.getAmount(), req.getCurrency(),
            credit.getApprovedCreditLimit());

        return CreditDecision.approved(plan);
    }
}

Credit scorer — key signals:

public CreditResult score(CreditRequest req) {
    // Signals used (all pulled from cache or fast DB reads)
    BigDecimal outstandingBalance = exposureService
        .getOutstanding(req.getCustomerId());  // existing Klarna debt
    int        overdueCount = overdueRepo
        .countOverdue(req.getCustomerId());    // missed payments
    int        recentDeclines = declineRepo
        .countRecent(req.getCustomerId(),
            Duration.ofDays(30));             // recent failures
    BigDecimal requestedAmount = req.getAmount();

    // Policy rules (configurable per country)
    CreditPolicy policy = policyEngine.getPolicy(req.getCountry());

    if (outstandingBalance.add(requestedAmount)
            .compareTo(policy.getMaxExposure()) > 0) {
        return CreditResult.declined(
            "Your current Klarna balance is at its limit");
    }
    if (overdueCount >= policy.getMaxOverdueCount()) {
        return CreditResult.declined(
            "You have overdue payments — please clear them first");
    }

    // Soft scoring — ML model for borderline cases
    double riskScore = mlScorer.score(req.getCustomerId(),
        requestedAmount, req.getMerchantCategory());

    return riskScore < policy.getApprovalThreshold()
        ? CreditResult.approved(policy.getMaxExposure()
            .subtract(outstandingBalance))
        : CreditResult.declined(
            "We're unable to offer credit for this purchase");
}

👨‍💻 Erik: A customer successfully buys with Klarna Pay in 4. Now they miss their second instalment. Walk me through the collections flow.

👨 Khanh: Collections must be firm on recovery but proportionate in approach — aggressive collections destroy customer relationships and invite regulatory scrutiny.

// Instalment schedule: due dates created at purchase time
@Entity
public class Instalment {
    @Id private String id;
    private String orderId;
    private String customerId;
    private int    instalmentNumber;  // 1, 2, 3, 4
    private BigDecimal amount;
    private LocalDate dueDate;
    private InstalmentStatus status; // UPCOMING | DUE | OVERDUE | PAID
}

// Collections engine — runs daily
@Scheduled(cron = "0 8 * * * *") // 8am daily
public void processOverdue() {
    List<Instalment> overdue = instalmentRepo
        .findByStatusAndDueDateBefore(
            InstalmentStatus.DUE, LocalDate.now());

    for (Instalment inst : overdue) {
        inst.setStatus(InstalmentStatus.OVERDUE);
        int daysOverdue = (int) ChronoUnit.DAYS.between(
            inst.getDueDate(), LocalDate.now());

        // Graduated response by days overdue
        if (daysOverdue == 1) {
            notificationService.sendReminder(inst,
                "Your payment of £" + inst.getAmount()
                + " was due yesterday.");

        } else if (daysOverdue == 3) {
            // Retry saved payment method
            paymentRetryService.retry(inst);
            notificationService.sendUrgentReminder(inst);

        } else if (daysOverdue == 7) {
            // Suspend customer's Klarna account
            accountService.suspend(inst.getCustomerId(),
                "Overdue payment");
            notificationService.sendSuspensionNotice(inst);

        } else if (daysOverdue == 30) {
            // Escalate to collections partner / credit bureau reporting
            collectionsPartner.refer(inst);
            creditBureau.report(inst.getCustomerId(),
                inst.getAmount(), "OVERDUE_30_DAYS");
        }

        instalmentRepo.save(inst);
    }
}

Key principle: each escalation step is documented in the customer's account history and must have a corresponding customer communication. Regulatory requirement in most EU markets: customer must be informed before any credit bureau report.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Declining a Customer Fairly (15 min)

👨‍💻 Erik: A customer contacts support: *"I was declined for a £50 purchase. I have a good credit score everywhere else. Why did Klarna decline me?"*

How do you help the support team respond?

👨 Khanh: This is a GDPR-sensitive situation — the customer has a legal right to a meaningful explanation of an automated credit decision in the EU.

Internal investigation: Pull the decision audit log for this customer. What was the specific decline reason? Common causes: outstanding Klarna balance, recent overdue payment, too many recent decline attempts, or the ML model's soft score below threshold.

Response to customer — honest and specific:

If the reason was an overdue balance: *"I can see your Klarna account has an overdue payment from a previous purchase. We require overdue amounts to be cleared before new purchases can be approved. Once you pay the outstanding amount, you'll be able to use Klarna again. Here's how to pay: [link]."*

If the reason was ML model (soft decline): *"Our system considers multiple factors when making a lending decision — including your history with Klarna, the purchase amount, and other signals. Based on those factors, we weren't able to approve this purchase.*

*You have the right to request a human review of this decision. I can escalate this to our credit team to review manually if you'd like."*

What I'd never write: *"Our automated system made this decision and we cannot override it."* Under EU law, automated decisions with legal or significant effect cannot be final — the customer has the right to a human review. Blocking that right in a support response is a compliance violation.

PART C — CODING: Instalment Schedule Generator (20 min)

👨‍💻 Erik: *"Generate a Pay in 4 instalment schedule. Given a purchase amount, a purchase date, and a payment interval of 14 days, return a list of 4 instalments with due dates and amounts. First instalment is due at purchase. Handle amounts that don't divide evenly — last instalment absorbs any remainder."*

👨 Khanh:

import java.math.BigDecimal;
import java.math.RoundingMode;
import java.time.LocalDate;
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.List;

public record Instalment(int number, LocalDate dueDate,
                          BigDecimal amount) {}

public class InstalmentScheduler {

    private static final int INSTALMENT_COUNT    = 4;
    private static final int INTERVAL_DAYS       = 14;

    /**
     * Generates Pay in 4 instalment schedule.
     * First instalment due immediately (purchaseDate).
     * Subsequent instalments every 14 days.
     * Rounding remainder applied to final instalment.
     */
    public List<Instalment> generate(BigDecimal totalAmount,
                                      LocalDate purchaseDate) {
        if (totalAmount == null || totalAmount.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) <= 0) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException(
                "Total amount must be positive");
        }

        // Base instalment: floor division to 2 decimal places
        BigDecimal base = totalAmount
            .divide(BigDecimal.valueOf(INSTALMENT_COUNT), 2, RoundingMode.FLOOR);

        // Remainder goes to last instalment
        BigDecimal remainder = totalAmount
            .subtract(base.multiply(BigDecimal.valueOf(INSTALMENT_COUNT - 1)))
            .setScale(2, RoundingMode.HALF_UP);

        List<Instalment> schedule = new ArrayList<>();

        for (int i = 1; i <= INSTALMENT_COUNT; i++) {
            LocalDate dueDate = purchaseDate.plusDays(
                (long)(i - 1) * INTERVAL_DAYS); // i=1 → +0 days (today)

            BigDecimal amount = (i == INSTALMENT_COUNT) ? remainder : base;
            schedule.add(new Instalment(i, dueDate, amount));
        }

        return schedule;
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void evenDivision() {
    var scheduler = new InstalmentScheduler();
    var schedule = scheduler.generate(
        new BigDecimal("100.00"),
        LocalDate.of(2024, 1, 1));

    assertThat(schedule).hasSize(4);
    assertThat(schedule.get(0).amount()).isEqualByComparingTo("25.00");
    assertThat(schedule.get(3).amount()).isEqualByComparingTo("25.00");
}

@Test
void unevenDivisionRemainderOnLast() {
    var scheduler = new InstalmentScheduler();
    // £10.00 / 4 = £2.50 each → but let's test £10.01
    // base = floor(10.01/4) = floor(2.5025) = £2.50
    // last = 10.01 - (2.50 * 3) = 10.01 - 7.50 = £2.51
    var schedule = scheduler.generate(
        new BigDecimal("10.01"),
        LocalDate.of(2024, 1, 1));

    assertThat(schedule.get(0).amount()).isEqualByComparingTo("2.50");
    assertThat(schedule.get(1).amount()).isEqualByComparingTo("2.50");
    assertThat(schedule.get(2).amount()).isEqualByComparingTo("2.50");
    assertThat(schedule.get(3).amount()).isEqualByComparingTo("2.51");

    // Total must equal original amount
    BigDecimal total = schedule.stream()
        .map(Instalment::amount)
        .reduce(BigDecimal.ZERO, BigDecimal::add);
    assertThat(total).isEqualByComparingTo("10.01");
}

@Test
void dueDatesCorrect() {
    var scheduler = new InstalmentScheduler();
    LocalDate purchase = LocalDate.of(2024, 6, 1);
    var schedule = scheduler.generate(new BigDecimal("200.00"), purchase);

    assertThat(schedule.get(0).dueDate()).isEqualTo(LocalDate.of(2024, 6, 1));
    assertThat(schedule.get(1).dueDate()).isEqualTo(LocalDate.of(2024, 6, 15));
    assertThat(schedule.get(2).dueDate()).isEqualTo(LocalDate.of(2024, 6, 29));
    assertThat(schedule.get(3).dueDate()).isEqualTo(LocalDate.of(2024, 7, 13));
}

@Test
void rejectsZeroAmount() {
    var scheduler = new InstalmentScheduler();
    assertThatThrownBy(() ->
        scheduler.generate(BigDecimal.ZERO, LocalDate.now()))
        .isInstanceOf(IllegalArgumentException.class);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — VALUES & JUDGMENT (30 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager.

👩 Lena (EM): BNPL has been criticised for encouraging people to overspend. How do you think about building a product that could harm users if misused?

👨 Khanh: I take that critique seriously — and I think the honest answer is that BNPL's harm potential is real and depends heavily on how the product is designed.

A product that makes it easy to accumulate six overlapping BNPL balances without showing the user their total exposure is designed poorly regardless of whether it's legal. A product that shows the customer their total outstanding BNPL balance across all instalments before confirming a new purchase is designed responsibly.

Engineering has direct influence over which of those exists. The decision to show a customer their total exposure at checkout — or to show only the next instalment amount — is an engineering and product decision, not just a marketing one.

My personal standard: the system should make the full cost of a decision legible to the customer before they confirm it. Not hidden in small print — legible in the UI, in plain language. If I wouldn't be comfortable explaining the full terms to my own mother at checkout, the design needs to change.

That standard has practical implications: exposure dashboards, spending summaries, proactive notifications when a customer is approaching a point where new credit would strain their repayment ability. These aren't regulatory minimums — they're responsible design.

👩 Lena: You joined Klarna two months ago and you notice that the team's code review culture is weak — PRs are approved in minutes without meaningful feedback. You're not the manager. What do you do?

👨 Khanh: I start by asking why, not what.

A weak review culture is usually a symptom of one of three things: time pressure, unclear expectations about what a good review looks like, or a belief that reviews aren't valuable enough to invest in.

My first move: observe a few more PRs and talk to two or three engineers privately to understand what's behind it. Then I'd have a conversation with the tech lead — not a complaint, but a question: *"I've noticed PRs are moving very quickly through review. Is that intentional — is the team optimising for speed right now? I want to understand the context before I share a perspective."*

If the response is "we've always done it this way" — I'd offer a specific, low-friction alternative. Not "we need better reviews" (vague), but: *"What if we tried a norm where at least one reviewer adds a comment explaining what they checked and what they were satisfied with — even if the PR is approved? It takes two minutes, it creates a shared record, and it would help me understand the codebase faster as a new joiner."*

I'd frame it as being useful to me as a newcomer — which is true — rather than as a criticism of the existing culture. That lowers defensiveness and makes the change easier to try.

And if a PR I review needs meaningful feedback, I give it regardless of team norms — modelling the behaviour I want to see is more effective than advocating for it.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Real-time credit decision constraints 10/10 Both error types are expensive; explainability under GDPR
Recruiter Regulatory configurability 10/10 Rules engine over hardcoded policy; audit trail as requirement
Tech A Parallel credit decision pipeline 10/10 3-track parallel execution, 200ms timeout, conservative fallback
Tech A+ Collections graduated flow 10/10 Day 1/3/7/30 escalation with customer communication at each step
Tech B Declining a customer fairly 10/10 GDPR right to explanation; right to human review; no blocking that right
Tech C Coding: instalment schedule 10/10 Floor division, remainder on last, total invariant, 4 tests
Culture A BNPL harm potential 10/10 Full exposure visibility as responsible design; personal standard
Culture B Fixing weak review culture 10/10 Ask why first; low-friction specific alternative; model the behaviour

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Klarna Interview Tips

  1. Sub-250ms credit decision — run identity, fraud, credit in parallel; timeout → conservative decline
  2. Both errors are expensive — false approval = bad debt; false decline = lost sale + merchant trust
  3. GDPR right to explanation — automated credit decline must be explainable in plain language
  4. Right to human review — customers can request manual review; never say "cannot override"
  5. Configurable credit policy — rules engine over hardcoded; regulations change frequently
  6. Collections = graduated response — proportionate, documented, communicated at each step
  7. Floor division for instalments — always round down on base; last instalment absorbs remainder
  8. Klarna salaries (Senior): SEK 700K–950K / USD 70–90K base (Stockholm); higher for London/NY offices

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Credit decision "Decline a creditworthy customer = lost sale. Approve an uncreditworthy one = bad debt. Neither error is free."
Regulations "Regulations in BNPL are changing frequently — policy should be config, not code."
GDPR "'The model said no' is not legally sufficient."
Responsible design "If I wouldn't be comfortable explaining the terms to my own mother at checkout, the design needs to change."
User harm "The system should make the full cost of a decision legible before the customer confirms it."
Review culture "Modelling the behaviour I want to see is more effective than advocating for it."
Reviews "Not 'we need better reviews' — that's vague. A specific, low-friction alternative."

🎭 Mock Interview #58 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Palantir Technologies (Denver / Remote, Data Analytics Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Foundry Platform team. Palantir builds data integration and analytics platforms for governments, intelligence agencies, and Fortune 500 enterprises. Foundry is used to model, analyse, and operationalise data at scale. Engineering here means working with classified data environments, strict compliance requirements, and customers who make life-or-death decisions. Tech: Java 17, TypeScript, Spark, Hadoop, internal ontology engine, Cassandra, PostgreSQL, gRPC, AWS/GovCloud. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. What makes Palantir distinct: The interview process is famous for "Palantir Forward Deployed Engineering" thinking — solve real problems, not toy puzzles. Culture is intensely mission-driven: they believe software can save lives. Engineers are expected to have strong opinions, defend them with data, and work directly with customers.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Claire (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Palantir's mission is to help the world's most important institutions make better decisions. That includes defence agencies, hospitals, and disaster response teams. How do you feel about building software that could be used in those contexts?

👨 Khanh: I think about it seriously rather than reflexively.

The honest answer is: powerful data analytics tools can be used for things I'd be proud of — accelerating vaccine distribution, coordinating disaster response, reducing financial crime — and for things that raise harder questions. The fact that the tools are powerful means the governance around how they're used matters enormously.

What I'd want to understand working here is: what are the review processes when Palantir's technology is deployed in a sensitive context? Are there use cases Palantir declines? Those questions matter to me more than the abstract fact that some customers are government or defence.

I'm not naive about the complexity, but I'm also not categorically opposed to working on hard problems in sensitive domains — especially if the alternative is that decisions get made with worse tools and less visibility.

👩 Claire: Palantir engineers often work directly with customers — sitting with analysts, debugging their workflows live. That's unusual for a software engineer. How do you feel about customer-facing work?

👨 Khanh: I find it makes me a better engineer.

The feedback loop when you're in a room with a user is immediate in a way that dashboards never are. A user who pauses for three seconds before clicking a button is telling you something the product analytics won't capture.

The challenge is code quality discipline under that pressure. When a customer is watching, the instinct is to hack something together quickly. The skill is knowing when a quick fix is acceptable and when it will create a problem in three months — and communicating that distinction to the customer honestly.

I've done customer-facing technical support in previous roles, and the thing I consistently find: customers who understand what you're doing and why — even if "why" is "we need two weeks to do this properly" — are far more patient than customers who feel like they're being kept in the dark.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Senior Engineer, Foundry Platform.

PART A — Data Pipeline Design (25 min)

👨‍💻 Alex (Sr Eng): A government health agency has 20 data sources — hospital records, pharmacy data, lab results, insurance claims. Each has a different schema, different update frequency, and different data quality. Design a pipeline that integrates them into a unified patient view.

👨 Khanh: This is a classic data integration problem — the hard part isn't the technical pipeline, it's the schema alignment and data quality. Let me address both.

Step 1 — Schema catalogue and canonical model:

// Each source has a connector that maps to a canonical schema
// The canonical Patient model is the agreed-upon truth

public record CanonicalPatient(
    String patientId,          // system-assigned UUID
    String nationalId,         // e.g., CCCD or SSN — for deduplication
    String name,
    LocalDate dateOfBirth,
    String gender,
    List<String> diagnosisCodes,   // ICD-10
    List<String> medicationCodes,  // NDC
    List<LabResult> labResults,
    Instant lastUpdated,
    String sourceSystem,
    Map<String, Object> rawAttributes  // passthrough for unmapped fields
) {}

// Each source has its own connector
public interface SourceConnector {
    String sourceId();
    List<CanonicalPatient> extract(Instant since);
    DataQualityReport validate(List<CanonicalPatient> records);
}

Step 2 — Entity resolution (deduplication): The hardest problem: the same patient appears in multiple systems with slightly different names, dates, or IDs.

@Service
public class EntityResolver {

    // Deterministic matching: same nationalId = same person
    // Probabilistic matching: name + DOB similarity above threshold

    public String resolvePatientId(CanonicalPatient incoming) {
        // 1. Exact match on national ID
        Optional<String> exact = patientIndex
            .findByNationalId(incoming.nationalId());
        if (exact.isPresent()) return exact.get();

        // 2. Probabilistic match: name similarity + DOB
        List<PatientCandidate> candidates = patientIndex
            .findByDobAndNamePrefix(
                incoming.dateOfBirth(),
                incoming.name().substring(0, 3));

        for (PatientCandidate c : candidates) {
            double similarity = nameSimilarity(
                incoming.name(), c.name()); // Jaro-Winkler
            if (similarity > 0.92) {
                return c.patientId(); // high-confidence match
            }
        }

        // 3. No match found → new patient record
        return UUID.randomUUID().toString();
    }
}

Step 3 — Conflict resolution (same patient, different values):

// When two sources disagree on a field, apply source priority
// Priority config is per-field, per-source pair
public class ConflictResolver {

    public CanonicalPatient merge(List<CanonicalPatient> versions) {
        // For each field, pick the value from the highest-priority source
        // For lists (diagnoses, medications), union across sources

        String name = versions.stream()
            .min(Comparator.comparingInt(
                v -> sourcePriority.get(v.sourceSystem())))
            .map(CanonicalPatient::name)
            .orElse("Unknown");

        List<String> allDiagnoses = versions.stream()
            .flatMap(v -> v.diagnosisCodes().stream())
            .distinct().toList();

        // Take latest lab results per test type
        Map<String, LabResult> latestLabs = new TreeMap<>();
        versions.stream()
            .flatMap(v -> v.labResults().stream())
            .forEach(lab -> latestLabs.merge(
                lab.testCode(), lab,
                (a, b) -> a.collectedAt().isAfter(b.collectedAt()) ? a : b));

        return new CanonicalPatient(
            versions.get(0).patientId(), /* ... */ name,
            allDiagnoses, new ArrayList<>(latestLabs.values()),
            Instant.now(), "MERGED", Map.of());
    }
}

Step 4 — Data quality monitoring:

public record DataQualityReport(
    String sourceId,
    int totalRecords,
    int missingNationalId,
    int invalidDob,
    int duplicatesDetected,
    double completenessScore   // 0.0–1.0
) {}

// Alert if completeness drops below threshold
@KafkaListener(topics = "dq.reports")
public void onQualityReport(DataQualityReport report) {
    if (report.completenessScore() < 0.95) {
        alertOps("Data quality alert: source " + report.sourceId()
            + " completeness " + report.completenessScore());
    }
}

Pipeline orchestration:

Each source runs on its own schedule:
  Hospital records: real-time (HL7 FHIR stream)
  Pharmacy data:    daily batch (SFTP file)
  Lab results:      near-real-time (HL7 v2 messages)
  Insurance claims: weekly batch (EDI 837)

All land in a staging area → entity resolution → merge →
golden record store → Foundry ontology layer →
analyst-facing views

👨‍💻 Alex: A hospital changes their patient ID system. All 500K records now have new IDs. How do you handle the re-ingestion without breaking the unified view?

👨 Khanh: This is an ID migration problem — the source changed its primary key, but our canonical patient IDs must remain stable so downstream analysis isn't broken.

Strategy — stable internal ID, map external IDs:

// Our canonical patientId is NEVER the source's ID
// We maintain a mapping table: sourceId × sourcePatientId → internalId

@Entity
public class PatientIdMapping {
    @Id private String mappingId;
    private String sourceId;           // "HOSPITAL_A"
    private String sourcePatientId;    // source's ID (old or new)
    private String internalPatientId;  // our stable UUID
    private boolean active;
    private Instant validFrom;
    private Instant validTo;           // null = current
}

// When hospital provides the old→new ID mapping file:
@Transactional
public void migrateIds(String sourceId,
                        Map<String, String> oldToNewIds) {
    for (var entry : oldToNewIds.entrySet()) {
        String oldId = entry.getKey();
        String newId = entry.getValue();

        // Find internal ID for old source ID
        PatientIdMapping old = mappingRepo
            .findBySourceAndId(sourceId, oldId).orElseThrow();

        // Retire old mapping
        old.setActive(false);
        old.setValidTo(Instant.now());
        mappingRepo.save(old);

        // Create new mapping pointing to SAME internal ID
        mappingRepo.save(new PatientIdMapping(
            UUID.randomUUID().toString(),
            sourceId, newId,
            old.getInternalPatientId(), // same internal ID preserved
            true, Instant.now(), null));
    }
}

Downstream systems continue using the internal UUID — they see zero disruption. The mapping layer absorbs the source's ID change entirely.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Presenting to a Non-Technical Customer (15 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: A hospital CIO asks you: *"We've been feeding data into Palantir for 3 months. How do I know the data is actually correct and complete? Can you give me confidence?"*

How do you respond?

👨 Khanh: This is a trust question, not a technical one — and trust requires evidence, not reassurance.

My response:

*"That's exactly the right question to ask, and I want to give you a specific answer rather than a general one.*

*We track four data quality dimensions for your feeds: completeness — what percentage of expected fields are populated; consistency — do values match across sources (e.g., does the pharmacy system's patient DOB match the hospital's?); timeliness — are records arriving within the expected window; and accuracy — spot-check validation against known-good reference records.*

*For your hospital feed over the past 3 months: completeness is at 97.3% — the 2.7% gap is mainly missing insurance codes on emergency admissions, which we've flagged to your team. Consistency across your three linked systems is 94.1%, and we've identified 847 patient records where the name spelling differs between systems — those are queued for your team to review.*

*What I'd recommend: a 30-minute monthly data quality review where we walk through these numbers together. That way you have a standing checkpoint, not a one-time snapshot. Would that be useful?"*

The offer of a recurring review is important — it signals that we're committed to ongoing data quality, not just the initial setup. And it turns an anxiety into a process.

PART C — CODING: Graph Reachability (20 min)

👨‍💻 Alex: *"Given a directed graph of data source dependencies (source A feeds source B, etc.), find all sources reachable from a given starting source. Detect cycles — they represent circular dependencies."*

👨 Khanh:

import java.util.*;

public class DependencyGraph {

    private final Map<String, List<String>> adj = new HashMap<>();

    public void addEdge(String from, String to) {
        adj.computeIfAbsent(from, k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(to);
        adj.putIfAbsent(to, new ArrayList<>());
    }

    /**
     * BFS: all nodes reachable from 'start' (excluding start itself).
     */
    public Set<String> reachable(String start) {
        Set<String> visited = new LinkedHashSet<>();
        Queue<String> queue = new ArrayDeque<>();
        queue.add(start);

        while (!queue.isEmpty()) {
            String node = queue.poll();
            for (String neighbour : adj.getOrDefault(node, List.of())) {
                if (!neighbour.equals(start) && visited.add(neighbour)) {
                    queue.add(neighbour);
                }
            }
        }
        return visited;
    }

    /**
     * DFS-based cycle detection using three-colour marking.
     * WHITE = unvisited, GRAY = in current path, BLACK = done
     * Returns list of cycles found (each cycle as ordered node list).
     */
    public List<List<String>> detectCycles() {
        Map<String, Color> color = new HashMap<>();
        adj.keySet().forEach(n -> color.put(n, Color.WHITE));

        List<List<String>> cycles = new ArrayList<>();
        Deque<String> path = new ArrayDeque<>();

        for (String node : adj.keySet()) {
            if (color.get(node) == Color.WHITE) {
                dfs(node, color, path, cycles);
            }
        }
        return cycles;
    }

    private void dfs(String node, Map<String, Color> color,
                     Deque<String> path, List<List<String>> cycles) {
        color.put(node, Color.GRAY);
        path.addLast(node);

        for (String neighbour : adj.getOrDefault(node, List.of())) {
            if (color.get(neighbour) == Color.GRAY) {
                // Back edge found → cycle
                List<String> cycle = new ArrayList<>();
                boolean inCycle = false;
                for (String n : path) {
                    if (n.equals(neighbour)) inCycle = true;
                    if (inCycle) cycle.add(n);
                }
                cycle.add(neighbour); // close the cycle
                cycles.add(cycle);
            } else if (color.get(neighbour) == Color.WHITE) {
                dfs(neighbour, color, path, cycles);
            }
        }

        path.removeLast();
        color.put(node, Color.BLACK);
    }

    enum Color { WHITE, GRAY, BLACK }
}

// Tests
@Test
void reachableFromStart() {
    var g = new DependencyGraph();
    g.addEdge("A", "B");
    g.addEdge("B", "C");
    g.addEdge("A", "D");

    assertThat(g.reachable("A"))
        .containsExactlyInAnyOrder("B", "C", "D");
}

@Test
void detectsCycle() {
    var g = new DependencyGraph();
    g.addEdge("A", "B");
    g.addEdge("B", "C");
    g.addEdge("C", "A"); // cycle: A→B→C→A

    List<List<String>> cycles = g.detectCycles();
    assertThat(cycles).hasSize(1);
    assertThat(cycles.get(0)).contains("A", "B", "C");
}

@Test
void noCycleInAcyclicGraph() {
    var g = new DependencyGraph();
    g.addEdge("A", "B");
    g.addEdge("B", "C");

    assertThat(g.detectCycles()).isEmpty();
}

@Test
void isolatedNodeHasNoReachable() {
    var g = new DependencyGraph();
    g.addEdge("A", "B");
    g.addEdge("C", "D"); // separate component

    assertThat(g.reachable("C")).containsExactly("D");
}

💬 ROUND 3 — JUDGMENT & CULTURE (30 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager.

👩 Diane (EM): Palantir has been controversial — some engineers publicly resigned over government contracts. How do you think about working for a company whose work you might not always agree with?

👨 Khanh: I think the honest answer requires separating two different questions: "Can I do good work here?" and "Do I agree with every decision this company makes?"

The second question, applied literally, would make it impossible to work anywhere. Every large company makes decisions that reasonable people disagree with.

The first question is the one I actually use. Can I do good work here — work I'm proud of — and does the organisation have mechanisms for engineers to raise concerns about specific uses of the technology?

If a contract or deployment crossed a line I considered clearly unethical — and I had raised it through legitimate channels without resolution — I'd leave. That's a real option, not a theoretical one. But I'd exercise it for a specific, concrete reason, not for a general discomfort with the mission.

The engineers who resigned made a choice I respect. What I'd push back on is the framing that discomfort with complexity equals having principles. Working in grey areas with clear personal standards is also a principled position.

👩 Diane: Palantir's culture values direct, blunt feedback. How do you give difficult feedback to a colleague without damaging the relationship?

👨 Khanh: Blunt and respectful aren't opposites — they only feel that way when blunt is mistaken for rude.

The formula I use: be specific about the behaviour, honest about the impact, and curious about the cause — in that order.

Specific: *"In the last three design reviews, you presented conclusions without walking through the tradeoffs."* Not: "You're not rigorous."

Honest about impact: *"It makes it hard for the team to trust the recommendation, because we don't know what alternatives were considered."*

Curious about cause: *"Is that intentional — do you prefer to share conclusions and discuss tradeoffs live? Or is something else going on?"*

The curious question matters because it leaves space for me to be wrong about my interpretation. Maybe they're under time pressure. Maybe they think the tradeoffs are obvious. Maybe there's context I don't have.

Blunt without that curiosity is just criticism. Blunt with it is a conversation that has a chance of actually changing something.

👩 Diane: Last question. You've been debugging a production issue for four hours. You've narrowed it down to two possible causes but can't definitively confirm which one. Your manager needs an update. What do you say?

👨 Khanh: I give an honest state-of-play, not a false certainty or a vague "still investigating."

*"I've been on this for four hours. The issue is definitely in the data ingestion pipeline — I've ruled out the API layer and the storage layer. I have two hypotheses left: either the source connector is silently dropping records with malformed timestamps, or the entity resolver is creating duplicate IDs under concurrent load. I believe it's more likely to be the first — I have one log entry that supports it — but I haven't been able to reproduce it reliably yet.*

*I need roughly two more hours to confirm. If I'm right about hypothesis one, the fix is a 30-minute patch. If I'm wrong and it's hypothesis two, that's a deeper concurrency bug — probably a day's work.*

*Do you want me to continue to confirmation, or would you prefer I implement a workaround now and investigate the root cause separately?"*

That update does three things: it shows progress, it gives a time estimate, and it hands the manager a decision — which is appropriate when the timeline matters for other stakeholders.

What I wouldn't say: "I'm still looking." That's technically true and practically useless.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Mission sensitivity 10/10 Nuanced view; asks about governance; not reflexive rejection or acceptance
Recruiter Customer-facing engineering 10/10 Immediate feedback loop; knowing when quick fix vs proper fix
Tech A Data integration pipeline 10/10 Entity resolution, conflict merge, DQ monitoring, source priority
Tech A+ ID migration without disruption 10/10 Stable internal UUID + mapping table; downstream sees zero change
Tech B CIO confidence conversation 10/10 Four DQ dimensions with specific numbers; recurring review offer
Tech C Coding: graph reachability + cycle detection 10/10 BFS reachable + 3-colour DFS, 4 tests
Culture A Controversial company mission 10/10 Principled nuance; specific trigger for leaving vs general discomfort
Culture B Debugging update to manager 10/10 Progress + two hypotheses + time estimate + decision handed back

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Palantir Interview Tips

  1. Entity resolution — deterministic (exact ID match) then probabilistic (name + DOB similarity)
  2. Stable internal IDs — never use source IDs as canonical; mapping table absorbs external changes
  3. Data quality = 4 dimensions — completeness, consistency, timeliness, accuracy
  4. 3-colour DFS for cycle detection — WHITE/GRAY/BLACK; back edge to GRAY = cycle
  5. Mission sensitivity — Palantir expects you to have thought about this; vague answers fail
  6. Debugging updates — progress + hypotheses + time estimate + decision for manager
  7. "Blunt without curiosity is just criticism" — the Palantir feedback philosophy in one line
  8. Palantir salaries (Senior/SWE-3): USD 180–260K base + significant equity; GovCloud roles may vary

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Mission "Powerful tools mean the governance around how they're used matters enormously."
Customer "The feedback loop when you're in a room with a user is immediate in a way that dashboards never are."
Data quality "Trust requires evidence, not reassurance."
DQ presentation "A recurring review turns an anxiety into a process."
Controversy "Working in grey areas with clear personal standards is also a principled position."
Feedback "Blunt without curiosity is just criticism. Blunt with it is a conversation."
Debug update "'I'm still looking' is technically true and practically useless."
Debug update "I hand the manager a decision — which is appropriate when the timeline matters."

🎭 Mock Interview #59 — Senior Java Engineer

Company: Figma (San Francisco / Remote, Collaborative Design Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer — Real-Time Collaboration team. Figma is the design tool used by 4M+ teams. Its core differentiator: true multiplayer — multiple designers editing the same file simultaneously, like Google Docs but for design. The backend handles real-time sync, version history, and rendering data for complex vector graphics. Tech: Java 17 (backend services), TypeScript (frontend), Rust (engine), WebSockets, CRDTs, PostgreSQL, AWS, Redis. Balance: 50% English communication · 50% technical depth. What makes Figma distinct: Real-time collaboration is the product. Latency, consistency, and conflict resolution are not edge cases — they're the entire problem. Engineers must deeply understand distributed state synchronisation. The culture is collaborative, design-forward, and strongly values engineers who can communicate with designers and PMs clearly.

📞 ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (15 min, English)

👩 Maya (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Figma's core feature is real-time multiplayer — multiple designers editing the same canvas at once. Why is that technically hard?

👨 Khanh: It's hard because of the consistency problem.

When two designers edit the same canvas simultaneously — one moves a rectangle, one changes its colour — their edits are generated independently on their own devices. The server receives them out of order due to network latency. The question is: what does each designer see?

If we just apply edits in arrival order, the two designers might see different final states — one sees rectangle moved then colour changed, the other sees colour changed then moved. That violates the expectation that they're in the same shared workspace.

The solution space involves either locking (only one person edits at a time — defeats the purpose) or conflict resolution algorithms like Operational Transformation (Google Docs' approach) or CRDTs — Conflict-free Replicated Data Types — which are designed so that any order of applying the same operations produces the same result. The catch is that designing a CRDT for hierarchical design data — nested layers, styles, components — is significantly harder than for plain text.

👩 Maya: Figma was acquired by Adobe for $20B, then the deal collapsed. How does that kind of external uncertainty affect how you'd think about joining?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, I'd treat it as a useful signal rather than a deterrent.

The deal collapsing means Figma remains an independent company with full control over its product direction. For engineers, independence often means faster decisions, clearer mission, and fewer integration politics. The alternative — being absorbed into a large incumbent — often introduces the exact constraints that slow down product development.

What I'd want to understand: did the Adobe acquisition attempt change anything about Figma's internal culture or hiring strategy? Companies that went through that level of external pressure and came out independent sometimes emerge with stronger clarity about what they're building and why. That's actually attractive.

The risk I'd assess isn't the Adobe situation — it's the standard early-IPO question: what does the growth trajectory look like, and is the current product still the one that justifies continued investment? Based on what I've read, Figma's market position looks strong.

💻 ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min, English)

Setting: Staff Engineer, Real-Time Platform.

PART A — Real-Time Sync Architecture (25 min)

👨‍💻 Sam (Staff Eng): Two designers are editing the same Figma file. Designer A moves a rectangle. Simultaneously, Designer B changes the same rectangle's fill colour. Both edits are sent to the server 50ms apart. Design the sync system so both designers see the correct final state.

👨 Khanh: This is a classic concurrent edit problem. The target state is: rectangle is both moved AND colour-changed, and both designers converge to that same final state.

Core model — operation-based CRDT:

Each edit is an immutable, timestamped operation with a vector clock for causal ordering:

public record Operation(
    String  operationId,    // UUID
    String  fileId,
    String  nodeId,         // which design element
    String  userId,
    OperationType type,     // MOVE | FILL | RESIZE | DELETE | etc.
    Map<String, Object> payload,
    VectorClock vectorClock,  // logical timestamp per user
    Instant wallClock
) {}

public record VectorClock(Map<String, Long> counters) {
    // A happens-before B if A.counters[u] <= B.counters[u] for all u
    public boolean happensBefore(VectorClock other) {
        return counters.entrySet().stream().allMatch(e ->
            e.getValue() <= other.counters().getOrDefault(e.getKey(), 0L));
    }

    public VectorClock increment(String userId) {
        Map<String, Long> next = new HashMap<>(counters);
        next.merge(userId, 1L, Long::sum);
        return new VectorClock(next);
    }
}

Server sync service:

@Service
public class SyncService {

    // In-memory operation log per file (or Redis for multi-instance)
    private final Map<String, List<Operation>> opLog =
        new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

    @MessageMapping("/file/{fileId}/ops")
    public void receiveOperation(@DestinationVariable String fileId,
                                  Operation op,
                                  SimpMessageHeaderAccessor headers) {

        // 1. Persist operation
        opLog.computeIfAbsent(fileId,
            k -> Collections.synchronizedList(new ArrayList<>())).add(op);
        persistenceService.save(op);

        // 2. Check for concurrent ops on same node (conflict candidates)
        List<Operation> concurrent = findConcurrent(fileId, op);

        // 3. Apply CRDT merge: for independent properties (move vs fill),
        //    both apply — no conflict. For same property from two users,
        //    last-write-wins by wall clock as tiebreaker.
        List<Operation> resolved = crdt.merge(op, concurrent);

        // 4. Broadcast resolved state to ALL clients in this file session
        messagingTemplate.convertAndSend(
            "/topic/file/" + fileId + "/ops",
            new BroadcastMessage(resolved, computeDocumentState(fileId))
        );
    }

    private List<Operation> findConcurrent(String fileId, Operation op) {
        return opLog.getOrDefault(fileId, List.of()).stream()
            .filter(o -> !o.operationId().equals(op.operationId()))
            .filter(o -> isConcurrent(o.vectorClock(), op.vectorClock()))
            .filter(o -> o.nodeId().equals(op.nodeId()))
            .toList();
    }

    // Concurrent: neither happens-before the other
    private boolean isConcurrent(VectorClock a, VectorClock b) {
        return !a.happensBefore(b) && !b.happensBefore(a);
    }
}

CRDT merge rules for design properties:

public class DesignCRDT {

    // Different properties on the same node = always composable
    // e.g., MOVE + FILL_COLOUR = apply both, no conflict
    public List<Operation> merge(Operation incoming,
                                  List<Operation> concurrent) {
        List<Operation> conflicts = concurrent.stream()
            .filter(c -> c.type() == incoming.type()) // same property
            .toList();

        if (conflicts.isEmpty()) {
            return List.of(incoming); // no conflict, apply as-is
        }

        // Same property conflict: last-write-wins by wall clock
        // Pick the operation with the latest timestamp
        Operation winner = Stream.concat(Stream.of(incoming),
                conflicts.stream())
            .max(Comparator.comparing(Operation::wallClock))
            .orElse(incoming);

        return List.of(winner);
    }
}

What the two designers see:

Designer A: MOVE rectangle to (200, 300)       @ t=0ms
Designer B: FILL colour = #FF0000               @ t=50ms
Server receives A first, then B.
Both are on different properties (position vs fill) → composable.
Final state: rectangle at (200,300) with fill #FF0000.
Both designers receive this broadcast → converge to same state. ✅

👨‍💻 Sam: A designer closes their laptop mid-edit for 2 hours, then reopens it. How does their client reconcile with 2 hours of operations they missed?

👨 Khanh: Reconnection sync — the client needs to catch up on operations that happened while it was offline.

// Client sends its last known operation ID on reconnect
// Server sends all operations since that point

@MessageMapping("/file/{fileId}/sync")
public void syncOnReconnect(@DestinationVariable String fileId,
                             SyncRequest req,
                             SimpMessageHeaderAccessor headers) {
    String userId = getUserId(headers);

    // Find all ops since client's last known state
    List<Operation> missed = opLog.getOrDefault(fileId, List.of())
        .stream()
        .filter(op -> op.wallClock().isAfter(req.getLastSeenAt()))
        .sorted(Comparator.comparing(Operation::wallClock))
        .toList();

    // Also check if client has pending local ops (offline edits)
    List<Operation> pending = req.getPendingOps();

    // Merge: apply missed server ops, then re-base client's pending ops
    List<Operation> rebaseResult = rebase(pending, missed);

    // Send full current document state + rebase instructions
    messagingTemplate.convertAndSendToUser(userId,
        "/queue/file/" + fileId + "/reconnect",
        new ReconnectResponse(
            computeDocumentState(fileId),
            rebaseResult
        )
    );
}

For 2 hours of missed ops — we don't replay 2 hours of individual operations. Instead: send the current canonical document state (snapshot), and the client replaces its local state entirely. Local pending edits are rebased on top of the snapshot.

This is the same pattern Git uses: git pull --rebase rather than replaying all commits since divergence.

PART B — COMMUNICATION: Explaining a Sync Bug to a Designer (15 min)

👨‍💻 Sam: A designer files a bug: *"I was editing with my colleague. I typed new text into a text layer. Then suddenly my text disappeared and my colleague's older text appeared instead. Figma lost my work."*

How do you investigate and respond?

👨 Khanh: This is a conflict resolution bug — a case where our last-write-wins logic picked the wrong winner. I'd investigate before responding to confirm the cause.

Internal investigation: Pull the operation log for their file session and find the two concurrent text-edit operations. Check the wall clock timestamps — if the client clocks were slightly out of sync, our server might have chosen the colleague's op as "later" even though the designer typed after. This is the classic problem with wall-clock tie-breaking: it assumes client clocks are reliable, which they aren't.

Response to the designer — honest and specific:

*"Thank you for the detailed report — this is really helpful. I've reviewed the operation log for your session.*

*What happened: you and your colleague both edited the same text layer within milliseconds of each other. Our system uses a 'last edit wins' rule for this case — but in your session, there was a small clock difference between your devices that caused your colleague's edit to be treated as 'later,' even though you typed after.*

*I want to be direct: this is a bug in our conflict resolution. Your work was not lost due to a network error — it was incorrectly overwritten by our own logic.*

*We're tracking this issue and working on a fix that uses server-assigned logical timestamps instead of device clocks, which eliminates the clock skew problem.*

*In the short term, Figma preserves your version history — you can recover your text from the 'Show version history' option in the File menu. I'm happy to walk you through that if it would help."*

What I wouldn't do: blame network conditions or tell them to "try again." This was our bug.

PART C — CODING: Last-Write-Wins Register (20 min)

👨‍💻 Sam: *"Implement a thread-safe LWW (Last Write Wins) register for a design property. Store a value with its timestamp. On write: only update if the new timestamp is strictly greater. On read: return current value. Thread-safe."*

👨 Khanh:

import java.util.concurrent.atomic.AtomicReference;

public class LwwRegister<T> {

    public record Entry<T>(T value, long timestampMs) {}

    // AtomicReference for lock-free thread safety
    private final AtomicReference<Entry<T>> ref;

    public LwwRegister(T initialValue, long initialTimestamp) {
        this.ref = new AtomicReference<>(
            new Entry<>(initialValue, initialTimestamp));
    }

    /**
     * Write value only if timestampMs > current timestamp.
     * Returns true if the write succeeded.
     */
    public boolean write(T value, long timestampMs) {
        while (true) {
            Entry<T> current = ref.get();
            if (timestampMs <= current.timestampMs()) {
                return false; // stale write — reject
            }
            Entry<T> next = new Entry<>(value, timestampMs);
            if (ref.compareAndSet(current, next)) {
                return true; // CAS succeeded
            }
            // CAS failed = another thread updated concurrently → retry
        }
    }

    /**
     * Read current value.
     */
    public T read() {
        return ref.get().value();
    }

    public long timestamp() {
        return ref.get().timestampMs();
    }
}

// Tests
@Test
void acceptsNewerWrite() {
    var reg = new LwwRegister<>("red", 100L);
    assertThat(reg.write("blue", 200L)).isTrue();
    assertThat(reg.read()).isEqualTo("blue");
}

@Test
void rejectsOlderWrite() {
    var reg = new LwwRegister<>("red", 200L);
    assertThat(reg.write("blue", 100L)).isFalse();
    assertThat(reg.read()).isEqualTo("red");
}

@Test
void rejectsEqualTimestamp() {
    var reg = new LwwRegister<>("red", 100L);
    assertThat(reg.write("blue", 100L)).isFalse();
    assertThat(reg.read()).isEqualTo("red"); // original preserved
}

@Test
void threadSafeUnderConcurrency() throws InterruptedException {
    var reg = new LwwRegister<>(0, 0L);
    int threads = 100;
    var latch = new java.util.concurrent.CountDownLatch(threads);

    for (int i = 1; i <= threads; i++) {
        final long ts = i;
        Thread.ofVirtual().start(() -> {
            reg.write((int) ts, ts);
            latch.countDown();
        });
    }
    latch.await();
    assertThat(reg.read()).isEqualTo(100); // highest timestamp wins
    assertThat(reg.timestamp()).isEqualTo(100L);
}

💬 ROUND 3 — CULTURE & CRAFT (30 min, English)

Setting: Engineering Manager.

👩 Priya (EM): Figma's users are designers, not engineers. How does that change how you build and communicate?

👨 Khanh: It changes two things fundamentally: the vocabulary I use and the empathy I bring to the problem.

Designers experience software differently from engineers. An engineer dealing with a bug thinks about root cause. A designer dealing with the same bug thinks "is my work safe? Can I still deliver?" Those are different questions, and the response needs to address both.

When communicating with designers about a technical limitation or bug: lead with what it means for their work, not how the system works. "Your text might not sync correctly if you're offline" matters more than "our CRDT doesn't support causal consistency under partition." Both are true; only one is useful to them.

The other change: I'd actively seek out designers as the primary users of anything I build. Not just product managers' interpretation of what designers need — direct observation. What are they doing in Figma when they're frustrated? Where do they lose work? Where does the interface feel slow or unreliable? Those are the engineering problems worth solving.

Engineers who build for users they've never observed make different mistakes than engineers who have.

👩 Priya: Your team is split on a technical approach — half want to use CRDTs for a new collaboration feature, half want to use Operational Transformation. You have a strong opinion. How do you facilitate the decision without steamrolling?

👨 Khanh: I try to separate two things: helping the team reach a well-informed decision, and advocating for my own position.

First step: make sure both sides are understood clearly. I'd write up both approaches — not as "my way vs wrong way," but as a fair comparison: what each approach is good at, where it breaks down, what it would cost to implement, and what our specific constraints are. That document should be readable by someone who hasn't followed the debate.

Second: identify the deciding criteria. For a collaboration feature, I'd push the team to agree on the questions first — "which approach handles our specific data types better?", "which is easier to debug in production?", "which has more implementation examples we can learn from?" — before arguing the answers. Agreeing on criteria often resolves the disagreement faster than arguing from positions.

Third — my opinion: I'd advocate for CRDTs specifically because our data model is hierarchical (nested layers, groups, components), and OT is notoriously hard to extend to tree structures. I'd make that case clearly and with evidence.

Fourth — disagree and commit: if the team reaches a different conclusion through the process, I'd commit to it fully. A decision made with good process and good information that I disagree with is still a decision I'll build well. I'd save my "I told you so" for the retrospective, if it ever comes to that — and even then, I'd frame it as a learning, not a score.

📊 FULL SCORECARD

Round Category Score Notes
Recruiter Real-time sync fundamentals 10/10 CRDTs vs OT; vector clocks; composable vs conflicting ops
Recruiter Adobe acquisition context 10/10 Independent = faster decisions; assess growth, not external drama
Tech A Real-time sync architecture 10/10 Vector clock ops, concurrent detection, CRDT merge, broadcast
Tech A+ Reconnection reconciliation 10/10 Snapshot + rebase; Git pull analogy; avoid replaying 2h of ops
Tech B Sync bug for designer 10/10 Own the bug; explain clock skew clearly; version history recovery path
Tech C Coding: LWW register 10/10 AtomicReference CAS loop, 4 tests including concurrent thread safety
Culture A Building for designers 10/10 Lead with work impact not system mechanics; observe real users
Culture B CRDT vs OT team split 10/10 Fair write-up → decide criteria first → advocate clearly → commit

🏆 VERDICT: STRONG HIRE

💡 Figma Interview Tips

  1. Vector clocks for causality — A happens-before B if all of A's counters ≤ B's; concurrent if neither
  2. CRDTs for design — different properties on same node = composable; same property = LWW with server timestamp
  3. LWW register = AtomicReference + CAS loop — lock-free, thread-safe, retry on CAS failure
  4. Reconnection = snapshot + rebase — don't replay 2h of ops; send current state, rebase local pending
  5. Clock skew is a real bug — client wall clocks are unreliable; use server-assigned logical timestamps
  6. Designer communication — "is my work safe?" before "here's how the system works"
  7. Team disagreements — write up both sides fairly → agree on criteria → advocate → commit
  8. Figma salaries (Senior): USD 180–260K base + RSU; strong remote-first culture

📝 Strong English Phrases from This Interview

Context Phrase
Real-time sync "CRDTs are designed so any order of applying the same operations produces the same result."
CRDTs "Different properties = composable. Same property = last-write-wins with server timestamp."
Reconnection "Send the snapshot; the client replaces its local state. Same pattern as 'git pull --rebase'."
Bug response "This is a bug in our conflict resolution. Your work was not lost — it was incorrectly overwritten by our own logic."
Designer comms "Lead with what it means for their work, not how the system works."
User empathy "Engineers who build for users they've never observed make different mistakes."
Team decision "Agree on the criteria before arguing the answers."
Commit "A decision made with good process that I disagree with is still a decision I'll build well."

🎭 Mock Interview #60 — Stripe

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Payments Infrastructure

Format: Full Loop (Recruiter → Technical → System Design → Behavioral)

Context: Stripe is the global payments infrastructure company — processing hundreds of billions of dollars annually for millions of businesses. The Backend Infrastructure team builds the systems that move money: payment processing, idempotency, event delivery, ledger consistency, and developer APIs. This is one of the highest-stakes backend engineering environments in the world. Every bug costs real money.

ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (30 min)

👩 Sophie (Recruiter): Hi Khanh! Thanks for applying. I'm Sophie, engineering recruiter at Stripe. Before we dive in — Stripe's mission is to increase the GDP of the internet. The role you applied for is on our Payments Infrastructure team. Can you start by telling me about your background?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. I'm a Java backend engineer with about 4.5 years of experience. I started in healthcare IT building patient data systems — strict compliance, audit trails, zero-downtime expectations. Then I moved to a middleware platform handling around 15,000 API calls per day, sitting between a customer-facing app and a global account system.

What draws me specifically to Stripe's infrastructure team: I've worked at the edges of payment systems — token exchange, auth flows, webhook delivery — and I want to go deeper. Build the core, not just the wrapper.

👩 Sophie: That's a compelling reason. What makes you confident you can handle the scale and reliability requirements we have here? Stripe's systems process millions of API calls per minute.

👨 Khanh: Honest answer: I haven't operated at Stripe's scale yet. But I've built the patterns that scale requires: idempotent APIs, retry-safe event processing, circuit breakers, distributed tracing. The thinking is the same — the operational complexity is higher, and that's exactly what I'm here to learn by doing.

I'm not pretending I have 10 years of payments infrastructure experience. But I'm technically rigorous, I care deeply about correctness, and I learn fast in high-stakes environments.

👩 Sophie: Refreshingly honest. We appreciate that. Compensation — Stripe is competitive. We do base + equity. What range are you targeting?

👨 Khanh: For a senior backend role with this scope, I'm targeting a base in the $X–$Y range, and I understand Stripe's equity is meaningful. I'm more focused on the role and growth trajectory than the exact number — if the fit is right, I'm flexible.

👩 Sophie: Good. I'll move you forward. Next step is a technical screen with one of our engineers. Anything you want to know about the team?

👨 Khanh: One question: for someone joining Payments Infrastructure — what does the first 90 days look like? Is it structured onboarding, or more "find a bug and fix it"?

👩 Sophie: It's both. You'll shadow oncall in week two, own a small improvement project by week four, and be expected to design something non-trivial by month three. It's fast, intentional, and supported.

ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL SCREEN (60 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus (Senior Engineer): Hey Khanh — I'm Marcus, I work on the payment processing core. Today we'll do a Java/Spring deep dive and some live coding. Let's start.

Part A — Java & Spring (20 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: At Stripe, money movement is sacred. If we debit an account, we must credit an account. No exceptions, no lost events. With that in mind: how would you implement an idempotent payment API in Spring Boot?

👨 Khanh: Idempotency is the most critical property for payment APIs. Here's how I'd implement it:

The contract: every payment request carries a client-generated Idempotency-Key header. If we receive the same key twice, we return the same response as the first call — without re-processing.

Implementation:

@RestController
@RequestMapping("/v1/payments")
public class PaymentController {

    private final IdempotencyStore idempotencyStore;
    private final PaymentService paymentService;

    @PostMapping
    public ResponseEntity<PaymentResponse> createPayment(
            @RequestHeader("Idempotency-Key") String idempotencyKey,
            @RequestBody PaymentRequest request) {

        // 1. Check cache first
        Optional<PaymentResponse> cached = idempotencyStore.get(idempotencyKey);
        if (cached.isPresent()) {
            return ResponseEntity.ok(cached.get()); // replay stored response
        }

        // 2. Lock to prevent concurrent duplicate requests
        boolean acquired = idempotencyStore.acquireLock(idempotencyKey);
        if (!acquired) {
            throw new ConflictException("Request with this key is in progress");
        }

        try {
            PaymentResponse response = paymentService.process(request);
            // 3. Store result durably BEFORE returning
            idempotencyStore.store(idempotencyKey, response, Duration.ofDays(7));
            return ResponseEntity.ok(response);
        } finally {
            idempotencyStore.releaseLock(idempotencyKey);
        }
    }
}

The store: backed by Redis for fast lookup, with a TTL of 7 days (Stripe's actual window). The lock prevents the "double-charge on concurrent retry" edge case — two identical requests arriving simultaneously both check the cache, both miss, and both process.

The critical ordering: store the result BEFORE returning the response. If we return first and crash before storing, the next retry will re-process.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Good. What happens if the store write fails after the payment processes but before we return?

👨 Khanh: That's the hard case. The money moved, but we couldn't store the idempotency record. Options:

  1. Compensating transaction: roll back the payment. But this is

expensive and not always possible (especially with external systems).

  1. Write-ahead log: record the intent to process before we process,

with a status of IN_PROGRESS. On retry, if we see IN_PROGRESS, we check the payment processor directly for the result. This is the pattern Stripe actually uses internally.

  1. Accept the risk with monitoring: for very low probability failures,

accept that the customer might be charged twice and have a reconciliation process. Not acceptable for payments, but fine for analytics events.

For payments: write-ahead log + reconciliation is the right answer.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Let's talk about @Transactional in Spring. You're processing a payment that involves: 1) creating a payment record, 2) calling an external payment processor, 3) updating a ledger. What's your transactional strategy?

👨 Khanh: This is the "don't wrap external calls in a transaction" lesson.

@Service
public class PaymentService {

    // ❌ WRONG — holds DB transaction open during external HTTP call
    @Transactional
    public PaymentResponse processWrong(PaymentRequest req) {
        Payment payment = paymentRepo.save(new Payment(req)); // DB tx open
        ProcessorResponse result = externalProcessor.charge(req); // network call
        ledgerService.credit(payment.getId(), result); // still in tx
        return mapResponse(result);
    }

    // ✅ CORRECT — DB tx as short as possible
    public PaymentResponse processCorrect(PaymentRequest req) {
        // 1. Save intent (short transaction)
        Payment payment = createPaymentRecord(req);

        // 2. Call external processor (outside any transaction)
        ProcessorResponse result;
        try {
            result = externalProcessor.charge(req);
        } catch (ProcessorException e) {
            markPaymentFailed(payment.getId(), e);
            throw e;
        }

        // 3. Update outcome (short transaction)
        return finalizePayment(payment.getId(), result);
    }

    @Transactional
    private Payment createPaymentRecord(PaymentRequest req) {
        return paymentRepo.save(Payment.builder()
            .status(PaymentStatus.PENDING)
            .idempotencyKey(req.getIdempotencyKey())
            .amount(req.getAmount())
            .currency(req.getCurrency())
            .build());
    }

    @Transactional
    private PaymentResponse finalizePayment(String paymentId,
                                             ProcessorResponse result) {
        Payment payment = paymentRepo.findById(paymentId).orElseThrow();
        payment.setStatus(result.isSuccess() ? SUCCEEDED : FAILED);
        payment.setProcessorId(result.getProcessorTransactionId());
        ledgerService.record(payment);
        return mapResponse(payment);
    }
}

Holding a DB connection open during an external HTTP call (which could take 500ms–5s) is a connection pool killer. At scale, you'd exhaust the pool fast. Keep transactions short and atomic around DB work only.

Part B — Live Coding (40 min)

👨‍💻 Marcus: Here's a problem based on something real we deal with.

You have a stream of payment events. Each event has an accountId, type (CREDIT or DEBIT), and amount. Write a method that, given a list of events, returns the final balance per account. Then extend it to return accounts that have gone negative at any point — even if their final balance is positive.

👨 Khanh: Let me think through this step by step.

import java.math.BigDecimal;
import java.util.*;

public class LedgerAnalyzer {

    public enum EventType { CREDIT, DEBIT }

    public record PaymentEvent(
        String accountId,
        EventType type,
        BigDecimal amount  // always positive
    ) {}

    // Part 1: Final balance per account
    public Map<String, BigDecimal> finalBalances(List<PaymentEvent> events) {
        Map<String, BigDecimal> balances = new HashMap<>();
        for (PaymentEvent event : events) {
            balances.merge(
                event.accountId(),
                event.type() == EventType.CREDIT
                    ? event.amount()
                    : event.amount().negate(),
                BigDecimal::add
            );
        }
        return balances;
    }

    // Part 2: Accounts that went negative at any point
    public Set<String> accountsEverNegative(List<PaymentEvent> events) {
        Map<String, BigDecimal> runningBalance = new HashMap<>();
        Set<String> everNegative = new HashSet<>();

        for (PaymentEvent event : events) {
            BigDecimal delta = event.type() == EventType.CREDIT
                ? event.amount()
                : event.amount().negate();

            BigDecimal newBalance = runningBalance.merge(
                event.accountId(), delta, BigDecimal::add
            );

            if (newBalance.compareTo(BigDecimal.ZERO) < 0) {
                everNegative.add(event.accountId());
            }
        }
        return everNegative;
    }
}

Key decisions:

  • BigDecimal not double — money requires exact decimal arithmetic.

0.1 + 0.2 == 0.30000000000000004 with doubles. Unacceptable for financial systems.

  • Map.merge() — cleaner than get-null-check-put pattern.
  • Stream events sequentially — order matters for the "ever negative"

check. Parallel streams would require synchronization.

👨‍💻 Marcus: Good instinct on BigDecimal. Now scale it: you have 100 million events per day. How would you process this efficiently?

👨 Khanh: At that scale, in-memory Map won't work across a distributed system. My architecture:

Kafka + partitioned consumers:

Payment Events → Kafka Topic (partitioned by accountId)
                     ↓
              Consumer Group (each partition → one consumer)
                     ↓
              Running balance per account (in Redis)
                     ↓
              Ledger DB (append-only, source of truth)

Partitioning by accountId means all events for one account go to the same partition, maintaining ordering — critical for correct balance computation. Each consumer maintains a running total in Redis with INCRBYFLOAT (or better, store as integer cents to avoid float issues).

Exactly-once semantics: use Kafka transactions + idempotent producers. Write the balance update and the Kafka consumer offset in the same transaction. That way, if the consumer crashes after updating Redis but before committing the offset, re-processing produces the same result.

ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min)

👩‍💻 Priya (Principal Engineer): Hey Khanh. Let's design something core to Stripe: a webhook delivery system. When a payment succeeds or fails, we need to notify merchants via HTTP POST to their registered endpoint. Requirements:

  • At-least-once delivery guaranteed
  • Retry with exponential backoff
  • Per-merchant delivery ordering not required
  • 99.9% delivery success rate
  • Scale: 10 million events/day, peak 5,000 events/second

How would you design this?

👨 Khanh: Let me think through this. Webhook delivery is deceptively hard — you're making outbound HTTP calls to endpoints you don't control, to servers that might be slow, down, or returning errors.

High-level architecture:

Payment Service
      │ publishes event
      ▼
 Kafka Topic: "payment-events"
      │
      ▼
 Webhook Dispatcher Service (consumers)
      │ reads events, enqueues delivery tasks
      ▼
 Delivery Queue (Redis Sorted Set or Kafka)
      │ scored by next_attempt_at timestamp
      ▼
 Worker Pool (HTTP senders)
      │ HTTP POST to merchant endpoint
      ▼
 Delivery Log (PostgreSQL)
      │ records attempt + outcome
      ▼
 Retry Scheduler
      │ re-enqueues failed deliveries with backoff

The delivery queue: I'd use a Redis Sorted Set with the score being the next_attempt_at Unix timestamp. Workers poll for items where score <= now. This gives us natural priority ordering and efficient range queries.

Retry strategy with exponential backoff:

@Service
public class WebhookRetryPolicy {

    private static final int MAX_ATTEMPTS = 15;

    // Stripe's actual backoff: 1m, 5m, 30m, 2h, 5h, 10h, ...
    public Duration getNextDelay(int attemptNumber) {
        long seconds = switch (attemptNumber) {
            case 1  -> 60;        // 1 minute
            case 2  -> 300;       // 5 minutes
            case 3  -> 1_800;     // 30 minutes
            case 4  -> 7_200;     // 2 hours
            case 5  -> 18_000;    // 5 hours
            default -> Math.min(
                (long) Math.pow(2, attemptNumber) * 60,
                72 * 3600         // cap at 72 hours
            );
        };
        // Add jitter ±20% to avoid thundering herd
        long jitter = (long) (seconds * 0.2 * (Math.random() - 0.5));
        return Duration.ofSeconds(seconds + jitter);
    }

    public boolean shouldRetry(int attempts, HttpStatus status) {
        if (attempts >= MAX_ATTEMPTS) return false;
        // Don't retry permanent failures
        if (status == HttpStatus.GONE) return false;        // endpoint deleted
        if (status.is4xxClientError() &&
            status != HttpStatus.TOO_MANY_REQUESTS) return false;
        return true; // retry 5xx, network errors, 429
    }
}

At-least-once guarantee: the event is committed to the delivery log BEFORE we mark the Kafka offset as consumed. If the worker crashes after a successful HTTP call but before updating the log, we re-deliver. The merchant endpoint must be idempotent (we include a Stripe-Signature and event ID so they can deduplicate).

Handling slow merchant endpoints: we set a hard timeout of 30 seconds. Tarpitting (intentionally slow endpoints) would exhaust our worker threads without timeouts. We also apply per-merchant rate limiting — one bad merchant can't block delivery to others.

👩‍💻 Priya: Good. What's your bottleneck at 5,000 events/second peak?

👨 Khanh: The HTTP workers. Each outbound call can take 100ms–10s. To sustain 5,000/s, if each call averages 500ms, I need:

5,000 req/s × 0.5s = 2,500 concurrent connections

That means a large worker pool using async/reactive HTTP clients — Spring WebClient or Netty-based non-blocking I/O. Blocking HTTP clients (RestTemplate, OkHttp with a thread per request) would need 2,500 threads, which is expensive.

Alternative: Separate fast and slow merchants. Merchants with consistently fast endpoints (< 100ms) and merchants with consistently slow ones go into different queue partitions with different worker pools. Fast merchants don't get blocked by slow ones.

👩‍💻 Priya: One more — how do you handle the case where a merchant's endpoint is down for 3 days?

👨 Khanh: Three concerns:

  1. Queue depth: with exponential backoff, events waiting for retry

with 72-hour intervals don't pile up fast. But we still need a maximum event age. I'd set 72 hours as the abandonment threshold — consistent with Stripe's actual policy.

  1. Merchant notification: proactively email the merchant after

consecutive failures (e.g., after 5 failed attempts) — not just when they stop getting events. Give them time to fix their endpoint.

  1. Event replay: once their endpoint recovers, they can call our

API to replay events from a specific timestamp. We store all events durably in S3 (with 30-day retention), so replay is cheap.

ROUND 4 — BEHAVIORAL (30 min)

👩 Jordan (Engineering Manager): Hi Khanh. I'm Jordan, manager of the Payments Infrastructure team. I care a lot about how engineers work under pressure and how they communicate when things go wrong. Let's talk.

👩 Jordan: Tell me about a time you caused a production incident. What happened and what did you do?

👨 Khanh: Yes — and I think this is actually an important question to answer honestly.

About 8 months into my middleware role, I deployed a configuration change that adjusted the timeout on our downstream API client from 30 seconds to 3 seconds. My reasoning was good — 30 seconds was too long and was causing thread pool exhaustion. But I deployed on a Friday afternoon and didn't account for a batch process that ran on Friday evenings and made large requests that legitimately took 8–15 seconds.

The batch started at 7 PM. Within 20 minutes, our error rate spiked to 40%. I got paged, figured out the cause within about 15 minutes, rolled back the config, and the system recovered within 5 minutes of rollback. Total impact: about 40 minutes of degraded service.

What I did wrong: deployed on Friday afternoon, didn't check for existing long-running processes before changing timeouts, no staged rollout. What I changed after: we added a deployment freeze on Friday afternoons, I wrote a runbook for timeout changes specifically, and I added a query to check for active long-running operations before any timeout configuration change.

👩 Jordan: How did you communicate during the incident?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, my communication during the incident was not great. I was heads-down debugging for about 25 minutes before I updated anyone. That was wrong. My team lead found out about the incident from the monitoring dashboard, not from me.

Afterwards, I changed my approach. Now during any incident affecting customers, I post a status update in Slack every 10 minutes, even if the update is just "still investigating, no resolution yet." People need to know you're working on it. Silence during an incident is the worst form of communication.

👩 Jordan: We work across many timezones and teams at Stripe. Tell me about a time you had to influence a technical decision you didn't own.

👨 Khanh: In my current project, a team from the client's side wanted to use a polling approach to sync account state — they'd call our API every 30 seconds to check for changes. Our team wanted to use event-driven webhooks instead.

I didn't own the decision — the client's team did. So I prepared a short technical document comparing both approaches: latency characteristics, load on both systems, failure modes, and migration path. I shared it with both teams and asked for a meeting to discuss the trade-offs.

What made the difference: I framed it as "here are the costs of polling at scale" rather than "webhooks are better." I showed them a calculation — 30-second polling across 10,000 accounts = 20 API calls/second permanently, even during quiet periods. That made the trade-off concrete.

They chose webhooks. But more importantly, the client's team felt like they'd made the decision based on information, not pressure. That mattered for the working relationship.

👩 Jordan: One final question — where do you want to be in three years?

👨 Khanh: I want to be an engineer who can design and own a complex distributed system end-to-end — from initial design, through implementation, to production operations. Not just write good code, but understand the full lifecycle: reliability, cost, scalability, observability.

At Stripe, I think that means becoming someone who can work on the hardest consistency problems in payments — the ones where getting it wrong costs real money and damages trust. I want to be the engineer other engineers come to when they're not sure if their design handles the edge cases.

📊 POST-INTERVIEW ANALYSIS

Scorecard

Dimension Score Notes
Technical Depth ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ BigDecimal, idempotency, transactional strategy all correct
System Design ⭐⭐⭐⭐½ Webhook system solid, good bottleneck analysis
Problem Solving ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Correct instincts on every hard case
Communication ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Honest about incident, clear technical framing
Behavioral ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Owned mistakes, specific learnings, no BS
Overall Strong Hire Ready for Stripe's bar

💎 Power Phrases (Stripe-Specific)

Context Phrase
On reliability "Money movement is sacred — if we debit, we must credit. No lost events."
On idempotency "Store the result before returning the response — not after."
On transactions "Keep DB transactions as short as possible. Never hold a connection open during an external call."
On BigDecimal "Money requires exact decimal arithmetic. 0.1 + 0.2 with doubles is wrong by definition."
On incidents "Silence during an incident is the worst form of communication."
On influence "I framed it as trade-off costs, not opinions. That made it their decision, not mine."

⚡ Key Technical Patterns Demonstrated

  1. Idempotency — write-ahead log, distributed lock, TTL-based cache
  2. Transaction boundaries — never wrap external calls in DB transactions
