  3. Webhook reliability — Kafka + Redis sorted set + exponential backoff with jitter
  4. Financial arithmetic — BigDecimal, integer cents for Redis
  5. Capacity math — 5,000 req/s × 500ms avg = 2,500 concurrent workers
  6. At-least-once delivery — commit offset after, not before, confirmation

🎭 Mock Interview #61 — Coinbase

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Trading Infrastructure

Format: Full Loop (Recruiter → Technical → System Design → Behavioral)

Context: Coinbase is the largest regulated cryptocurrency exchange in the US, processing billions of dollars in daily trading volume. The Trading Infrastructure team builds the order matching engine, real-time price feeds, ledger systems, and settlement pipelines that power millions of trades. Engineering here means extreme consistency requirements, microsecond-sensitive latency, and zero tolerance for money loss — in a domain that operates 24/7/365 with no scheduled maintenance windows.

ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (30 min)

👩 Aisha (Technical Recruiter): Hey Khanh! I'm Aisha from Coinbase's engineering recruiting team. Before we jump in, a quick note: Coinbase has a strong engineering culture around ownership — engineers here are expected to take a feature from design to production and own it in oncall. With that said, tell me about yourself.

👨 Khanh: Happy to. I'm a Java backend engineer with 4.5 years of experience — starting in healthcare IT where correctness was non-negotiable, then into middleware handling authentication, API routing, and event processing at around 15,000 requests per day.

What draws me to Coinbase specifically: I've been working at the integration layer of financial systems, and I want to go deeper into the core. Trading infrastructure — order matching, ledger consistency, settlement — those are the problems where getting it wrong has immediate, visible consequences. I want to work where that pressure is real.

👩 Aisha: I like that framing. Crypto specifically — are you comfortable with the volatility in the industry? The past few years have been turbulent.

👨 Khanh: I'm realistic about it. What I focus on is this: regardless of what price BTC is trading at, someone needs to build reliable, correct infrastructure to execute those trades. The engineering problems don't go away in a bear market. If anything, the stress-testing of systems is more intense when volumes spike during volatile periods.

I'm here for the engineering challenge, not just the industry hype.

👩 Aisha: Fair answer. Compensation — are you familiar with Coinbase's structure? We offer base, bonus, and equity in both COIN stock and sometimes crypto options.

👨 Khanh: I'm familiar with the structure. For a senior backend role of this scope, I'm targeting a total package around $X–$Y in cash equivalent. I understand equity here has unique characteristics and I'm open to discussing the mix.

👩 Aisha: Good. Process is four rounds: technical screen, two system design rounds, and behavioral. Moving you forward — good luck.

ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL SCREEN (60 min)

👨‍💻 Daniel (Senior Engineer): Hey Khanh — I'm Daniel, I work on the order book service. Today: Java/Spring concepts, then live coding. Let's go.

Part A — Java Deep Dive (20 min)

👨‍💻 Daniel: At Coinbase, our order book is a critical, high-throughput data structure. We process thousands of orders per second and need to match buyers and sellers as fast as possible. Starting with fundamentals: **explain the difference between HashMap, TreeMap, and LinkedHashMap and when you'd choose each.**

👨 Khanh: All three implement Map, but they have different performance and ordering characteristics:

HashMap — O(1) average for get/put, O(n) worst case with hash collisions. No ordering guaranteed. Choose this for pure lookup performance when order doesn't matter — configuration caches, ID-to-object lookups.

TreeMap — O(log n) for get/put, backed by a Red-Black tree. Keys are sorted in natural order or by a custom Comparator. Choose this when you need range queries or ordered iteration — for example, finding all orders with price between $45,000 and $46,000:

TreeMap<BigDecimal, Queue<Order>> bids = new TreeMap<>(Comparator.reverseOrder());
// Get the best bid and all bids above $45,000
NavigableMap<BigDecimal, Queue<Order>> range = bids.tailMap(new BigDecimal("45000"));
BigDecimal bestBidPrice = bids.firstKey(); // O(log n)

This is exactly what an order book uses internally — TreeMap keyed by price level, value is a queue of orders at that price.

LinkedHashMap — O(1) for get/put like HashMap, but maintains insertion order (or access order if configured). Choose for LRU caches:

new LinkedHashMap<K, V>(capacity, 0.75f, true) { // accessOrder=true
    protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K,V> eldest) {
        return size() > MAX_SIZE; // evict on overflow
    }
};

For an order book: TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> on both bid and ask sides. Bids sorted descending (highest price first), asks ascending. Match when bestBid >= bestAsk.

👨‍💻 Daniel: Good. Let's talk concurrency. Our order book receives orders from multiple threads simultaneously. What are your options for making it thread-safe, and what are the trade-offs?

👨 Khanh: Several approaches, each with different trade-offs:

Option 1: synchronized method

public synchronized boolean placeOrder(Order order) {
    // entire method locked
}

Simple but coarse-grained. One thread at a time across all operations. For an order book, this means reads (market data queries) block writes and vice versa — bad for throughput.

Option 2: ReadWriteLock

private final ReadWriteLock lock = new ReentrantReadWriteLock();

public void placeOrder(Order order) {
    lock.writeLock().lock();
    try { /* mutate order book */ }
    finally { lock.writeLock().unlock(); }
}

public BigDecimal getBestBid() {
    lock.readLock().lock();
    try { return bids.firstKey(); }
    finally { lock.readLock().unlock(); }
}

Better — multiple concurrent reads, exclusive writes. Good for read-heavy workloads like a market data feed.

Option 3: Single-threaded with queue (the real answer)

// Orders come in from multiple threads via a queue
private final BlockingQueue<OrderCommand> commandQueue =
    new LinkedBlockingQueue<>();

// Single dedicated thread processes all commands
private void runMatchingEngine() {
    while (true) {
        OrderCommand cmd = commandQueue.take(); // blocks until available
        processCommand(cmd); // no locks needed — single threaded
    }
}

This is the pattern real exchange matching engines use. The order book itself needs zero synchronization because only one thread ever touches it. You trade a bit of latency (queue round-trip) for massive simplification and eliminating lock contention entirely. LMAX Disruptor is a lock-free variant of this pattern used in production trading systems.

👨‍💻 Daniel: Which would you use at Coinbase?

👨 Khanh: The single-threaded command queue, without question. It eliminates an entire class of bugs — no race conditions, no deadlocks, no lock ordering issues. The order book is a stateful, sequential process by nature: order A arrives before order B, so A should be processed first. A queue preserves that invariant naturally. With ReadWriteLock, you still have to reason about every edge case in concurrent access.

Part B — Live Coding (40 min)

👨‍💻 Daniel: Classic trading problem. Implement a simplified order book that supports:

  1. placeOrder(side, price, quantity) — add a buy or sell order
  2. cancelOrder(orderId) — remove an order by ID
  3. getBestBid() / getBestAsk() — O(log n) retrieval
  4. match() — execute trades when bid >= ask

👨 Khanh: Let me build this up:

import java.math.BigDecimal;
import java.util.*;

public class OrderBook {

    public enum Side { BUY, SELL }

    public record Order(
        String id,
        Side side,
        BigDecimal price,
        int quantity
    ) {}

    public record Trade(
        String buyOrderId,
        String sellOrderId,
        BigDecimal price,
        int quantity
    ) {}

    // Bids: highest price first (buyers want to buy as cheap as possible,
    // but the best bid is the HIGHEST price a buyer will pay)
    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> bids =
        new TreeMap<>(Comparator.reverseOrder());

    // Asks: lowest price first (sellers want to sell as high as possible,
    // but the best ask is the LOWEST price a seller will accept)
    private final TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> asks =
        new TreeMap<>();

    // For O(1) cancellation
    private final Map<String, Order> orderIndex = new HashMap<>();

    public void placeOrder(Order order) {
        orderIndex.put(order.id(), order);
        TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> book =
            order.side() == Side.BUY ? bids : asks;
        book.computeIfAbsent(order.price(), k -> new ArrayDeque<>())
            .offer(order);
    }

    public boolean cancelOrder(String orderId) {
        Order order = orderIndex.remove(orderId);
        if (order == null) return false;
        TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> book =
            order.side() == Side.BUY ? bids : asks;
        Deque<Order> level = book.get(order.price());
        if (level != null) {
            level.remove(order); // O(n) at price level, acceptable
            if (level.isEmpty()) book.remove(order.price());
        }
        return true;
    }

    public Optional<BigDecimal> getBestBid() {
        return bids.isEmpty() ? Optional.empty()
                              : Optional.of(bids.firstKey());
    }

    public Optional<BigDecimal> getBestAsk() {
        return asks.isEmpty() ? Optional.empty()
                              : Optional.of(asks.firstKey());
    }

    public List<Trade> match() {
        List<Trade> trades = new ArrayList<>();

        while (!bids.isEmpty() && !asks.isEmpty()) {
            BigDecimal bestBid = bids.firstKey();
            BigDecimal bestAsk = asks.firstKey();

            // No match possible
            if (bestBid.compareTo(bestAsk) < 0) break;

            Deque<Order> bidQueue = bids.get(bestBid);
            Deque<Order> askQueue = asks.get(bestAsk);

            Order bid = bidQueue.peek();
            Order ask = askQueue.peek();

            // Trade executes at the resting order's price (ask price here)
            int tradeQty = Math.min(bid.quantity(), ask.quantity());
            trades.add(new Trade(bid.id(), ask.id(), bestAsk, tradeQty));

            // Update or remove orders
            updateOrRemove(bids, bestBid, bidQueue, bid, tradeQty);
            updateOrRemove(asks, bestAsk, askQueue, ask, tradeQty);
        }
        return trades;
    }

    private void updateOrRemove(TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>> book,
                                BigDecimal price,
                                Deque<Order> queue,
                                Order order,
                                int tradedQty) {
        if (order.quantity() == tradedQty) {
            queue.poll();
            orderIndex.remove(order.id());
            if (queue.isEmpty()) book.remove(price);
        } else {
            // Partial fill — replace with reduced quantity
            queue.poll();
            Order partial = new Order(order.id(), order.side(),
                                      order.price(), order.quantity() - tradedQty);
            queue.offerFirst(partial); // back to front of queue
            orderIndex.put(order.id(), partial);
        }
    }
}

Design decisions:

  • Price-time priority (FIFO at each price level) via Deque — standard

for exchange matching engines.

  • BigDecimal for price — crypto prices have up to 8 decimal places.

Floating point would cause incorrect matching.

  • HashMap index for O(1) cancel by ID.
  • Trade price = ask price (resting order) — standard exchange convention.
  • Partial fills handled — real orders often partially match.

👨‍💻 Daniel: What's the time complexity of match()?

👨 Khanh: Per trade execution: O(log n) for firstKey(), O(1) for peek() and poll(). Total for k trades: O(k log n) where n is the number of distinct price levels. In practice, n is bounded (active price levels are a small range around the current market price), so this is effectively O(k).

ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN I (45 min)

👨‍💻 Priya (Staff Engineer): Khanh, let's design **a real-time price feed system**. When a trade executes on Coinbase, millions of clients — mobile apps, trading bots, third-party dashboards — need to see the updated price within milliseconds. Design this.

Scale: 5M connected clients, 10,000 trade events/second peak, sub-100ms end-to-end latency target.

👨 Khanh: This is a fan-out problem at extreme scale. One trade event needs to reach 5 million clients. Let me design this:

Architecture:

Matching Engine
      │ trade executed
      ▼
 Kafka Topic: "trades" (partitioned by trading pair: BTC-USD, ETH-USD...)
      │
      ▼
 Price Aggregator Service
      │ computes OHLCV, rolling averages, ticker data
      ▼
 Redis Pub/Sub (per trading pair channel)
      │
      ▼
 WebSocket Gateway Cluster (stateful, ~1000 nodes)
      │ each node holds ~5,000 client connections
      ▼
 Client Apps (WebSocket persistent connection)

The fan-out problem: A single trade event must reach 5M clients. We can't broadcast from one server to 5M connections. Solution: the WebSocket Gateway cluster, where each node holds a manageable number of connections (~5,000 per node = 1,000 nodes for 5M clients).

Redis Pub/Sub for coordination: when a new price is computed, we publish to channel:BTC-USD. All WebSocket Gateway nodes subscribed to that channel receive it and forward to their local connected clients subscribed to BTC-USD. This is an O(subscribers) operation across the cluster.

Handling connection failures: clients reconnect with a last_event_id. The gateway fetches missed events from a short-lived Redis sorted set (30-second window of recent events per trading pair) and replays them.

👩‍💻 Priya: At 5M clients, what's your biggest scaling concern?

👨 Khanh: Memory on the WebSocket gateways. Each WebSocket connection holds state: session ID, subscribed channels, buffer. At 5,000 connections per node, assuming 50KB per connection, that's 250MB per node — manageable. But if we allow each client to subscribe to 10 trading pairs, the subscription fan-out multiplies. I'd limit subscriptions to 10 per client and use a subscription registry in Redis so gateways can route efficiently.

The second concern: Redis Pub/Sub message loss. Redis Pub/Sub is fire-and-forget — if a gateway misses a message (e.g., during restart), those events are lost. Mitigation: Kafka as the durable store, Redis Pub/Sub as the delivery mechanism. Gateways can replay from Kafka if they detect a gap.

ROUND 4 — BEHAVIORAL (30 min)

👩 Marcus (Engineering Manager): Hi Khanh. Coinbase operates 24/7 with no downtime windows. Crypto markets never close. Tell me how you handle sustained on-call pressure and operational responsibility.

👨 Khanh: It's something I think about seriously. In my current role, I've been on-call for our middleware platform — nothing at crypto scale, but the principles translate.

What I've learned: the quality of your alerting determines your quality of life on-call. Early in my role, our alerts were too noisy — 40% were non-actionable. I spent a week going through every alert, deleting the ones that never required action, and tuning thresholds on the rest. After that week, our alert volume dropped by 60% and the remaining 40% were all genuine issues. That matters at 2 AM.

On sustained pressure: I think about on-call not as a burden but as the feedback loop that makes me a better engineer. Every page is information about a system flaw. If I fix the flaw rather than just the symptom, I pay down that stress permanently.

👩 Marcus: Good. Last question: crypto is a politically charged space. Some engineers are uncomfortable working on it. Where do you stand?

👨 Khanh: My honest view: I'm here for the engineering problems, which are genuinely hard and interesting regardless of the underlying asset. Financial infrastructure at scale — consistency, low latency, correctness — these are real engineering challenges. I'm not a crypto maximalist, but I'm not uncomfortable with the domain either.

What I do care about is building something legal, regulated, and operating with integrity — which Coinbase, as a public company with strong compliance infrastructure, does. That's enough for me.

📊 POST-INTERVIEW ANALYSIS

Scorecard

Dimension Score Notes
Java Fundamentals ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ TreeMap/HashMap trade-offs nailed, concurrency options clear
Concurrency ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Correctly chose single-threaded queue for order book
Live Coding ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Full order book with partial fills, price-time priority
System Design ⭐⭐⭐⭐½ Fan-out architecture solid, Redis Pub/Sub trade-offs clear
Behavioral ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Alert quality story is gold, honest on crypto stance
Overall Strong Hire Technically sharp, operationally mature

💎 Power Phrases (Coinbase-Specific)

Context Phrase
On concurrency "Single-threaded command queue eliminates an entire class of bugs — no locks, no race conditions, by design."
On order book "Price-time priority via a Deque at each level — buyers who arrived first get matched first at the same price."
On alerting "The quality of your alerting determines your quality of life on-call. Non-actionable alerts are technical debt."
On BigDecimal "Crypto prices go to 8 decimal places. Floating point would cause incorrect trade matching."
On fan-out "One trade event, five million clients — that's a fan-out problem, not a throughput problem."
On crypto "I'm here for the engineering challenge. The markets never close, and neither do the hard problems."

⚡ Key Technical Patterns Demonstrated

  1. Order book data structure — TreeMap<BigDecimal, Deque<Order>>, price-time priority
  2. Concurrency pattern — single-threaded command queue (LMAX Disruptor style)
  3. Partial fills — in-place order quantity update without re-insert
  4. WebSocket fan-out — Redis Pub/Sub + sharded gateway cluster (5,000 conns/node)
  5. Event replay — Redis sorted set (30s window) + Kafka for durable history
  6. Alert hygiene — actionable-only alerts as on-call operational practice

🎭 Mock Interview #62 — DoorDash

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Order Platform

Format: Full Loop (Recruiter → Technical → System Design → Behavioral)

Context: DoorDash is the largest food delivery platform in the US, connecting 37M+ consumers with 700K+ merchants and 7M+ Dashers. The Order Platform team owns the core systems that create, track, route, and settle every order — from the moment a customer taps "Place Order" to when the Dasher marks delivery complete. This means high-throughput write paths, real-time state machines, geospatial routing, and strict SLA requirements (order assignment must complete in < 500ms).

ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (30 min)

👩 Lena (Technical Recruiter): Hi Khanh! I'm Lena from DoorDash's recruiting team. I want to ask you something upfront: DoorDash's core challenge is reliability under unpredictable spikes — lunch rush, dinner rush, bad-weather ordering surges. Does that kind of volatility appeal to you or concern you?

👨 Khanh: It appeals to me, genuinely. Predictable load is a solved problem — any system can handle steady state. What's interesting is designing for the spike: order volume can 10x in 10 minutes during a storm. The engineering decisions that hold under that pressure are the ones worth making carefully.

In my current role, I've dealt with smaller-scale versions of this — traffic spikes during client campaigns that we hadn't provisioned for. I learned that the systems that survive spikes aren't the ones with the most capacity; they're the ones with the best backpressure, shedding, and graceful degradation. That thinking is what I want to apply at real scale.

👩 Lena: Good framing. Background walkthrough — tell me about your experience.

👨 Khanh: Java backend, 4.5 years. Started in healthcare — strict data integrity, audit trails, zero-downtime deployments. Then moved to middleware: API routing, authentication flows, event processing between a customer-facing app and a global account system. About 15,000 API calls per day today, which I know is small relative to DoorDash's scale.

What I bring is engineering discipline: idempotent APIs, proper transaction boundaries, structured logging, meaningful observability. The scale is the thing I want to grow into.

👩 Lena: Perfect. Process here is: technical screen, system design, then a behavioral with the hiring manager. Let's get you scheduled.

ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL SCREEN (60 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan (Staff Engineer): Hey Khanh — I work on the order lifecycle service. Let's dig into some Java and then a coding problem.

Part A — Java & Spring Deep Dive (20 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: DoorDash orders go through many state transitions — CREATED → ASSIGNED → PICKED_UP → DELIVERED (or CANCELLED at various points). We use Spring state machines in some services. But let's talk first: **what's the difference between `@Transactional(propagation = REQUIRED) and REQUIRES_NEW)`, and when would you use each?**

👨 Khanh: This is one of the most practically important @Transactional questions.

REQUIRED (default): joins the existing transaction if one is active. If no transaction exists, creates a new one. This means the inner method participates in the outer transaction — if either rolls back, both roll back.

REQUIRES_NEW: always suspends any existing transaction and creates a completely independent one. The inner transaction commits or rolls back independently of the outer one.

@Service
public class OrderService {

    @Autowired
    private AuditLogService auditLogService;

    @Transactional // REQUIRED — default
    public void processOrder(Order order) {
        orderRepo.save(order);
        paymentService.charge(order); // may throw

        // WRONG: if payment fails, audit log is also rolled back
        auditLogService.log("ORDER_PROCESSED", order.getId());
    }
}

@Service
public class AuditLogService {

    // REQUIRES_NEW — audit log commits independently
    // Even if processOrder() rolls back, the audit entry survives
    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void log(String event, String orderId) {
        auditRepo.save(new AuditEntry(event, orderId, Instant.now()));
    }
}

When to use REQUIRES_NEW:

  • Audit logs — you want to record what happened even when the main

operation fails

  • Metrics/counters — should not roll back with business logic
  • Operations that must be independently committed (e.g., sending a

notification before the parent transaction commits, though this has risks of its own)

The trap with REQUIRES_NEW: calling it on a bean method from the same class bypasses the Spring proxy — the propagation is ignored. You must inject the bean and call it externally.

👨‍💻 Ryan: Good. Now tell me — we have an OrderRepository with a method findByStatus(OrderStatus status). With millions of orders, this will do a full table scan. How do you approach this?

👨 Khanh: Several layers of attack:

1. Database index: the obvious first step:

CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status ON orders(status);
-- Better: partial index for active orders only (CREATED, ASSIGNED, PICKED_UP)
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_active ON orders(status)
    WHERE status NOT IN ('DELIVERED', 'CANCELLED');

A partial index is smaller and faster — most queries are on active orders, not historical ones.

2. Spring Data — use the right query:

// Instead of loading all fields:
@Query("SELECT o.id, o.status, o.dasherId FROM Order o WHERE o.status = :status")
List<OrderSummary> findActiveOrderSummaries(@Param("status") OrderStatus status);

Projections avoid loading large fields (delivery instructions, metadata blobs) that aren't needed for routing decisions.

3. Pagination:

Page<Order> findByStatus(OrderStatus status, Pageable pageable);
// called as:
orderRepo.findByStatus(ASSIGNED, PageRequest.of(0, 100));

4. Denormalization for hot queries: maintain a separate table or Redis set of active_order_ids — only orders in non-terminal states. A background job prunes it as orders complete. This trades consistency for query speed, which is often worth it for real-time routing.

5. Read replicas: route read queries to replicas, writes to primary. Spring supports this with routing DataSource configuration.

Part B — Live Coding (40 min)

👨‍💻 Ryan: Real problem from our dispatch team. You have a list of Dashers, each with a current location (lat/lng) and a status (AVAILABLE or BUSY). You have a new order at a restaurant location. Write a method that finds the top 3 nearest available Dashers, then simulates assigning the order with a timeout — if no Dasher accepts within 30 seconds, try the next one.

👨 Khanh: Let me build this step by step.

import java.util.*;
import java.util.concurrent.*;

public class DasherDispatch {

    public record Location(double lat, double lng) {}

    public enum DasherStatus { AVAILABLE, BUSY }

    public record Dasher(
        String id,
        String name,
        Location location,
        DasherStatus status
    ) {}

    public record Order(String id, Location restaurantLocation) {}

    // Haversine formula — great-circle distance in km
    private double distanceKm(Location a, Location b) {
        final double R = 6371.0;
        double dLat = Math.toRadians(b.lat() - a.lat());
        double dLng = Math.toRadians(b.lng() - a.lng());
        double sinDLat = Math.sin(dLat / 2);
        double sinDLng = Math.sin(dLng / 2);
        double h = sinDLat * sinDLat
            + Math.cos(Math.toRadians(a.lat()))
            * Math.cos(Math.toRadians(b.lat()))
            * sinDLng * sinDLng;
        return 2 * R * Math.asin(Math.sqrt(h));
    }

    // Step 1: find top-3 nearest available Dashers
    public List<Dasher> findNearestAvailable(
            List<Dasher> dashers, Location orderLocation, int topN) {
        return dashers.stream()
            .filter(d -> d.status() == DasherStatus.AVAILABLE)
            .sorted(Comparator.comparingDouble(
                d -> distanceKm(d.location(), orderLocation)))
            .limit(topN)
            .toList();
    }

    // Step 2: assign with timeout fallback
    public Optional<String> assignOrder(Order order, List<Dasher> dashers) {
        List<Dasher> candidates = findNearestAvailable(
            dashers, order.restaurantLocation(), 3);

        for (Dasher dasher : candidates) {
            System.out.printf("Requesting Dasher %s (%s)...%n",
                dasher.name(), dasher.id());

            boolean accepted = requestWithTimeout(order, dasher,
                                                  30, TimeUnit.SECONDS);
            if (accepted) {
                System.out.printf("✅ Assigned to Dasher %s%n", dasher.name());
                return Optional.of(dasher.id());
            }
            System.out.printf("⏱ Dasher %s did not respond, trying next%n",
                dasher.name());
        }
        System.out.println("❌ No Dasher accepted — escalating to dispatch");
        return Optional.empty();
    }

    // Simulates async request to Dasher's device with timeout
    private boolean requestWithTimeout(Order order, Dasher dasher,
                                       long timeout, TimeUnit unit) {
        ExecutorService executor = Executors.newSingleThreadExecutor();
        Future<Boolean> future = executor.submit(() -> {
            // Simulate network round-trip to Dasher's device
            return sendPushNotificationAndAwaitResponse(order, dasher);
        });
        try {
            return future.get(timeout, unit);
        } catch (TimeoutException e) {
            future.cancel(true);
            return false;
        } catch (InterruptedException | ExecutionException e) {
            Thread.currentThread().interrupt();
            return false;
        } finally {
            executor.shutdownNow();
        }
    }

    private boolean sendPushNotificationAndAwaitResponse(
            Order order, Dasher dasher) {
        // In production: push notification → Dasher app response via webhook
        // Simulated here:
        try { Thread.sleep(new Random().nextInt(35_000)); }
        catch (InterruptedException e) { Thread.currentThread().interrupt(); }
        return new Random().nextBoolean();
    }
}

Production improvements I'd call out:

  • The ExecutorService should be a shared pool, not created per request —

this leaks threads at scale. Inject a @Bean ExecutorService with a bounded thread pool.

  • In real dispatch, use non-blocking async (CompletableFuture + WebClient)

to avoid blocking a thread during the 30-second wait.

  • The Dasher timeout should trigger a callback — not poll with

Future.get(). Push notification → Dasher app → webhook back to dispatch service.

👨‍💻 Ryan: Good catch on the thread pool leak. What's the complexity of findNearestAvailable?

👨 Khanh: O(n log n) due to sorting across all available Dashers. At DoorDash scale, you wouldn't sort all Dashers globally — you'd use a geospatial index (PostGIS, Redis GEOSEARCH, or Uber H3 hexagonal grid) to retrieve only Dashers within a search radius first, then sort that much smaller set.

ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min)

👩‍💻 Sofia (Principal Engineer): Design **DoorDash's order tracking system** — the real-time feed that shows customers where their order is. Requirements:

  • Customer sees live location of their Dasher on a map
  • Updates every 5 seconds while order is active
  • 5M concurrent active orders at peak
  • Dasher app sends location every 3 seconds
  • < 500ms latency from Dasher GPS to customer screen

👨 Khanh: Classic real-time fan-out with location data. Let me structure this:

Write path (Dasher → system):

Dasher App
    │ HTTP POST /location every 3s
    ▼
Location Ingestion API (stateless, auto-scaled)
    │ validates, enriches (orderId from session)
    ▼
Kafka Topic: "dasher-locations"
    │ partitioned by dasherId
    ▼
Location Processor
    │ filters (only active orders), deduplicates
    ▼
Redis: dasher:{dasherId}:location  (TTL: 30s)
    │ SET with expiry — automatically cleaned up when Dasher goes offline
    ▼
Redis Pub/Sub: channel order:{orderId}:location

Read path (Customer → screen):

Customer App
    │ WebSocket connection on order open
    ▼
WebSocket Gateway (long-lived connection)
    │ subscribes to Redis Pub/Sub: order:{orderId}:location
    ▼
Receives updates and pushes to customer

Sizing the WebSocket gateways:

  • 5M active orders = 5M customer WebSocket connections
  • Each gateway holds 10,000 connections → 500 gateway instances
  • Each connection: ~50KB memory → 500MB per gateway → manageable

Why WebSocket over polling:

  • Polling every 5s: 5M clients × 0.2 req/s = 1M req/s just for location reads
  • WebSocket push: location update sent once per tier, gateway fans out locally
  • 10–100x more efficient

The 5-second display update vs. 3-second Dasher send: Buffer in the gateway or client — no need to push every 3-second Dasher update to the customer. Coalesce at the gateway, push every 5s. This halves our Pub/Sub message rate.

👩‍💻 Sofia: What happens when an active order's Dasher goes offline — GPS signal lost?

👨 Khanh: Three behaviors:

  1. Short gap (< 30s): Redis TTL on the location key hasn't expired yet.

Show last known position with a "signal lost" indicator in the UI. Client app marks the location as stale after 15s of no update.

  1. Medium gap (30s–5min): Redis TTL expired. Location unknown.

Show "Dasher is on the way" without map marker. Don't panic the customer — this is common in tunnels, basements, etc.

  1. Long gap (> 5min): trigger a support workflow. Dasher may be

incapacitated. Escalate to the dispatch team with the last known location and timestamp.

👩‍💻gia Sofia: Good. How do you prevent the Dasher location data from being used to stalk Dashers?

👨 Khanh: Critical privacy concern. Mitigations:

  • Location only visible during active order: the WebSocket subscription

is bound to an active orderId. When the order completes (DELIVERED), the gateway closes the subscription and clears the location from Redis.

  • Obfuscated location near pickup/dropoff: within 200m of the

restaurant or customer address, snap the displayed location to a fixed point — prevents revealing exact positions.

  • No historical location API: customers cannot query where a Dasher

went after delivery. Location data is ephemeral (30s TTL).

  • Rate limiting on location reads: prevents enumeration attacks.

ROUND 4 — BEHAVIORAL (30 min)

👩 James (Engineering Manager): Hi Khanh. DoorDash moves fast — weekly deploys, on-call rotation, and we operate through lunch and dinner rush every single day. Tell me about a time you had to make a production decision under time pressure with incomplete information.

👨 Khanh: About six months into my current role, we started seeing elevated error rates on our authentication service — around 8% of requests failing. It was 11 AM on a weekday, heading into peak hours for our client.

I had two options: roll back the most recent deploy (from the night before) or investigate further. The rollback would fix the problem if the deploy was the cause — but our recent deploy had a critical security patch. Rolling back meant re-exposing a vulnerability.

I had about 10 minutes before our SLA dashboard would turn red and our client's team would start calling.

I made a judgment call: I pulled the logs from the failing requests and saw a pattern — all failures came from one specific API endpoint with a particular request format. That was not something our deploy touched. I kept the deploy up, added a temporary feature flag to bypass the broken code path for that endpoint, and filed a proper fix for the next cycle.

What I learned: the instinct to "just roll back" is often right, but not always. Having enough logs and observability to make a different call in 10 minutes is only possible if you've invested in observability before the incident. We didn't have great structured logging at the time — I got lucky that the error message was specific enough. After that, I pushed the team to standardize structured logs with requestId, endpoint, and errorCode on every log line.

👩 James: Great story. What's the hardest feedback you've received from a colleague and how did you respond?

👨 Khanh: About a year ago, a senior engineer on my team told me that my code reviews were "technically thorough but sometimes unkind." Specifically — I was leaving very direct comments like "this is wrong, do it this way" without explaining why, and without acknowledging what the author did well.

It stung a bit because I thought being direct was a virtue. But when I thought about it from the other engineer's perspective — especially junior engineers — receiving that kind of review without explanation is demoralizing, not educational.

I changed my approach: I lead with what I liked first, then explain the concern and the reasoning behind my suggested change, not just the change itself. My reviews got longer but more useful. And the same junior engineers started asking me to review their PRs — which I think is the real signal.

📊 POST-INTERVIEW ANALYSIS

Scorecard

Dimension Score Notes
Java/Spring ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Transactional propagation, index strategy, projection — all sharp
Live Coding ⭐⭐⭐⭐½ Haversine, timeout dispatch, self-identified thread pool leak
System Design ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Kafka + Redis + WebSocket fan-out, location privacy handled
Behavioral ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Specific incident, honest feedback response, concrete learnings
Overall Strong Hire Production-minded, communicates trade-offs clearly

💎 Power Phrases (DoorDash-Specific)

Context Phrase
On reliability "The systems that survive spikes aren't the ones with the most capacity — they're the ones with the best backpressure and graceful degradation."
On REQUIRES_NEW "Audit logs must commit independently — even when the business transaction fails, I need to know it failed."
On polling vs. WebSocket "5M clients polling every 5 seconds = 1M req/s just for location reads. WebSocket push is 10–100x more efficient."
On observability "The ability to make a different call than rolling back in 10 minutes depends entirely on the observability you invested in before the incident."
On code review "My reviews got longer but more useful — and the real signal was junior engineers started asking me to review their PRs."
On geospatial scale "You wouldn't sort all Dashers globally — you'd use a geospatial index to retrieve only Dashers within a radius first."

⚡ Key Technical Patterns Demonstrated

  1. @Transactional(REQUIRES_NEW) — audit log independence from business tx
  2. Partial index — WHERE status NOT IN ('DELIVERED','CANCELLED') for active orders only
  3. Haversine distance — great-circle formula for accurate geo distance
  4. Timeout dispatch pattern — sequential fallback with Future.get(timeout, unit)
  5. WebSocket fan-out sizing — 5M connections ÷ 10K/node = 500 gateway instances
  6. Location privacy — TTL expiry, obfuscation near endpoints, subscription scoping

🎭 Mock Interview #63 — ThoughtWorks

Role: Senior Java Consultant (Outsourcing / Consulting)

Format: Full Loop — ThoughtWorks Style (Tech Screen → TDD Coding → Architecture → Culture Fit)

Context: ThoughtWorks is a global technology consultancy (outsourcing) with a strong reputation for engineering excellence — they pioneered TDD, continuous delivery, and domain-driven design in mainstream software development. Unlike pure outsourcing shops, ThoughtWorks consultants are embedded in client teams as equal partners, not just "body shoppers." Interview bar is high: clean code, test-first thinking, XP practices, and strong communication with non-technical stakeholders. You will be placed on client projects across banking, retail, healthcare, and government — so breadth matters as much as depth.

ROUND 1 — HR / CULTURE SCREEN (30 min)

👩 Mei (Talent Acquisition): Hi Khanh. ThoughtWorks is different from most employers — we're a consultancy, not a product company. That means your "team" changes every 6–18 months as you move between clients. Some people love that; others find it destabilising. How do you feel about it?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, it's one of the main reasons I'm interested. My background has already exposed me to that dynamic — I've worked on middleware that serves different clients, each with different domain requirements, different legacy constraints, and different communication styles. Adapting quickly to a new codebase and building trust with a new team is something I've had to do, and I've found I actually like it.

What I want to avoid is becoming too narrow — one codebase, one domain, five years. ThoughtWorks forces breadth: different domains, different tech stacks, different kinds of problems. That breadth makes you a better engineer long-term.

👩 Mei: We care deeply about diversity and social justice as a company. Does that resonate with you?

👨 Khanh: It does. What resonates most practically is the part about building software with intention — considering who it affects and whether it genuinely helps. I've worked in healthcare where that question is literal: this system handles patient data, and if it fails, someone's care is delayed. That kind of accountability makes you careful in ways that abstract engineering values don't.

I can't claim to have a perfectly formed view on every social issue, but I think good engineering teams include diverse perspectives by default, because hard problems don't yield to monocultures.

👩 Mei: Good. Next step is our technical interview. ThoughtWorks uses a TDD coding exercise — you'll be given a problem and expected to solve it test-first. You'll also have an architecture session. Does that sound okay?

👨 Khanh: Absolutely. TDD is how I try to work anyway — starting with a failing test forces me to think about the contract before the implementation.

ROUND 2 — TDD CODING EXERCISE (90 min)

👨‍💻 Wei (Senior Consultant): Hey Khanh — I'm Wei. Today we'll pair on a TDD exercise. I'll watch your thinking and occasionally play the role of "rubber duck." The goal isn't a perfect solution — it's to see how you think, how you test, and how you communicate while coding.

The problem: implement a vending machine that:

  • Holds products with a name, price, and stock count
  • Accepts coins (1, 5, 10, 25 cents)
  • Dispenses a product if enough money is inserted
  • Returns change
  • Handles edge cases: insufficient funds, out of stock, invalid selection

Start with tests.

👨 Khanh: Okay. I'll start with the simplest behaviour and work outward. Red → Green → Refactor.

// Step 1: define the test class first
class VendingMachineTest {

    private VendingMachine machine;

    @BeforeEach
    void setUp() {
        machine = new VendingMachine();
        machine.addProduct("Cola", 100, 5);   // 100 cents, 5 in stock
        machine.addProduct("Water", 50, 3);
        machine.addProduct("Chips", 75, 0);   // out of stock
    }

    // Test 1: simplest case — exact change
    @Test
    void shouldDispenseProductWhenExactChangeInserted() {
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25);

        VendingResult result = machine.selectProduct("Cola");

        assertThat(result.success()).isTrue();
        assertThat(result.dispensedProduct()).isEqualTo("Cola");
        assertThat(result.change()).isEqualTo(0);
    }

    // Test 2: overpayment — change returned
    @Test
    void shouldReturnChangeWhenOverpaid() {
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25); // 125 cents for a 100-cent item

        VendingResult result = machine.selectProduct("Cola");

        assertThat(result.success()).isTrue();
        assertThat(result.change()).isEqualTo(25);
    }

    // Test 3: insufficient funds
    @Test
    void shouldRejectSelectionWhenInsufficientFunds() {
        machine.insertCoin(25);

        VendingResult result = machine.selectProduct("Cola");

        assertThat(result.success()).isFalse();
        assertThat(result.errorMessage()).contains("Insufficient funds");
        assertThat(result.currentBalance()).isEqualTo(25); // money not lost
    }

    // Test 4: out of stock
    @Test
    void shouldRejectWhenOutOfStock() {
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(25);

        VendingResult result = machine.selectProduct("Chips");

        assertThat(result.success()).isFalse();
        assertThat(result.errorMessage()).contains("Out of stock");
    }

    // Test 5: invalid product
    @Test
    void shouldRejectUnknownProduct() {
        machine.insertCoin(25);

        VendingResult result = machine.selectProduct("Beer");

        assertThat(result.success()).isFalse();
        assertThat(result.errorMessage()).contains("Product not found");
    }

    // Test 6: cancel — return all inserted coins
    @Test
    void shouldReturnAllMoneyOnCancel() {
        machine.insertCoin(25);
        machine.insertCoin(10);
        machine.insertCoin(5);

        int returned = machine.cancel();

        assertThat(returned).isEqualTo(40);
    }

    // Test 7: stock decremented after purchase
    @Test
    void shouldDecrementStockAfterPurchase() {
        insertExactChange(machine, 50);
        machine.selectProduct("Water");

        insertExactChange(machine, 50);
        machine.selectProduct("Water");

        insertExactChange(machine, 50);
        machine.selectProduct("Water");

        // 4th attempt: out of stock
        insertExactChange(machine, 50);
        VendingResult result = machine.selectProduct("Water");
        assertThat(result.success()).isFalse();
        assertThat(result.errorMessage()).contains("Out of stock");
    }

    private void insertExactChange(VendingMachine vm, int amount) {
        while (amount >= 25) { vm.insertCoin(25); amount -= 25; }
        while (amount >= 10) { vm.insertCoin(10); amount -= 10; }
        while (amount >= 5)  { vm.insertCoin(5);  amount -= 5;  }
        while (amount >= 1)  { vm.insertCoin(1);  amount -= 1;  }
    }
}

Now the implementation (making tests pass, one at a time):

public class VendingMachine {

    private record Product(String name, int priceInCents, int stock) {
        Product withReducedStock() {
            return new Product(name, priceInCents, stock - 1);
        }
    }

    public record VendingResult(
        boolean success,
        String dispensedProduct,
        int change,
        int currentBalance,
        String errorMessage
    ) {
        static VendingResult ok(String product, int change) {
            return new VendingResult(true, product, change, 0, null);
        }
        static VendingResult fail(String reason, int currentBalance) {
            return new VendingResult(false, null, 0, currentBalance, reason);
        }
    }

    private final Map<String, Product> inventory = new LinkedHashMap<>();
    private int insertedCents = 0;
    private static final Set<Integer> VALID_COINS = Set.of(1, 5, 10, 25);

    public void addProduct(String name, int priceInCents, int stock) {
        inventory.put(name, new Product(name, priceInCents, stock));
    }

    public void insertCoin(int cents) {
        if (!VALID_COINS.contains(cents)) {
            throw new IllegalArgumentException("Invalid coin: " + cents);
        }
        insertedCents += cents;
    }

    public VendingResult selectProduct(String name) {
        Product product = inventory.get(name);

        if (product == null) {
            return VendingResult.fail("Product not found: " + name, insertedCents);
        }
        if (product.stock() == 0) {
            return VendingResult.fail("Out of stock: " + name, insertedCents);
        }
        if (insertedCents < product.priceInCents()) {
            return VendingResult.fail(
                "Insufficient funds — need " + product.priceInCents()
                + " cents, have " + insertedCents, insertedCents);
        }

        int change = insertedCents - product.priceInCents();
        insertedCents = 0;
        inventory.put(name, product.withReducedStock());
        return VendingResult.ok(product.name(), change);
    }

    public int cancel() {
        int refund = insertedCents;
        insertedCents = 0;
        return refund;
    }
}

👨‍💻 Wei: Walk me through your design choices.

👨 Khanh: Three things I'm happy with and one I'd change:

Happy with:

  • VendingResult as a result object rather than exceptions for expected

failures (insufficient funds, out of stock). Exceptions are for exceptional situations. An empty wallet at a vending machine is not exceptional.

  • Tests encode the behaviour, not the implementation. I didn't test

insertedCents directly — I tested what the machine does when you select a product. That means I can refactor internals freely.

  • The cancel() method is minimal but complete.

Would change:

  • LinkedHashMap for inventory maintains insertion order but doesn't

add much value here. A plain HashMap is fine.

  • For a real system, int priceInCents should probably be

BigDecimal priceInDollars — but for a vending machine with whole cents, integer arithmetic is acceptable and simpler to reason about.

  • I'd add a displayBalance() method — useful for UI integration.

👨‍💻 Wei: One refactor — the selectProduct method has multiple reasons to fail. How would you make it more readable?

👨 Khanh: Extract validation into a guard method:

private Optional<String> validate(String name, Product product) {
    if (product == null)           return Optional.of("Product not found: " + name);
    if (product.stock() == 0)      return Optional.of("Out of stock: " + name);
    if (insertedCents < product.priceInCents())
        return Optional.of("Insufficient funds — need " + product.priceInCents());
    return Optional.empty();
}

public VendingResult selectProduct(String name) {
    Product product = inventory.get(name);
    Optional<String> error = validate(name, product);
    if (error.isPresent()) return VendingResult.fail(error.get(), insertedCents);

    int change = insertedCents - product.priceInCents();
    insertedCents = 0;
    inventory.put(name, product.withReducedStock());
    return VendingResult.ok(product.name(), change);
}

Now selectProduct reads as: validate → handle error → execute → return result.

ROUND 3 — ARCHITECTURE / CONSULTING SCENARIO (45 min)

👩‍💻 Akira (Principal Consultant): Khanh, this is a real client scenario we encounter often. A mid-size retail company (500 stores, 2M customers) is running a 10-year-old Java EE monolith for their e-commerce platform. They've hired ThoughtWorks to help them modernise. The CEO says "move everything to microservices." The CTO says "we just need to be faster." The development team says "we can't even deploy without a 2-week regression test cycle."

What do you recommend, and how do you approach the first 90 days?

👨 Khanh: This is a consulting scenario, not a coding one. The right first move is to understand the problem before prescribing a solution. "Move everything to microservices" is a solution; what the CEO actually wants is probably "faster feature delivery" and "higher reliability."

What I'd do in the first 90 days:

Days 1–14: Discovery

  • Shadow the development team during a deploy cycle — observe the 2-week

regression manually. Where does the time go? Automated tests? Manual QA? Environment setup? Approval gates?

  • Map the monolith's domain boundaries — are there modules that are

already loosely coupled? Those are microservice candidates.

  • Identify the pain points that affect the business directly: what

features took longest to ship in the last 6 months, and why?

Days 15–45: Quick wins

  • Don't touch the monolith architecture yet. Improve the build and test

pipeline first. A monolith with a 30-minute CI pipeline and reliable automated tests is more productive than microservices with manual QA.

  • Add structured logging, distributed tracing (even within the monolith),

basic dashboards. You can't improve what you can't observe.

  • Identify one module that can be extracted with low risk — the "strangler

fig" starting point.

Days 46–90: First extraction

  • Extract one well-bounded domain — often something like notifications,

file uploads, or reporting — into a separate service.

  • Deploy it alongside the monolith. Prove the deployment pipeline works.

Build confidence before touching the core order or inventory domains.

The honest conversation with the CEO: Microservices are not faster — they're more scalably maintainable. A poorly-executed microservices migration is slower than staying on the monolith. The goal in year 1 is reliable, fast deployments of the monolith. Microservices come after.

👩‍💻 Akira: The CTO pushes back — "Our investors are asking about our cloud-native strategy. We need to show progress on microservices."

👨 Khanh: This is a political constraint, not a technical one — and that's okay. We can work with it.

"Cloud-native strategy" and "microservices" are often conflated. I'd reframe it: containerise the monolith first (Docker + Kubernetes). You get cloud-native deployment, auto-scaling, and rollback capability without splitting the codebase. That's a legitimate and honest "cloud-native strategy" story for investors.

Then extract 2–3 services over 12 months — visible microservices milestones that demonstrate the direction without betting the company on a full rewrite. That's what I'd recommend and why.

ROUND 4 — CULTURE FIT / FINAL (30 min)

👩 Priya (Delivery Lead): Hi Khanh. ThoughtWorks consultants often have to deliver difficult feedback to clients — telling a CTO their architecture is wrong, or a product manager their timeline is impossible. Can you tell me about a time you delivered difficult feedback?

👨 Khanh: Yes — in my current role, about a year in, I was asked by my manager to estimate a new feature. The PM's expectation was 2 weeks. After reviewing the codebase, I estimated 6 weeks — the feature required touching a core authentication module that had no tests, was poorly documented, and had implicit dependencies we didn't fully understand.

I delivered that estimate directly to the PM and explained specifically why: not "it's complex" but "here are the 4 things that are unknown, here is what it would take to make them known, and here is my estimate once those unknowns are resolved." I also offered an alternative: deliver a scoped version in 3 weeks that covers 80% of the use cases, deferring the riskiest integration.

The PM was frustrated. But the estimate turned out to be accurate — the full feature took 5.5 weeks. What I earned from that was credibility: my estimates are honest, and when I give a number, I've done the thinking behind it. That trust is worth more than any single deadline met by over-promising.

👩 Priya: Last question: why ThoughtWorks specifically, not another consultancy?

👨 Khanh: Honestly, ThoughtWorks has a reputation I want to earn. It's one of the few consultancies where "we use TDD and clean code" isn't a slogan — it's enforced. Engineers who work here tend to be fundamentally better at the craft after a few years, because they pair-program, do code reviews seriously, and work on codebases where the practices are non-negotiable.

I also want the breadth. Banking, retail, healthcare, government — seeing those different domains makes me a more versatile engineer. I don't want to be the person who can only think about one type of problem.

📊 POST-INTERVIEW ANALYSIS

Scorecard

Dimension Score Notes
TDD Mindset ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Tests first, behaviours not internals, red-green-refactor demonstrated
Code Quality ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Result object over exceptions, guard clause refactor, self-critique
Architecture ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Strangler fig, discovery-first, containerise before splitting
Client Communication ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Political constraint handled with reframe, not confrontation
Behavioral ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Estimate story shows credibility-over-comfort, honest motivation
Overall Strong Hire ThoughtWorks-calibre: craft + communication + consulting instinct

💎 Power Phrases (Outsourcing / Consulting Context)

Context Phrase
On TDD "Starting with a failing test forces me to think about the contract before the implementation."
On result objects "An empty wallet at a vending machine is not exceptional — don't use exceptions for expected failures."
On modernisation "A monolith with a 30-minute CI pipeline and reliable automated tests is more productive than microservices with manual QA."
On politics "This is a political constraint, not a technical one — and that's okay. We can work with it."
On estimates "Not 'it's complex' — here are the 4 unknowns, here is what it takes to resolve them, and here is my number."
On ThoughtWorks "It's one of the few places where clean code and TDD aren't a slogan — they're enforced."

⚡ Key Technical Patterns Demonstrated

  1. TDD cycle — tests define behaviour, implementation follows, refactor last
  2. Result object pattern — VendingResult instead of exceptions for expected failures
  3. Guard clause refactor — extract validation into validate(), keep selectProduct() readable
  4. Strangler fig pattern — extract one low-risk module first, prove the pipeline
  5. Discovery before prescription — understand the real problem before proposing solutions
  6. Reframe political constraints — containerise monolith = "cloud-native" without full microservices migration

🎭 Mock Interview #64 — Grab

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Payments & Wallet Platform

Format: Full Loop (Recruiter → Technical → System Design → Behavioral)

Context: Grab is Southeast Asia's leading super-app — ride-hailing, food delivery, grocery, financial services — operating across 8 countries and 700 cities with 35M+ monthly active users. The Payments & Wallet Platform team powers GrabPay: the in-app wallet used to pay for rides, food, and merchant purchases. Engineering here means multi-currency transactions, regulatory compliance across 8 jurisdictions, near-zero downtime requirements, and tight integration with local banking rails (PromptPay, PayNow, DuitNow, etc.). This is a Singapore-headquartered team with engineers across Singapore, Vietnam, India, and Indonesia.

ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (30 min)

👩 Linh (Technical Recruiter, Vietnam office): Hi Khanh! I'm Linh from Grab's recruiting team in HCMC. Grab has a strong engineering presence in Vietnam — our HCMC office works on the same core services as Singapore. Before we start, any questions about the team structure?

👨 Khanh: Yes, actually — I'm curious about the ownership model. Do Vietnam-based engineers own services end-to-end, or is ownership held by Singapore while Vietnam does implementation?

👩 Linh: Full ownership. HCMC engineers are on the same oncall rotations, make the same architecture decisions, and ship to the same production systems as Singapore. It's truly distributed — not satellite.

👨 Khanh: That's exactly what I want to hear. That's the main thing I'm assessing.

👩 Linh: Good. Tell me about your background.

👨 Khanh: Java backend, 4.5 years. Started in healthcare IT — strict data integrity, audit trails, regulatory compliance. Then moved to a middleware platform handling API routing, authentication, and event processing between a customer app and a global account system.

The Grab angle that specifically appeals to me: I've worked adjacent to payment systems — token exchange, auth flows — and I want to work in the core. Multi-currency wallets, local banking rail integrations, compliance across jurisdictions — that's exactly the complexity I want to own, not just integrate with.

👩 Linh: How do you feel about oncall with production incidents that affect millions of Southeast Asian users?

👨 Khanh: I take it seriously without treating it as a burden. The oncall rotation is how you actually learn what your system does under real conditions. Every page I don't fix permanently is debt I'm paying in sleep. My approach: investigate, fix the symptom, then fix the root cause in the next sprint, and add a runbook so the next person handles it in half the time.

👩 Linh: Great. Four rounds: technical, system design, hiring manager behavioral. Let's schedule.

ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL SCREEN (60 min)

👨‍💻 Wei (Senior Engineer, Payments Core): Hey Khanh — I work on the GrabPay transaction engine. Let's start with some Java fundamentals, then live coding.

Part A — Java & Spring (25 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: Payments require bulletproof consistency. Walk me through how Spring @Transactional actually works under the hood — not just how to use it, but the mechanism.

👨 Khanh: It works through Spring AOP proxies — either JDK dynamic proxies (for interface-based beans) or CGLIB proxies (for class-based beans).

When you annotate a method with @Transactional, Spring wraps the bean in a proxy. When the method is called externally, the proxy intercepts the call, and the TransactionInterceptor runs: 

Caller
  │
  ▼
Spring Proxy (TransactionInterceptor)
  │
  ├─ PlatformTransactionManager.getTransaction()
  │     → checks ThreadLocal for existing transaction
  │     → creates new or joins existing per propagation rule
  │
  ├─ [your method executes]
  │
  ├─ success → commit()
  └─ RuntimeException → rollback()
      (checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback by default)

Key implication — self-invocation doesn't work:

@Service
public class WalletService {

    @Transactional
    public void transfer(String from, String to, BigDecimal amount) {
        debit(from, amount);
        credit(to, amount);
    }

    @Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void debit(String accountId, BigDecimal amount) {
        // This @Transactional is IGNORED when called from transfer()
        // because it goes through 'this', bypassing the proxy
    }
}

The transaction context is stored in a ThreadLocal via TransactionSynchronizationManager. This is why:

  • @Transactional doesn't work across threads by default
  • Calling a @Transactional method from the same class instance bypasses

the proxy and ignores the annotation

The checked exception trap:

@Transactional
public void processPayment(Payment p) throws PaymentException {
    // If PaymentException is checked, Spring will NOT roll back!
    // Must use: @Transactional(rollbackFor = PaymentException.class)
}

For payments, I always use rollbackFor = Exception.class to ensure any unexpected failure rolls back — money is too important to risk a silent commit on a checked exception.

👨‍💻 Wei: Good. Our wallet service needs to process concurrent top-up requests for the same user. What happens without proper handling, and how do you fix it?

👨 Khanh: Classic lost update problem. Scenario:

Thread A: reads balance = 100
Thread B: reads balance = 100
Thread A: writes balance = 100 + 50 = 150 → commits
Thread B: writes balance = 100 + 30 = 130 → commits  ← Thread A's update lost!
Final: 130 instead of 180

Fix Option 1 — Pessimistic locking:

// SELECT ... FOR UPDATE — database row lock
@Lock(LockModeType.PESSIMISTIC_WRITE)
@Query("SELECT w FROM Wallet w WHERE w.userId = :userId")
Optional<Wallet> findByUserIdForUpdate(@Param("userId") String userId);

@Transactional
public void topUp(String userId, BigDecimal amount) {
    Wallet wallet = walletRepo.findByUserIdForUpdate(userId)
        .orElseThrow(() -> new WalletNotFoundException(userId));
    wallet.setBalance(wallet.getBalance().add(amount));
    walletRepo.save(wallet);
}

Safe but slow under high concurrency — threads queue for the lock.

Fix Option 2 — Optimistic locking:

@Entity
public class Wallet {
    @Version
    private Long version; // Hibernate manages this
    private BigDecimal balance;
}

@Transactional
public void topUp(String userId, BigDecimal amount) {
    Wallet wallet = walletRepo.findByUserId(userId).orElseThrow();
    wallet.setBalance(wallet.getBalance().add(amount));
    // If version changed since read → OptimisticLockException → retry
    walletRepo.save(wallet);
}

Better throughput but requires retry logic on OptimisticLockException.

Fix Option 3 — Atomic SQL update (my preference for simple balance ops):

@Modifying
@Transactional
@Query("UPDATE Wallet w SET w.balance = w.balance + :amount " +
       "WHERE w.userId = :userId AND w.balance + :amount >= 0")
int topUp(@Param("userId") String userId, @Param("amount") BigDecimal amount);

Push the arithmetic to the database — the UPDATE is atomic by default. The AND w.balance + :amount >= 0 guard prevents negative balance in one shot. Returns 0 rows affected if guard fails.

For GrabPay: I'd use pessimistic locking for transfers (low volume, high value) and atomic SQL updates for top-ups (high volume, simpler logic).

Part B — Live Coding (35 min)

👨‍💻 Wei: Implement a rate limiter for our payment API — a user can make at most N transactions per minute. We need this to be fast (Redis-backed) and correct under concurrent requests.

👨 Khanh: I'll implement the sliding window log algorithm — more accurate than fixed windows:

import org.springframework.data.redis.core.RedisTemplate;
import org.springframework.stereotype.Component;
import java.time.Instant;
import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit;

@Component
public class SlidingWindowRateLimiter {

    private final RedisTemplate<String, String> redisTemplate;
    private static final int MAX_REQUESTS = 10;
    private static final long WINDOW_SECONDS = 60;

    public SlidingWindowRateLimiter(RedisTemplate<String, String> redisTemplate) {
        this.redisTemplate = redisTemplate;
    }

    /**
     * Returns true if request is allowed, false if rate limit exceeded.
     * Uses Redis Sorted Set: score = timestamp, member = unique request ID
     */
    public boolean isAllowed(String userId) {
        String key = "rate_limit:" + userId;
        long now = Instant.now().toEpochMilli();
        long windowStart = now - (WINDOW_SECONDS * 1000);
        String requestId = userId + ":" + now + ":" + Math.random();

        // Lua script for atomicity — all or nothing
        String luaScript = """
            local key = KEYS[1]
            local now = tonumber(ARGV[1])
            local windowStart = tonumber(ARGV[2])
            local requestId = ARGV[3]
            local maxRequests = tonumber(ARGV[4])
            local windowSeconds = tonumber(ARGV[5])
            
            -- Remove timestamps outside the window
            redis.call('ZREMRANGEBYSCORE', key, '-inf', windowStart)
            
            -- Count remaining requests in window
            local count = redis.call('ZCARD', key)
            
            if count < maxRequests then
                -- Add this request
                redis.call('ZADD', key, now, requestId)
                redis.call('EXPIRE', key, windowSeconds)
                return 1
            else
                return 0
            end
            """;

        Long result = redisTemplate.execute(
            new DefaultRedisScript<>(luaScript, Long.class),
            List.of(key),
            String.valueOf(now),
            String.valueOf(windowStart),
            requestId,
            String.valueOf(MAX_REQUESTS),
            String.valueOf(WINDOW_SECONDS)
        );

        return Long.valueOf(1L).equals(result);
    }
}

// Usage in controller:
@RestController
public class PaymentController {

    @Autowired
    private SlidingWindowRateLimiter rateLimiter;

    @PostMapping("/payments")
    public ResponseEntity<?> createPayment(
            @RequestHeader("X-User-Id") String userId,
            @RequestBody PaymentRequest request) {

        if (!rateLimiter.isAllowed(userId)) {
            return ResponseEntity.status(HttpStatus.TOO_MANY_REQUESTS)
                .header("Retry-After", "60")
                .body(Map.of("error", "Rate limit exceeded — max 10 transactions/minute"));
        }
        // process payment...
    }
}

Why Lua script: all operations (remove old entries, count, add new) execute atomically in Redis. Without Lua, two concurrent requests could both read count = 9, both decide they're allowed, and both add — giving 11 requests when max is 10. The Lua script prevents this without application-level locking.

👨‍💻 Wei: What's the memory usage of this approach?

👨 Khanh: Each entry in the sorted set is the request ID (~50 bytes) plus the score (8 bytes). At 10 requests/minute max per user: 10 × 60 bytes ≈ 600 bytes per user. For 1 million active users: 600 bytes × 1M = 600MB. Manageable for Redis. The TTL ensures inactive users' keys expire automatically.

ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min)

👩‍💻 Priya (Principal Engineer): Design **GrabPay's wallet top-up system**. Users can add money to their GrabPay wallet from a bank account or credit card. Requirements:

  • At-least-once processing (money should never be lost)
  • Exactly-once credit to wallet (never credited twice)
  • Support 5 payment methods: credit card, debit card, PayNow, PromptPay, DuitNow
  • Peak: 50,000 top-ups/minute
  • Regulatory: each transaction must be logged for audit (7-year retention)

👨 Khanh: Top-up is a two-phase operation: collect money from the source, then credit the wallet. The hard problem is ensuring these two operations are consistent — money collected but wallet not credited (loss), or wallet credited but money not collected (fraud).

Architecture:

User App
    │ POST /wallet/topup
    ▼
API Gateway (auth, rate limiting)
    │
    ▼
TopUp Service
    │ 1. Create TopUpRequest record (status=PENDING) in DB
    │ 2. Publish event to Kafka: "topup.initiated"
    │ 3. Return 202 Accepted + requestId to user
    ▼
Payment Method Router (consumes "topup.initiated")
    │ routes to correct processor based on payment method
    ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Payment Processors (per method)    │
│  - CardProcessor (Stripe/Adyen)     │
│  - PayNowProcessor (SGD wire)       │
│  - PromptPayProcessor (THB wire)    │
│  - DuitNowProcessor (MYR wire)      │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
    │ callback/webhook when payment confirmed
    ▼
TopUp Service (webhook handler)
    │ 1. Verify payment confirmed
    │ 2. Update TopUpRequest status=COLLECTED
    │ 3. Publish "topup.collected" to Kafka
    ▼
Wallet Service (consumes "topup.collected")
    │ 1. Check idempotency key (topUpRequestId)
    │ 2. Credit wallet balance (atomic SQL UPDATE)
    │ 3. Publish "topup.completed"
    ▼
Audit Log Service (consumes all events)
    │ writes immutable ledger entry (S3 + RDS)
    │ 7-year retention
    ▼
User App: notified via push notification

Idempotency for exactly-once wallet credit:

@Transactional
public void creditWallet(TopUpEvent event) {
    // Idempotency check first
    if (topUpRepo.existsByIdempotencyKey(event.requestId())) {
        log.info("Duplicate event, skipping: {}", event.requestId());
        return; // already processed
    }
    walletRepo.addBalance(event.userId(), event.amount());
    topUpRepo.markProcessed(event.requestId());
    // Both in same transaction — atomic
}

Handling payment processor webhooks: Bank webhooks are unreliable — they may arrive late, out of order, or not at all. I'd add a reconciliation job that runs every 15 minutes, queries the payment processor API for any PENDING top-ups older than 10 minutes, and force-checks their status. This catches missed webhooks.

👩‍💻 Priya: A user reports "I was charged but my wallet wasn't credited." What's your debugging process?

👨 Khanh: Follow the event chain with the requestId:

  1. Check TopUpRequest table — what status is it?
  • PENDING → payment processor was never called or webhook never arrived
  • COLLECTED → Kafka event published but Wallet Service failed to consume
  • COMPLETED → wallet was credited (user may be checking wrong account)
  1. Check Kafka consumer lag — is the Wallet Service consumer behind?
  1. Check the Wallet Service idempotency table — was the event received

but rejected as duplicate incorrectly?

  1. Check the payment processor directly via their admin API — did they

actually collect the money?

If money was collected but wallet not credited: manual credit via ops tooling + incident report. If neither happened: refund or re-initiate. The audit log gives us the immutable ground truth regardless.

ROUND 4 — BEHAVIORAL (30 min)

👩 Tan Wei (Engineering Manager, HCMC): Hi Khanh. Working in a distributed team — Vietnam, Singapore, India — means asynchronous collaboration and timezone overlap challenges. How do you communicate across those boundaries?

👨 Khanh: My approach has evolved. Early in my career I'd send a Slack message and wait for a reply, which in a timezone-gap situation could mean 8 hours of blocked progress. I've learned to write better async documents.

What that means in practice: when I'm blocked or need a decision, I don't just ask "should we use approach A or B?" — I write up both approaches with trade-offs, state my recommendation and why, and ask for confirmation or counter-argument. The other person can respond in their morning and I can continue in my morning. We lose one day max, not multiple.

I also over-communicate progress in daily standups — specifically what I finished, what I'm working on today, and what's blocking me. In distributed teams, silence is ambiguous and ambiguity is expensive.

👩 Tan Wei: Tell me about a time you disagreed with a technical decision your team made. What did you do?

👨 Khanh: About a year ago, my team decided to use a polling approach to sync data from an external partner API — checking every 60 seconds for changes. I believed webhooks from the partner were the right approach.

I raised it once in the sprint planning discussion, made my case, and was outvoted — the team was concerned about complexity and the partner's webhook reliability history. I disagreed but committed to the decision.

Six months later, the polling was causing load issues during peak hours — exactly the concern I'd had. I didn't say "I told you so." I wrote a brief retrospective doc: here's what the data shows about load impact, here's the webhook option we originally considered, here's what it would take to switch now. We migrated over the next quarter.

What I learned: disagreeing once clearly is the right move. Raising it twice makes you annoying. Waiting for the data and returning with evidence is the most effective way to change a decision you believe was wrong.

📊 POST-INTERVIEW ANALYSIS

Scorecard

Dimension Score Notes
Java Depth ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Proxy mechanism, locking strategies, checked exception trap
Concurrency ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Lost update, 3 options with trade-offs, chose atomic SQL
Live Coding ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Lua script for atomic rate limiting, memory sizing
System Design ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Two-phase top-up, idempotency, reconciliation job
Behavioral ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Async communication, disagree-and-commit with follow-through
Overall Strong Hire Payments-calibre correctness mindset, distributed team maturity

💎 Power Phrases (Grab / Payments Context)

Context Phrase
On transactions "For payments, I always use rollbackFor = Exception.class — money is too important to risk a silent commit on a checked exception."
On locking "Pessimistic locking for transfers (low volume, high value), atomic SQL updates for top-ups (high volume, simpler logic)."
On Lua scripts "Without Lua, two concurrent requests could both read count=9, both decide they're allowed — Lua makes it atomic."
On idempotency "Check idempotency key first, credit wallet, mark processed — all in one transaction. The key is the atomic unit."
On async comms "I write up both approaches with trade-offs and state my recommendation. They respond in their morning, I continue in mine."
On disagreement "Raising it once clearly is right. Raising it twice makes you annoying. Return with data."

⚡ Key Technical Patterns Demonstrated

  1. Spring AOP proxy internals — ThreadLocal, self-invocation bypass, checked exception trap
  2. Three locking strategies — pessimistic, optimistic, atomic SQL with guard
  3. Sliding window rate limiter — Redis sorted set + Lua script for atomicity
  4. Two-phase top-up — Kafka events + idempotency table + reconciliation job
  5. Webhook reliability — background reconciliation for missed callbacks
  6. Debugging by event chain — follow requestId through all system states

🎭 Mock Interview #65 — Sea Limited (Shopee)

Role: Senior Backend Engineer — Order & Fulfillment Platform

Format: Full Loop (Recruiter → Technical → System Design → Behavioral)

Context: Sea Limited is Southeast Asia's largest tech conglomerate — Shopee (e-commerce, #1 in SEA), Garena (gaming), and SeaMoney (fintech). The Order & Fulfillment Platform team owns the systems that handle Shopee's order lifecycle: creation, payment confirmation, warehouse routing, seller notification, logistics tracking, and buyer delivery confirmation. Shopee processes tens of millions of orders daily — with major traffic spikes during 9.9, 10.10, 11.11, and 12.12 flash sales where order volume can spike 50–100x in minutes. The HCMC engineering office is a core product team, not a satellite.

ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (30 min)

👩 Huong (Technical Recruiter): Hi Khanh! I'm Huong from Sea's recruiting team. Sea moves extremely fast — we ship multiple times a day across services used by 500M+ users. Does that pace suit you?

👨 Khanh: Yes — high velocity is what I'm looking for. In my current role, our release cycle is too conservative. I've pushed for more frequent deploys, better feature flags, and cleaner rollback capability precisely because I believe shipping smaller changes more often is safer than batching everything into a big fortnightly release.

👩 Huong: Tell me your background quickly.

👨 Khanh: Java backend, 4.5 years. Healthcare IT first — compliance, audit, data integrity. Then middleware: API routing, auth, event processing for a customer-facing platform. I've been close to e-commerce workflows — payment events, order state changes — and I want to own the core.

Shopee specifically: the engineering problems around flash sales are fascinating. Every 11.11, you have to handle 100x normal load, route orders correctly, avoid double-confirms, and maintain data integrity under extreme write pressure. That's exactly the class of problem I want to work on.

👩 Huong: Strong answer. Process: technical screen, system design, then behavioral with the EM. Let's move forward.

ROUND 2 — TECHNICAL SCREEN (60 min)

👨‍💻 Minh (Senior Engineer, Order Platform): Hey Khanh — I work on the order lifecycle service. Let me dig into some Java internals first, then we'll code.

Part A — Java & Spring (25 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: Our order service processes massive write throughput during flash sales. Explain Java's memory model — specifically, when do you need volatile, and when is it not enough?

👨 Khanh: The Java Memory Model (JMM) defines how threads interact through memory. The key problem it solves: without explicit guarantees, each CPU core can cache variables locally and never flush to main memory — so Thread A's write to a variable may never be visible to Thread B.

volatile gives you two guarantees:

  1. Visibility: writes to a volatile variable are immediately

flushed to main memory and reads always come from main memory — no thread-local CPU cache.

  1. Happens-before: a write to volatile happens-before any

subsequent read of that same variable.

public class OrderStatusPoller {

    private volatile boolean running = true; // volatile needed!

    public void start() {
        new Thread(() -> {
            while (running) { // reads from main memory each iteration
                pollForNewOrders();
            }
        }).start();
    }

    public void stop() {
        running = false; // flushed to main memory immediately
    }
}

Without volatile, the JVM could hoist running out of the loop (it never changes inside the thread), and the thread runs forever even after stop() is called.

When volatile is NOT enough — compound actions:

private volatile int orderCount = 0;

// BROKEN — read-increment-write is not atomic even with volatile
public void incrementOrderCount() {
    orderCount++; // actually: read → add 1 → write — three steps!
}
// Thread A reads 5, Thread B reads 5, both write 6 → lost update

For compound operations (check-then-act, read-modify-write), use:

  • AtomicInteger, AtomicLong — lock-free CAS operations
  • synchronized — mutual exclusion
  • ReentrantLock — explicit lock with tryLock / fairness options

At Shopee scale: I'd use AtomicLong for counters like active order counts, volatile for simple flags (shutdown signal, feature toggles), and Redis for distributed counters shared across service instances.

👨‍💻nh Minh: Good. What's the difference between HashMap and ConcurrentHashMap, and how does ConcurrentHashMap achieve thread safety without locking the whole map?

👨 Khanh: HashMap is not thread-safe at all — concurrent modification can corrupt the internal linked list structure, causing infinite loops in Java 7 (fixed in Java 8 with tree bins, but still produces wrong results).

ConcurrentHashMap achieves thread safety through **segment-level locking (Java 7) → node-level CAS + synchronized (Java 8+)**:

Java 8 ConcurrentHashMap internal structure:

Bucket 0  → Node(key=A, value=1) → Node(key=E, value=2)  [synchronized on head]
Bucket 1  → null
Bucket 2  → Node(key=C, value=3)                          [synchronized on head]
...
Bucket n  → TreeNode(key=...)                             [Red-Black tree if > 8 nodes]

Write path:

  • If bucket is empty: CAS to insert first node — no lock needed
