  • If bucket has nodes: synchronized(firstNode) — locks only that bucket's

chain, not the whole map

  • 16 threads can write to 16 different buckets simultaneously

Read path: fully non-blocking — reads use volatile references, so they always see the latest committed write without locking.

size() is approximate — counts are maintained per-segment with LongAdder and summed. This is why size() may not be perfectly accurate under concurrent modification — acceptable for most use cases.

For Shopee's flash sale order tracking: ConcurrentHashMap for in-memory session data per order, but Redis ConcurrentHashMap-equivalent (HSETNX, INCR) for distributed state shared across instances.

Part B — Live Coding (35 min)

👨‍💻 Minh: Flash sale scenario. We have a limited-stock item — say 1,000 units. Implement a flash sale stock reservation system that:

  • Handles 50,000 concurrent requests safely
  • Never oversells (stock goes negative)
  • Is as fast as possible

👨 Khanh: Three approaches, increasing in performance:

Approach 1 — Database pessimistic lock (safe but slow):

@Transactional
public boolean reserveStock(String productId, int quantity) {
    Product product = productRepo.findByIdWithLock(productId); // SELECT FOR UPDATE
    if (product.getStock() < quantity) {
        return false; // insufficient stock
    }
    product.setStock(product.getStock() - quantity);
    productRepo.save(product);
    return true;
}

Correct, but SELECT FOR UPDATE serialises all requests at the DB. 50,000 concurrent requests = 50,000 queued transactions. Latency spikes.

Approach 2 — Atomic SQL UPDATE (better):

@Modifying
@Transactional
@Query("UPDATE Product p SET p.stock = p.stock - :qty " +
       "WHERE p.id = :id AND p.stock >= :qty")
int decrementStock(@Param("id") String id, @Param("qty") int qty);

public boolean reserveStock(String productId, int quantity) {
    return stockRepo.decrementStock(productId, quantity) > 0;
}

The database engine handles atomicity per-row. No application-level lock. Much higher throughput, but still hits the DB on every request.

Approach 3 — Redis atomic decrement (fastest for flash sales):

@Component
public class FlashSaleReservation {

    private final RedisTemplate<String, String> redis;
    private final ProductRepository productRepo;

    // Pre-load stock into Redis before sale starts
    public void initSaleStock(String productId, int stock) {
        redis.opsForValue().set("flash_stock:" + productId,
                                String.valueOf(stock));
    }

    public ReservationResult reserve(String productId, int quantity) {
        String key = "flash_stock:" + productId;

        // DECRBY is atomic — returns new value after decrement
        Long remaining = redis.opsForValue().decrement(key, quantity);

        if (remaining == null) {
            return ReservationResult.error("Sale not initialised");
        }

        if (remaining < 0) {
            // Oversold — compensate by incrementing back
            redis.opsForValue().increment(key, quantity);
            return ReservationResult.soldOut();
        }

        // Successfully reserved in Redis — write to DB asynchronously
        persistReservationAsync(productId, quantity);
        return ReservationResult.success(remaining);
    }

    @Async
    private void persistReservationAsync(String productId, int quantity) {
        // Write reservation to DB for durability
        // If this fails, reconciliation job syncs Redis ↔ DB
        reservationRepo.save(new Reservation(productId, quantity));
    }
}

Why this works: Redis DECRBY is a single atomic command — no race condition. If 50,000 requests hit simultaneously, Redis processes them sequentially in its single-threaded command loop. The one that pushes remaining below 0 gets compensated immediately.

The tradeoff: Redis memory is the source of truth during the sale. DB is eventually consistent via async writes + reconciliation. If Redis crashes between DECRBY and the DB write, we could lose reservation records — mitigated by Redis AOF persistence and reconciliation jobs.

👨‍💻 Minh: What if someone reserves but never pays?

👨 Khanh: Add a TTL-based reservation expiry:

// Reserve with 10-minute expiry
redis.opsForValue().set("flash_reservation:" + userId + ":" + productId,
                        "RESERVED", 10, TimeUnit.MINUTES);

// Payment service checks this key before confirming
// If expired, release back to stock
public void releaseExpiredReservations() {
    // Scheduled job — scans reservation keys, releases expired ones
    // In practice: Kafka delayed message or Redis keyspace notifications
}

ROUND 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (45 min)

👩‍💻 Lan (Staff Engineer): Design Shopee's 11.11 flash sale system. Requirements:

  • 10 million items go on sale at exactly 00:00:00
  • Expected peak: 5 million concurrent users hitting "Buy Now"
  • No overselling — stock must be correct
  • Seller inventory is pre-declared (we know the exact quantity)
  • Buyer sees real-time "X items remaining" counter
  • Normal Shopee traffic must not be degraded

👨 Khanh: This is a traffic isolation + pre-computation problem. The key insight: at 00:00:00, 5 million users submit simultaneously. The backend cannot process 5 million DB writes per second. We need to absorb the spike without hitting the database hot path.

Pre-sale preparation (before 00:00:00):

1. Load all flash sale stock into Redis (FLASH_STOCK:{productId} = quantity)
2. Pre-warm CDN caches for product pages
3. Scale up flash sale service pods (pre-scaling, not reactive)
4. Disable non-essential features (recommendations, reviews loading)
5. Circuit breakers ready on all downstream services

At 00:00:00 — request flow:

5M users → CDN (absorb static content)
              ↓
           API Gateway (rate limit: 1 req/s per user)
              ↓
         Flash Sale Queue (Kafka — absorbs spike)
              ↓
      Flash Sale Workers (consume from queue)
              │ Redis DECRBY — atomic stock check
              │ success → write reservation to DB async
              │ failure → "Sorry, sold out"
              ↓
         Order Service (normal creation flow)

Traffic isolation: Flash sale runs on dedicated pods with dedicated Kafka topics. Normal Shopee traffic uses separate pods and topics. A flash sale meltdown cannot cascade to regular orders.

Rate limiting at the gate:

  • Each user gets at most 1 "Buy Now" submission per 2 seconds
  • Implemented via Redis sliding window per userId
  • Prevents bots from flooding the queue

Real-time "X items remaining" counter:

Redis FLASH_STOCK:{productId}
    │ polled by Flash Sale Service every 500ms
    ▼
Redis Pub/Sub: "stock_updates"
    │
    ▼
WebSocket Gateway → User browsers (every 1–2 seconds)

We don't push every single decrement — too noisy. We publish a coalesced update every 500ms with the current count.

👩‍💻 Lan: What's your plan if Redis goes down at 00:00:01?

👨 Khanh: Fallback layers:

  1. Redis Cluster with replicas: automatic failover in < 30 seconds.

During failover, we serve requests from local in-memory cache with a conservative stock count (last known value).

  1. Circuit breaker: if Redis is unreachable, the circuit opens and

we immediately return "temporarily unavailable" instead of cascading failures to the DB.

  1. Graceful degradation: pause the "Buy Now" button with a countdown:

"Back in 30 seconds." Better than a 500 error.

  1. Post-recovery reconciliation: once Redis is back, sync stock levels

from DB (source of truth) before re-enabling purchases.

ROUND 4 — BEHAVIORAL (30 min)

👩 Thu (Engineering Manager): Hi Khanh. Shopee ships fast. Sometimes that means cutting corners on tests or documentation. Where do you draw the line?

👨 Khanh: My line is: never skip tests on the write path that involves money or state changes. Skip documentation stubs? Fine. Skip unit tests on a pure-read reporting endpoint? Acceptable in a crunch. Skip integration tests on order creation or payment confirmation? Non-negotiable.

In practice, I draw the line by asking: "What breaks if this code is wrong, and how long before we notice?" If the answer is "user gets charged twice" or "order disappears," we write the test. If the answer is "the chart has the wrong label," we ship and add a ticket.

Speed and quality aren't opposed if you're strategic about where you spend the quality budget.

👩 Thu: Tell me about a time you had to deliver something under a very tight deadline. How did you decide what to cut?

👨 Khanh: About 8 months ago, my team had a deadline to ship an integration with a new payment provider in 2 weeks. Realistic estimate was 4 weeks for the full feature: all payment methods, full error handling, admin tooling, and complete test coverage.

I sat down and listed everything in priority order:

  • Core happy path (user initiates, pays, gets confirmation): must ship
  • Error handling for network timeouts: must ship — money is at risk
  • Retry on payment failure: must ship
  • Admin tooling to manually retry failed payments: defer
  • Support for 3 of 5 payment methods: ship (the other 2 had < 5% usage)
  • Complete unit test coverage: cover the write path fully, defer read path

I presented this scope to the PM with the risk explicitly stated: deferred admin tooling means ops can't self-serve on failures — they'll come to engineering. We shipped in 2 weeks and had 3 manual escalations in the first month that we handled directly. We shipped the admin tooling 3 weeks later and haven't had a manual escalation since.

👩 Thu: Why Shopee specifically?

👨 Khanh: Scale and domain. Shopee is the largest e-commerce platform in Southeast Asia by active users, and you operate in the same market I understand — Vietnamese consumers, Vietnamese logistics, local payment methods. Working on systems that solve problems I see every day when I open the app is motivating in a way that abstract technical problems aren't.

Also: the 11.11 engineering challenge is one of the most interesting traffic engineering problems in the world. That's not a problem every engineer gets to work on.

📊 POST-INTERVIEW ANALYSIS

Scorecard

Dimension Score Notes
Java Memory Model ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ volatile guarantees, atomic vs compound, practical examples
ConcurrentHashMap ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Node-level CAS, non-blocking reads, size() approximation
Live Coding ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ 3 approaches, Redis DECRBY rationale, reservation expiry
System Design ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Traffic isolation, pre-warming, graceful Redis degradation
Behavioral ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ Quality budget thinking, scope negotiation with evidence
Overall Strong Hire Flash sale–calibre thinking, pragmatic under pressure

💎 Power Phrases (Shopee / E-commerce Context)

Context Phrase
On volatile "Without volatile, the JVM can hoist the flag out of the loop — thread runs forever after stop() is called."
On ConcurrentHashMap "16 threads can write to 16 different buckets simultaneously — only the bucket head is synchronized, not the whole map."
On flash sale "Redis DECRBY is single-command atomic. If 50,000 requests hit simultaneously, Redis processes them sequentially."
On quality "Speed and quality aren't opposed if you're strategic about where you spend the quality budget."
On scope cuts "I presented the risk explicitly: deferred tooling means escalations come to engineering. They accepted that trade-off knowingly."
On 11.11 "The key insight: we cannot process 5M DB writes per second. We absorb the spike before it hits the database."

⚡ Key Technical Patterns Demonstrated

  1. Java Memory Model — visibility, happens-before, volatile scope, CAS atomics
  2. ConcurrentHashMap internals — segment locking → node CAS + synchronized, non-blocking reads
  3. Flash sale stock reservation — 3 approaches (pessimistic lock → atomic SQL → Redis DECRBY)
  4. Reservation expiry — TTL-keyed reservation + keyspace notification for release
  5. Traffic isolation — dedicated pods + Kafka topics for flash sale vs. normal traffic
  6. Redis fallback — circuit breaker + local cache + post-recovery reconciliation

Java / Spring / Hibernate / System Design — Interview Q&A

Based on: Grokking The Java Interview + Grokking The Spring Boot Interview + Pham Khanh Tuong's CV Scope: Theory only (no coding problems)

PART 1 — CORE JAVA

OOP Fundamentals

Q1. What is method overloading vs method overriding?

  • Overloading: same method name, different parameter types/count; resolved at compile time (static polymorphism).
  • Overriding: subclass redefines a superclass method with the same signature; resolved at runtime (dynamic polymorphism).

Q2. Can we override a static method? No. Static methods belong to the class and are resolved at compile time. You can hide it by declaring the same static method in the subclass, but it is not true overriding.

Q3. Can we override a private or final method? No. Private methods are not visible to subclasses. Final methods are explicitly locked from overriding.

Q4. What is covariant return type in overriding? Since Java 5, an overriding method may return a subtype of the return type declared in the parent. Useful with clone().

Q5. What is the difference between abstract class and interface?

Abstract Class Interface
Methods Can have concrete methods Java 8+: can have default/static methods
Fields Any fields Only public static final constants
Inheritance Single Multiple
Use case Share default behaviour Define contract/type

Q6. What are the SOLID principles?

  • S – Single Responsibility: one class, one reason to change.
  • O – Open/Closed: open for extension, closed for modification.
  • L – Liskov Substitution: subtypes must be replaceable for their base types.
  • I – Interface Segregation: prefer small, specific interfaces.
  • D – Dependency Inversion: depend on abstractions, not concretions.

Q7. Composition vs Inheritance? Composition is more flexible — behaviour can be changed at runtime by swapping the composed object. Inheritance is static. Prefer composition over inheritance (GoF principle).

Q8. Association, Aggregation, Composition?

  • Association: general relationship between objects.
  • Aggregation: weak "has-a"; parts can exist independently (department → employee).
  • Composition: strong "has-a"; parts cannot exist without the whole (house → room).

Q9. Strategy vs State pattern?

  • Strategy: switch algorithms at runtime; the context delegates to the chosen strategy.
  • State: object changes behaviour based on internal state; the state itself drives transitions.

Q10. Decorator vs Proxy vs Adapter?

  • Decorator: adds behaviour without changing the class (wraps it).
  • Proxy: controls access to another object.
  • Adapter: makes incompatible interfaces work together.

Q11. Singleton pattern — when to use and thread-safe implementation? Use when exactly one instance is needed globally (e.g., config, connection pool). Thread-safe approach: double-checked locking with volatile, or an enum-based singleton.

Q12. Factory vs Abstract Factory?

  • Factory: creates one family of products.
  • Abstract Factory: creates families of related products without specifying concrete classes.

Java Core — String, Collections, Exceptions

Q13. String vs StringBuffer vs StringBuilder?

  • String: immutable; every change creates a new object.
  • StringBuffer: mutable, synchronized (thread-safe, slower).
  • StringBuilder: mutable, not synchronized (faster, single-thread use).

Q14. How does HashMap work internally? HashMap uses an array of buckets. Key's hashCode() determines the bucket index. On collision, entries are chained (linked list, or red-black tree from Java 8 when size > 8). equals() is used to locate the exact key within a bucket.

Q15. equals() and hashCode() contract? If a.equals(b) then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(). The reverse is not required. You must override both together; breaking the contract corrupts HashMap/HashSet behaviour.

Q16. HashMap vs Hashtable vs ConcurrentHashMap?

HashMap Hashtable ConcurrentHashMap
Thread-safe No Yes (full lock) Yes (segment/bucket lock)
Null keys 1 null key Not allowed Not allowed
Performance Best single-thread Poor multi-thread Best multi-thread

Q17. ArrayList vs LinkedList?

  • ArrayList: backed by array; O(1) random access; O(n) insert/delete in middle.
  • LinkedList: doubly-linked list; O(n) random access; O(1) insert/delete at head/tail.

Q18. fail-fast vs fail-safe iterator?

  • fail-fast: throws ConcurrentModificationException if collection is modified during iteration (e.g., ArrayList, HashMap iterators).
  • fail-safe: works on a copy; no exception (e.g., CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap).

Q19. Checked vs Unchecked exceptions?

  • Checked: must be declared with throws or caught; represents recoverable conditions (e.g., IOException).
  • Unchecked (RuntimeException): programming errors; no forced handling (e.g., NullPointerException).

Q20. Comparable vs Comparator?

  • Comparable (natural order): implemented inside the class; compareTo().
  • Comparator (custom order): external; compare(); multiple comparators possible; does not require source access.

Q21. What is transient vs volatile?

  • transient: field excluded from serialization.
  • volatile: guarantees visibility of changes across threads; prevents caching in registers; establishes happens-before.

Q22. Serializable vs Externalizable?

  • Serializable: marker interface; default JVM serialization.
  • Externalizable: two methods readExternal()/writeExternal(); developer controls serialization fully; faster.

Q23. What is SerialVersionUID? A static final long that acts as a version number for a Serializable class. JVM uses it to verify that a deserialized class matches the loaded class. If they differ, InvalidClassException is thrown.

Multithreading & Concurrency

Q24. Thread vs Process? A process has its own memory space. Threads within the same process share heap memory but each has its own stack. Context-switching between threads is cheaper than between processes.

Q25. Runnable vs Callable?

  • Runnable.run(): no return value, cannot throw checked exception.
  • Callable.call(): returns a value, can throw checked exception; used with ExecutorService.submit() returning Future.

Q26. start() vs run()? start() spawns a new OS thread and internally calls run(). Calling run() directly executes it on the current thread — no new thread is created.

Q27. synchronized vs ReentrantLock?

synchronized ReentrantLock
Try-lock No tryLock() yes
Fairness No Configurable
Interruptible No Yes
Condition wait/notify Condition object
Auto-release Yes (block exit) Must call unlock()

Q28. What is volatile? Guarantees that reads/writes go directly to main memory (no CPU cache). Prevents instruction reordering. Suitable for single-writer flags; not sufficient for compound operations (use AtomicXxx instead).

Q29. What is deadlock and how to avoid it? Deadlock: two or more threads wait for each other's locks forever. Prevention: acquire locks in a fixed global order; use tryLock() with timeout; avoid nested locks; prefer higher-level concurrency utilities.

Q30. What is the Java Memory Model (JMM)? JMM defines visibility and ordering guarantees across threads. The happens-before rule ensures that actions before a synchronized write or volatile write are visible to subsequent reads.

Q31. CyclicBarrier vs CountDownLatch?

  • CountDownLatch: one-time; threads wait until count reaches zero; cannot be reset.
  • CyclicBarrier: reusable; threads wait for each other at a barrier point; useful for phased computation.

Q32. ThreadLocal? Provides a per-thread variable. Each thread has its own independent copy. Useful for per-request data (e.g., user session, DB connection) without synchronization. Always call remove() in thread-pool threads.

Q33. What is thread pool? Why use it? A pool of pre-created threads that pick up tasks from a queue. Avoids the overhead of thread creation/destruction for every task; controls max concurrency; prevents resource exhaustion.

Q34. What is double-checked locking for Singleton?

private static volatile Singleton instance;
public static Singleton getInstance() {
    if (instance == null) {
        synchronized (Singleton.class) {
            if (instance == null) instance = new Singleton();
        }
    }
    return instance;
}

volatile prevents partially-constructed object visibility.

Q35. What is a race condition? When the correctness of a program depends on the relative timing/interleaving of threads. Classic example: i++ (read-modify-write) without synchronization.

Java 8 / Functional Programming

Q36. What is a functional interface? An interface with exactly one abstract method (SAM). Can be annotated with @FunctionalInterface. Enables lambda expressions.

Q37. Stream intermediate vs terminal operations?

  • Intermediate (lazy): filter(), map(), flatMap(), sorted(), peek() — return a new Stream.
  • Terminal (trigger execution): collect(), forEach(), reduce(), count(), findFirst().

Q38. map() vs flatMap()?

  • map(): transforms each element; 1-to-1.
  • flatMap(): transforms each element to a stream, then flattens; 1-to-many (useful for nested collections).

Q39. What does "Stream is lazy" mean? Intermediate operations are not executed until a terminal operation is called. The pipeline is only traversed once at the terminal call.

Q40. Predicate, Supplier, Consumer, Function?

Interface Signature Use
Predicate<T> boolean test(T t) filtering
Supplier<T> T get() factory/lazy init
Consumer<T> void accept(T t) side effects
Function<T,R> R apply(T t) transform

Garbage Collection

Q41. What is the structure of the Java Heap? Heap is divided into Young Generation (Eden + Survivor spaces S0/S1) and Old Generation (Tenured). PermGen was replaced by Metaspace in Java 8.

Q42. Minor GC vs Major GC?

  • Minor GC: collects Young Generation; frequent, fast.
  • Major/Full GC: collects Old Generation (and Metaspace); infrequent, slow (stop-the-world pause).

Q43. When is an object eligible for GC? When no live (reachable) reference points to it. Circular references between two unreachable objects are also collected (GC uses reachability, not reference counting).

Q44. Can we force GC? System.gc() is a suggestion; JVM may ignore it. You cannot force GC.

PART 2 — SPRING / SPRING BOOT

Spring Core & IoC

Q45. What is the Spring Framework? An open-source framework that simplifies Java development through Dependency Injection (IoC container), AOP, and a rich ecosystem (MVC, Data, Security, Cloud, etc.).

Q46. What is Inversion of Control (IoC)? Control of object creation and wiring is inverted from the developer to the Spring container. The container reads configuration, creates beans, injects dependencies, and manages their lifecycle.

Q47. What is Dependency Injection? Types? DI is when an object's dependencies are provided externally rather than created by the object itself. Spring supports:

  1. Constructor injection — mandatory deps; immutable; preferred.
  2. Setter injection — optional deps; allows circular dependency resolution.
  3. Field injection — @Autowired on field (not recommended for testability).

Q48. Constructor injection vs setter injection?

Constructor Setter
Mandatory deps ✅ ❌
Immutability ✅ ❌
Circular deps ❌ (fails) ✅ (resolvable)
Readability Index-based Named setters
Spring recommendation Preferred Optional deps

Q49. What is BeanFactory vs ApplicationContext?

  • BeanFactory: basic IoC container; lazy initialization; minimal features.
  • ApplicationContext: extends BeanFactory; adds event publishing, i18n, AOP, eager initialization. Use ApplicationContext in production.

Q50. What is the default bean scope? Singleton — one instance per Spring container.

Q51. What are the bean scopes?

Scope Lifecycle
singleton One per container
prototype New instance per request
request One per HTTP request
session One per HTTP session
application One per ServletContext

Q52. Spring Bean Lifecycle?

  1. Instantiation → 2. Populate properties → 3. BeanNameAware, BeanFactoryAware → 4. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization → 5. @PostConstruct / InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet() / init-method → 6. Bean ready → 7. @PreDestroy / DisposableBean.destroy() / destroy-method.

Q53. Are singleton beans thread-safe? Not by default. Spring does not manage thread-safety. Singleton beans should be stateless or use synchronization.

Q54. What is component scanning? Spring scans the classpath for classes annotated with @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller and registers them as beans automatically.

Q55. @Component vs @Service vs @Repository vs @Controller? All are specializations of @Component. @Repository also adds persistence exception translation. @Service signals service layer. @Controller signals MVC controller. Semantically meaningful for readability and tooling.

Q56. What is @Autowired? How does it work with @Qualifier? @Autowired tells Spring to inject a matching bean by type. When multiple beans of the same type exist, @Qualifier("beanName") selects the correct one.

Q57. What is @Value? Injects property values from application.properties or environment variables. Supports SpEL expressions: @Value("${server.port}") or @Value("#{someBean.property}").

AOP

Q58. What is AOP and what problem does it solve? Aspect-Oriented Programming separates cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, transactions, caching) from business logic, avoiding code scattering and tangling.

Q59. Key AOP terms?

Term Meaning
Aspect Module encapsulating a cross-cutting concern
Join Point Point in execution (Spring AOP: always a method call)
Pointcut Predicate matching join points
Advice Action at a matched join point
Weaving Linking aspects with application code

Q60. Types of advice?

  • @Before — before the method.
  • @After — after (regardless of outcome).
  • @AfterReturning — after successful return.
  • @AfterThrowing — after exception.
  • @Around — surrounds; can control invocation with ProceedingJoinPoint.proceed().

Q61. Spring AOP proxy types?

  • JDK Dynamic Proxy: used when bean implements an interface.
  • CGLIB Proxy: used when bean has no interface (subclasses the class). Methods must be non-final and public.

Spring MVC & REST

Q62. What is DispatcherServlet? The front controller of Spring MVC. All HTTP requests pass through it. It delegates to HandlerMapping → Controller → ViewResolver → Response.

Q63. @RequestParam vs @PathVariable?

  • @PathVariable: extracts value from the URI path (/users/{id}).
  • @RequestParam: extracts value from query parameters (/users?id=1).

Q64. @Controller vs @RestController? @RestController = @Controller + @ResponseBody on every method. Returns JSON/XML directly without a view resolver.

Q65. What HTTP methods does REST use?

  • GET — read (safe, idempotent)
  • POST — create (not idempotent)
  • PUT — full update (idempotent)
  • PATCH — partial update
  • DELETE — delete (idempotent)

Q66. What is idempotency? Calling the same operation multiple times produces the same result. GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent; POST is not.

Q67. Is REST stateless? Yes. Each request contains all information needed to process it; no server-side session state between requests.

Q68. What is @Transactional? Wraps a method in a transaction. Spring creates a proxy that begins a transaction before the method and commits/rolls back after. Default rollback on RuntimeException; checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback by default.

Q69. Transaction propagation — what are the key types?

Propagation Behaviour
REQUIRED (default) Join existing or create new
REQUIRES_NEW Always create new; suspend existing
SUPPORTS Join if exists; otherwise no tx
NOT_SUPPORTED Suspend existing; run without tx
MANDATORY Must have existing tx; else exception
NEVER Must not have tx; else exception

Q70. Self-invocation problem with @Transactional? When a @Transactional method calls another @Transactional method in the same class, the AOP proxy is bypassed — the inner transaction annotation is ignored. Solution: inject the bean into itself, or use ApplicationContext to get the proxy.

Spring Boot

Q71. What is Spring Boot? An opinionated, convention-over-configuration framework built on top of Spring. It provides auto-configuration, embedded servers (Tomcat/Jetty/Undertow), and starter POMs to get production-ready apps running with minimal setup.

Q72. What is auto-configuration? Spring Boot reads META-INF/spring.factories (or AutoConfiguration.imports in Boot 3), detects JARs on the classpath, and conditionally configures beans using @Conditional* annotations. E.g., if DataSource driver is on classpath, a DataSource bean is auto-configured.

Q73. @SpringBootApplication = ? Combines: @Configuration + @EnableAutoConfiguration + @ComponentScan.

Q74. What is Spring Boot Actuator? Provides production-ready monitoring endpoints: /health, /metrics, /info, /env, /loggers, /threaddump, etc. Accessible over HTTP or JMX. Endpoints should be secured.

Q75. What is a Spring Boot Starter? A curated dependency descriptor (POM) that pulls in all necessary libraries for a feature. E.g., spring-boot-starter-web includes Spring MVC, Jackson, and embedded Tomcat.

Q76. Where does Spring Boot look for properties? Priority (highest to lowest): command-line args → SPRING_APPLICATION_JSON → OS env vars → application-{profile}.properties → application.properties (classpath root).

Q77. How to change embedded Tomcat port? Set server.port=8081 in application.properties or --server.port=8081 as CLI arg.

Spring Data JPA & Hibernate

Q78. What is JPA? Java Persistence API — a specification (not implementation) for ORM in Java. Hibernate is the most popular implementation.

Q79. What is Hibernate? Key advantages? Hibernate is a JPA ORM provider that maps Java objects to relational tables. Advantages: reduced JDBC boilerplate, HQL/JPQL queries, caching, lazy loading, dirty checking, database portability.

Q80. What is the difference between get() and load() in Hibernate?

  • get(): hits DB immediately; returns null if not found.
  • load(): returns a proxy; hits DB only when properties are accessed; throws ObjectNotFoundException if not found when accessed.

(In Spring Data JPA: findById() vs getOne()/getReferenceById())

Q81. Hibernate caching levels?

  • L1 Cache (Session cache): enabled by default; per-session; flushed when session closes.
  • L2 Cache: optional; shared across sessions; configured per entity (e.g., EhCache, Redis).
  • Query Cache: caches query results (needs L2 enabled).

Q82. What is lazy vs eager loading?

  • Lazy: associated entities are loaded on first access (default for @OneToMany, @ManyToMany).
  • Eager: associated entities are loaded immediately with the parent (default for @ManyToOne, @OneToOne).

Q83. What is N+1 query problem? When fetching N entities triggers N additional queries for each association. Solutions: JOIN FETCH in JPQL, @EntityGraph, @BatchSize, or @Fetch(FetchMode.SUBSELECT).

Q84. What is dirty checking in Hibernate? At the end of a transaction, Hibernate compares the current state of managed entities with their snapshot taken at load time. If different, it auto-generates UPDATE SQL — no need to call save() explicitly.

Q85. @OneToMany mapping — best practices? Use FetchType.LAZY, map the owning side with @JoinColumn, use Set over List for better performance, avoid bidirectional mappings unless necessary, and use @BatchSize or JOIN FETCH to avoid N+1.

Q86. What is PlatformTransactionManager? The Spring abstraction for transaction management. Different implementations: DataSourceTransactionManager (JDBC), JpaTransactionManager (JPA/Hibernate), JtaTransactionManager (distributed).

Q87. Spring Data JPA repository naming convention? Methods are parsed from their name: findByFirstNameAndLastName, findByAgeGreaterThan, findTop3ByOrderByCreatedAtDesc. Spring generates the JPQL automatically.

Spring Security

Q88. What is Spring Security? A powerful, customizable authentication and authorization framework for Spring applications. Based on servlet filters.

Q89. Authentication vs Authorization?

  • Authentication: verifying who the user is (login).
  • Authorization: determining what the user is allowed to do (access control). Authentication must come first.

Q90. What is the Security Filter Chain? An ordered chain of servlet filters (SecurityFilterChain) that intercepts every HTTP request. Key filters: UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter, BasicAuthenticationFilter, ExceptionTranslationFilter, FilterSecurityInterceptor.

Q91. What is SecurityContextHolder? Stores the authenticated Principal in a ThreadLocal per request. Access the current user with SecurityContextHolder.getContext().getAuthentication().

Q92. What is password encoding? Never store plain-text passwords. Spring Security's PasswordEncoder (e.g., BCryptPasswordEncoder) hashes passwords with a salt. BCrypt is the recommended default.

Q93. @Secured vs @PreAuthorize?

  • @Secured({"ROLE_ADMIN"}): simple role check.
  • @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') and #id == principal.id"): supports SpEL expressions for fine-grained control.

Q94. How to implement JWT authentication in Spring Boot?

  1. User logs in → /auth/login → server validates credentials → returns JWT.
  2. Client sends JWT in Authorization: Bearer <token> header.
  3. A custom OncePerRequestFilter validates JWT, sets Authentication in SecurityContextHolder.
  4. Stateless (SessionCreationPolicy.STATELESS).

PART 3 — SYSTEM DESIGN (RELEVANT TO CV)

Q95. What is microservices architecture? Pros/Cons?

  • Pros: independent deployment, technology diversity, fault isolation, scalability per service.
  • Cons: distributed system complexity, network latency, eventual consistency, operational overhead (many services to monitor).

Q96. How do microservices communicate?

  • Synchronous: REST (HTTP/HTTPS), gRPC.
  • Asynchronous: message brokers (Kafka, RabbitMQ, AWS SQS/SNS) — decoupled, resilient.

Q97. What is an API Gateway? Single entry point for all client requests. Handles routing, rate limiting, authentication, SSL termination, logging, and request aggregation. Examples: AWS API Gateway, Kong, Nginx.

Q98. What is event-driven architecture? Services communicate by publishing and consuming events via a message broker. Producers do not know consumers. Enables loose coupling, replay capability, and scalability. Kafka is the common choice.

Q99. What is Kafka? Key concepts? Distributed, append-only log for high-throughput event streaming.

  • Topic: category of events.
  • Partition: unit of parallelism; ordered within partition.
  • Offset: position of a message in a partition.
  • Consumer Group: each partition consumed by one consumer in a group; enables parallel consumption.
  • Retention: messages kept for a configurable duration (not deleted on consume).

Q100. What is Redis? How do you use it? In-memory key-value store supporting strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams. Use cases: caching, session storage, rate limiting, pub/sub, leaderboards. Sub-millisecond latency.

Q101. Caching strategies?

Strategy How
Cache-Aside App checks cache; on miss, loads from DB and populates cache
Write-Through Write to cache and DB simultaneously
Write-Behind Write to cache; async write to DB
Read-Through Cache sits in front; fetches from DB on miss

Cache invalidation: TTL expiry, event-driven invalidation, or version-based keys.

Q102. What is CAP theorem? A distributed system can guarantee only 2 of 3: Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance. Since partitions are unavoidable in real networks, systems choose between CP (consistent) or AP (available) when partitions occur.

Q103. How do you design a high-availability system?

  • No single point of failure (active-active or active-passive redundancy).
  • Load balancing across multiple instances.
  • Health checks and auto-recovery.
  • Circuit breaker pattern (Resilience4j, Hystrix).
  • Multi-AZ / multi-region deployments.
  • Graceful degradation and fallback logic.

Q104. What is a circuit breaker pattern? Monitors failures for a service call. When failure rate exceeds threshold → circuit OPENS → fallback is returned immediately (no waiting). After a timeout → HALF-OPEN → probes if service recovered → CLOSE if healthy. Prevents cascade failures.

Q105. What is database sharding? Horizontal partitioning: data is distributed across multiple DB instances (shards) by a shard key (e.g., user_id % N). Enables horizontal scaling. Challenges: cross-shard queries, rebalancing, hotspots.

Q106. SQL vs NoSQL — when to choose?

SQL (PostgreSQL, Aurora) NoSQL (DynamoDB, MongoDB)
Schema Fixed Flexible
Transactions ACID Eventual consistency (varies)
Scale Vertical (+ read replicas) Horizontal
Use case Complex relations, reporting High-scale, document/KV

Q107. What is CQRS? Command Query Responsibility Segregation — separate read model and write model. Writes update the command side; reads use optimized projections (read replicas, caches, Elasticsearch). Often combined with Event Sourcing.

Q108. Observability: Metrics, Logging, Tracing?

  • Metrics: numeric measurements over time (Prometheus + Grafana, CloudWatch).
  • Logging: structured log events (ELK Stack, CloudWatch Logs).
  • Tracing: distributed trace of a request across services (Jaeger, AWS X-Ray, OpenTelemetry).

Q109. What is a distributed lock? A mechanism to ensure only one node executes a critical section at a time. In Redis: SET key value NX PX <ttl> (Redlock algorithm for multi-node Redis). In databases: SELECT FOR UPDATE.

Q110. Rate limiting strategies?

  • Token Bucket: tokens replenish at fixed rate; allows bursts.
  • Leaky Bucket: requests processed at fixed rate; excess queued or dropped.
  • Fixed Window: count requests per window; edge case at window boundary.
  • Sliding Window: smoothed version; avoids boundary spikes.

PART 4 — AWS / CLOUD (FROM YOUR CV)

Q111. AWS Aurora vs RDS? Aurora is AWS's cloud-native relational DB (MySQL/PostgreSQL compatible). Up to 5× faster than standard MySQL. Auto-scaling storage, up to 15 read replicas, automatic failover in < 30 seconds. Better HA than standard RDS.

Q112. DynamoDB — key concepts? Managed NoSQL (key-value + document). Partition key (hash) determines shard. Optional sort key for range queries. Provisioned or on-demand capacity. Supports DAX for microsecond caching. Global Tables for multi-region replication.

Q113. What is AWS WAF? Web Application Firewall. Protects against common web exploits (SQL injection, XSS, bad bots). Deployed in front of ALB, API Gateway, or CloudFront. Rules can be custom or managed (AWS Managed Rules).

Q114. What is an SLA and how do you maintain it? Service Level Agreement defines uptime/latency commitments (e.g., 99.9% = 8.7h downtime/year). Maintained through: proactive monitoring (CloudWatch alarms), runbooks, auto-scaling, incident response, chaos engineering.

Q115. What is MTTR and MTBF?

  • MTTR (Mean Time To Recover): avg time to restore after failure. Reduce with good runbooks, automation, alerting.
  • MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures): avg time between failures. Improve with code quality, testing, redundancy.

Total: 115 Q&A covering Java Core, Spring/Boot, Hibernate/JPA, System Design, and AWS — tailored to Pham Khanh Tuong's CV.

Interviewer Question Bank — Senior/Expert Java Engineer (46 Questions)

You are the interviewer. Use these questions to evaluate Senior/Expert candidates.

Difficulty: HARD | Expected: Deep understanding, not memorized answers

SECTION A — JAVA CORE (25 Questions)

A1. Memory Model & Concurrency

Q1: Explain the Java Memory Model's happens-before guarantees. Give 3 specific rules and a code example where violating them causes a bug.

Expected answer must include: volatile write/read ordering, monitor lock/unlock, thread start/join. The candidate should demonstrate that without happens-before, one thread may never see another's writes due to CPU caching and instruction reordering.

Red flag: candidate only says "volatile makes variables visible to all threads" without explaining the ordering guarantees.

Score: 10 if mentions memory barriers + transitivity. 5 if only mentions visibility.

Q2: What is the difference between synchronized, ReentrantLock, and StampedLock? When would you choose each?

Expected: synchronized = intrinsic monitor, non-interruptible, no try-lock. ReentrantLock = explicit, supports tryLock, lockInterruptibly, multiple Conditions. StampedLock = optimistic read mode (no lock acquisition for reads), converts to read/write lock if validation fails. Choose StampedLock for read-heavy workloads where writes are rare.

Red flag: cannot explain optimistic read mode or when StampedLock is dangerous (not reentrant).

Q3: Explain virtual thread pinning in Java 21. What causes it, how do you detect it, and how do you fix it?

Expected: synchronized blocks pin the virtual thread to its carrier thread. Native JNI calls also pin. Detect with -Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full. Fix by replacing synchronized with ReentrantLock. Must mention HikariCP pre-5.1 used synchronized internally.

Q4: What is the ABA problem in lock-free programming? How does AtomicStampedReference solve it?

Expected: Thread reads value A, another thread changes A->B->A. First thread's CAS succeeds (sees A) but the state has changed. AtomicStampedReference adds a version stamp — CAS checks both value AND stamp.

Q5: Design a thread-safe LRU cache without using synchronized on every operation. What data structures do you use?

Expected: ConcurrentHashMap for O(1) lookup + a lock-striped doubly-linked list for LRU ordering. Or use Caffeine library which implements W-TinyLFU. Candidate should discuss trade-offs: strict LRU vs approximate LRU, contention under high concurrency.

Red flag: suggests Collections.synchronizedMap(new LinkedHashMap()) — single global lock, terrible under concurrency.

A2. Generics & Type System

Q6: Explain PECS (Producer Extends, Consumer Super) with a real API example. Why can't you add elements to a List?

Expected: The list could be List\ or List\ — compiler cannot know which, so writing is unsafe. Reading is safe because every element is at least a Number. Real example: Collections.copy(dest, src) where dest is ? super T and src is ? extends T.

Q7: What is type erasure? Give 3 concrete consequences. What is a bridge method and when does the compiler generate one?

Expected: Cannot instanceof generics, cannot create generic arrays, cannot overload by generic parameter. Bridge method generated for covariant return types and generic method overrides to maintain polymorphism after erasure. Must mention Method.isBridge() for reflection-aware frameworks.

Q8: What is a recursive generic type bound? Write the signature for a Comparable self-referencing pattern.

Expected: class Enum> — the classic self-referencing bound. Purpose: ensures that compareTo, etc. operate on the same type, not a parent type. Candidate should be able to write: > and explain why it's more type-safe than .

A3. Collections Internals

Q9: Explain HashMap's internal structure in Java 8+. What happens when two keys have the same hashCode? What is treeification?

Expected: Array of Node buckets. Collision = linked list in bucket. At 8+ entries AND capacity >= 64, list converts to Red-Black Tree (O(log n) worst case vs O(n)). Must mention load factor 0.75, capacity doubling, rehashing.

Q10: What is the difference between ConcurrentHashMap and Hashtable? How does ConcurrentHashMap achieve thread safety without locking the entire map?

Expected: Java 8 ConcurrentHashMap uses CAS (compare-and-swap) for writes to empty buckets, synchronized on the first node of each bucket for collision cases. No global lock. Read operations are mostly lock-free. Hashtable locks the entire map on every operation.

Red flag: says "ConcurrentHashMap uses segment locking" — that was Java 7, not Java 8+.

Q11: Implement a bounded blocking queue using ReentrantLock and Condition. Explain why you need two Conditions.

Expected: One Condition for "not full" (producers wait), one for "not empty" (consumers wait). With Object.wait/notify you only have one wait set — you'd wake up producers and consumers indiscriminately.

A4. JVM Internals

Q12: Explain the G1 garbage collector's region-based approach. What are humongous objects? How do you tune G1 for a low-latency service?

Expected: Heap divided into equal-sized regions (1-32MB). Regions can be Eden, Survivor, Old, or Humongous (object > 50% region size). G1 targets a max pause time (-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis). Tuning: increase heap, increase region size for large objects, avoid humongous allocations.

Q13: What are the 5 types of OutOfMemoryError? For each, explain the cause and the fix.

Expected: Java heap space (leak/insufficient Xmx), Metaspace (classloader leak), unable to create native thread (too many threads), Direct buffer memory (NIO buffers), GC overhead limit exceeded (thrashing). Candidate should mention monitoring tools: jmap, jcmd, NativeMemoryTracking.

Q14: Explain class loading delegation model. What is the difference between ClassNotFoundException and NoClassDefFoundError?

Expected: Parent-first delegation: App -> Platform -> Bootstrap. CNFE = explicit Class.forName() miss (checked exception). NCDFE = class existed at compile time but missing at runtime (Error). Static initializer failure chains: first access = ExceptionInInitializerError, subsequent = NCDFE.

A5. Functional & Modern Java

Q15: Explain Stream lazy evaluation. What happens internally when you call .filter().map().findFirst() on a million-element list?

Expected: No processing until terminal operation. Elements flow one-by-one through the pipeline. findFirst() short-circuits — stops after first match. Only processes elements until the first match is found, not all million.

Q16: What is the difference between thenApply, thenCompose, and thenCombine in CompletableFuture? What happens to exceptions in a CF chain if no handler is added?

Expected: thenApply = map (T -> U), thenCompose = flatMap (T -> CF\), thenCombine = merge two independent CFs. Unhandled exceptions are silently swallowed unless .get()/.join() is called or a terminal .exceptionally() is added.

Q17: What are ScopedValue and StructuredTaskScope in Java 21? Why are they better than ThreadLocal for virtual threads?

Expected: ScopedValue = immutable, auto-scoped, no remove() needed, efficient VT inheritance without copying. StructuredTaskScope = structured concurrency: subtasks are bound to the scope's lifetime, cannot outlive the parent.

SECTION B — SPRING BOOT (20 Questions)

B1. Spring Core & DI

Q18: Explain the Spring Bean lifecycle in detail. What are the hooks available at each stage?

Expected: Constructor -> @PostConstruct -> InitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet -> custom init-method -> Bean ready -> @PreDestroy -> DisposableBean.destroy -> custom destroy-method. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessBeforeInitialization/After runs around the init phase. AOP proxies are created in BeanPostProcessor.

Q19: What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, and @Controller? Is there a functional difference beyond semantics?

Expected: @Repository adds automatic exception translation (PersistenceExceptionTranslationPostProcessor converts JPA exceptions to Spring DataAccessException hierarchy). @Controller enables handler mapping for MVC. @Service and @Component have NO functional difference — pure semantic.

Red flag: says "they all do the same thing" without mentioning @Repository exception translation.

Q20: Explain Spring's BeanPostProcessor. How does Spring AOP use it to create proxies? What is the difference between JDK dynamic proxy and CGLIB?

Expected: BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization wraps beans in AOP proxies. JDK proxy = interface-based (Proxy.newProxyInstance), CGLIB = subclass-based (bytecode generation). Since Spring 6, CGLIB is default even for interface beans. JDK proxy cannot intercept final methods; CGLIB cannot proxy final classes.

Q21: What is a circular dependency in Spring? How does Spring resolve it for singleton beans? When does it fail?

Expected: Spring resolves constructor injection cycles by using a three-level cache (singletonObjects, earlySingletonObjects, singletonFactories). It creates an early reference before full initialization. Fails with: constructor injection (cannot create early ref), prototype scope (no caching), and @Async beans (proxy creation timing).

B2. Spring Security

Q22: Walk through the Spring Security filter chain. Name at least 5 filters in order and explain what each does.

Expected: SecurityContextPersistenceFilter, UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter (or BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter for JWT), ExceptionTranslationFilter, AuthorizationFilter. Candidate should explain how a JWT token is validated: extract from header -> decode -> verify signature -> check claims -> set SecurityContext.

Q23: Explain the difference between @PreAuthorize, @Secured, and @RolesAllowed. How does method-level security work internally?

Expected: @PreAuthorize uses SpEL (most powerful: @PreAuthorize("hasRole('ADMIN') and #userId == authentication.principal.id")). @Secured and @RolesAllowed are simpler, role-based only. Internally: Spring creates AOP proxies using MethodSecurityInterceptor that checks authorization before method invocation.

B3. Spring Data & Performance

Q24: What is the N+1 query problem in Spring Data JPA? Give 3 different solutions.

Expected: Loading a parent entity with lazy collection -> 1 query for parent + N queries for each child. Solutions: (1) JOIN FETCH in JPQL, (2) @EntityGraph, (3) @BatchSize on the collection, (4) Specification with fetch join. Must understand that JOIN FETCH with pagination causes "HHH90003004: firstResult/maxResults specified with collection fetch" warning.

Q25: How does Spring Boot auto-configuration work? Explain the role of META-INF/spring/org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.AutoConfiguration.imports and @Conditional annotations.

Expected: Spring Boot scans the AutoConfiguration.imports file (replaced spring.factories in Boot 3), loads configuration classes, and applies @Conditional checks (@ConditionalOnClass, @ConditionalOnMissingBean, @ConditionalOnProperty) to decide which beans to create. The application's explicit beans always override auto-configured ones.

Q26: Explain Spring's caching abstraction. What happens when you put @Cacheable on a method? What are the pitfalls?

Expected: AOP proxy intercepts method call, checks cache by key, returns cached value if hit, calls method and stores result if miss. Pitfalls: (1) self-invocation bypasses proxy (same as @Transactional), (2) cache key must implement equals/hashCode, (3) null values are cached by default (use unless="#result == null"), (4) @CacheEvict with allEntries=true on write methods.

SECTION C — HIBERNATE / JPA (20 Questions)

C1. Entity Lifecycle & Caching

Q27: Explain the 4 JPA entity states. What transitions cause SQL statements to be generated?

Expected: New (transient) -> Managed (persistent) -> Detached -> Removed. persist() = New -> Managed (INSERT on flush). merge() = Detached -> Managed (SELECT + UPDATE). remove() = Managed -> Removed (DELETE on flush). The EntityManager's persistence context is the first-level cache — managed entities are identity-guaranteed (same PK = same object reference).

Q28: What is the Hibernate first-level cache vs second-level cache? When would you enable L2 cache and what are the risks?

Expected: L1 = per-EntityManager/session, always on, not configurable. L2 = shared across sessions, optional (EhCache, Infinispan, Hazelcast). Enable L2 for read-heavy, rarely-modified reference data (countries, config). Risks: stale data when modified outside Hibernate (direct SQL, another service), memory consumption, cache invalidation complexity in clustered environments.

Q29: What is the difference between FetchType.LAZY and FetchType.EAGER? What is LazyInitializationException and how do you avoid it?

Expected: LAZY = load collection on first access (proxy/bytecode enhancement). EAGER = load immediately with parent (JOIN or subselect). LazyInitializationException = accessing lazy collection after EntityManager/session is closed. Avoid: (1) Open Session in View (anti-pattern but common), (2) DTO projections, (3) JOIN FETCH, (4) @EntityGraph, (5) @Transactional on service method to keep session open.

Red flag: suggests Open Session in View as the primary solution without mentioning it's an anti-pattern.

Q30: Explain the difference between @OneToMany(mappedBy) and @JoinColumn. What are the SQL implications?

Expected: mappedBy = the child owns the FK column (child table has the FK). @JoinColumn on @OneToMany without mappedBy = parent owns but Hibernate generates a JOIN TABLE (unless @JoinColumn explicitly specified). With mappedBy: INSERT child directly with FK. Without: INSERT child + INSERT into join table (or UPDATE child's FK column). Always prefer mappedBy for bidirectional.

C2. Query Optimization

Q31: What is the difference between JPQL, Criteria API, and native SQL? When do you use each?

Expected: JPQL = string-based, entity-oriented, DB-agnostic, validated at startup. Criteria API = type-safe, programmatic, good for dynamic queries (variable WHERE clauses). Native SQL = full DB feature access (window functions, CTEs, RETURNING, FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED). Use Criteria/Specification for dynamic search filters, native for DB-specific features.

Q32: Explain Hibernate's dirty checking mechanism. What is the performance implication and how do you optimize it?

Expected: Hibernate snapshots entity state at load time. At flush, compares current state with snapshot field-by-field. For N managed entities with M fields each, dirty checking is O(N*M). Optimize: (1) @DynamicUpdate (only update changed columns), (2) read-only queries with @Transactional(readOnly=true) (skips dirty checking), (3) DTO projections for read-only use cases, (4) detach entities you don't need to modify.

Q33: What is the @Version annotation? How does optimistic locking work in JPA? What happens on conflict?

Expected: @Version field (int/long/timestamp) is auto-incremented on each update. UPDATE sets WHERE version = :currentVersion. If another transaction already incremented the version, the UPDATE affects 0 rows -> OptimisticLockException. The application must handle this: retry the operation with fresh data, or return a conflict error to the client.

Q34: Explain batch inserts/updates in Hibernate. Why is the default behavior slow and how do you enable batching?

Expected: Default = one INSERT per entity per flush. Enable batching: spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size=50, hibernate.order_inserts=true, hibernate.order_updates=true. With IDENTITY generation strategy, batching is DISABLED (Hibernate needs the generated ID immediately). Use SEQUENCE or TABLE strategy for batch-compatible ID generation.

Red flag: doesn't know that IDENTITY strategy breaks batching.

C3. Advanced Mapping

Q35: Explain @Inheritance strategies in JPA. Compare SINGLE_TABLE, JOINED, and TABLE_PER_CLASS. Which do you recommend and why?

Expected: SINGLE_TABLE = one table, discriminator column, NULLable columns for subtype-specific fields. Best performance (no JOINs), worst data integrity (NULLs). JOINED = one table per class, JOINs on query. Best normalization, worst query performance. TABLE_PER_CLASS = separate tables, no JOINs but polymorphic queries use UNION. Recommend: SINGLE_TABLE for most cases (speed), JOINED only when data integrity is critical and subtypes have many fields.

Q36: What is an aggregate root in DDD? How does it affect your JPA repository design?

Expected: Aggregate root = single entry point for a cluster of domain objects. Only the root has a repository. CascadeType.ALL + orphanRemoval=true on child collections. No OrderItemRepository — all operations go through OrderRepository. One transaction per aggregate.

SECTION D — TRANSACTIONS (15 Questions)

D1. Spring @Transactional Internals

Q37: Explain how @Transactional works under the hood. What is the self-invocation problem and how do you fix it?

Expected: Spring creates an AOP proxy. External calls go through the proxy which starts/commits/rolls back the transaction. Self-invocation (this.method()) bypasses the proxy — no transaction management. Fix: (1) inject self, (2) use ApplicationContext.getBean(), (3) extract to a separate service, (4) use TransactionTemplate programmatically.

This is the #1 most important question. If the candidate doesn't know this, they are not senior level.

Q38: Explain all 7 propagation levels. Give a real use case for REQUIRES_NEW and NOT_SUPPORTED.

Expected: REQUIRED (default, join or create), SUPPORTS (join or none), MANDATORY (join or throw), REQUIRES_NEW (always new, suspend outer), NOT_SUPPORTED (suspend outer, run without tx), NEVER (throw if tx exists), NESTED (savepoint within outer tx). REQUIRES_NEW use case: audit logging that must persist even if the main transaction rolls back. NOT_SUPPORTED: long-running read operations that shouldn't hold a transaction open.

Q39: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in @Transactional rollback behavior? How do you customize it?

Expected: Default: rollback on unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException, Error). Checked exceptions do NOT trigger rollback by default. Customize with @Transactional(rollbackFor = Exception.class) or noRollbackFor. This is a common source of bugs — throwing a checked exception assumes the transaction will roll back, but it commits instead.

Red flag: doesn't know the checked exception behavior — this causes real production data corruption.

Q40: Explain REQUIRES_NEW + deadlock scenario. How can an inner REQUIRES_NEW transaction deadlock with the outer transaction?

Expected: Outer tx holds a row lock (SELECT FOR UPDATE or UPDATE). Inner tx (REQUIRES_NEW) tries to lock the same row — but the outer tx's lock won't release until the outer tx commits, which won't happen until the inner tx completes. Deadlock. Fix: avoid locking the same rows in nested transactions, or use async processing for the inner operation.

D2. Isolation Levels & Locking

Q41: Explain the 4 transaction isolation levels. What anomalies does each prevent? Which is the PostgreSQL default?

Expected: READ_UNCOMMITTED (allows dirty reads), READ_COMMITTED (prevents dirty reads — PostgreSQL default), REPEATABLE_READ (prevents non-repeatable reads), SERIALIZABLE (prevents phantom reads). Must explain: dirty read = reading uncommitted data, non-repeatable read = same query returns different values, phantom read = new rows appear in range query.

Q42: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking? When do you use each?

Expected: Optimistic = @Version field, no DB lock held, conflict detected at commit time. Best for low-contention, read-heavy. Pessimistic = SELECT FOR UPDATE, DB row lock held for transaction duration. Best for high-contention, short transactions. Must mention: optimistic locking scales better but requires conflict handling; pessimistic prevents conflicts but risks deadlocks.

Q43: Explain SELECT FOR UPDATE, SELECT FOR UPDATE NOWAIT, and SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED. Give a use case for each.

Expected: FOR UPDATE = acquire row lock, wait indefinitely. FOR UPDATE NOWAIT = fail immediately if locked (throw exception). SKIP LOCKED = skip locked rows, return only unlocked ones. Use case for SKIP LOCKED: job queue — multiple consumers each grab unlocked jobs, no contention.

D3. Distributed Transactions

Q44: Why is 2PC (two-phase commit) considered an anti-pattern in microservices? What alternatives exist?

Expected: 2PC requires a coordinator, blocking participants, and a global lock — doesn't scale. If coordinator fails, participants are stuck in uncertainty. Alternatives: (1) Saga pattern (choreography or orchestration), (2) Outbox pattern (transactional outbox + event relay), (3) eventual consistency with idempotent consumers.

Q45: Explain the Outbox pattern. How does it guarantee exactly-once message delivery without distributed transactions?

Expected: Write business data AND outbox event in the same local transaction. A separate process (CDC with Debezium, or polling) reads the outbox table and publishes to Kafka. If publishing fails, it retries from the outbox. If publishing succeeds but the outbox row isn't marked as sent, the consumer must be idempotent. The local transaction guarantees atomicity between business data and the event.

Q46: What is a Saga? Compare choreography vs orchestration. What happens when a step fails midway?

Expected: Choreography = each service listens for events and reacts (decentralized, harder to trace). Orchestration = a central coordinator directs the flow (centralized, easier to monitor). On failure: execute compensating transactions for all completed steps in reverse order. Must mention: compensating transactions must be idempotent and may fail themselves.

SCORING GUIDE

Candidate Levels

Level Score Range Signal
Junior 0-15 correct Knows syntax, not internals
Mid 16-28 correct Understands concepts, some gaps
Senior 29-38 correct Deep understanding, can explain trade-offs
Expert 39-46 correct Knows edge cases, production war stories

Red Flags (Immediate Reject)

  • Cannot explain @Transactional self-invocation problem
  • Says "ConcurrentHashMap uses segment locking" (Java 7 knowledge, not updated)
  • Suggests Open Session in View as primary LazyInitializationException fix
  • Cannot explain checked vs unchecked exception rollback behavior
  • Uses Collections.synchronizedMap for concurrent cache
  • Says HashMap is thread-safe
  • Cannot explain the difference between optimistic and pessimistic locking

Green Flags (Strong Hire Signal)

  • Mentions production incidents they debugged related to these topics
  • Discusses trade-offs without being asked ("it depends on...")
  • Knows library internals (HikariCP, Caffeine, Resilience4j)
  • Brings up monitoring and observability when discussing transactions
  • Mentions Effective Java or specific JEP numbers

Expert Deep Dive — Principal-Level Java Interview

Interviewer Persona: Principal Engineer, Platform Infrastructure

Candidate Target: Senior/Expert Java Backend Engineer

Tone: Architectural trade-offs. No "What is..." questions.

PILLAR 1 — JVM INTERNALS & OPTIMIZATION

Q1: Describe the internal mechanism when HotSpot decides to JIT-compile a method. How do C1 and C2 compilers interact under Tiered Compilation, and how does On-Stack Replacement (OSR) fit in?

Expert-Level Insight:

HotSpot uses Tiered Compilation (default since Java 8) with 5 levels:

  • Level 0: Interpreter — no compilation, collects basic profiling
  • Level 1: C1 simple compile — no profiling, fast startup
  • Level 2: C1 with limited profiling (invocation + backedge counters)
  • Level 3: C1 with full profiling (branch, type, call-site profiles)
  • Level 4: C2 full optimization — aggressive inlining, escape analysis, loop unrolling, vectorization

Typical path: L0 -> L3 (C1 with profiling) -> L4 (C2 optimized). If C2 queue is backed up, methods may stay at L1 or L2 temporarily.

Compilation thresholds: method invocation count + backedge count (loop iterations). Default: ~10,000 invocations for C2. Tunable via -XX:CompileThreshold.

On-Stack Replacement (OSR): allows JIT to replace an interpreted method WHILE IT IS RUNNING — specifically inside a long-running loop. Without OSR, a method with a million-iteration loop would run entirely in the interpreter because JIT only kicks in on the NEXT invocation. OSR compiles the loop body and transfers execution mid-loop.

Code Cache (-XX:ReservedCodeCacheSize): JIT-compiled native code is stored here. If it fills up, JIT stops compiling — performance cliff. Monitor with jcmd Compiler.codecache. Default 240MB. For large apps with many hot methods, increase to 512MB.

Deoptimization: C2 makes speculative optimizations (monomorphic call sites, branch predictions). If assumptions are violated at runtime, C2 deoptimizes — drops back to interpreter and recompiles. Common triggers: new subclass loaded (invalidates devirtualization), uncommon trap hit.

A top-tier candidate mentions: -XX:+PrintCompilation to trace JIT decisions, -XX:+UnlockDiagnosticVMOptions -XX:+LogCompilation for XML compilation log analysis with JITWatch.

Q2: Compare the architectural impact of G1, ZGC, and Shenandoah for a payment processing service that requires sub-5ms p99 latency at 50K TPS. Which would you choose and how would you tune it?

Expert-Level Insight:

G1GC (default since Java 9):

  • Region-based, generational. Pause target: -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 (default).
  • Young GC: stop-the-world (STW), typically 5-30ms. Mixed GC: STW, can exceed 50ms for large heaps.
  • For sub-5ms p99: G1 CANNOT reliably achieve this. Young GC alone often exceeds 5ms.

ZGC (production-ready since Java 15):

  • Concurrent, region-based, uses colored pointers (metadata in pointer bits) and load barriers.
  • Max STW pause: < 1ms regardless of heap size (even terabyte heaps). Pause is O(1) — not proportional to heap or live set.
  • Concurrent relocation: moves objects while application runs. Load barrier checks if pointer is stale and self-heals.
  • Trade-off: ~5-15% throughput overhead vs G1 due to load barrier cost on every pointer dereference.

Shenandoah (Red Hat, similar goals to ZGC):

  • Concurrent compaction using Brooks forwarding pointers (extra word per object header).
  • Sub-10ms pauses. Slightly higher memory overhead than ZGC (forwarding pointer per object).
  • Available in OpenJDK but NOT in Oracle JDK.

For sub-5ms p99 at 50K TPS: ZGC is the clear choice. Tuning:


-XX:+UseZGC -XX:+ZGenerational    # Generational ZGC (Java 21+, much better throughput)
-Xms4g -Xmx4g                     # Fixed heap — avoid resize pauses
-XX:SoftMaxHeapSize=3g             # Prefer returning memory to OS
-XX:ConcGCThreads=2                # Limit GC CPU — leave headroom for application

A top-tier candidate mentions: generational ZGC (Java 21) dramatically reduces allocation rate pressure. Also mentions that sub-5ms p99 requires more than just GC tuning — safepoint time, thread scheduling jitter, and kernel scheduling (-XX:+UseTransparentHugePages) all contribute to tail latency.

Q3: How would you perform a memory footprint analysis of a Java application running in a container with a 2GB memory limit that is being OOM-killed by the kernel (not JVM OOM)?

Expert-Level Insight:

Kernel OOM-kill means the PROCESS exceeded the container's cgroup memory limit — this includes heap, metaspace, thread stacks, native memory, direct buffers, and mapped files. JVM OOM only fires when the HEAP is full.

Step-by-step analysis:

1. Enable NativeMemoryTracking: -XX:NativeMemoryTracking=summary (5-10% overhead)

2. Baseline: jcmd VM.native_memory summary

3. Diff over time: jcmd VM.native_memory summary.diff (shows growth)

Memory breakdown:


Java Heap:     -Xmx (configured)
Metaspace:     class metadata (~100-300MB for Spring Boot apps)
Thread Stacks: num_threads * -Xss (default 1MB per thread)
Code Cache:    JIT compiled code (~100-240MB)
Direct Buffers: NIO ByteBuffer.allocateDirect (Netty, gRPC)
GC overhead:   G1 remembers sets, card tables (~10% of heap)
Native libs:    JNI, zlib, SSL
Mapped files:   shared libraries, JAR files

Common culprits for container OOM-kill:

  • Too many threads: 500 threads * 1MB stack = 500MB not counted in -Xmx
  • Metaspace leak: CGLIB proxy generation, Groovy scripts, hot-reloading
  • Direct buffer leak: Netty ByteBuf not released
  • Heap too large: -Xmx=1.5g leaves only 500MB for everything else in a 2GB container

Container memory formula:

-Xmx + MaxMetaspaceSize + (threads * Xss) + ReservedCodeCacheSize + DirectMemorySize + GC_overhead + native_libs

Rule of thumb: set -Xmx to no more than 75% of container memory limit. For 2GB container: -Xmx1400m -XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=256m -XX:ReservedCodeCacheSize=128m -Xss512k.

PILLAR 2 — FRAMEWORK INTERNALS

Q4: Describe the internal mechanism when Spring creates an AOP proxy for a @Transactional bean. How does proxyTargetClass affect this, and what fails silently when a final method is involved?

Expert-Level Insight:

Proxy creation pipeline:

1. BeanPostProcessor.postProcessAfterInitialization() — AbstractAutoProxyCreator checks if bean has AOP advice (e.g., @Transactional)

2. If yes, creates a proxy using ProxyFactory:

- proxyTargetClass=false (legacy default): JDK Dynamic Proxy — only if bean implements an interface. Creates $Proxy0 implementing the interface.

- proxyTargetClass=true (Spring Boot default since 2.0): CGLIB — generates a runtime subclass BeanClass$$SpringCGLIB$$0. Overrides methods to add advice.

Final method trap (CGLIB):

CGLIB creates a subclass. Java forbids overriding final methods. So @Transactional on a final method is silently ignored — no compilation error, no runtime error, no warning. The method is called directly, bypassing the proxy. The transaction is never started.


@Service
public class OrderService {
    @Transactional
    public final void placeOrder(Order o) { // SILENTLY IGNORED — no tx!
        repository.save(o);
    }
}

@Configuration(proxyBeanMethods = false) (Lite mode):

By default, @Configuration classes are CGLIB-proxied so that @Bean method calls within the same class return the singleton bean (inter-bean references). With proxyBeanMethods=false, no CGLIB proxy is created — faster startup, less memory, but @Bean methods become regular methods (each call creates a new instance). Spring Boot 3 auto-configurations use lite mode for performance.

A top-tier candidate mentions: spring.aop.proxy-target-class=true is the Boot default. They also mention that private methods are also silently ignored by both JDK proxy and CGLIB (private methods cannot be overridden/proxied).

Q5: How does @Transactional propagation break when an async boundary is introduced? Describe the failure scenario with @Async + REQUIRES_NEW.

Expert-Level Insight:

@Transactional context is bound to the current thread via ThreadLocal (TransactionSynchronizationManager). When @Async dispatches to another thread, the new thread has NO transaction context — the ThreadLocal is empty.

Failure scenario:


@Service
public class OrderService {
    @Transactional
    public void processOrder(Order order) {
        repository.save(order);           // TX-1 on thread-1
        notificationService.sendAsync(order); // @Async — runs on thread-2
        // TX-1 commits on thread-1
    }
}

@Service
public class NotificationService {
    @Async
    @Transactional(propagation = REQUIRES_NEW)
    public void sendAsync(Order order) {
        // REQUIRES_NEW creates TX-2 on thread-2
        // But TX-2 is completely independent of TX-1
        // If TX-1 rolls back AFTER sendAsync was dispatched, 
        // TX-2 has already committed — data inconsistency
    }
}

The deeper problem: @Async dispatches immediately — the method is queued to the async executor BEFORE the caller's transaction commits. If processOrder fails after dispatching, TX-1 rolls back but sendAsync has already started (or finished) on TX-2.

Correct patterns:

1. TransactionSynchronization: register a callback that fires AFTER commit:


TransactionSynchronizationManager.registerSynchronization(
    new TransactionSynchronization() {
        public void afterCommit() {
            notificationService.sendAsync(order); // only fires after TX-1 commits
        }
    });

2. @TransactionalEventListener(phase = AFTER_COMMIT): Spring 4.2+ event-driven approach.

3. Outbox pattern: write event to outbox table within TX-1. Separate process polls and dispatches async.

Q6: In a distributed microservice environment, describe how Hibernate L2 cache becomes a consistency risk. How would you mitigate stale reads without disabling the cache entirely?

Expert-Level Insight:

Hibernate L2 cache is JVM-local by default (EhCache, Caffeine). In a multi-instance deployment, Instance A updates an entity and invalidates its local L2 cache. Instance B still has the stale version cached — serves stale data until TTL expiry.

Cache concurrency strategies and their distributed behavior:

Strategy Behavior Distributed risk
READ_ONLY Never invalidated Safe only for truly immutable data
NONSTRICT_READ_WRITE No lock, eventual consistency Stale reads during write windows
READ_WRITE Soft lock during update Still JVM-local — other instances stale
TRANSACTIONAL JTA 2PC with cache Slow, complex, rarely used

Mitigation options (without disabling L2):

1. Distributed cache provider: Replace EhCache with Infinispan (clustered, invalidation mode) or Hazelcast (replicated/distributed map). Cache invalidation is broadcast to all cluster nodes via multicast or TCP.

2. Short TTL + read-through: Set timeToLiveSeconds=30. Accept 30s staleness window. On write, explicitly evict: sessionFactory.getCache().evictEntityData(Entity.class, id).

3. Cache only reference data: Countries, currencies, config values — data that changes rarely. Never L2-cache transactional entities (orders, payments).

4. Invalidation via messaging: On entity update, publish a cache invalidation event to Kafka/Redis Pub-Sub. Other instances subscribe and evict locally.

A top-tier candidate mentions: L1 cache (persistence context) is per-session and always consistent. The problem is L2 only. They also mention @Cache(usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_WRITE, region = "entity.cache") with Infinispan in invalidation mode as the production-grade solution.

PILLAR 3 — CONCURRENCY & LOW-LEVEL JAVA

Q7: Describe the internal mechanism of a lock-free stack (Treiber Stack) using CAS. What is the ABA problem in this context, and why does weakCompareAndSet exist?

Expert-Level Insight:

Treiber Stack: a lock-free LIFO stack using a single AtomicReference for the head.


public class TreiberStack<T> {
    private final AtomicReference<Node<T>> head = new AtomicReference<>();

    public void push(T value) {
        Node<T> newNode = new Node<>(value);
        Node<T> oldHead;
        do {
            oldHead = head.get();
            newNode.next = oldHead;
        } while (!head.compareAndSet(oldHead, newNode)); // CAS retry loop
    }

    public T pop() {
        Node<T> oldHead;
        do {
            oldHead = head.get();
            if (oldHead == null) return null;
        } while (!head.compareAndSet(oldHead, oldHead.next));
        return oldHead.value;
    }
}

ABA problem in pop():

1. Thread-1 reads head=A (A->B->C)

2. Thread-1 is preempted

3. Thread-2 pops A, pops B, pushes A back. Stack: A->C (B is gone)

4. Thread-1 resumes, CAS(head, A, A.next) — succeeds because head is still A

5. But A.next was B (saved before preemption) — Thread-1 sets head=B. B was already freed!

Fix: AtomicStampedReference — CAS checks both pointer AND a version stamp. Even if A returns, the stamp has incremented, so CAS fails correctly.

weakCompareAndSet: on some architectures (ARM, POWER), CAS requires memory barriers (acquire/release semantics). weakCompareAndSet may skip these barriers — it can fail spuriously (return false even when the comparison should succeed) but is faster. Use only in retry loops where spurious failure is harmless (you just retry). Java 9+ deprecated it in favor of compareAndExchange variants with explicit memory ordering: compareAndExchangeAcquire, compareAndExchangeRelease.

Q8: Compare the architectural impact of thread-per-request (platform threads) vs virtual threads (Project Loom) on a Spring Boot 3 REST API handling 10K concurrent requests with 100ms average DB latency.

Expert-Level Insight:

Platform threads (traditional):

  • Each thread: ~1MB stack. 10K threads = 10GB stack memory alone.
  • Thread scheduling: OS context switches. At 10K threads, scheduler overhead is significant.
  • Thread pool sizing: pool_size = CPU_cores (1 + wait_time/service_time). With 100ms DB wait and 5ms CPU work on 16 cores: pool = 16 (1 + 100/5) = 336 threads.
  • 10K concurrent requests with 336 threads: requests queue. p99 latency spikes under load.

Virtual threads (Java 21+):

  • Each VT: few KB stack (grows on demand). 10K VTs = ~50MB total.
  • VT mounted on carrier (platform) thread. On blocking IO, VT unmounts — carrier serves another VT. No OS context switch.
  • Thread pool: ForkJoinPool with availableProcessors() carriers. 16 cores = 16 carrier threads serving 10K VTs.
  • All 10K requests in-flight simultaneously. No queuing. p99 stays close to p50.

Where virtual threads fail or need care:

1. Pinning: synchronized blocks pin VT to carrier — defeats the purpose. Replace with ReentrantLock. HikariCP < 5.1 used synchronized internally.

2. ThreadLocal proliferation: VTs are cheap to create — applications may create millions. Each ThreadLocal entry is per-VT. Use ScopedValue instead.

3. CPU-bound work: VTs provide no benefit for CPU-intensive tasks. The carrier pool has limited threads. A CPU-bound VT blocks a carrier.

4. Structured concurrency (StructuredTaskScope): ensures child VTs cannot outlive parent scope. ShutdownOnFailure cancels siblings when one fails. ShutdownOnSuccess returns first result.


try (var scope = new StructuredTaskScope.ShutdownOnFailure()) {
    Subtask<User> userTask = scope.fork(() -> fetchUser(id));
    Subtask<List<Order>> ordersTask = scope.fork(() -> fetchOrders(id));
    scope.join().throwIfFailed();
    return new UserProfile(userTask.get(), ordersTask.get());
} // child VTs cannot escape this scope

PILLAR 4 — THE PRODUCTION RUNBOOK

The Senior Engineer's Incident Triage Protocol

When your service is down in production, follow this exact sequence. Time is measured in minutes — every minute of downtime costs money.

SCENARIO A: CPU Spike (Service responding slowly, CPU at 95%+)

Step 1 — Confirm and scope (0-2 min):


# Which process is consuming CPU?
top -H -p <pid>    # show per-thread CPU usage

Step 2 — Capture thread dump (2-3 min):


# Take 3 thread dumps, 10 seconds apart
jstack -l <pid> > dump1.txt; sleep 10; jstack -l <pid> > dump2.txt; sleep 10; jstack -l <pid> > dump3.txt

Step 3 — Correlate thread IDs (3-5 min):

top -H shows OS thread IDs (LWP) in decimal. jstack shows thread IDs in hex (nid=0x...). Convert decimal to hex to find which Java thread is burning CPU.

Step 4 — Analyze patterns:

  • All threads in same method: infinite loop or very hot code path. Check recent deployments.
  • Many threads BLOCKED on same monitor: lock contention. Find the thread holding the lock.
  • Many threads WAITING in thread pool queue: downstream service is slow. Check connection pool and dependent service health.
  • GC threads consuming CPU: check GC logs. Likely heap pressure.

Step 5 — If GC-related:


# Real-time GC monitoring
jstat -gcutil <pid> 1000    # 1-second intervals: Eden, Survivor, Old, Metaspace usage + GC counts/times
# Check GC log (if -Xlog:gc* enabled)
grep "Pause" gc.log | tail -20   # pause durations

Advanced (if time permits): Use async-profiler for CPU flame graph:


./profiler.sh -d 30 -f flamegraph.html <pid>

SCENARIO B: Memory Leak (OOM or steadily growing heap)

Step 1 — Confirm leak vs undersized heap (0-2 min):


jstat -gcutil <pid> 5000   # Watch Old Gen usage over time
# If Old Gen grows after every Full GC: LEAK. If it stabilizes: undersized heap.

Step 2 — Capture heap dump (2-5 min):


# BEFORE the app OOMs — configure auto-dump:
# -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/log/app/
# Manual dump:
jmap -dump:live,format=b,file=heap.hprof <pid>
# WARNING: this triggers Full GC and may pause the app for seconds

Step 3 — Analyze with Eclipse MAT or VisualVM:

  • Dominator tree: which objects retain the most memory?
