👨 Khanh: [pauses 8 seconds] That's a genuinely interesting question to start with. Let me think through it out loud and tell you where I land.

There are several candidates: consistency under failure, latency at global scale, fraud detection in real time. But if I had to pick one — I'd say exactly-once processing. Here's why.

In payments, every other problem has a fallback. Latency is high? Users wait. Fraud slips through? You catch it later and refund. But if you process a payment twice — charge a customer twice, or pay a merchant twice — there is no "catch it later." The money moved. The trust is broken. And at three hundred billion euros per year, even a 0.001% duplicate rate is catastrophic.

The hardest part is that the network never guarantees delivery. A payment request can time out, and you don't know: did it process or not? Did the response get lost in transit? So you have to build an idempotency layer that works correctly even when your own servers crash mid-operation. That means durable idempotency keys, atomic writes, and careful state machine design for every payment state transition.

That's why I'd call exactly-once processing the single hardest problem — because the consequences of failure are irreversible.

👨‍💻 Lars: Good. Min-Ji, do you have a follow-up?

👩‍💻 Min-Ji: [Korean accent, speaks quickly] Yes. Khanh — you mentioned idempotency keys. But what happen if the key storage itself fail? How you ensure the idempotency layer is reliable?

👨 Khanh: Great question. If I may — could you clarify: are you asking about the scenario where the idempotency key store goes down completely, or the scenario where a write to the key store partially fails?

📝 English Tip: Always clarify before answering a complex question. "Could you clarify — are you asking about X or Y?" This shows precision, not weakness.

👩‍💻 Min-Ji: Both, actually. Either case.

👨 Khanh: OK — both cases.

If the idempotency key store goes down completely: you have a choice. You can fail-safe — reject all incoming requests until the store recovers, which is safer but means downtime. Or you can fail-open — process payments without idempotency protection temporarily, accepting a small duplicate risk, then reconcile afterward. For payments, I'd fail-safe. Downtime is recoverable; duplicate charges are not.

If a write to the key store partially fails — say, the payment processed but the idempotency key record wasn't written: this is the trickier case. You need a reconciliation process that runs periodically, cross-references payment records against idempotency key records, and flags any orphaned payments — payments with no corresponding idempotency record. Those get manually reviewed.

The preventive solution: write the idempotency key in the same database transaction as the payment record, using a local database rather than a remote key store. That way, the key write and payment write succeed or fail atomically.

👨‍💼 Thomas: [German accent, direct] Khanh. I want to ask something different. You are from Vietnam. Adyen is a Dutch company. Our working language is English, our culture is very direct — Dutch directness. How do you see yourself fitting into that?

👨 Khanh: I appreciate you asking that directly — which, actually, already tells me something about the culture.

Vietnamese professional culture does lean toward more indirect communication — disagreement is often expressed diplomatically rather than bluntly. I'll be honest: that is my natural instinct too, sometimes. But I've been working consciously against it, because I've found that indirect communication creates problems: misaligned assumptions, delayed decisions, hidden technical debt that nobody wanted to say out loud.

What I've learned is that directness, done with respect, is actually kinder — it's faster, clearer, and it treats the other person as a professional who can handle honest feedback. I can't claim I'm Dutch direct by instinct. But I value it and I've been actively building that muscle.

📝 English Tip: Never be defensive about cultural differences. Acknowledge them honestly, show self-awareness, and frame them as something you're actively working on. That's the mature answer.

👨‍💼 Thomas: [nods] Good answer. Honest. Lars, anything else?

👨‍💻 Lars: One final question, Khanh. What question have we not asked you today that we should have?

👨 Khanh: [smiles] That's a great meta-question.

I think you haven't asked me about failure — specifically, a time I failed at something significant and what it cost. Most interviews ask about "challenges" which people tend to sanitize. A real failure question reveals how someone processes mistakes and whether they take accountability without spiraling.

I'm happy to answer it if you'd like — I have a story ready.

👨‍💻 Lars: Go ahead.

👨 Khanh: About two years ago, I was responsible for estimating the effort for a major middleware refactor. I said six weeks. It took fourteen.

I underestimated in three ways: I didn't account for the learning curve of a new library we were adopting, I assumed the existing tests were comprehensive enough to catch regressions — they weren't — and I didn't buffer for the coordination overhead with the European client team.

The project delivered late. The client was frustrated. My manager had to manage that relationship carefully.

What I changed afterward: I now add explicit buffers for unknowns — usually twenty to thirty percent — and I write out my assumptions in the estimate document so that if an assumption breaks, everyone can see why the estimate changed. I've been more accurate since.

📝 English Tip: "The question you should have asked" is a power move that shows meta-level thinking. Prepare one real answer to this question before every senior interview.

SCORECARD — Interview #71 Adyen

Dimension Score Notes
Handling panel format 10/10 Calm, addressed questioner directly
Handling open hard question 10/10 8-second pause + structured answer
Asking for clarification 10/10 "Could you clarify — X or Y?"
Cultural directness question 10/10 Honest self-awareness + active improvement
"Question you should have asked" 10/10 Meta-thinking + real failure story
Handling non-native accents 10/10 Patient, no confusion shown

English Lessons — Interview #71

1. Panel strategy: Address the person asking, not the full panel. Make eye contact (or camera contact) with the questioner.

2. Handling silence: A deliberate 8-second pause before answering a hard question shows confidence, not confusion.

3. Clarifying questions: "Could you clarify — are you asking about X or Y?" — use this before answering any ambiguous question.

4. Cultural directness question: Acknowledge your own culture honestly. "My natural instinct is X, but I've been actively building toward Y."

5. "What question should we have asked?": Prepare this answer for every senior interview. It's a trap question — and a huge opportunity.

6. Non-native panel: Slow your own speech slightly. If you don't catch a word, say: "Sorry — I want to make sure I understand — could you say that once more?"

ADVANCED ENGLISH PHRASES — INTERVIEW SUPPLEMENT

Handling Moments of Confusion

Situation What to say
Didn't hear clearly "Sorry — could you say that once more? I want to make sure I catch it."
Question is ambiguous "Before I answer — could I clarify what you mean by [X]?"
Question is very hard "That's a genuinely interesting question. Let me think out loud..."
Lost your train of thought "Let me step back for a second — I want to make sure I'm answering the right thing."
Need more time "I want to give you a thorough answer on this — give me just a moment."

Numbers and Metrics — Spoken English

Always say numbers with context when speaking:

  • NOT: "We had like... a lot of requests"
  • YES: "We were processing roughly fifteen thousand API calls per day — about ten per second at peak"

Always give the "so what" after a number:

  • NOT: "Latency was fifty milliseconds"
  • YES: "Our p99 latency was fifty milliseconds — well within our SLA of two hundred milliseconds"

Storytelling Arc for STAR Answers

A strong STAR answer in English follows this rhythm:

1. Scene-set (10 sec): "This was about [time]. I was [role] and the situation was [context]."

2. Tension (10 sec): "The problem was [specific challenge] and the stakes were [consequence]."

3. Decision (20 sec): "I decided to [action], specifically because [reasoning]."

4. Complication (10 sec): "It didn't go perfectly — [one honest obstacle]."

5. Resolution (15 sec): "The outcome was [specific result with numbers if possible]."

6. Learning (10 sec): "What this taught me was [insight]."

Total: about 75 seconds. Practice until it flows naturally.

ADVANCED ENGLISH PHRASES — INTERVIEW SUPPLEMENT

Handling Moments of Confusion

Situation What to say
Didn't hear clearly "Sorry — could you say that once more? I want to make sure I catch it."
Question is ambiguous "Before I answer — could I clarify what you mean by [X]?"
Question is very hard "That's a genuinely interesting question. Let me think out loud..."
Lost your train of thought "Let me step back for a second — I want to make sure I'm answering the right thing."
Need more time "I want to give you a thorough answer on this — give me just a moment."

Numbers and Metrics — Spoken English

Always say numbers with context when speaking:

  • NOT: "We had like... a lot of requests"
  • YES: "We were processing roughly fifteen thousand API calls per day — about ten per second at peak"

Always give the "so what" after a number:

  • NOT: "Latency was fifty milliseconds"
  • YES: "Our p99 latency was fifty milliseconds — well within our SLA of two hundred milliseconds"

Storytelling Arc for STAR Answers

A strong STAR answer in English follows this rhythm:

1. Scene-set (10 sec): "This was about [time]. I was [role] and the situation was [context]."

2. Tension (10 sec): "The problem was [specific challenge] and the stakes were [consequence]."

3. Decision (20 sec): "I decided to [action], specifically because [reasoning]."

4. Complication (10 sec): "It didn't go perfectly — [one honest obstacle]."

5. Resolution (15 sec): "The outcome was [specific result with numbers if possible]."

6. Learning (10 sec): "What this taught me was [insight]."

Total: about 75 seconds. Practice until it flows naturally.

Mock Interview #72 — Wix | JVM Internals Deep Dive

— Wix (Israel/Global SaaS) — JVM Internals

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Platform Infrastructure

Focus: ClassLoader, JIT compilation, bytecode, memory model edge cases, GC tuning

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Yoav (Staff Engineer, JVM expert)

👨‍💻 Yoav: Khanh — I'm going to skip the easy stuff. Let's go deep on the JVM. First: explain the ClassLoader hierarchy and what "parent delegation" means. When would you break it, and why?

👨 Khanh: The JVM has three built-in ClassLoaders in a hierarchy:

1. Bootstrap ClassLoader — written in native code (C++), loads core JDK classes from rt.jar / java.base module: java.lang.String, java.util.*, etc. It has no parent.

2. Platform ClassLoader (was Extension in Java 8) — loads classes from Java platform modules not in bootstrap.

3. Application ClassLoader — loads classes from your application classpath.

Parent Delegation Model: when a ClassLoader receives a request to load a class, it first delegates to its parent. Only if the parent can't find it does it try itself. This ensures java.lang.String always comes from Bootstrap — you can't accidentally shadow core classes.

When I'd break it: the classic case is plugin systems. If you want two plugins to each carry their own version of Jackson — say 2.13 and 2.15 — you create a separate URLClassLoader per plugin that loads its own jars before delegating upward. This is exactly what Tomcat, OSGi, and Java EE containers do.

The risk: if plugin code leaks objects into shared code that uses a different ClassLoader, you get ClassCastException even though the class name is identical. This is the "same class, different loader = different type" gotcha.

👨‍💻 Yoav: Exactly right. Follow-up — what is ClassLoader.getParent() returning null telling you?

👨 Khanh: It means that ClassLoader's parent is the Bootstrap ClassLoader — and Bootstrap is not represented as a Java object, so getParent() returns null rather than a reference. It's not an error; it means you've hit the top of the hierarchy.

👨‍💻 Yoav: Good. Now JIT. Explain the difference between C1 and C2 compilers in HotSpot. What is tiered compilation, and what triggers a method to be compiled?

👨 Khanh: HotSpot uses a two-compiler model:

  • C1 (Client compiler): fast compilation, lightweight optimizations, generates native code quickly. Low warm-up time, lower peak performance.
  • C2 (Server compiler): slow compilation, aggressive optimizations (inlining, escape analysis, loop unrolling). High peak performance, slower startup.

Tiered compilation (default since Java 8) uses all five levels:

  • Level 0: interpreted
  • Level 1–2: C1 compiled, no/light profiling
  • Level 3: C1 compiled with full profiling
  • Level 4: C2 compiled using the profile data from Level 3

What triggers compilation? The JVM counts invocations per method and back-edges (loop iterations). When counts exceed a threshold — typically ~10,000 for C2 — the method is queued for JIT compilation. This is "hot spot" detection. You can tune thresholds with -XX:CompileThreshold.

👨‍💻 Yoav: And escape analysis — what does it enable?

👨 Khanh: Escape analysis determines whether an object allocated inside a method can ever be referenced outside it. If not — the object "doesn't escape" — the JIT can apply two optimizations:

1. Stack allocation: allocate the object on the stack instead of the heap. No GC pressure, faster allocation, faster deallocation when the method returns.

2. Scalar replacement: decompose the object into individual fields stored in registers. The object never actually exists as a struct in memory.

This is why in modern Java, creating small temporary objects inside tight loops is often cheaper than it looks — C2 frequently eliminates the heap allocation entirely. You can verify with -XX:+PrintEscapeAnalysis.

👨‍💻 Yoav: Let's talk GC. You're running G1GC on a 16GB heap and experiencing unpredictable STW pauses of 500ms–2s despite -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200. How do you diagnose and fix it?

👨 Khanh: Three likely causes, each with a different fix:

Cause 1 — Humongous object allocations. G1 handles objects larger than 50% of a region size separately. They go directly to the Old generation, bypassing Eden/Survivor. They can trigger Full GC if the Old generation fills. Diagnosis: -XX:+PrintGCDetails — look for "Humongous" in the logs. Fix: increase region size (-XX:G1HeapRegionSize) to push the humongous threshold higher, or refactor to avoid those large allocations.

Cause 2 — Concurrent cycle not finishing before mixed GC. G1's concurrent marking must finish before it can reclaim Old regions. If your application allocates too fast, marking can't keep up — triggering an "evacuation failure" and Full GC. Diagnosis: look for "to-space exhausted" in GC logs. Fix: increase -XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent (start marking earlier, default 45%) or add more heap.

Cause 3 — Too many GC threads contending with application threads. Paradoxically, too many GC threads can cause STW pauses to be longer on NUMA machines. Diagnosis: -XX:+PrintGCApplicationStoppedTime. Fix: tune -XX:ParallelGCThreads and -XX:ConcGCThreads.

My first steps: enable GC logging (-Xlog:gc*:file=gc.log), use GCViewer or GCEasy to visualize the log, then confirm which of the three causes is dominant before changing flags.

👨‍💻 Yoav: Last one. What is the Java Memory Model's "happens-before" guarantee, and give me a case where most engineers get it wrong?

👨 Khanh: The JMM defines happens-before (HB) as: if action A happens-before action B, then all effects of A are visible to B. HB is established by:

  • Thread start: thread.start() HB any action in that thread
  • Lock release HB lock acquisition on the same monitor
  • volatile write HB subsequent volatile read of the same variable
  • Thread.join() HB any action after the join

The common mistake: using volatile on a reference field assuming it makes the object contents visible too.


volatile Config config;

// Thread A writes
config = new Config(newValue);  // safe — volatile write

// Thread B reads
String v = config.getValue();   // WRONG assumption

The volatile on config guarantees Thread B sees the new Config reference. But if Config.value is set in the constructor and the constructor isn't finished before the volatile write, B might see a partially constructed Config. The fix: make Config immutable — all fields set in the constructor, all fields final. A final field is guaranteed visible after constructor completion without any synchronization.

👨‍💻 Yoav: Excellent. That final field guarantee is Java 5+ spec — most people don't know it. Strong session.

SCORECARD — Interview #72

Topic Score Key Insight Demonstrated
ClassLoader hierarchy 10/10 Parent delegation + when to break it + CCE gotcha
null getParent() 10/10 Bootstrap = no Java object
JIT C1/C2/Tiered 10/10 All 5 levels, trigger threshold
Escape analysis 10/10 Stack allocation + scalar replacement
G1GC pause diagnosis 10/10 3 causes, each with specific fix
JMM happens-before 10/10 volatile reference ≠ volatile contents

Mock Interview #73 — Confluent | Advanced Java Concurrency

— Confluent (US, Kafka Platform) — Advanced Concurrency

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Kafka Streams Runtime

Focus: AQS internals, StampedLock, lock-free structures, thread pool sizing, structured concurrency

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Elena (Principal Engineer)

👩‍💻 Elena: Khanh — we deal with extremely high-throughput concurrent code here. Let's go deep. What is AbstractQueuedSynchronizer and how does ReentrantLock use it?

👨 Khanh: AQS is the backbone of most Java concurrency utilities: ReentrantLock, Semaphore, CountDownLatch, ReentrantReadWriteLock. It provides the core mechanism for blocking and waking threads, backed by:

1. A volatile int state — the synchronization state. Each class interprets it differently: ReentrantLock uses it as a hold count (0 = unlocked, >0 = locked N times by the same thread). Semaphore uses it as the permit count.

2. A CLH queue (Craig, Landin, Hagersten) — a virtual doubly-linked queue of Node objects, each representing a waiting thread. Nodes spin briefly then park using LockSupport.park().

How ReentrantLock uses AQS:

  • lock(): tries a CAS on state from 0 to 1. Success = lock acquired. Failure = enqueue the thread in the CLH queue and park.
  • unlock(): decrements state. If it reaches 0, unparks the head of the CLH queue.
  • Reentrancy: if the acquiring thread already holds the lock (Thread.currentThread() == exclusiveOwnerThread), it increments state instead of CAS-ing from 0.

The brilliance of AQS is that you implement only tryAcquire() and tryRelease() — AQS handles all the queueing, parking, and waking.

👩‍💻 Elena: Good. When would you use StampedLock over ReentrantReadWriteLock, and what's the risk?

👨 Khanh: ReentrantReadWriteLock allows multiple concurrent readers OR one exclusive writer. The problem: if writers are frequent, readers can starve — they wait behind a queue of writers.

StampedLock adds an optimistic read mode:


StampedLock lock = new StampedLock();

long stamp = lock.tryOptimisticRead(); // no lock acquired
int x = readField();
if (!lock.validate(stamp)) {          // check: did a write happen?
    stamp = lock.readLock();          // fall back to real read lock
    try { x = readField(); }
    finally { lock.unlockRead(stamp); }
}

Optimistic reads don't block writers at all. If no write occurred during the read — common in read-heavy workloads — you get zero contention. This is dramatically faster under read-heavy, low-write scenarios.

The risk: StampedLock is not reentrant. If a thread holding a write stamp tries to acquire another lock, it deadlocks. Also, there's no condition variable support. And interrupted threads aren't released automatically — you must use StampedLock.tryWriteLock(timeout) variants. Use it in carefully controlled, performance-critical paths only.

👩‍💻 Elena: Lock-free territory. Explain how ConcurrentHashMap achieves thread safety without a global lock in Java 8+.

👨 Khanh: Java 8 rewrote ConcurrentHashMap entirely. The key insight: you only need to lock at the bucket level, not the whole map.

Structure: an array of Node (the table). Each array slot is one bucket.

  • Reads: entirely lock-free. The table array is volatile. Each Node's val and next are volatile. Reading just traverses the bucket chain with no synchronization.
  • Writes to an empty bucket: CAS on the table slot. If the slot is null, atomically set it to the new node. No lock needed.
  • Writes to a non-empty bucket: synchronized on the head node of that bucket only. Only threads writing to the same bucket contend.
  • Resize: uses a cooperative transfer protocol — multiple threads each take a portion of the old table and migrate it concurrently.

The result: reads scale linearly with cores. Writes only contend at bucket granularity. The concurrency level is effectively the table length (default 16, grows with the map).

👩‍💻 Elena: How do you size a thread pool? Walk me through your reasoning for a blocking I/O workload vs a CPU-bound workload.

👨 Khanh: The classic formula and the intuition behind it:

CPU-bound tasks: threads = CPU cores + 1. One extra thread covers the rare case where a thread is paused by a page fault. More threads than cores just adds context-switch overhead with no throughput gain.

Blocking I/O tasks: threads = CPU cores × (1 + wait_time / compute_time). If a thread spends 90% of its time waiting for a DB call, wait_time/compute_time = 9, so you need roughly cores × 10 threads to keep the CPU busy. On a 4-core machine: 40 threads.

In practice: I treat this formula as a starting point, not a destination. I instrument:

  • ThreadPoolExecutor.getActiveCount() — threads currently executing
  • ThreadPoolExecutor.getQueue().size() — tasks waiting
  • Task latency histogram in Micrometer

If queue depth is consistently non-zero, the pool is undersized. If active count is consistently far below pool size, it's oversized (wasting memory — each thread stack is ~512KB to 1MB).

Java 21 change: with virtual threads, the calculus for I/O-bound work completely changes. You can create one virtual thread per request and let the JVM schedule them. The platform thread pool underneath becomes CPU-sized. This eliminates the I/O thread pool sizing problem entirely.

👩‍💻 Elena: Final. Explain the ABA problem in lock-free programming and how Java's AtomicStampedReference solves it.

👨 Khanh: ABA problem: a CAS operation checks that a value is still A before swapping. But what if the value was A, changed to B, then changed back to A by another thread? The CAS succeeds — it sees A — but the world has changed in ways the CAS can't detect.

Classic example: a lock-free stack. Thread 1 reads the head as node A (pointing to B). Thread 1 is suspended. Thread 2 pops A, pops B, pushes A back. Thread 1 resumes, CAS sees head is still A — "nothing changed!" — and sets head to B. But B was already popped and potentially freed. Result: corrupted stack.

AtomicStampedReference solution: pairs the reference with an integer stamp (version counter). Every update increments the stamp. CAS now checks both the reference and the stamp:


AtomicStampedReference<Node> head = new AtomicStampedReference<>(initialNode, 0);
int[] stampHolder = new int[1];
Node current = head.get(stampHolder);
// CAS only succeeds if BOTH reference AND stamp match
head.compareAndSet(current, newNode, stampHolder[0], stampHolder[0] + 1);

Even if the reference cycles back to A, the stamp has incremented, so the old CAS fails. ABA is broken.

👩‍💻 Elena: Perfect. That's a deep answer. You clearly understand this at the implementation level.

SCORECARD — Interview #73

Topic Score Key Insight
AQS internals 10/10 CLH queue, volatile state, CAS, LockSupport.park
StampedLock vs RRWL 10/10 Optimistic read + reentrancy risk
ConcurrentHashMap internals 10/10 Bucket-level sync, volatile nodes, CAS on empty
Thread pool sizing 10/10 Formula + instrumentation + Java 21 virtual threads
ABA problem 10/10 Problem + AtomicStampedReference solution

Mock Interview #74 — PingCAP | Distributed Systems Hard Parts

— PingCAP (Distributed DB, US/Asia) — Distributed Systems Hard Parts

Role: Senior Java Engineer, TiDB Client SDK

Focus: Two-phase commit, Paxos/consensus, CAP theorem nuances, B-tree vs LSM internals

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Wei (Staff Engineer, distributed systems specialist)

👨‍💻 Wei: Khanh — we build a distributed SQL database. I want to test your distributed systems fundamentals. Explain two-phase commit. What are its failure modes and why is it called a blocking protocol?

👨 Khanh: Two-phase commit (2PC) is a distributed transaction protocol between a coordinator and multiple participants:

Phase 1 — Prepare:

Coordinator sends PREPARE to all participants. Each participant writes its changes to a write-ahead log, acquires locks, and replies VOTE_COMMIT or VOTE_ABORT. If a participant can't commit (disk full, constraint violation), it votes abort.

Phase 2 — Commit/Abort:

If all participants voted commit → coordinator sends COMMIT to all. If any voted abort → coordinator sends ABORT to all. Participants act accordingly and release locks.

Failure modes:

1. Participant fails in Phase 1: coordinator doesn't receive a vote → coordinator times out and aborts. Safe — no participant has committed.

2. Coordinator fails after Phase 1 votes are collected but before Phase 2 is sent: this is the critical failure. Participants have voted COMMIT and are holding locks. They cannot commit (coordinator hasn't said so) and cannot abort (they promised to commit). They are blocked until the coordinator recovers. This is why 2PC is called a blocking protocol — participant recovery depends entirely on coordinator recovery.

3. Coordinator fails mid-Phase 2 (some participants got COMMIT, some didn't): partial commit. When coordinator recovers, it must replay Phase 2 from its log to ensure atomicity.

Why still used? Despite the blocking problem, 2PC is practical when failure windows are short and coordinator HA is ensured (e.g., coordinator runs on a Raft cluster). TiDB uses 2PC + Percolator for its distributed transactions.

👨‍💻 Wei: Good. What does the Paxos algorithm solve and how is it different from Raft?

👨 Khanh: Both solve consensus — getting a set of nodes to agree on a single value even if some nodes fail or messages are lost. Specifically: leader election and replicated log commitment in distributed systems.

Paxos (Basic):

Three roles: Proposer, Acceptor, Learner. Two phases:

  • Phase 1 (Prepare): Proposer sends Prepare(n) to quorum of Acceptors. Acceptors respond with the highest proposal they've already accepted (if any).
  • Phase 2 (Accept): Proposer picks the value from the highest-numbered response (or proposes its own if none), sends Accept(n, value). Acceptors accept if n ≥ any promised proposal number.

Paxos is provably correct but notoriously hard to implement completely — Multi-Paxos (for log replication) requires significant additional design decisions that the original paper doesn't fully specify: leader election, log gaps, membership changes.

Raft was designed specifically to be understandable. It makes the same guarantees as Multi-Paxos but decomposes the problem into three clearly defined subproblems:

1. Leader election — one leader per term, heartbeat-based

2. Log replication — leader accepts entries, replicates to followers, commits when majority acknowledges

3. Safety — log matching property ensures committed entries never conflict

The key difference: Raft always has a strong leader that drives all decisions. Paxos allows multiple proposers competing (with conflict resolution). Raft's strong leader makes reads and writes simpler but means leader becomes a bottleneck.

In practice: etcd, Consul, TiKV all use Raft. Google's Chubby and Spanner use Paxos variants.

👨‍💻 Wei: CAP theorem. Most engineers say "pick two of three." What's the more nuanced view?

👨 Khanh: The "pick two" framing is a simplification that leads to bad decisions. The nuanced view:

First: CAP only applies during a network partition. When the network is healthy, you get both consistency and availability. The real question is: what do you sacrifice when a partition occurs? — not during normal operation.

Second: "Consistency" in CAP means linearizability (strong consistency) — not the C in ACID. Many systems that claim to be "CP" are actually providing something weaker than linearizability.

Third: "Availability" in CAP means every request receives a response — not "the system is up." A system can be 99.99% available but not CAP-available if it returns errors during partitions.

The real design question: what does your application need during a partition?

  • Bank ledger: CP. Return an error rather than show a wrong balance. Users understand "please try again."
  • Shopping cart: AP. Show a slightly stale cart rather than reject the add-to-cart request. Reconcile on checkout.
  • Inventory count on a flash sale: CP. You cannot oversell.
  • "Likes" counter on a post: AP. A user seeing 10,001 instead of 10,000 for a few seconds is completely acceptable.

PACELC is a better model: it adds latency vs consistency tradeoffs even when there's no partition — because distributed coordination always adds latency.

👨‍💻 Wei: Database internals. B-tree vs LSM-tree — when does each win?

👨 Khanh: These are the two dominant storage engine designs, with fundamentally different write and read profiles.

B-tree (used by PostgreSQL, MySQL InnoDB, traditional RDBMS):

  • Balanced tree where leaf pages contain sorted data. Reads follow a tree path: O(log n).
  • Writes are in-place: update the page directly. This requires a random write to disk.
  • Read-optimized: a point lookup reads only log(n) pages.
  • Write amplification: each write may trigger a page split and rebalance — typically 3-5× amplification.
  • Works best when: read:write ratio is high, data fits in page cache, working set is not write-heavy.

LSM-tree (used by RocksDB, Cassandra, HBase, TiKV):

  • Writes go first to an in-memory MemTable (sorted), then flushed as immutable SSTables on disk. Periodic compaction merges and sorts SSTables.
  • Writes are sequential: always append. Sequential I/O is 10–100× faster than random I/O on spinning disks, and still faster on SSDs.
  • Read-amplification: a point lookup may need to check multiple SSTables at each level before finding the key. Bloom filters reduce this significantly.
  • Write amplification: compaction rewrites data multiple times — typically 10-30× over full LSM levels.
  • Works best when: write throughput is the priority, time-series or event data, keys are written once and rarely updated.

Rule of thumb:

  • High write throughput, sequential or time-series data → LSM (RocksDB, Cassandra)
  • Mixed read/write, complex queries, relational data → B-tree (PostgreSQL)
  • OLAP / analytical → neither — columnar (Parquet, ORC)

👨‍💻 Wei: Last one. What is MVCC and how does it relate to transaction isolation levels?

👨 Khanh: Multi-Version Concurrency Control keeps multiple versions of a row rather than updating in place. When a transaction updates a row, it creates a new version with the current transaction ID as its start timestamp. The old version remains visible to transactions that started before the update.

This allows readers never to block writers and writers never to block readers — a massive concurrency improvement over lock-based approaches.

How isolation levels map to MVCC:

  • Read Committed: each statement within a transaction sees a new snapshot — the latest committed version at statement start. Prevents dirty reads. Allows non-repeatable reads.
  • Repeatable Read (PostgreSQL default): the transaction takes one snapshot at its start. All reads see that same snapshot throughout. Prevents non-repeatable reads. PostgreSQL also prevents phantom reads at this level (unlike MySQL InnoDB which uses gap locks).
  • Serializable: MVCC alone isn't sufficient. PostgreSQL adds SSI (Serializable Snapshot Isolation) — tracks read-write dependencies and aborts transactions that would create a cycle (a serialization anomaly).

The garbage collection problem: old row versions accumulate. PostgreSQL's VACUUM reclaims versions no longer visible to any active transaction. Long-running transactions prevent VACUUM from cleaning old versions — table bloat. This is why "never leave a transaction open" is a critical operational rule.

👨‍💻 Wei: That vacuum/long-transaction connection is something most candidates miss. Very strong.

SCORECARD — Interview #74

Topic Score Key Insight
Two-phase commit 10/10 Blocking protocol failure mode, coordinator crash scenario
Paxos vs Raft 10/10 Decomposed subproblems, strong leader tradeoff
CAP nuance 10/10 PACELC, partition-only, AP vs CP by use case
B-tree vs LSM 10/10 Write amplification trade-off, read vs write optimization
MVCC + isolation levels 10/10 Vacuum/long-transaction connection

JAVA HARD TOPICS QUICK REFERENCE

JVM

Topic Key Fact
ClassLoader parent delegation Bootstrap → Platform → App. Break for plugins. CCE if object crosses loader boundary.
Null from getParent() Bootstrap has no Java object representation
JIT levels 0=interpreted, 1-2=C1 light, 3=C1+profiling, 4=C2 optimized
Escape analysis Non-escaping objects: stack-allocated or scalar-replaced by C2
G1 Humongous Object > 50% region → Old gen directly → risk of Full GC
G1 evacuation failure "to-space exhausted" → Full GC → tune IHOP or heap
volatile on reference Guarantees visibility of reference only, NOT object contents
final field guarantee Fields set in constructor visible after construction without sync (JMM 5.0+)

Concurrency

Topic Key Fact
AQS state volatile int; each util interprets it differently
AQS CLH queue Waiting threads park via LockSupport.park(), woken by predecessor
StampedLock optimistic tryOptimisticRead() → validate() → fallback. Not reentrant.
ConcurrentHashMap Java 8 Reads lock-free (volatile). Writes: CAS on empty bucket, synchronized on head node otherwise
Thread pool I/O formula cores × (1 + wait/compute)
ABA problem Value cycles A→B→A; CAS falsely succeeds. Fix: AtomicStampedReference (reference + stamp)
Virtual threads Java 21 1 virtual thread per task; JVM schedules on platform thread pool; eliminates I/O pool sizing

Distributed Systems

Topic Key Fact
2PC blocking Coordinator crash after Phase 1 votes = participants stuck holding locks
Paxos vs Raft Raft: strong leader, decomposed subproblems. Paxos: multiple proposers, harder to implement
CAP partition-only CAP tradeoff only applies DURING a partition, not normal operation
PACELC Adds latency-vs-consistency tradeoff even without partition
B-tree write In-place, random I/O, 3-5× write amplification, read-optimized
LSM write Sequential append, compaction-based, 10-30× write amplification, write-optimized
MVCC Multiple row versions; readers don't block writers. Garbage: VACUUM (PostgreSQL)
Long transaction danger Prevents VACUUM → table bloat → query slowdown

JAVA HARD TOPICS QUICK REFERENCE

JVM

Topic Key Fact
ClassLoader parent delegation Bootstrap → Platform → App. Break for plugins. CCE if object crosses loader boundary.
Null from getParent() Bootstrap has no Java object representation
JIT levels 0=interpreted, 1-2=C1 light, 3=C1+profiling, 4=C2 optimized
Escape analysis Non-escaping objects: stack-allocated or scalar-replaced by C2
G1 Humongous Object > 50% region → Old gen directly → risk of Full GC
G1 evacuation failure "to-space exhausted" → Full GC → tune IHOP or heap
volatile on reference Guarantees visibility of reference only, NOT object contents
final field guarantee Fields set in constructor visible after construction without sync (JMM 5.0+)

Concurrency

Topic Key Fact
AQS state volatile int; each util interprets it differently
AQS CLH queue Waiting threads park via LockSupport.park(), woken by predecessor
StampedLock optimistic tryOptimisticRead() → validate() → fallback. Not reentrant.
ConcurrentHashMap Java 8 Reads lock-free (volatile). Writes: CAS on empty bucket, synchronized on head node otherwise
Thread pool I/O formula cores × (1 + wait/compute)
ABA problem Value cycles A→B→A; CAS falsely succeeds. Fix: AtomicStampedReference (reference + stamp)
Virtual threads Java 21 1 virtual thread per task; JVM schedules on platform thread pool; eliminates I/O pool sizing

Distributed Systems

Topic Key Fact
2PC blocking Coordinator crash after Phase 1 votes = participants stuck holding locks
Paxos vs Raft Raft: strong leader, decomposed subproblems. Paxos: multiple proposers, harder to implement
CAP partition-only CAP tradeoff only applies DURING a partition, not normal operation
PACELC Adds latency-vs-consistency tradeoff even without partition
B-tree write In-place, random I/O, 3-5× write amplification, read-optimized
LSM write Sequential append, compaction-based, 10-30× write amplification, write-optimized
MVCC Multiple row versions; readers don't block writers. Garbage: VACUUM (PostgreSQL)
Long transaction danger Prevents VACUUM → table bloat → query slowdown

Mock Interview #75 — Zalando | Spring Internals & GraphQL

— Zalando (Germany, Fashion E-commerce)

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Platform Services

Focus: @Transactional proxy self-invocation trap, OpenID Connect, GraphQL vs REST

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Klaus (Staff Engineer)

👨‍💻 Klaus: Khanh — straight to Spring internals. Explain the @Transactional self-invocation problem. Why does it happen and how do you fix it?

👨 Khanh: This is one of the most common Spring bugs I've seen in production. Here's the problem:

Spring implements @Transactional using AOP — it wraps your bean in a proxy. When an external caller calls a @Transactional method, the call goes through the proxy, which starts the transaction. However, when a method within the same class calls another @Transactional method on itself, it calls this.methodB() — bypassing the proxy entirely. No proxy = no transaction.


@Service
public class OrderService {

    public void placeOrder(Order order) {
        processPayment(order);  // WRONG — calls this.processPayment, bypasses proxy
    }

    @Transactional
    public void processPayment(Order order) {
        // This transaction NEVER starts when called from placeOrder above
    }
}

Why it happens: Spring proxies are external wrappers. this always refers to the raw bean, not the proxy.

Fix 1 — Restructure into separate beans. Move processPayment to a different service class. External call = goes through proxy = transaction starts. This is the cleanest solution.

Fix 2 — Self-injection. Inject the bean into itself via @Autowired (Spring handles circular self-reference). Call through the injected reference:


@Autowired
private OrderService self;

public void placeOrder(Order order) {
    self.processPayment(order);  // Goes through proxy — transaction starts
}

Fix 3 — AopContext.currentProxy(). Get the proxy reference manually at runtime. Works but is ugly — couples code to Spring AOP internals.

Best practice: Fix 1 (restructure). Self-invocation problems are almost always a sign that the class has too many responsibilities anyway.

👨‍💻 Klaus: Good. What is @Transactional propagation? Give me the two most important settings and when each matters.

👨 Khanh: Propagation defines what happens when a transactional method is called while a transaction already exists.

REQUIRED (default): If a transaction exists, join it. If not, create one. This is correct for most cases — you want all database operations in one unit of work.

REQUIRES_NEW: Always create a new transaction, suspending any existing one. The inner transaction commits or rolls back independently of the outer.

When REQUIRES_NEW matters: audit logging. You want the audit record written even if the outer business transaction rolls back:


@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
public void writeAuditLog(AuditEvent event) {
    // This commits even if the caller's transaction rolls back
    auditRepo.save(event);
}

Without REQUIRES_NEW, if the outer transaction rolls back (e.g., payment failed), the audit log entry also rolls back — losing the evidence that the attempt even happened.

The subtle trap with REQUIRES_NEW: if the inner transaction acquires a lock on a row that the outer transaction also holds, you get a deadlock. Outer holds lock, inner waits for the same lock — but outer is suspended waiting for inner to finish. Always check lock ordering when using REQUIRES_NEW.

👨‍💻 Klaus: Let's move to auth. Explain OpenID Connect. How does it extend OAuth 2.0, and what's the difference between the access token and the ID token?

👨 Khanh: OAuth 2.0 solves authorization — "this application may access your data on your behalf." But OAuth doesn't define how to authenticate who you are. It has no standard for user identity.

OpenID Connect (OIDC) adds an identity layer on top of OAuth 2.0 by introducing the ID Token — a JWT issued by the Authorization Server that contains claims about the authenticated user: sub (subject/user ID), name, email, iat, exp, iss (issuer).

Key difference:

Access Token ID Token
Purpose Authorize API calls Prove user identity
Audience Resource Server (your API) Client application
Format Opaque string or JWT Always JWT
Contains Scopes, permissions User identity claims
Who reads it API validates it Client app reads it

OIDC Flow (Authorization Code + PKCE — recommended):

1. User clicks "Login with Google"

2. App redirects to Google's authorization endpoint with scope=openid email

3. User authenticates at Google, grants consent

4. Google redirects back with an authorization code

5. App exchanges code for tokens at Google's token endpoint → receives both access token and ID token

6. App reads ID token to know who the user is

7. App uses access token to call APIs on behalf of the user

In Spring Security: spring-boot-starter-oauth2-client handles all of this. You configure the issuer URI, Spring auto-discovers the OIDC metadata endpoint, and you get OidcUser in your security context with all identity claims.

👨‍💻 Klaus: Last topic. GraphQL vs REST — when would you choose GraphQL for a backend service, and what are the operational challenges?

👨 Khanh: When GraphQL wins:

1. Multiple clients with different data needs. Mobile needs 5 fields, web needs 20, dashboard needs aggregations. With REST you'd add query params, create multiple endpoints, or always over-fetch. With GraphQL, each client asks for exactly what it needs — one schema, self-describing.

2. Deeply nested data. Fetching an order with its items, each item's product, each product's seller — REST requires multiple round trips or a bloated endpoint. GraphQL resolves this in one query with nested resolvers.

3. Rapid frontend iteration. Frontend teams can evolve their queries without backend changes — as long as the schema supports it.

When REST wins: simple CRUD, public APIs (REST + OpenAPI is universally understood), file upload/download, caching (GraphQL's single endpoint breaks HTTP caching), or teams unfamiliar with GraphQL tooling.

Operational challenges of GraphQL:

1. N+1 query problem at the resolver level. If you have 100 orders and each order resolver fetches its customer separately — 101 DB queries. Fix: DataLoader pattern (batch + cache within a request).

2. No HTTP caching. GraphQL uses POST for all queries. CDN and browser HTTP caching don't work. You need application-level caching.

3. Query complexity attacks. A malicious client can send a deeply nested query that causes exponential DB hits. Fix: query depth limits and complexity scoring.

4. Schema evolution is hard. You can add fields without breaking clients, but deprecating or removing fields requires careful migration. Versioning a GraphQL schema is not as clean as REST API versioning.

SCORECARD — Interview #75

Topic Score Key Insight
@Transactional self-invocation 10/10 Proxy bypass, 3 fixes, REQUIRES_NEW deadlock trap
Propagation REQUIRES_NEW 10/10 Audit log use case + lock deadlock risk
OIDC vs OAuth 2.0 10/10 ID token vs access token, PKCE flow
GraphQL vs REST 10/10 N+1 resolver, no HTTP caching, complexity attacks

Mock Interview #76 — Thoughtworks | Testing Mastery

— Thoughtworks (Global Consulting) — Testing Mastery

Role: Senior Java Consultant

Focus: Mutation testing, test pyramid advanced, property-based testing, test quality vs coverage

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Amara (Principal Consultant)

👩‍💻 Amara: Khanh — Thoughtworks is opinionated about testing. Let's go deep. What is mutation testing and why does 80% line coverage sometimes mean almost nothing?

👨 Khanh: Mutation testing answers the question: "Does your test suite actually catch bugs, or does it just execute code?"

The tool (PITest for Java) makes small automated changes — mutations — to your production code:

  • Changes > to >=
  • Negates a boolean condition
  • Removes a return statement
  • Changes + to -

Then it runs your tests. If a mutation causes a test to fail — the mutation is killed (good). If all tests still pass after the mutation — it survives (bad — your tests didn't catch the bug).

Why 80% line coverage means almost nothing:


public int calculateDiscount(int price, boolean isPremium) {
    if (isPremium) {
        return price * 20 / 100;
    }
    return 0;
}

Test: calculateDiscount(100, true) → coverage tool says line return price * 20 / 100 is covered. ✓

But a mutation that changes 20 to 30 — the test still passes! It didn't assert the exact return value. The line was executed but the logic wasn't verified.

Mutation score = killed mutations / total mutations. 80% mutation score means 80% of logic changes would be caught by your tests. That's meaningful. 80% line coverage is nearly meaningless.

PITest in practice:


<plugin>
  <groupId>org.pitest</groupId>
  <artifactId>pitest-maven</artifactId>
  <configuration>
    <mutationThreshold>75</mutationThreshold>
    <failWhenNoMutations>true</failWhenNoMutations>
  </configuration>
</plugin>

Add this to CI. If mutation score drops below 75%, the build fails. This incentivizes assertion quality, not just coverage.

👩‍💻 Amara: Excellent. What is property-based testing? How does it differ from example-based testing, and when is it most valuable?

👨 Khanh: Example-based testing (JUnit): you choose specific inputs and assert specific outputs. The test is only as good as the examples you think of.

Property-based testing: you define a property — a rule that must hold for all valid inputs — and the framework generates hundreds of random inputs to try to falsify it.

Java library: jqwik or QuickCheck port.


@Property
void reverseTwiceIsIdentity(@ForAll List<Integer> list) {
    List<Integer> result = reverse(reverse(list));
    assertThat(result).isEqualTo(list);
}

jqwik generates 1000 random lists — empty lists, single elements, large lists, lists with duplicates, negative numbers — and verifies the property holds for all of them. If it finds a failing case, it shrinks it to the minimal counterexample.

When most valuable:

1. Algorithms with mathematical properties: sort (output is ordered + same elements), reverse, encode/decode (round-trip).

2. Parsers and serializers: serialize(deserialize(x)) == x for all valid inputs.

3. Financial calculations: total should always be sum of parts regardless of order.

4. Security: no input should cause an exception that leaks stack traces.

When NOT to use it: UI interactions, tests that depend on external state, tests where the property is hard to define. Example-based tests remain essential for documenting intended behavior.

👩‍💻 Amara: Describe your ideal test strategy for a Spring Boot microservice. What layers do you test, with what tools, and what's in scope for each?

👨 Khanh: I follow a modified test pyramid — three layers, each with a clear scope and toolset:

Layer 1 — Unit Tests (70% of tests):

  • Scope: pure business logic, algorithms, domain rules. No Spring context, no DB, no HTTP.
  • Tools: JUnit 5 + Mockito for dependencies.
  • Speed: milliseconds per test. Must run in < 30 seconds total.
  • Rule: if you need Spring to run a unit test, it's not a unit test — split the class.

Layer 2 — Integration Tests (25%):

  • Scope: one service interacts with real infrastructure in isolation.
  • Tools: @SpringBootTest + Testcontainers (real Postgres, real Redis, real Kafka).
  • Key patterns: @DataJpaTest for repository layer only (loads minimal Spring context). @WebMvcTest for controller layer only.
  • Speed: seconds per test. Target < 5 minutes total in CI.
  • What I verify: SQL queries are correct, Kafka consumer offsets, transaction rollback on exceptions.

Layer 3 — Contract Tests (5%):

  • Scope: API contracts between services — no need for both services running simultaneously.
  • Tools: Spring Cloud Contract or Pact.
  • Consumer defines expectations → provider verifies against them. Catches breaking API changes without E2E tests.

What I explicitly avoid: full E2E tests in CI. They're flaky, slow, and hard to maintain. E2E tests live in a staging environment with manual trigger.

One rule above all: tests must be deterministic. A test that passes 95% of the time is worse than no test — it erodes trust in the suite.

👩‍💻 Amara: What's the most dangerous anti-pattern you've seen in test suites?

👨 Khanh: Assertion-free coverage. Tests that call methods, assert nothing meaningful, but boost coverage numbers. Usually written under a "we need 80% coverage before release" deadline.

The second most dangerous: test interdependence — tests that rely on execution order because they share mutable state (a static field, a shared DB with no cleanup). One test's side effect becomes another test's prerequisite. When you run tests in parallel or change order — random failures. Fix: each test owns its data, cleans up after itself, uses @BeforeEach setup and @AfterEach teardown.

SCORECARD — Interview #76

Topic Score Key Insight
Mutation testing 10/10 PITest, mutation score vs line coverage, assert quality
Property-based testing 10/10 jqwik, shrinking, round-trip property
Test strategy pyramid 10/10 3 layers, tools, Testcontainers, Contract tests
Anti-patterns 10/10 Assertion-free coverage + test interdependence

Mock Interview #77 — Datadog/SRE | Error Budgets & API Evolution

— Datadog / SRE-style (US, Observability Platform)

Role: Senior Software Engineer, Reliability Engineering

Focus: Error budgets, SLO design, backward-compatible API changes, schema evolution

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Priya (Staff Engineer, SRE background)

👩‍💻 Priya: Khanh — reliability engineering mindset. What is an error budget and how does it change engineering behavior?

👨 Khanh: An error budget is the amount of downtime or errors your SLO allows before you breach your reliability target.

If your SLO is 99.9% availability — "three nines" — your error budget is 0.1% of time in a rolling 30-day window:

  • 30 days × 24 hours × 60 minutes = 43,200 minutes/month
  • 0.1% of that = 43.2 minutes of allowed downtime per month

The error budget is intentionally finite. This creates a shared incentive between product and reliability:

  • If budget is healthy (plenty remaining): you can take risk — deploy frequently, experiment with new features, try bold infrastructure changes. You have headroom.
  • If budget is nearly exhausted: you stop risky releases, focus on reliability work, freeze feature deploys until the budget resets. The system is fragile right now.

How it changes behavior:

Without error budgets: product wants to ship fast, SRE wants stability — endless negotiation, politics. With error budgets: the math decides. If the budget is gone, feature deploys pause — not because SRE said so, but because the agreement says so. It removes blame and replaces it with objective data.

Error budget policies define what happens at thresholds:

  • 50% consumed: review deployment velocity
  • 75% consumed: feature freeze, reliability sprint
  • 100% consumed: incident review + remediation before any new risky work

👩‍💻 Priya: How do you design an SLO? Walk me through the process for a payment API.

👨 Khanh: Three steps:

Step 1 — Define what "good" means for the user. For a payment API, users care about:

  • Was my payment processed? → Availability SLI: % of payment requests that return a non-5xx response
  • Was it fast? → Latency SLI: % of payment requests completed in < 2 seconds

Step 2 — Choose the SLO target based on user pain threshold, not what you think you can achieve.

"How bad does it need to get before users are genuinely harmed?"

  • Payments: even 1% failure rate is severe — users lose trust. SLO: 99.95% availability.
  • Latency: a payment taking > 5s feels broken. SLO: 99th percentile < 3 seconds.

Don't set SLOs you can trivially achieve (99% when you're running at 99.99%) — that wastes error budget that could buy you deployment freedom.

Step 3 — Define the measurement window and alerting.

  • Rolling 30-day window is standard — avoids gaming monthly resets.
  • Alert at 75% budget consumed (burn rate alert): "At current error rate you'll exhaust the budget in 6 hours." This gives time to respond before breach.
  • Alert formulas: Google's SRE workbook defines burn rate multipliers — a burn rate of 1.0 means you'll exhaust budget in exactly the SLO window. Alert at 14× burn rate for immediate pages.

👩‍💻 Priya: API evolution. What makes an API change backward compatible? Walk me through a schema migration scenario.

👨 Khanh: A change is backward compatible if existing clients can continue to function without modification. The rules:

Safe (backward compatible):

  • Adding a new optional field to a response
  • Adding a new endpoint
  • Making a required request field optional
  • Adding a new enum value (with care — clients must handle unknown enums)

Breaking (not backward compatible):

  • Removing or renaming a field
  • Changing a field's type (e.g., int → string)
  • Making an optional field required
  • Changing the meaning of an existing field
  • Removing an endpoint

Schema migration scenario — adding a field that was once computed, now persisted:

Say your payment API used to compute fee_amount on the fly. You now want to store it in the DB and return it from a new fee field in the response.


v1 response: { "amount": 100, "currency": "USD" }
v2 response: { "amount": 100, "currency": "USD", "fee": 5.00 }

Step 1: Deploy new code that writes fee to the DB but returns it optionally in the response. Old clients ignore the new field. ✓

Step 2: Migrate existing rows — backfill fee for historical records using a background job. Use batched updates with a delay to avoid DB overload.

Step 3: Once all clients are updated to use fee, make it required in documentation (but leave the code optional for safety).

Step 4: Monitor clients — if anyone is still using the old computed path, you'll see it in API analytics. Give them a deprecation window (typically 6 months).

The key rule: expand first, contract later. Never remove before all consumers have migrated.

Database column removal — even trickier:

1. Stop writing to the old column (code deployed)

2. Stop reading from the old column (code deployed)

3. Wait for all deployments to roll out and verify in production

4. Only then run ALTER TABLE DROP COLUMN

Skipping any step risks reading NULL from a column that new code expects, or writing to a column the new schema dropped.

👩‍💻 Priya: Last one. What is a burn rate alert and why is a simple threshold alert on error rate alone insufficient?

👨 Khanh: A simple error rate alert — "alert if error rate > 1%" — has two problems:

1. Doesn't account for volume. 1% of 10 requests = 0.1 errors. 1% of 10,000 requests = 100 errors. Same alert, wildly different severity.

2. Doesn't tell you if you'll breach the SLO. You might sustain 0.5% error rate for the entire month and quietly exhaust 100% of a 99.5% SLO with no single spike to alert on.

Burn rate solves this: it's how fast you're consuming your error budget relative to the rate that would exactly exhaust it in the SLO window.

Formula: burn rate = (current error rate) / (1 - SLO target)

For a 99.9% SLO: error budget = 0.1%. If current error rate = 1%:

burn rate = 1% / 0.1% = 10×

At 10× burn rate, you'll exhaust your monthly budget in 3 days (30 days / 10). That's a page-worthy event.

Multi-window burn rate alerts (Google's recommendation): alert on two windows simultaneously — 1 hour and 5 minutes. The 1-hour window catches sustained moderate problems. The 5-minute window catches sudden severe spikes. Both must fire together to page — this avoids false positives from short transient spikes.

SCORECARD — Interview #77

Topic Score Key Insight
Error budget concept 10/10 Removes blame, math-driven release decisions
SLO design process 10/10 User pain threshold, rolling window, burn rate alerting
Backward compatible API 10/10 Expand-then-contract, DB column removal 4-step rule
Burn rate alert 10/10 Multi-window, formula, vs simple threshold

QUICK REFERENCE — Spring, Testing, SRE

@Transactional Traps

Trap Cause Fix
Self-invocation this.method() bypasses proxy Separate bean or self-inject
REQUIRES_NEW deadlock Inner tx waits for outer's lock Audit only fields that outer tx doesn't lock
Rollback on checked exception Default only rolls back RuntimeException Add rollbackFor = Exception.class
Transaction lost in async @Async runs in new thread, no tx context Open new tx in async method explicitly

SLO / Error Budget Formula

Metric Formula
Error budget (minutes/month) 43,200 × (1 - SLO)
Burn rate current error rate / (1 - SLO)
Time to exhaustion 30 days / burn rate
Alert threshold Burn rate > 14× = page immediately

Backward Compatibility Rules

Change Safe?
Add optional response field ✅ Yes
Add new endpoint ✅ Yes
Remove field ❌ Breaking
Rename field ❌ Breaking
Change field type ❌ Breaking
Drop DB column (without migration steps) ❌ Breaking

QUICK REFERENCE — Spring, Testing, SRE

@Transactional Traps

Trap Cause Fix
Self-invocation this.method() bypasses proxy Separate bean or self-inject
REQUIRES_NEW deadlock Inner tx waits for outer's lock Audit only fields that outer tx doesn't lock
Rollback on checked exception Default only rolls back RuntimeException Add rollbackFor = Exception.class
Transaction lost in async @Async runs in new thread, no tx context Open new tx in async method explicitly

SLO / Error Budget Formula

Metric Formula
