Mock Interview #78 — Redis Labs | Caching Internals & Distributed Locking

— Redis Labs / Upstash (US, Caching Platform)

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Platform Core

Focus: Cache stampede, cache-aside pattern, split brain, fencing tokens, distributed locking edge cases

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Daniel (Staff Engineer, distributed systems)

👨‍💻 Daniel: Khanh — caching is deceptively complex. Start with this: what is a cache stampede and how do you prevent it?

👨 Khanh: A cache stampede — also called a thundering herd — happens when a popular cache key expires and many concurrent requests all simultaneously find a cache miss, all query the database at once, and all try to repopulate the same key. Instead of one DB query, you get hundreds.

Classic scenario: a product page cached for 60 seconds. At expiry, 500 concurrent users all hit the cache simultaneously, find it empty, and all fire a query to the product DB — which gets hammered and may fall over.

Prevention strategies:

1 — Probabilistic early expiration (XFetch algorithm). Instead of waiting for expiry, each request has a small probability of refreshing the cache before it expires, proportional to how close to expiry the key is. The probability increases as expiry approaches, so eventually one request refreshes it while the cache is still warm — no stampede.

2 — Lock-based single-flight. When a cache miss occurs, one request acquires a Redis lock (SET NX PX) and refreshes the cache. All other requests for the same key wait briefly, then read from the freshly populated cache:


String value = redis.get(key);
if (value == null) {
    boolean locked = redis.set(lockKey, "1", "NX", "PX", 5000);
    if (locked) {
        try {
            value = db.query(id);
            redis.setex(key, TTL, value);
        } finally {
            redis.del(lockKey);
        }
    } else {
        Thread.sleep(50); // wait for winner to populate
        value = redis.get(key); // retry
    }
}

3 — Background refresh. Cache entries have a "soft TTL" (when to refresh) and a "hard TTL" (when to serve stale). When soft TTL passes, one background thread refreshes the cache asynchronously. All requests continue serving stale data until refresh completes — zero stampede, slightly stale reads.

My preference: background refresh for read-heavy, tolerance-for-stale workloads. Lock-based for strong consistency requirements.

👨‍💻 Daniel: Good. Explain the cache-aside pattern versus write-through. When does cache-aside lead to data inconsistency and how do you handle it?

👨 Khanh: Cache-aside (lazy loading): application is responsible for cache management. On read: check cache → if miss, load from DB, populate cache, return. On write: write to DB directly, then invalidate the cache key (don't update — invalidate).

Write-through: every write goes to the cache AND the DB synchronously. Cache is always warm, always consistent — but every write pays the cache write cost, and you cache data that may never be read.

Where cache-aside leads to inconsistency — the race condition:

1. Request A reads key → cache miss → queries DB → gets value V1

2. Request B writes new value V2 to DB → invalidates cache key

3. Request A (still running) → populates cache with stale V1

Now cache has V1, DB has V2. This race is rare but possible under concurrent load.

Mitigations:

  • Short TTL: even if stale data enters cache, it expires quickly. For most use cases, eventual consistency within seconds is acceptable.
  • Versioned cache keys: include a version/timestamp in the cache key. Invalidation updates the version, old keys naturally expire without serving stale.
  • Compare-and-swap on cache update: before writing to cache, verify the DB timestamp matches what you fetched. If it differs, skip the cache write.

For financial data — I'd use write-through or skip caching that specific field entirely rather than accept any window of stale data.

👨‍💻 Daniel: Distributed locking. What is split brain in a distributed system and how do fencing tokens solve the problem?

👨 Khanh: Split brain occurs when a network partition causes a distributed system to have two groups of nodes, each believing it is the only active partition. Each group may elect a leader and accept writes — resulting in two "authoritative" states that conflict.

Classic example: Redis Sentinel with 3 nodes. A network partition splits them into [node1] | [node2, node3]. Sentinel on each side elects a master. Now two Redis masters accept writes. When the partition heals, data has diverged — one master's writes will be lost.

The lock safety problem this creates: Thread A acquires a distributed lock. A GC pause or network hiccup causes Thread A to miss the lock renewal heartbeat. The lock expires. Thread B acquires the same lock. Thread A resumes, doesn't know the lock was stolen, and continues operating on the shared resource. Two threads now hold what they think is an exclusive lock — data corruption.

Fencing tokens solve this. When a lock is granted, the lock service issues a monotonically increasing token:

  • Thread A acquires lock → receives token 33
  • A is paused → lock expires → Thread B acquires lock → receives token 34
  • Thread A resumes → tries to write to storage with token 33
  • Storage sees: "I already processed token 34 — token 33 is stale" → rejects A's write

The storage layer enforces the token. Even if the lock client thinks it holds the lock, the resource enforces ordering. This requires the resource (DB, file system) to store and check the latest seen token.

In practice: this is why ZooKeeper ephemeral nodes + monotonic version numbers (zxid) are preferred over pure Redis locks for critical sections. Redis Redlock is probabilistic — under network stress, fencing tokens are the correct solution.

👨‍💻 Daniel: What's the difference between Redis SET NX and Redlock, and when is Redlock insufficient?**

👨 Khanh: SET key value NX PX ttl on a single Redis node: atomic, fast, correct — as long as the Redis node doesn't fail. If the node crashes after granting the lock but before the client releases it, the lock is held until TTL expires (or forever if TTL wasn't set). No safety issue — but availability issue.

Redlock extends to multiple Redis nodes (typically 5) for fault tolerance. The client acquires the lock on the majority (3 of 5) with the same TTL. A lock is considered held only if acquired on a majority within TTL/2 time. If any single node fails, the lock is still held on the majority.

When Redlock is insufficient:

1. Clock skew: Redlock relies on TTL measured by each node's local clock. If node clocks drift significantly, a lock can expire on some nodes while still valid on others — violating safety.

2. Process pause after majority acquire: Client acquires lock on 3/5 nodes. Then a GC pause causes the client to exceed the TTL. Locks expire on all nodes. Another client acquires. Original client resumes, thinks it still holds the lock. No fencing token → data corruption.

Martin Kleppmann's 2016 analysis of Redlock demonstrated these failure modes rigorously. For safety-critical distributed locks — where correctness matters more than availability — use a consensus-based system (ZooKeeper, etcd) with fencing tokens. Use Redis locks for availability-critical scenarios where brief lock collisions are tolerable.

SCORECARD — Interview #78

Topic Score Key Insight
Cache stampede 10/10 3 prevention strategies, probabilistic + lock + background
Cache-aside vs write-through 10/10 Race condition + 3 mitigations
Split brain + fencing token 10/10 Token enforced by resource, not by lock client
Redlock limitations 10/10 Clock skew + GC pause failure modes

Mock Interview #79 — Okta | Security Deep Dive

— Okta (US, Identity Platform) — Security Deep Dive

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Authorization Services

Focus: Spring Security filter chain internals, prepared statements, secret rotation, advisory locks

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Nadia (Staff Security Engineer)

👩‍💻 Nadia: Security mindset first. Walk me through Spring Security's filter chain. What happens before your controller sees a request?

👨 Khanh: Spring Security intercepts every HTTP request through a chain of servlet filters registered in a specific order. The chain is implemented as SecurityFilterChain — a list of Filter instances. Each filter does its job and either passes the request to the next filter or short-circuits (returns an error response).

Key filters in order (simplified):

1. DisableEncodeUrlFilter — prevents session IDs from leaking into URLs.

2. SecurityContextPersistenceFilter — loads the SecurityContext (who is logged in) from the session at the start of the request, and saves it back at the end. In stateless APIs (JWT), this is replaced by a custom filter.

3. UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter — processes form login (POST /login). Extracts credentials, calls AuthenticationManager, sets SecurityContext on success.

4. BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter (when using OAuth resource server) — extracts the Authorization: Bearer header, validates the JWT (signature, expiry, issuer), and sets the SecurityContext.

5. ExceptionTranslationFilter — catches AuthenticationException and AccessDeniedException thrown by downstream filters. Converts them to 401 or 403 responses.

6. AuthorizationFilter (was FilterSecurityInterceptor) — checks if the authenticated principal has the required roles/authorities for the requested URL. Uses AuthorizationManager to evaluate @PreAuthorize, hasRole(), etc.

Customization: SecurityFilterChain bean in @Configuration: 


@Bean
SecurityFilterChain filterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    return http
        .csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable())           // stateless API
        .sessionManagement(s -> s.sessionCreationPolicy(STATELESS))
        .authorizeHttpRequests(a -> a
            .requestMatchers("/public/**").permitAll()
            .anyRequest().authenticated())
        .oauth2ResourceServer(oauth2 -> oauth2.jwt(Customizer.withDefaults()))
        .addFilterBefore(customRateLimitFilter, BearerTokenAuthenticationFilter.class)
        .build();
}

👩‍💻 Nadia: Good. Explain exactly why prepared statements prevent SQL injection. What does the DB actually do differently?

👨 Khanh: SQL injection works by inserting SQL syntax into a user input that gets concatenated into a query string. The DB parses the combined string as a single SQL statement — the injected SQL becomes part of the query structure.

Vulnerable:


String sql = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '" + input + "'";
// Input: ' OR '1'='1
// Query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = '' OR '1'='1'
// Returns all rows — authentication bypassed

Why prepared statements prevent this:

When you use a parameterized query, the SQL template and the parameters are sent to the database separately, in two distinct protocol messages:

1. Prepare phase: SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ? — the DB parses, compiles, and caches the query plan. At this point, the structure of the query is fixed.

2. Execute phase: the value ' OR '1'='1 is sent as a data binding, not as SQL text. The DB treats it as a literal string value for the name column — it is never interpreted as SQL syntax. The query plan is already compiled; there's no parsing step where the value could alter the structure. 


PreparedStatement ps = conn.prepareStatement(
    "SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?");
ps.setString(1, input);  // input treated as data, never as SQL
ResultSet rs = ps.executeQuery();

The key insight: injection attacks exploit the fact that SQL mixes control structure (SQL syntax) with data (values). Prepared statements enforce a hard separation — the control structure is fixed before the data is ever provided.

Additional benefit: the query plan is compiled once and reused — performance improvement for repeated queries.

👩‍💻 Nadia: How do you implement secret rotation without downtime? Walk me through the database credential rotation scenario.

👨 Khanh: Secret rotation without downtime requires the application to support multiple valid credentials simultaneously for a window of time. Here's the process for rotating a database password:

Step 1 — Create new credential. In the DB, create a new user or set a new password for the existing user. At this point, both old and new passwords are valid (if using a new user, grant it the same permissions).

Step 2 — Update secret store. Write the new credential to AWS Secrets Manager (or Vault) with a versioning label — e.g., AWSPENDING. The old credential stays as AWSCURRENT.

Step 3 — Application picks up new credential. Applications using Secrets Manager SDK can be configured to automatically reload credentials. For HikariCP:


// On connection acquisition, HikariCP calls getPassword()
// Override to always fetch from Secrets Manager:
dataSource.setPassword(secretsManager.getSecret("db/password"));

Or use a scheduled refresh — every 60 seconds, fetch the latest credential and update the pool. When connections are checked out, they use the current credential.

Step 4 — Test the new credential. Secrets Manager's rotation Lambda (or your rotation job) verifies the new credential works against the DB before promoting it.

Step 5 — Promote new credential. Swap AWSPENDING to AWSCURRENT. Old credential becomes AWSPREVIOUS.

Step 6 — Drain old connections. HikariCP's connection validation (testOnBorrow) will reject connections still using the old credential. They'll be evicted and replaced with new connections using the new credential. Set connectionTestQuery and validationTimeout accordingly.

Step 7 — Revoke old credential. Once all connection pool connections have cycled (within maxLifetime — default 30 minutes in HikariCP), disable or delete the old credential.

Key principle: never rotate a secret in one atomic step. Always dual-support → verify → promote → drain → revoke.

👩‍💻 Nadia: What is a PostgreSQL advisory lock and when would you use it instead of a table row lock?**

👨 Khanh: A PostgreSQL advisory lock is an application-level lock stored in the DB's lock manager — not tied to any table row or object. You define the lock by an arbitrary integer key that you choose. It's purely cooperative — the DB doesn't enforce what the lock protects; your application code must honor it.


-- Session-level: held until released or session ends
SELECT pg_advisory_lock(12345);
-- ... critical section ...
SELECT pg_advisory_unlock(12345);

-- Transaction-level: automatically released at transaction end
SELECT pg_advisory_xact_lock(12345);

When to use advisory locks instead of row locks:

1. No suitable row to lock on. You want to prevent two instances from running a batch job simultaneously — there's no "batch job row" to lock. Use pg_advisory_lock(hashtext('batch-job-daily-report')).

2. Cross-table critical sections. A SELECT FOR UPDATE locks a specific row. If your critical section spans multiple tables and you don't want to lock every row involved, an advisory lock is cleaner.

3. Non-database resources. You want to coordinate access to a file system path or an external API, using PostgreSQL as the lock service (since it's already in your stack).

4. Try-lock semantics without blocking. pg_try_advisory_lock() returns false immediately if the lock is held — useful for "skip if already running" job schedulers.

Compared to distributed Redis locks: advisory locks are transactionally consistent with your DB operations (transaction-level advisory locks auto-release on commit/rollback), require no additional infrastructure, but are limited to your PostgreSQL cluster and don't work across DB instances.

SCORECARD — Interview #79

Topic Score Key Insight
Spring Security filter chain 10/10 Order of filters, JWT filter, custom filter insertion
Prepared statements 10/10 Two-phase protocol, data vs control separation
Secret rotation without downtime 10/10 Dual-support → verify → promote → drain → revoke
Advisory locks 10/10 No row needed, try-lock semantics, vs Redis locks

Mock Interview #80 — Instana/IBM | Performance Profiling & Reactive

— Instana / IBM (Germany, APM Platform)

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Agent Instrumentation Team

Focus: Flame graphs, CompletableFuture error handling, direct buffers, reactive backpressure

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Tobias (Principal Engineer, performance specialist)

👨‍💻 Tobias: Performance engineering. What is a flame graph and how do you read one to find a performance bottleneck?

👨 Khanh: A flame graph is a visualization of profiler stack traces, aggregated across thousands of samples. Each bar represents a stack frame (a method). The width of a bar is proportional to how often that frame appeared in samples — wider = more CPU time.

Reading a flame graph:

  • Y-axis: call stack depth. Bottom = entry points (threads, main), top = leaf methods actually executing.
  • X-axis: NOT time — it's alphabetically sorted merged samples. Width = percentage of total CPU time.
  • The bottleneck: look for wide "plateaus" near the top — wide frames at the top of the stack that don't call anything deeper. These are the methods consuming the most CPU.

Example pattern — finding N+1 in profiling:

If you see HibernateJdbcCoordinator.getStatementPreparer consuming 40% width, and it appears under OrderService.findAll → your repository is making many small DB calls inside a loop. Immediately suspect N+1.

Tools:

  • async-profiler (Linux): samples at native level, captures both Java and native frames. Command: ./profiler.sh -d 30 -f flamegraph.html
  • Java Flight Recorder + JDK Mission Control: low-overhead built-in profiling
  • IntelliJ Profiler: integrates flame graph view in IDE

What to look for:

1. Wide frames near the top → CPU hotspot

2. Tall stacks with narrow top frames → deep call chains, possibly reflection or serialization overhead

3. sun.misc.Unsafe.park dominating → threads spending time waiting (I/O blocking, lock contention)

👨‍💻 Tobias: CompletableFuture error handling. What's the difference between exceptionally, handle, and whenComplete, and what mistake do most engineers make?

👨 Khanh: All three handle errors in CompletableFuture, but with important differences:

exceptionally(fn) — runs fn only if the stage completed exceptionally. Returns a recovery value. The resulting stage is always successful (exception is consumed):


future.exceptionally(ex -> {
    log.error("Failed", ex);
    return defaultValue;  // recovery
});

handle(fn) — runs fn regardless of success or failure. Receives (result, exception) — one of which is always null. Can inspect both and return a new value:


future.handle((result, ex) -> {
    if (ex != null) return handleError(ex);
    return transform(result);
});

whenComplete(fn) — runs fn regardless of outcome but cannot change the result. Used for side effects (logging, metrics). The original result or exception propagates unchanged: 


future.whenComplete((result, ex) -> {
    metrics.record(ex != null ? "failure" : "success");
    // return value ignored — original outcome unchanged
});

The common mistake — swallowing exceptions with exceptionally in a chain:


CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> callService())
    .exceptionally(ex -> null)     // swallows exception, returns null
    .thenApply(result -> result.transform());  // NPE here!

exceptionally consumed the exception and returned null. The next stage receives null and blows up with NullPointerException — which is now harder to trace than the original exception.

Fix: either recover with a meaningful default, re-throw, or use handle which forces you to deal with both success and failure explicitly.

Another mistake — missing exceptionally at the end of a chain entirely. Unhandled exceptions in CompletableFuture are silently swallowed unless something calls .get() or .join(). Always add a terminal error handler: 


future
    .thenApply(...)
    .thenAccept(...)
    .exceptionally(ex -> { log.error("Unhandled", ex); return null; });

👨‍💻 Tobias: Memory. When would you use Java NIO direct buffers or memory-mapped files, and what are the GC implications?

👨 Khanh: Direct buffers (ByteBuffer.allocateDirect()):

Regular heap buffers (ByteBuffer.allocate()) live in the Java heap — subject to GC. When you do I/O, the JVM copies data from heap memory to a native buffer for the OS to use. That copy has CPU cost.

Direct buffers are allocated outside the Java heap, in native memory managed by the OS. The JVM can hand the buffer pointer directly to the OS for DMA (direct memory access) I/O — zero copy. No heap-to-native copying.

When to use:

  • High-throughput NIO networking (Netty uses direct buffers internally for this reason)
  • Large I/O operations where the copy cost is measurable

GC implication: direct buffers are not on the heap, so GC doesn't see them for size. But they have a Cleaner registered on the Java heap — a phantom reference. When the ByteBuffer object is GC'd, the cleaner deallocates the native memory. If you allocate many direct buffers and GC doesn't run (because heap pressure is low), the native memory can grow unboundedly. Monitor with -XX:NativeMemoryTracking=summary.

Memory-mapped files (FileChannel.map()):

Maps a file (or region) directly into the process's virtual address space. Reads and writes to the MappedByteBuffer access the file without explicit read/write syscalls — the OS handles paging pages in/out.

When to use:

  • Large files you want to access randomly without reading sequentially (log file indexing, search indexes)
  • Inter-process shared memory (same file mapped by multiple processes)
  • Files that are mostly read after initial load (read-only memory-mapped is very efficient)

GC implication: like direct buffers, the mapping lives outside the heap. Additionally, unmapping a MappedByteBuffer is not guaranteed on all JVMs until GC runs — forcibly unmapping requires sun.misc.Cleaner reflection hack or using JDK 14+ Arena API. Holding a mapping prevents file deletion on Windows.

👨‍💻 Tobias: Last one. Reactive backpressure — what is it, why does it matter, and how does Project Reactor implement it?

👨 Khanh: Backpressure is the mechanism by which a consumer signals to a producer how much data it can handle at a given moment. Without backpressure, a fast producer overwhelms a slow consumer — buffers fill, memory grows, the system OOMs or drops data.

Real example without backpressure: a Kafka consumer reads 10,000 messages/second but downstream DB writes can only handle 1,000/second. Without backpressure, you buffer 9,000 messages/second in memory — heap grows → GC pressure → eventual OOM or eviction.

How Project Reactor implements backpressure (Reactive Streams specification):

The Reactive Streams protocol defines 4 interfaces: Publisher, Subscriber, Subscription, Processor. The key is Subscription.request(n) — a subscriber explicitly requests n items from the publisher. The publisher sends at most n items. 


Subscriber → subscription.request(100)  // "I can handle 100"
Publisher  → onNext() × 100              // sends exactly 100
Subscriber → subscription.request(50)   // "I processed some, send 50 more"
Publisher  → onNext() × 50

Reactor operators for backpressure:

  • Flux.onBackpressureBuffer(maxSize) — buffer up to maxSize items. If exceeded, apply overflow strategy.
  • Flux.onBackpressureDrop() — drop items the downstream can't handle. Appropriate for live telemetry (losing one data point is acceptable).
  • Flux.onBackpressureLatest() — keep only the most recent item. Appropriate for real-time dashboards.
  • Flux.onBackpressureError() — emit error when downstream is too slow. Appropriate for financial transactions where dropping is unacceptable.

In practice with Spring WebFlux: if your controller returns a Flux, the HTTP client receives events as fast as it requests them. If the client is a slow consumer (SSE stream on a mobile device), Reactor applies backpressure upstream automatically — the source slows its emission rate, preventing buffer bloat.

SCORECARD — Interview #80

Topic Score Key Insight
Flame graph reading 10/10 Width = CPU%, wide top plateaus = hotspot
CompletableFuture error handling 10/10 exceptionally vs handle vs whenComplete + swallowing trap
Direct buffers + memory-mapped 10/10 Native memory, GC cleaner, unmapping caveat
Reactive backpressure 10/10 request(n) protocol, 4 overflow strategies

QUICK REFERENCE — Caching, Security, Performance

Cache Patterns

Pattern Write path Read path Consistency
Cache-aside DB write → cache invalidate Cache miss → DB → cache Eventual (race window)
Write-through Cache write → DB write Always cache hit Strong
Write-behind Cache write → async DB Always cache hit Eventual (crash risk)
Read-through DB on miss (cache manages) Cache manages load Eventual

Cache Stampede Prevention

Strategy Mechanism Best for
Lock + single-flight Redis NX lock, others wait Strong consistency needed
Background refresh Async refresh before hard TTL High read throughput
Probabilistic early expiry XFetch: P(refresh) ∝ proximity to TTL Read-heavy, tolerance for slight stale

CompletableFuture Error Handling

Method Runs when Changes result Use for
exceptionally(fn) Exception only Yes (recovery) Default value on error
handle(fn) Always Yes Inspect both success and failure
whenComplete(fn) Always No Side effects: logging, metrics

Backpressure Overflow Strategies

Strategy Behavior Use case
onBackpressureBuffer Buffer up to limit Bursty traffic, slow consumers
onBackpressureDrop Drop excess items Live telemetry, dashboards
onBackpressureLatest Keep only newest Real-time state display
onBackpressureError Error on overflow Financial, must-process-all

Cache-aside DB write → cache invalidate Cache miss → DB → cache Eventual (race window)
Write-through Cache write → DB write Always cache hit Strong
Write-behind Cache write → async DB Always cache hit Eventual (crash risk)
Read-through DB on miss (cache manages) Cache manages load Eventual

Cache Stampede Prevention

Strategy Mechanism Best for
Lock + single-flight Redis NX lock, others wait Strong consistency needed
Background refresh Async refresh before hard TTL High read throughput
Probabilistic early expiry XFetch: P(refresh) ∝ proximity to TTL Read-heavy, tolerance for slight stale

CompletableFuture Error Handling

Method Runs when Changes result Use for
exceptionally(fn) Exception only Yes (recovery) Default value on error
handle(fn) Always Yes Inspect both success and failure
whenComplete(fn) Always No Side effects: logging, metrics

Backpressure Overflow Strategies

Strategy Behavior Use case
onBackpressureBuffer Buffer up to limit Bursty traffic, slow consumers
onBackpressureDrop Drop excess items Live telemetry, dashboards
onBackpressureLatest Keep only newest Real-time state display
onBackpressureError Error on overflow Financial, must-process-all

Mock Interview #81 — Booking.com | JPA & Data Access Deep Dive

— Booking.com (Netherlands) — JPA & Data Access

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Accommodation Search Platform

Focus: Criteria API, JPQL vs native SQL, cursor-based pagination, aggregate root in JPA

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Marek (Staff Engineer, data access specialist)

👨‍💻 Marek: Khanh — let's go deep on JPA. When would you use the Criteria API over JPQL, and what's the trade-off?

👨 Khanh: Both JPQL and Criteria API produce the same SQL ultimately — the difference is how you build the query.

JPQL is a string-based query language. Simple to write, easy to read, but the query is validated at runtime — a typo in a field name only fails when the query executes:


@Query("SELECT o FROM Order o WHERE o.status = :status AND o.userId = :userId ORDER BY o.createdAt DESC")
List<Order> findByStatusAndUser(@Param("status") OrderStatus status, @Param("userId") Long userId);

Criteria API builds queries programmatically using a type-safe metamodel. Compile-time validation — a wrong field name is a compilation error:


CriteriaBuilder cb = em.getCriteriaBuilder();
CriteriaQuery<Order> query = cb.createQuery(Order.class);
Root<Order> order = query.from(Order.class);

List<Predicate> predicates = new ArrayList<>();
if (status != null)
    predicates.add(cb.equal(order.get(Order_.status), status));
if (userId != null)
    predicates.add(cb.equal(order.get(Order_.userId), userId));

query.where(predicates.toArray(new Predicate[0]))
     .orderBy(cb.desc(order.get(Order_.createdAt)));
return em.createQuery(query).getResultList();

When Criteria API wins: dynamic queries where the number and combination of WHERE clauses vary at runtime based on filter input. You cannot safely build dynamic JPQL with string concatenation — SQL injection risk + unmaintainable code. Criteria API handles optional filters cleanly.

Trade-off: Criteria API is verbose and hard to read. For static queries, JPQL (or Spring Data's @Query) is always preferred. For dynamic search filters — Criteria API or Spring Data Specification.

Spring Data Specification is a cleaner abstraction over Criteria API for common use:


Specification<Order> spec = (root, query, cb) ->
    status != null ? cb.equal(root.get("status"), status) : cb.conjunction();
orderRepository.findAll(spec, pageable);

👨‍💻 Marek: Good. JPQL vs native SQL — when do you choose native, and what are the risks?

👨 Khanh: JPQL is database-agnostic — it operates on entity objects and JPA translates it to SQL for your specific dialect. Native SQL queries bypass JPA's abstraction.

Choose native SQL when:

1. DB-specific features not available in JPQL. Window functions (ROW_NUMBER() OVER), CTEs (WITH clause), RETURNING clause, ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (PostgreSQL upsert), full-text search with tsvector. JPQL has no equivalent.

2. Performance-critical queries. Sometimes the SQL JPA generates for complex JPQL is not optimal. Native SQL lets you control the exact query plan — add FORCE INDEX, use specific join hints.

3. Batch operations. UPDATE orders SET status = 'EXPIRED' WHERE expires_at < NOW() — a native bulk update avoids loading entities into memory. 


@Query(value = "SELECT * FROM orders WHERE status = :status FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED LIMIT :limit",
       nativeQuery = true)
List<Order> findNextBatch(@Param("status") String status, @Param("limit") int limit);

Risks of native SQL:

1. No portability. PostgreSQL-specific SQL won't work on MySQL. Important if you might switch DBs (unlikely but possible).

2. No automatic entity mapping for joins. JPQL join results map to entity graphs. Native queries return raw Object[] unless you use @SqlResultSetMapping or projections.

3. No compile-time validation. A native query typo fails at runtime.

4. JPA first-level cache bypass. Native queries don't interact with the EntityManager's cache the same way — you can read stale entity state if you run native updates and then load entities in the same session.

👨‍💻 Marek: Pagination. What's wrong with offset-based pagination at scale, and how does cursor-based pagination fix it?

👨 Khanh: Offset pagination problem:


SELECT * FROM orders ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 20 OFFSET 10000;

The database must scan and discard 10,000 rows before returning the 20 you want. At page 500 (offset 10,000), this is expensive — O(offset) cost regardless of index. As users page deeper, queries get slower.

Additional problem: if a row is inserted or deleted between page requests, the offset shifts — users see duplicate items or skip items. Classic "ghost row" problem.

Cursor-based (keyset) pagination:

Instead of an offset, use the last seen value of the ORDER BY column(s) as a cursor:


-- First page
SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = ? ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC LIMIT 20;

-- Next page — cursor = (last_created_at, last_id) from previous result
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE user_id = ?
  AND (created_at, id) < (:lastCreatedAt, :lastId)  -- keyset condition
ORDER BY created_at DESC, id DESC
LIMIT 20;

The DB uses the index on (created_at, id) to jump directly to the cursor position — O(log n), not O(offset).

Trade-offs of cursor pagination:

  • ✅ Consistent performance regardless of depth
  • ✅ No duplicate/skipped rows on insert/delete
  • ❌ Cannot jump to page 47 directly — must paginate sequentially
  • ❌ Cursor must include all ORDER BY columns (must be unique enough to be a stable position)
  • ❌ Harder to implement than offset — requires composite cursor encoding/decoding

In practice: use cursor pagination for infinite scroll, feed-style APIs, event logs. Use offset only for admin UIs with small datasets where "go to page X" matters.

👨‍💻 Marek: Last one — what is an aggregate root in DDD and how does it affect your JPA mapping strategy?

👨 Khanh: In Domain-Driven Design, an aggregate is a cluster of domain objects that are treated as a single unit for the purpose of data changes. The aggregate root is the single entry point — all operations on the aggregate go through the root, and external objects hold references only to the root, never to inner objects.

Example: Order is an aggregate root. OrderItem and ShippingAddress are inner objects — they only exist in the context of an Order. You never manipulate an OrderItem directly; you call order.addItem(...), order.removeItem(...).

How this affects JPA mapping:

1. Only the root has a repository. OrderRepository exists. No OrderItemRepository — that would bypass the aggregate boundary and allow external code to corrupt order state.

2. CascadeType.ALL on collections. Since Order owns its Items, cascade all operations:


@OneToMany(mappedBy = "order", cascade = CascadeType.ALL, orphanRemoval = true)
private List<OrderItem> items = new ArrayList<>();

orphanRemoval = true ensures items removed from the collection are deleted — critical for maintaining the aggregate boundary.

3. Load the whole aggregate. Use JOIN FETCH or @EntityGraph to load root + children in one query. Avoid lazy loading for aggregates that need all children for business logic — it leads to N+1 queries and potentially lazy-loading outside a transaction.

4. Aggregate boundaries map to transaction boundaries. One transaction = one aggregate. If a business operation touches two aggregates (Order and Shipment), use eventual consistency + domain events — not a single transaction spanning both.

SCORECARD — Interview #81

Topic Score Key Insight
Criteria API vs JPQL 10/10 Dynamic queries → Criteria, static → JPQL/Specification
Native SQL trade-offs 10/10 Window functions, bulk ops, L1 cache bypass risk
Cursor pagination 10/10 Keyset = O(log n), offset = O(n), ghost row problem
Aggregate root in JPA 10/10 One repo per root, CascadeAll + orphanRemoval, tx boundaries

Mock Interview #82 — N26 | Architecture Patterns

— N26 (Germany, Digital Bank) — Architecture Patterns

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Core Banking Architecture

Focus: Hexagonal architecture, decorator + command patterns, DDD bounded contexts, ports and adapters

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Sofia (Principal Architect)

👩‍💻 Sofia: Khanh — let's talk architecture. Explain hexagonal architecture — what problem does it solve and how do you implement it in a Spring Boot service?

👨 Khanh: Hexagonal architecture (also called Ports and Adapters, coined by Alistair Cockburn) solves the problem of technology coupling — your business logic becoming dependent on frameworks, databases, and HTTP details.

The problem it solves: traditional layered architecture (Controller → Service → Repository) lets framework concerns leak into business logic. The @Transactional annotation is Spring-specific. The JpaRepository return type ties business logic to JPA. If you want to switch the DB or test business logic without a database, you can't.

The hexagonal model:


[Driving Adapters] → [Input Ports] → [Domain/Application Core] → [Output Ports] → [Driven Adapters]

Driving Adapters:   REST Controller, Kafka Consumer, CLI, gRPC server
Input Ports:        Java interfaces your application defines (use cases)
Domain Core:        Pure Java — business rules, entities, domain services
Output Ports:       Java interfaces the domain defines for persistence, messaging
Driven Adapters:    JPA implementation, Redis implementation, Kafka producer

Spring Boot implementation:


// Input Port — defined by the application core
public interface PlaceOrderUseCase {
    OrderId placeOrder(PlaceOrderCommand command);
}

// Domain Core — pure Java, no Spring annotations
public class PlaceOrderService implements PlaceOrderUseCase {
    private final OrderRepository orderRepo;   // output port interface
    private final PaymentGateway paymentGw;    // output port interface

    public OrderId placeOrder(PlaceOrderCommand command) {
        Order order = Order.create(command);
        paymentGw.charge(order);               // calls interface, not implementation
        return orderRepo.save(order);
    }
}

// Output Port — defined by domain
public interface OrderRepository {
    OrderId save(Order order);
}

// Driven Adapter — Spring/JPA knows about the domain, not vice versa
@Component
public class JpaOrderRepository implements OrderRepository {
    private final SpringDataOrderRepo repo;
    public OrderId save(Order order) {
        OrderJpaEntity entity = mapper.toEntity(order);
        return OrderId.of(repo.save(entity).getId());
    }
}

What you gain: the domain core has zero Spring dependencies. You can test PlaceOrderService with mock implementations of OrderRepository and PaymentGateway — no Spring context, no database, millisecond unit tests. You can swap JPA for MongoDB by writing a new MongoOrderRepository without touching domain code.

👩‍💻 Sofia: Good. Explain the Decorator pattern and give me a realistic Spring production use case.

👨 Khanh: The Decorator pattern wraps an object to add behavior without modifying the original class. It implements the same interface as the wrapped object, calls through to it, and adds pre/post logic.

Production use case — adding metrics + caching to a service without touching the service:


public interface PricingService {
    Price calculatePrice(ProductId id, UserId userId);
}

// Core implementation — pure business logic
@Service("pricingServiceImpl")
public class PricingServiceImpl implements PricingService {
    public Price calculatePrice(ProductId id, UserId userId) {
        // complex pricing logic
    }
}

// Decorator 1 — adds caching
public class CachingPricingService implements PricingService {
    private final PricingService delegate;
    private final Cache cache;

    public Price calculatePrice(ProductId id, UserId userId) {
        String key = id + ":" + userId;
        return cache.get(key, () -> delegate.calculatePrice(id, userId));
    }
}

// Decorator 2 — adds metrics
public class MeteredPricingService implements PricingService {
    private final PricingService delegate;
    private final MeterRegistry registry;

    public Price calculatePrice(ProductId id, UserId userId) {
        return Timer.builder("pricing.calculate")
            .tag("userId", userId.toString())
            .register(registry)
            .record(() -> delegate.calculatePrice(id, userId));
    }
}

Wire them together in Spring:


@Bean
public PricingService pricingService(
    @Qualifier("pricingServiceImpl") PricingService impl,
    Cache cache, MeterRegistry registry) {
    return new MeteredPricingService(
        new CachingPricingService(impl, cache), registry);
}

Each decorator has a single responsibility. You can add/remove/reorder them without touching business logic. Spring's @Transactional AOP proxy is itself a decorator.

👩‍💻 Sofia: Command pattern — what problem does it solve and when do you use it in backend Java services?

👨 Khanh: The Command pattern encapsulates a request as an object, separating the request's definition from its execution. Each command carries all data needed to execute itself.

Problem it solves: you want to:

  • Queue or schedule requests for later execution
  • Support undo/redo
  • Log every operation for audit
  • Retry failed operations without re-building the request

Backend use case — audit trail with retry:


// Command interface
public interface Command<T> {
    T execute();
}

// Concrete command
public class TransferFundsCommand implements Command<TransferResult> {
    private final AccountId from;
    private final AccountId to;
    private final Money amount;
    private final TransferService service;

    public TransferResult execute() {
        return service.transfer(from, to, amount);
    }
}

// Command bus — handles execution, logging, retry
@Component
public class CommandBus {
    public <T> T dispatch(Command<T> command) {
        auditLog.record(command);
        try {
            T result = command.execute();
            auditLog.recordSuccess(command, result);
            return result;
        } catch (RetryableException e) {
            retryQueue.enqueue(command);
            throw e;
        }
    }
}

Where I've seen this pattern in practice:

  • Axon Framework uses it as its core model (Command → CommandHandler → Event)
  • Spring Batch ItemProcessor chain is a command pipeline
  • MediatR (C# world) — Java equivalent: Spring's ApplicationEventPublisher for events, custom command bus for commands
  • Outbox pattern — persisting commands to an outbox table is cleaner when each command is a serializable object

SCORECARD — Interview #82

Topic Score Key Insight
Hexagonal architecture 10/10 Zero Spring deps in domain, swap adapters freely
Decorator pattern 10/10 Caching + metrics as composable decorators
Command pattern 10/10 Audit, retry, queue — command as serializable object

Mock Interview #83 — Confluent | Kafka Advanced & K8s Production

— Confluent / Apache Kafka (US) — Kafka Advanced + K8s Production

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Streaming Platform

Focus: Consumer group rebalance, idempotent consumer, Istio sidecar, SIGTERM/preStop graceful shutdown

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Jamal (Principal Engineer, Kafka internals)

👨‍💻 Jamal: Khanh — Kafka deep internals. Explain what happens during a consumer group rebalance and why it can cause duplicate processing.

👨 Khanh: A consumer group rebalance is triggered when:

  • A new consumer joins the group
  • An existing consumer leaves (crash, shutdown, heartbeat timeout)
  • Topic partitions are added
  • subscribe() is called with a new topic list

What happens:

1. The Group Coordinator (a Kafka broker elected per group) detects the trigger.

2. It sends a JoinGroup request to all active consumers — they must pause processing and respond.

3. One consumer is elected Group Leader (typically the first to respond).

4. The Group Leader runs the partition assignment algorithm (RangeAssignor, RoundRobinAssignor, or StickyAssignor) and sends the assignment back to the Group Coordinator.

5. Group Coordinator distributes assignments to all consumers via SyncGroup response.

6. Consumers resume processing their newly assigned partitions.

Why duplicates happen:

During rebalance, consumer A was processing a batch from partition 3 but hadn't committed the offset yet. Rebalance happens — partition 3 is reassigned to consumer B. Consumer B starts from the last committed offset — which is before consumer A's in-progress batch. Consumer B reprocesses those messages.

Mitigation:

  • Commit offsets before rebalance: implement ConsumerRebalanceListener.onPartitionsRevoked() — called just before partitions are taken away. Commit synchronously here: 

consumer.subscribe(topics, new ConsumerRebalanceListener() {
    public void onPartitionsRevoked(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {
        consumer.commitSync(currentOffsets); // flush in-flight work
    }
    public void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {
        // resume from committed offset
    }
});
  • StickyAssignor: minimizes partition movement during rebalance — existing assignments kept where possible. Fewer partitions move = fewer potential duplicates.
  • Cooperative/incremental rebalance (Kafka 2.4+): instead of "stop the world" rebalance, only the partitions that need to move are revoked. Other partitions continue processing during the rebalance — dramatically reduces throughput impact.

👨‍💻 Jamal: Good. What is an idempotent consumer and how do you implement one in Java?

👨 Khanh: An idempotent consumer can process the same message multiple times and produce the same result as if it processed it once. This is essential because Kafka guarantees at-least-once delivery — duplicates are normal, especially after rebalances or retries.

Implementation strategies:

1 — Idempotency key in the DB:


@Transactional
public void processPayment(PaymentEvent event) {
    // Check if already processed
    if (paymentRepo.existsByIdempotencyKey(event.getEventId())) {
        log.info("Duplicate event {}, skipping", event.getEventId());
        return;
    }
    // Process and record the idempotency key atomically
    Payment payment = paymentService.charge(event);
    payment.setIdempotencyKey(event.getEventId());
    paymentRepo.save(payment);
}

The idempotency_key column has a UNIQUE constraint. If the same event is processed twice, the second insert fails with a unique constraint violation — caught and swallowed. The entire check-and-insert happens in one transaction.

2 — Upsert semantics:

For state updates (not financial), use INSERT ... ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE (PostgreSQL) or MERGE (SQL standard). The result is the same regardless of how many times you run it.

3 — Natural idempotency:

Some operations are naturally idempotent. SET account_status = 'VERIFIED' — running it 10 times has the same result as running it once. Design your operations to be naturally idempotent where possible.

What makes an idempotency key good:

  • Must be unique per logical operation (not per Kafka message — use business IDs)
  • Must be carried in the message, not generated at processing time
  • Should include a version/attempt number if the same logical operation can legitimately recur (e.g., monthly billing)

👨‍💻 Jamal: Kubernetes production. What is an Istio sidecar and what does it do to your service's traffic?

👨 Khanh: Istio is a service mesh. It injects a sidecar proxy (Envoy) as an additional container into every pod alongside your application container. The sidecar intercepts all inbound and outbound network traffic for the pod — your application code doesn't know it exists.

What the sidecar does:

1. Mutual TLS (mTLS): sidecar automatically encrypts all service-to-service traffic. Services authenticate each other using certificates managed by Istio's control plane (Istiod). Your app doesn't implement TLS — the sidecar handles it.

2. Traffic management: Istio VirtualService and DestinationRule resources let you route traffic by weight (canary: 10% to v2, 90% to v1), by header (x-user-type: premium → premium service), or by source.

3. Observability: every request passing through Envoy is traced (Jaeger integration), metered (Prometheus metrics: request rate, error rate, latency percentiles), and logged. You get distributed tracing without instrumenting your code.

4. Resilience: circuit breaking and retry policies defined in Istio config — not in your code. DestinationRule can specify: "if 5 consecutive 503s, open circuit for 30 seconds."

Impact on your service:

  • Each request adds ~1-5ms of sidecar latency (Envoy proxy hop)
  • Pod startup time increases — Envoy must be ready before traffic is sent
  • Debugging is harder — traffic flows through Envoy, so tcpdump on the app port shows encrypted traffic
  • Resource overhead: ~50MB memory, ~0.1 vCPU per sidecar

👨‍💻 Jamal: Last one. What happens when Kubernetes sends SIGTERM to your Java pod, and how do you implement proper graceful shutdown?

👨 Khanh: When Kubernetes terminates a pod (rolling update, scale-down, node drain):

1. Pod moves to Terminating state

2. Kubernetes executes the preStop hook (if configured) — runs before SIGTERM

3. SIGTERM is sent to PID 1 in the container

4. Kubernetes waits up to terminationGracePeriodSeconds (default 30s)

5. If the process hasn't exited, SIGKILL is sent — no cleanup possible

Problem without graceful shutdown:

  • In-flight HTTP requests are abruptly terminated → 500s for clients
  • Kafka consumer loses its partition assignment without committing offsets → duplicate processing
  • Database transactions mid-flight are rolled back → potential data loss or inconsistent state
  • HikariCP connections are forcibly closed → connection pool errors in the DB

Implementing graceful shutdown in Spring Boot:

Spring Boot 2.3+ has built-in graceful shutdown:


# application.yml
server:
  shutdown: graceful
spring:
  lifecycle:
    timeout-per-shutdown-phase: 20s

On SIGTERM, Spring: stops accepting new HTTP requests, waits for in-flight requests to complete (up to timeout), then shuts down.

Kafka consumer shutdown:


@Component
public class KafkaConsumerShutdown implements DisposableBean {
    @Autowired
    private KafkaListenerEndpointRegistry registry;

    public void destroy() {
        registry.getListenerContainers().forEach(container -> {
            container.stop();  // stops polling, waits for current record processing
        });
    }
}

preStop hook for Kubernetes — buy time before SIGTERM:


lifecycle:
  preStop:
    exec:
      command: ["sleep", "5"]

The 5-second sleep gives the load balancer time to remove the pod from the endpoints list before SIGTERM arrives — prevents new requests from hitting a shutting-down pod.

Full sequence with preStop:

1. K8s removes pod from Service endpoints (iptables update — takes 1-3s)

2. preStop runs (sleep 5s — ensures no new traffic arrives)

3. SIGTERM sent → Spring graceful shutdown begins

4. Spring stops accepting new requests, drains in-flight

5. Kafka consumer commits offsets and stops

6. HikariCP closes connections gracefully

7. JVM exits → K8s sees exit 0

Key config:


terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60  # must be > preStop + drain time

SCORECARD — Interview #83

Topic Score Key Insight
Consumer group rebalance 10/10 onPartitionsRevoked commitSync, StickyAssignor, incremental rebalance
Idempotent consumer 10/10 Unique constraint + transactional check, 3 strategies
Istio sidecar 10/10 mTLS, traffic management, observability, latency cost
SIGTERM + preStop graceful shutdown 10/10 Full sequence, preStop timing, terminationGracePeriod

QUICK REFERENCE — JPA, Patterns, Kafka+K8s

JPA Query Strategy

Scenario Tool Why
Fixed query, simple JPQL / @Query Readable, validated at startup
Dynamic filters Specification + Criteria API Type-safe, composable predicates
Window functions, CTEs Native SQL JPQL has no equivalent
Bulk update/delete Native SQL or JPQL bulk Avoid loading entities into memory
Deep pagination (page > 100) Cursor / keyset O(log n) vs O(n) offset cost

Kafka Consumer Reliability

Problem Solution
Duplicates on rebalance onPartitionsRevoked commitSync
Excessive rebalance impact StickyAssignor or incremental rebalance
Duplicate processing Idempotency key + unique DB constraint
Consumer lag Increase partitions or consumer instances
Poison pill message Dead letter topic + max retry config

Kubernetes Graceful Shutdown Checklist

Step Config
HTTP drain server.shutdown=graceful
Drain timeout spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase=20s
Kafka commit before stop onPartitionsRevoked + DisposableBean.destroy()
LB de-registration time preStop: sleep 5
Total grace period terminationGracePeriodSeconds ≥ preStop + drain

Scenario Tool Why

Bulk update/delete Native SQL or JPQL bulk Avoid loading entities into memory
Deep pagination (page > 100) Cursor / keyset O(log n) vs O(n) offset cost

Kafka Consumer Reliability

Problem Solution
Duplicates on rebalance onPartitionsRevoked commitSync
Excessive rebalance impact StickyAssignor or incremental rebalance
Duplicate processing Idempotency key + unique DB constraint

Poison pill message Dead letter topic + max retry config

Step Config

Drain timeout spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase=20s

Mock Interview #84 — JetBrains | Modern Java 21+ Features

— JetBrains (Czech Republic) — Modern Java 21+

Role: Senior Java Engineer, IDE Platform Team

Focus: Virtual thread pinning, ScopedValue, record/sealed classes, pattern matching, text blocks, sequenced collections

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Andrei (Principal Engineer, JVM enthusiast)

👨‍💻 Andrei: Khanh — you know virtual threads from Java 21. What is virtual thread pinning and when does it occur?

👨 Khanh: Virtual threads are scheduled by the JVM onto a pool of platform (OS) threads called carrier threads. Normally, when a virtual thread performs a blocking operation — Thread.sleep(), Socket.read(), database I/O — the JVM unmounts the virtual thread from its carrier thread. The carrier thread is freed to run other virtual threads. This is the scalability win.

Pinning is when a virtual thread cannot be unmounted from its carrier thread during a blocking operation. The carrier thread is stuck — blocking both the virtual thread AND the carrier thread, defeating the purpose of virtual threads.

Two causes of pinning:

1. synchronized blocks and methods. The JVM cannot unmount a virtual thread while it holds a monitor lock (synchronized). If the synchronized block contains blocking I/O, the carrier thread is pinned for the duration.


synchronized (this) {
    // Virtual thread blocks here waiting for DB → carrier thread PINNED
    result = jdbcStatement.executeQuery();
}

2. Native method calls. Code calling into native libraries (JNI) cannot be unmounted.

Detection: JVM flag -Djdk.tracePinnedThreads=full logs a stack trace whenever a virtual thread is pinned. Also visible in JFR events.

Fix: replace synchronized with ReentrantLock:


private final ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();
lock.lock();
try {
    result = jdbcStatement.executeQuery(); // virtual thread can unmount here
} finally {
    lock.unlock();
}

ReentrantLock cooperates with the virtual thread scheduler — the virtual thread can be unmounted while waiting for the lock or during I/O inside the lock.

Practical impact: most connection pools (HikariCP pre-3.x) used synchronized internally. HikariCP 5.1+ was updated to use StampedLock to be virtual-thread compatible. Always check your library versions when migrating to virtual threads.

👨‍💻 Andrei: Good. What is ScopedValue in Java 21 and why is it better than ThreadLocal for virtual threads?

👨 Khanh: ThreadLocal stores a value per thread — each thread has its own copy. It works for platform threads but has two problems with virtual threads:

1. Inheritance cost. InheritableThreadLocal copies parent thread values to child threads. With millions of virtual threads, this copying overhead is significant.

2. Mutable state leaks. ThreadLocal values are mutable and accessible from anywhere in the call stack — making it a form of hidden global state. A child virtual thread can modify the parent's ThreadLocal value in unexpected ways.

ScopedValue (Java 21, preview in 20, finalized in 21): an immutable, scoped binding. A value is bound for the duration of a specific code scope — not for the lifetime of a thread:


static final ScopedValue<User> CURRENT_USER = ScopedValue.newInstance();

// Bind the value for this scope — immutable within the scope
ScopedValue.where(CURRENT_USER, user)
    .run(() -> {
        processRequest();  // can read CURRENT_USER.get() anywhere in the call tree
    });

// After the scope exits, the binding is gone — no cleanup needed

Advantages over ThreadLocal:

  • Immutable — no accidental mutation from nested code
  • Automatically scoped — no remove() needed, no thread pool pollution
  • Efficient inheritance — child virtual threads in StructuredTaskScope share the parent's scoped values without copying
  • Stack-confined — value only exists while the scope is running; semantics are clear

Use case: per-request context (user identity, trace ID, tenant ID) — classic ThreadLocal use cases but safer and more performant at virtual thread scale.

👨‍💻 Andrei: Modern Java language features. What are record classes and sealed classes — when would you use each?

👨 Khanh: Record classes (Java 16+):

Records are immutable data carriers. The compiler auto-generates constructor, equals(), hashCode(), toString(), and accessor methods: 


record Point(int x, int y) {}

// Equivalent to a class with:
// - final fields x, y
// - canonical constructor
// - equals/hashCode based on all fields
// - toString: "Point[x=1, y=2]"

When to use records: DTOs, value objects, API request/response bodies, return types from methods that carry multiple values. Any class whose purpose is just to hold data — not to encapsulate mutable state or behavior.

Sealed classes (Java 17+):

Sealed classes restrict which classes can extend or implement them. The permits clause lists the allowed subtypes — they must be in the same package or module:


sealed interface Shape permits Circle, Rectangle, Triangle {}

record Circle(double radius) implements Shape {}
record Rectangle(double width, double height) implements Shape {}
record Triangle(double base, double height) implements Shape {}

When to use sealed classes: when you want an exhaustive, closed set of subtypes — like an algebraic data type. Combined with pattern matching switch expressions, the compiler verifies you've handled all cases:


double area = switch (shape) {
    case Circle c    -> Math.PI * c.radius() * c.radius();
    case Rectangle r -> r.width() * r.height();
    case Triangle t  -> 0.5 * t.base() * t.height();
    // No default needed — compiler knows all cases are covered
};

If you add a new subtype to Shape, every switch that doesn't handle it becomes a compile error. This is exhaustiveness checking — safer than open inheritance.

Together: sealed classes + records + pattern matching is Java's answer to algebraic data types (like Kotlin sealed classes or Rust enums). Use them for modeling domain variants: PaymentResult (Success, Failure, Pending), ApiResponse (Ok, Error, Redirect).

👨‍💻 Andrei: Two quick ones. What problem do text blocks solve and what is SequencedCollection?

👨 Khanh: Text blocks (Java 15+): multi-line string literals without escape hell. Before:


String json = "{\n  \"name\": \"Khanh\",\n  \"role\": \"engineer\"\n}";

With text blocks:


String json = """
    {
      "name": "Khanh",
      "role": "engineer"
    }
    """;

The compiler strips the common leading whitespace (determined by the closing """). Indentation in the source becomes the indentation of the string — no manual \n or \". Invaluable for SQL strings, JSON templates, HTML in tests.

SequencedCollection (Java 21): a new interface in the collections hierarchy that adds consistent first/last element access and reversed-view methods to ordered collections:


SequencedCollection<String> list = new ArrayList<>(List.of("a","b","c"));
list.getFirst();  // "a"
list.getLast();   // "c"
list.addFirst("z"); // ["z","a","b","c"]
list.reversed();    // reversed view

// Also works on LinkedHashSet, LinkedHashMap.sequencedKeySet(), Deque, etc.

Before Java 21: getting the last element of a List was list.get(list.size()-1) — verbose. LinkedHashSet had no direct first/last access at all. SequencedCollection unifies these across List, Deque, and LinkedHashSet.

SCORECARD — Interview #84

Topic Score Key Insight
Virtual thread pinning 10/10 synchronized + native → pin → fix with ReentrantLock
ScopedValue vs ThreadLocal 10/10 Immutable, scoped, no remove(), efficient VT inheritance
Record classes 10/10 Immutable data carriers, auto-generated methods
Sealed classes + pattern matching 10/10 Exhaustiveness checking, algebraic data types
Text blocks + SequencedCollection 10/10 Practical Java 21 knowledge

Mock Interview #85 — Red Hat/Quarkus | Cloud Native Java

— Quarkus / Red Hat (US) — Cloud Native Java

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Quarkus Runtime Team

Focus: Spring AOT, GraalVM native image compilation, reactive Kafka, MapStruct, R2DBC

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Emmanuel (Principal Engineer)

👨‍💻 Emmanuel: Khanh — cloud native Java. What is Spring AOT and what does it do at build time?

👨 Khanh: Spring AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation was introduced in Spring 6 / Spring Boot 3. It shifts work that Spring traditionally does at runtime (application startup) to build time.

What Spring normally does at startup (runtime):

  • Scans classpath for @Component, @Service, @Configuration classes
  • Resolves bean definitions, builds the application context
  • Processes @Conditional annotations
  • Generates CGLIB proxies for @Transactional, @Cacheable, etc.
  • Performs reflection to inject dependencies

All of this requires reflection, class loading, and introspection — expensive at startup, and incompatible with GraalVM native image (which requires all reflection to be known at compile time).

What Spring AOT does at build time:

  • Runs the Spring application context in a "dry-run" mode during the Maven/Gradle build
  • Generates Java source code representing the fully resolved bean definitions — no reflection needed at runtime
  • Generates reflect-config.json and proxy-config.json for GraalVM native image
  • Pre-generates CGLIB proxy classes as source code

Result:

  • Much faster startup (seconds → milliseconds for native image)
  • Lower memory footprint (no reflection infrastructure loaded)
  • Enables GraalVM native image compilation without manual configuration

What you lose: fully dynamic behavior. If you register beans conditionally based on runtime config (@ConditionalOnProperty reading a value only known at runtime), AOT may not handle it perfectly. Spring AOT supports most common patterns but exotic runtime dynamism needs manual hints.

👨‍💻 Emmanuel: What are the build-time constraints of GraalVM native image compilation?

👨 Khanh: GraalVM native image uses static analysis to determine all code paths reachable from main() and compiles them to a native binary. Anything not reachable at analysis time is excluded.

The constraints:

1. Reflection must be declared. Any class/method/field accessed via Class.forName(), Method.invoke(), or constructor reflection must be listed in reflect-config.json. Spring AOT generates most of this automatically; custom reflection code needs manual configuration.

2. Dynamic class loading is impossible. ClassLoader.loadClass() with a class name known only at runtime doesn't work — native image can't include a class it never analyzed. Plugin architectures that load JARs at runtime won't work.

3. Serialization must be declared. Java serialization of classes must be declared in serialization-config.json.

4. JVM features not available. No JVMTI agents, no JIT, no JFR (partially available in newer GraalVM). No dynamic bytecode generation (CGLIB, Byte Buddy — Spring replaces these with generated source at AOT time).

5. Initialization order is locked. Code that runs in static initializers is executed at build time — the result is baked into the binary. This can cause issues if static initializers have side effects (opening connections, reading files).

Practical implication: libraries that use heavy reflection (some older ORMs, dynamic proxying) need @RegisterReflectionForBinding hints or RuntimeHintsRegistrar in Spring. Most of the Spring ecosystem now supports native image natively.

👨‍💻 Emmanuel: Explain MapStruct — what does it do, how does it work under the hood, and why is it better than manual mapping or reflection-based mappers like ModelMapper?

👨 Khanh: MapStruct is a compile-time code generator for object mapping — converting between DTOs and entities.

How you use it:


@Mapper(componentModel = "spring")
public interface OrderMapper {
    OrderDto toDto(Order order);
    Order toEntity(OrderDto dto);

    @Mapping(source = "user.fullName", target = "customerName")
    @Mapping(source = "createdAt", target = "orderDate", dateFormat = "yyyy-MM-dd")
    OrderSummaryDto toSummary(Order order);
}

MapStruct's annotation processor reads this interface at compile time and generates a concrete implementation class with explicit field assignments:


// Generated by MapStruct at compile time:
@Component
public class OrderMapperImpl implements OrderMapper {
    public OrderDto toDto(Order order) {
        OrderDto dto = new OrderDto();
        dto.setId(order.getId());
        dto.setStatus(order.getStatus());
        // ... explicit field-by-field assignment
        return dto;
    }
}

Why better than alternatives:

vs Manual mapping: same performance, but MapStruct eliminates boilerplate. When you add a field to Order, MapStruct can warn (or error) if the mapper doesn't handle the new field — no silent omissions.

vs ModelMapper / Dozer (reflection-based): reflection mappers are slow (reflective field access), error-prone (field name mismatches fail at runtime), and invisible to GraalVM native image. MapStruct generates plain Java — zero reflection, compile-time validation, native image compatible, debuggable (you can step through the generated code).

Annotation processor integration: MapStruct works with javac -processor. In Maven: mapstruct-processor in the annotationProcessorPaths. In Gradle: annotationProcessor "org.mapstruct:mapstruct-processor:...".

👨‍💻 Emmanuel: Reactive Kafka with Spring — what is the key difference in how you consume messages compared to the standard @KafkaListener?

👨 Khanh: Standard @KafkaListener runs in a thread-per-partition model — each partition is processed by a blocking thread from a thread pool. This works well for most cases but blocks a thread during I/O.

Reactive Kafka (ReactiveKafkaConsumerTemplate from reactor-kafka):


@Bean
ReactiveKafkaConsumerTemplate<String, OrderEvent> reactiveConsumer(
    ReactiveKafkaConsumerTemplate<String, OrderEvent> template) {
    return template;
}

// Consumer
template.receiveAutoAck()
    .flatMap(record ->
        reactiveOrderService.process(record.value())  // returns Mono
            .doOnSuccess(result ->
                log.info("Processed: {}", record.offset()))
    )
    .subscribe();

Key differences:

1. Non-blocking processing. flatMap allows concurrent processing of multiple records without blocking threads. With @KafkaListener, each record blocks a thread while processing — reactive processing continues scheduling work on event loops.

2. Backpressure. ReactiveKafkaConsumerTemplate integrates with Project Reactor's backpressure model. If downstream processing slows, the Kafka poll rate slows — no unbounded buffering.

3. Concurrency control. flatMap(n) sets max concurrent in-flight records. With @KafkaListener, concurrency is controlled by thread pool size — indirect.

4. Manual offset management. receive() (vs receiveAutoAck()) gives you ReceiverOffset — you call offset.acknowledge() only after processing succeeds. Fine-grained control over at-least-once delivery.

When to use reactive Kafka: when your processing chain is already reactive (WebFlux service calling reactive DB via R2DBC, returning Mono/Flux). Mixing reactive Kafka with blocking services loses most benefits — use @KafkaListener with virtual threads in that case.

SCORECARD — Interview #85

Topic Score Key Insight
Spring AOT 10/10 Build-time context resolution, GraalVM config generation
GraalVM native constraints 10/10 No dynamic classloading, reflection must be declared, static init at build time
MapStruct 10/10 Compile-time codegen, zero reflection, GraalVM compatible
Reactive Kafka 10/10 flatMap concurrency, backpressure, manual offset ACK

Mock Interview #86 — Zalando | Engineering Strategy & Architecture

— Zalando (Germany) — Engineering Strategy

Role: Senior Java Engineer / Tech Lead Track

Focus: Zero downtime migration, architectural fitness functions, tech debt quadrant, OOM killer/container memory, load balancing algorithms

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Lars (Engineering Director, Architecture Board)

👨‍💻 Lars: Khanh — strategy level. How do you approach a zero-downtime database schema migration? Walk me through a rename-column scenario.

👨 Khanh: Zero-downtime migration requires deploying schema changes in phases — never changing schema and application code atomically. The expand-and-contract pattern:

Scenario: rename orders.customer_name to orders.buyer_name.

Phase 1 — Expand (backward compatible add):

  • Add new column buyer_name to the table (nullable initially)
  • Deploy new application code that writes to BOTH customer_name AND buyer_name
  • Reads still use customer_name
  • Run a backfill: UPDATE orders SET buyer_name = customer_name WHERE buyer_name IS NULL
  • Verify backfill is complete

Phase 2 — Migrate reads:

  • Deploy code that reads from buyer_name (now fully populated)
  • Still writes to both columns
  • Monitor for any nulls in buyer_name — catch stragglers

Phase 3 — Contract (remove old):

  • Deploy code that writes to buyer_name ONLY (stops writing customer_name)
  • Verify no application reads customer_name anymore (search codebase + DB column usage stats)
  • Run: ALTER TABLE orders DROP COLUMN customer_name

Why this is safe: at every phase, the current live application code works correctly. No deployment requires a coordinated schema + code change. Each phase is independently rollback-safe.

Tools: Flyway and Liquibase handle the SQL migration scripts. The phasing is an application deployment concern — not just a DB concern.

👨‍💻 Lars: What is an architectural fitness function and how do you use one?

👨 Khanh: The term comes from Building Evolutionary Architectures (Ford, Parsons, Kua). An architectural fitness function is an automated check that verifies an architectural characteristic is maintained as the codebase evolves.

It's the architectural equivalent of a unit test — but instead of testing business behavior, it tests that the architecture stays healthy.

Examples:

1. Dependency direction enforcement:


// ArchUnit test — domain must not depend on infrastructure
@Test
void domainShouldNotDependOnInfrastructure() {
    JavaClasses classes = new ClassFileImporter().importPackages("com.example");
    noClasses()
        .that().resideInAPackage("..domain..")
        .should().dependOnClassesThat()
        .resideInAPackage("..infrastructure..")
        .check(classes);
}

If a developer accidentally adds a Spring JPA import to a domain class, this test fails — immediately in CI.

2. Cycle detection: ArchUnit can detect circular dependencies between packages — which slowly make code unrefactorable.

3. Layer enforcement: controllers can only call services, services can only call repositories — no layer skipping.

4. Performance SLA as fitness function: a JMeter test that asserts p99 checkout latency stays below 500ms — run on every deploy to staging. If a PR degrades latency, it's caught before production.

5. Test coverage threshold: mutation score must stay above 70% (enforced by PITest in CI).

Why important: architecture degrades by a thousand small violations, each of which seems harmless individually. Fitness functions make architectural constraints machine-enforceable instead of relying on code review discipline.

👨‍💻 Lars: Explain the tech debt quadrant and how you use it to prioritize refactoring.

👨 Khanh: The tech debt quadrant (Martin Fowler) classifies debt on two axes:

Axis 1 — Reckless vs Prudent:

  • Reckless: "We don't have time for design" — shortcuts taken knowingly or unknowingly, often causing future pain
  • Prudent: deliberate, accepted trade-offs — "we know we're doing this the quick way and we'll pay the cost later"

Axis 2 — Inadvertent vs Deliberate:

  • Inadvertent: "Now we know we should have used CQRS" — discovered in hindsight, not a bad decision at the time
  • Deliberate: consciously chosen to ship faster

The four quadrants:

Reckless Prudent
Deliberate "No time for design" "Ship now, refactor later"
Inadvertent "What is layering?" "Now we know better"

How I use it in practice:

  • Prudent-Deliberate debt: document it. Create a tech debt ticket with the original decision, the known cost, and a target resolution date. This is managed debt — acceptable.
  • Prudent-Inadvertent debt: schedule it. The team learned something — prioritize a refactoring sprint before the codebase grows more entangled.
  • Reckless-Deliberate debt: flag it in code review. "This was done the quick way because of deadline — please don't pattern-match from this."
  • Reckless-Inadvertent debt: the most dangerous. Often discovered during an incident. Treat as a P1 remediation item.

When prioritizing: debt that blocks feature delivery or causes incidents first. Debt that just "feels bad" but doesn't slow the team — lower priority, do during slack time.

👨‍💻 Lars: JVM in containers. What is the OOM killer and how do you configure JVM heap to avoid it in Kubernetes?

👨 Khanh: The Linux OOM (Out-of-Memory) killer is invoked by the kernel when the system runs out of memory. It selects a process to kill — typically the one with the highest oom_score (large memory usage, low priority). In Kubernetes, when a pod exceeds its memory limit (resources.limits.memory), the container's cgroup enforces the limit and the OOM killer terminates the Java process — the pod restarts with OOMKilled status.

Why Java in containers needs special attention:

Before Java 11, the JVM didn't respect cgroup memory limits. It read total host memory and set heap size as a fraction of that — leading to OOM kills when the heap exceeded the container limit.

Java 11+ container awareness: JVM reads cgroup memory limits and adjusts automatically. But you still need to configure correctly:


resources:
  requests:
    memory: "512Mi"
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"  # set request = limit for guaranteed QoS


# JVM flags for container:
-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0   # heap = 75% of container limit
# Leaves 25% for: metaspace, direct buffers, thread stacks, GC overhead

Why not -Xmx512m directly? If you hardcode -Xmx and change the K8s memory limit, you must also update the JVM flag. MaxRAMPercentage scales automatically.

The non-heap memory trap: developers set -Xmx to the container limit, forgetting that Java also uses memory for:

  • Metaspace (~100-200MB for Spring Boot)
  • Thread stacks (~1MB per thread × 200 threads = 200MB)
  • Direct/off-heap buffers (Netty, NIO)
  • GC overhead

A Spring Boot app with -Xmx512m in a 512Mi container will be OOMKilled because total process memory = heap + metaspace + stacks + native = 700MB+.

Rule of thumb for Spring Boot: container_limit = heap + 200MB overhead. Container 768Mi → MaxRAMPercentage=70 → heap ~537Mi. Monitor actual process RSS with kubectl top pods to validate.

👨‍💻 Lars: Last one. Explain consistent hashing and rendezvous hashing — when would you use each?

👨 Khanh: Both solve the same problem: distributing keys across a variable set of nodes such that adding/removing a node minimizes key remapping.

Consistent hashing:

Nodes and keys are mapped to positions on a virtual ring (hash space 0 to 2³²). A key is assigned to the first node clockwise on the ring from its hash position.

  • Adding a node: only keys between the new node and its predecessor move. On average: K/n keys move (K=total keys, n=nodes). Not all keys.
  • Removing a node: only that node's keys move to the next node.
  • Virtual nodes (vnodes): each physical node gets multiple positions on the ring for uniform distribution. Used by Cassandra, DynamoDB.

Rendezvous hashing (highest random weight):

For a key, compute hash(key, node) for every candidate node. Assign the key to the node with the highest hash value.


def assign(key, nodes):
    return max(nodes, key=lambda node: hash(f"{key}:{node}"))
  • No ring structure needed
  • Minimal disruption on node addition/removal — same O(K/n) property
  • Simpler to implement for small node sets

When to use each:

Consistent Hashing Rendezvous Hashing
Node count Large (100s) Small to medium (< 50)
Implementation More complex (ring + vnodes) Simpler
Lookup time O(log n) with sorted ring O(n) — scan all nodes
Used by Cassandra, DynamoDB, Memcached CDNs, small load balancers

For a distributed cache with 5-10 nodes (like a microservice's Redis cluster behind a client), rendezvous hashing is simpler and performs just as well. For a large distributed storage system with hundreds of nodes, consistent hashing with vnodes is standard.

SCORECARD — Interview #86

Topic Score Key Insight
Zero downtime migration 10/10 Expand-contract 3 phases, backfill, drop last
Architectural fitness function 10/10 ArchUnit, performance SLA as test, PITest threshold
Tech debt quadrant 10/10 4 quadrants, prioritization strategy per type
OOM killer + JVM in containers 10/10 MaxRAMPercentage, non-heap trap, RSS monitoring
Consistent vs rendezvous hashing 10/10 Ring vs HRW, O(n) vs O(log n), use case split

QUICK REFERENCE — Modern Java, Cloud Native, Engineering Strategy

Java 21 Key Features

Feature Problem Solved Key Detail
Virtual thread pinning synchronized blocks carrier thread Replace with ReentrantLock
ScopedValue ThreadLocal mutable/copy overhead Immutable, auto-scoped, no remove()
Record classes Boilerplate data classes Immutable, auto equals/hashCode/toString
Sealed classes Open inheritance, non-exhaustive switch Compile-time exhaustiveness checking
Pattern matching switch verbose instanceof + cast Type patterns, exhaustive, no default needed
Text blocks String escape hell Common whitespace stripped, readable
SequencedCollection No consistent first/last API getFirst/getLast/addFirst/reversed()

GraalVM Native Image Constraints

Constraint Workaround
Reflection must be declared Spring AOT generates config; use @RegisterReflectionForBinding
No dynamic class loading Avoid runtime classpath scanning
Static initializers run at build time Delay I/O to @PostConstruct
No CGLIB Spring AOT pre-generates proxy source code

JVM Container Memory Formula


container_limit = heap + metaspace (150MB) + threads (200MB) + direct buffers
Use: -XX:MaxRAMPercentage=70-75
Monitor: kubectl top pods, jcmd <pid> VM.native_memory
OOMKilled reason: total RSS > limits.memory

Load Balancing Algorithms

Algorithm Best for Key property
Round Robin Homogeneous backends Simple, even distribution
Least Connections Variable request cost Routes to least busy
Consistent Hashing Stateful/cached backends Minimal remapping on node change
Rendezvous (HRW) Small node sets Simple, same disruption as consistent
Random High throughput, stateless No state needed, surprisingly effective
