Mock Interview #87 — Revolut | Resilience4j & HTTP Internals

— Revolut (UK/Lithuania) — Resilience4j + HTTP Internals

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Payments Reliability

Focus: Bulkhead, rate limiter, time limiter, half-open state, HTTP/2 multiplexing, TLS overhead, HTTP connection pooling

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Dmitri (Principal Engineer)

👨‍💻 Dmitri: Khanh — resilience patterns. Explain the bulkhead pattern. What problem does it solve that a circuit breaker alone doesn't?

👨 Khanh: The bulkhead pattern isolates failures by limiting the resources any one dependency can consume. The name comes from ship design — watertight compartments (bulkheads) prevent one flooded compartment from sinking the whole ship.

The problem circuit breaker alone doesn't solve: a circuit breaker opens after failures accumulate. But before it opens, slow calls to a degraded dependency still consume threads/connections. If all 200 threads in your thread pool are waiting on a slow payment processor, no threads remain to serve unrelated endpoints — the entire service becomes unresponsive even though the payment processor failure is isolated.

Bulkhead in Resilience4j — two types:

1 — Thread Pool Bulkhead: each dependency gets its own bounded thread pool. Slow calls to PaymentService only consume PaymentService threads — FraudService threads are unaffected. 


BulkheadConfig config = BulkheadConfig.custom()
    .maxConcurrentCalls(25)        // max simultaneous calls
    .maxWaitDuration(Duration.ofMillis(100))  // queue wait before reject
    .build();
Bulkhead bulkhead = Bulkhead.of("paymentService", config);

// Decorate the call
Supplier<PaymentResult> decorated = Bulkhead.decorateSupplier(bulkhead,
    () -> paymentClient.charge(request));

If 25 concurrent calls are in flight and a 26th arrives, it waits up to 100ms then gets BulkheadFullException — fast fail rather than joining a growing queue.

2 — Semaphore Bulkhead: limits concurrent calls without a separate thread pool. Lighter weight but doesn't isolate thread consumption from the caller's pool.

Combined with circuit breaker: bulkhead limits concurrency (prevents thread exhaustion), circuit breaker limits duration (stops calling a broken dependency). Use both together.

👨‍💻 Dmitri: Resilience4j rate limiter and time limiter — explain each and their internal mechanisms.

👨 Khanh: Rate Limiter controls how many calls per time period are allowed — protecting a downstream API from being overwhelmed.

Resilience4j uses an atomic reference to a token bucket with a refresh period:


RateLimiterConfig config = RateLimiterConfig.custom()
    .limitRefreshPeriod(Duration.ofSeconds(1))
    .limitForPeriod(100)           // 100 calls per second
    .timeoutDuration(Duration.ofMillis(25))  // wait up to 25ms for a permit
    .build();

Every limitRefreshPeriod, the available permits reset to limitForPeriod. A call acquires a permit (CAS operation) or waits up to timeoutDuration then throws RequestNotPermitted. This is token bucket semantics — burst allowed up to limitForPeriod, then throttled.

Key difference from circuit breaker: rate limiter is proactive (limit calls per unit time regardless of error rate), circuit breaker is reactive (open after failure threshold).

Time Limiter sets a maximum duration for a call — wraps a CompletableFuture or Future with a timeout: 


TimeLimiterConfig tlConfig = TimeLimiterConfig.custom()
    .timeoutDuration(Duration.ofSeconds(2))
    .cancelRunningFuture(true)     // cancel the future on timeout
    .build();
TimeLimiter timeLimiter = TimeLimiter.of("paymentService", tlConfig);

Future<PaymentResult> future = executor.submit(() -> paymentClient.charge(request));
PaymentResult result = timeLimiter.executeFutureSupplier(() -> future);
// Throws TimeoutException if > 2 seconds

cancelRunningFuture(true) — calls future.cancel(true) on timeout, interrupting the thread. Without this, the future continues running even after TimeoutException is thrown — resource leak.

The half-open state (circuit breaker): after waitDurationInOpenState, the circuit breaker moves from OPEN to HALF_OPEN. It allows a configured number of test calls (permittedNumberOfCallsInHalfOpenState). If those calls succeed → CLOSED. If they fail → back to OPEN. This prevents the circuit from slamming shut again immediately on a flapping dependency.

👨‍💻 Dmitri: HTTP internals. What does HTTP/2 multiplexing solve, and what new problems does it introduce?

👨 Khanh: HTTP/1.1 problem — Head-of-line (HOL) blocking at the application layer:

HTTP/1.1 processes requests serially on one connection. If request A is slow, requests B and C wait — even though they are independent. Browsers work around this by opening 6 parallel TCP connections per domain — wasteful and limited.

HTTP/2 multiplexing: multiple request/response pairs share one TCP connection as independent streams, each with a stream ID. The server interleaves responses — stream 1 data, stream 3 data, stream 1 data — without waiting for one stream to complete. No HOL blocking at the application layer.

Other HTTP/2 improvements:

  • Header compression (HPACK): headers are compressed using a shared dynamic table — critical for REST APIs where headers like Authorization and Content-Type repeat on every request
  • Server push: server can proactively send resources the client hasn't requested yet
  • Binary framing: binary protocol vs text — more efficient parsing

New problems HTTP/2 introduces:

1. TCP-level HOL blocking. HTTP/2 runs over TCP. If a TCP packet is lost, TCP waits for retransmission before delivering any subsequent packets — all HTTP/2 streams stall. HTTP/2 moved HOL blocking from the application layer to the TCP layer.

2. Single connection changes failure modes. One TCP connection failure drops all in-flight requests simultaneously — HTTP/1.1 would only lose one.

3. Server push complexity. Push is rarely implemented correctly — clients may already have the resource cached, wasting bandwidth.

HTTP/3 / QUIC solves TCP HOL blocking by running over UDP with independent stream reassembly — a lost packet only blocks the stream it belongs to, not all streams.

👨‍💻 Dmitri: HTTP connection pooling — what is keep-alive, TLS handshake overhead, and how do you tune an OkHttp/Apache client pool for a high-throughput Java service?

👨 Khanh: Keep-alive (persistent connections): HTTP/1.1 defaults to keeping TCP connections open after a response — reusing them for subsequent requests. Without keep-alive, every request requires: TCP 3-way handshake (~1 RTT) + TLS handshake (~1-2 RTT) before any HTTP bytes transfer. At 100ms RTT, that's 200-300ms of overhead before the actual request.

TLS handshake overhead:

  • TLS 1.2: 2 RTTs (ClientHello → ServerHello+Cert → ClientKeyExchange → Finished)
  • TLS 1.3: 1 RTT (0-RTT resumption possible for repeat connections)
  • CPU cost: RSA/ECDH key exchange is expensive — reusing connections avoids repeated handshakes

Connection pool tuning (OkHttp example):


OkHttpClient client = new OkHttpClient.Builder()
    .connectionPool(new ConnectionPool(
        50,               // maxIdleConnections — idle connections to keep alive
        5, TimeUnit.MINUTES  // keepAliveDuration — how long to keep idle conn
    ))
    .connectTimeout(2, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
    .readTimeout(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS)
    .build();

Tuning principles:

  • maxIdleConnections = expected peak concurrent requests to that host. Too low → connections closed and recreated (TLS overhead). Too high → wasted file descriptors and server-side connection slots.
  • keepAliveDuration must be less than the server's connection idle timeout (e.g., Nginx default is 75s). If client keeps a connection that the server already closed → SocketException: Connection reset on the next request. Set client keepalive to 70% of server timeout.
  • Monitor connection pool exhausted events — sign that pool is undersized.
  • HTTP/2 clients: one connection per host with multiplexing — pool of 1 is often correct.

SCORECARD — Interview #87

Topic Score Key Insight
Bulkhead pattern 10/10 Thread exhaustion problem CB doesn't solve, semaphore vs thread pool
Rate limiter + time limiter 10/10 Token bucket internals, cancelRunningFuture leak risk
Half-open state 10/10 Test calls, success→CLOSED, fail→OPEN
HTTP/2 multiplexing 10/10 App-layer HOL fixed, TCP-layer HOL introduced
HTTP connection pooling 10/10 TLS overhead, keepalive vs server timeout, pool sizing

Mock Interview #88 — Shopify | Database Internals & Sharding

— Shopify (Canada) — Database Internals

Role: Senior Backend Engineer, Data Platform

Focus: Covering index, index-only scan, EXPLAIN ANALYZE, VACUUM ANALYZE, sharding strategy, shard key selection

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Aisha (Staff Engineer, database specialist)

👩‍💻 Aisha: Khanh — database deep dive. What is a covering index and an index-only scan? When does PostgreSQL use one?

👨 Khanh: A covering index is an index that contains all the columns needed to satisfy a query — both the filter columns (WHERE) AND the projection columns (SELECT). This allows PostgreSQL to answer the query entirely from the index without touching the heap (table data).

An index-only scan is what happens when PostgreSQL can use a covering index: it reads the index B-tree pages, finds the matching rows, and returns the projected columns directly from the index — zero heap access.

Example:


-- Query
SELECT user_id, status FROM orders WHERE created_at > '2024-01-01';

-- Without covering index: index scan on created_at, then heap fetch for user_id + status
-- With covering index:
CREATE INDEX idx_orders_covering ON orders (created_at) INCLUDE (user_id, status);
-- Now: index-only scan — created_at in key, user_id + status in INCLUDE columns

The INCLUDE clause (PostgreSQL 11+): adds columns to the leaf pages of the index without including them in the B-tree key. This avoids bloating the index structure while still covering the query.

The visibility map caveat: PostgreSQL index-only scans still need to check the visibility map (a bitmap tracking which heap pages are all-visible to all transactions) to determine if a row is visible. If heap pages aren't all-visible (recent inserts/updates not yet VACUUMED), PostgreSQL falls back to heap fetches. This is why running VACUUM regularly is critical for index-only scan effectiveness.

👩‍💻 Aisha: Walk me through reading a PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE output. What are the key numbers to look for?

👨 Khanh: EXPLAIN ANALYZE runs the query and shows actual vs estimated metrics:


EXPLAIN ANALYZE SELECT * FROM orders WHERE user_id = 123 AND status = 'PENDING';

Output (simplified):


Index Scan using idx_orders_user_status on orders  (cost=0.43..8.45 rows=3 width=120)
                                                    (actual time=0.121..0.143 rows=2 loops=1)
  Index Cond: (user_id = 123)
  Filter: (status = 'PENDING')
  Rows Removed by Filter: 5
Planning Time: 0.3 ms
Execution Time: 0.2 ms

Key numbers:

1. cost=X..Y: planner's estimated cost. X = startup cost (before first row), Y = total cost. These are in arbitrary units — useful for comparing plans, not absolute time.

2. rows=N (estimated) vs actual rows=N: the ratio reveals stale statistics. If estimated=3 but actual=50,000 — the planner had bad statistics and may have chosen a wrong plan (e.g., Nested Loop when Hash Join would be faster).

3. Rows Removed by Filter: 5: 5 rows passed the index condition but were filtered out by a non-index column condition. This means the index is partially selective — consider a composite index including status.

4. loops=N: how many times this node executed. In nested loops, inner nodes execute once per outer row. actual time=X..Y is per loop — multiply by loops for total.

5. Buffers: shared hit=N read=M: hit = pages from shared buffer cache (fast), read = pages from disk (slow). High read with low hit → cold cache or cache too small.

6. Execution Time: wall clock time. If this is high but individual node times are low, look for many loops or sequential scans on large tables.

Red flags: Sequential Scan on a large table (missing index), estimated rows << actual rows (stale statistics → run ANALYZE), many Nested Loop iterations (join order problem).

👩‍💻 Aisha: What does VACUUM ANALYZE do and when should you run it?

👨 Khanh: VACUUM: PostgreSQL's MVCC keeps old row versions for concurrent readers. When rows are updated or deleted, old versions (dead tuples) accumulate in table pages. VACUUM removes dead tuples, freeing space for reuse, and updates the visibility map (enabling index-only scans).

VACUUM does NOT shrink the table file on disk (unless VACUUM FULL, which locks the table and rewrites it). It reclaims space for PostgreSQL's internal reuse.

ANALYZE: collects statistics about table data distribution — number of distinct values, most common values, histogram of distribution — and stores them in pg_statistic. The query planner uses these statistics to estimate row counts and choose optimal plans.

VACUUM ANALYZE: does both in one pass. PostgreSQL has autovacuum which runs automatically, but it can lag behind in high-write tables.

When to run manually:

1. After a bulk load/delete: autovacuum triggers at thresholds (default: 20% of table changed). For a table with 10M rows, you'd insert 2M before autovacuum triggers. Run ANALYZE orders immediately after a large batch insert.

2. Before a critical query: if you just refreshed a reporting table, run ANALYZE before the query planner generates the execution plan — otherwise it uses stale statistics.

3. After index creation: CREATE INDEX runs an implicit analyze, but if data has changed significantly since, re-analyze.

4. Investigation: if EXPLAIN shows wildly wrong row estimates, run ANALYZE tablename then re-check the plan.

Monitor with:


SELECT relname, n_dead_tup, last_vacuum, last_autovacuum, last_analyze
FROM pg_stat_user_tables ORDER BY n_dead_tup DESC;

👩‍💻 Aisha: Sharding — how do you choose a shard key, and what are the trade-offs of different strategies?

👨 Khanh: Sharding horizontally partitions a table across multiple database instances. The shard key determines which shard a row lives on. A bad shard key is very hard to fix after the fact.

Properties of a good shard key:

1. High cardinality: enough distinct values to distribute evenly across shards. status (3 values) is terrible. user_id (millions) is good.

2. Even distribution: avoids hotspots. created_at seems high-cardinality but all new writes go to the current time partition — creating a write hotspot on one shard.

3. Query locality: queries that filter on the shard key go to one shard. Queries that don't filter on the shard key must scatter across all shards (scatter-gather) — expensive.

Common strategies:

Hash sharding: shard = hash(shard_key) % num_shards. Even distribution, no hotspots. Problem: adding shards requires rehashing most keys — expensive migration.

Range sharding: shard 1 = user_id 1-1M, shard 2 = 1M-2M. Enables range scans (all users in a range hit one shard). Problem: sequential IDs create write hotspots (all new users → shard N).

Directory/lookup sharding: a separate mapping table records which shard each key lives on. Most flexible — you can migrate specific keys. Problem: the mapping table becomes a bottleneck and single point of failure.

Cross-shard queries — the hardest problem: queries that join or aggregate across shards (e.g., "total revenue across all users") must either:

  • Scatter-gather: query all shards, merge in application layer
  • Pre-aggregate: maintain a summary table updated by events
  • Accept limitations: some queries simply aren't supported efficiently

Shopify's approach: shard by shop_id. Every table has shop_id. All queries for a shop hit one shard — strong query locality. Cross-shop analytics is done in a separate data warehouse (BigQuery/Snowflake), not the OLTP shards.

SCORECARD — Interview #88

Topic Score Key Insight
Covering index + index-only scan 10/10 INCLUDE clause, visibility map caveat
EXPLAIN ANALYZE reading 10/10 6 key metrics, red flags
VACUUM ANALYZE 10/10 Dead tuples, statistics, when to run manually
Sharding strategy 10/10 Hash vs range vs directory, cross-shard problem

Mock Interview #89 — Airbnb | AWS Services & Storage

— Airbnb (US) — AWS Services + Storage Internals

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Infrastructure Platform

Focus: S3 multipart upload, presigned URLs, S3 consistency, SNS fanout, EventBridge, write amplification SSD, IOPS

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Priya (Staff Engineer)

👩‍💻 Priya: Khanh — AWS deep dive. When and how do you use S3 multipart upload? Walk me through the API.

👨 Khanh: S3 multipart upload is required for objects > 5GB (hard limit for single PUT) and recommended for objects > 100MB. Benefits: parallel upload of parts, resilience (failed parts retry individually without restarting), resume interrupted uploads.

The API — 3 phases:

Phase 1 — Initiate:


CreateMultipartUploadRequest initRequest = CreateMultipartUploadRequest.builder()
    .bucket("my-bucket").key("large-file.zip").build();
String uploadId = s3.createMultipartUpload(initRequest).uploadId();
// uploadId ties all parts together

Phase 2 — Upload parts (parallel):


List<CompletedPart> completedParts = new CopyOnWriteArrayList<>();
ExecutorService pool = Executors.newFixedThreadPool(5);

for (int partNumber = 1; partNumber <= totalParts; partNumber++) {
    final int part = partNumber;
    pool.submit(() -> {
        byte[] data = readChunk(part); // 5-100MB per part
        UploadPartRequest req = UploadPartRequest.builder()
            .bucket("my-bucket").key("large-file.zip")
            .uploadId(uploadId).partNumber(part)
            .contentLength((long) data.length).build();
        String etag = s3.uploadPart(req, RequestBody.fromBytes(data)).eTag();
        completedParts.add(CompletedPart.builder().partNumber(part).eTag(etag).build());
    });
}
pool.shutdown(); pool.awaitTermination(1, TimeUnit.HOURS);

Phase 3 — Complete:


completedParts.sort(Comparator.comparingInt(CompletedPart::partNumber));
s3.completeMultipartUpload(CompleteMultipartUploadRequest.builder()
    .bucket("my-bucket").key("large-file.zip").uploadId(uploadId)
    .multipartUpload(CompletedMultipartUpload.builder().parts(completedParts).build())
    .build());

Abort on failure: always call abortMultipartUpload on failure — incomplete uploads incur storage charges. Use S3 lifecycle rules to auto-abort incomplete multipart uploads after N days as a safety net.

👩‍💻 Priya: What is a presigned URL and what are its security considerations?

👨 Khanh: A presigned URL grants temporary, unauthenticated access to a specific S3 object — signed with the IAM credentials of the generating entity (user or role).


S3Presigner presigner = S3Presigner.create();
PresignedGetObjectRequest presigned = presigner.presignGetObject(r -> r
    .signatureDuration(Duration.ofMinutes(15))  // expiry
    .getObjectRequest(GetObjectRequest.builder()
        .bucket("my-bucket").key("report-2024.pdf").build()));
URL url = presigned.url();
// Share this URL — anyone with it can download the file for 15 minutes

Security considerations:

1. Expiry duration: set to the minimum needed. 15 minutes for a download link, 1 hour for an upload link from a mobile app. Long-lived presigned URLs are effectively public access.

2. HTTPS only: presigned URLs work over HTTPS by default. Enforce HTTPS via S3 bucket policy: "Condition": {"Bool": {"aws:SecureTransport": "false"}} → deny.

3. Credential source matters: if signed with a long-lived IAM user key, the URL is valid until expiry even if the IAM user is deleted. If signed with an IAM role (STS assumed role), the URL is also invalidated when the STS token expires — earlier than the presigned URL expiry. Use role-based credentials.

4. URL sharing risk: anyone with the URL has access. Log all presigned URL generation with CloudTrail. Don't embed presigned URLs in client-side JavaScript that gets committed to git.

5. Upload presigned URLs: for PUT operations — restrict with conditions (Content-Type, max size) using presigned POST policies rather than presigned PUT.

👩‍💻 Priya: S3 consistency model — what changed in 2020 and why does it matter?

👨 Khanh: Before December 2020, S3 offered eventual consistency for:

  • Read-after-write for overwritten objects (you write a new version, might read old version briefly)
  • List operations after PUT/DELETE (newly added objects might not appear in LIST)

This caused real bugs: applications would PUT an object, then immediately LIST to check it existed, and not find it. Or read the old version of an overwritten object and process stale data.

Since December 2020: S3 offers strong read-after-write consistency for all operations — PUT, GET, LIST, DELETE. After a successful PUT, any subsequent GET returns the new object. After a DELETE, LIST doesn't include the deleted object.

Why it matters for Java applications:

Before 2020: you needed workarounds — wait delays, DynamoDB as a consistency layer (Amazon's own recommendation), or accept stale reads. After 2020: all of these workarounds are unnecessary for S3 standard operations.

One caveat: S3 bucket listing pagination can still show "eventual" behavior across very large buckets with rapid concurrent modifications due to internal partitioning — not a strong-consistency violation in practice, but high-throughput list operations during concurrent writes may see inconsistencies. For event-driven architectures, use S3 event notifications (SQS/SNS) to react to new objects rather than polling with LIST.

👩‍💻 Priya: SNS fanout pattern and EventBridge — when do you choose each?

👨 Khanh: SNS fanout: one message published to an SNS topic is delivered to all subscribed endpoints simultaneously — multiple SQS queues, Lambda functions, HTTP endpoints. Classic pub-sub.


S3 event → SNS topic → SQS queue A (inventory service)
                      → SQS queue B (analytics service)
                      → Lambda (thumbnail generator)

All three receive the same event in parallel. Each processes independently — decoupled, resilient. If analytics SQS queue is slow, it doesn't affect inventory.

When to use SNS fanout:

  • Simple fan-out: one event type → multiple consumers
  • Low filtering needs (SNS supports basic attribute-based filtering but it's limited)
  • Lambda + SQS triggers directly

EventBridge: a fully managed event bus with rich content-based routing, schema registry, and event replay. Unlike SNS (push-based delivery), EventBridge has a rules engine:


{
  "source": ["com.myapp.orders"],
  "detail-type": ["OrderPlaced"],
  "detail": {
    "amount": [{"numeric": [">", 1000]}],
    "region": ["APAC"]
  }
}

This rule delivers only high-value APAC orders to a specific target — content-based filtering impossible with SNS.

EventBridge advantages over SNS:

  • Content-based routing: filter on any field in the event body
  • Schema registry: discover and document event schemas
  • Event replay: replay events from an archive — useful for debugging or onboarding new consumers
  • SaaS integrations: Shopify, Zendesk, Datadog publish events directly to EventBridge
  • Cross-account/cross-region: built-in support

When to use EventBridge:

  • Complex routing logic based on event content
  • Domain events in an event-driven microservices architecture
  • Integration with SaaS event sources
  • Need event replay capability

Rule of thumb: SNS for simple fan-out to 2-5 consumers with no content filtering. EventBridge for complex event-driven architectures where routing rules matter.

👩‍💻 Priya: Storage internals — what is write amplification on SSDs, IOPS, and how does it affect database performance?

👨 Khanh: Write amplification on SSDs:

SSDs can only erase data at the level of a full erase block (typically 128KB–4MB), but writes happen at page level (4–16KB). When you write a small update to a page, the SSD must: read the entire erase block, modify the page in memory, erase the entire block, write the entire block back. A 4KB write might cause 4MB of actual NAND flash writes — 1000× write amplification.

This degrades over time as the SSD fills — fewer free blocks available for write-ahead buffering, more erase-before-write cycles. SSDs slow down significantly when near capacity (~80% full). Always provision SSDs with 20% over-provisioning.

Implication for databases (LSM vs B-tree again):

  • LSM-tree's sequential writes align well with SSD erase blocks — sequential writes reduce SSD-level write amplification
  • B-tree's random writes cause more SSD wear and higher effective write amplification

IOPS (Input/Output Operations Per Second):

SSDs are rated in IOPS for random read and write. AWS gp3 EBS: 3,000 IOPS baseline (free), up to 16,000 with provisioning. io2 Block Express: up to 256,000 IOPS.

Database IOPS math:

PostgreSQL with 8KB pages: 10,000 random write IOPS = 80MB/s write throughput. If your WAL write rate exceeds this → write latency spikes → query latency spikes.

Throughput vs latency trade-off:

  • High IOPS = low latency random I/O (OLTP databases — many small random reads/writes)
  • High throughput = large sequential I/O (analytics, backup, bulk load)

A single io2 volume can deliver high IOPS with low latency for random access. For sequential scan throughput, striping multiple volumes (RAID 0) or using gp3 with higher throughput configuration is more cost-effective.

Monitoring: iostat -x 1 on Linux — %util near 100% = I/O bottleneck. await (average I/O wait time) > 1ms for SSD = saturation. On AWS: CloudWatch VolumeReadOps + VolumeWriteOps per second vs provisioned IOPS.

SCORECARD — Interview #89

Topic Score Key Insight
S3 multipart upload 10/10 3-phase API, abort on failure, lifecycle rule
Presigned URL security 10/10 Expiry, role vs user creds, HTTPS enforcement
S3 consistency model 10/10 2020 change, strong read-after-write, LIST caveat
SNS fanout vs EventBridge 10/10 Content-based routing, schema registry, replay
Write amplification + IOPS 10/10 SSD erase block physics, IOPS math, monitoring

QUICK REFERENCE — Resilience4j, Database, AWS

Resilience4j Pattern Summary

Pattern Protects against Key config
Circuit Breaker Cascade failure from broken dependency failureRateThreshold, waitDurationInOpenState
Bulkhead Thread exhaustion from slow dependency maxConcurrentCalls, maxWaitDuration
Rate Limiter Overloading downstream API limitForPeriod, limitRefreshPeriod
Time Limiter Hanging calls blocking threads timeoutDuration, cancelRunningFuture=true
Retry Transient failures maxAttempts, exponentialBackoff, jitter

PostgreSQL EXPLAIN ANALYZE Key Metrics

Metric What it means Red flag
estimated rows vs actual rows Planner accuracy Ratio > 10× → run ANALYZE
Rows Removed by Filter Index selectivity High → add column to index
Buffers: read (vs hit) Disk vs cache access High read → cold cache or missing index
loops=N Node executed N times High loops on nested join → join order issue
Sequential Scan on large table Full table read Always check for missing index

SNS vs EventBridge

Feature SNS EventBridge
Routing Topic-level Content-based rules
Filtering Attribute-based (limited) Any JSON field
Schema registry No Yes
Event replay No Yes (archive)
SaaS sources No Yes
Latency Lower Slightly higher
Use case Simple fan-out Complex event-driven architecture

Mock Interview #90 — Google | Java Streams & Functional Deep Dive

Mock Interview #90 — Google (US) — Java Streams & Functional Deep Dive

Role: Senior Software Engineer

Focus: Stream internals, Spliterator, lazy evaluation, Collectors, Optional, method references, Predicate compose

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Mei (Staff Engineer)

👩‍💻 Mei: How does the Stream pipeline execute internally? What is lazy evaluation?

👨 Khanh: A Stream pipeline has three parts: a source, zero or more intermediate operations, and a terminal operation. Intermediate operations (filter, map, sorted) are lazy — they don't execute until a terminal operation (collect, forEach, count) is called.

Internally, each intermediate operation wraps the previous stage in a ReferencePipeline.StatelessOp or StatefulOp. Nothing runs until the terminal operation calls spliterator() on the source, then drives consumption by pulling elements through each pipeline stage.

This means:


list.stream()
    .filter(x -> { System.out.println("filter: " + x); return x > 3; })
    .map(x -> { System.out.println("map: " + x); return x * 2; })
    .findFirst();
// Only processes elements until findFirst() is satisfied — short-circuits

Elements flow one-by-one through the entire pipeline. findFirst() short-circuits — stops as soon as one match is found. Without laziness, filter would process all elements first, then map, costing O(2n) operations instead of O(k) where k is the position of the first match.

Stateless vs stateful operations: filter/map are stateless — each element processed independently. sorted/distinct/limit are stateful — must buffer or track all elements before passing downstream. Stateful ops prevent full laziness and short-circuit optimization.

👩‍💻 Mei: What is a Spliterator? When would you implement a custom one?

👨 Khanh: A Spliterator (Splittable Iterator) is the traversal and partitioning mechanism underlying streams. It has four key methods:

  • tryAdvance(Consumer) — process one element, return false if none left
  • forEachRemaining(Consumer) — process all remaining elements
  • trySplit() — split into two Spliterators for parallel processing
  • estimateSize() — estimate remaining elements (for parallelism work-stealing)

Characteristics flags: ORDERED, SIZED, DISTINCT, SORTED, IMMUTABLE, CONCURRENT, SUBSIZED. These tell the stream framework what optimizations are safe.

Custom Spliterator use case: streaming a large paginated result from a database without loading all pages into memory:


public class PagedSpliterator<T> implements Spliterator<T> {
    private final Function<Integer, List<T>> pageLoader;
    private int currentPage = 0;
    private Iterator<T> currentIterator = Collections.emptyIterator();

    public boolean tryAdvance(Consumer<? super T> action) {
        if (!currentIterator.hasNext()) {
            List<T> page = pageLoader.apply(currentPage++);
            if (page.isEmpty()) return false;
            currentIterator = page.iterator();
        }
        action.accept(currentIterator.next());
        return true;
    }
    public Spliterator<T> trySplit() { return null; } // no parallel split
    public long estimateSize() { return Long.MAX_VALUE; }
    public int characteristics() { return ORDERED; }
}

// Usage:
StreamSupport.stream(new PagedSpliterator<>(page -> repo.findPage(page, 100)), false)
    .filter(order -> order.getStatus() == PENDING)
    .forEach(processor::process);

👩‍💻 Mei: Explain Collectors.groupingBy, teeing, and the difference between flatMap and map.

👨 Khanh: groupingBy: downstream grouping with optional secondary collector:


// Simple grouping
Map<OrderStatus, List<Order>> byStatus = orders.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Order::getStatus));

// Counting per group
Map<OrderStatus, Long> countByStatus = orders.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Order::getStatus, Collectors.counting()));

// Multi-level grouping
Map<String, Map<OrderStatus, List<Order>>> byRegionAndStatus = orders.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.groupingBy(Order::getRegion,
        Collectors.groupingBy(Order::getStatus)));

teeing (Java 12+): applies two collectors to the same stream simultaneously, then merges results:


// Get both count and sum in one pass — no double iteration
record Summary(long count, BigDecimal total) {}
Summary summary = orders.stream()
    .collect(Collectors.teeing(
        Collectors.counting(),
        Collectors.summingDouble(Order::getAmount),
        (count, total) -> new Summary(count, BigDecimal.valueOf(total))
    ));

Before teeing, you'd need two separate stream traversals or manual aggregation.

flatMap vs map:

  • map(f) applies f to each element — one-to-one: Stream> → Stream>
  • flatMap(f) applies f then flattens one level — one-to-many: Stream> → Stream 

List<Order> orders = ...;
// map: Stream<List<Item>> — each order's items as a list
orders.stream().map(Order::getItems);
// flatMap: Stream<Item> — all items from all orders flattened
orders.stream().flatMap(o -> o.getItems().stream());

Use flatMap whenever each element produces a collection and you want to process all elements uniformly.

👩‍💻 Mei: Optional — what are the anti-patterns and correct usage?

👨 Khanh: Optional is designed for method return types to express "this might not have a value." It is NOT a general-purpose nullable wrapper.

Anti-patterns:

1. Optional.get() without checking — defeats the purpose:


Optional<User> user = findUser(id);
user.get().getName(); // NoSuchElementException if empty — worse than NPE

2. Optional as field/parameter — not serializable, extra allocation, API confusion: 


// BAD
private Optional<String> middleName; // Don't do this
public void process(Optional<String> name) {} // Don't do this

3. isPresent() + get() — verbose imperative style:


if (user.isPresent()) { return user.get().getName(); } // Use map instead

Correct patterns:


// orElse — default value (always evaluated)
String name = findUser(id).map(User::getName).orElse("Anonymous");

// orElseGet — lazy default (lambda only evaluated if empty)
String name = findUser(id).map(User::getName).orElseGet(() -> computeDefault());

// orElseThrow — throw specific exception (Java 10+ default: NoSuchElementException)
User user = findUser(id).orElseThrow(() -> new UserNotFoundException(id));

// flatMap — when mapping returns another Optional
Optional<String> city = findUser(id)
    .flatMap(User::getAddress)       // getAddress returns Optional<Address>
    .map(Address::getCity);

// ifPresentOrElse (Java 9+)
findUser(id).ifPresentOrElse(
    user -> sendEmail(user),
    () -> log.warn("User not found: {}", id)
);

👩‍💻 Mei: Method references and Predicate composition — explain the four types and how to compose predicates.

👨 Khanh: Four types of method references:

Type Syntax Lambda equivalent
Static method Integer::parseInt s -> Integer.parseInt(s)
Instance method (unbound) String::toUpperCase s -> s.toUpperCase()
Instance method (bound) "prefix"::concat s -> "prefix".concat(s)
Constructor reference User::new () -> new User()

Predicate composition:


Predicate<Order> isPending = o -> o.getStatus() == PENDING;
Predicate<Order> isHighValue = o -> o.getAmount() > 1000;
Predicate<Order> isUrgent = o -> o.getPriority() == HIGH;

// AND composition
Predicate<Order> needsAction = isPending.and(isHighValue);

// OR composition
Predicate<Order> wideNet = isPending.or(isUrgent);

// NOT
Predicate<Order> notPending = isPending.negate();

// Chaining
orders.stream()
    .filter(isPending.and(isHighValue).or(isUrgent))
    .forEach(this::process);

BiFunction — takes two inputs, returns one output:


BiFunction<String, Integer, String> repeat = (s, n) -> s.repeat(n);
String result = repeat.apply("abc", 3); // "abcabcabc"

// andThen composes with a Function on the result:
BiFunction<String, Integer, Integer> lengthAfterRepeat = repeat.andThen(String::length);

SCORECARD — Interview #90

Topic Score Key Insight
Stream lazy evaluation 10/10 Terminal drives, stateless vs stateful, short-circuit
Spliterator 10/10 trySplit for parallel, custom paged DB streaming
groupingBy / teeing / flatMap 10/10 Single-pass teeing, flatten semantics
Optional patterns 10/10 3 anti-patterns, flatMap for nested Optional
Method references + Predicate 10/10 4 types, and/or/negate composition

Mock Interview #91 — Amazon | Java Collections & Concurrency Utilities

— Amazon (US) — Java Collections & Concurrency Utilities

Role: Senior SDE II

Focus: HashMap treeify, LinkedHashMap LRU, TreeMap, Phaser, CountDownLatch vs CyclicBarrier, Condition await/signal, ThreadLocal leak, ScheduledExecutorService

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Raj (Principal SDE)

👨‍💻 Raj: What is HashMap's treeify threshold and why was it added in Java 8?

👨 Khanh: Before Java 8, HashMap used a simple linked list for each bucket. With a poor hash function or many hash collisions, a bucket could accumulate O(n) entries — making get() O(n) instead of O(1). This was exploited as a HashDoS attack: send crafted keys with identical hash codes, degrade a server's HashMap to O(n) lookup, causing CPU exhaustion.

Java 8 introduced treeification: when a single bucket's linked list grows beyond 8 entries (the treeify threshold), it converts from a linked list to a Red-Black Tree. This caps worst-case bucket lookup at O(log n) even under collision attack.

When entries fall below 6 (the untreeify threshold), the tree converts back to a linked list (less memory overhead for small buckets).

Conditions for treeification: the bucket must have ≥ 8 entries AND the table capacity must be ≥ 64. Below capacity 64, HashMap prefers to resize rather than treeify.

Treeified nodes use TreeNode — a subclass of Node that adds parent/left/right/red fields. Memory cost: roughly 2× per entry. This is why treeification only happens at threshold 8 — the memory overhead isn't worth it for small buckets.

👨‍💻 Raj: How do you implement an LRU cache using LinkedHashMap?

👨 Khanh: LinkedHashMap maintains insertion order by default, but with accessOrder=true it maintains access order — the most recently accessed entry moves to the tail. Combined with removeEldestEntry, this makes a perfect LRU cache: 


public class LRUCache<K, V> extends LinkedHashMap<K, V> {
    private final int capacity;

    public LRUCache(int capacity) {
        super(capacity, 0.75f, true); // accessOrder=true
        this.capacity = capacity;
    }

    @Override
    protected boolean removeEldestEntry(Map.Entry<K, V> eldest) {
        return size() > capacity; // evict LRU when over capacity
    }

    // Thread-safe wrapper:
    public static <K,V> Map<K,V> syncLRU(int cap) {
        return Collections.synchronizedMap(new LRUCache<>(cap));
    }
}

When get(key) is called on an accessOrder=true map, the entry is moved to the tail. The head is always the least recently accessed. removeEldestEntry is called after each put — return true to evict the head (eldest = LRU entry).

TreeMap ceiling/floor:


TreeMap<Integer, String> map = new TreeMap<>();
map.put(1,"a"); map.put(3,"c"); map.put(5,"e");

map.floorKey(4);    // 3 — greatest key ≤ 4
map.ceilingKey(4);  // 5 — smallest key ≥ 4
map.lowerKey(3);    // 1 — strictly less than 3
map.higherKey(3);   // 5 — strictly greater than 3
map.subMap(2, 5);   // {3=c} — keys in [2, 5)

Use TreeMap for: range queries, sliding window problems, finding nearest neighbor keys, scheduling by time.

👨‍💻 Raj: Phaser vs CyclicBarrier vs CountDownLatch — when do you use each?

👨 Khanh: All three are synchronization barriers — threads wait until a condition is met.

CountDownLatch: one-time countdown. Cannot be reset. Parties call countDown(), waiters call await(). Use for: "wait until N tasks have started/completed": 


CountDownLatch ready = new CountDownLatch(workers.size());
CountDownLatch go = new CountDownLatch(1);
for (Worker w : workers) {
    executor.submit(() -> { ready.countDown(); go.await(); w.run(); });
}
ready.await();  // wait for all workers to be ready
go.countDown(); // fire all workers simultaneously

CyclicBarrier: reusable barrier. All parties call await(), all released together when all arrive. Optional runnable executes before release. Use for: iterative algorithms where threads must sync at each step:


CyclicBarrier barrier = new CyclicBarrier(4, () -> mergePartialResults());
// Each thread: do work → barrier.await() → next iteration

Key difference from CountDownLatch: CyclicBarrier resets automatically for the next round. CountDownLatch is single-use.

Phaser (Java 7+): dynamic participant count — parties can register/deregister at runtime. Multi-phase support. Use for: complex parallel workflows with varying participant counts: 


Phaser phaser = new Phaser(1); // register self as overseer
for (Task t : tasks) {
    phaser.register(); // dynamic registration
    executor.submit(() -> {
        t.phase1();
        phaser.arriveAndAwaitAdvance(); // sync after phase 1
        t.phase2();
        phaser.arriveAndDeregister();   // done
    });
}
phaser.arriveAndDeregister(); // overseer done

Phaser replaces both CountDownLatch and CyclicBarrier — it's more flexible but also more complex.

👨‍💻 Raj: Condition await/signal — what problem does it solve that Object.wait/notify doesn't?

👨 Khanh: Object.wait/notify has two limitations:

1. A monitor can only have one wait set — you can't have "wait for not-full" and "wait for not-empty" as separate conditions on the same object.

2. notifyAll() wakes all waiting threads — expensive if only one needs to proceed.

Condition (from Lock) allows multiple named wait sets per lock:


ReentrantLock lock = new ReentrantLock();
Condition notFull  = lock.newCondition(); // producers wait here
Condition notEmpty = lock.newCondition(); // consumers wait here

// Producer
lock.lock();
try {
    while (queue.size() == capacity) notFull.await(); // only producers wait
    queue.add(item);
    notEmpty.signal(); // wake exactly one consumer
} finally { lock.unlock(); }

// Consumer
lock.lock();
try {
    while (queue.isEmpty()) notEmpty.await(); // only consumers wait
    T item = queue.poll();
    notFull.signal(); // wake exactly one producer
} finally { lock.unlock(); }

This is how ArrayBlockingQueue is implemented internally. signal() vs signalAll(): use signal() when you know only one waiter can proceed (one item added → one consumer can run). Use signalAll() when unsure.

👨‍💻 Raj: ThreadLocal memory leak — how does it happen in a thread pool?

👨 Khanh: ThreadLocal stores values per-thread in a ThreadLocalMap that lives on the Thread object itself. In a thread pool, threads are reused indefinitely — they never die. This means:

1. Thread T1 handles request A, stores userId=42 in a ThreadLocal.

2. Request A finishes. T1 is returned to the pool.

3. Thread T1 handles request B — but userId=42 is still in the ThreadLocal. Request B reads stale data from request A.

4. Worse: if the ThreadLocal holds a large object (a heavy Spring context, a DB connection), it's never GC'd because the Thread holds a strong reference chain: Thread → ThreadLocalMap → Entry → value.

Fix — always remove after use:


ThreadLocal<User> currentUser = new ThreadLocal<>();
try {
    currentUser.set(user);
    processRequest();
} finally {
    currentUser.remove(); // ALWAYS in finally
}

Spring's pattern: SecurityContextHolder uses ThreadLocal and calls SecurityContextHolder.clearContext() in the security filter chain's finally block for exactly this reason.

WeakReference in ThreadLocalMap: ThreadLocalMap.Entry extends WeakReference>. If the ThreadLocal variable itself goes out of scope (key becomes weakly reachable), the GC can collect the key — leaving an orphaned entry with a null key and a live value. These "stale entries" are cleaned lazily but not immediately. For long-lived thread pools, explicit remove() is the only reliable solution.

👨‍💻 Raj: ScheduledExecutorService — how do you schedule a recurring task and what's the difference between scheduleAtFixedRate and scheduleWithFixedDelay?

👨 Khanh: ScheduledExecutorService replaces Timer (which had a fatal flaw: one exception kills all scheduled tasks).


ScheduledExecutorService scheduler = Executors.newScheduledThreadPool(2);

// One-shot delay
scheduler.schedule(() -> sendReminder(), 5, TimeUnit.MINUTES);

// Fixed rate: executes every 10s regardless of task duration
// If task takes 3s: next run at T+10s. If task takes 15s: next run immediately.
ScheduledFuture<?> fixedRate = scheduler.scheduleAtFixedRate(
    () -> collectMetrics(), 0, 10, TimeUnit.SECONDS);

// Fixed delay: waits 10s AFTER task completes before running again
// If task takes 3s: next run at T+13s. If task takes 15s: next run at T+25s.
ScheduledFuture<?> fixedDelay = scheduler.scheduleWithFixedDelay(
    () -> processQueue(), 0, 10, TimeUnit.SECONDS);

When to use which:

  • scheduleAtFixedRate: metrics collection, health checks — you want consistent intervals regardless of execution time. Risk: if the task runs longer than the period, subsequent runs pile up (but execute sequentially, not concurrently — single-threaded pool).
  • scheduleWithFixedDelay: queue draining, polling tasks — you want breathing room between runs. Safe when task duration is variable.

Exception safety: unlike Timer, a thrown exception cancels only that specific ScheduledFuture, not the entire scheduler. Always wrap task body in try/catch to prevent silent cancellation:


scheduler.scheduleAtFixedRate(() -> {
    try { doWork(); }
    catch (Exception e) { log.error("Scheduled task failed", e); }
}, 0, 30, TimeUnit.SECONDS);

SCORECARD — Interview #91

Topic Score Key Insight
HashMap treeify 10/10 HashDoS attack, threshold 8, capacity 64, TreeNode memory
LinkedHashMap LRU 10/10 accessOrder=true, removeEldestEntry
TreeMap ceiling/floor 10/10 Range queries, subMap
Phaser vs CyclicBarrier vs Latch 10/10 Dynamic parties, reusability, multi-phase
Condition await/signal 10/10 Multiple wait sets, signal vs signalAll
ThreadLocal leak 10/10 Thread pool reuse, always remove(), WeakReference stale entries
ScheduledExecutorService 10/10 fixedRate vs fixedDelay, exception safety

Mock Interview #92 — Stripe | Java Language Deep Cuts

— Stripe (US) — Java Language Deep Cuts

Role: Senior Java Engineer, Payments Infrastructure

Focus: Dynamic proxy, Reference types, String internals, Custom serialization, AutoCloseable, suppressed exceptions

ROUND — TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (60 min)

Interviewer: Laura (Principal Engineer)

👩‍💻 Laura: Explain Java dynamic proxies — how does Proxy.newProxyInstance work and what can you build with it?

👨 Khanh: A dynamic proxy creates a class at runtime that implements one or more interfaces, delegating all method calls to an InvocationHandler:


public interface OrderService {
    Order findById(Long id);
    void save(Order order);
}

// InvocationHandler intercepts every method call
InvocationHandler loggingHandler = (proxy, method, args) -> {
    long start = System.currentTimeMillis();
    try {
        Object result = method.invoke(realOrderService, args); // delegate to real impl
        log.info("{} took {}ms", method.getName(), System.currentTimeMillis() - start);
        return result;
    } catch (InvocationTargetException e) {
        log.error("{} threw {}", method.getName(), e.getCause().getClass().getSimpleName());
        throw e.getCause();
    }
};

OrderService proxy = (OrderService) Proxy.newProxyInstance(
    OrderService.class.getClassLoader(),
    new Class[]{OrderService.class},    // interfaces to implement
    loggingHandler
);

How it works internally: Proxy.newProxyInstance generates a class named $Proxy0 (and incrementing) using ProxyGenerator — it implements all specified interfaces, and for every method, calls InvocationHandler.invoke(this, method, args). The generated class is loaded via the provided ClassLoader.

What you can build with dynamic proxies:

  • Logging/tracing proxies (as above) — Spring AOP uses this for interface-based beans
  • Transaction proxies — Spring's @Transactional uses CGLIB for classes, JDK proxy for interfaces
  • Caching proxies — intercept methods, cache results in a map
  • Retry proxies — retry on exception automatically
  • Permission checks — check SecurityContext before delegating
  • Mock frameworks — Mockito generates proxy-based mocks

Limitation: JDK dynamic proxy only works for interfaces. For classes without interfaces, Spring uses CGLIB (bytecode subclassing). Since Java 16, CGLIB is included in Spring to avoid module access issues.

👩‍💻 Laura: Explain the four reference types in Java — Strong, Soft, Weak, Phantom — and the Cleaner API.

👨 Khanh: Java has four reference strength levels that affect GC eligibility:

Strong reference (default): Object obj = new Object(). GC never collects as long as a strong reference exists.

Soft reference: collected only when JVM is under memory pressure (before throwing OutOfMemoryError). Use for: memory-sensitive caches where entries should survive if memory allows: 


SoftReference<byte[]> imageCache = new SoftReference<>(loadImage());
byte[] img = imageCache.get(); // null if GC collected it
if (img == null) img = loadImage(); // reload

Weak reference: collected at the next GC cycle, regardless of memory pressure. Use for: canonical maps (e.g., interning), preventing memory leaks when the referent's lifecycle is tied to external code:


WeakReference<Widget> widgetRef = new WeakReference<>(widget);
// widget can be GC'd even though widgetRef exists

WeakHashMap — keys are weak references. When a key is GC'd, the entry is automatically removed. Used for metadata maps where the metadata should disappear when the key object disappears.

Phantom reference: the referent is already finalized. get() always returns null. Used for post-GC cleanup actions — you enqueue a PhantomReference in a ReferenceQueue, and a cleanup thread processes the queue to release native resources: 


ReferenceQueue<Object> queue = new ReferenceQueue<>();
PhantomReference<Object> ref = new PhantomReference<>(object, queue);
// When object is GC'd, ref is enqueued in queue

Cleaner (Java 9+): the modern replacement for finalize(). Registers a cleanup action run after the object becomes phantom-reachable:


public class NativeResource implements AutoCloseable {
    private static final Cleaner CLEANER = Cleaner.create();
    private final Cleaner.Cleanable cleanable;
    private final long nativeHandle;

    public NativeResource(long handle) {
        this.nativeHandle = handle;
        // Cleanable holds a Runnable — must NOT hold a reference to NativeResource itself!
        this.cleanable = CLEANER.register(this, () -> freeNative(handle));
    }
    public void close() { cleanable.clean(); } // explicit cleanup
}

If close() is not called, the Cleaner runs the action when NativeResource is GC'd. The cleanup Runnable must be a separate object — if it references this, the Cleaner's PhantomReference can never be collected (circular reference). This is also why finalize() is deprecated — it can cause object resurrection and unpredictable timing.

👩‍💻 Laura: String pool and String.intern() — how does it work and when is it useful or dangerous?

👨 Khanh: The JVM maintains a String pool (also called the string intern table) — a hash set of canonical String instances stored in the native heap (moved out of PermGen to the heap in Java 7+).

String literals are automatically interned at class loading:


String a = "hello";  // interned — goes into pool
String b = "hello";  // same pool reference
a == b; // true — same object

new String("hello") creates a new heap object outside the pool:


String c = new String("hello");
a == c;             // false — different objects
a.equals(c);        // true — same value
a == c.intern();    // true — intern() returns the pool reference

String.intern() use cases:

1. Memory optimization for repeated strings: if your application stores millions of rows with a small set of repeated string values (country codes, status strings), interning can reduce memory dramatically — all duplicates reference the same object.

2. Fast equality check: interned strings can be compared with == instead of equals() — constant time vs O(n) character comparison. Used in parsers and compilers.

When it's dangerous:

1. String pool is fixed-size. Before Java 7u40, the pool was in PermGen — interning too many strings caused OutOfMemoryError: PermGen. Now it's in the heap and GC'd, but still: interning arbitrary user input (usernames, search queries) can bloat the pool unboundedly.

2. Not thread-safe without care. intern() is thread-safe (uses native lock internally) but the interning of identical strings from concurrent threads can cause contention.

Modern alternative: use String.intern() sparingly. For repeated constant strings, use a WeakHashMap as a manual intern table with GC-eligible entries.

👩‍💻 Laura: Custom serialization — writeObject/readObject, readResolve, and suppressed exceptions in try-with-resources.

👨 Khanh: Custom serialization:


public class SecureUser implements Serializable {
    private static final long serialVersionUID = 1L;
    private String username;
    private transient String password; // excluded from default serialization

    private void writeObject(ObjectOutputStream oos) throws IOException {
        oos.defaultWriteObject(); // write non-transient fields
        // write encrypted password
        oos.writeObject(encrypt(password));
    }

    private void readObject(ObjectInputStream ois) throws IOException, ClassNotFoundException {
        ois.defaultReadObject(); // read non-transient fields
        this.password = decrypt((String) ois.readObject());
    }

    // readResolve — return a singleton or canonical instance after deserialization
    private Object readResolve() {
        return UserRegistry.get(username); // return existing instance, not deserialized one
    }
}

readResolve() is critical for singletons and enums — without it, deserialization creates a new instance bypassing the constructor, breaking the singleton contract. Java enum automatically implements readResolve.

Suppressed exceptions in try-with-resources:


try (Connection conn = getConnection()) {
    conn.query(); // throws SQLException
} // conn.close() also throws IOException

Without try-with-resources: close() exception would suppress the original exception — original exception lost. With try-with-resources: the original exception is thrown, and the close() exception is attached as a suppressed exception: 


catch (SQLException e) {
    Throwable[] suppressed = e.getSuppressed(); // contains the close() IOException
    // Both exceptions preserved
}

Multiple resources are closed in reverse order of opening — each close exception is suppressed into the primary exception.

AutoCloseable vs Closeable: Closeable extends AutoCloseable. Closeable.close() declares throws IOException. AutoCloseable.close() declares throws Exception — broader. For non-I/O resources (locks, database connections), implement AutoCloseable. For I/O resources, implement Closeable.

SCORECARD — Interview #92

Topic Score Key Insight
Dynamic proxy / InvocationHandler 10/10 Runtime class generation, interface-only, CGLIB for classes
Reference types + Cleaner 10/10 Soft=memory pressure, Weak=next GC, Phantom=post-finalize; Cleaner replaces finalize
String interning 10/10 Pool in heap (Java 7+), intern() dangers, WeakHashMap alternative
Custom serialization 10/10 writeObject/readObject, readResolve for singleton
Suppressed exceptions 10/10 Original preserved, close() suppressed, reverse close order

JAVA DEEP DIVE QUICK REFERENCE

Stream Collectors

Collector Output type Use for
toList() List Collect elements
groupingBy(f) Map> Group by classifier
groupingBy(f, counting()) Map Count per group
partitioningBy(pred) Map> Split into two groups
joining(delim, prefix, suffix) String Concatenate strings
teeing(c1, c2, merger) R Two collectors in one pass
toUnmodifiableMap(k, v) Map Immutable map

Reference Types Summary

Type Collected when Use case
Strong Never (while referenced) Default — everything
Soft Memory pressure (before OOM) Memory-sensitive cache
Weak Next GC cycle Canonical maps, WeakHashMap
Phantom After finalization Post-GC native cleanup (Cleaner)

Collection Internals

Structure Internal Key threshold/property
HashMap Array of linked lists → Red-Black Tree Treeify at 8 entries + capacity≥64
LinkedHashMap HashMap + doubly-linked list accessOrder=true for LRU
TreeMap Red-Black Tree O(log n) all ops, ceiling/floor/subMap
PriorityQueue Min-heap (array) peek O(1), poll O(log n), heapify O(n)
CopyOnWriteArrayList Array copy on write Read-lock-free, expensive writes

Mock Interview #93 — Meta | Java Deep Dive Full Simulation (3 Rounds)

Role: Senior Software Engineer, Infrastructure Platform

Duration: 3 Rounds (~90 min)

Focus: ForkJoinPool work-stealing, CompletableFuture chains, JPMS, var + primitive streams, Thread interruption

ROUND 1 — RECRUITER SCREEN (20 min)

Interviewer: Jordan, Technical Recruiter

👨 Jordan: Hey Khanh — thanks for making time. I want to understand your motivation first. Meta infrastructure operates at a scale most engineers never see. Why Meta, and why now?

👨 Khanh: Two reasons. First, scale. My current middleware handles fifteen thousand API calls a day. That sounds meaningful until you realize Meta sees that many requests in milliseconds. I want to understand what engineering looks like when every small design decision affects billions of people.

Second, infrastructure focus. I have been moving toward platform-level thinking — how do you build the layer other services rely on? Meta infrastructure IS that layer. That is where I want to be.

As for timing: my current project has matured. I am maintaining more than building. That is the signal to find a harder problem.

👨 Jordan: What do you know about how Meta does engineering?

👨 Khanh: Engineers own things end-to-end: design, implementation, rollout, monitoring, incidents. Code review is serious but velocity is valued. If you ship something broken, that is on you — and that accountability builds better engineering judgment faster than any protected environment.

👨 Jordan: Does ambiguity stress you out?

👨 Khanh: I separate two kinds. Requirements ambiguity — uncomfortable but manageable. I document assumptions and flag when they break. Approach ambiguity I actually enjoy — that is where engineering judgment matters most.

The type I am still learning: when the business goal itself is unclear. I have finished a feature and discovered it did not solve the underlying problem. I now push back early with: what does success look like before I write a line of code?

👨 Jordan: Good self-awareness. Technical rounds start now.

ROUND 2 — JAVA TECHNICAL DEEP DIVE (70 min)

Interviewer: Priya, Staff Engineer

👩‍💻 Priya: I am going to go deep on JVM internals. Ready?

👨 Khanh: Ready. Let us go.

👩‍💻 Priya: Question 1 — ForkJoinPool work-stealing. Explain the algorithm and why it outperforms a standard ThreadPoolExecutor for recursive tasks.**

👨 Khanh: A standard ThreadPoolExecutor has one shared BlockingQueue. All threads compete for the same queue — under high task throughput, lock contention becomes measurable overhead, especially for short tasks.

ForkJoinPool gives each thread its own double-ended deque. When a thread forks a subtask, it pushes to the bottom and pops from the bottom — LIFO. LIFO is good for cache locality: the most recently pushed task has the most relevant data still in CPU cache.

When a thread runs out of work, instead of blocking, it steals — it takes from the top of another thread's deque — FIFO from the victim's perspective. Owner uses the bottom, stealer uses the top — opposite ends means minimal contention.

Why this excels for divide-and-conquer: recursive tasks form trees. A thread splits a task and pushes children. Idle threads steal large older subtasks from tops of busy queues — work distributes automatically without central scheduling.

The join mechanism: when a thread calls join() on a ForkJoinTask, instead of blocking it checks if the joined task is in its own deque and executes it directly — called task in-lining. If not, it processes other tasks while waiting. Threads almost never truly block.

👩‍💻 Priya: Follow-up — commonPool() versus a dedicated ForkJoinPool. When do you use each?

👨 Khanh: commonPool() is JVM-wide, shared across all parallel streams and CompletableFuture.supplyAsync() calls without a custom executor. Parallelism defaults to availableProcessors - 1.

Use commonPool() when tasks are pure CPU-bound, short-lived, no isolation needed.

Use a dedicated pool when:

1. Blocking I/O inside tasks — a task that blocks on a DB call parks a commonPool() thread, degrading every parallel stream in the JVM. A dedicated pool isolates the impact.

2. Different priority workloads — a background job should not steal threads from latency-sensitive work sharing the same pool.

3. Custom parallelism level — some pipelines need thread counts tuned to their specific bottleneck.

One useful flag: asyncMode=true switches the deque to FIFO for owner pops — better for event-driven non-recursive tasks where ordering matters more than cache locality.

👩‍💻 Priya: Question 2 — CompletableFuture error handling. Build a real chain with thenCompose, exceptionally, handle, whenComplete and trace exactly what happens when an exception fires at each stage.**

👨 Khanh: Here is the chain:


CompletableFuture<Order> result = CompletableFuture
    .supplyAsync(() -> fetchUser(userId))          // Stage A
    .thenCompose(user -> fetchOrders(user))        // Stage B
    .thenApply(orders -> orders.get(0))            // Stage C
    .exceptionally(ex -> {                         // Stage D
        log.warn("Recovering: {}", ex.getMessage());
        return Order.empty();
    })
    .whenComplete((order, ex) ->                   // Stage E
        metrics.record(order != null ? "success" : "failure")
    )
    .thenApply(order -> enrich(order));            // Stage F

Scenario 1 — exception in Stage A:

Stages B and C are skipped — thenCompose and thenApply do not call their functions on exceptional input. Stage D fires, returns Order.empty(). Stage D completes normally. Stage E fires with order=Order.empty(), ex=null. Stage F fires — enrich() is called.

Scenario 2 — exception inside Stage D itself:

Stage D completes exceptionally. Stage E fires with order=null, ex=the exception — but whenComplete CANNOT change the result. Exception propagates. Stage F is skipped.

Scenario 3 — exception in Stage F:

The chain completes exceptionally. No terminal handler — exception is silently swallowed unless someone calls result.get() or result.join(). This is why I always add a terminal .exceptionally() to log unhandled exceptions.

thenCompose vs thenApply: use thenApply when your mapping function returns a plain value T. Use thenCompose when it returns CompletableFuture — otherwise you get CompletableFuture>.

👩‍💻 Priya: How do you add global unhandled exception reporting?

👨 Khanh: Three approaches:

1 — Terminal handler on every chain:


result.exceptionally(ex -> { log.error("Unhandled CF error", ex); return null; });

2 — Custom executor wrapper: wraps every runnable to catch any throwable and route to a global handler.

3 — Pool-level uncaught exception handler: pass a (thread, throwable) handler as the third argument to ForkJoinPool constructor. Catches exceptions that fully escape thread boundaries.

In practice I combine 1 and 3: terminal handlers for known chains, pool-level handler as a safety net.

👩‍💻 Priya: Question 3 — Java Module System. Explain module descriptors, strong encapsulation, and the real problems you hit migrating a Spring Boot app.**

👨 Khanh: The module descriptor is module-info.java at the source root. Key directives:

  • requires — compile + runtime dependency on another module
  • requires transitive — re-exports to modules that depend on yours
  • requires static — compile-only (annotation processors, Lombok)
  • exports pkg — makes package accessible to all modules
  • exports pkg to mod — restricted to specific module
  • opens pkg to mod — allows reflective access (Spring, Jackson, Hibernate)

module com.myapp.orders {
    requires java.sql;
    requires transitive spring.context;
    exports com.myapp.orders.api;
    opens com.myapp.orders.model to spring.core, com.fasterxml.jackson.databind;
}

Strong encapsulation: before JPMS, every public class in every JAR was reflectively accessible. JPMS makes even public classes in unexported packages inaccessible — InaccessibleObjectException at runtime.

Problems migrating a Spring Boot app:

1. Reflection. Spring, Hibernate, Jackson reflect on private fields and constructors. Without opens directives, they throw InaccessibleObjectException at startup. You must opens every package those frameworks introspect — or use --add-opens JVM flags as a workaround.

2. Split packages. Two modules cannot contribute to the same package. Many legacy libraries split packages across JARs — moving them to the module path fails immediately at JVM startup with "module reads package from two modules."

3. Testing. JUnit and Mockito need reflective access — you need opens in test module descriptors or --add-opens in your build tool Surefire config.

My honest assessment: for application code, JPMS migration cost is high relative to benefit unless you need jlink for custom runtime images. Most teams run the unnamed module (classpath) for application code.

👩‍💻 Priya: Tell me about jlink.

👨 Khanh: jlink creates a custom JRE containing only the JDK modules your application actually uses. A standard JDK is 300+ MB. A jlinked runtime for a microservice might be 40-60 MB — only java.base, java.sql, java.logging, and your application modules.

For containerized services this matters: smaller images mean faster pulls, less disk per replica, smaller attack surface. In a multi-stage Docker build — build in a full JDK stage, copy the jlinked runtime to a minimal final stage — you can ship production images under 100MB for many services.

The catch: all dependencies must be fully modular named modules. If any JAR on your classpath is unnamed, you cannot use jlink cleanly. This is why jlink and GraalVM native image both push toward full JPMS adoption.

👩‍💻 Priya: Question 4 — the var keyword. Rules, limitations, and the one case where it changes semantics unexpectedly.**

👨 Khanh: var is local variable type inference — compile-time only. The bytecode is identical to explicit typing.

Rules: only for local variables with an initializer — not fields, parameters, or return types. Initializer must be present and non-null. Cannot use array initializer shorthand: var arr = {1, 2, 3} is illegal.

Good uses:


var userMap = new HashMap<UserId, List<Order>>(); // avoids redundant repetition
try (var conn = dataSource.getConnection()) { }   // AutoCloseable type inferred

Problematic uses:

  • var x = processOrder() — reader must look up the method to know the type
  • var list = new ArrayList<>() — inferred as ArrayList
